Review:
I like to keep my reviews short and sweet...

In full disclosure, I did not purchase my Garmin Vivosmart HR+ from Amazon, but from Target.

Likes:
- Very reasonable 5 day battery life
- Fast, accurate GPS
- Strong, reliable heart rate tracking
- Compact, non-intrusive size
- Low-end price for GPS enabled fitness watches
- Water resistant to 5 ATM (Withstands pressures equivalent to a depth of 50 meters)
- Garmin App is good and always getting better

Dislikes:
- ‘Grabby’ band holds onto sleeves
- Looks are lackluster
- Proprietary charger

Meh (i.e. no biggie):
- Companion smartphone app is okay, but it's constantly getting better.

My recommendation:
Would I recommend any semi-serious runner? Yes. It does everything I need it to do as a runner (both indoors and outdoors). The GPS is extremely accurate and the interface isn't overly complicated. The price was right (anything in the $200+ region is just too high in my opinion).

Update:
After about 6 months of ownership the screen developed three small cracks. I contacted Garmin and they promptly responded by sending a replacement unit. I've had the replacement for about 6 more months without any issues. I left my review at 4 stars for the prompt support provided by Garmin.

Review:
I purchased several fitness trackers to try. The Garmin VivoSport is the clear winner.

I bought a Fitbit, MorePro, and Garmin and wore each for several days, which included several days of normal wearing, daily 5k runs, and nights of sleep. This allowed me to experience each device’s performance and attributes. The Garmin VivoSport was the clear winner.

Some of the biggest takeaways:

- Continuously viewable screen
- Readable screen in daylight
- Comfortable and compact
- Programmable display of pace, splits, distance
- Accurate GPS tracking and mapping
- Programmable milestone alerts of distance, time, or pace
- State of the art optical heart sensor
- VO2Max calculation

Some things needing improvement:

- Nonintuitive menu system (example: finding the VO2Max screen)
- Nonintuitive app (example: finding screen to set programmable running alerts)
- Nonintuitive gestures (example: swiping screen to end a running event)

This is not a pick-up-and-use device. Unless you know the secret code, you’ll booger your running session. It’s like the super Nintendo cheat code: up-down-left-right-down-double tap. You just need to learn a few gestures, but don’t expect to use it straight away without reading some instructions.

The hardware seems to be capable of the following, but Garmin seems to be withholding a few features:

- Although the stress sensor is presumably based on HRV (Heart Rate Variability), the RR time and HRV raw data is not accessible
- Although the VivoSport is ANT+ compatible it does not transmit heart rate data (but the very similar VivoSmart does)

In summary, this is the best band I’ve tried. HRV is still in the early stages, so I’m looking forward to future firmware and software releases to enable more HRV content.

Review:
I've had the Fitbit Flex, Fitbit Force, original Vivofit, Vivosmart, Fitbit Charge HR and now have the Vivofit 2 AND the Fitbit Zip.

After having gone back and forth between Fitbit and Garmin, I am going with the Vivofit 2 (for now). Here are some reasons I switched from the most recent devices, which might help you decide between these.

*** Garmin Vivosmart *** I liked the Vivosmart but realized that I didn't really like the alerts from my phone because I already have a smartwatch (Asus Zen). If you are often in a noisy environment or can't easily access your phone but want to see alerts (and don't have some other device that gives you alerts), then the alerts on a Vivosmart could be helpful. However, it was somewhat difficut to see full messages on the screen and really best serves to let you know that a message or call has happened - it's not great for a substantive alert. Additionally, the screen is just slightly hazy. It bothered me initially although I did get over it - but it definitely has a somewhat 'frosted plastic' appearance to it. This is something other reviewers of the Vivosmart have noted. The Vivosmart is more narrow and less thick then the Vivofit 2. I suspect this is partly because of the battery size in the Vivofit 2. I found the swiping or selection responsiveness to sometimes be a little inconsistent but nothing big on that front. There is a "Find Your Phone" feature on the Vivosmart that I did find useful. The Vivofit 2 does have auto-sleep tracking. The Vivosmart did not. (A software update may have changed this.) This meant that I either put the Vivosmart in sleep mode via my phone before I fell asleep or I put the time in on the app after I woke. The Vivofit 2 doesn't require this manual effort - it does it automatically. Both devices are safe to shower and swim in.

[Battery Life in Vivosmart and Vivofit 2] I had the original Vivofit and did a lot of international travel at the time. I really liked that I didn't have to think twice about bringing a charger for it with me and I very quickly grew accustomed to not having to think about charging it. I charge a lot of devices regularly and all of them use micro-USB - not the Vivosmart. That was more annoying than I thought. To be fair, none of the most popular tracking devices use a micro-USB so the options are either a specific charger or a long lasting battery.

So for me, Vivofit2 > Vivosmart because:
1) I don't need alerts (on both wrists)
2) I don't have to charge it or even really think about charging it
3) Readability of the screen
4) Vivofit 2 has auto-sleep tracking (this may have been added via a software update to the Vivosmart - I do not know.)

If Alerts are very important to you and you don't mind charging the device regularly, the Vivosmart might be great.

*** Fitbit Charge HR *** I had this for about six weeks. The first thing I disliked rather quickly was that it instantly looked dirty or dusty. I don't know if a textured surface was the way to go. (I had a black one - maybe another color wouldn't have appeared so dirty so quickly.) Having said that, I think some people will like the design more than the Vivofit2. (Though, the Vivofit2 will now have some different bracelets to place it in - an option not available to the Fitbit Charge HR.) I was not a fan of the watch styled clasp on the Fitbit Charge HR. This is really rather minor but it made me feel like I was wearing two watches - one on each wrist. I felt over-accessorized. Fitbit Charge HR measures flights of stairs climbed - the Vivofit 2 does not. Like the Vivofit 2, the Fitbit Charge HR does auto-sleep tracking. I'll boil it down to the deal breaker for me - it's only "splash proof". It's recommended that you not shower with it and you can't swim with it. (I did see a video on youtube where DC Rainmaker said a month of showers hadn't broken the thing but it's not rated for that kind of water exposure.) That may not be a big deal for many people but more than once, I got into the shower, got it wet and then took it off and dried it immediately. I would also take it off to do dishes or bathe the dog and then forget about it. The battery life was shorter than I expected, as well. I was charging it twice a week. Alerts for calls usually didn't hit the Fitbit Charge HR until I'd already answered a call so, again, not particularly useful for me but YMMV. I realized I don't really track my HR very much. I sometimes do while I exercise but realized I don't really care about my HR at every moment. If there was a way to turn it off to save battery life and only have it kick in during a tracked activity, that would be really helpful and I might find more use for it. The Fitbit Charge has an optical HRM. These are not as accurate as a chest-strap based HRM (which the Garmin devices connect to/with) but are more convenient. Last, I found that the little nagging red bar on the Garmin devices (Vivofit, Vivofit 2, Vivosmart) really does make me get up and move. There wasn't any kind of "Hey, get up" alert on the Fitbit Charge HR.

Fitbit gives you a 10,000 step daily goal which you can manually change. Garmin devices start you at 7,500 and adjust automatically based upon your activity to encourage you to increase your steps each day. I found that I disliked the straight 10k step goal in Fitbit and liked the more substantive step-goal setting that Garmin offered. Here's where I think that makes a big difference. If you are like a lot of people who aren't very active and you start off at 5,000 steps and you do 5,000 steps - that a successful day for you. The next day, Garmin will ask you to do 5,200 (for example). And if you do it, you've had another successful day of reaching your goal. Then the next day, you do 5,450, etc. And each day, you would be encouraged to push a bit more. I find that effective for a lot of people. However, all of those days are 'fails' in Fitbit. I think that could be really discouraging to someone trying to improve their activity levels. Again, you can adjust the step goal in Fitbit but I really love that Garmin does it for me and I find it personalizes the effort for me. Again, YMMV.

Vivofit2 > Fitbit Charge HR because
1) the Vivofit 2 is waterproof to 5 ATM
2) battery life is amazing
3) the inactivity alert does work for me and
4) it connects to a HRM when I want it to (which is not FULL time)
5) some style alternatives
6) adjusting daily step goals

*** Fitbit Platform/Dashboard v Garmin Platform/Dashboard *** Here's what will be a big factor for many people that is not going to be obvious from a hardware review. 1) The dashboards for Fitbit and Garmin are different. Most people prefer Fitbit - it integrates with more applications than Garmin and it is a pretty flawless experience. Having said that - I got used to the Garmin dashboard quickly and find it easy to navigate, too. 2) If you want motivation from friends, most people have Fitbit over Garmin. This was ultimately why I tried going back to Fitbit. I wanted the motivation I got from competing with friends. I found, though, that I just didn't like their wrist-based wearables. So I ended up getting the Fitbit Zip because I could just clip it on to the center of my bra and forget about it. It does little more than track steps for me and that's the basis of all of my competitions with friends in Fitbit so... that's how I keep the wrist-based wearable I like but can pretty easily still compete with friends on Fitbit (if you find yourself also debating these things). If you happen to use both, like me, when you do "activities" in Garmin, those *can* sync over to Fitbit so your jogs, workouts, swims - all of that - can be synced with Fitbit and show up there. (Fitdatasync.com)

I realize this isn't an in-depth review of all of the features of the Vivofit2. It's really intended to point out some differences between the Vivosmart and the Fitbit Charge HR.

In the end, these are the things that made me choose Vivofit2:

1) Battery Life
2) Water proof
3) The red bar (and audible noise) really does get me up to do a quick one or two hundred steps until it disappears
4) Adjusting daily step-goals

Edited to add: I would give it 4.5 stars in reality because it is slightly larger than other trackables in its class.

*** Potential Sync Issue in Garmin Connect *** I have a Note 4 and when I first tried to pair the Vivofit 2 through Garmin Connect (the mobile app), it would initially pair, show as connected - and then immediately disconnect. This is NORMAL. The Vivofit 2 should not always show up as "connected" when you look at your devices in Garmin Connect. When it auto-syncs or when you manually sync, it will connect. Having said that, I was having trouble with it syncing. I'd try to manually sync and it wouldn't sync. I called Garmin and was told that I may have too many devices paired via bluetooth, even if only one device was actively connected. I was skeptical but I deleted about 5 of the paired devices I used the least (I started with about 10-12 and got down to 5-7 that I use the most) and sure enough, that fixed it. It now syncs flawlessly.

I’ve had this watch for a couple of weeks now and it is AMAZING! I can go days between charging, and it does virtually everything the Apple Watch can for a fraction of the price. It even looks just like it so there is no way to tell it isn’t the apple product. I have run into a few bumps but have found simple solutions:

Despite these small issues, I absolutely recommend this for anyone looking for a smart watch and not wanting to spend the money on an expensive one!

Review:
An ineffective barometer (for keeping track of stairs and elevation change) makes this a disappointing fitness tracker for mountain biking. One of the stats I like to track is elevation gain. The vivoactive 3 generally tells me it's -900 feet or so. Calibration to gps is not especially effective, and it doesn't compare to GPS at all. I know gps can be inaccurate, but on rides that should be 1000-1500' of elevation gain shows less than 500'. I will climb dozens of flights of stairs, with an understanding of how Garmin claims they are counting them, with only a few flights recorded. A disappointing key feature in a device that is otherwise excellent. This is after a couple of months of use and several email exchanges with support. Knowing this beforehand I would have either paid more for a higher end Garmin fitness watch or went with a competitor. On the plus side, battery life is great (even with gps on and 1 second recording), the touch screen is responsive and visible in varying light conditions, and integration with Strava is pretty seamless. Hopefully it's line Garmin says and a software issue that they will fix soon. Alternatively, the ability to select gps elevation (or compare the two frequently) would be useful, but as customer support told me, they have no idea how the engineers are planning on fixing it.

The altimeter is is a known issue to Garmin, I recommend waiting for them to fix it or trying another option if you care about stairs or elevation tracking.

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Notifications, Heart Rate Monitor, Daily Workout Memory,
Review:
I originally received a FitBit Charge 2 from my father in law as a Christmas gift, and I was so excited that I tried it out the same day at the gym. Much to my disappointment, heart rate tracking was woefully inaccurate during my strength training. In addition, the vibration motor was pathetic, and I could not feel any notifications. There is currently an explosion of smart watch/fitness tracker hybrids, and it was really difficult to figure out what I wanted. At the end, my priority was to have a fitness tracker and not a smart watch which is how I ultimately landed on the Vivoactive 3.

A little about myself: I workout casually with most my exercise in the form of strength training. I do not run outside so I do not use this watch's GPS features. I mostly just wanted to be able to track my heart rate and general activity throughout the day.

Pros:
+ Good vibration motor (see quality control issues below)
+ Tracking of reps during strength training is great. The automatic tracker is hit or miss, but you can easily edit your number of reps during or after your workout. Amazingly, the watch is somehow able to guess your exercise and can get it 70% of the time (although you will have to edit the nuances of the exercise: i.e. back squat vs front squat).
+ Heart rate monitoring during rest is very responsive (but so is the FitBit). Always fun to see how low your heart rate goes with sleep.
+ Many different activities that you can use to accurately track what you are doing
+ Access to tons of applications made by other users including watch faces with different data displayed based on your preferences. Most all of them are free, though some watch face cost money for additional features.
+ Water resistant (but will NOT work with swimming given that the capacitive screen goes nuts with water on the screen)
+ Garmin is very aggressively patching the watch to work out bugs
+ Watch feels very durable

Cons
- QUALITY CONTROL ISSUES! For my first watch, I could not feel the vibration for the life of me unless I was touching the watch with my fingers from the opposite hand. In addition, the watch drained its entire battery over 12 hours while I was asleep. Suspecting a dud, I exchanged the watch, and the next one was perfect. The vibrations were strong enough to wake me (but I am a light sleeper), and the battery actually lasted several days instead of 12 hours.
- Heart rate monitoring during exercise has tremendous lag time. During strength training, the heart rate monitor simply does not pick up short bursts of increased heart rates. On few occasions, the watch will suddenly pick up your elevated heart rate and have an accurate reading for the remainder of your workout. I feel like this is some sort of software issue, for the following reason. If you do not signify to the watch that you are exercising (for example, during a warm-up), it will pick up an elevated heart rate, but when you start a work-out on the watch, it will become inaccurate again. Then, after anywhere from 5-15 minutes (or never) the watch will suddenly get up to speed with your heart rate. Either way, I think many optical heart rate monitors have issues from what I read, and the Vivoactive 3 is no exception.
- Screen is not super responsive to double taps which are needed during strength training to advance the set. It's not a huge deal because it will eventually read after two or three tries. I think the screen sensitivity is likely not great to conserve battery power.
- Back light is ugly and washes out the display, but I didn't buy this watch for strict aesthetics
- Cost: this watch is NOT worth $270-$300. It seems to be going for $200 on occasion which is much more reasonable for this watch. If you are getting around the $300+ price range, look at Garmin's Fenix line though they are bulkier and much sportier in appearance.

All in all, I would buy this watch again for $200, but not more. I am very satisfied with my purchase, and I will be trying a cheap heart rate strap to address the heart rate monitoring issues. Stay tuned for updates!

Review:
After using the watch for a few days I can make the following observations

Pros:
- Responsive touch screen
- Simple UI
- Appealing main screen
- The following various features work well: Do not disturb, Find my phone, Music control, Quick Exercise start (left button)
- The app is working well so far and is easy to use. My only complaint is that the English text in it is terrible, confusing, and difficult to understand
- The battery seems to last 2-3 days

Cons:
- The cable is way too short (one foot or so)
- It's not really waterproof. Took a (cold-warm) shower with the watch. The UI glitched until the morning (I guess until it dried out, pressing the ON button delayed and flickered the screen)
- Email notifications only show my email address, not the sender's email, subject, or content. It's totally useless
- Sleep analysis doesn't seem very accurate. I was up and active for about 45 minutes, yet it included it in the Sleep analysis as "Awake" and part of my sleep
- The call notification is strange: It keeps notifying you for several seconds after you answer the call
- You cannot set the time and date of the alarm on the watch. You have to do that from the app. You can only activate and deactivate it from the watch
- Sometimes pressing the Delete icon does not remove the notification
- The watch only sometimes activates when I move my arm to see the time. It's somewhat frustrating. It never activates if you wear your watch on the bottom/back side of your wrist
- There is a delay in seeing your steps (several seconds at least), and seems to update them as a bulk. That is, it's not "real time"
- There is only a limited number of apps you can get notifications from (SMS, Calendar, Email, Whatsapp, Facebook, Yahoo)

I didn't expect a lot from the watch given its price (you get what you pay for), so I didn't have high expectations. I think you will be satisfied with it if you think of it as a fitness tracker with some very limited SmartWatch functionality

Customizable watch face is a plus, but limited selection of faces is a minus. For example, I cannot get the watch face shown in product description nor the face shown in the app product display (top right in screen capture).
Seller is very responsive and claims 100+ faces available, but most look like juvenile toyish designs rather than the elegant tech look in the product page. They advise app is currently in maintenance by engineers so the face cannot be provided.
The option to customize the watch face essentially places 1-3 lines of digital info on top of a selectable (uploadable) background - current time + 2 other info lines.
This product would greatly benefit from a tool or API to edit watch faces by selecting analog and digital component placement and appearance, beyond the very basic feature provided.
The notification feature works, but there is no support for RTL languages - the phone display shows them LTR.
I suppose this is typical for a smart watch, but most of the time it is just a blank black screen. The longest on-period available in battery saver settings is 30 seconds. There seem to be some motions or touches that wake up the display but I haven't figured out a reliable way to do that without resorting to pressing a button, so you can't just glance at the watch to see the time - you have to wake it up first.
Battery lasted around 2 days on a full charge. I have not tried sleep mode.
Overall it's a convenient tech gadget for Android users, but the unavailability of the elegant tech look in the watch face selection is a disappointment.

Review:
As a first time owner of a so-called smart watch, the Ticwatch E does what it claims to do quite well. It is reliable, functional, and relatively inexpensive compare to its competitors. I got the watch for two reasons; fitness tracking and google maps for when I go adventure riding.

I was brought up with Casio, ones with barometers, altimeters, compasses... the cheap ones not the expensive ones. Those did their jobs really well and I still have a couple on my desk. The one thing that I wanted the most was a watch that could help me navigate to my next destination without having to mount my smartphone onto my bicycle. Being able to see my route from my watch has been very helpful and has allowed me to focus more on the road rather than the safety of my smartphone.

While tracking my fitness and being able to see my route has been a great novel experience, there are a few things I wish for future smart watches. First and foremost, battery life. Second, design. For design, I would have to dish out several hundred dollars more with usually a lower battery life. To get larger battery life, the watch either has to become bigger and more expensive, or cheaper but with less functionality and reliability. The Ticwatch E is a good balance between price and reliability.

I do recommend the Ticwatch E and believe it to be the best in affordability and reliability. I hope in the near future there will be more appealing models!

Review:
The package came with the Smart Watch, unique magnetic smartwatch charge cable, User manual, a couple of issues. The first notable issue is the text on the back of the box. Look at the first two lines of text:
Scanthe QR code toenter the
app down loadaddress.

It looks like someone randomly added and remove spaces without a clue on how sentence or words work. Then it gets worst. Space between words are gone completely in the next section. It's like the spacebar gave up and left the keyboard when this body of text was typed up. Also, I noted that the letter "Funtion" maybe missing a "c" in there. This did not give me confidence about the product. I hoped the User Manual would be better but it had a few glaring issues besides missing letters. It listed features like Body temperature function that is not on the Smart Watch function list nor on the phone app. Another issue is that the User Manual tells you to swipe to get to the Functions menu to access all the functions but it only goes to the "messages" Menu. This User Manual doesn't even tell you what the two side key does. FYI, the top side key with red marking goes to the main watch interface screen on first press and the goes to the "Functions" Menu. You can swipe up and down to see all the various functions. Please note, there is no 19. body temperature function as mentioned in the User manual's list of features.. There's also a function labelled "Woman" (16.female health function?) but it states "Function isn't enabled." when it is selected. The bottom all black side button is to open the Exercise or Activity Type Menu. These are issues that can be address and fixed with a new manual and some proofreading.
There are two other issues I did not like about this Smart Watch. The first is that the Camera Control is is note accessible from the Smart Watch. You have to go to the APP and choose "Device" at the bottom then scroll down to tap on "Shake Photograph" to activate it. Next the camera comes up and you have to shake the Smart Watch to start the 3 second count down before it also snaps a photo. Taking a photo should not require so make extra steps. If I'm at the phone anyways, it would be faster to just open up the camera and take a picture. It would be more useful to have the camera control on the Smart Watch and let it be "Tap" on the Smart Watch to snap a picture. The second is issue is that the step count doesn't feature doesn't seem reliable or stable. I walked 50 steps and the steps shows 0 in the walking activity display. When it is semi-working, it doesn't really count steps accurately. I think it calculates steps by GPS distance detected. I'm not sure how other Smart Watches work but this is what this Smart Watch seems to do. Aside from these issues, the other features seems to be working correctly. Features like Find Phone, Heart Rate, Blood Oxygen, Blood Pressure, Messaging Alerts, Stopwatch, and Timer seems to be working okay. The battery life on this Smart Watch seems okay for now. I'll do another update if encounter any problems later. Overall, this Smart Watch has some good functions and most of the functions do work. But with the unstable steps counter and User manual that does not match up with the product, I can't give this product better than a 3/5 stars.

Review:
At the time of writing this review in April 2015, this product will receive only 3 stars due to issues with the automatic syncing, but if this is addressed in future updates I will change the rating.

I have used this band for a few days now and I really do love its features and its reliability. I used to primarily use a Fitbit Flex, but had three of them go through the RMA process from batteries that refuse to charge after 3 or 4 months. Having a device with a real battery that may last up to a year with no charging was a great solution to unreliable rechargeable devices and was the primary reason I purchased this Garmin. It was also super annoying with the FitBit to have to leave it charging for hours over night either sacrificing steps or sleep tracking while it was off charging.

Another feature I was interested in (and the one that resulted in the lower star review) was the automatic background syncing. This is a slightly disappointing feature of the vivofit (and cost it 1 star) in that this background sync is very infrequent. On an average day, it may sync 2-3 times. Once in the very early morning, maybe once in the late afternoon, and often before bed). The user has the option of forcing a sync by holding the device button for a few seconds, but the automatic sync would have been nice in much higher frequency.

The audio alert is very very light and subtle. A very quick, light chirp which is enough to signal to the owner that they have not been as active, but not loud or repetitive enough to bother anyone else in the room. I would not be afraid to leave the audio signals on during business meetings.

I have found the band to be accurate with steps and good at filtering out noise. Since the steps are visible on the screen (whereas they were not on a FitBit Flex) I was able to watch the band while driving and not one step was registered. While watching and walking I agreed with the steps actually registered during activity. Steps seem to be accurately computed on treadmill runs, though distance was slightly off, likely due to not knowing my own personal stride lengths.

The new lock on the band is great. I actually lost one Fitbit Flex in the ocean while swimming due to the clips coming undone, but the new Garmin horizontal locking feature holds this band very firmly and is easy enough to clasp with one hand. This is a simple but impressive feature.

I cannot yet comment on longevity or how accurate the 1-year battery estimate is, but the fact that this band NEVER needs to come off, even for showers, swimming, or charging, gives it a fantastic edge over the competition. I am also very pleased that Garmin chose to keep this model active vs. attempting to mimic other "smart watch wanna-be" devices out there. I want a fitness tracker, not a sub-par smart watch!

Edited 04/29/2015: I modified the review in that my device is now auto syncing (possibly due to a Garmin connect software update) The frequency of these updates is still very rare and costs the device 1 star, but I upgraded the score from 3 to 4 stars because it does at least automatically sync as advertised, but nowhere near as much as one might expect.

Review:
This is a pretty new field here, so I've got high hopes for the future. Also, I saw a LOT of smartwatches at CES this year, so I think this is the Year of the Smartwatch. Just to do some comparing here in comparison to a few popular models:

Pebble vs Sony SW2: Sony's got the color screen, a bigger screen, a touch sensitive screen.
Galaxy Gear vs Sony SW2: Sony's got compatibility. Yes, I have a Samsung phone, but that doesn't mean I'm going to when my contract is up. Sony SW2 is compatible with pretty much any Android phone that hasn't been hopelessly mutated by the manufacturer (and Samsung and HTC really push the envelope on that one).

Here's what I like about it:
1. Screen size is good, resolution is fine for reading text and a casual glance at an SMS pic
2. Touch response is great
3. Navigation via "android" buttons on bottom of screen is great and familiar to anyone who's familiar with stock Android.
4. Takes standard watch straps. I replaced mine the next day with a nice leather strap.
5. Native audio controls work great, but could use some alterations.
6. Most native apps work great.
7. Waterproof (IP57 rating, which is pretty good for a complex electronic)
8. NFC pairing is simple and worked great.

Here's what I Don't like:
1. Native facebook extension app doesn't appear to function properly on the SPH-L710, not sure of issue. Partially resolved by using a generic notification app.
2. no native non-Gmail email apps unless you're using a Sony phone (resolved by using a generic notification app). For example, I wanted notifications for my work MS Exchange email.

Only two downsides. I think this is the best CURRENT Smartwatch. I expect to see more features coming up, as smartwatches were a "thing" at CES 2014, so everyone and their mother will be pushing into this territory soon, but that also means more generic apps to interact with smartwatches in general. Good job on this watch, Sony.

The Connect App from Garmin is easy to use and provides lots of information on activities you specifically use the watch to record (a run, a walk, a swim, etc) and others the watch automatically keeps track of (sleep, steps, heart rate, etc). At this point, I think the only thing I'm slightly disappointed about is the built-in temperature sensor. Apparently you have to take the watch off for several minutes to allow the sensor to equilibrate to the environment, rather than your wrist temperature.

Otherwise, I'm really happy with the purchase.

Review:
There are a lot of things I like about this smartwatch. The face is smaller than other smartwatches I've looked at and tried, about 1.09" which is perfect for me. Bigger faces like many smartwatches out there are uncomfortable for me to wear, especially when I'm trying to sleep. The design of the watch is really pretty, and I like how the neutral color goes with many outfits. One thing I do wish is that the watchband was changeable so that I could change it if the band wore out or broke (or if I wanted a different style). Most of the smartwatches with changeable bands are those with the large face, a little disappointing. Battery power is good. I use the smartwatch quite often so I tend to hook it up to the battery charger twice a week. I like how I could choose from a variety of screen displays to fit what my preferences are.

The app this smartwatch uses is great, the Da Fit app. It's super easy to use, you don't have to use Bluetooth to find the watch, you just use the app to find it. I like how it has a variety of physical activity you can choose to monitor, jogging, biking, walking, etc. The sleep feature is pretty good, the biggest detractor is that it only follows typical sleeping hours. It won't work for anyone who sleeps different hours due to working night shift. Hopefully they'll update that in the app someday. The blood pressure feature seems to be pretty accurate to what the doctor tells me my blood pressure is.

Since I really don't want to put out the money for the brand name smartwatches, I am very pleased that I can still enjoy quite a few of the features for a more reasonable price. I'd recommend this watch and it would make a great gift.

Update- After a couple weeks of use, the on/off button on the side of the watch popped off and a spring came out. It's so tiny I'm unable to find it. I wasn't using it excessively, so I'm disappointed that something on the watch would break within weeks of use. It's a really nice watch, and they have great customer care so hopefully my issue is not a common problem.

Review:
Overall, I like this smartwatch!

There are some quirky things about it, like how in the settings on the watch, the vibrate on/off button is called "shake on" or "shake off," which I think is kind of funny. Also when there are no notifications to view, it says, "no notice" instead of no notifications. Neither of these things is a hassle, I just found them worth noting as it took me a few minutes to figure out what they meant.

I do have quite a few things that I like about this smartwatch:

- Seamless Bluetooth connection - oftentimes my watch will alert me to a text a few seconds before my phone even does!
- Waterproof!! This is such an amazing feature because as a busy mom, I'm constantly washing my hands (or arms, depending on how the diaper change went), so not having to worry at all about taking off my watch while washing my hands, wrists & arms is a really great feature! And honestly, you don't get that feature in some of the higher-end smartwatches, unless they're labeled in the "sports" category. So for this watch to be waterproof at this affordable price is really awesome! I also like that you can wear it while swimming!
- Battery life: it took an hour or less to fully charge up the first time after opening it, and it's been a week of day-long wearing (not at night though), and the battery is at 36%, which I feel is pretty good!
- Theater mode: this is basically a do not disturb mode that you can use when you don't want the watch to vibrate!
- Music player controls: I have found that these work really well when just controlling music on your phone, but when you add in another element of controlling music from your phone as it is being projected to a Bluetooth device, the watch has a hard time being able to start and stop the music, which, fair enough - that's multiple levels of connections!
- Weather feature: I like that this is an included aspect of the smartwatch and that it has the next 6 days worth of weather forecasts listed! I had trouble with the app confusing the city I live in with another city that also has the same name, so I just used a nearby location to get a basic estimate of the weather in my area.

Annoyances:
- Although there are app-specific on/off notification options on this smartwatch, once you move beyond the apps that this smartwatch recognizes (i.e. Calls, texts, FB & Instagram, and a few other well-known apps), then there is an option for turning on notifications for "other apps," and if you have it on, every single phone notification you get will show up on your watch, which can be a bit annoying. Although I can also see the value in it, depending on how you plan on using this watch.

A few sidenotes:
- I don't use this watch to track my sleep because I don't wear it at night, so I can't comment on that.
- I want to note in response to other review comments, mentioning the inaccuracy of the step counter on this watch - there is a spot under the profile where you can adjust your step length manually just like any other pedometer, which should help greatly with the accuracy of step counting for this watch. I hope that helps!

Review:
A the time of this review I've been using the TicWatch Pro 4G / LTE for one week. I purchased the watch with a pair of Bluetooth ear buds from Amazon for $240. This is my first smartwatch. In general, this has exceeded my expectations.

This is an Android Wear OS device with a decent suite of health and utility applications built-in or downloadable from Mobvoi, the OEM. There are also many apps and additional watch face available from the Google Play store.

The communications, messaging and notification features work well as an extension of my mobile phone when connected via bluetooth. The watch functions adequately as a standalone 4G/LTE phone when out of Bluetooth range of my mobile phone. Activating the watch on my Verizon account (the only carrier option in the US) was a snap. I recommend calling Verizon support for activation if you are using the number sharing option with your existing Verizon phone.

I've been getting about 1.5 days of use between full recharges with constant heart monitoring, an hour of exercise (while listening to Spotify via Bluetooth), sleep monitoring, all radios enabled (Bluetooth, Wifi, NFC, 4G/LTE) and a few brief phone calls.

The low power LCD "essential" display is adequate and can be easily read in daylight / bright ambient lighting without activating the LED watch face. This LCD display shows time, date steps and battery status only. Locking the watch into essential mode should extend battery life as a basic watch for up to 4-weeks . I have not verified this.

On the downside ...

The watch charging base requires the use of a low power USB charging supply. The charging does not work with quick charging sources (I cannot share my phone's USB quick charger) and Mobvoi does not ship the watch with a low power charging source.

The included documentation is poor and Mobvoi support is nearly non-existent. Fortunately there is decent community support and lots of stuff on YouTube to guide you through configuration and features.

Review:
Am over the moon happy with this purchase. Got because when at Dr. visit for moving to senior Medicare category, the folks acted like I was about to have a heart attack. More like the chance was my blood pressure was high, because I sincerely believe all the medicines my husband was taking, helped him pass on to the next life. Over medication is real. Could not get his Drs to take notice.
I also wanted the capability of reading text messages when they came in, without having to necessarily pick up the phone. This even alerts with incoming calls. No talking or sending through this watch but that’s not what I wanted. My need is for notification. Not wanting to spend additional money on a watch, that has the ability to connect to the Internet. I’ve only recently adopted a smart phone, and I must say I do love my iPhone 12.
With that said, this watch is worth the purchase. Disclaimer is it may not be a medical device. Duh! I’m only looking for a ballpark and that’s exactly what I get. BP is right on according to comparing the cuff. Oxygen levels, For my age, at the elevation I live at, are as normal as can be. I enjoy having the ability to be reminded to drink water throughout the day, as I live in the desert and can forget. I also like knowing when I’m stationary too long. Getting up to move around. Or perhaps because I am standing in one place, as in baking and stirring, I need to be reminded to do other movements.
It doesn’t hurt that the watch faces can be changed. I do this on occasion. It’s a pretty sweet feature. I also like knowing how my sleep is. I make adjustments as necessary and I am on a path to greater movement, walking more, to hit the 10,000 stride mark! For me and my needs, this watch is amazing with what it is capable of doing.
I would highly recommend for anyone looking to do as I am, to purchase this watch for your own needs. I have not had a need for Customer Service. I am grateful that everything works as designed. Crazy enough it seems as though my life is now easier. Could because I’m drinking more water, I am getting more exercise, making sure I breathe deeply, and my overall attitude has definitely improved. This watch is not a miracle worker. All we can do is give data. It’s up to you to make any changes you feel you need.

Review:
Edit: After a couple of weeks, I am reducing the star rating from a 4 to a 3. The watch still works for sending me notifications, keeping time and the steps, heart rate and oxygen. However, one of the main features I wanted from this watch was the weather. The weather aspect only worked for 2 weeks and will no longer sync. I have restarted the watch and app multiple times and finally had to completely reset the watch, losing all of my previous settings. This made the weather halfway work for 2 hours, then stopped working again. This being my first smartwatch, I'm less impressed with the functionality.

************************************************************************************************************************

This is my first smartwatch and I have nothing to compare it to, other than friends and family with Apple Watches. Compared to the price of an Apple Watch, this little guy works great for me!

The instructions are not the best. Someone that has no technical experience may struggle with setting it up. It also takes some trial and error to find some of the settings, such as the strength of the vibration on your wrist for notifications. I stumbled upon many settings just hitting buttons.

I read others had problems syncing. So far everything has worked for me except the call function, I'm unable to use that option. I have no desire to use that option anyway, so I'm not worried about it.

The screen itself has nice colors and is easy to read, albeit a bit small. I do like that you can choose different watch "faces" in the app you need to download, but it would be nice if you could edit some of these to get the information you'd like to see the most. I was only able to find one option that I liked that allowed me to see the current outdoor temperature, as well as the time and date. Speaking of the temperature, mine rarely updates and is definitely NOT showing the correct outdoor temp, so not really sure how to fix that either. However, this is a personal preference, and does not affect the overall function of the watch.

I have noticed the "wake" feature to see the time doesn't always work and I sometimes have to shake my arm or push one of the buttons to get the watch to come on. Other times it just randomly comes on when I move my wrist. It will also only stay on for 6 seconds maximum to conserve the battery, but it would be nice to have more control over how long you want the time to display, even if you have to charge the battery more.

The screen has been easy to use and has always worked when touched. As a side note, I tested the watch when taking a shower, and the water was actually causing the watch to come on and move from screen to screen. I ended up powering down the watch to avoid deleting something or changing a setting without me knowing it.

When getting notifications, the message will quickly display across the top and I have yet to find a way to make it the full screen, or even just a little bigger. It also only stays on for a second, so if you don't look down when it vibrates, you'll probably miss the notification. Just to be clear, you can't respond to any messages, these are only notifications. It would be nice to be able to make some of these minor changes.

The sleep function worked surprisingly well. I have also enjoyed seeing the health data; blood pressure, heartbeat and oxygen. They seem to be pretty accurate.

With the control buttons being on the right side, I've noticed the watch will come on and go to the menu screen when I bend my wrist too far back. Again, this is a nuisance to me with the way I'm wearing the watch, but it may also affect others.

Overall, for the price, this is a nifty little gadget!

Review:
This is my daily watch now -- consider this a three -month long-term review.

tldr I like it a lot, for being a functional and stylish enough round watch, that has access to the Google store (meaning a huge selection of watch faces!)

Although built off Google's os, the software tweaks by Mobvoi and fast hardware combined equal a much smoother, lag-free experience. Much improved from my experiences w plain Jane Wear OS on previous tech (including the previous iteration of this guy, the C2) which always were a bit laggy.

Speaking of the back to back comparison, the two watches are roughly the same size, diameter -wise, but I was surprised to see how much thicker the c3 is, presumably given an the new tech they cram beneath it.

Health readings, steps, all related health telemetry works perfectly fine to the extent that I use it (minimal, aside from step count). It does pick up an O2 oxygen reading better than any other wearable I've tried, but this is still the most difficult thing to measure accurately, I'd guess, from the wrist.

The screen is big and great and bright enough for any amount of daylight--i err between the dimmest setting for inside to the 2nd dimmest outside (1-5 iirc). Quite nice screen, esp since I usually do 'always screen on' with the attached watchface..

Battery life is acceptable, but that's still a low bar for smart watches. I've had some of the smaller devices with longer battery life (measured in weeks not days or, god forbid, hours) and it's really great to be confident ditching the charger on short multi-day trips.

This is not necessarily THAT watch. (Mine in that case is the Ticwatch GTH pro with an honest week-long life on one charge, used judiciously.)

The C3 improves on the C2 in battery length, but I ended up turning always screen on off --if worn to bed, I didn't find a habit that reliably allowed me to charge it enough to keep it charged. Losing battle charging in the morning. This was with afaik all the settings 'ON'. (!)

Currently, I'm simply not wearing it to bed often-again, rather thick!- so it has time to charge overnight.

That's my solution. If you're efficient with battery use, this can go 3-4 days--but you'd need to be planning from jump street.

Overall, it's a great watch! And sometimes wearables don't even meet the standard of crappy watch... The device is snappy in menus/responding to commands it looks great, like a time piece, and uses a regular watch strap (meaning I have a ton already compatible--10mm iirc).

The screen is great but not whack -your-socks-off colorful and vibrant. It's highly legible and a high enough resolution to where there's no visible pixels.

For the price, it's a phenomenal watch

Review:
I bought this on sale on the Amazon Prime days, but even at regular price, this is a great smart watch for the price. It uses Google Wear OS. It has a bright screen and is easy to use, especially if you are used to Android phones.

Pros:
- Easy to use
- Affordable
- Tracks steps
- Integrates with Google Fit and has a number of other fitness apps available.
- Easy to pair with my Android 7.0 phone.
- Accuracy when using GPS (internal or connected to phone) is good.
- Reminders to walk.
- Connects to calendar and email to display alerts on screen.
- Can load watch specific apps on it.
- Can answer phone from it (although you still have to talk on the phone itself - this might be a setting I need to change).
- Can change the watch face.

Cons:
- The battery life is not great, even with disabling GPS and not turning on "always on". I can't really go more than one day without charging. I work out at night and sometimes I have to trickle charge it before my work out if I didn't start the day with a full charge.
- I had some issues with it locking up at the start of my work out for a few days in a row, where I had to reboot the watch. I think it might have been related to a Google Wear OS update though.
- The accuracy on a treadmill is about 1/4 mile off every time as long as I swing my arms. If I don't, then its way off. This is a Google Wear OS issue and not an issue with the watch itself (so if you buy a really expensive watch that uses Google Wear OS, you'll have the same issue).

Honestly, besides the battery life, this is a great watch. My complaints are all with Google Wear OS and would be an issue with any other Google Wear OS based watch.

Review:
Ok - first of all, I love Garmin products. The Vivosmart HR+ is the newer generation of the Vivosmart HR with the addition of GPS.

Best uses - Daily activity tracking, route tracking, running. I have not really used the HR+ for route tracking in remote areas, so I cannot say how reliable that might be, however, it is highly accurate for any marked roads/routes.

Battery - I can get the battery to last about a week if I am not using the Bluetooth or GPS. Depending on how often you use the GPS and frequency of uploading your data, your battery life will fluctuate. I run three to four times per week and upload after each run and my battery will typically last four days. If you are not using those features you should be able to get up to 7 days of battery life.

Features - steps, calories, heart rate, sleep, activities, and health stats. All work pretty good. Have to use Garmin Connect to offload the data, which is fine with me. I then sync my running data with Nike Plus and that works great as well.

Construction is really good - rubber type band that does not hold odor (good thing). Face is touch sensitive to switch between views and works really well. HR Sensor takes a lot of abuse and seems to be very accurate. Charger is a little fussy to snap in at times - just make sure you get the battery charging progress bar once you connect.

Only shortfall for me has been the GPS signal connection. There are times when I hit GPS and it connects to satellites right away, then at other times it seems to take way too long. Granted, that is possibly the size of the device, battery charge, and position of the satellites. Still, though, it is a little slow.

All-in-all a great device and I highly recommend this activity tracker over some other much more expensive devices. Give it a try.

Depending on how often you use the GPS and frequency of uploading your data, your battery life will fluctuate. I run three to four times per week and upload after each run and my battery will typically last four days. If you are not using those features you should be able to get up to 7 days of battery life.

Review:
As an owner of the original Pebble watch (I bought the black plastic version over a year ago), I was hoping the Pebble Time Steel (PTS) would be an upgrade in all respects, especially in appearance. While I'm satisfied with the PTS (it looks much more refined), I'm somewhat disappointed with the readability of the new color display. I would have to say that the original Pebble watch is easier to read in typical indoor lighting, including an office environment. With the PTS, I find myself having to turn my wrist to just the right angle to get the ambient light to reflect toward me. And even then, I often find myself having to flick my wrist to get the display to light up for a few seconds. I also agree with several other reviewers who have pointed out that the bezel is rather large compared to the active display area.

I think Pebble recognizes these two shortcomings, and has deliberately avoided exposing them to potential buyers by (1) only publishing photos that hide the true extent of the bezel area, and (2) supplying retailers with non-functioning mock-ups that don't allow a buyer to evaluate the display's true legibility. Shame on you, Pebble.

Despite these two shortcomings, I am satisfied with the PTS. I replaced the original leather strap with a brushed-steel band that compliments the watch's matte-steel finish, so now the watch looks great. I'm also satisfied with the way the watch acts as an extension of my iPhone -- echoing my notifications so I don't have to pull my phone out during meetings. The new OS is nice, too. I like the timeline concept.

Review:
I had an old apple watch that developed a battery problem. Apple wouldn't fix it and said that even though it was a hardware issue, all I could do was pay several hundred dollars to get a new one. That wasn't financially possible so I forgot about it for the most part, although occasionally I would still miss having it.

When I saw this, which looks quite a lot like my old apple watch but for a fraction of the price, I wanted to give it a try. It does a good job with notifications and has many functions for tracking heart rate, blood oxygen, steps, calories burned etc. It's small enough that I don't really notice it's on but I still think I wouldn't be comfortable wearing it to sleep to take advantage of the sleep tracking.

That being said, there is one big negative for me. It is related to the app connected to the watch. When setting up the watch, you have to make an account on their app. The app itself is simple but has some nice features like achievements. The thing I really have a problem with comes after you are asked for your height and weight at sign up. I don't object to putting that info in since it does factor into things like blood oxygen and blood pressure and all. What I object to is that it then immediately sets a goal for you to lose weight (I assume if your BMI isn't "healthy"). You can adjust this goal (I changed it to my current weight) but I couldn't figure out a way to make it go away completely. This can be incredibly detrimental to people with body image issues or eating disorders. I don't mind it having the ability to set a weight loss goal if that is your desire but it is forced on you at sign up, which I don't agree with.

My other big negative personally is that the customizations that made the apple watch so fun aren't present at all here. There are a few watchface options but very few I cared for. And as far as I can tell you're stuck with the one band forever. I don't mind the purple but it doesn't match everything and part of the fun is customizing the band/watch face as you chose. It's a pretty minor qualm as things go, given the huge price difference, but it is something to take into account.

If those things don't bother you and you're looking for an inexpensive apple watch substitute, this one is pretty nice. The interface on the phone itself is usable although it's not as fancy or modern as my old one was. Still, for the price point, I can't complain.

Review:
If I were just rating the watch hardware itself, and taking into consideration the $50 price tag, this would be an amazing five-star value. Again, in the context of $50, the watch case and strap are well-done and don't scream "cheap." The display is competent and the user interface is good. Everything the watch itself is responsible for seemed to work really well.

You charge the watch with a magnetically coupled connector on the underside of the watch which then plugs in to a USB charger that your provide. It all works well, and at this price point is a perfectly reasonable solution (vs. wireless charging).

But as is often the case with hardware designed by thoughtful engineers, the software people come in a screw everything up! (kidding... we love you, software people!) The app that you are instructed to use with the watch is READSport available on the Google Play Store for Android. My first hint at trouble was the fact that the app was rated with 3 stars, which I now think is generous.

My first complaint about the app was the poor translation of certain phrases to English. It slowed me down, but through trial and error I was able to figure out what was going on. It wasn't too bad. Once I got the app installed and connected to my watch (easy) I thought things were going well. But at the end of the first day my phone suddenly complained about only having 12% battery left! Typically at the end of my day I'll have no less than 40% charge remaining. Not making the connection to the new app, I recharged and started my next day only to have the same thing happen. Then I realized it was probably the new app.

Checking the Android-reported estimate of app battery usage, sure enough: Almost 30%!

That is grossly unacceptable. But the watch won't do much beyond tell the time, count steps and measure your pulse without the app. This largely makes the watch useless in my view as I need my phone's battery to last the entire day. (And for those who question whether my battery is healthy, yes, it is a new phone and uninstalling the app immediately resolved the battery drain issue.)

Other problems I noticed:

- When using the app and receiving an SMS, the app will only look up the name in your Contacts if you have stored the phone number in this format: +15555551234. If that's in your Contacts as 555-555-1234 or even 1-555-555-1234 the app won't figure it out and will display +15555551234 on the watch instead of the name. Many people complained about this in their app reviews, though I don't think they figured out this workaround. So, if you want to see contact names on your watch, be prepared to edit every one of your contacts to match the hard-to-read format! Not!

- The product listing claims there is a blood pressure and blood oxygen function. I think "blood pressure" is another poor translation to English issue and they mean "pulse" which is indeed a function of the watch. I could not find any way, however, to access the blood oxygen content (SPO2) information.

At $50 I am not expecting an Apple Watch experience. But even at $1 the watch is useless as a smartwatch because of the battery consumption of the companion app. Fix that, and this would be an amazing value!

Review:
My previous smartwatch was the Garmin Vivoactive HR. It was my first smart-watch and I chose it specifically because it had GPS and it was waterproof. An Apple watch with similar features either didn’t exist yet or was 3x the price – and that device needs to be charged every night (SMH). Long battery life and HR monitor were nice bonuses that I now feel are necessary in any decent smartwatch. As for how it looked, however, not much better than a plain old fitness band or bracelet.

When the V3 came out, I started to envy the cool looking watch-ness of it … but when my VivoActive HR went bad, Garmin replaced it with another HR! Very cool of them but that meant I was in for the long haul because I couldn't justify buying something else. THankfully, I wore the HR playing hockey a few months back and a puck saved the DAY! SMASH! Once again Garmin tried to save me by offering 20% off with a damage trade-in but that wasn't going to cut-it for me ... I needed to look around at the hundreds of wearables before I decided ...

So I ended up with the Coros Apex 42mm. WHY? The month-long battery life was appealing to me and it seemed to be a nice sports watch with a good look. Looking back I wonder if it just had better photos online than the Garmin VA3!

I added some comparison images to this review if you care to see what the Coros Apex looks like in comparison. But either way that Coros Apex watch really made me miss the Garmin VA HR! Yes the Coros battery can last so long you forget about it, but I think that's because the screen is just really DIM! I missed the touch screen, the Garmin ecosystem, the music controls, the almost excessive number of sports and workouts you can choose, the quick access to many available menu items and just the general finished and ready feeling the Garmin has. I don't want to slam the Coros but it just isn't ready for prime time. Turns out I really like some of the SMARTwatch stuff rather than just the fitness stuff and the Coros is pretty much for ultra runners who want to track their activity with GPS for 24 hours straight.

OK back to the product at hand … or on wrist … The Vivoactive 3 has everything the HR did but it entirely more stylish. It also has a ton of custom faces which make more fun if you are into that sorta thing. Seriously though - who doesn't like fun? The most surprising thing was how good this watch looks. It is the perfect size for my alien-like thin-guy wrists. Big enough to read but it doesn't feel like I'm carrying around a discus on my arm. I added a comparison image with the HR as well (don’t mind that the HR is smashed)! Hey, who doesn’t get smashed every now and then?

In conclusion, Vivoactive 3 is the bomb diggidy. Great look with all the features I enjoy. Life’s just too dang good.

Review:
If you're going to get a "smart" watch, I'd recommend this one.
Negative:
- none of the watch faces look real. Some look REALLY bad. Meaning, if you get the Apple Watch, you can choose watch faces that look like you have an actual analog watch, high class and pretty. This one is very clearly just a picture of a high-class watch. The colors aren't vibrant enough.
Positives:
- it reminds you to stand up and walk around if you've been sitting too long. It tracks your heartbeat. It gives an alternate estimate of calories burned in an activity. So for a smart sports watch, it seems to hit all the main things you need.
- Battery life is great. I can wear this for almost 2 weeks, and then charge it within a few hours.

Overall, I'm not a huge fan of smart watches. The Apple Watch seems almost useless. All it does, really, is give you heart rate and the ability to not take your phone out of your pocket about half the time.
It was more of an annoyance than a help.
So this watch, not being intricately linked to iOS, only vibrates for phone calls. That's enough. It doesn't really do much with my iPhone, but that's okay, because anything I want to do on my phone, I'm going to actually use my phone instead of an Apple Watch anyway.

So *this* Amazfit watch is better for me. Far cheaper, but far more functions.

I haven't used the sleep tracking much, because I was having some problems falling asleep for a while, and wearing a watch (which I'm not used to) was jut making it harder to fall asleep.
Although I just realized it detected the nap I took yesterday, which is interesting.

Overall, this watch does everything it says it will, and does it well. The only shortcoming is the watch faces don't look that great. And smart watches in general seem to be *most* useful to people who bike, run, or do other fitness activity that don't already have built-in calorie burning counters.

Still, I like this enough to not return it, so that's a solid 4 in my book.

Review:
This is a good introductory smart watch. The biggest knock on this product is the quality of instructions (those with the product lack a lot of information though the product page is much better though still incomplete) for setting the watch up and connecting it to your phone. The app used to pair the device to a phone is also basic and doesn't provide the same information the watch does - there's no real command center to manage the device.

Other than the trial-and-error in getting it set up, the watch is quite full-featured. Once you turn on the hidden apps, as well as find the icon menu for all the functionality (press the side button from the home screen), you can gather workout data of all types including a step counter, check the weather, monitor your sleep rhythms, check messages, make a call, and check your pulse rate, blood oxygen level, and blood pressure! This watch pulls a lot of information. There's no great home screen to see everything, so you have to go to the various apps on the watch to get certain data, but the small touch-screen is sharp and easy to read.

For the price, this is a good introductory smart watch. This product is made in China, as is the app that needs to be installed on your phone. Note that per the FitCloudApp data policy, they will capture and analyze your personal and health information.

Review:
Although I'm not the person glued 24x7 to my phone, I work in an environent that makes pulling out my phone to check a text difficult, so I originally opted for the TicWatch S2 and have enjoyed the quality and build of it. As soon as I saw the Pro 4G go on sale, I snapped it up! It has the durability of the S2, the style of the Pro, and the weight of a real watch-- not plastic feeling.

After a couple weeks now of wearing it, I honestly can't find one thing I dislike. The textured bezel made it so I didn't feel it needed an extra case and the aluminium rear feels better than icy steel putting it on. The ceramic feel to the case doesn't weight down on your arm even after 12 hour shifts.

One feature I wasn't expecting, but is ingeniously useful is the speaker clearing from water following submersion or exposure. You hold down the top function button and select it from the list. A tone plays through the speaker to pop any water surface tension and clear the speaker out.

The Mobvoi mobile app synced without issue and gives a nice stylish overview of your steps, heart rate, and sleep tracking. I'm not into fitness enough to appreciate all the tracking, but when I have done workouts with this, it keeps up nicely.

If you're new to smart watches, this is a much better experience than starting off low-end or from an unheard of company.

Speaking of price, if you're nervous about wearing a $300 watch on your wrist, like I was at first, the FSTN display makes this look like a regular LCD watch. Still nice and stylish, but not like it's a smart watch.

If it's within budget, buy it! If not, the S2 is a really close second, just not as snappy.

Review:
I tried the 5X, and it was good, but the Tactix Charlie looks better and feels much lighter on the wrist. The extra features of goggle mode and robust materials make the Charlie my preference. Coming from Pebble (First Gen and Steel), Huawei watches (multiple first gen, the Classic 2 and Sport 2), LG (Watch R, G watch), and Asus (Zenwatch Gen 1), the 10 plus day battery life is just awesome.

I am not a big fan of the OEM strap though. is is too tight, or too loose. There are tons of aftermarket options though. The canvass ones are cheap at about $10 and are one of my favorite feels. They require the special star nut screw driver; make sure you have it or the one you purchase comes with it.

There are also a lot of custom watch faces to be had in the Garmin Connect IQ application/website. Most have free functionality, but there are numerous ones that charge to customize further. For $2-3, worth it.

I did run into an issue where the Charlie wouldn't accept the new update via phone transfer, but installing from their website and my PC was successful. It also updated the maps and GPS info that way. Not convenient, but it worked.

Update 12/3/18: The more I wear this watch, the more I like it. Beats EVERY other smartwatch, for me. Battery life is just wonderful. The workout tracking is superb. I didn't know this, but with strength training, it can actually guess what exercise you're doing. It isn't 100% spot on, but in the 60% range. This is such a beneficial feature that I was not aware of. I've never kept track of my weightlifting prior to this watch. Now there is a interface that is encouraging! Obviously I'm no Adonis, but I'm beating yesterday! (Garmin plug/pun)

Above all others, this is the tops!! I'm so happy I skipped on the 5x plus.

Review:
I wish Pebble didn't go under, as (so far) it's the only smartwatch I'll buy. It's affordable, doesn't have unnecessary extra features like GPS or a touch screen, amazing battery life, and stylish. I bought a pebble time round as I thought it was the perfect smartwatch, but after a week of using it I bought this one to replace it. I thought I could live with the 2-3 day battery life, but once you're used to only having to charge a watch about once a week it's hard to switch. I previously owned and wore a Pebble original steel, but when I heard Pebble went under I wanted a replacement as the original steel has a known screen tearing issue and mine was already starting to show early onset. I've had no problems with this watch and I've owned it for almost half a year now.

When I first got a smartwatch I thought I would regret it and I made a mistake, but it's been a valuable tool for me. I have ADHD and am very forgetful, so this helps me not forget things or know when I get an important message or call while at school or work as I always have my phone on silent. I use google calendar, but I don't always look at my phone so sometimes I can miss a reminder. This also helps me look at my phone less, and keep it on silent instead of vibrate, since I can look at my watch and know if I need to address the notification right away. I also blocked some notifications (like facebook) from showing on my watch, so I know if my watch vibrates it's probably something I want to check.

Customization is great with this watch. There is a huge pool of watch face options thanks to users being able to create and upload their creations, and unlike the original pebble steel the watchband is standard so you can easily swap bands. There is also a company that creates skins for the bezel.

As mentioned I love the battery life. I've gotten over a week with this watch, especially if I keep it on a simple watchface. One reason I prefer this over a regular watch is the fact I can recharge it. Even though watch batteries last awhile, sometimes it was just easier for me to buy a new cheap watch than to try to see if a store would replace the battery (at the time I wore traditional watches still, I lived in a more rual area, so I was limited by big box stores and if they would actually change it out or say "sorry we don't cover this"). I could do it myself, but I always got water resistant watches and knew with my inexperience I'd probably ruin the seal.

Which leads into another great aspect of this watch, while it's not a good idea to go swimming in it (despite what Pebble says), it's great to not worry about getting water on it while doing dishes or washing your hands. Some people have had no problems taking showers and swimming with it, but since Pebble can no longer honor warranties it's not worth the risk as I've heard of others having their watch get ruined.

TL:DR, this watch is great and has what you need for a decent price (especially now). I hope it lasts a few years until hopefully another company comes along and makes similar watches. I am not interested in the expensive, not always on display ones that are currently in the market with terrible battery life.

Review:
I decided to start GeoCaching and for a reasonably priced GPS that will work with GPX files to make GeoCaching much easier, the Magellans were the ones in my Budget. I chose this one over the 110 model since you can load additional maps on it, though the regional topo map that came 'bundled' with my particular unit takes up ~364MB of the 500MB free space on the device.

That brings me to another point. I purchased the Topo Map Bundle edition for $10 more than the base model. With the regional topo maps being $49, this seemed like a good buy to me. Be forwarned, my unit had a sticker on the box with a code and URL to redeem it with. The URL comes up with a 404 Not Found and then redirects to the magellangps.com homepage. It took a call to support and speaking with 3 agents to find out how we could redeem our code for the free map.

First you have to register your unit online. You will see very quickly the magellan site is not user friendly. It constantly opens stuff in new windows, the URLs in the product manual no longer exist and don't redirect properly to the new page, and it likes to randomly dump you to a login page without telling you why. So to register your product, first set up an account and then try to register and it is a breeze. You can't register your product without setting up an account.

To redeem your code, you have to go through their store like you are purchasing the map bundle you want free and enter all of your information including credit card number before you get to a final confirmation page that will let you enter your code in the 'coupon code' field before hitting submit. That's right you have to divulge your credit card number to get something that is supposedly bundled. Anyway on the last page, once you get a zero balance for your bundled map, hit submit order and a few minutes later you will get an email with a download link.

The download is small but opens a program to download the actual map files. You would think then it would prompt you to connect the device and install them for you, but it just finishes and leaves you with a cancel button. All you have to do is get the two files, connect your GPS and go to the removable drive and then the MAPS folder and copy them there and reboot your GPS. Now you go to Tools -> Options -> Maps and select your new maps to enable use.

My GPS also came with outdated software on it and to update it, you have to download a file from the magellan site and this program you download will actually walk you through and will copy the update files to the GPS for you. I should mention you need to be on the latest update before downloading maps.

So the initial setup and actually getting my bundled maps was annoying and time consuming. The customer support reps were not very helpful and the 3rd person actually at least understood what we were trying to do and helped us get the maps, but didn't seem to care that it was a hassle. I am a GPS snob and it's always been Garmins or nothing, but a good Geocaching Garmin GPS is $300-$450 so I wanted to try out the Magellan at the $159 price point. I wasn't sure I was going to like Geocaching (I do now that I have gotten some) and didn't want to spend any more money to get started and certainly didn't want to try to use the GPS in my phone.

This Magellan is very easy to use for Geocache finding and natively supports GPX files to make loading caches and related data like descriptions, logs, and hints very easy. I've been out using the GPS the past two days and found my positioning to be accurate and the features like notifying when there is a cache nearby handy. I also agree with another reviewer's suggestion to disabled night mode and turn off auto zoom and want to pass those along in my own review. The unit seems to be nice and sturdy and ready to take the abuse that comes along with finding geocaches. I do recommend ordering screen protectors. I ordered them after the fact, but left the little plastic sheet on the screen that came on the unit until my proper protectors arrive. Also, it may just be Energizer Lithium batteries that came with the unit, but I've had it on for 12 hours over the past two days and it still show the battery full on the meter. Considering this is rated for 18 hours, I would say the battery life is excellent. It is worth noting that the dollar off coupon for energizer lithiums that came with the unit expired 12/31/2012.

In summary, once you get the unit updated and all set up, it works great. I am not impressed at all with the Magellan website or the interactions we had with the support line and hope and pray I never have a problem to where I have to deal with them again. I guess that is the trade off for a less expensive unit. Also, my unit was made in China for whatever that is worth to people.

Review:
I've owned a lot of Android Wear and Wear OS watches and this is the first Wear OS watch I truly like! The Snapdragon 4100 CPU is the real deal. No lags, no crashes, it just works! I've always been unhappy with previous watches because they were underpowered. This Ticwatch E3 is feature packed with NFC, continuous heartrate monitor, blood oxygen detection, GPS, sleep monitoring, and more!

Battery life lasts almost 2 days for me even with continuous heart rate monitoring turned on and wearing it during sleep. Everything syncs perfectly to Google Fit and its own TicHealth app. I like that it can read blood oxygen level too. There is also a decibel level reading and stress level reading. Stress level reading takes a very long time to read though so I didn't like that.

Watch comes with a lot of built in watchfaces, several that I like and some are customizable. You can download more using the Mobvoi app or of course get a lot more from the Play Store. Watch comes with a simple black silicone band. It only only has 1 loop, I would have preferred 2 loops so that the band doesn't flap around. I'm disappointed it does not use an OLED screen like a lot of other watches. I've also noticed that placing your hand on the screen doesn't turn it off like a lot of other watches. The default display timeout is 5 seconds.

Everything else about this watch works great, NFC for Google Pay. The speaker and microphone works well and is loud enough to hear outside for Google Assistant and calls. Lots of settings available to customize the display, gestures, sounds, vibration, etc...

I'm a big watch collector and tech nerd and I really like this Ticwatch E3 and will be using it as my main watch for the foreseeable future. Also I am VERY happy at the recent announcement that Google has confirmed that the Ticwatch E3 will receive the upcoming new Wear OS 3!
