Review:
Pebble makes an awesome product and this is what a smart watch should be. This type of product has been going through an identity crisis lately. Is it a watch that does other things... a cell phone on your wrist... a GPS fitness tracker? Pebble invented the smart watch and hasn't strayed too far from the original concept. Don't be distracted by the $300+ "competition" that attempts to place a cell phone on your wrist. If you want to make phone calls from your wrist, view high resolution postage-stamp sized pictures or hand-write tiny messages on a touch screen, this is not the watch for you. If you want a literal "smart" upgrade from a standard watch, this is the product for you. It tells the time and allows some functionality with your phone... great for not having to pull you phone out every time you receive a notification.

PROs:
-Tells the time, first and foremost, with an "always on" e-paper display
-Watch faces are infinitely customizable (you'll spend the first few days draining the batter while playing around with the different faces)
-Battery lasts 5-7 days, depending on use
-Waterproof up to 30m
-Fitness functionality - steps/sleep/HR. This is adequate for most people who aren't elite athletes.
-Notifications from just about every app can be sent to your phone. I have an iPhone and haven't had any issues.
-Ability to send and reply to texts from your wrist, using "canned responses" or voice to text. Both work great.
-A large selection of apps (e.g. Uber, weather, commute times, voice to text to-do lists, etc.)
-Simplicity and a clear display that's easy to read in sunlight.
-Reasonable cost. I was able to get this for $80 through a Black Friday deal.
-OS is intuitive and easy to operate

CONs:
-B&W Display. No surprises, I knew this when buying it. However, having owned a PTR, the color e-paper display is far superior. Why not permanently upgrade the product line and retire the B&W display? This is a minor complaint.
-Design. The plastic case has a cheap look to it. You can overcome this with one of the many "wraps" available for purchase. I got a nice wood-grain wrap that gives it a much more sophisticated look. The silicone band, on the other hand, is incredibly comfortable with a stretchiness to it.
-Limited App Functionality. There are some great apps for this watch, but many have their limits. Take the Yelp App. If you're looking for a highly rated restaurant nearby, this helps identify one quickly. It shows contact information and even pictures of the meals, just like the full app. If I'm going that deep into a Yelp review, I'd rather just pull out my phone.

If you're reading this review, you're probably well aware of the rumored acquisition of Pebble by Fitbit. The speculation on how this will impact the future of Pebble has created panic. As an original Fitbit supporter (I think the Zip is still one of their best products), I see this as a good opportunity for both companies. Fitbit makes a hell of a fitness tracker and Pebble makes a superiod OS/watch ecosystem. As the market struggles to come to grips with what a smart watch should be, Fitbit/Pebble has the opportunity to redefine it by leveraging the best of both companies. The continuation of e-paper displays, especially if a higher resolution option is available, can help give them a definitive edge in prolonged battery life against the Apple Watch and Samsung products. Don't get me wrong, the competition has some amazing products. Playing around with the Apple Watch reminded me of seeing the iPhone for the first time. It's beautiful, but do I really need all of those capabilities... no. I also don't like the hassle of having to charge it every night. With the P2, you could conceivably forget your charging cable on a short trip and not have to sweat whether or not your battery will last. Garmin also makes some good watches, but those are best geared towards more elite athletes, campers/hikers or anyone else that has frequent need for GPS with extended battery life.

Bottom line: Pebble represents a great product that excels in most people's common needs for a smart watch. It keeps me more focused at work without having to constantly check my phone.

It has a speaker, mic, pedometer, heart rate, and all the app Android wear has to offer. The battery life is my only complaint with EVERY smart watch. So if you are thinking about this watch and can spend the extra 50 I would recommend the pro. If you want something cheaper, the Ticwatch E is the exact same for 40 dollars less the difference between the two is the more advanced GPS in the arm band in the S model for better tracking when taking walks and runs.

Lost story short, I love this watch and would recommend anyone, new to smartwatches or not!

Review:
Coming from a Ticwatch 2, I had high expectations for the TicKasa N1, even though it is about a fourth of the price. I scrapped my old watch since its battery life has since decayed beyond day to day use after multiple years. Still, given my excellent experience with that watch, I thought I would try again with Mobvoi (now known as TicKasa) to see how they have progressed in the industry of budget watches. Initially, I was taken aback by the lighter feel of the watch as it seems to have a casing made of plastic instead of metal, and the touch of the screen operates from directional gestures rather than following your finger. However, aside from those initial drawbacks, I have come to realize in my past month of using it that it provides a stunning experience that I would have never imagined.
Firstly, the battery life on this watch is incredible. It has been three to four weeks since I first charged it and it still has nearly 40% battery left. Gone are the days of needing to drop my smartwatch on the charger the second I get home. You can easily put this watch on and off daily just like any traditional watch and not worry about charging at all. Moreover, since the features are relatively basic and easy to use, I can wear this watch and not get distracted by all the options and features of a full-blown smartwatch (like my previous). I realized that this watch was amazingly simple to set up and the companion phone app was very easy to use. I truly appreciated that there were not weird hoops to jump through just to get it working and to make sure it continued working. I’d imagine the low maintenance of this watch could make for a good gift for non-tech savvy friends or family due to its simplicity to use.
A few gripes I do have about this watch are that (1) it does not have an always-on display, (2) the watch face selection thus far is limited, and (3) I wish some of the features from the app were available directly from the watch. Unfortunately, no other method can truly beat the convenience of an always-on display; however, TicKasa has done an excellent job of implementing the wrist gesture to turn on the display to show the time and put it down to hide it. If this is something you are okay with, it should work well (bear in mind, this probably helps make the battery life so long). The watch face selection could use work since there are only four faces available, two analog and two digital. It would be nice to see some sort of watch face store in the future for custom watch faces. Lastly, to turn on and off the wrist sensing and alarm features of the watch, you have to enter the mobile app. If there were simply buttons on the watch interface to turn on and off these features, I would prefer it since in cases where I’d rather have the wrist sensing off (such as going to sleep), that would mean every time I go to sleep I would have to open the app to flick the setting off.
Regardless, all in all, this watch has been quite amazing in the month so that I’ve had it. I could not touch upon all the fitness features in this review, but I have used them and found them quite satisfactory, including the automatic heart rate detection, sleep tracker, and walking tracker.
As a whole, the watch does have some drawbacks, but at an unbeatable price, this watch is capable of looking high-end and stylish without the $250+ price tag. I would highly recommend it to anyone looking for a budget smartwatch, even if you/they are not particularly tech-savvy.

Review: Great smartwatch! Blows the Moto 360 and Huawei Watch out of the water. It has all the features I need, and is a pretty fast, responsive device. Review:
I have been wearing this watch for about three days. I am still learning some of the features. I love being able to ask Alexa quick questions on the go, like how to say something in Spanish, or check the weather. I am really only scratching the surface with what we could do there, but we have not fully embraced the IOT quite yet. You do need to be within Bluetooth range of your phone to use Alexa. The health trackers are very handy. It does not measure blood pressure or body temp, but it covers many of the other features you would expect.

It took me a couple of days to figure out how to get to the less common apps. Activity, Heart Rate, Stress, Exercise and Alexa. It has easy access apps that you can swipe right from the initial watch face. Some of the other features, like Phone, Blood Oxygen, Breath Training, Sleep, Clock, Flashlight, etc. require you to hit an icon on the main watch face then hit the back button at the top to see the list of all the apps available. It could be there is a better way to add these for more direct access, but despite looking through the online manuals, and the many different “settings” a few times, I have not seen how to do this yet. Some watch faces do not have access to these features, so you might need to be selective in choosing the watch face that is best for your needs.

The watch battery life seems as expected. While some of the health trackers can be set to continually monitor you, that will seriously affect battery usage. I have turned all of the continual monitoring off, but I do have notifications on for email and text messages. I also have my phone integrated and have done a few short calls. (Audio is fair and would not be ideal for a long conversation.) I fully charged before wearing and after 2.5 days I have 63% battery left. Not sure it would quite make it to 7 days at my current usage but that level seems fair to me. I do believe my phone battery is draining a bit faster due to the app associated with the watch. I recommend this product! The app could be better and maybe there will be an update that will fix some of these issues.

Probably my biggest issue is that, while it seems to connect with Google Fit, neither Google Fit nor the VeryFit app that drives the watch, will hook up with my employers health rewards app. I think the watch is still useful enough that I will continue to wear it, but sadly, I will have to keep wearing my Fitbit too so I can get credit for steps and exercise.

This watch is fairly simple to use and is straightforward. I've owned a few Android Smart Watches and this, by far, is the best one. At first glance, the watch looks like an Apple iWatch, and it's safe to say that it can do almost everything its competitor can for a fraction of the price! All in all, this is a good watch with pretty solid features.

Review:
I have another watch that uses the same "Da Fit" app. They both have very similar features and user interface, but this one seems less polished.

The heart rate and blood oxygen measurements seem to be pretty accurate when compared to my pulse oximeter; however, I've found that it usually displays a random measurement when you first select the hear rate app - you need to tap on it to take a new measurement, and sometimes the random measurement is much higher or lower than the actual rate.
The blood pressure measurement isn't very accurate when compared to a dedicated device. I would not rely on that measurement at all.
It does have a skin temperature measurement, but I don't have an easy way to evaluate its accuracy, but it does seem close to my normal body temperature.

As a smart watch, I was pleased to discover that the calling features work perfectly with my Pixel 6 - I am able to start and answer calls from the watch and actually speak and listen through the watch.

There are 4 different built in watch faces available and a one slot available to select free faces from a large library. None of them are really what I'd like, but there are a few that I find acceptable.

Battery life is decent - it's still at 75% after two full days.

Unfortunately, notification support is not great. The "Da Fit" app allows enabling notifications for a limited set of apps (Phone, Messages, Facebook, Twitter, WhatsApp, Skype, Instagram, Line, KakaoTalk, WeChat) then a generic toggle for "Others". This means that if you want to get notifications for Google Maps, you'll also get notifications for every other app, which can be pretty annoying. That being said, it is still better than some other watches I've tried in the past.

At the $70 price point, this isn't a bad deal, but I would suggest spending a bit more and getting something more capable.

Review:
I put the Pebble Time Round on my Christmas wish list, and my kids bought it for me! To tell the truth, I really expected that I would return it and trade it in for the cheapest Apple watch--almost all of my other technology devices are Apple: iPhone, iMac, PowerBook, Apple TVs, Airport Extreme, Airport Expresses. But since I received it as a gift from the kids, I figured I had to give it a try.

I already own an original, plastic Pebble watch. It was just too clunky. And the band didn't fit me well. The Time Round is a different experience. It's thin--as thin as or thinner than any other watch I've ever worn. The round display really works and I think adds to the usability of the device. In this age of "material design", a watch should look like a watch. The Time Round certainly does that. The leather band is comfortable (although a bit short). If you decide you don't like it, you can always upgrade it with any standard 20mm band.

There are a multitude of watch faces available in the Pebble store, and the color screen makes a big difference from the original. Although I liked the battery life of the original Pebble, the combination of the form factor of the Time Round and the round screen makes it much more useful and more usable. There are hundreds of watch faces available for the Time Round. Many are a bit busy for me, but I had no problem finding a dozen or more that I like. That number will undoubtedly increase as this watch grows in popularity.

The app selection is a bit more limited, but I'm really not looking to use the watch as a computer. It's a watch. It does exactly what I want it to do, and I really didn't miss much from the apps that aren't there yet. Given that it's a new device, it will take some time for developers to target the Time Round. That doesn't bother me at all. It's a watch. And I'll use it mainly as a watch. That being said, I did install a fitness tracker app and a sleep tracker app. I use it primarly as a watch, but I certainly don't want to invest in other wearable devices to do the same things that can be done by the Pebble Round.

To sum it up: the Pebble Time Round gives me everything I need in a smart watch. It is primarily a watch. It doesn't try to be something it's not. Ignore the reviews that say it isn't an Apple Watch, or that its battery only lasts two and half days. Or that it its more expensive than it should be. The Pebble Time Round does what a smart watch should do: it's tells you the time, keeps you informed of your notifications, monitors your steps and sleep patterns, and does it all while not looking like you're lugging the kitchen sink around on your wrist.

Review:
This watch was received as one of the better packaged/organized products I have ever unboxed. The impression I had upon opening the package was that exceptional care was given to arranging all of the components (the watch body, 2 bands -- rubber & metallic, multiple band-mounting stems, a metal band link removal tool, a USB magnetic charging cord & a small printed user guide). The watch measures approximately 1 3/4" in diameter & weighs 2.2 ozs with the rubber wrist band attached; the additional/optional metal link band weighs 3.1 ozs -- more than the actual watch.

The 2 3/4" x 3 3/4" instruction guide provided with the watch contains minimal information for changing settings and operation of the watch, although it does refer the owner to more detailed information, which is available via the "Da Fit" app (Google Play Store). User guide info reveals either Android version ≥ 4.4 or iOS version ≥ 7.1 is required to operate the watch's application.

I was also impressed with how clean appearing the watch and packaged components were; the product appears to have been assembled in a dust-free environment. Although possibly a bit large for some, many will appreciate its size and weight. I used the included rubber wrist band with the watch and found it to be extremely comfortable & large enough to accommodate from smaller to larger wrist sizes.

Regarding battery life, some reviewers have claimed very short battery operation before a recharge was necessary; I did not find this to be true. Although I have not challenged the various operation options on a regular basis, I am now at 72% battery power left on day 6 after the initial charge of the watch. A hopefully helpful tip regarding charging -- the supplied USB magnetic charger will only "lock" into position in one direction over the two copper-colored small circular charging points on the back of the watch. Should you have difficulty getting the USB cord's charging end to grip the back of the watch's connection points, simply rotate it 180 degrees. The charging cord functions very similar to Apple laptops with this type of charging mechanism.

There are several options for setting a default for the face of the watch. I chose one that was attractive to me (see photo); colors are much more vibrant than my attached picture reveals. I have not "studied" the "Da Fit" app very extensively as yet, but additional & customized face settings are available to the user through the app.

There has been some discussion in other reviews about the accuracy of various exercise/health monitoring operations with the app. I also encountered some difficulty as well, especially with the step-counting function. It is obviously not a perfectly operating product/app in every respect, but for the price I think many will appreciate the variety of options the watch does provide, including numerous physical fitness/health-related monitoring features & linkage to one's smart phone for call/message notification, etc.

I would particularly recommend this watch to those who enjoy spending more (than less) time exploring & utilizing/manipulating new smaller data-generating devices. Thank you for the opportunity to review this product.

Special Features: Time Display, Sleep Monitor, Calendaring, Text Messaging, Alarm Clock, Notifications
Review:
I've had this watch for about 24 hours now and I absolutely love it. I've been going back and forth on getting a smartwatch. I figured it was extremely convenient especially since I'm a college student and need to stay organized. Also when I'm on the go, my phone is usually stuck in my purse or backpack (since it won't fit in my pockets) and if someone is calling me or something, I won't know. However, what held me back was both the prices of many smartwatches and their size. I have very small wrists and getting a watch to fit has been a life-long struggle. Smartwatches were even worse because most are big, bulky, and masculine. Even if it managed to fit my wrist, the device would still look humongous. The Pebble Time Round, however, does not. Not only does it fit my wrist (although granted I have the strap on the last notch) but it also doesn't look too big on my wrist. It's light and comfortable, and unlike most watches it doesn't move all over my arm. It's perfect for my skinny frame!

Onto the actual workings of the device. When I was researching smartwatches, one of the main complaints about this watch was its battery life and the fact that it's not water-proof. Both of those are true. Pebble advertises that the battery life is 2 days. I got about 1 day before having to charge it, but I also spent much of the battery through setting it up, downloading apps, etc. I wouldn't be surprised if I do manage to get 2 days out of this since I'm through with the set-up. It's also not water-proof, but it is splash-proof. Basically you can wash your hands with this on and it can withstand a few splashes of water, but you can't go swimming with it. Both of these were sacrifices for the smaller, thinner frame. For me personally, this was a sacrifice I was willing to make. If you don't have such small wrists as I do, then that sacrifice may not seem worth it.

I've also had a few issues with the bluetooth connection. It's sometimes randomly lost connection even though I'm sitting right next to it. However, this has only happened once or twice and after about 10 seconds it reconnects. There's also a limit to how far away you can get from your phone before it loses its connection. I've managed to get to the other side of the house before it tells me its lost connection, which really isn't all that bad.

The software, though, is great. It's not touchscreen which I actually prefer, and it pushes my notifications from my phone to my watch almost seamlessly. Because I'm an iPhone user, there are some limitations. For example, I can only dismiss an email notification, I can't choose to delete it or anything. However, if your carrier is Verizon or AT&T then you can send text-messages through voice-to-text. The voice dictation is also remarkably accurate (I would even suggest it's better than Siri).

Another complaint I've often heard about this watch was that the already limited app selection for Pebble was even more limited because of the round watch face. This, however, is an outdated complaint. Due to a series of updates, there are plenty of apps and watchfaces for the Pebble Round.

There are a few apps they give you from the get go. There's Pebble Health which tracks your steps and your sleep (it will even have a smart alarm where it will wake you up in your lightest sleep). I haven't tried sleeping with it yet (personally I don't like wearing a watch to bed, but perhaps one night I'll try it) but the steps are pretty accurate. There's also the Weather app sponsored by the Weather Channel. It'll basically give you today's and tomorrow's temperature (lows and highs as well) and whether it's rainy, cloudy, sunny, etc. Then there's Send Text (I'm not sure how this works with non-Verizon/AT&T iPhone users, so you might want to look that up yourself), Music where you can control the music playing on your phone (you can pause it, play it, adjust the volume, and skip or go back to certain songs - however I would set it up on the phone first if you want to go to a certain playlist, song, or album). Then there's your typical Notifications, Alarms, Watchfaces (where you can change your watchface), and after that will be the rest of the apps that you downloaded yourself. Again, there's plenty of apps to choose from, so don't worry about that.

One thing that does annoy me is how the apps are organized. They're organized in a list so you have to constantly press the down button if you want to get to the app that happens to be last on your list. This can get a bit tiresome, so I hope that Pebble will make it into a continuous list (where if you wanted to get to your last app, you could just hit the up button and get to it quicker instead of having to keep hitting the down button until you finally get to the end of the list...if that makes any sense?)

The screen is easy to read outside, inside it can be a bit difficult but all you have to do is click on the power button (or any button really) to access the backlight. Or you can flick your wrist to access the backlight, but this isn't as convenient as it should be since you have to flick your wrist a couple of times before it actually does anything. Basically if I need the backlight, I just press the power button.

If you're looking for a smartwatch that can completely replace your phone and tell you the secrets of the universe, then this isn't for you. It won't completely replace your phone, but for me I didn't want it to. I wanted it because of its convenience, especially for a college student like myself. This watch is perfect for people who have small wrists, want a smartwatch that looks more feminine, and organizes and makes their lives more convenient. While I think the $199 price is a bit high (I bought it on sale for $149 and found that more agreeable) hopefully Pebble will lower the price in time. In general, it's a great, cheaper alternative to the Apple Watch which too many iPhone users think they're stuck with.

I got about 1 day before having to charge it, but I also spent much of the battery through setting it up, downloading apps, etc. I wouldn't be surprised if I do manage to get 2 days out of this since I'm through with the set-up.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:350e3817567f87bc84ef560346de4b48]] Notice: This product was received at no charge as part of the Amazon Vine program but this is an unbiased review, and see my video review for more info.

The Smart Watch is an option for people who want a multifunction watch to help track your exercise, health, sleep and other helpful functions all on your wrist, and it’s something that fit most budgets.

The Smart Watch comes in nice looking box. You download the phone app for either Android or the iPhone to configure the watch.

The watch is solidly built with a fairly scratch resistant face. After two weeks of general use, I have a couple of small scratches but they’re not noticeable when the display is lit up. The screen is large at 1.8 inches or about 5 cm, but it’s not so big as to feel clunky. The display is bright and crisp and it’s responsive to swiping your finger between screens, but sometimes when you’re exercising and sweating it’s hard to get the screen to respond. They say there’s an AI function for handsfree use with some apps, but I didn’t activate this. You have a choice of ten preloaded watch faces. I believe there are even more for download on the phone app if you want.

The display auto locks from between 5 to 30 seconds, you decide on how long it stays awake on the phone app. For me, this is too short. To activate the display you tap the watch stem or you can flick your wrist to wake it up. I know they do this to save the battery, but I’d like the option to keep it on longer.

The apps are already installed and you swipe and tap your finger to launch the app you want. Most are fairly basic, but they say you can make a phone call or you can be alerted to an incoming call or make calls if you pair to your phone, I don’t use this phone function, but if your phone is in your bag or in a deep pocket it could be very convenient to call from your paired watch. You can read text messages but not send messages. There’s a timer, stopwatch, calculator, four games, and a music controller for your phone, they’re not fancy but they do the basic job.

Many folks will use the exercise app. There are lots different categories, such as running, and walking. The app will record your time, your heartbeat, distance, and calculate your calories. There is also one for biking, but it only does time and heartbeat and not distance. The watch is listed as water resistant for the swimmers out there. And there are dozens more activities from dancing to tennis to football.

Other handy apps are the weather app, find my watch, and there’s also find my phone. Both send an alert to locate your device if it’s close by.

To charge the phone you use the included USB charger with magnetic attachments. It looks like it’s proprietary, so keep it in a safe place since it might be hard to replace if lost. The phone kept its charge for a couple of days. I never had the battery go low during a day of moderate use.

I’m giving this four stars. It’s a decent basic smart watch. The down side is the less than accurate sensors for heartbeat and oxygen and the short display awake time. But for the price and other functionality, its definitely worth considering.

Review:
This watch is what the Bravo SHOULD have been. The bravo added steps and a couple other things that the original Tactix did not have. However, Bravo did away with the ability to upload maps :( You cannot really it call a GPS watch without maps, ya ya but it showed coordinates. Blah. Then Garmin came out with Fenix 5x and Charlie. I was super skeptical about tossing money at it after wasting money on the Bravo.

Well...I went for it and I am glad I did. The maps alone are the best selling point for a GPS watch. It has “around me”, POIs, topo and full routing maps with turn by turn instructions. The only point that I would knock off for is the Heart Rate monitor. I can be pumping iron and my HR up to 155+ but the watch will read 95 which then translates to not providing an accurate calorie burn. I have followed Garmins suggestions about placing it above the wrist bone and making it comfortable but not loose around the wrist. Still the same issue...so what did I try next? I shaved my wrist. It helped slightly but later I found the HR monitor to work best when not moving. Not too good if you like to bike, jog, swim, etc. if you like sitting on the couch you are golden.
Other than that, I think this is a solid watch that will get more use then it already has.
I ended up giving my Bravo to my wife so she has a glorified Garmin step counter.

Review:
For my original review keep going down.

I'm changing my rating to a two star, the accuracy on this thing is way off. Yesterday I proved it with my wife's Polar A300, we went for a 5 mile walk and my Garmin Vivofit 2 estimated 7,76 miles while my wife's Polar calculated a little bit over 5 miles, I got home and opened up Google Maps and my Garmin Nuvi GPS program and did the measurements of the walk we did and it was 5,26 miles, that's almost a 40% error.

I know that the accuracy on steps without a GPS is never going to be accurate, but 40%?? Come on Garmin, that's simply not acceptable. I've always used Polar products and switched to Garmin because I liked very much the Vivofit line, but with this kind of inacuracy I'm going back to Polar.

If you want your steps closer to reality avoid this product and any other Garmin product for that matter, or simply get a GPS anebled device...

[[ASIN:B00RY1Z9NQ Garmin vívofit 2 Activity Tracker, Black]]

Awesome product, I've been wearing mine since last year only not wearing it like twice a week when I'm showering.

I've used the Polar Loop and this one and by far I like this one a whole lot more.

PROS:
- One year battery life, you have no idea you are going to like this feature until you wear one that you have to charge it once every 3-4-5 days like the Polar Loop.
- Waterproof, this is one of the key features I look for in a fitness tracker, I can't understand how Fitbit still doesn't make a fitness tracker thas fully waterproof.
- Confortable, this band is really light and very confortable to wear 24/7.
- Good app, Garmin Connect can tell you a whole bunch of information in your mobile phone (iOS and Andorid) and it is very pleasent to navigate.
- Backlight.

CONS:
- Accuracy, the biggest gripe about this bnad is it's accuracy, sometimes when I'm driving in a bumpy road it counts step as well as when I'm showering (putting on shampoo or somthing like that) it also counts as steps.

That's why I'm giving it only 4 stars instead of 5, I know accuracy in any fitness trackers is not even close to 100%, but when I'm driving for about an hour it counts as many as 1k steps, in a total of 7-8k, so thats roughly 15% more steps.

Review:
After a year of use, I'm generally happy with the Vivoactive 3 as a fitness watch. I like the Garmin Pay feature quite a lot more than I expected. It's no longer necessary to carry cash as often, now that many retailers use touchless payment systems compatible with Garmin Pay.

As a runner's watch, it lacks a few features that matter if you train and race a lot. However in the year since I've owned this unit, the pandemic restrictions have made most of those issues irrelevant. But when things are normal again, things I'd want would include:

* 2 button controls - The VivoActive has only one button, unlike most Garmin watches which have two. Instead of a second button for things like lap timing, it substitutes a double-screen tap, which is wildly inefficient. You never know when your double tap will work, and it often doesn't.

*Screen tap required for activity start - On other Garmn models, if running is your main activity, clicking the start button twice selects and starts your preferred activity. With VivoActive, you have to choose the activity on the screen before starting it, which is a hassle in cold weather when you have to take off your gloves.

Nevertheless, I like the product as a smartwatch/fitness tracker, and would definitely recommend it if that's what you're after. The GarminPay feature alone is worth the while.

Review:
I have been testing this Efolen Smartwatch model I50 for a little over a week now, and I have found it to be an excellent value for the money. It was easy to pair with my Google Pixel 7 Pro, and it requires an app called FitCloudPro (which by the way, has had at least 2 updates since I installed it; this is great as it means it is actively being developed and bugs are being fixed). Setting up the app was easy as well. The watch comes with 3 different bands, and they all look nice. It also comes with a USB charging cable, which attaches to the watch on the back using magnets.

The screen is nice and bright, and pretty easy to read. The watch has 2 buttons on the side; the top one to access the menus and functions, and the bottom one to return to the previous screen, and it also has a touch screen. Notice you cannot just touch the screen to wake it up, but you can use either of the side buttons, or the raise to wake function as well. There are tons of watch faces to choose from, but you can only install one at a time, no big deal for me. I have had no problem at all getting notifications or using the included features; the watch is very consistent and reliable this way. You can set it up separately to make and receive calls directly on the watch, as long as your phone is within Bluetooth range. I have been using all the health functions, including the sleep tracker, heart rate and blood pressure monitor, and the SpO2 monitor. The steps counter seems to be counting fewer steps than my brand name smartwatch. The heart rate, blood pressure, and SpO2 monitors seem consistent, by I don't know how accurate they are. With these watches it is best to use them to track trends over time and detect sudden changes, more so than to treat them as accurate medical devices.

Battery life is very good; even with all functions on, and 24/7 health monitoring, it lasts about 4-5 days for me so far.

My watch has a build quality defect, where the whole screen is tilted to the left a little, not centered with the frame, which detracts from the overall look and experience. The other thing I don't like is that the sleep tracker only gives you metrics for deep sleep, light sleep, and awake durations, but not REM sleep, which most other fitness watches do.

All in all, I am quite impressed with the Efolen I50 smart watch. It would not be fair to compare it to a name brand super expensive smart watch, but for its cost and the functionality it offers, it is hard to beat. I do recommend it.

Review:
I bought this on Prime Day when my 19 mo old Fitbit Ionic suddenly died. The Vivoactive 3 offers very similar features with on board GPS (which you'd expect from Garmin) and out of the box the set up was a breeze compared to my prior experience with other Fitbit devices. Download the app and a few simple steps the Vivo was already receiving notifications.

After a couple of weeks of use the GPS looks to be very accurate on 2 mile runs, may have to tweak the step count/stride length but that's typical. As mentioned the notifications work very well for Gmail and Verizon text but the phone call alerts were problematic. Garmin online chat support confirmed this was a software glitch and that an update was forthcoming; indeed it did the next week but I don't get many calls and haven't had a chance to see if the remedy worked. The Garmin Connect app is intuitive to use and visually appealing in how it displays your data.
Heart rate tracking is very accurate in my estimation, not sure about things like floors climbed (it does have a built in altimeter) and like all of these watches calories burned is a WAG based on some sort of calculation. I also have not tried the sleep tracking function yet; the watch is kind of large and clunky to wear to bed comfortably in my opinion. I opted not to get the Vivoactive 3 with onboard music but the watch will easily control music playback on your phone controlling volume, pause/play, and skip ahead and back for tracks with the installed widget.

The face is large and the screen is clear and very legible even outdoor in sunny conditions. There's only one button and there is a clever sensor on the left side of the case that you can swipe up or down to scroll through the various info screens. The case is largely plastic or resin with a stainless steel bezel and case back and is very lightweight; it's holding up OK after only 2 weeks but it doesn't feel as sturdy as the Fitbit Ionic's all aluminum case. The silicone watch band provided feels flimsy and is quite long for those of us with small wrists. Like most of these devices bands can be swapped out easily and there are many aftermarket options to choose from if you want a different fit. Battery life is nothing special, after 3 days it’s below 30% with daily wear.

Overall I'd recommend the Vivoactive 3 to those who are new to the wearable device world who are looking for upper end features (like notifications and GPS) and want robust fitness tracking features. It's simplicity in set up and reliability in synching with your phone without fuss makes it an ideal choice for someone who's not super tech savvy.

Review:
Having a Fitbit Versa for 1.5 years until it started glitching out on me, I decided to try a cheaper Samsung fitness tracker. Due to Fitbit being one of the worst companies I have had the displeasure of purchasing a product from, I assumed that a seemingly quality-minded company like Samsung would have a tracker & app that would rival if not overtake that of Fitbit....nope.
My Fitbit started working again as soon as I put the Samsung on my other wrist so I was in a unique position to compare the two against one another.
The Versa probably overcounts steps by up to 10% but the Samsung misses so many steps from normal moving around, such as pivoting, stopping for only a moment...then moving again, or moving a few steps & turning around. The funny thing is that the Samsung counts more steps in my sleep than the Versa.
Sleep detection is horrific, the Samsung thinks I'm sleeping if I'm just chilling at my computer desk for an hour & the app doesn't allow sleep duration editing so I can't delete a miscounted hour if I wake up in the morning & immediately sit at my desk...a feature that Fitbit has in its app.
The battery life isn't so much of an issue as the charging...it takes up to 2 hrs to fully charge this tiny little tracker.
Atrocious calorie estimating: as a 6' 180lb man working a fairly active day outside & at 11pm the Samsung has my calories at 2282 & the Versa has me at 2920.
I would have returned this POS but I threw away the box it came in.

Review:
Smart watches, like so many other things, tend to specialize in their features. Some are oriented toward health in general, others toward kids with many games and some like this one towards sports and rugged use.

Setup was fairly easy, although it took me a while to get around the interface, in particular where to find the "unit" setting. To setup, just scan the QR code and download the app, answer some basic health and goal questions and you're set to go. Everything else seemed to connect itself.

All your basic features are here, like time, date, music control, text reading, weather, stop watch, alarms, etc. This watch however, is oriented toward the active sport/health enthusiest. It uses the GloryFit app to track various sporting activities and set goals for daily workouts. Some of the specific data that can be tracked is distance, speed, duration and heart rate for running, walking, cycling, alpinism, yoga, skipping, tennis, baseball, basketball, badmittion, football, rugby and table tennis.

The app keeps track and even includes GPS tracking to document where you were and went during the training. The watch also has features to track your sleep patterns, heart rate and blood oxygen levels.

Some of this watch's best features include it's rugged design and looks. Manufactuer states it is waterproof down to 5 atmospheres, (about 50 meters) which would make it execellent for diving and surface water sports where it could be subject to water impacts from skiing and tubing. It has a battery life of 60 days when idle and three weeks, of active daily use.

This watch is missing a lot of features found on other smart watches, but I still give it 5 stars based on its purpose-oriented design and low price. If this is what you're looking for it seems like a good deal.

Review:
Garmin 245 Music

Pros:
Great design and feel, the watch is very light weight and you often forget that it is on your wrist.
The activity tracking is extremely accurate and the device is quite tailored to runners but it provides many profiles for other training types to cater to other activities.
The display of the device has also been improved and it is very legible in the direct sunlight.
The built in Wi-Fi makes transferring your data very convenient.
Garmin's Connect app is quite robust and when paired with their devices you gain so much insight into your training and how it benefits you toward reaching your goal.

Cons:
Beside having a great light weight design the Forerunner 245 still feels quite plastic. It is by no mean's cheap plastic but it would have been better had there been a matte black stainless steel finish on the rim around the screen.
Battery life on the other hand while being decent is not remarkable - I have managed to get just over 5 days of usage while not performing too many GPS intensive activities. I have no doubt that if I was using the music feature I would have probably gotten 3-4 days at maximum.

Conclusion

The Garmin Forerunner 245 is a brilliant product aimed mainly at runners and those who seek a brilliant, robust well rounded smart watch with accurate tracking and more metrics about your well being and your training.

Review:
This is my first smart watch. The time is very accurate and I like the different watch faces available to choose from. Set up wasn't too difficult, although the text of the little booklet was tricky for my ageing eyes to read. Once I downloaded the app it wasn't too bad really. One thing I'm not sure I like is when you have Bluetooth paired with the watch from your phone, ALL sounds go to the watch. So, if you're scrolling in Facebook and watch a video, the sound will come from the watch on your wrist. That's not a huge problem, except that the sound quality of the watch isn't a good as my phone. I had no problem adding contacts to the watch and then making calls from the phone. The same with answering calls from the watch. The sound quality was very clear. I haven't tested how far I can be from my phone and still make and receive calls since I'm not sure why I wouldn't just pull my phone from my pocket. Am I too old school? I suppose if I were a woman and had my phone in my purse, the watch would be much more convenient.

So, according to the watch, my blood pressure has been super fantastic the past several days, better than when I test using my BP cuff. I'm not sure if I've just been more relaxed, or if the watch just isn't all that accurate. Can I trust it for my heart rate readings? They do seem more like what I get at the doctor's office. Is the step count accurate? Probably yes as the number is less than I'd like. When I had a step counter on my phone the count was always high. I'll leave a comparison test to someone else since it's not all that important to me, or I'm too lazy to bother.

One thing I really like is the sleep monitor. It tells me how long I've slept, and it really does break it down into sessions. So, when I wake up to pee (more times than I care to admit) it keeps track of the number of times I'm up at night and breaks my sleep into chunks, then tells me the total time. It was strange getting used to wearing a watch in bed, but it is lightweight and has a low profile.

I didn't have a place to charge the watch, so I just plug the USB-A charger into my laptop to charge it. I've ordered a charger block from Amazon so I can plug it in in the morning when I wake up. Yes, I'll lose some steps, but I'd rather keep track of my sleep then have the accurate step count...I never get to 10K anyway.

The battery seems to last a little more than 24 hours. Maybe it depends on how many times you check your BP and heart rate, or the other things it can do that I haven't bothered to figure out yet.

It was a good acquisition for me and I'm glad I have it.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:83aaf1a920f4a6c44127c622f518bc4c]] To start, let me just say, if you are expecting all the bells and whistles of one of the big name smart watches, you will be disappointed. If you are curious about smart watches and just want to explore and get a basic intro level watch, then you might consider this.

Pros
Nice looking
Not too big not too small
Easy to pair with phone
Mostly easy to use app
Exercise functions work fairly good
You can make and receive calls
Phone dial pad decent size
You can read messages
Heart Rate seems accurate
Hours slept seems accurate
Can somewhat customize a watch face
Fun reminders for moving and drinking water
A few games
A stress function for breathing exercises
Can hear music or audio from your phone and skip or go back, decent volume
Can get more detailed info and reports from health functions in app
Can customize list or grid view, degrees in F or C, and more in app
Weather forecast is accurate
Timer and stopwatch work fine
Charging cable included

Cons
Can't send messages
Instruction manual not very good
Push notifications from social media gets error message/doesn't work
Most functions have to be set in BOTH app and watch to work (watch doesn't fully have control)
Alarm function not very good - only vibrates
Step tracker doesn't seem very accurate when not using exercise function
Blood Pressure doesn't seem very accurate
Can't turn off vibrations to a select function only for everything
Have to wear watch to sleep for tracking
Music quality is marginal
Flashlight barely functional
Have to use app on phone to get more details for each function
Reset button is easy to press by accident
Some things can't be customized
Watch face only somewhat customizable
Most watch faces are cartoonish and better suited to young people

This is a good basic watch. It has some customization but I did find myself wishing I could move around or modify a few more things. The watch face only lets you choose to add 2 or three items to your picture. If I wanted the face to have HR, steps, time and date and battery level, I could not. I could only have three of those. The work around is to choose one of their pre-sets that has all of these but then you have to go with their design and not one of your own pictures as the design.

It took me a minute to find that there is a battery indicator already in the watch and I didn't necessarily need to add it to the face. From the home face, you swipe down for some settings and there is a small battery indicator there. It is just a picture of a battery with a portion of it full or not. It doesn't give an actual number but at least it gives you an idea.

I couldn't figure out the social media notifications. I kept getting error messages. It would just say "false" on the watch. I tried resetting, making sure permissions were on in the app and on my phone and watch. But I couldn't fix it. (I was able to set up another watch fine so I am not sure what I am missing on this one.)

The flashlight doesn't really work. It is very faint.

The alarm is silent. It only vibrates so I don't find it useful. It would never wake me up. The find phone bell is loud and the alarm should be like that to work better. The alarm also has to be set up in the app and then turned on in the watch. It's multiple steps.

Many of the functions have to be set in two places - app and watch. If something is not working, check both places.

The step tracker doesn't seem accurate when it tracks your daily steps. It seems better when using a specific exercise function though. This was disappointing as I mostly wanted this for the fitness and health functions.

The sleep tracker does seem to track my hours slept. I am not sure how it determines my light vs deep sleep or rapid eye movement though. I have no way of knowing if that is accurate.

The instruction manual is pretty minimal. It tells you how to pair the watch and download the app. Once you have that done, I recommend exploring the app. Tap on everything to see all of the additional settings. The "others" setting also has settings for units and time format and water and movement reminders and more. So keep exploring. Note that you may have to turn notifications on in the app and give permission on your phone. Most functions are turned on BOTH on the watch and in the app. Detailed reports on health functions can be viewed in the app. When you open it and see "Heart Rate" you need to tap on that to get to a more detailed report. Just keep exploring.

However, stay away from the RESET buttons once you get things set the way you like it! It will wipe out everything and you will have to start customizing your settings all over. I hit it by accident when exploring so just don't tap on that one. It does what it says. Duh, right?

Overall, I like this watch. It's not perfect but it has some fun and useful functions. I can answer calls and read text messages. It can track my heart rate. The step tracker works well enough in the exercise function but I wished it worked better for general step tracking throughout the day. I like that the 5 day weather forecast works too. Given the price point, I think it functions fairly well.

If you really want to be able to send messages and get social media notifications it might not be the best for you.

It works fairly well for fitness. The heart rate worked well, the exercise settings worked ok, it can remind you to drink water. The timer and stop watch work too. But again, no big bells and whistles here.

Overall it is a good intro smart watch for the price. I just wish a few of the functions worked better. Maybe a better instruction manual could help too.

Review:
Update:
-Looooong battery life!!! Lasted at least 7 days even all-day heart rate monitoring is turned on. Fantastic and I forgot apple watch.

I have an apple watch SE, but it needs to be charged every day. Well, I know I can't charge it every day, which prevents it form being used frequently, so I have to for alternatives.
In comparison:

-Shape: Compared with the apple watch, this smart watch has a larger 1.52" screen, a clearer HD retina display and a thinner metal frame. Stylish design and look slim.

-Customizable dial: Like the apple watch, this watch has multiple built-in dials to choose from, and it can choose your own photo as the blackground. The dial can display time/date/steps/calories/heart rate and other data in real time which is very convenient.

-Sleep Tracker: Both watches have a sleep tracking function, but I prefer this. I can see my sleep analysis (Deep sleep, Light sleep or Wide sleep) on the smartphone or watch. The Apple Watch can only check the sleep time, there is no actual sleep analysis.

-Heart Rate and Bloody Oxygen Monitering: I don't know how accurate the heart rate or blood oxygen is. But as far as I know, it gives believable results (the heart rate I measured is very close to the Apple watch). It is worth mentioning that apple watch does not have the function of blood oxygen monitering, so it is impossible to compare the data.

-Battery Life: Stiive watch charges really quickly and only used half of its battery in four day! I will update this review once I have a chance to use the set for a few weeks and see how the battery fares.

Review:
I been looking for a reasonably priced smart watch to work as seamlessly as possible with my phone(s). One that I don't lose features if I switch phones. Samsung, Pixel, Apple, Fossil, etc way, way out of my budget, plus no BP monitoring.
So a friend recommended the LIGE, going to be honest, was worried over quality, features actually working.
Color me impressed, paired no problem to my Android phone (and for giggles, paired with my wife's iPhone (not at the same time), works great with both.
Was a little weird setting up because you actually have to pair/connect watch AND software. But once done, works really great, O2,heart rate, blood pressure (yup BP that the more expensive watches don't have available in US yet), sleep and step tracking and more health related functions. And easy enough to access.
Also an option for woman to track monthly cycle.
Plus, yeah here is the crazy part, neat watch, many ( nit as many as with the bigger (= $$$$) brands, but enough of a varied selection to make most folks happy. There may be a way to get third party ones as well, but has not been an issue for me).
As for phone functions, they are basic, but what most, but hard core users need. Notifications for calls, messages, calendar, as well as basic controls for there use.
Navigation is a little weird, but fast, and easy to get the function you want to access.
Water resistant/proof, so far. Gotten it wet and submerged, still chugging along.
Adjustments via knobs and touch screen. Touch screen is bright, can be seen in daylight. Easy enough to read for the watch size.
I have big wrists, so the two (nice darkish silvergrey metal band, and a black silicone sport type band. Both have quick release pins, to make switching them out relatively easy-ish.) included watch bands don't quite fit. Good for small to medium wrists.
So I ordered a XXL band for a more comfortable fit.
Battery life. I have had great battery life with newbie, geek new toy playing around with as many functions as I can. Ten days after full charge so far, and sitting in standby for another three weeks, still fires up.
Now, this is NOT a three to six hundred dollar smart watch. Not the quality (but close enough. So far.) Nor the same features. BUT, the flip side is it has a couple the others don't, and as for me, all that I really need without overloading it with stuff I don't want.
And at a insanely economical price. This DEFINITELY a fantastic value for price item.
As I use it I will update the review.

Review:
When the Toq was first released, I gave it 3.5 stars for an okay smartwatch with potential. As time has gone by, additional software updates have improved the usefulness of the watch and the price has been cut. Because of these, I would currently rate up the Toq a 4 star product. There's still a lot of things that should be done software-wise that could elevate the Toq considerably. I actually own a Pebble watch and a Qualcomm Toq and would like to do a comparison. In summary, I would say Pebble wins in overall function but the Toq carries it's own sense of elegance. I would make the Pebble my primary everyday watch because it's cheaper, I can put all these protective skins on it with less fear of banging it up, and it tells me what I want to know, thanks to a plethora of 3rd party apps such as Pebble Glance. At the moment, it's more practical, functional, and customizable than the Toq. But if I want something a little more interesting, showy, and elegant, with the basic elements for a smartwatch, I'd wear the Toq. Smartwatches to me, are an accessory, a luxury to your smartphone. This is my answer to the question of why anyone would even want a smartwatch. My primary use for this is to tell the time, date, notifications. Why not just go with a regular watch or pull my phone out of my pocket? Because of all the additional benefits of immediately knowing the weather, calls, texts, emails, battery status either at a glance or a vibration. I don't know if I'd want extra things like a camera or a speaker because I haven't experienced it yet.
----------------------------------------------------------------
Qualcomm Toq
PROS
+Vibrates on Calls, Texts, notifications
+Mirasol Color Display (Always on) "3-4 day" battery life
+Touchscreen
+Easy Watch Interface
+Easy Phone App Interface
+SMS Voice-to-text response
+Responsive Customer Service
+Open SDK
+Wireless Charging
+Subjectively Elegant Appearance
+Splashproof

CONS
-More Expensive
-Less popular
-Barely any 3rd party applets despite open SDK
-Limited to 8 Watchfaces
-Unchangeable Watchband (Cut to fit)
-Bigger, Heavier
-Mediocre Viewing Angles
-Questionable Future Support
-Can't read full email
----------------------------------------------------------------
Pebble Smartwatch
PROS
+Vibrates on Calls, Texts, Gmail notifications
+Less Expensive
+E-Paper (Always on) "5-7 day" battery life
+Buttons
+Cult user support
+Open SDK (TONS of 3rd Party Watchfaces and Apps)
+More Accessories (Skins, Protectors, Watchbands)
+Shake to Light
+Lighter Weight
+Easy Magnetic Charge Cable
+Waterproof ATM

CONS
-Limited to only 8 installed watch apps/watch faces
-Colorless, Low Res Display
-Mediocre Viewing Angles
-Subjectively Toyish Appearance
-Can't read full email
-Only supports Gmail out of the box
----------------------------------------------------------------
DISPLAY
Mirasol vs. Epaper. Mirasol easily wins, but the perfect smartwatch display still remains elusive. Mirasol, a transflective display has color, which can look great... when brightly lit... at a 90 degree angle. To me, this was to be the Toq's best selling point. Unfortunately, as utilized and presented, it is nice but not amazing. The viewing angles aren't perfect. This is the same with Pebble's low power LCD. If I just shift the display by 20 or so degrees, it's almost like a shadow gets cast over the whole screen. It's still visible but not something I'm 100% satisfied with. On Mirasol, the color also tints differently at different viewing angles. It's not like e-ink, or ips monitors that you won't notice a change in viewing angles. The color on Mirasol looks nice but isn't as vibrant or saturated as LEDs. The resolution on Mirasol sometimes look crisp and other times the gradients sometimes look rather grainy. Qualcomm has definitely been pumping up (doctoring) the color saturation in their product images. Actually, the most visible watchfaces have to do with contrasts in light and dark. Unfortunately, the darker you get, the less color you see, and vice versa.

Here's the conundrum on reflective vs. LED displays. Reflective displays sip power, look great in sunlight, but crap in darkness. LED displays suck battery life, look great in darkness but crap in sunlight. Between these two, I'd rather have reflective with an activated backlight. On the light function, Mirasol's back-light, which is activated by double tapping the top watchband is quite bright. However, it actually improves the viewing angle of the display. Unfortunately, the color tint shift still occurs. The same viewing angle improvement occurs in the Pebble watch too. The Pebble watch's light can be activated by flicking one's wrist or pushing a button. A method I by far prefer over the Toq's. A flick of the wrist is a no hands affair. The Pebble's light is also a softer back-light, which I actually prefer over the Toq's stronger light. I would even joke that you can use the Toq's light as a flashlight! Pebble definitely has a more discrete light. Both lights time out after a few seconds. I wish there were a way to adjust the light like in Kindle's Paperwhite. If future smartwatches had any smarts, they'd automatically adjust for brightness with variable yet subtle light strengths.

There's always pros and cons to all these displays. LEDS look great but use too much power. Epaper uses low power but is colorless, mediocre viewing angles and low res. Mirasol uses low power, has color, mediocre viewing angles, and mediocre res. E-ink, from what I've seen on paperwhite, uses low power, has good viewing angles, and crisper resolution, but colorless, and blinks to refresh. I think I'd want to see in the future a smartwatch with e-ink color with adjustable led back lit brightness. Did I forget to mention that LEDS, low power lcd, and Mirasol's screens can play animation? Pebble has animated watchfaces that, from time to time, animate like animated gifs. Woefully underutilized, Mirasol seems to have a decent refresh rate as well. I have yet to see e-ink do animations smoothly. From what I've seen (not in person) in comparisons between Mirasol and e-ink Triton 2, is that the Triton 2's colors look more dull but I'd want that over colorless e-ink or mediocre viewing angles. Currently however, Mirasol remains one of if not THE most attractive and sensible smartwatch displays.
DISPLAY EDGE: TOQ
----------------------------------------------------------------
OS
The Toq's watch operating system is run on touch alone. Touch the bottom part of the band for a home page. Swipe to get around. For the watchface mode, you swipe the bottom bar to change watchfaces. The Toq phone app is very easy, simple, and straightforward. The home page has a comm hub, calendar, music, weather, stocks, status, settings, notifications. You can see call history, sms history. You can adjust the appearance of the watch's home screen too. Hopefully in the future, more developers will make more functional things for the Toq when the SDK gets opened up. I use Yahoo Mail and get that pushed through notifications but it doesn't show much email history and not even the whole email. It's weird, I tap on notifications, see the new email, but little preview. It tap on it again and it opens up a new card that shows the same thing. But overall, a smooth and easy experience to setup. One design note, when I'm browsing through applets, at the bottom a bar with dots appears to show you where you are, but most times, when I'm swiping through things, my finger covers that up. They should've put that nav bar at the top where my finger isn't blocking it. One awesome update the Toq received opened up the microphone's ability to respond to an SMS via "voice-to-text". This function gives you 9 seconds to turn whatever message you speak, into text, for an SMS response. Its accuracy is hit and miss, but when it hits, it's quite impressive.

Out of the box, the Pebble is a sucky watch. Gmail support only, crappy watchfaces. All they have is music, alarm, watchfaces, settings. You can't even see call, sms, or email history. Thankfully 3rd party developers have lots of cool and useful apps and watchfaces to download. Unfortunately, you're only limited to installing 8 additional watchfaces/apps. They've also since updated their app that makes for an easier experience searching and uploading watchfaces and watch apps on the Pebble. Sometimes the app would have loading problems since it relies on an internet connection though.

In terms of notifications, both watches vibrate on your wrist whenever a new sms, call, designated notification comes in. The Pebble has a stronger and louder vibration.
OS EDGE: PEBBLE
----------------------------------------------------------------
WATCHFACES
Okay, here's what I want with watchfaces. I usually want two desired faces. One to show off, and one to use practically. The practical watchface that I want has the time, date, weather, weather conditions, notifications on new/missed email, sms, calls, phone battery life, and watch battery life. The greatest would be to customize what information you want but neither watch offers that ability.

At the moment, the Toq's practical watchfaces include faces with time and date. It also has a watchface for calender events. Another for Stocks. Another for time and weather. That's it. It's showier watchfaces are the colorful ones, and they also have some interesting designs. My favorite is the blue "blossom" watchface style. Second favorite the time and weather face. It'd be awesome to customize and change the colors, but you can't really. One does allow you to switch between warm and cool colors and other faces change colors automatically, but I want more control over this. One update added an activity tracker. Not lacking in watchfaces to show off, and they have some cool designs, but none of these watchfaces come close to my desired functions of a smartwatch face. More animated watchfaces are a possibility with the Toq, but sadly, none of the faces take advantage of that.

For the Pebble, I usually use the 3rd party watchface app Pebble Glance. This tells me the time, date, weather, weather conditions, sms, calls, and phone battery. I look at time and phone battery status the most. Other watchfaces can show you calendars, activity, The watchface I use to show off is usually a steamboat willie watchface that animates every 15 minutes. Pebble has a plethora of fun and useful watchfaces even though the black and white low res e-paper leaves something to be desired. Pebble has been updated to show you things like bluetooth connection and Pebble battery too.

With Toq's mirasol display, it definitely has some snazzy color watchfaces. However, when compared with the Pebble, he Toq deeply lacks in function and information. Which is better depends if you value better form (Toq) or better function (Pebble). Actually, Pebble has a lot of fun and great looking watchfaces but they'll never beat a color display.
WATCHFACE EDGE: TRADEOFF TIE (Slight edge to Pebble)
----------------------------------------------------------------
TOUCH vs. BUTTONS
Touchscreen interaction is cool but I also still like having buttons to press. Unfortunately, both watches have one without the other. Toq is a pure touch interface. Pebble is a pure button interface: up, down, back, select. Here, between touch vs buttons, I would actually choose buttons. Here's why: With touchscreen, it's not always precise and responsive. Buttons, you instantly know you're at the right spot and actually activating by feel. I could push buttons blindfolded and know exactly what I'm pressing by memory. I can't do that accurately on touchscreen alone. However, touchscreens can be a more intuitive interface. And touchscreens are better on bigger watchfaces. It is a bit of a draw here, but I personally love pressing a button on the Pebble just to turn on a brief light vs double tapping the top of a tactile-less watchband. The press of a button is still satisfying. Another quirk to touchscreens, greasy display smears. Not so with buttons.
INTERFACE EDGE: TRADEOFF TIE (Slight edge to Toq)
----------------------------------------------------------------
APPEARANCE
The Toq is bigger and heavier than the Pebble. It's subjective, but the Toq looks very techy because the watchface looks like a smartphone/tablet screen, complete with bezels. The Pebble looks like a toy BUT, thanks to a changeable watchband and skins you can make it look LESS like a toy. It comes with rubber watchbands. The watch itself can be bought in several different colors. I bought a wood watchband and wood skin for my pebble. And it's less conspicuous. The Toq's rubber watch bands don't cry "premium", but they're okay. Unfortunately, the watch's band is unreplacable and you have to physically cut the rubber watchband with scissors to fit it in it's clasp. Oh yeah, did I mention the big battery integrated on the bottom clasp? It's actually not an eye sore but if you're typing on a laptop, and rest your wrists on the edge, the bulk of the clasp will annoy you. That annoyance doesn't happen with a Pebble watch. I would describe the Toq as a more elegant looking albeit bulkier watch than the original Pebble. However, I wouldn't say it's luxurious, just more refined. Fortunately, the Pebble is very customizable in appearance, with lots of skins and watchbands to alter. Pebble also later came out with a more expensive, albeit better looking Pebble Steel.
APPEARANCE EDGE: SUBJECTIVE TIE
----------------------------------------------------------------
CHARGING
Between wireless charging and a magnetic stick-on cable, wireless wins. You pretty much never have to think about battery life with wireless, unless you never take off your watch for a couple days. I take my watch off at the end of the day, so I just put the Toq down on the charge case and it charges, and I never have to even think about battery life. Pebbles' magnetic charging was also rather hassle free but when the battery indicator shows low, it does become one more thing you have to think about. I actually turn my Pebble watch off when I'm not using it to save battery. (the buttons can be used to turn the Pebble on again) The Toq can also be turned off but with no buttons, the only way to turn it on again is placing it on the charger. The Toq's charge case is kind of bulky and has this other bulky half that is meant for ear pieces that's sold separately. Since I don't plan to get those, it's kind of a waste of space. Seeing as how both watches hold charge for several days, I hardly notice the charging. On the Toq, you can check the battery status of the watch.
CHARGING EDGE: TOQ
----------------------------------------------------------------
There's probably more things I could write about this but I think that's enough for now.

Review:
I've bee wearing a watch for years. I prefer digital because I like being able to check very quickly and get a precise time. My previous watch was a $60 thing that didn't keep great time, but looked fairly good and got me a lot of complements.

I pre-ordered the watch and it never shipped to me even though I know shipments were going out. I saw it was up on Amazon and that I'd be able to get it the next day. Usually this would be a complaint, but it turned out to be one of the best customer service experiences I've ever had. Called customer service, told them I wanted to cancel my pre-order. The guy I was speaking with asked why and I told him. He was really nice and reasonable about it and told me he'd gotten calls from a few people doing the same thing. He didn't try to push anything onto me, just thanked me and left it at that.

The watch itself is pretty nice. in terms of a watch it's a massive upgrade to what I had before. The screen is clear and easy to see. Even when I'm driving at night the light from a street light makes the watch easy to read. It also has a light which is a great feature I didn't have before. I like having weather right there and being able to send a quick text or answer a phone call is nice. The bluetooth range is good. I lose it on different levels of my house, but it works fine. The battery also lasts a pretty long time. I charge it every 4-6 days.

I have small writs so I was worried it would look too big. Qualcomm did a good job of making it a reasonable size. Cutting it was a bit nerve wracking. I suggest going a link too large just in case. I did, wore it for a bit and cut it down and it's very comfortable now. It looks good that it draws compliments too.

All in all I'm quite happy with the watch. Native speech to text would be nice, but that is something they want people to use their headset for. It hasn't come out yet. The one thing I wish I could do is manually update the weather from the watch. It is currently 5:30pm and the weather updated at 3:00pm last time.

Honestly, I'm a bit of a nerd and I like this kind of stuff. A watch is also something I use everday. For regular people like pretty much everyone else I know, a smartwatch is not a product I think they need or would even be too thrilled with. However, if someone was in the market this would be my top suggestion.

Review:
I have used this product for over six months before writing a review, i wanted to make sure that i gave the readers great reviews.

Pros:
The Garmin Vivo Fit II fits well and also feels great! This activity tracker is also pretty accurate. The battery last for about a year and you do not have to take it off for showering/bating and you can keep this on all the time which is great for tracking your every move.

Step counting: i have figured out that this device is sometimes of by 6 or 7 steps.

Sleep tracking: this function i used a lot! I wanted to see how i slept through the night and check for any disturbances, because i have severe insomnia. This function seems accurate.

iPhone application:
The application looks great however, the device has a hard time syncing to the application to transfer fitness activity and it takes a while to-do so. It will transfer but takes a few try's which is a software issue not an hardware.

This is a great product that is very customizable with different color bands, for the most part accurate, stylish and its multi-function capabilities and price point makes this device a great buy! I have recommended this to many of my friends.

Review:
I wanted to wait a few months before writing this review. I purchased this 5 months ago after careful consideration. I do like Samsung products. Originally I only got 3½ days of battery life which was extremely annoying. I did overlook that with the hope that a software upgrade would arrive some day to fix that and other blatant issues and they certainly did!

I now get 8 days of battery life which is more than double the original. It now has a battery meter on the display (if you choose do display one). Prior you had to go into the app on your phone to see battery life, which was stupid at best. Now it's on the fit. Now there's many more features and faces you can tailor the display for. Originally I pared my display items to heart rate, steps and sleep to get my 3½ days of battery life. Now I have that and more and the battery last SO much longer.

I got mine in summer and swim, kayaked and showered with it. It still works great. I take it off only to charge it. It does have a swim mode, which I think turns off the display so it does not come on with every swim stroke. But it is waterproof as I shower daily with it.

You can now display your calendar agenda, control music, display weather plus all the usual and more. I would guess more will be coming with future software upgrades to the Fit.

I used to have a Garmin Vivosmart HR+ and this now is a huge improvement. The display is beautiful and crystal clear. Performance wise with steps I think it is very generous. Sleep tracking it seems dead on. Heart rate for me is important to watch and it's close enough for what I want/need.

It still can be improved in a few main categories: one is screen brightness. It needs to adapt to brighter in the sun and much dimmer at night and even dimmer while in the total dark like looking at it in the middle of the night in bed. But I'm patient as the software upgrades are huge improvements every time.

It also needs a slightly better band. The band is pretty good. But everyone one in a while I find it loose or very loose. It's an unusual design that does work. But I would prefer to see the usual adjustable watch clasp or even a material band with Velcro like the kind that some Luminox watches have.

Charging time is now around 2 hours to charge to full. That's MUCH better than originally as it needed 4+ hours. Half that is much better.

All in all it now deserves it's 5 stars in all categories. And I'm sure even more improvements and enhancements thru software upgrades for the band will come out in the future. Just make sure to check for software updates every few months. A new one has been coming out once a month.

I highly recommend the Galaxy Fit. It's an excellent activity tracker with excellent battery life and customization.
