Review:
I'm been wearing an Apple Watch since they first launched in the summer of 2015, and have been a big fan. Since then, I've seen the evolution of smart watches from Motorola, Samsung, and other companies evolve into nicer and better products and this Samsung Gear really caught my attention when I saw it. The best part for me is it works with iOS, so I could connect it to my iPhone and compare it against my Apple watch, which is the new Series 3 Ceramic with LTE. I was shocked to discover both a lot of similarities as well as a lot of differences between the 2 watches. While the Samsung took a little longer to connect to my iPhone than the Apple watch, it wasn't difficult and the watch helps guide you in the process. I am going to try not to make this an Apple watch vs Samsung Gear Sport review, because that isn't the point of this review. I just wanted to point out that I have experience with both watches so I kind of knew what features to look for that are things I actually use. For example, the barometer app is a fun novelty, but it's not something I will be using daily like the calorie counter feature.

First, build quality. It is excellent on the Gear Sport. The metal casing is a shiny gunmetal color and feels very solid. The outer ring feels satisfying to turn, with small clicks as you rotate it. This is one of my favorite features of this watch. The overall look of this watch is very nice and classy, and aside from the relatively cheap looking rubber strap, it looks like a nice analog watch, and not this weird rectangle thing. The side buttons have rubber on them and are easy to press. I give the build quality of this watch 5 stars. It feels good to wear and doesn't seem big or heavy at all. I wore 2 watches for almost a week to test this watch and hardly noticed it unless I got a vibrating notification.

The screen on this watch is also beautiful and has vibrant colors. I was immediately captivated by the round screen and how nice all the watch faces look. Of course, if you look very closely you can see pixels, but when you look down at your watch, it looks crisp and bright and colorful. I also give the screen 5 stars.

So far, this watch has really impressed me. It comes with a cool plastic stand that charges it sideways and is similar to Apple's "nightstand" mode except it retains your same watch face instead of the plain green look. The only gripe I have with the charging stand is I keep it by my bed and the red or green charging light is always on and was distracting to me when I was sleeping. A piece of tape solves that problem if it bothers you, but I did think that was an interesting design decision since it's so easy to see that the watch is charged by looking at the screen.

The touchscreen itself works fine and the watch uses haptic feedback well to let you know that a tap of the screen was successful. I found myself swiping around a lot until I realized the outer ring could cycle through various apps quickly and easily. One issue I had with the touchscreen is that I wasn't able to wake it up just by tapping it. So I tap a side button or turn the ring instead. This is annoying when you raise your wrist and the sensor doesn't turn the screen on, which did happen to me quite a lot. I think I'm just used to the way my other watch works, and this one requires just a little more wrist movement to trigger the screen. In Samsung's defense, you can set the screen to always be on, but that does wear the battery down considerably faster. Overall, it wasn't a big issue for me, just something I noticed.

The included software is pretty good. The step tracking, activity tracker, and heart rate monitor all function similar to the Apple Watch and work pretty well once you enter your weight, height and age. If you don't do that, the numbers will be way wrong. The heartrate monitor was consistently within 1-2 BPM of my other watch, but I did notice the sensor is more sensitive and I had to have it positioned just right or it wasn't able to take a measurement. Again, the Apple watch is not that picky (with Series 3) and seems to work if the watch is tight or loose and in different positions on the wrist. I did have more issues with my Series 1 watch, so I'm sure this is something Samsung can improve on with future iterations.

One thing I didn't love but is a great feature idea was the automatic activity tracking. Maybe this is an edge case, but I found it would automatically start a workout whenever I would be in a hurry to do something. It sees my heart rate is up and detects movement and starts an auto workout. It was kind of funny and it sort of served as a reminder to chill out and not be in such a rush to do stuff. But I did end up turning that off. It's so easy to start a workout manually. Just twist the outer ring and choose your activity and start it. Very simple and it works great.

Other features I use often are alerts for email and texts. Those worked great, although there is no easy way to reply on the Gear Sport. I'm sure there is a way but it wasn't obvious to me. On my other watch, I can reply with voice to text or a list of pre written responses, as well as a scribble mode to type short messages. This is a minor thing since I usually pull out my phone when I get a text, and I never reply to email via my watch but I thought it was worth mentioning.

A thing I love about this watch was the option to download new watch faces from the Samsung App or directly on the phone. You can also download games and utility apps. There is even an app to watch YouTube videos, which seems really cool but I couldn't get it to work, even after trying several times. The videos just wouldn't load for me. But that's fine. Novelty apps are fun and it's cool to play a game of Snake or Tetris on your watch, but after you've had it for a while, it's nice to just have a nice looking watch that works well, has a nice looking screen, records your workout activity and gives you alerts. This watch is water resistant and I had no trouble with it being in a few feet of water for a while.

The Samsung Gear app was easy to install and connect to my iPhone. It's a bit weird going from the way Apple does things to the Samsung way, so Android users will no doubt have an easier and better experience than I did, but I still had a very nice experience overall with this watch. I find myself wearing it more than I thought I would because the screen is so nice to look at, but at the end of the day, it's a very tough decision if I had to pick one watch. For Android users, it's a no brainer. Get this watch if you want a sporty but nice looking smart watch with a lot of features. I like that this watch doesn't go nuts with the features you don't need, like a camera.

The Samsung Gear Sport is just a clean looking watch that works great with Android OS or iOS, and with so many choices of watch face and optional watch bands, you can give it a personality to match whatever mood you are in. Overall, I give this watch 4 stars. Maybe 4.5. It's not perfect, but it's much, much nicer than I expected and has given me a lot of respect for Samsung's design choices and manufacturing ability. The fit and finish of this watch is excellent, and very comparable to Apple. I forgot to mention that battery life is pretty good, and with the settings I had, the watch could last just about 3 days with normal use, although I prefer to charge it nightly out of habit since it's so easy with the inductive charger they include. Battery life was very similar to the Series 3 42mm Apple watch, maybe a touch more. In either case, both watches were around 65-70% at the end of the day with my use. I want to note that the Gear Sport gives a lot of options for how long the screen stays on, vibration intensity, wifi or bluetooth mode, etc that can really affect battery life.

I've included some photos of the 2 watches next to each other for comparison. These were just shot with my phone and I'm not a professional photographer so I apologize for the quality but it might be useful to someone to compare them side by side. I'm sure there are tech websites that do a better job at this, but I wanted to at least post some photos. (I'm sorry if some are sideways, it has to do with the way Amazon formats the photos)

I am a huge fan of smart watches in general and just like some people own several different analog watches, I don't think there is anything wrong with owning different smart watches. If you are really concerned with fitness tracking over time, you may want to stick with one smart watch, but for casual working out, both watches do a good job of counting calories and keeping track of your workout activity, if multiple watches is something you are considering. I know most people are going to get one or the other, and both watches are a great choice in my opinion.

Review:
I am a fan of SoundPEATS so when I saw they make a smart watch I was more than willing to give it a try, and I am very impressed by the features of this watch at this price point. The overall setup was super easy, the app is simple to use and the quality of watch itself seems solid. I'm especially impressed by the battery life; I wore it for 3 days straight and the battery level was still above 50%.
The main features of the watch that I like is the sleep tracker (one of the main reasons I got it) and the step/heartrate tracker. This is my first smartwatch and, based on the number of times I glance at it daily, I think it's becoming harder to live without. Although the external app features are basic, it still saves me a lot of time as I'm now able to see e-mails and texts at a glance to determine if I need to immediately pick up my phone for follow-up.
The only improvements I would like to see would be to add more functionality to the countdown timer (which I use a lot for working out) to enable changes to the shortcut timer buttons. Right now there are 1, 5 and 10 min shortcut buttons but I'd like to be able to change these to better suit my needs without going into the submenu each time. The other improvement I'd like is to be able to set the watch to remain on a certain screen (such as countdown timer) if you're using it; the way it is now the watch reverts back to the home screen each time the display shuts off. Another option would be to be able to customize the home screen view to only look at things most important to you; the static options available are nice but customization would be great.
Overall I'm very happy with this watch and definitely would recommend it as a starter smartwatch for anyone.

Special Features: Health Monitor, GPS
Review:
I am upgrading to the E3 from my previous Ticwatch S2, which I already thought was an incredible watch.
For the record, my S2 is still working well and in wonderful shape, but I just felt after 2 years with that watch, I wanted to try out a new one. And boy am I glad that I did, this thing rocks!
I am loving how fast and smooth the operating system works, the battery life is excellent (went from 100% to 74% throughout an entire day), and the look is minimalistic and stylish.
Some new things that my S2 did not have that I am really happy with:
-The automatic workout detection. My wife and I go on a lot of walks together and it's nice that this watch will automatically start timing them and tracking the distance/laps without me having to interact with it.
-The NFC payments. I have been using NFC on my phone for a couple of years now, and it's really great to now be able to do it without taking my phone out of my pocket.
-The 24 hour heart rate monitor is essential for me, as I do have heart problems. And even though it is not technically a "new" feature, I would say it is way more intuitive on this device than it was on my S2.
-Phone Calls! The quality of the phone calls through the watch is incredible. I've always been one to make fun of people who made phone calls through their watch, because of how silly they look. But with this watch, I can actually just walk around my house with my hands by my sides and make phone calls through the watch. I can hear everything the person on the other line is saying, and based on feedback from those I'm talking to, they can also hear me clearly without me having to have my wrist close to my face.
Overall, I would highly recommend this watch to anybody looking for an affordable Android compatible watch that does it all.
It runs so smoothly and has all the features you could ask for with a pricetag lower than the watched developed by the flagship phone brands.

Review:
There are many affordable smartwatches, but this one ensures you’ll not shell out more $$ for additional straps. The leather, silicone, and black steel mesh easy-to-replace (tool-free) straps are enough to cover most occasions.

The FitCloudPro app required for this smartwatch doesn’t need you to create an account to use the essential features of the watch.

These affordable smartwatches rely primarily on BT to pair with your phone & use its (phone’s) GPS for most of its features, including activity-tracking. Their batteries last long because they have less built-in hardware, including GPS. But the watch’s BT capability is robust enough to operate simultaneously with other BT-enabled accessories like earbuds or headphones.

I’ll credit this smartwatch’s good looks, clear & colorful screen, and spare straps. The app is typical of the multitude of others in its price range. I don’t expect much from an unknown company and compare it with others with multi-billion R&D budgets for software development alone.

It’s no secret that most companies (including the big boys) churning out these gadgets design their products for planned obsolescence – more so with ones with a limited-lifespan battery inside. So, if this smartwatch lasts me only a couple of years (or less), it fails or no longer functions as intended – I won’t despair a lot as it’s genuinely affordable.

Review:
I got the Garmin Vívoactive 3 (I'll call it the VA3) back in July 2018 so this is a great long-term review. I love it as it is not the typical square faced smart watch and the face is customizable to hundreds of different faces. Super lightweight too = Nice. The silicone band is nice, has a pleasant texture, and not irritating if you keep it clean. Your sweat can leave behind salt/mineral crystals that can irritate you if you don't wash the inside of the band occasionally...yep, I let it get dirty and my skin didn't like it, so now I wash the band when it gets gross.

I started using this with an ancient relic called the Galaxy S3 and had issues with notifications not showing up on the watch. When I finally got a phone that was current (Galaxy S9) - ALL my problems went away. Bottom line - stay current with technology and you'll avoid most problems. I absolutely love the vibration feature as I use it to wake up in the morning and not disturb anyone else. I get texts and emails in meetings and no one knows. Heck, I can eve respond with custom responses too like "I'm in a meeting" or "Call you later" or "I'm driving" - all with a simple touch to the watch face. I can't recommend you do that while driving, but if you learn the moves, you can do it without looking at the watch. Ninja skill enabled.

It keeps my steps, GPS route, heart rates, and all the other cool health data so I can see if I am being a lazy slug or if I was really active. There are all kinds of add-ons you can download, I just haven't tried them out.

Of course, there was one problem. Along the way, my vibration stopped. After a quick chat with customer service, I was able to send it in under warranty and they gave me a replacement - fast, and back in business. I really recommend Garmin customer service, they were really helpful! It had survived bumps, bangs, and other abuse without any damage to the face. Even the stainless steel ring looks great. Overall, great watch for me, just sad that I don't wear my old metal dumb watches anymore - ha!

Overall, if you are looking for a decent smart watch but don't want to spend the ridiculous amounts that some of the higher end watches command then definitely give this one a try. For the price you pay can you really go wrong?

Review:
I'm a first time smartwatch user. I got this one because I mainly wanted a fitness tracker but was a better smartwatch than fitbit. Let's start working outside in:
Looks:
I think its a great looking watch. I was a bit worried about getting a fitness focused watch that was stainless steel versus the s2 design that was more rubber like. With a different watch band you could use this in just about any situation as you can turn the display off or put a less athletic watch face on to fit the scenario. I don't have big wrists for a guy, about 7 inches around at the thinnest part and it fits my wrist well and I don't notice the weight. Mostly just focus on not bumping it on things. I don't believe you can get much thinner than this for a sport smartwatch which is a big reason i went with this over a LG watch sport. I never got to see the blue version in person but heard it was an understated blue which could've been nice, but I went with black just so it could be more versatile with clothing. The finish is great, more of a chrome smoke than dark black.
Fitness Tracking:
The fitness tracking is pretty good with the S health app. I live far north so right now most of my cardio work is indoors so the GPS functionality hasn't come into play yet. One of the only if not the only where you can add meal calories from the watch into your tracker. Being as it has all the hardware a health tracker needs(unless you're hardcore cardio and want third party stuff like chest HR sensors which the watch doesn't support) and right now the only thing limiting it is software, it can only improve with time.
Functionality:
The bezel is amazing and makes navigation easy. The software is pretty intuitive so customizing the widgets and apps is easy. Notifications are OK. It can handle multiple text messages from different people well, but multiple from one person only get displayed as the most recent which is annoying. Single ones from multiple people get separated nicely and are easy to navigate.
responding to texts can be a pain. If you need a short response then drawing on the screen can get the job done pretty well with occasional weird capitalizations and things. The S voice on the phone is pretty craptastic. On my pixel if I talk casually it'll sometimes make mistakes but if I enunciate and focus it'll be perfect just about every time. With the watch, if I put in as much effort as I can to talk clearly it'll still be a struggle to get a text right.

I give it 4/5 for now because it is built with quality, display is beautiful, easy to navigate, software is pretty intuitive, standard 20mm bands make it easy to change the look and decent functionality
downsides are the notification management needs improvement and responding can be a pain because of poor voice recognition, which should be the bread and butter of a smart watch and done with ease.

Review:
If you're thinking about the Sony SmartWatch 2 then you're probably also thinking about the alternatives such as the Pebble or the Samsung Galaxy Gear. Having taken them all for a spin, here is what I have found...

The SONY SmartWatch 2 (see [[ASIN:B00G13B69E The (Unofficial) Sony SmartWatch 2 Book]]) does everything the Pebble does (see [[ASIN:B00E0TDRSE The (Unofficial) Pebble SmartWatch Book]])... but in color, and with an intuitive touch-screen interface. This matters if you're using something like the third-party GPS Maps app for the Sony that shows your current location on the smartwatch screen. But if you want to receive message notifications from your iPhone, you will still need the Pebble rather than the Sony because the Sony only pairs with Android 4.0+ smartphones.

The Samsung Galaxy Gear (see [[ASIN:1440491801 The (Unofficial) Samsung Galaxy Gear Smartwatch Book]]) does a lot more than the Sony because it has a camera, a speech interface, and a mic / speaker to conduct a telephone conversation via your wrist. But it costs twice as much (or more) and currently only works with the Samsung Galaxy Note 3 smartphone.

The bottom line:

If you have an iPhone, choose the Pebble.
If you have or want a Samsung Galaxy Note 3, choose the Samsung Galaxy Gear.
In all other cases, providing your smartphone runs Android 4.0 or higher, choose the Sony SmartWatch 2.

Review:
I've owned the watch four months now. I LOVE THIS WATCH. It is hands-down the best smart watch I've owned, and I've owned many. It's the first and only Wear OS watch I've had that was buttery-smooth and lag-free.

The battery life is also amazing -- I wear it from 5:30am until 10pm every day, and when I put it on the charger it's typically still around at least 40-50% battery. That's with heavy use and tons of notifications, near-constant heart rate monitoring, etc.

The option to go into a low battery mode that converts the watch into a more traditional watch is also a great feature. I've used it a couple of times but not for any extended length, but I feel like it would last as advertised. A great idea especially for times when a charger isn't handy or feasible, e.g., going camping.

The silicone band is nice, and there are tons of third-party options available as well since it takes a standard 22mm band.

I don't use the 4G/LTE feature because I don't have Verizon, so I can't speak to that functionality.

Overall, this is a great watch and I highly recommend it!

Review: There is virtually nothing about the Vivoactive 3 to not like. After having two Fitbit 3s each consecutively die on me after 6 months, I was looking for a better quality tracker and one with a display that you could actually read. Outside, even when shading the bezel face of the Fitbit with your hand, you could not read the display unless it was heavily overcast. Ambient outdoor lighting was the Fitbit’s nemesis. With the Fitbit, you had to touch the face of the screen which would leave smears and sweat making it impossible to read. The Garmin app is similar to Fitbit but an improvement. Finally, the Garmin relies on GPS to track you, not the movement of your wrist like Fitbit. Much more accurate tracking. I made the mistake of wearing my Fitbit on a motorcycle ride once and afterward, it credited me with 27,000 steps during which my butt was riding the bike....totally Unsatisfactory. At this point, I’m thrilled with the Vivoactive 3.

Review:
I have been wearing my Ticwatch-E for about a month now and it is my first real smartwatch. Up until this point I have had Fitbits (Flex, Charge HR, Charge 2) and when it came time to upgrade I wasn't sure I was willing to spend $150+ for the next gen of the Charge (3) or more for that smartwatch-feel without actually being a smartwatch. I researched and the Ticwatch-E was found to be a good entry-level smartwatch and at $129.99 I thought so too, so I took the plunge and am very happy I did.

My criteria for a smartwatch was affordability, style, quality, and performance. I wanted a round face watch, I wanted it to be under $200, and I wanted it to not be glitchy or cheaply made. I'm not gonna lie, the round face was at the top of my list for things I wanted. Just so you know how I operate :)

Coming from Fitbit software and relying on Google Fit for my activity-tracking data was a bit of an adjustment as Google Fit doesn't have sleep tracking, and with their recent update, eliminated the widget. This seems counter-intuitive to me, but whatever. The Mobvoi software for Ticwatch is comparable to Google Fit. I've read complaints that the software is not extremely accurate, but as long as it is in the general ballpark, I am happy. Not gonna complain unless it just is literally not working at all or completely wrong. I did experience one glitchy moment which occurred after it updated immediately following initial set-up. The watch was giving me the computer equivalent of the neverending screen loading. It was suggested in the manual that if there were issues after updating, to do a reset, and after I did that, it was fine and has been since even after another update just two days ago.

It came packaged in a cool tin, with a charging cable which has a magnetic contact so that the watch is secure during charging, however, I purchased a charging dock so that I could keep the cable in my bag for use at work or in my vehicle. The watch charges quickly both on the cable and the dock, and has what I consider to be decent battery life. I put it on the charger overnight, but once a week I let it drain out and it usually takes a little over two days of average use. My average use consists of always on display turned on, checking weather and Google Fit data periodically, receiving text or phone call notifications, with social media notifications turned off (I had them on for the first couple of days, but the volume of notifications became distracting).

I really like this smartwatch, and of course, the true beauty of it lies in Wear OS (formerly Android Wear). There are thousands of beautiful, functional, and customizable watch faces available to make my watch uniquely my own. So although switching from Fitbit has been an adjustment, I am really loving this watch! I did buy a third-party band, but the band that came with it was reasonably comfortable, which is unusual for me. None of my Fitbit bands were comfortable, so the fact that this one actually is, says something.

Overall, I would definitely recommend the Ticwatch-E. It is a great entry-level smartwatch with plenty of features and comes in a variety of styles. At the time when I purchased mine, there was a promotion going on that included a free Ticband activity tracker, a $50 value. ;)

Review: As a smartwatch this device works fine, as a fitness tracker it is shit. I honestly cannot recommend this device for anyone who is interested in using the fitness tracking features. I started going to the gym again and focusing on heavy cardio. The heart rate monitor on this watch rarely shows the correct heart rate. My heart rate was at 145+ and the watch said 84. If you use any of the tracking functions (turning on cardio mode) it gets a little better, but the battery drains way too quick. A 90 minute workout with cardio turned on drained 20% of the batter. If you are using any of the tracking features, you will probably get 3.5 days on battery, nowhere near the 7 days advertised. Best batter life I have gotten was 6.5 days with all features turned off.

I've had the ticwatch pro for three months and really like it.
Pros

Cons

Review:
I am in love with this Garmin and it has been on my hand consistently since the day it arrived. I take it off only to charge it every 3 weeks. I did rush and put a screen protector on it because it has already collected a number of scratches on the bezel from meeting hard objects at work. I am very pleased with the functionality offered and the price paid. I sure wish that I didn't have to wear a heart rate chest strap, but I am used to this from my previous Garmin devices. It is compatible with most other Garmin chest straps and other peripherals (like bike cadence sensor). I have only used it extensively on hikes as it is still rather rainy outside, but I will start using it instead of my Garmin Edge 500 to track bike rides soon. I will report on battery life with the GPS on as it is significantly shorter (rated at 10 hrs from full charge) compared to regular tracking mode.
I am very pleased that the device now supports auto-sleep function. It did not do this when it first arrived, but software updates have been swift from Garmin. I believe I received 3 updates in the past 2 months and hope to see future support for this great smart activity tracker. I have enjoyed the alerts on emails and phone calls as well as the silent buzzing alarms. I wish there was more control over the alerts to "move" as it can get a bit annoying when it alerts you to move just 30 minutes after you walked extensively but that's a setting that is not adjustable yet. I did notice a significant drain on my Android phone (Nexus 5X) initially under bluetooth connections. The only device it was connected to was this Garmin. Subsequent software updates and updates to the android Garmin app have reduced bluetooth usage. I wish this was a setting in the app to reduce sync-ing over bluetooth to only a few times a day instead of constantly pushing data through. I am certain there is more to come for the Vivoactive. It has definitely hit the spot with my needs. I will report later if I run into serious issues or if I have other comments after further usage.

Update 10/6/2016: The loop that holds the end of the silicon strap failed today. The band itself still holds just fine, but now the edge catches onto everything I brush against. I am considering the magnetic milanese band for this watch as I hit the gym 6 days a week and I need the band to be vented of some sorts so leather will not do. I also had the chance to do quite a few bike rides with the vivoactive. It has replaced my Garmin Edge 500 completely. It offers more or less the same functionality, accuracy and the battery lasts up to 8 hours with the GPS running. You may need to consider this if you're going on really long bike rides or long hikes. I keep my rides under 6 hours and this leaves me enough battery life to not worry about having to charge right away. It also lasts through 10+ one hour long gym exercises with heart rate tracking enabled. All in all a very solid device and a battery life that's extremely hard to beat for the functionality you get out of it.

Special Features: Time Display, Email, Calendaring, Text Messaging, Notifications, Music Player
Review:
I have the previous edition SW1 and I decided to upgrade to this year's model. Compared to last year's edition, this is a complete redesign. Sony basically addressed all the complaints from last years model and has taken most of the advantages of the Pebble Smartwatch away. Everything is better including the look, fit, and finish. I get a lot of compliments for how good the watch looks. Last year's model was almost a iPod Nano with a watch strap.

Here are the pros:

-bigger and better screen (1.3" vs 1.6"-full Transflective display 220x176 & scratch-resistant)
-longer battery life (3-7 days depending on usage vs 2-3 days previously)
-more touch sensitive
-back/home/menu buttons like all Android phones
-menu is easier to use (no touch controls to close apps or swiping up and down)
-settings in watch to switch clocks and apps (some apps still require in phone settings)
-easier setup with NFC and tap with phone (make sure you enable NFC in your phone)
-water resistant IP57 (30 minutes in FRESH water up to a depth of 1 meter & dust proof)
-lots of free apps (weather, currency, photo slideshow, Twitter, Facebook, GPS, games like Blackjack etc.)
-easier to charge with standard micro USB charger (proprietary connection previously)
-easier to see in daylight
-watch is always on to tell time but dims to save battery, push power button to lighten up (previous model turned off with no time display)
-works with any Android smartphone running version 4.0 (Ice Cream Sandwich) or later
-aluminum body (plastic body previously)
-ability to change watch band (it can take any standard watch strap that is 24 millimeters wide).
-all previous apps work from last year's model

cons:
-limited apps out made for the SW2, but this will change as it's just been released
-currently only 5 watch faces in settings (there are paid apps with more)

What do I use my watch for?
-Tell time of course, but also with the ability to change the look each day (almost a new watch daily).
-Notifications for emails, texts, tweets, and calls (discreetly reading a text during a meeting or while driving or in a theater).
-Never miss a call again when you are outside (watch vibrates when calls come in, you can answer or end call--still need phone to talk).
-Finding my phone (I can call my phone from the watch to hear where I left it).
-Never leave your phone behind again (because it's connected by bluetooth, the watch will vibrate to notify if you are too from it or the connection has been lost).
-There are unlimited uses as more apps become available. Some others include a flashlight, viewing my camera while I'm away from my phone, remotely turning phone to silent or vibrate mode etc.

Note: Sony created "Open Smarthwatch Project" this past summer to third-party firmware which enables any developer to create apps for their smartwatches. So there is no need to worry about a lack of future support. This watch is actually Sony's third smartwatch. In 2010 Sony Ericsson unveiled the "LiveView Watch" and 2012 Sony released "SmartWatch 1" (bought out Ericsson).

Update Oct 23rd, 2013
I've had the watch for just over two weeks now. I've managed to get almost 7 days battery life by turning the watch off each night before bed. Without turning it off, I managed to get about 5 days. So if you are lazy at charging, turn off the watch to get an extra couple of days. Another tip for a fast charge (approx. 1 hour), I use a 10 watt AC adapter charger that came with my Nexus 7--like I mentioned, that standard micro USB is handy. These 10 watt AC adapter chargers can be found online for $10.

Update 2 Dec 6, 2013
I received a free update for the watch last night, Sony has doubled the total watch faces to 10! There are some very nice designs from digital, analog, and classic museum styles.

Anyone trying to decide between the Sony SW2 or Samsung Gear may want to read this article:
Sony outsmarts Samsung with SmartWatch 2
[...]

Special Features: Fitness Tracker, Sleep Monitor, Bluetooth call, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
This is my first smartwatch as I wasn’t really looking for one. But, reading the features, it seems like a cool one at a decent price and it looks very stylish as well.

What do you get:

1- The smart watch
2- Charger
3- One metal and one rubber band
4- The band length adjusting tool
5- User manual

This smart watch comes in a nice box that has inserts for every piece. The watch itself has plastic wrappers to prevent the scratches until the first use. It has a bunch of cool features, such as sport tracker, O2 and heartbeat sensors, text/call and etc. The trackers seem to be using the features of your phone so they should give you accurate numbers. The heartbeat sensor doesn’t seem to be that accurate, on the other hand. The watch has some themes available that you can choose from the way you like it. The dial screen is also customizable using their app on the phone.

The user manual is not practically that useful, but the pairing is super easy, regardless. All you need is to download the GloryFit app on your android which you can get to by the barcode that is available in the smartwatch. The app is not hard to work with. It gives you the option to activate the notifications for text, call, and some apps, separately. You can also sync your contacts which wasn’t that hard but there is a limit on how many you can sync. The app also lets you to customize the dial screen by downloading some of the pre-made themes or just uploading your own pictures.

After pairing with the phone and enabling the required permissions (the app didn’t ask for any weird permission unlike some others I have seen), everything works perfectly. You see both the text and call notifications pretty quick and can take the calls on your watch or call someone from it if you need. However, you cannot send texts from the watch. Going through the menus was super easy and the touchscreen is not a pain to work with. Everything is easily accessible as well.

Cons:

1- The watch cannot send text messages. 2- The metal band is very stylish but doesn’t seem to be scratch-resistant.

Overall, I would totally recommend this smart watch. If you are not looking for some crazy features and don’t want to pay hundreds on your watch, this is an amazing deal.

Review:
Let me first say that I researched many of the wrist HR monitors out there and Garmin seemed to offer the best bang for the buck. In terms of functionality, visibility in the sun, swipe menu's etc. I won't go into much detail here about that because all of those functions are great. Also, in terms of the app, the UI is ok. A bit hard to navigate and not as intuitive as Fitbit ( my wife has the fitbit Charge 2). Also, the integration into MyFitnessPal works great. Finally, it's water resistant! The sleep tracking function is also pretty good. Maybe not 100% accurate but good enough to get a general idea of your sleep patterns.Now the reason I'm giving this watch a solid 3 stars:

1. I discovered a major bug in the Garmin Connect app for iPhone a week after I had the watch. It turns out that you cannot turn off or change the alarm time setting to the vivosmart hr+ in the app. Every time you try to either turn it off or change the time it looks like it works and syncs but then you go back into it and it's reverted back. After a few frustrating tries and the annoyance of having to turn off the watch at night (don't want to get up at 5:45am on a Saturday) I went to Garmin support and apparently they are aware of it and are "working on it". No specific timeline available, just "working on it". BUT in the meantime if I want to change my alarm setting I must do it via my computer with the sync cable. HOW ANNOYING.

2. The watch sucks for tracking my weight training HR and interval training sessions. I know for a fact that my HR was at 115-120 at different times and the vivosmart hr+ read at 89. It almost seems like it's taking an average.

2.b I think because it's terrible at tracking accurate HR intervals for weight training / cross training sessions, the App doesn't have that option on their "Activities" menu. You have have to select "Other" to track this and even then it's not totally accurate. It just tracks your time but HR intensity is laughable.

Overall it's a great watch with these pitfalls. If you're more of a runner or biker and want to track your HR rate with GPS and other app integration it's great. If on the other hand you're like me an more of a cross athletic trainer that wants a more accurate HR for interval training, it may be worth looking into other options.

Review:
I have had this watch almost a year and use it every day. It helps manage notifications when my phone is in my pocket or perhaps in another room in the house. It tells time - shocker, but that's one thing I actually wanted it for. I use the Google Fit app to track my workouts, and Ticwatch sends heartrate data. I also use Google Pay, and it is so easy (and cool) to hold my wrist up to the payment kiosk to pay.

I've never used the watch for sleep tracking because I don't want something on my wrist in bed. As a result, I charge my Ticwatch every night. During times when I couldn't charge it, the battery would last through the second day. I think the layered display really helps with battery life. As far as I'm concerned, my battery rarely dips below 50%, so it is a non-issue.

I recently had an issue where the watch started acting oddly. Even after charging, it wouldn't turn on. I thought it was dead but was still under warranty. I contacted Mobvoi, they asked me about my problem, sent me a new charging cable, and that was the whole issue. Watch is fine. So kudos to their responsive customer service as well.

Overall, Ticwatch does everything I want and more in a smartwatch. I've very happy with my purchase.

Review:
Upon receiving it, I paired it to the Vband app. The watch had some issues with the steps counter, all the other numerous functions worked well.
I needed a little time to understand the menus, and how they control the watch functions, the manual sheet is not very detailed.
Next day I found an update for the watch firmware (V1.25) in the App. After installing it, everything worked fine.
I found the audio portion of the watch is quite interesting, the device has 4 GB of memory which can store a lot of music and a mp3 player., the watch has a speaker with a very good sound for its size, it is possible to use it for the music stored in the watch or in the phone mp3 player, also, the earbuds can be used instead of the speaker, additionally, the earbuds can be paired with other mp3 players with BT.

Review: I've owned an Up band, Fitbit Flex, and Misfit Shine.  This is by far the superior activity tracker. I'm not wowed by the app, but the device itself is awesome.  I thought the Shine was great because of the battery life over the weekly recharging on the Up (original version) and Flex (also the Up and Flex both crapped out in less than 6 months).  What I didn't like about the Shine is it vanished on me by easily popping out of its band in 2 months.  I also was getting tired of wearing a watch and an activity band.  The Shine would tell time for you if you could see the little lights and only after you tapped it which didn't always result in a response.  Now with the Vivofit 2, I'm getting the best of both worlds...a great watch and a well designed activity tracker.  The red bars across the top are a great reminder that I am sitting on my butt too much and to start moving.  I enjoy keeping track of my steps and seeing a count to reach or how much I've exceeded my daily goal. I also appreciate the ever changing daily goal it gives you based on your average activity.  While the band is amazing in design with the locking clasp, I have already lost one.  But to its defense, I was racing my Yamaha Waveblaster (jet ski) and it vanished to the depths of the water I was on when I crashed.  Besides that, I've never had an issue with the band, and quickly bought another to replace the lost one.  Yes, it's bulkier than others, but I don't care...it serves as a watch so I'm no longer dealing with both a watch and some other band next to it.  The app is so so, not the best and definitely could be improved, but it's usable.  The auto tracking (or manual if you chose to do so) of sleep seems spot on compared to the other three I've owned.  It will count steps when it shouldn't, like driving, but all of them did...and this seems to be minimal.  Overall, this is a great activity tracker and I will hopefully get the most life out of this one unlike my experience with the others.

Review:
I actually have an original TicWatch that I got as a backer on their Kickstarter from a long time ago (which sadly ceased to function due to hardware/software limitations). I was always happy with the watch and the features you got comparable to a Samsung or FitBit type product. This version has some great upgrades based on the new technology, most notably the health trackers. I will say you need to wear the watch quite tightly in order to measure pulse/oxygen, etc., but that makes sense since you are trying to monitor things though your skin. Since it's based on Android, it has all the features you'd expect like access to your calendar, text messages, phone notifications, calculator, timer and even some things like the Google Translate app, that I thought were nice additions. The interface is pretty simple with swipes up, down, left and right to get to the various features. There are two physical buttons on the side to control power and some of the app access if you don't want to swipe the watch face. The watch face is customizable through the Mobvoi app but they've started charging you for some of them when they used to be free before. There is still a nice free selection of both classic analog watch faces along with digital and novelty ones as well. And it's a round watch face so looks more like a traditional watch than a health tracker.

I will have to ding them them on the proprietary charger. I just dislike the fact that you need to carry around their specific charger. It really should be wireless through a QI charger at this point. Their charger is magnetic so snaps right into place but still, you're locked into that and would have to order a new one if you lost or damaged the original.

The app is nice and if you give it permission, it tracks things for you. All of which is totally optional. I like to keep track of my movement since it's a wearable and counts all activity unlike a phone which you have to remember to pick up and carry with you. Some of the other health stuff is interesting like the pulse rate, blood oxygen, etc. You can even set that to 24 monitoring so it tracks that stuff along with your sleep. There is even a "Zen" function to help you relax. Sometimes there is a bit of a lag between when the app updates to your current information, but it gets there.

Battery life seems good. I've been using it as a standard watch/health tracker and don't do much in the way of messaging or making/receiving phone calls, so I'd expect the battery to drain a bit faster in that case. I can go most of a day and still be at about 90% with general use.

All in all, it's a very full featured smartwatch and a great value for the price. The hardware is quality and it feels solid. I miss my original TicWatch but this one was a nice upgrade for me and has everything I want in terms of the current technology.

Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
***UPDATE 12/7/2016***

Fitbit is buying out Pebble:

Fitbit will maintain services so that Pebble devices continue to work as normal until at least the end of 2017. Pebble functionality and service may be reduced in the future. Pebble Time 2 won't come to life at this point now will the core.

This is truly sad as I loved my first gen and I really like this watch. I am happy with it but the Heart Rate monitor is currently flawed. I don't think I could recommend buying this watch at this point but I might buy another Pebble 2 without the HEart Rate Monitor if they go on discount before they disappear forever.

All over the tech blogs people are talking about the death of the smart watch section and how they failed to materialise anything worthwhile. I really couldn’t disagree more but I had the first gen pebble for over a year and pebble 2 for over a month now. I am sad that they are now defunct but it represented what a smart watch was supposed to do. It fit great battery life, I only missed call or the notifications I wanted if my phone was off(on android I heard apple was a different story but that’s because of the walled garden), and it didn’t try to do things it shouldn’t like lower me doodle on a tiny screen on my wrist. The only feature that I really doing it lacking on was NFC/mobile wallet, however that had the potential to be addressed if smart straps had come to fruition before pebble died, sadly that will never be the case now. I really blame the people that review smartwatches for this demise in part besides any fault of the company. They promote apple or Samsung but so few ever even wrote about pebble, good or bad. Don’t get me wrong the pebble 2 had it’s flaws, the great rate monitor is a joke but overall it is the best smart watch out there at the moment. The time 2 looked great too, I’m sad I’ll never see one. That’s just my two cents but I agree most of the avast watches out there are gimmicky and fall short, but that category didn’t your coverage did. I’m hoping by the time my pebble 2 needs to be replaced there will be something worthy out there

My original review:

I was one of the Kickstarter backers for the P2. I got mine in Black/flame. Previously I had an original pebble smart watch that I really loved and I wanted to upgrade it after a few years on my wrist. There was nothing wrong with the old watch, which I think speaks pretty well for Pebble and the product they made. This review is specifically written comparing it to the first gen Pebble. This review isn't to convince you that you need a smart watch, I like mine as I can keep my phone on silent 100% of the time but I don't miss calls, and I can see when important notifications come through. I love my pebble because it gets good battery life so I don't have to worry about charging it every other day and it just "works" I don't need to constantly tinker with it. Because of the battery life it is the only smart watch I would consider. I consider Pebble smart watches to be the best smart watches out there, I don't need a touch screen or to doodle on my watch, it exists to prevent me from taking out my phone every time it buzzes or I need to log in with an authentication key and this watch does that perfectly

Please feel free to ask any questions and I will answer if I can!

Design / feel in hand / on wrist

So this is much sleeker than the first gen Pebble, more compact and it feels much nicer on the wrist, moving the charger port to the back makes it looks much sleeker. The case watch feels very nice, the tolerances are all very tight and the buttons all have a nice satisfying click. The mic works well for voice texting, I haven't tried it to much outdoors but it works well every time I have tested it. The band feels nice and is very similar to the first gen band which I also thought was very high quality. The real difference here is the gorilla glass screen is slightly raised compared to the flat surface of the entire first gen Pebble. It doesn't take anything away from the watch the only problem I could see would be it would be harder to add a third party skin like I did on my first gen, however this watch looks really good in black / flame so I'm not looking for a wrap at the moment. If I do I will add what I add and what I think of it

Software

So this is a huge improvement from the first gen watch. The main settings menu is a bit flatter and gives you battery life right on the settings line in a 10% (it goes by 10% increments) It also gives me the local forecast on the main settings page when (see the attached picture for clarity) I don't run a ton of apps on my Pebble, the main one I use is the QuickAuth app to run my google authentication key. I would highly recommend this app. I use MisFit instead of Pebble Health because my work health program can sync with MisFit and not Pebble so it imports my steps to Virgin Pulse. A small bonus, otherwise I would use Pebble Health, it seemed that for the day that I had both turned on Pebble Health seemed to track fewer steps than MisFit but I am not sure which is more "accurate" I don't really care about that one way or another. Allowing to luanch quick launch off the center and back buttons is also nice as it lets you get to 2 more apps quickly. 4 is more than enough for my personal uses but of course that is personal preference

Battery Life

So from my attached picture you can see I am at 30% battery, that should last me easily until tomorrow when I will charge it before I leave work for the day. I could most likely go 2 more days and charge it in the morning when I first get to work. It was last charged 5 days ago. I would rate my usage of it as moderate so I am well within the battery range advertised. I actually think it gets slightly worse battery life than my first gen pebble but I also have the weather app enabled so it is pulling that data from my smart watch more often. I also am playing with the new "toy" more often so I can't say with 100% certainty. Of course it could always have more battery life but I am very content with the battery life this watch gets over all. I also have Pebble Health turned off (and the heart rate monitor) as I use MisFit to count steps for my company fitness program. If Virgin Pulse would every sync with Pebble Health I would drop MisFit in a heart beat but that is another matter. I imagine this has a positive impact on my battery life not having the heart rate sensor enabled

Overall I am very pleased with this watch, it was a great upgrade from the first gen Pebble and I would buy another hands down, I wouldn't mind getting a Pebble Time 2, but that isn't in the budget any time soon this will happily stay on my wrist every day for the next few years. The software upgrade and the sleeker form factor are much appreciated and the price point is very reasonable, if you don't care about the heart rate sensor (which I don't) they are even coming out with one that is just the Pebble 2

Review:
I've been meaning to get a smartwatch for a while now, as I needed something that could not just help me make calls without removing my phone from its holster, but could also monitor my heart health (as I suffer from hypertension).

This NX3 model is small enough to not be obtrusive, and sleek enough to not look like I have a large mini-computer strapped to my wrist. The unit came with relatively simple instructions to set up and synch to my phone, and learning the functions was easy enough, though the instruction sheets had a rather small print style that made them a little hard to read at times.

The battery cable has a magnetic function that makes it simple to snap on to charge the watch battery, and it does last awhile with simple use.

The unit also came with a spare wristband, which is good. The only negative thing here is that the wristbands are barely long enough to go around my wrist! (I have 9.5” wrist size, and the watch does fit but with only the last tab in place) Would recommend that anyone with a large wrist look into getting a longer strap. Will update this later.

The only other nit I have is that the watch-synch overrides the phone’s speaker when videos or media play on the phone (i.e. Facebook reels, YouTube Vids. etc,). If this can be overridden, I would like to know how.

Apart from that, this is still a solid smartwatch. It works for the reasons I bought it for -- health monitoring, making calls, etc. -- and so far it has stood up to the daily routine I have with my work/job. If you are looking for a new smartwatch, this would be a good one for beginners.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
I just want to say "I am in love!" and this is only after 1 use. I ordered the 910xt November 25th, 2011 after googling it several hundred times over the prior 2 weeks. It finally arrived February 29th, 2012 (it is my understanding these types of delay are not unusual for Garmin when releasing new products). My husband stayed home all day to be sure he didn't miss the UPS truck while I was at work, bless his heart!

I didn't have high expectations when deciding to purchase the watch. I am an age group triathlete that just enjoys the journey (and the excuse to cross train for variety). Previously, I used a cat eye bike computer, a basic water proof sports watch, and my iPhone to track my workouts. For a long time the iPhone served my needs just fine for biking and running with the TrainingPeaks app, but then for some reason (after an iPhone OS update) my TrainingPeaks app stopped recording GPS data well and I wasn't getting good data and often my routes didn't get recorded. Hence, I started looking for other GPS solutions and fell in love with the idea of a watch that could do it all from entering the water to finishing a run. That is when I got it stuck in my mind the new Garmin 910xt was what I needed and would add a lot of new fun data to track. Again, I am not a serious athlete, but I do have fun watching improvements in my fitness data and I believe the data helps in understanding my body better as an athletic adult entering her 40's this year.

During my persistent googling of the Garmin 910xt to read every review I could before pulling the trigger on a $449 watch with a heart rate strap (plus another $75 or so for the quick release and bike speed/cadence sensor I wanted), I came across DCRainmaker who did an extensive review on it. That website is the greatest site for all sporty tech things you want reviewed, including the 910xt. There is one extensive review on the 910xt, including a video of a wetsuit removal with the watch on and another article on the comparison to the swimsense. I suggested if you want detailed review information, you check out DCRainmaker's website.

I haven't yet used the bike or run features yet, but I did buy the quick release kit and the bike speed/cadence sensor. They were both very easy to set up and I look forward to having new bike data (cadence) that I have never captured before. Also the ANT+ stick for the computer installed easy and data transfer was not a problem. The only thing to note is at first I thought the watch wasn't reading the heart rate strap or ANT+ stick, but it was settings on the watch that needed to be changed to allow for it to pair. After I found those settings everything worked great. The quick release system seems to be sturdy, yet also easy to transfer from wrist to bike with a quarter turn.

What is really awesome, but also really time consuming, is setting the watch up. For example, lap swimming mode has 35 some different data fields that are captured and you can set the watch up to display 4 fields at a time and you can have upto 4 different screens (16 maximum fields displayed). Trying to determine what is most important to you and how you want to be able to scroll through those items can send your head spinning. Then you factor in that you can set each activity's data screens differently, you have 5 modes (lap swimming, open water swimming, biking, running, and other) all with the ability to customize upto 4 screens of data with upto 4 items on each screen ... It is like a restaurant menu that just has toooooo many choices and you want to eat it all. I have already re-organized every possible data screen at least twice, and sure I will do it again. It will take me awhile to determine what data is most important to me and probably will even change as my training goals change. So much to choose from, especially now that I can see open water swim distances, swim efficiencies, bike cadence, heart rate, along with the usual distance, time, pace choices (and more) ... so much new data for me to collect and I can do it with one device the 910xt.

My first (and so far only activity use) was lap swimming this morning. Hey, I only got the watch yesterday after getting home from work and had to go to work today (okay, I did think about calling in so I could do a brick workout in all three disciplines, but didn't). I was a swimmer from the time I learned to swim through high school, so lap swimming is very natural to me. This being said, it means I have very good swimming form; therefore, had very few issues with the watch having inaccuracies. It did record two of my freestyle laps as backstroke, but it did record every lap. Everyone needs to remember that it will never be perfect in the water, especially if you break swimming form (ie to adjust goggle straps, stop mid length, make quick accelerations, or do other funny actions). Newer swimmers will probably experience more inaccuracies due to poor form, slow awkward turns, ect, but this is not the fault of the watch as much as the limitations of how an accelerometer works. Also it does not really handle a workout with timed intervals (like 10x100 on 1:45) well, but if you remember to push the stop/start and also the lap button, you can get some good interval data to dissect later on while still using the lap timer (or clock) at most pools.

All I already know is this one device will capture lots of training information. I used to have to wear a water proof watch to capture swimming time data, had to write down lap workout data (after keeping it in my memory during the swim), had to estimate open water swimming distances, had a bike computer coupled with my iPhone for my bike workouts, and again my iPhone for run workouts (which if you remember correctly, stopped working well). In addition, when the GPS feature works on an iPhone, it wears down the battery life down very quickly, so workouts can not be very long and still be accurately captured. Now one device captures it all and uploads it nicely to the computer for easy dissection. No more manual entries for swimming, no more estimating open water swim distances, no more recharging the iPhone between long brick sets, ect ... While the $449 w/heart rate strap may seem like a high price, for me I love that I now have one device to capture all my workout data and track my routes and don't have much to manually record anymore.

This watch is a Jack of All Trades, but you do have to remember it is a Master of None.

If Garmin representatives come across this review, the only thing I ask is that future updates consider the ability to create workouts for swimming based on the option of yards or meters that include the ability to do timed interval sets. For now, I will continue to plan my swim workouts like I used to, but am glad that I will be able to easily upload the result of my workout without relying on my memory so much, and I can now enjoy a bunch of new data to analyze.

I want to also add for those of you commenting about it not being a "sports watch". It does have a 20 hr GPS battery life, so would run through the day and just need to be plugged in every night (like an iPhone). Additionally, I took the "other mode" which I don't have a use for and set the main screen to 3 fields (time of day - big and on top) and sunrise and sunset (smaller and on the bottom). If I wore it daily, this makes it like any other watch and has the great inclusion of sunrise and sunset times. It is too big for my small wrists to wear daily, even at that, in the business world I function in, I would never be wearing a sports watch all day anyways. I still can go to a workout location using it like a regular watch (primary screen of other mode) and then I can change modes for whatever my workout will be. Additionally, I have read that if you turn off the GPS (set watch to indoors) you would have over 50 hours battery life. So those who knock this great cross training device based on it not being a watch I think are just being a little crabby and unbending, because you can make adjustments to make it function like a regular sports watch.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: 20 hr GPS battery life, over 50 hours battery life if you turn off the GPS (set watch to indoors)
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a battery life of at least 20 hours when using GPS. The customer needs a smart watch that can achieve over 50 hours of battery life when GPS is turned off for indoor use.

Review:
It's a great smart watch for under $200. I got my first smartwatch at $130. The battery starts out a little wonky, but consistent recharging seems to have alleviated this problem. The material is great a thin, light rubber-like band and a super light plastic watch with a nice screen. It is amazingly lightweight, sometimes I forget I'm wearing. Runs wear OS so with an android phone (which I have) it connects so easily I thought I did it wrong. Again, the only issue I had was with the battery and that was solved by itself.

As far as the software, it runs smoothly and many watch faces to choose from. Plenty of connectivity to your phone of your choice. You DO NOT need a Samsung or iPhone to operate this watch. I have a newish (summer 2018) Huawei Honor 7X and what's nice is if I upgrade due to a break or something, I won't have to get a new watch. Just log in on my new phone with the app. Like I said, plenty of options for apps to work with, including the basics: messaging, notifications, phone calls. You can take and make phone calls on the watch as well, very cool feature for a smartwatch beginner who thought this budget smartwatch would cut some corners, but it seemingly didn't.

All in all, I love this smartwatch, I wear lots of different watches for style and timekeeping, but when I know I'm going to be away or separated from my phone, or I need to see a certain phone call or text, or any situation that would be beneficial to have a smart watch, I wear my Ticwatch. It's still a little expensive compared to some REALLY budget smartwatches, but I bet those aren't up to snuff. Additionally, the lightweight aspect can make the watch feel a little cheap and not so durable, but I don't think this is the case and I haven't had the opportunity to compare it to another smartwatch.

In conclusion, 10/10 for everything about the watch including the watch except battery. 9/10 for battery, since it doesn't seem to work straight out the box but still a happy ending.

Review:
Update 7/30/18: Screen started acting weird and display is showing blue streaks when things move on the screen. Having a couple syncing issues, and the stock band gives me a rash. Upgraded to a Forerunner 935 and its a HUGE improvement. If you're thinking about spending a little extra, I would do the same!

Review:
[[VIDEOID:1dc9aa1c1997f07cca07603a31c30bd7]] USAGE:
I have a number of smartwatches. I'm always on the hunt to add to my collection. This watch has a great price point and will fit into my usage scenarios. My primary use of a smartwatch is to tell time, and to manage my phone, such as notifications and take and make phone calls. Alarms are important, simply because it's close and handy and I don't have to fumble for the phone when it goes off. This watch does that. I will use this lower priced watch when I'm in a more rough environment. I do expect it to be rugged and hold up because the construction seems solid.

LIKES:
- I can make calls and take calls on this phone! Very important. I would have no interest in this smartwatch if it lacked this feature.
- Bright touch screen. The display is gorgeous and easily on par with my other more expensive smartwatches.
- Very nice set of built in apps!
- The Voice Assistant will open Google Assistant. I'm not sure if this watch works with Apple and Siri, because I don't have an iPhone. If I connect it to a family member's iPhone down the road to experiment, I will update this review.
- The battery life is pretty good. I haven't charged it yet and I still have 80% and have been using the watch for a week. That's the advantage the manufacturer has by not providing too many "active" health features. The battery can last quite some time.
- I do trust the pulse rate measurements, and this watch has an "Elevated Heart Rate Measurement" setting which will alert me should my pulse get too high.
- I can find my watch from the phone, and I can find my phone from the watch. This is an essential smartwatch feature and this watch has it.
- This is kinda handy. I can setup my phone to take a photo by shaking the watch. This is only useful when I need to be stepped away from my phone perhaps for an extended selfie. Perhaps you can think of more uses for this feature than I have.
- It has a pedometer! I love keeping track of my daily steps.
- It's one of the more comfortable smartwatches I have worn.

DISLIKES:
- Blood pressure inaccuracy. To be fair, only a select few watches are reliable with blood pressure (BP). This is the second inexpensive watch I've used BP on and it is in a word, laughable. I would be happy even with the BP delta's to have some semblance of usable BP data, but nope, not even that. According to this watch, my BP fluctuates minimally, and I'm very very very healthy. It's a joke and should NOT be taken seriously.
- Surprisingly, I actually use the blood oxygen saturation feature on my other watches. I found this watch to be too optimistic. As an example, I have the Flu right now and my O2 saturation is diminished from normal. This watch shows me at 98-99% while my handy dandy set of pulse oximeters show 94-96%. Another watch I use shows a bit lower at 97%. In this case, I'm use the delta to simply subtract 4. I still have my doubts because I haven't seen it below 96%.
- No App Store! What comes with the watch is what you get, nothing more.

THINGS YOU SHOULD KNOW:

This watch has quite a library of workout activities in which it records the workout data. This is of no interest to me so I did not experiment.

The only exception to the above statement is that it has a pedometer. I do like to keep track of steps taken each day.

I will not be showering with this smartwatch on. It is a well known problem that smartwatches, while waterproof, don't seem to handle the heated water and will eventually leak. Just don't do it, or one of these days, probably after the warranty, your watch will short out.

This smartwatch will not connect on it's own to a set of Bluetooth ear buds. It will however use the music player app to control the phone volume and music player, such as Youtube. This also means that if you take a call on the watch, it's on speaker and you will have no call-privacy.

There is no "App Store" to download additional apps. What you get is what you get, that's it. I did update to the latest firmware, and I suppose they can add apps that way, but don't count on it. Now with that said, You can download quite an assortment of watch faces or dials. I'm picky about my watch interfaces and I found several that I like.

The watch has a raise-to-wake feature which is nice. That simply means you don't have to touch the watch face, or push a button to get the watch to light up.

I doubt I will find any protective watch face covers for purchase, because this watch is a one-off. Now with that said, due to the price point, I simply don't mind some scratches and scuffs that are going to appear on my watch over time.

This is a fantastic watch for the price and a great introductory smartwatch to own. I'm pleased to have it in my collection.

UPDATES:
None at this time. I will update this review with any changes or important additions should any arise.

1. Don't like that on the app in the personal information the height and weight are only in cm and kg, it can't be changed to feet or lbs pounds.

Review:
The Vivoactive has a dimmer screen than the Fitbit Versa, understandable as this is their simpler model. The LCD is a front-lit or reflective type, and it always stays on so if you choose an inverted (all-white or bright-colored) watch face in the sunlight, it actually looks pretty good. When this breaks I'll probably upgrade to the 245 or whatever the equivalent is later on, one that has an OLED display or something bold and striking. Although it is far more reliable when connecting to my phone, it is too limited when showing messages or e-mails, often in another language.

We are two decades into the 21st century... It is not acceptable to flood the market with IOT devices, wearables, car stereos, cellphones, etc., with text displays that do not support multiple languages and Unicode text - it is as simple as that. Bi/multilingual users that need more than English, French and Spanish need to stay clear of this one as it does not correctly support multiple languages - I hope their more top-of-the-line watches do a better job than this does.

The Connect IQ app is far more stable than my Fitbit, which has been getting progressively worse with each update. It is also far more accurate tracking RHR and steps - next up we'll see how well GPS-tethered runs work.

Two Simple words, Garmin - UNICODE SUPPORT.

Review:
I initially selected this activity tracker over other models and brands for its features, sleek appearance, and unique touch screen display. I was aware of the issue of the band coming apart and purchased the upgraded model with a keeper and purchased some extra after-market keepers with my order to avoid losing my investment. When I received it, I loved everything about it, except that it seemed like the display was missing a narrow line of "pixles" along the top edge. As time went on, I noticed that more and more "pixles" began disappearing. I later found out, through a Garmin users group chat room that the missing "pixles" are actually broken LEDs. Many folks on the chat string shared their stories about sending their device back to Garmin (or other authorized place) to have it replaced only to experience the same problem within months of receiving the refurbished device. Others talked about how they loved the device so much, they relied on their mobile connection to retrieve all their data until all the LEDs were completely broken and they were no longer able to connect to their mobile device. Today I can barely make out the numbers on the display and am very disappointed to find myself in the same position as so many others that truly love a product that has failed them miserably.

I cannot provide any useful feedback on the HR monitor band because I never used it - seemed like more trouble than what it was worth.

I will research the new Vivosmart HR as a replacement for my current device. Although the display on the new model reverts back to the previous technology, I have not seen any complaints about this style of display becoming illegible or dysfunctional. So, while I'm not crazy about the style of the display on the new model, I am happy that Garmin discontinued the touch screen for the good of their reputation and for the sanity of their consumers.

Review:
I bought, tried and returned several smart watches before landing on the Ticwatch Pro. Samsung was the best hardware but required adapting to their proprietary apps which I wasn't interested in doing. Next was the then most recent Fossil which runs on Wear OS. Once I knew I preferred the Wear OS I then researched and found Ticwatch Pro. Swapping out thin band it came with for a 46mm Galaxy Watch band (yes they fit perfectly) my watch has the look of the Samsung but the brains of Wear OS and since its a more recent build it operates much smoother than the Fossil.
It seems to do fitness activities fine. I primarily use it for monitoring calls and messages when my hands are busy. Part of this watch's claim to fame is the long battery life associated with how it will toggle from the attractive illuminated LED watch face to a low draw, LCD digital display when not in use. That way the battery isnt wasted lighting up the face for no reason. Makes sense. Theoretically when I flip my wrist up to see the time it will sense the motion and light up. For the most part yes. However, when a call or text comes in, I find myself staring at the blank screen for 2 to 3 beeps or rings before it "wakes up" and shows me the caller or text message. That's too long for me so I use the "Always On" setting which gets me a quicker "wake up" for calls and texts but does limit my battery to just about 7am to 6pm under medium to light use. I sometimes recharge during lunch if I know I have a long afternoon but mainly because I'm a nerd about charging my devices rather than out of necessity.
And then today, 4 months after purchase, it seems the battery must have gone bad. Rapid loss of power 2 hours after taking off the charger. Seems to have gone semi-brick mode with only a lightning bolt icon showing up. A quick google found other ticwatch owners of various models having same. Its under warranty (Very annoying process to submit a ticket btw) so I'm confident they'll repair or replace it. That's fine but I treat my tech stuff pretty well so it is a bit disappointing to have this come up all the same.

Special Features: Blood Pressure, Heart Rate Monitor, Step Counter, Sleep Tracker
Review:
There is a lot to say about a smart watch since they have so many features, but I'll stick to what I know about this one. I bought this watch because I was looking one that could monitor vital statistics and track sleep patterns. This one has this feature, and a lot more. This is definitely a lower end watch compared to the Apple Watch and FitBit but for the money it does some pretty neat tricks.

First off, I figured the interface would be terrible with all the little, tiny buttons, but once I saw it in action it wasn't bad at all. The watch is mostly accessed from the App that comes with it. I also figured the setup would be a pain, but I was pleasantly surprised. After downloading the app by clicking the provided QR code, it installed on my phone and stepped me through the initial setup. The app prompts you for some simple info, like weight, height, age and sex. Afterwards, you can connect to your watch with your phone. (Fully charging takes about an hour.) The charge cord is magnetic. Just move it close to the right spot and it will jump into place.

Now I turned on all the health monitoring features and put the watch on my wrist. It was ready to go. I could touch a button and take my blood pressure. You can also get your temperature, number of steps taken, heart rate, blood oxygen, sleep time and after you wake up you can view your sleep pattern. The sleep monitor scores your overall sleep and details restful, light and REM sleep. Very much what I was looking for. I was able to compare the blood pressure reading from the watch to an Omron arm band blood pressure cuff and a wrist band reading. The cuff reading was 128/87, the wrist band was 116/90, and the watch was 105/75. I like what the watch had to say best, but I would guess the cuff was most accurate.

Without any additional setup, I could control streaming music off YouTube and view incoming text with the watch and view weather info on the watch screen. Supposedly, you can answer and make phone calls as well, but I opted not to enable this feature. All of the features offer some basic feedback on the watch screen, but the app is bigger and better.

You can choose and download hundreds (maybe thousands) of Watch faces from the app. The watch is water resistant but not waterproof. One neat feature is to press the watch's camera button, then position your phone where you like and use the watch to snap as many pics as needed. That's better than using the timer.

There are a number of other features including setting alarms, find watch and creating favorite contacts. There is even a button called "AI Voice," but it doesn't appear to do anything, and I could find nothing about it in the manual. Overall, I'd say this is a fairly useful smart watch that is very easy to get started with and the price is way cheaper than a new model Apple Watch. If you can use its features, for the money, I'd say it's a good deal.
