Special Features: Time Display, Email, Calendaring, Text Messaging, Notifications, Music Player
Review:
I bought the Sony Smart Watch 2 (SW2) used. I have every kind of gadgets and I wanted something new to play with. Well, I bought the SW2 for the value and flexibility over the Samsung Galaxy Gear because it works only for certain Samsung smartphones. Sony SW2 works with any android smartphone which is a big plus for me as I like to change phones often.

As for the hardware, I really like it. Very light and durable. (Dropped few time on tile floor) It's water resistant and has replaceable standard 24mm wrist strap. It has again standard micro USB which is a HUGE plus for me as I hate to mess with another dedicated special cable or charging dock. The battery life is very good and should last you several days without any problem once the "new"ness disappears and you don't constantly play with it. The display is on all the time to function as a watch which is another big plus. It's without back light to save battery. You can double tap to turn on the backlight to get time in the dark, but you have to push the side button to use the "smart" watch features and check notifications. (Notification do flash the info when you get them.) The watch itself doesn't have mic or camera. That's ok as I would use my phone with LG Tone for mic. Camera isn't really necessary and it will be more of a gimmick. NFC works well to connect and bluetooth connection with the phone is very good and stable with good range.

Software is very good. The Sony base software to communicate with the SW2 is good and stable. I love that you can customize the watch face to your heart's content. It's so flexible with so many possible options is various information you can set up to view on the watch face or have widgets. (Info is great but widgets are pretty useless. They are not very responsive to be useful and you still have to push the button on the side to use them.) Now for the additional softwares. The addition for facebook and gmail works well enough. It even has Kakao Talk addition to get the notification which but good surprise. So I get all the useful notifications I need. (yes it has instagram, twitter, and few other popular social app notification additions you can download for free.) So notification is good enough in my opinion.

There are many 3rd Party Apps. There are several "cool" apps that I can use and show off with. Still, cool apps are slow as the processing power of the SW2 wasn't meant to run complex apps. Remote camera lags drastically to make it all but unusable for example. I do like the music remote and toggle apps where I can use it to run my music with LG Tone. Now I have a display for selecting music. I also can turn on and off my wifi tethering without taking my phone out when I use my tablet when I am out. I also use the find my phone app now and then.

Improvements: This is HUGE. I WISH I CAN CONTROL THE VIBRATION INTENSITY. It's TOO LOUD and STRONG. It feels like it's going to break the watch as it shakes it so violently. I don't understand why you can't. Now, these are minor "wants". I want to my SW2 to hold music independently and connect to LG tone when I am not connected to my phone via Bluetooth to use it when I work out. I want the SW2 to be faster with those "cool" apps without sacrificing battery life.

Conclusion: I don't "need" a Smart Watch when I have a Smart Phone. Still, if I am going to have a watch anyways, I want that watch to be cool~ SW2 is a cool watch and a cool toy to play with. I like it, but I know Smart Watches are going to improve in short time. I currently does almost everything I want a "smart" watch to do, so I got it for a very cheap price. (Price of a normal watch) I know better smart watches are coming out. Still, I wouldn't want to spend hundreds of dollars on a smart watch that does what SW2 does better. I rather spend that money on a new tablet or other cool gadgets, not a watch that "displays" information like time, notifications, schedule, and weather, and act as a remote for your phone that is most likely in your pocket...

Review:
I paired this with my iPhone 5s. It was difficult to pair at first, my phone would say it's connected, but the tracker would still say sync. It doesn't come with an owners manual so I had to download it, but it doesn't have good troubleshooting instructions. Basically, I had to connect the tracker to my laptop to update, then restart my phone so it would pair properly.
Pros:
-Find my phone: awesome feature, even tells you when you're getting closer!
-Sleep tracker: estimates when you sleep lightly, deeply, and are awake
-Notification/caller ID: tells you who's calling, when you get an e-mail, text message, or Facebook updates
-Move motivator: tells you you've been sitting too long
-Garmen Connect: the app that you need to download really have some cool and detailed customizable features
-Water Resistant: This is one of the few trackers that I found to be water resistant
-Heart Rate: helps you increase accuracy of calorie burn (with chest strap)
-Pairing: you can stay connected while still connecting to other devices
Cons
-Drains cell phone battery: I get a ton of emails each day so I had to turn off the notifications feature because it would drain my battery quickly which is upsetting
-Accuracy: Doesn't measure altitude so it doesn't know when you're going up a hill or steps. I also hit my step goal while I was brushing my hair, I guess I confused it.
-Chest Strap: not something you're going to wear all day
-Music Controls: not compatible with Pandora or You Tube
-Mode Shifting: you have to tell the device when you're going to perform and activity or sleep
-No User Manual: you have to figure it all out on your own
-Small Display: I'm young and still have good eyesight, but for some, this may be a little too hard to read. Plus there's only 2 lines of viewing which makes it almost impossible to read long e-mails in a short time
I think the Vivosmart HR is the upgraded version of this one, but I'm not fond of the HR monitor putting pressure on my wrist all the time. At least the chest strap is removable.

Review:
Update. Amazing customer service! The pin that holds the latch on the watch broke. And they offered to just send me a new watch! I still love this watch and I am just blown away by their customer service.

The TOZO S1 smart watch came well packaged with a charging cable as well as an extra length of wristband for smaller wrists. I was pleasantly surprised by the wristband material. While it is rubber, it has a comfortable, if not velvety feel to it.
The directions were lacking on functionality however.
This watch has several functions that it can do, from tracking exercise to being used as an alarm clock (set within the app) and acting as a heart rate monitor. It can actually act as a remote to take a photo with your cell phone. It will also find your phone (if you can’t) within a certain Bluetooth range. It can also be used a remote to control your music playing from your phone as well as display notifications that you receive on your phone, like texts and other alerts. So you can just read them on your wrist the size is about the size of an Apple Watch, the larger version.
While this is not an iWatch, it does have good functionality for the price point at $40.
It will keep track of fitness activities, however as of now they only offer four choices...Outdoor Run, Walking, Play Basketball or Mountaineering.
It will record those activities and show your heart rate over the length of time that you are Active.
However the calorie count is wildly inaccurate.
I hope as the software is updated, they will add more activities that are more like what people do. Elliptical machine would be one as well as bicycle riding.
I haven’t had a chance to verify its distance count on walking due to the inclement weather of late.
One nice feature of the watch is it also shows the weather outside, unfortunately it’s not accurate as you can see in the picture above.
So what does this watch do well? Well it is great for receiving those notifications from your phone and tracking your heart rate. I would say those are its 2 strengths.
In a future update, they need to make a 12 hour clock a choice versus just straight 24 hour clock. Which is the only option as of now.
Another issue is the app. When you enter your weight for calorie counting, if you choose imperial units, it only offers 14-68 libs. I have no idea what libs are but they are not lbs (pounds). I think whatever unit this is, that is throwing their calorie count way off. When I changed my info to Metric units of kg for weight, it gave a believable number for calories for my workouts.
So the software is Version 1.1 as I write this, so I hope they will take input to make the software better since they have a nice quality watch that could be a real winner with better software.

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a screen size comparable to the larger version of an Apple Watch.

Review:
A couple of months ago my coworker was trying to talk my into getting a FitBit.i asked what it did. He said "I can check my heart beat, blood pressure, how much I've walked in a day. My calories burnt. It even checks my q sleep cycles!"
I replied, "It kinda looks like a thick watch. Does it tell time?"
He said "No, I have to look on my phone to 1 see all this stuff."
I didn't want one, I knew I walked about 7 miles a day at work, I saw this, it's cool 1.i ordered it, it connects up my phone so the time is always spot on. With the manual watches, they would lose about 30 seconds a day. But with this I can set a goal for my steps, it tells me when I've reached out. I can set alarms on my phone, which helps with my breaks at work. I can see my progress immediately, without looking at my phone. When it says in the description, "It lasts 10 days!" Maybe if you turn on everything, but for whey I need, it uses maybe 2% of battery a day. I'm at work most days, so I can select Do Not Disturb, I can set the hours on the silent mode. This is awesome. The sleeping thing makes sense, when I wake up tired, I look at my watch and I can see why. "You slept 8.5 hours!" But they 8.5 would break down to something like 5.5 hours light sleep and 3 hours deep sleep. Because I've noticed that, I've been able to make slight changes to my sleep that help me wake up feeling good. I didn't have to look on my phone to see, IT'S ON THE WATCH! It's cool. I can see immediately how the mask effects my heart,I went to the gym once in 2020, it was the year of the pandemic where the me me wear a mask. I felt like I was about to die. People said it was in my head and I was crazy, "MASKS DON'T EFFECT YOU LIKE THAT MICHAEL!" More I can prove they do. My heart beat when I'm just chilling was about 46 bpm. At work, in a mask, it was between 85 and 122 bpm, averaging about 108bpm. This could be whatever. But I have the timeline! When I'm at work it's freaky high, then when I take off the mask, my bpm just drops!

I love it! I never thought I'd be that interested, but this watch was really cheap, it was an impulse buy. Just hoping for a good watch, it's so much more!

Review:
It's pretty amazing that you can get a smart watch for just $36. My husband has an Apple watch and has encouraged me to get one to also be in tune with exercise tracking. When he mentioned it I didn't know if I wanted to take the plunge in the hefty expense of a designer watch. I had given up on wearing watches ages ago when smart phones became like another mandatory limb we carry. But a smart watch is great way to keep track of your more exercise oriented activities. On the Hafury watch I've discovered that I like being able to check my heart rate, log in my steps and miles for the day and control my phones music from my wrist without having to take the phone out.

The accuracy of the this watch technology is difficult to gauge. I try to view it with self comparison and not just checking to see if I get 10,000 steps in for the day (although it does send you a vibration signal once you have completed the 10,000 for the day). So long as I'm consistently taking in a reasonable amount of steps/miles each week I'm feeling pretty good about my exercise level.

The Hafury smart watch takes your heart rate using a completely different system than the Apple watch. Hafury uses PPG where as Apple uses ECG. The PPG measures senses the volume of blood pumping through light. It seems to be fairly accurate. The ECG method is supposed to be much more accurate because it measures electric signals sent by the nervous system. Still the PPG is nothing to sneeze at, it is helpful and at this price point it's a great tool.

I took a short walk with my husband and we started our watches at the same time to see how they compared distance. His clocked in at 1.43 miles and mine at 1.26. Definitely a difference but still fairly comparable.

The watch design is simple, classic and nondiscript. I happen to have very narrow wrists so the watch is a little large and clunky on me. People with more standard sized wrists shouldn't have any problem at all. It would be nice to have one made for women that was designed with a smaller more feminine look but for again for $36 this is still a good simple design.

The app that corresponds with the watch is VeryFitPro, you will need to install it on your phone.This enables you to look over the specifics of your logged in activities. I find it to be a nicely designed and fairly easy to navigate app.

All and all I am enjoying using the watch and tracking my walks, it's absolutely worth the price and I don't plan on upgrading to the very expensive Apple because I'm having plenty of fun right now with the Hafury watch.

Review:
For a budget-friendly fitness tracker, this watch packs a punch. The feel of the band is premium (silky rubberized coating, not cheap-feeling) and the watch itself has a sharp (and bright) display.

This would make a good gift for somebody that does not have a smart watch/fitness tracker.

Packaging:
The packaging is a small, attractive box with minimal "fluff". The tracker is neatly nested in a block of foam with a sample display sticker covering the screen. A small box is inset beside the tracker which contains the magnetic USB charging cable. A manual and discount offer card lay under the tracker and charging cable inside the box.

Review:
Edit 11/7/2019: My watch recently would not turn on, but after contacting customer support, they were able to diagnose the problem as a faulty charger and promptly ship a new one. I am still very happy with my purchase

I have used this watch for a little while now and I absolutely love it. This is my first smartwatch, so I do not have a base of comparison, but it does everything i want it to. It feels good on the wrist, has good battery life, and feels snappy to use.

Look and Feel:
Look is rather subjective, thus these are my personal opinions. The watch has a bit of heft to it, but personally, i feel that it contributes to the overall great feel of the watch. I like the wristband, as it is comfortable to wear for extended periods. It is a large watch, and has a more sporty look, which i personally like. The buttons on the side feel sturdy. The entire watch feels well built. The quality of the charger is nothing to write home about. It is adequate for its function.

Performance:
The screen is bright enough to see in daylight, and seems vibrant enough. The watch feels snappy. Navigating menus, as far as i can tell, has no discernible latency. Notifications seem to hit my phone and my watch at the same time. I have not noticed any stuttering or freezing.

Features:
As for the features, I have not used NFC for payments, so i cannot comment on that, but the heart-rate monitor seems accurate. I do not have a way to test the step counter's accuracy, but the watch seems to give accurate numbers. The dual layer display is really nice. Even though the main display is bright enough to see in daylight, the layered display is much more readable. It delivers the time and date, as well as step count and battery life when the watch is booted up into WearOS. When the watch is put into essential mode, you can also read your heart rate by using one of the side buttons. That brings me to essential mode. Personally, i don't really use it. The only time i see it being rather useful is if your battery is just about dead and you want the watch to get you through the rest of your day. You give up all the smart features (with the exception of being able to read your heart rate) for much better battery life. Personally, I don't see essential mode as a deciding factor in my purchase, but more as a slight added bonus. The watch does have a mic and speaker. I have taken a few short phone calls on it, and it works fine. I wouldn't recommend an hour long call on it though. Audio quality is adequate for receiving calls and voice quality seems alright.

Battery:
Battery life seems to be a big selling point for this watch. I usually charge mine every night, but the watch still has over 60% battery after a moderate-use day. I don't think I could get 3 days out of a single charge, but your mileage may vary. Essential mode battery life is supposed to give you 30 days on a charge. I personally cannot speak for the accuracy of this, as i haven't used essential mode for a long enough period.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
I purchased the Garmin 610 at the Boston Marathon expo. I had the Garmin Forerunner 305 for 3 years previously and the watch worked great and I had little problems. The battery life was starting to die and I had it turn off during a wet 25k race, so since I truly forgot my Garmin 305 home for the race, I bought the 610 at the expo.

I wore it for the marathon without really testing it out and it worked fine (or so I thought).

However, when I started to run at home, I started to notice the following bug and annoyances:

1. When ever you press start at your runs, the GPS accuracy would go from < 15ft to > 150ft. In some runs I would be running for 30+ seconds before it every registered pace or distance. So my first mile would be off by 15-20 seconds.
2. It consistently measured < 1%+ on all the same routes I ran on for three years with the 305. Yes, people will say that that is within the GPS tolerance of error and maybe the 610 is more accurate, but this lead to really closely analyze the GPX data (the actual path data the unit records) and...
3. The path is all over the place at the start. This is the main cause of the incorrect pace at the start.
4. In a recent 12.5 mile run I ran with both my 305 and 610 on the same wrist. The 305 measured 12.52 while the 610 measured 12.40. The 305 agreed with what it had always said over 3 years and what a hand-measured distance on [...] showed.
5. About 10+ times in the GPX path data you can see the 610 either loops back on itself or totally loses track, while the 305 path is still smooth.
6. When measuring the actually distance of the GPX track (point to point and not what the watch shows) the 305 was at 12.57 and the 610 was at 12.69. So clearly shows the extra 0.29 in these GPS point glitches
7. On numerous occasions, especially after auto-lap it shows that I am running 1-2 min/mile pace. I can assure you I am no Usain Bolt.
8. The heart rate strap is rather comfortable, but it seems to eat into my skin on long runs. The 305 strap never did this.
9. On Auto Scroll the clock is one of the screen which is rather useless when running.

The watch to me seems like its all whizz-bang and has no meat. The watch itself is great. I wear it everyday. I have had zero problems with the touch screen, but I didn't spend $399 on a watch that had cool features. I mainly bought it because I "thought" it would have at least the same accuracy level as the 305 which you can buy on Amazon for $120. But it doesn't and it doesn't come close. Others have seen the problems I had detailed above. They are over on [...]. Maybe I got a lemon because it was the first batch of watches.

Also Garmin Support was horrible on this issue. Here is the reply I got from them. I don't think they even looked at the data. I think they assumed I didn't really know anything about this stuff.

Dear XXXX XXXXX,

Thank you for contacting Garmin International.

I was concerned about possible GPS Drift. The time stamp appears to show the 305 started at .01 seconds ahead of the 610.

After collaboration with the engineers and sending your data to them the difference of .12 is within the acceptable ranges for GPS watches which is 1%. Also you may have had consistent readings with your 305 but they may have been consistently wrong and now you have a watch with correct readings the natural assumption would be that the 610 is wrong when in fact the issue may be with the 305.

Updated June 2nd, 2011. Well I'm updating this review because I am still leaving it at 1-star and will most likely leave it here, because of the customer service run-around I've had. Garmin told me they would send me a new watch ASAP on May 20th, when I sent my watch back to them. So on May 23rd, I UPS'd the watch back to Garmin in Kansas, which they received on Thursday, May 26th. My RMA status as of this morning still stated (one week later) that they are still awaiting receipt of my package, even though it was sitting right in their warehouse for a week.

I emailed them back, which seem to get the ball rolling, but was then told that my new watch will not be shipped out until June 22nd. So, I will be without the watch for over one month. Bad customer service.

Update #2 for June 2nd, 2011. Got a personal phone call from Garmin stating that they found a Garmin 610 for me and it will be sent to me either today or tomorrow. Hopefully I'll get it next week. Why does it take the "angry email" guy to get the ball moving?

Update #3 for June 9th, 2011: I have received my new watch and eventually the new HR strap that comes with it. I have run 6 consecutive runs with it, 3 with the 305. I am upping my review a whopping one star to two stars. Why not higher you may ask? Because I don't think the user should have to constantly click/tap on the watch to clear the screen to get the GPS to work. All other features of the watch work well, the GPS is still quirky. It still measures shorter than my 305 but not by much anymore if you tap the screen.

And to all the doubters, [...]

Update #4 for June 18th, 2011: Updating this review back down to 1 star. Had a couple of consecutive frustrating runs where even tapping on the watch to clear all those screens doesn't even help with GPS accuracy. Today the 610 had an awful time. Took at least 100+ meters after I hit start to actually register any distance. A 12.50-12.52 run came in at 12.38. (Insert: you know that's within GPS error tolerance, stop complaining comments, blah blah).

Final Update #5 June 30th, 2011: Okay, I had some really horrible runs with the 610 about 1.5 weeks ago. Some where the % error was almost 3% off on distance. This was with Firmware v2.20. I requested a return from Garmin and was granted that request after I sent in proof of purchase.

The next day Firmware v2.30 is released which supposedly fixes the GPS bugs. So I delayed returning my watch and uploaded v2.30. I ran with v2.30 from Sunday-Thursday. I believe it fixes most of the GPS bugs, like the start bug/lap bug. Also the lap pace (which I use) seemed to be smoother. However, I did notice about 1% discrepancy between runs on the same course. (I ran the same course Tues-Thurs). The distances were 9.62, 9.70, 9.62. I never saw that much discrepancy in my 305. Also, I ran a USATF 5k on Monday evening and the distance measured came up short. It was slightly short, but I have never has a race come up short, since it is almost impossible to run the exact tangents a course is measured upon.

Other bugs were also introduced with v2.30 that really didn't effect my usage of it. A great place to see what problems people are having is over in the Garmin forums ([...]).

I have increased my rating to 3 stars. It's a nice watch, form factor is good, the vibrating alert is good. I can still hear it beep (though who knows when that will break). However, at $400 (vs. $128 for 305) and with the annoying distance discrepancies, I decided to return the watch and stick with my trusty 3+ year old 305. And when that goes, I'll just get another 305 on the cheap...

Review:
I've owned the Vicoactive for a few months now, and overall it's a great smartwatch and gps tracker.

Cycling: I've used it during long (50 miles+) bike rides, and it matches distance and speed with my bike-mounted detector almost perfectly the entire time. It's much more accurate than apps like Strava or Map my Ride, and as a bonus, it syncs to those apps if you like to use them. Because my ride involves some stop signs/busy intersections I enabled the auto-pause feature, which is a great option since it means the watch stops tracking when you stop moving, which keeps your average speed from plummeting if you get stuck at a long stop light.

Running: The watch performs as expected. It captures distance as speed pretty accurately.

Treadmill: The watch is surprisingly accurate on the treadmill. Over 2 miles it's only off by around .05 miles. It seems to slightly underestimate my running distance, but slightly over estimate my walking distance, so over a few miles it evens out.

Heart-rate monitor: The soft strap heart rate monitor that you can add as an option is surprisingly comfortable. It's really useful if you want to track how hard you're working out.

Other features: Survives the shower with no issue, is overall comfortable. I also like the interchangeable straps, and the different watch faces, since it lets you make the watch a bit more feminine. The white color isn't as nice as I would have hoped--I'd recommend the black option. The white looks a little like a kids watch. Overall pretty durable. I've knocked my wrist and arm on things and there's been no serious damage. Step counter seems accurate too. The alarm clock is great--it allows you to set multiple alarm clocks for different days, so you don't need to keep resetting the alarm. The vibration is enough to wake me up without annoying me.

Critiques:
1. You can't see the time when the watch is in GPS tracking mode. This is a little ridiculous. It provides you with numerous data screens, and yet the time of day isn't on one of them! If this wasn't an issue, I'd give it 5 stars.

2. A little clunky for reading emails/texts. It works, but it's not easy.

3. Not alot of back-light. In daytime conditions its fine, but if you're running or walking in the evening or in low light conditions, its hard to see the watch face without pressing the light button.

4. Scratches easy. You need to get a watch face cover. Within a month I had tiny scratches on the watch face, so I got an armor suite cover for it before I got any truly noticeable scratches. No scratches are noticeable unless you really look hard at the right angle, but it's covered now so I'm happy.

Review:
My wife and daughter ended up squabbling over this watch.

The variant they selected was the purple and white additional band model. First off, the metal band that comes fitted to it is far too long and tends to bunch up on your average woman's wrist. As a man with larger wrists, I still found the band long. While my daughter liked the look, it was simply uncomfortable. She then fitted the purple band and dowloaded the control app.

Here is her review:
"It's a pretty watch. Even though I have scrawny wrists, it fits well and doesn't look too bulky. It's lightweight and doesn't hinder my ability to move my wrists or write. I like the interchangeable watch face designs, and the duck face that came preloaded onto mine is absolutely adorable. It tracks BP and HR well, though it was still tracking while attached to the battery charger. The app is easy to navigate and taking a manual measurement (in addition to the every 5 minutes measurements) is simple, just a click of a button. The watch features itself are easy to navigate and you can add/remove features you don't want to see (I removed the weather and media 'apps'). Battery life is at least five days and i'd suspect I could get a week out of it if I tried."

It isn't an Apple watch nor is it a Withings Scanwatch. But at $60, it's an outstanding value in a basic heart rate monitoring and pulseox reading watch.

Review:
I got the Garmin forerunner 245 without music only because I always keep my phone on me and I don’t really need my music on my watch plus you can still use music controls on the watch when you aren’t using the watch for something else.

This will be my first Garmin watch I switched from a Fitbit blaze to this watch in hope of an upgrade and to try something different. I had the Fitbit blaze for about 5 years now and it did everything I needed except training status and load.

The type of sports I do is mostly weight lifting and jogging.

Pros:
1. What I like about the watch is the feel, it is very lightweight and fits on my wrist (i have very small wrists). My Fitbit would always slide off my wrist and there was no adjustment that fit my wrist perfectly on the band.

2. Always on display without compromising battery life.

3. The battery life is about the same as the Fitbit (5 days). One of the reasons why I chose this watch instead of an Apple Watch is because of battery life. The battery life could be a little better as I have seen the other garmin watches lasting up to 14 days on their site.

4. I really like how you are able to customize the watch and put things in the order you like or choose different watch faces.

5. I like the features that are available as mentioned above one of the main reasons I wanted this watch was because of the training status, load, and body battery it will give you. I wouldn’t say it is accurate just like the heart rate they all vary but it is interesting to see the results and compare to how you’re actually feeling.

Cons:
1. The step tracker is a bit slow to sync on the watch.

2. The sleep tracker isn’t as good as the Fitbit as it doesn’t keep track of your naps if they’re an hr or less.

3. It’s a little bit annoying to have to press the light button to view the screen when indoors because the watches screen is so dim. But, it’s not a huge deal and probably helps the battery life.

4. There’s a little bit of a learning curve as you have to learn which buttons do what and how to customize your watch.

5. There are no preloaded stretches on there like my Fitbit had exercises, stretches, and warm ups. It does allow you to create your own work outs and has a coach and training plans.

Overall, I give this watch 5 stars because it has everything I need and more. It has a stop watch, timer, tracking for work outs, GPS, training load, body battery, heart rate, pulse oximetry, sleep tracker/step tracker, able to receive texts messages and phone calls.

I chose this watch over the Apple Watch and Polar Vantage M. The Apple Watch doesn’t have a good battery life and the Vantage M lacked a timer/stop watch separate from the work out tracker and the Vantage M looked a bit bulkier and was a little bit heavier.

Review: Way better than expected!!  Right out of the box I was impressed; very low profile and light, not clunky like some fitness watches (I'm updating from the Garmin 310xt).  Setup was a little convoluted requiring use of the watch, phone app and website to accomplish everything, but overall pretty straightforward once you get it rolling.  So far I have only used the running app, but the GPS locked on very quickly and it was very intuitive to setup, use, and log a workout.  The touch screen is great during workouts to scroll through data pages and makes up for only having three lines of data per page (customizing the data fields is a snap too).  The bluetooth pairing with my iPhone was seamless, and though it does seem to drop every once in a while, it doesn't appear to have any significant impact to function or battery life (which is AWESOME by the way!).  The Garmin Connect app and website could use some updating and more user-friendliness (I was a TrainingPeaks user for quite some time), it serves the purpose for logging and analyzing basic workout and activity data.  If the swim and golf functions work as advertised (which I presume they will), this is hands down the best sport watch/activity tracker out there!

Review:
I've had a number of smart watches and this has been by far my fav. I'm upgrading from Fossil Sport and this is a huge upgrade! I always heard the 1gb watches make a big difference but this is the first time experiencing one. This does have a newer processor too but the combo makes a massive difference in speed while using it. I've yet to see any lag.

Battery is also pretty awesome too, WAY better than I expected. I've been getting close 2.5 days out of it but I would say I'm a light user. I also have been wearing it to sleep too which makes me more surprised by the battery life.

Tic seems to have their own OS skin of some sort along with their own health apps which seem solid so far. The OS changes are minor but very welcomed. I do like a lot of the stock watch faces. The sleep app seems to be pretty nice which has always been an issue on smart watches I've used minus my Pebble. I haven't used the speaker yet but it is neat that it has it.

It is a pretty comfy watch and doesn't feel large and I would say I have smaller wrists. I'll probably switch to a metal band sometime soon.

I'd say the only flaws are related to WearOS and notifications on the watch getting confused with the phone. It hasn't happened a lot so might be a fluke or just certain apps. Highly recommend and for the price, it is a no brainer if you want a smart watch.

Review:
As a former Apple Watch user, I was tired of constantly running out of battery and always worrying about how fragile they are. I oversee professional trail crews and have lead crews for 4 years now. When I bought this watch last year, I was looking for a watch that would track my steps, heart rate, track my various activities (hiking, climbing, trail running, skiing, weight lifting, etc) without dying within 8 hours. It has far more options for activities than my old Apple Watch.

This watch does all of that and more. The battery life is a significant pro of this watch. I charge it every week and a half, and have had it last up to 2.5 weeks on a single charge! I wear it while working, and it's gotten wedged between rocks during masonry construction, covered in mud, sprayed with saw dust. You name it, this watch has been through it. It's rugged and reliable.

I love the body battery aspect of this watch. It's a game changer with my super active lifestyle, and the reminders to take a rest day are super helpful when I'm constantly on the go. I used this watch on a 213 mile thru hike of the John Muir Trail in California. While I didn't track the entire journey to save battery, it lasted the whole time!

Another amazing feature is how your step goal adjusts to your baseline activity. When I'm sick and in bed, it picks up on my change in activity and automatically adjusts, making me feel a LOT less guilty. When I'm hiking 5-15 miles a day for work or enjoyment, my step goal finds a reasonable average to set as my goal.

My only complaint is that the sleep tracker isn't super accurate. I'll have nights where I’m tossing and turning, unable to fall asleep, and my watch will tell me I slept for 9 hours when in reality I probably slept for 6. When I'm in bed scrolling on my phone, it also thinks I'm asleep. Maybe one day I'll upgrade to a newer model, but for right now this watch matches all of my needs.

Highly recommended for folks who are active, rugged, and often away from electricity for long periods of time!

• Notifications for text messages, Incoming caller ID and etc.

Cons:

I really do like this watch, so far it is working great. At the time of this review the watch is only costs around $40 if apply a 30% saving coupon and that makes a great deal.

Review:
I can't say enough good things about this smartwatch. I wanted something feminine that didn't look like a square computer sitting on my wrist, and this has turned out to be perfect for my needs. I should mention that I'm an Android phone user, so I can take full advantage of all the features (without the limitations some iPhone users experience). My top priority was to have a watch that first and foremost displays the time all the time, which the Pebble Time's color e-ink display makes possible. I also wanted step counting, appointment notifications, text message notifications and ability to respond without grabbing my phone, and incoming call notifications. This watch does all of those functions beautifully. It did take me a bit of trial and error to come up with the best 3 or 4 watchfaces to maximize visibility in low light situations, but with so many options to choose from, and the ability to customize many of those, I have ended up very happy with this purchase. I have a narrow wrist and, at the present time, this is really the only option that isn't too bulky to fit me properly. While this Pebble isn't waterproof, it can resist splashes, and the two day rated battery life is more than enough to meet my needs. Highly recommended!!

Update: Still loving this watch! I went ahead and bought Pebble's stainless steel mesh band for it, which really looks great. I agree with other reviewers about the lack of durability about the leather band the watch comes with. Very comfortable (super soft leather) but gets dirty looking quickly. What is great about both the leather and the stainless steel band is that they are sized for smaller wrists. Because the watchface is larger than a typical watch, having the watchband sized accordingly is important so that it fits smaller wrists.

About battery life, I can easily go two days between charges. For people having issues with this, try choosing a watchface that doesn't have a moving second hand. Anything moving causes a reduction in battery life, so you want to minimize that as well as the use of the backlight.

Also, I made the mistake of purchasing a third party vendor charger to have a spare charger for travel. It looked fine but didn't work at all. I went ahead and bought one from Pebble and it works perfectly. So, just be careful if opting to save some money going with someone other than Pebble.

Still five stars for me!

Review: I bought this watch for its purple band because it's my favorite color and the gold watch which gave it a classy look. The battery life is great. I've only had to charge it twice since receiving it 2 weeks ago. I keep the dim setting at 2 which is bright enough for me to read the watch without a problem. I have wrist awake turned off since I find it annoying. The watch has almost all of the features I was looking for. It was easy to setup and the fitdock mobile app is one of the best I've used. I had no problem pairing the watch to my phone. The app is simple to use but having more watch faces to chose from would be nice. I love being able to answer calls on the watch since my job does not always allow  me to have my phone close by. I have had no issues with the accuracy of the blood oxygen and heart rate readings. So far the sleep monitoring has been pretty accurate and the data is easy to read. My biggest complaint about the watch is the band. It's not attached to the watch but instead snaps on. There is only one slider piece to hold the band in place which I find annoying since the band keeps popping out and flopping around. I like it when a band is securely held in place. Also the band does not seem to very durable. After just 2 weeks it's already showing signs of wear and tear. Other than the issues with the band this watch is a great value for the price. If your looking for a great smart watch for an affordable price I recommend this watch.

Review:
I understand I may not be the target audience for this watch. I'm an IT contractor I work in wiring walls/construction, building server racks and roof installations. I hike a couple times a month. I ended up buying the Samsung classic 4 instead. It is just a better overall timepiece for me and accurate enough for gps apps.

The construction is overall rubber/plastics. For $200... thats a bit steep. Of course if you build anything out of this stuff it's going to be super "rugged". But with this may come with it feeling like more of a toy or single operation tool than a good overall timepiece that you still have to charge up. I bought the tundra version as well and the inking on the lettering was misaligned looking like a chinese knockoff. Hopefully that was just me though. If you really appreciate and need only the GPS features I can at least see the use there. But I think getting a better Garmin watch would also just be better overall for my experience too.

The classic 4 works for me and I get some great android watch apps and with offline maps/gps info. But it requires more frequent daily charging for the added features. But honestly with a qi charging battery backup that I'd probably carry anyways on a trip it's not really a big problem with that in mind. Also I do not know how accurate the gps is in comparison but it's good enough for me.

So this is great watch for at a glance information it provides. But for the cost maybe consider other models.

I have downgraded my review after upgrading it (see my original and then previously edited reviews below). The reason is that Garmin has a known issue with data not syncing. I have spent a couple hours trying to sync one stupid 3 mile run. I gave up and manually entered the data. A couple hours of my life that I will never get back.

I have upgraded my review again after about two weeks. Below is my original (edited) review. Garmin Express is the key to downloading the data from the watch to your computer via the ANT (antenna). I have added a foot pod and now I am getting all the data I need to train to qualify for Boston. The footpod is helping me with cadence and for when satellites are obscured. The four metric display is also very helpful for feedback. Since pacing is so important, this watch helps me hold back early on. I still wish it interacted better with Strava.

I recently purchased this. I have used lower level Garmin Forerunners, all of which hard-line update Garmin Connect and Strava, very easily. On the positive side, you can get up to four types of data, other gps watches that I used lacked in versatility in that regard. I now can see heart rate, pace, distance, and lap (mile) distance at one time. What you need to know about this GPS, is that it communicates via a USB antenna. The hard line is for charging only (speaking of which the battery in this is outstanding). But how exactly you update Garmin Connect and Strava is not yet clear to me. So far I have rebooted, installed and uninstalled Garmin Communicator and Garmin Express. My first workout got uploaded and I do not know how. Then I had to save that file and upload it to strava manually. I am now trying to save my second workout to Garmin Connect and Strava so I will again spend more time than I should trying to figure this out.

Review:
Few things for the browsers who don't want to actually read.

1. No touch screen was a hesitation for me, but I hardly noticed after a few days
2. Screen is not the full size of the bezel, it actually has a fairly large "dead zone" around the display
3. Sleep and recovery take a month to get adjusted then work awesome
4. Work out and run tracking is FAR superior to Apple watch and the Garmin Connect is pro level insight into your activity
5. Battery life is amazing!!!
6. May be large for small wrists

I replaced my apple watch 6 with the Forerunner 945 after about a month of debate. My main reasons for doing so were battery life and performance tracking as I trained for some running races this year including a half and full marathon. My apple watch was lacking in a battery life that worked with my lifestyle. A day and a half -2 days was restricting me from getting the measurements I wanted especially while I slept. I have mild sleep apnea and have always wanted to see the results of how it effects my sleep at night. My Garmin gives me that in abundance. I have only charged the watch as I shower, and have never seen the battery below 60% even after tracking GPS for over 3 hours on a long marathon training run that started with battery at 75%. Garmin claims up to 2 weeks, but I think 7-10 days is more realistic if you actually use the watch. I'm sure with many power saving settings you can get more, but I don't need that much time especially with the performance I have seen thus far.

This is a true athletic device, not just a smartwatch. In fact its less of a smartwatch than it is a fitness device. Everything is centric around the users health and measurable data that it collects. It just also happens to display text, phone calls, and control music.

There is 5 buttons that control the watch and all its functions. It takes a few days to get used to, but after that I found myself navigating the menus without thought to which button did what. The display is clear and defined but by no means the HD res you get on an apple watch. My understanding is the touch screen HD display on the apple devices are the main culprit to the lack of battery life on them.

Overall I have used my Garmin for over a month and will likely never put my Apple Watch back on. I do occasionally miss the voice control of the Apple watch and the ease of turning down my Airpods by rolling the control on the watch, but the health and training data FAR outweigh these things in my mind.

I would definitely recommend this to a serious health nut/runner/hiker. They will geek out like crazy over the information. However for a casual user, or someone who doesn't need the overnight data, the Apple watch is more user friendly with its touch screen and has a better "fashion sense" with its interchangeable bands and such.

Review:
Will be reviewing based on the Price Point and the fact that it should do what you expect from a watch (tell time).

Likes:
+ The appearance. It looks very nice (especially for its price point).
+ The software "Da Fit" is very easy to use. Much simpler than the Garmin software.
+ User Interface - Also a lot easier to use than the Garmin Vivoactive 3.
+ Speed - Faster than the Garmin Vivoactive 3.
+ Battery life - Seems to last a while. Many days so far. I think they did a good job at using the sensor on the watch to turn the screen on/off to save battery when not using it.
+ SPO2 measurement. Nice feature. It's not constant but you can click measure to get an instant reading. Didn't expect it and it's not advertised on their Amazon page.
+ Blood Pressure measurement. Nice feature. It's not constant but you can click measure to get an instant reading.

Dislikes:
- Screen not turning on sometimes. This is the main point of having a watch (to be able to tell time). I think this has to do with Sensor Sensitivity. There's usually a 0.5-1.0 second delay to seeing the screen; however, sometimes it just doesn't turn on. I wish you could adjust the sensitivity for this; maybe let the customer adjust this while letting them know that it will affect battery life.
- The watch band isn't as comfortable as the Garmin Vivoactive 3. I think the holes on the band should be spaced slightly closer together. Its either too loose or too tight for me.

Neutral or To Be Determined:
- How long will this watch last?
- How long until the battery needs to be replaced? (can it be replaced?)
- Accuracy of steps. I haven't done a comparison to the Garmin Vivoactive GPS.

Review:
Sadly, pebble is going down the drain, they've been bought out by another company.

This is a great time to buy a heavily discounted watch, full price, not so much.

This can be looked at as a 'no frills' watch but it really isn't... the monochrome display isn't as entertaining as a full color touch screen so if you're looking for entertainment look elsewhere. Displays incoming phone number and reminders on the watch, has a heart monitor and displays steps (though I haven't calibrated steps yet) and does the rest of the things that make up a good smart watch.

Has a good iOS app and I believe it can link Apple's "Health" system but I don't feel the need for that so I haven't experimented.

For the price it's a great deal.

Beware: Pebble seems to have changed the model names on these watches:

Amazon sells a "pebble 2" that's actually a "pebble 2 SE" as it's called on the pebble web site, the difference being that the SE doesn't have the heart monitor. I'd you want the heart monitor from Amazon buy the "pebble 2 + heart rate"' which is the same name that the pebble site gives it.

Also the pebble web site has a spec sheet page that shows the "pebble 2" as having the heart monitor... I got bitten by that one and had to send mine back and bought the "pebble 2 + heart monitor" that I wanted in the first place. Not a big deal if you pay attention.

Review:
After more than 10 days of use, I now refer to my Garmin 945 as the Garmin “Coach” because it is the closest thing to an actual coach. Although I've purchased many products on Amazon over the years, this is the first time I've felt compelled enough to write a review. In short, this thing is absolutely phenomenal. However, it's not for everyone. I'll explain why later in this review, but first, let's get to the good stuff. Throughout this review I’ll mostly refer to the 945 as the “coach.” Just thought I should mention that so people aren’t confused.

My first Garmin device was the Vivoactive HR. Then I upgraded to the Vivoactive 3. And now the FR945, which is a big jump. The reason I made the leap (after weeks of extensive research) is because I need more granular metrics for my training. With the VA3 I used the stress metric to decide when and how hard to train, which was ok for a while but now I'm training for a major event in combat sports and I need a coach. In the last 10 plus days, the FR945 has been eerily accurate in predicting my performance and recovery requirements. I've also learned that my training is unbalanced and may very well account for my high incidents of sports injuries.

For example, the body battery and recovery. I woke up one morning with a pretty low body battery after a brutal workout the night before. I also had a terrible late-night meal before going to bed. The "coach" said I had another 18 hours of recovery remaining and suggested an easy or recovery workout. I still felt the previous night's workout, but I was at least good enough for morning yoga, right? Well, halfway through what is typically a moderate yoga class, my body felt miserable. I struggled throughout the class and couldn't believe how difficult everything felt. This is the same class I attend twice a week, every week. And my day didn't get better. I felt like crap the rest of the day. What I really needed was plenty of rest and a good night of sleep. This is a case where I was in one place psychologically and a totally different place physically. This is where injury happens, at least in my case. The "coach" tried to advise me, but I ignored it.

Another example, the performance condition. After about 90 minutes of late afternoon drilling, I decided to go for a run. I was pretty pumped and felt great. Yet, the "coach" said my drilling workout was a lot tougher than what I had planned. The training effect was in the "VO2 Max" category with an aerobic effect of 4.1 and anaerobic effect of 3.5. Translation? It was time to chill. Instead, I decided to go on a short 2.5 mile run and to beat my previous time. Then, about 20 minutes into my run this thing pops up on my watch screen called "Performance Condition" with a value of -3. I had no idea what this was and decided to look it up later. Well, I completed my 2.5 miles and beat my previous time by 2 full minutes. But to my surprise, the "coach" suggested a 4-day recovery window, which sounded absolutely ridiculous until I woke up the next morning. Everything hurt and I was exhausted. And I looked up the "performance condition" later that day and smacked myself in the forehead. The "coach" was trying to tell me to stop and get some rest.

So, the "coach" has shown me that I tend to do too many hard workouts without enough recovery time in between. Sometimes the mind wants to do what the body is not ready for, but how can you really know? It was mostly guesswork before I bought the FR945. And since I've been an athlete my entire life, pushing through pain has always been part of the lifestyle. Now I can finally focus on training smarter, not just harder. The only thing better would be an actual coach who reviews your stats in real time and plans your workouts accordingly. Such a coach costs far more than $600, so I consider the FR945 to be a damn good bargain.

Now I will very briefly go over some of the features of the watch

1. Battery life - With regular use including all-day activity tracking I get about 7 days. This is even with the sleep pulse ox on for a couple of nights just to try it out. I didn't do much with GPS during this time but used it quite a bit for music playback and several workouts.

2. Music - Next to the advanced metrics, this was a major motivator for the purchase, and it's fantastic. At first, I had lots of trouble getting my Jabra Elites to work consistently but I think I accidentally fixed it by solving a separate problem. The watch kept crashing whenever I launched the strength app. I saw in the Garmin forum that someone fixed this by deleting outdated .FIT files on the watch, so I decided to do a full factory reset and the strength app stopped crashing. I concluded the problem had to do with me copying data from my VA3 to the FR945. Once I reset and did not copy the data back, everything ran smoothly. Then I noticed the headphones consistently connected as well. However, they still won't auto connect. Once the headphones are on and I click “connect” from the watch, I have to give the power button on the headphones a single push. Then, they connect and stay connected. So, it's still not as seamless as other headphones but I'm not sure if that's a problem with Garmin, Jabra or a mix of the two.

There is no music "streaming" though. I use my Spotify account to download the playlists to the watch, which can take a while for a large playlist, but it's still an awesome feature. The first time I went into the gym without my phone, I was like a kid with a new toy. It was just freakin' awesome. Some people complain that the sound is not as good as coming from a phone, and this is true but only barely noticeable. The music playback still sounds pretty damn good.

3. Wrist HR - It's awesome for daily activity tracking and all-day HR, but it sucks for any type of gym or strength training workouts. This is true for all devices like this though. But if you're spending $600 on a watch like this, I suspect you're also wearing an external HR during workouts. If not, you should.

4. Sleep tracking - It's decent enough for tracking trends but sometimes needs adjusting. Although the FR945 does better than my VA3, I still need to adjust the sleep start from time once in a while. There are also times when it does not calculate my "awake" minutes correctly. For example, it might say I was "awake" during my sleep window for 10 minutes when I know for a fact it was more like 40 minutes. And the REM vs deep sleep I take with a grain of salt. Again, great for tracking trends but I'm not confident in the absolute numbers. So, this is still a good feature but far from perfect.

5. Buttons - It took me a day to get used to the buttons after coming from touch screen watches but now I could never go back to touch screens. I lost at least one workout on the VA3 due to inadvertent screen touches. There have also been complaints of the buttons losing their "springiness" and feeling mushy, but so far, I have not experienced this. Maybe it takes more time to manifest itself. However, I use the vibration feedback feature so that each button push gives off a vibration. I suspect this could be a good solution to those experiencing mushy buttons.

6. Sports (lots and lots and lots of them!) - There are tons of sports categories on this thing, most of which I will never use. The level of customizable data screens for each sports category is just mind-boggling. There's is something here for just about everyone. Honestly, this category alone requires a separate review. And configuring activities from the watch itself is very intuitive and user friendly.

7. Maps and navigation - I haven't used either of these beyond playing around with them at home but I have a couple of big hikes coming up. So, I'm really looking forward to trying these out and maybe I'll update this review.

8. Training metrics - This is another one that needs a separate review. The FR945 accurately tells me when to train, how hard or how little, and when to take it easy. It also tells me how long to rest. The training load and training focus features are amazing and will help keep my training more balanced. I haven't even gotten to the lactate threshold or FTP because I haven't been using the device long enough for that data to be available yet. And the body battery is so accurate it’s almost creepy. I’ve learned the hard way to listen to it.

9. Garmin pay – Set up is really easy but I have not actually used the feature in a store yet. I’ve never been super excited about contactless payments anyway.

10. Other stuff – Ok this includes heat acclimation, altitude, etc., etc. So, far I have 19% heat acclimation.

11. Look and feel – Despite being jam packed with incredible hardware and a slew of amazing features, the watch is so light weight that I barely notice it’s there. And on top of that it looks great too. Although I liked the VA3, I wanted a more rugged, sturdier looking watch. I can take the FR945 out for a 10-mile hike or just as easily wear it to a business meeting. It has both the sleekness of Bruce Wayne and the utility of Batman.

12. Smart watch features – This is not a smart watch. It’s a sports watch with some smart watch features. In contrast, the Apple watch is a smart watch with some sports features. Personally, I’ve never been a fan of the smart watch stuff. So, I turned off all smart features except phone calls. While I find it convenient to reject calls without pulling out my phone, the text and other iPhone alerts are just annoying. It would be more useful if I could customize text alerts to receive them only from a select group such as my wife and kids.

So, who is the FR945 for? In short, the very serious athlete. If all you are interested in is daily activity tracking, sleep tracking and recording short runs or Zumba classes, get something in the sub $300 range. This watch is for the person who trains for rigorous events such as Spartan races, marathons, triathlons, ultras, serious cyclists, combat sports and so on. In other words, the person who, despite having a day job, thinks about their event for much of the day. They get up to train early in the morning 5 to 7 days a week. They train in the evening and put away ridiculous amounts to calories to keep up. Or if you just want to splurge on a really nice sports watch, go for it. It’s no one’s business. But make no mistake about it. This watch is for serious, data-hungry athletes.

This watch is now my daily driver, so I’ll be collecting lots of data and feedback over the next several weeks. If I experience anything significant whether positive or negative, I will update this review. In the meantime, however, I find it difficult to give this product anything less than 5 starts.

Review:
It took me a while to warm up to this product and I almost considered sending it back. All of these watches are still in the early adopter stages so prepare for that.

The watch itself is great and a fairly neat piece of hardware especially for the price point. The LCD display definitely works to conserve battery life if you turn off other wear os features like GPS, gestures, wifi, etc... Basically, this less tech you use, the longer the battery life. I get between 1.5 to 2 days normally and with everything off until I need it, about 3 days. Sometimes I travel where there is no cellular service at all. Turning off the smartwatch and getting a week or more when I'm in the woods is one of reasons I bought it. (hint to ticwatch: a backlight feature would have been a good idea when wear os is off)

Other things that are great about this; it came with a screen protector which was easy to install. I've had it for a month now and not one scratch on the face or the ring around it so it's well made. It is a little bulky for me but coworkers have commented they feel it looks like an expensive watch. That was with the LCD display. Then I tilted my arm and the screen lit up and that was a fun reaction.

Now to the challenges and the actual experience of Wear OS on the Ticwatch:

Bluetooth: The first big issue was bluetooth and it's not an issue with the watch but my phone. When around the house, the watch pairs to the phone and all is well. Went I get in my truck however, I want my phone to connect to the bluetooth there. The WearOS / Ticwatch keep being prioritized as the connection. I tried numerous software apps and adjusting services with no luck. Ultimately, I turned off the phone voice setting in the watch and that fixed it but cost me the ability to answer calls on my watch.

Navigation: I check traffic before I go home to determine a time and route. I had wanted some solution on my watch. Unfortunately, if I use maps or ask google assistant, it keeps wanting to open navigation on the phone. So that kind fails as well.

Google Assistant: Good but slow to start and often says "can't reach google". A constant connection does not appear to be maintained so it can take several tries before it will work. That said, when it does work. I can ask Google to turn my lights on and off or a question and that's helpful. Not sure this works without the phone audio so I need to test that. (see bluetooth issue above)

Weather: OK this one I solved. There is a nice weather app on the watch and it works but all apps have a minor 1 second lag and you have to navigate to them. To solve I used the Byss watchface that has the weather forecast on it. It's an hourly forecast and so far, pretty accurate. Have to admit I like that a lot. Sadly to get more is $28 a year for multiday forecasts.

Overall great watch but there isn't much on the Wear OS end of things other than watch faces.

Review:
Focusing on what I believe to be important - the experience of the device and working with the company. It is how I roll.

Pros:
Purchase, Shipping, Delivery - All Good
Initial Set-up - Straight Forward no hiccups
Watchface - bright, easy to see and work with (even sweaty)
First Set of outdoor exercises, Run, Bike
Heart Rate Tracking - good, first mile always low
GPS - Accurate and quick satellite pickup
Good bluetooth connectivity to Android, notifications, etc.,..

Meh:
On Device Menuing takes some getting used to
Devices switches to random screens too easily (bumps, sliding on wrist)
Random Reboots after changing the watch face
Random Reboots with loss of days data (only on watch)
Random updates, no asking (never during workouts)

Needs work: The Company
Company Communications with Customer - This is where I ding the device, but more the company pretty badly. I went to submit a ticket about the rebooting issue, Garmin wanted me to 'Chat' and notified me that email would take 3-5 business days to respond. Did the email thing, notified on Social media, slow to respond there as well. Finally had a case number (3 days later). Template email, with an empathetic signature line. Suggested that I return the watch for a new one - seemed like an ok idea (except I put a screen protector on).

Here is the kicker - only two options available for RMA. Either I let them charge my credit card and they send out a new watch OR I send mine in and when the verify receipt they will send one out. This is UNACCEPTABLE customer service. I am already a customer, I bought the watch, now they do not trust me. They can have my CC number and charge if things do not work out (like Warby Parker does), sure.

I am going to push ahead and see if the reboots keep happening (seem isolated to playing with new watch faces). I wrote a blog post on my experiences on Medium for those interested. No links here, should not be too hard to find.

Review:
I did it. Made the switch from Fitbit to Garmin. And I'm so happy that I did. I was looking for a new fitness tracker (my Fitbit Flex was dying) and came upon this gem. I knew Garmin was a well-known brand, and after reading multiple other good reviews, I decided to purchase, and am glad I did.
Pros: I enjoy the customization of the screen - that I can change what information shows and what doesn't. I know what my step goal is, so I don't need to see it every time. I think the movement notification is a good reminder to get up and move every hour. I need that since I work from home and tend to end up sitting most of the day. The sleep tracking seems to work really well, too.
Cons: The button is pretty hard to press down until you get used to it. When I first got it and was trying to "long press" the button, I kept missing the Sync and opening the Stop Watch and couldn't get back out of it. It was a mess. It also is a bit bulkier than I would prefer, but I've gotten used to it.

Either way, I would definitely consider this a good purchase and recommend to others.

Review:
For this price, the watch is very good at what it does. I’ve used mine every day for a month. My wife has an Apple Watch 3 so I have something to compare it to. My watch is paired to an iPhone SE with the latest version of iOS, Mobvoi and Wear OS (more on the software later).

The screen is bright enough to see in direct sunlight, but it’s not crisp. It is extremely responsive when it’s on (as good as the Apple Watch) but it is slow to respond to taps to wake it up and hardly ever works when I twist my wrist to wake it up.

The battery life is solid; with regular use (~50 notifications a day, media control use for about an hour and exercising for 40 minutes to an hour) the battery lasts about 1.5 days. I exercise regularly but I’m not obsessed with stats; the heart monitor is just ok when it comes to accuracy, as is the GPS. The activity reminders and step counting from Google Fit are enough for me.

The watch is definitely durable. I swear quite a bit when I work out and haven’t had any issues. I wear it when I give my kids baths, wash the car, and do the dishes; still no issues.

The software was a pain in the rearend. Save yourself days of frustration and just ignore the printed instructions. All you need to get all of the functionality is Wear OS. The Mobvoi app is useless and largely redundant. I like the Google fitness app; it’s good enough for my mild exercises. I am pleasantly surprised by the media control app. It makes it easy to control my music when I’m driving (still have to use a 3.5 mm jack in my car). I use the timer and alarm pretty frequently too. Review:
This product is phenomenal and literally a lifesaver. This is coming roughly after two months of continuous use.

I am saying this product is five stars from a holistic standpoint: use, community, app, etc. I can definitely see the value in this product which makes me happier even further when I got this at the lower rate. I used to have a simple smart bracelet and so I wasn't too foreign to fitness watches.

From the top: the installation and the app is great. The only difficulty I even had was battery optimization, but once I found how to turn off active gps, the battery life easily carries 3-5 days. The apps are pretty much seamless and easy to manage through your phone. Bear in mind, Garmin watches rely on Garmin connect and Connect IQ (the app store).

The main app is a lovely, optimizable platform. You can move, add, or delete the information you want and you can find the information relevant for you on the tabs. These tabs hold the detailed information for your activities and health data. Literally the only room for improvement would be to add sleep data information on to the watch, but that data is again easily accessible on the app.

The watch itself is smooth and durable, which is a definite plus. I often bang my wrist along surfaces and no true scratches are on the glass. Another successful durability test is the waterproof feature. Feel free to splash and bathe with the watch on and this watch can even handle light to medium light periods of submerging underwater (Remember to follow the rating system on the resistance). For reference, I went to the water park and swam for around ten minutes or so and my watch is still pristine.

Now for the community: this community will embrace/welcome you. I recommend joining any online groups for you to receive challenges and to network. I added a good amount of friends following my addition to a Facebook group. Furthermore, this community might even help you point out how to improve your fitness and form.

Now onto the miracle lifesaver: the actual gps tracking during activities. In the midst of my run/hike along the trail, my wallet fell from my armband and I didn't realize until I was a good distance away. However, I remembered I had my activity tracked on gps and I used the map/GPS location to retrace my steps and found my wallet. I would have literally lost my information, cards, etc. without the detailed, integrated, and understandable GPS information.

Honestly, if this watch is within your price range and you want to elevate yourself to a new level with a nice community, definitely look at Garmin and this watch. The only things I have done to my watch was get a longer/different watch band for better wrist placement. After that, I was great: I even happened to remove my protective cover from my watch and don't regret it.

*Quality of life widgets/apps I recommend getting on the watch is a battery management widget, calculator, and a weather widget*

Review:
I did a lot of research before I bought this watch. My needs were for an accurate HR monitor, fitness tracker that crossed over to the smart watch side without compensating. I have owned it for about 4 months and so far I have enjoyed it. Here are my pros and cons
-The battery life is pretty decent. I charge it about every 3 days. It charges pretty fast thankfully. I keep the brightness at 10 percent and I don’t have a super elaborate watch face on there which im sure helps.
-the watch band is comfortable but it over time irritates my skin and bothers me so I will go back and forth with taking breaks at nigh every few days or loosening the band while I am working and then retighten before a workout. However I am a hairstylist so my hands and wrists are constantly wet and I also have fibromyalgia so i know that probably is part of my issue. Still very comfortable to wear. I have very small wrists and the smaller band is actually a pretty great size for me. The loose end of the stays in place which is a huge bonus for me.
-The heart rate monitor seems to be fairly accurate when I measure it against the machine I’m on. This was a big thing for me because I do workouts that require me to maintain a certain heart rate for periods of time so I would say I am pleased. It is most accurate when running or biking of course. I use the rowing machine a lot and I just really tighten the watch on my wrist and it does pretty good. The only downside is when I do CrossFit or lifting the hR monitoring seems to be super off. I really want to get a compatible chest strap eventually to solve that problem
When I’m not working out I’m not sure how accurate it is all the time because I move a lot with my job. The rest rate i get seems accurate. When my wrist gets wet or I’m super sweaty it can become a bit inaccurate.
- CON. The only thing that really frustrates me sometimes is the touch screen is fairly sensitive and I find that i will accidentally be messing with things on the watch without realizing it. Or while I’m in the shower it does the same thing. The easy fix is locking the screen until I need to use it for something. The good thing is typically this happens mostly when I’m lounging around or showering so I don’t have to lock it too often.
-CON the garmin app for the actual watch widgets is not the easiest to navigate but I get by. I don’t need to use it that much once I got everything the way I like it. However PRO. The reading for the watch are really nice and informative.
-PRO That leads me to the readings...So as I mentioned, I do CrossFit so I do some lifting and I was pleased to learn when I start the strength app on the watch, it can automatically read what kind of movement I am doing most of the time, so one day I was doing deadlifts and went to the app on my phone to look at stats and it labeled my lift “deadlift” without me having to do anything. That wowed me. When lifting I can tap the screen to adjust the reps and weight and then tap to send it to my rest time between sets. The cardio part is good for my CrossFit style workout for the basic tracking. It allows you to make notes in the app which is nice. Really I am thankful for this part. For a watch made mostly for running and biking it is friendly for the in betweeners out there. I wish there was a jump roping app. That would be life changing but who knows maybe I havent. dig deep enough. Side note, I have started wearing wrist wraps for support more often which keeps me from being able to wear my watch so I strap it to my ankle and i will use it during my workouts as a timer.
-the step counter was one of the things I really cared about because some trackers are absolutely crap in this department. As a hairstylist I move my hands like crazy all day long. I have notice there is still a bit of false counting that I have to deal with. Mostly I notice it when I’m driving and I see my steps going up a bit. But I have worn it long enough that I know now how to gauge the number it tells me. Again it is most accurate when it is set to running.
-the gps was great for me. Hiked a mountain and it allowed me to go back and see a satellite view of my hike. I didn’t activate the tracking app the whole time because I didn’t want to have to charge it on my trip but I wish I would have.
-I use this watch as a timer a lot and the only bad thing is unless you go and actually reset the timer when it goes off it will start over and over and over. I can see that would be good for workouts but it gets annoying. When the timer goes off it pops up and you stop it by pressing side button or the screen a couple of times and it disappears so you have to go back to open the widget to reset it.
-sleep tracker is pretty decent. It seems to do well. Although sometimes I will adjust it because it will get your fall asleep time wrong occasionally.
-i do receive notifications. I wish there was a way modify which notifications I get so that I only see my texts and calls. But maybe that will come eventually in an update. That is a small pet peeve for me. I don’t like feeling like I am having to constantly look at my watch.
-it is durable. I am super clumsy and I am always banging it on things or and it is still relatively blemish free. Not too big of a face for a small wrist. Not too sporty either.
Overall I think this watch was definitely worth the price. It works well for me. It’s mostly well rounded which was important. I don’t think I will be switching to anything else until it dies.

Review:
I bought the Pebble Time Round refurbished, and at the same time a new Pebble Time, to compare. They are both very cool and do what I bought them for, tracking a run and answering texts via voice recognition (both also have canned responses and emoji responses). Both have EXCELLENT voice texting, better accuracy than my Samsung phone.
The Round is very small and slim, and I barely notice I'm wearing it. I used to wear a Timex Ironman Triathlon exclusively, and the Pebble Round is flatter/lower profile, which shocked me. The Time is bulky and gets caught up in my sleeves which can be annoying, the Round does not.
I feel that the battery life is similar between the two, charge lasts several days and only takes maybe an hour to charge fully. I charge in the morning.
The Bluetooth seems to be better, stronger and father distance with the Time. I can leave my phone on the second floor and go in the basement and receive and answer texts with the Time. The Round will lose the Bluetooth connection. The Time will also reconnect on its own really easily if it does lose the connection, where the Round seems to need some babysitting.
The buttons are easier to push on the Time because of its square shape. And watch faces I love on the Time are not available on the Round.

Overall my favorite for function is the Time, but the Round is more wearable.

Review: I lost my Forerunner 935 - I have no idea what happened to it.  After hunting for a while, I gave up and bought this 945 Music.  I like the screen, I like the way the buttons work and feel.  I think the sensor is a nice improvement over the 935 in terms of accuracy.  I used to track my heartrate with a Wahoo Tickr and my phone and compare it against the 935 and the 935 didn't see as accurate - or maybe it was just consistently lower and I wanted my workouts to be intense, but I tended to believe the Tickr more than the 935.  With the 945, the Tickr and the 945 more or less exactly match and if they are off, it's like 1bpm out of ~150bpm not 5-6bpm or more between the 935 and the Tickr.  So I like that both because it matches my HR monitor but also the HR number is higher which makes me feel more proud of myself.  And the 945 seems to more consistently sync when I walk in the door on runs without my phone - with the 935, I often had to pull up the Garmin app but with the 945 it just automatically shows up.  Lastly, there's all the performance stats on the watch - lots more data.  I'm not sure I understand all of the new data, but I appreciate that there's lots more numbers to obsess over at the end of the run.  Overall, I think the 945 is a nice improvement - I'm still not happy with myself that I apparently lost my 935, but I'm less displeased the more that I use my 945.
