Review:
I was really excited to try this watch as it had all of the features I wanted without the Apple price. But as with most things in life, you get what you pay for.

First of all, there is a delicate balance at play with this watch. It's light and looks nice, but it doesn't feel solidly made. Perhaps I am asking for too much to have both, but that's just my observation. The GPS and App are great. I'm a runner and love the GPS and music storage (including Amazon Music). For the first few months of having this device, I thought I had found a great alternative to the Apple Watch (especially because the battery life is amazing).

But then month 4-5 rolled around and things started to go wrong. It has to be plugged in just right to charge... minor but sometimes annoying. The big issue is with the fitness tracking. When I run or am doing a HIIT workout, the watch buzzes and flashes like it's trying to stop and start constantly. I wait for a second and get it calmed down and start doing my thing again and it continues to buzz and flash. Clearly there is something wrong (accelerometer issues?). Garmin customer service has been less than helpful. They want me to send them a video of this happening. Jumping around or running and shooting some video is annoying. I wish they would just make it right.

The summary here is this: it's a great looking watch with some cool features, but I don't think it stands the test of time. If I could go back, I'd fork over the extra $100-200 and get the Apple Watch. In fact, I'll probably be doing that anyways.

Review:
I avoided buying this watch for the longest time. I just couldn't bring myself to buy a watch with so little color depth and limited app functionality so I skipped the Pebble and bought an Android Wear watch first (Moto 360 G1). I patted myself on the back for getting such a pretty and stylish smartwatch. But after about a year I realized I didn't use any of the "cool" features that set Android Wear watches apart from the Pebble. What's more I was constantly frustrated by the limited battery life, limited touch screen UI and frustratingly convoluted Android Wear crap. A charge usually lasted a day if I turned off enough battery eating features. If I didn't get the watch on the charger before I went to bed, the watch was pretty much useless the next day.

So when I heard Pebble was closing up shop and will no longer product their watches I jumped on one. I'm glad I did. It's nice looking. It's does everything I want it to do. The battery lasts almost a week. So instead of making sure I get it on the charger every night I can check the battery status every couple days. "Still have 40%? Eh, I'll charge it in a couple days." The interface doesn't get in it's own way like an Android Wear touch screen. Sometimes simple is better. And in this case simpler is definitely better in a resounding way.

My only regret is I didn't buy it sooner.

Review:
The watch is a good mix of features at this price range with an average battery life.

Pros:
1) The watch is easy to setup - You need to download the Wear OS app by Google and the official Mobvoi App - That's it.
2) The touch of the watch is very sensitive and reactive.
3) The watch offers a great operation system because of which it never hangs and the transition from one screen to another is extremely smooth.
4) A lot of customers think about how you can type on such a small screen - I have been using to reply to Emails/Messages without any effort from the screen itself as the interface is very smooth.
5) The watch also acts as a decently accurate fitness tracker - Be it be steps, calories, etc. The watch also offers the functionality to track heart rate, which is great.
6) The watch has a great Gestures feature with which you can navigate through your notifications, etc.

Cons:
1) As you might have heard before, the watch doesn't have the best battery life. I use all the major functionality of the watch as my watch is always connected to phone via bluetooth. This includes 'Ok Google', always on display with different wallpapers, different fitness tracking, etc. The watch has average battery life ~ 40 hours using all these features in one single charge.
2) While the gesture feature is great, the amount of time it takes for the screen to display when bringing your wrist up is slightly long (it is not instantaneous - it takes like a second or two).
3) The watch doesn't come with a great manual. Only basic stuff is covered in the manual. It has much more potential and capabilities than that. If you buy it, please go through youtube and different forums to take your watch to the next level.

Review: First things first for those who care – I'm pretty sure this watch and the app that go with it are Chinese, meaning there's a good chance you're sharing your data. That's just about the only con, though. I have been blown away by this watch considering it's considerably cheaper than the big name alternatives. The watch is light, looks good, and feels good. The screen is high-quality and the touch sensitivity is nice. It vibrates quite aggressively with notifications, and the phone call sound quality is surprisingly loud and clear for a watch. It does things like heart rate, sleep sensor, blood O2 sensor, etc. like other smart watches. What REALLY impressed me was the battery life. This thing has lasted over a week with normal use and Bluetooth connectivity. My Samsung watch didn't last nearly this long even brand new, and it was 4 times the price. The app is a bit clunky, but allows you to set up which apps you want notifications from and whatnot. I would HIGHLY recommend this to anyone looking for a smartwatch but not wanting to shell out the $100-$200 for the name brands.

Special Features: Time Display, Sleep Monitor, Phone Call, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
Poor battery life mars an otherwise good experience. I can't get thru a long day with out having to tether this watch to it's charging cable. I use the fitness tracking via Google Fit which uses the GPS and heart rate monitor fairly continuously for about 30-45 minutes on a daily walk with the dog. The GPS is okay but, on the fitness tracking map, the start and end points often times don't line up even though they're in the same place. This could be because the GPS doesn't get a good signal until I actually go outside but I'll start the tracking just before I leave. I initially felt I didn't have much use for a smartwatch but actually putting it in use was better than expected. As mentioned, I use the fitness tracking via Google Fit everyday but I also like being able to switch the watch face easily to fit my attire and receiving text message notifications among others is good too. One feature that surprised me was the step ranking app which compares your step totals to 20 other users in the area (up to 30 mi radius?). In some ways, it did encourage me to walk more and you can also give a thumbs up to other users (I never got a thumbs up though I have occasionally topped the ranking. I do give a thumbs up now and then.)

Partly your experience depends on the watch face you select. I have to rant a bit here as many of the available watch faces in Google Play Store are listed as free showing a myriad of features but you may not see any of those features without ponying up for a premium version. IOW, they show you the premium features but you can't use them. One watch face that didn't do that is the Marine Commander Watch face from Bosenko which features a premium version that removes a Demo Mode logo from the watch face but otherwise is fully functional with full support for complications in four quadrants and in some cases two complications are used in a single quadrant. For example, The day/date alternates with an appointment reminder counter which shows number of appointments in the next 24 hrs and time of the next appointment. Highly customize-able. I was able to place what complications I wanted where I wanted them and change the colors of the various watch face elements.

The watch band that comes with the watch is okay and feels comfortable but I changed mine to a Spiedel twist-o-flex which works great and looks nice too.

I've tried the recommended charge/discharge cycling to extend battery life but it is what it is. I don't feel II'm that heavy a user either. I don't get that many texts or calls and the watch face supports two power saving modes of which I use the more aggressive. I get around 12 hours of use before hitting the low power mode but I'll usually tether it to the charger during the day so it makes it to 18 hours plus. It does charge fairly quickly. If not for the less than a day charge, I would rate this at least a star higher.

Updated: After some additional rundown testing I decided to increase my rating by 1 star since I'm only infrequently tethering to the charger during the day. Typically, I'm able to go the entire day with out the battery being an issue. I've been turning off the battery saver when it kicks in sometimes to make it to the end of the day and running down in to single digits. Interestingly, I ran a simple watch face called Classical II which only has the clock watch face, a day/date and a watch battery level for a couple days and didn't gain significantly in reduced battery usage. It did use less battery but not as much as you might expect considering the difference in sensor use with the Marine Commander. watch face. Daily run time is 16-18 hours.

Review:
I really wanted to love it. I really did.

Lets preface this review with what I was hoping to get out of it - A fitness-focused watch that moonlights as a smartwatch. Overall I was unimpressed with the smartwatch features. Syncing it to my Google Pixel, the notifications it receives and displays are very limited. There isn't much you can do about that, but it didn't really feel like there was much to it. Also, they were often delayed by 10 minutes. My phone was always in my pocket near the watch, not sure if this was just a lemon or if it is something to do with the watch itself.
The screen was also pretty underwhelming. In comparison to what the fitbit iconic looks like, the garmin falls short. The iconic is more crisp and bright, the vivoactive is more pixel-y and dull.

What I was most interested in were

I was pretty happy with the battery life. It would be nice if you could extend the battery life by the fitness tracking aspects of it. It takes some customization to get it to do what you really want - I could not figure out how to threshold the activity tracking so that just normal walks to and from places weren't counted as exercise. I ended up turning the automatic tracking off.
The rep-counting was not accurate at all. Punching in data retrospectively was difficult as well. If I wanted to say I did squats with 150 pounds, I would have to sit there and wait for the numbers to go up one by one if logging it through the watch.
Swimming with the watch was great, I love that you can track SWOLF scores through the app. However when the watch is submerged it becomes difficult to operate and the touch sensitivity is pretty whacko. turning constant HR off, but that is not possible.

Overall, the basic features of the watch are fine but very difficult to control with swipes and the 1 button on the side.

The cons didn't justify the $300 price tag, so it was returned. Maybe once there have been some software updates and it has been on the market longer I will re-evaluate.

Review:
I bought this watch from the Mobvoi website and I am enjoying it very much. This is my first smartwatch and had to do some homework before taking the plunge.

One thing that I like about this watch is the LCD display that gives you the basic info that is usually on traditional watches. This saves your battery and doesn't look too flashy when you dont want it too. This is to say that you can turn this simpler screen option off and have a dimmed version of your smartwatch face if you like that more.

Another point is that there are a lot of options to customize how you use it. So you can tap to wake, tilt to wake, or use the buttons to wake. You can designate on of your buttons to open a specific app and add apps on you main watch face as a shortcut. You can even turn off the fitness stuff if you don't like them, such as steps, automatic heart rate monitor, and so on.

This watch is comfortable as I wear it at the gym and at work. The design is nice and suits my casual/sporty style.

One thing that can improve is the performance. Wear OS can be slow at times but once the watch warms up after a couple seconds of use, there is no lag but one would notice some lag when first opening an app or using the watch first thing in the morning. One thing that would help this problem is increasing the RAM, which Mobvoi has done with the newer Ticwatch pro LTE, but I haven't had the chance to use it so I can not say if this would help but reviewers claim it does. The watch will update to a newer OS version when powering up the first time and I can't wait for the next update!

Overall, I love this watch and I will continue to use it for years to come until it breaks. My experience with Mobvoi has been good as they delivered the item as expected and checked on my experience after I had used the watch for a week or two.

Review:
First off, I had a pebble time smartwatch. Which in my opinion was the one of the best smartwatches period. After pebble was purchased by Fitbit it was hard to find another smartwatch that came close to the battery life and had an always-on screen. This watch does all that.

Let me say, I get an easy 7-8 days off battery life out of this watch. That is with my personal settings, you will not get 7-8 days if use the stock settings of the watch. More like 5 days with stock settings.

The watch is extremely comfortable to wear and it's pretty much unnoticeable after a few days of wearing it.

Another upside is that the watch is not too thick. I also tried out the ticwatch pro but it was just too thick for my liking. Coming in at 11.7mm this is one of the thinner smartwatches out there.

The Garmin connect app is also very detailed and intuitive. And the Garmin IQ app and gives you thousands of watch faces to choose from.

So it's thin has great battery life and it has an always-on screen. What's not to like.

To achieve a 7-8 day battery life set the backlight to 0%. Then change the face on the watch to have a white background and black text for the numbers and info. Then the watch is pretty much readable in any lighting condition except complete dark. That will give you the best battery life. Checkout the photos to see what I mean about the watchface. The last photo was taken in a very dark room and you can clearly see how visible it is. Also not using GPS everyday helps too. I still get that 7-8 days battery life with about an 2 hours of GPS usage, responding to notifications and using the Garmin pay features. The blacklight turns on every time you interact with the watch, that's why turning it off by going to 0% is crucial for battery life in watch.

This watch is great, just get it. It's worth every penny.

Review: The first thing that you need to know about this watch is that it is not comparable to the $200 plus watches that are on the market. This is a economical smartwatch. It is a very clean, stylish watch that does pair easily with your device and yet the app is somewhat sketchy on a daily basis as is whether or not it will connect with the device. One thing that I do want you to know is you will notice the picture with the two watches, the pink one is the one that I bought for my wife first and then the second one is the black one that I bought for myself. You will notice a difference in the watch faces in that the one on the pink watch is larger than the one on the black watch. Special Features: Health Monitor, GPS
Review:
The first thing I noticed about the Ticwatch E3 is that it's *really* fast. Apps open quickly, animations don't hitch, content doesn't take forever to load; it's hard to make this watch miss a beat, the result of which is a much more polished and usable experience than I've had on any other Wear OS watch. The watch also packs GPS for workout tracking and NFC for Google Pay, which still aren't guaranteed on every model of watch.
Mobvoi's fitness suite is fully featured and well-designed. I'm not a fitness guru, but I like being able to view my steps and sleep info, and i particularly appreciated that I could tell the watch I'm not after mad gains, just trying to stay healthy.
But the cracks start to show with little annoyances, such as the lack of auto brightness and a rotating crown. Having to click your brightness up or down a couple times a day isn't a huge deal, but it adds up over a long time, and you'll feel the lack of a crown anytime you scroll through a long message.
Unfortunately typical of Wear OS, the battery will only last just over a day with medium use, mostly music controls. Mobvoi did throw in a custom Essential Mode, however, which activates automatically on low battery and claims to greatly extend your uptime, in exchange for only showing the time, steps, and heart rate when it blinks on the display. That's a display you probably won't be keeping on all the time; the LCD gets the job done, but you can see the backlight bleeding through in dark rooms, which may be distracting for some people.
Overall, it's a rock-solid wear OS watch on a budget, and it's earned its spot in my daily smartwatch rotation. For $50 less, it would be an instant recommend. But with the Samsung Galaxy Watch 4 looming overhead, offering improvements to every spec for a starting price of just $50 more, the Ticwatch E3 might struggle to find a niche.

Review:
Didn't buy this on Amazon, but paid about the same. For the low price of $40 it's seriously an awesome watch.
I've been wearing it on the weekends since I bought it 4 months ago. (I wear a G-Shock during the week because I work with heavy machinery) The notifier is particularly awesome when I'm driving and can glance at who texted or called me easily. This is also great when I'm home because I usually leave my phone around. I put a screen protector on and a 22mm NATO band and it looks great. The face isn't a touch screen btw, the watch just has a motion sensor in it, so you can trigger it by flicking your wrist instead of tapping the screen. The battery does last days. I only charge it every few weeks since I only wear it a couple days a week. Bluetooth drains my phone though...

Only things I wish could be done better is all software related. You can only check a notification from the last five minutes, would be nice if you could check older messages. Also since it already has a motion sensor in it, a step counter would be nice. Only hardware issue I have is I wish it was water resistant, but I wash my hands with it carefully, and my son has drooled all over it with no problems.

It doesn't look like a $300 watch. It isn't a fashion piece. It's a little bulky. But at the end of the day its a very functional and good looking bare bones smart watch. If this one breaks I would probably buy another one, but might consider upgrading to the next expensive Martian.

Review: I had been using another fitness teaching watch, but find I was wanting more than it was capable of as I started doing more training and different running workouts. I had waited a while to jump to Garmin because I never thought of myself as a true "runner", but as I started doing more the Garmin watches did everything I was wanting and more. I did a lot of research before deciding, but the price point for the vivoavtive 3 music was within reach, it allows me to run without my phone and still have music (hate the armbands!, most of my running pants have pockets now but still not ideal), and the size of the watch face is now smaller to better for my wrist while still being able to see all the tracking easily during my run. It's super comfortable to wear as a daily watch, you can change the watch face to suit what you like and what you want to do for daily tracking, and can easily scroll through your commonly used widgets, and has enough smartwatch capabilities like text and call notification, weather, calendar reminders, (plus you can download more apps if you want). Tracking exercises is super easy, GPS locks quickly and is accurate, and Garmin gives you tons of dynamics about your run or workout. Heart rate monitoring seems accurate (or as accurate as any other wrist based model), and gives you further info about your training and day. Garmin has some pre-set workouts,in or you can create your own and load them, which I really liked! I had no issues losing music into it, I use Google play music and just downloaded songs onto my computer then transferred them to the watch (more detailed directions on Garmin or various YouTube videos). I don't have one of the verified Bluetooth wireless headphones, or even one of the big brands, but had no trouble pairing them and so far so good... Will try on a longer run this weekend. It tracks your steps and you can turn on a "move reminder" if you've been inactive for too long, nice for at work as a gentle reminder to get up from my desk. The vibration alerts can be set to different vibration levels (low/med/high) which is nice. I haven't had any issues with battery life wearing it daily to work and doing a few runs with GPS and no music before needing to charge. Of course running with GPS and music will drain it more quickly, but it charges pretty fast. Even though the construction and materials seem solid and study, I did decide to purchase a screen protector because I'm a little rough on my watches, so better safe than sorry. Overall after just a week I'm really loving it and those I made the jump over to Garmin.

Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I've been using the smart watch for about a week so far. Overall, it's been a good watch at a pretty good price point.

Pros
1. It's pretty lightweight, and the band seems to be pretty flexible, sturdy and will be long enough even for the largest wrist. I can definitely work out or play sports while wearing this thing. The watch came with a couple of extra pins in case you want to swap out the band.
2. All of the sensors seem to work pretty well and are pretty accurate. There is a heart rate, O2, and blood pressure sensor. The watch counts steps and calculates calories burned.
3. The display is round and just about the right size for me. And, it's plenty bright. Brightness is adjustable.
4. The Android application downloaded from the Google Play Store is easy to download to your phone and to set up. My app synced up very quickly to the watch using bluetooth.
5. There are about 30 or so watch faces (combination of digital and analog faces) that come with the app and can be downloaded to the watch from the app (but see the limitations in the Con section below). I have not found any way to download any additional watch faces to the app, so you're stuck with the pre-installed watch faces in the app.
6. You can create your own watch face background from a picture that you have on your phone, but the actual digital/analog watch associated with that custom background picture is really limited.
7. Very good battery life. I ran it a full day and only used less than half the battery.
8. Very easy to set up notifications from your phone to the watch. You can see your incoming phone calls, emails, texts, weather alerts, and other things that you sync from apps on your phone. You have to go into the settings and enable notifications from each app on your phone that you want to push to your watch. Of course, your watch needs to be within blue tooth range to get the real time notifications on your watch. But, the watch operates like a normal watch when not in blue tooth distance of your phone (you just don't get the notifications).
9. It claims it is waterproof, but I'm not going to submerge it to ensure it is.

Cons:
1. The watch came with 5 watch face options pre-installed and the watch will only hold a max of 5 watch faces at any one time. Four of the pre-installed watch faces cannot be removed from the watch, where the other one of the pre-installed watch faces can be replaced by a watch face downloaded from the phone app. The app on the phone offers about 30 or so other watch face options (some digital, some analog), and it's pretty easy to download one of those to the watch. But, you can only download at most one watch face, which replaces the one pre-installed watch face or replaces a watch face that you previously downloaded (remember, you cannot remove or replace 4 of the preinstalled watch faces). Of the 5 watch faces that are loaded on the watch, it's pretty easy to switch between them.
2. The picture of the watch face on the Amazon listing is actually not available as one of the 5 pre-installed watch faces, nor is it available for download from the phone app.
3. You charge the phone using a cable supplied in the package. The cable is about 3 feet long and has a USB-A connector on one end, and a custom smallish magnetic connector on the other end. The magnetic connector on the cable interfaces to the back of the watch. I wish the watch had a USB-C connector for charging so I don't have to carry around the custom cable. But, it is easy to charge. I plugged the USB-A connector into a laptop to charge the watch. The watch package does not contain an AC plug that you can plug into an AC outlet in your home.

Review:
Having owned 2 previous smartwatches, I can compare them to the Motorola Moto 360 and make an accurate assessment.

I have owned the Pebble Steel as well as the Sony Smartwatch 3 and have found them to be solid choices. However, compared to the Moto 360, they fall short:

The Pebble Steel is a reasonably priced smartwatch with a 1.26" black display It has a limited number of watchfaces and apps that can be used with it.

The Sony Smartwatch 3 is somewhat pricier model with a larger 1.6" display. Using Android Wear provides many more watchfaces and apps than the Pebble Steel. Its small display makes it hard to read. The watch's most problematic issue is that charging requires a micro-USB cable that plugs directly into the back of the watchface.

The Moto 360 trumps both watches and provides a more solid smartwatch experience. It's larger 1.8" round face makes messages much easier to read than either phone. It provides a space above and below the message, making them easier to read--even without glasses.

The watch comes with a magnetic charging dock that simplifies charging. Just place your phone on the dock (no plug in required!) and within an hour you watch is fully charged!

Using Android Wear, you have a large selection of watch faces and apps from which to chose. The larger size makes it even easier to read nearly all of the faces. None of the squinting problem like some other watches require.

For all of the features provided, Motorola's pricing is quite reasonable compared to other manufacturer's most notably Apple. I purchased the Moto 360 Light Metal with a stainless steel band at a cost just slightly over $200. For that amount, you get a large display with a clean bezel that is easy to read. Can other manufacturer's offer that?

Target Audience: Men, Father, Grandpa, Husband, Adventurer, Explorer, Hikers, Climbers, Runner
Review:
This watch works well. It's comfortable. The band is soft rubber that is somewhat stretchable. Why is this advertised as a military watch? There are many sports modes, with settings designed to get you to exercise and get metrics. Records on the watch are only the last exercise ran (saved for each type). On the phone it give day, week, month, and year totals. It automatically records the steps you take (reset on the watch at midnight; day, week, and month totals on the phone). It automatically records a sleep quality rating (the phone has day, week, and month records). I have avoided repeating things you can read on product photos and description. See my complaint about location sharing at the bottom.

There are 10 slots for watch faces. You can cycle through them on your watch. The two slots that are adjustable (1,10) must be set up / changed with your phone. Those settings are transferred to your watch when you select save on the top right on your phone. --- The first slot --- With the first one you can set the background to a photo that's stored on your phone. You can move the time to the top or bottom (the photo has it on the bottom), change the text color, and you can have the date, heart rate, steps, and sleep quality displayed above or below the time. The heart rate is the last calculated from a mode, as opposed to real-time. --- Slots 2 through 9 --- theses are pre-defined watch faces. --- Slot 10 --- You can select a watch face from a large list of available watch faces. See the photo (blue spirals).

The photos. This one with the blue spirals is a downloaded watch face. The other one is a photo I took of a small section of tapestry.

What I don't like.
1) The screen turns off after 5 seconds. There's no way to change that. It's a pain. Plus, I would like an always-on watch face.
2) There's no way to save downloaded watch faces. You can only have one downloaded at a time.
3) You have to pair your phone to initially set the correct time (it's automatically set upon initial pairing).
3) Location permission was required to initially pair the phone with Bluetooth. I went to a coffee shop to pair this watch with my phone. I then disabled the location permission for this app in the app settings area on my phone. The watch has continued to work fine (and with the phone) with this setting.
Attribute_Related_Content: Screen Size: The screen turns off after 5 seconds. There's no way to change that. I would like an always-on watch face.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The screen should remain visible without turning off automatically.

Review:
I switched to Vivoactive 3 after using Pebble watches for years, and so far I'm happy with my purchase.
Pros:
Transflective screen - always on, great visibility in sunlight, usable in dark with gesture-activated backlight
Solid heart rate sensor. I've never worn an HR-enabled device and thought hairy arms would mess with the optical sensor, but this one seems to be accurate
Onboard bluetooth music - don't have to carry my phone around gym anymore. Some people mentioned stuttering, but I haven't had any problems using the watch with my cheap basic headset
Pretty good sports features out of the box
Additional features like NFC payments, GPS, Wi-fi
There's ANT+ for connectivity with other fitness devices, but I don't have any of those at the moment so can't testify how well that works

Cons:
Personally I'd prefer more physical buttons. There's only one button which acts either as the home button or start/stop for timers and activities
Material quality is not great, the watch feels and looks cheap. I'd pay extra for a metal-body version
The display is round just for the sake of looking like a mechanical watch. The UI is not really built for a round display, so in places like menus you get cropped corners where you'd be able to see the full picture on a square display
There aren't that many apps, and the SDK is really limited, so you won't see any super interesting apps. AFAIK the platform doesn't support music streaming at all right now, there's a couple of apps for streaming services, but you're supposed to preload music into the memory

I truly think this is a fantastic product. It's super easy to use and has so many awesome features. This product is great for people of all ages. This watch has a lot of health and activity features that are typically found on more expensive models. Review:
If you're looking for a low cost intro into fitness trackers or smart devices that also looks good, then this might be the watch for you. It's not so much a smartwatch but more a fitness tracker with smartwatch-like capabilities. Aesthetically, this is really nice looking. The aluminum alloy dial looks premium and it's also lightweight and comfortable to wear.

As an activity tracker, it's good if you don't need super accurate measurements. I compared the steps/distance to both a Misfit Ray (which was worn on the same hand as the UWatch) and Google Fit on my phone. The step count was within an acceptable range (+/- a few hundred steps when I'm moving for a while) but the distance was way off, giving me an extra mile each time no matter if the step count was over or under the count from the other apps (there is a setting in the app under "Profile" to adjust your step length and my length is correct so there might be an issue with the watch itself. I'm being sent a replacement and will update this review after it comes.)

UPDATE: The replacement watch is now correctly measuring my distance. It seems that the previous unit has a slight bug in it.

Also, be aware that because it seems to measure your steps via sensors in the watch, if you're moving your arms around a lot even while stationary, it can add extra steps to your total. However, this seems to be true of a lot of fitness trackers -- my dad's Samsung Gear Fit 2 (which is much more expensive) does this as well.

I had bigger accuracy issues with the blood pressure measurements. Compared to a blood pressure cuff, it kept measuring my blood pressure about 15-20% higher than it was. However, the heart rate seemed fairly accurate. As I don't have another way to measure my blood oxygen I can't really speak to how accurate that is.

As for the smartwatch-like capabilities, it does have options to notify you about messages/notifications from several popular apps (including Facebook, Twitter, Instagram) but you can't reply to messages.

The app itself (Da Fit) is easy to use, has a nice interface, and lot of options to customize -- you can have move reminders, full time heart rate monitoring, turning on/off the quick view (which is very sensitive) and adjusting the period where quick view operates, and customizing your watch face. With the last one there's plenty of pre-made ones to choose from (though they don't seem to be sorted in any way so it can get tedious scrolling through your options) or you can upload your own photo to make one yourself. This option however, does highlight a quirk of the watch face -- although the face is round, the actual display is a square (which is noted in the product description) which can lead to some wonky looking backgrounds.

Battery-wise it's very good when you're using it as a standalone watch and not connected to your phone (which is how I use it). Constantly wearing it, I've gotten to 11 days with about 10% left. If I wore it more like how I normally wear a watch (only when I'm out and about and taking it off when I get home) then I think I can get to their advertised 15 days. If idle, it possibly can get to the 40 days of standby as I left it untouched for 2 days and the battery didn't drop. Obviously if you use it more often and keep it connected to your phone at all times it's going to be less. Charging is easy with the included charging cable (it doesn't come with the wall adapter so you'll have provide your own).

Overall, I think if you're looking at testing the smart device market, don't want to spend much, but still want something nice, this is a good starter. The activity tracking seems to on par with other trackers I use (Misfit Ray and Google Fit). Be sure you set your stride length to get a more accurate distance measurement. Health measurements sort of depend on what you're tracking -- I think heart rate is pretty accurate, but if you had a serious condition I wouldn't recommend this watch to keep track of your blood pressure, but for a ballpark measurement (and as long as you know your baselines and the quirks in the watch's measurements) it's fine.

TL:DR; If you're considering a smart watch, and don't want your watch to be another phone, the pebble time is a great choice. I'd recommend it.

I really like the pebble steel. It's been generally very comfortable and not overly heavy or intrusive. I bought a nice metal band to compliment it; It looks quite classy in a business casual setting.

The always on screen is severely under rated. Anyone considering a smart watch is going to want this - whether they know it or not. Not having to "turn on" to see the time makes the pebble feel like an actual watch.

I specifically bought it for a few reasons.
1. Fitness tracker - needed something that monitored steps and provided an easy way of monitoring rest periods in the gym
2. Needed a watch
3. Monitoring notifications from the phone

For the few days I've used this device, it has been successful at meeting all of my requirements. I'd recommend it to anyone considering a smart watch.

As for the battery life, I've chosen a watch face with no "flashing stuff" so the screen only updates once a minute when the minute hand moves. It's been 3 days, and i'm reading 80% battery life. Really impressed. Of course, I could throw on a novelty screen like the pokemon battles or what have you, but i'm pretty sure the constant screen activity would drain the battery much faster.

I'd definitely recommend

Review:
I am enjoying the Galaxy Fit so far, although I'm not yet ready to give up my Fitbit. Obviously, I like the watch aspect of the Galaxy Fit and really didn't enjoy wearing the Fitbit AND watch at the same time (on the same arm). Being able to wear only one device is much easier and more comfortable. I've continued to wear both for comparison purposes and find there to be some large discrepancies between "steps" counted by the two products. The Galaxy Fit seems to be higher (more steps) generally. I don't recall specifically but think the Fitbit had a calibration step as part of the setup (I am tall but have relatively short legs for my height). Batter life is good and its easy to control, customize.

On the downside, the Galaxy Fit can't be worn in the shower and is a bit bulky to wear to bed (for sleep tracking). Samsung has caught up with the sleep tracking although you have to use a separate app (not exactly sure why its not all one app like Fitbit). I don't find myself using the alerts aspect of the device a lot. Review:
PROS:
- Battery life is excellent
- Light and comfortable
- Watch band is durable and comfortable.
- Very good software
- Customizable watch faces and many to choose from
- A lot of functionality

CONS:
- Blood pressure readings are wildly inaccurate

I really like this smart watch and will continue to use it as my daily timepiece, but a potential buyer needs to know that these devices should not be used as a health monitor. As a fitness tracker, it works very well in most respects. And as with most smart watches, the software it pairs with on your mobile device makes a huge difference in the overall experience you will have.

Thankfully, the "DaFit" software is really quite nice and very functional. The software allows you to change watch faces and control various settings of the watch. It makes this device punch above its price point and is one of the reasons I really enjoy using this watch.

The pulse readings seem to be consistent with my exercise bicycle. I cannot speak on the accuracy of blood oxygen, as I have nothing to compare that to, but there is a serious issue that needs to be addressed with the blood pressure readings.

The blood pressure readings from this device are very, very inaccurate. I have high blood pressure and was very interested to see how that feature would perform on this device. I can very confidently say that this device consistently gives readings that are in the normal range, when in reality my blood pressure was quite a bit higher. I also have an at-home blood pressure testing device that is calibrated and gives accurate readings, even when compared to the doctor's office when I go there.

So, as long as you are not depending on the blood pressure readings, I can still recommend this watch for its wealth of features and price.

Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I wanted to love this watch. I really did. It's lightweight, cute, customizable, and for the price it packs a lot of features into a tiny package.

The problem is, I was looking for an Android Wear device primarily to help me count steps and activity time, and it's in this arena that the Ticwatch E falls far short of the mark.

Pros: Easy to set up and sync to your Android phone (can't speak for iOS). Smooth scrolling through the app list. Lightweight. Can use any 20 mm watch band with it. Comes with Google Assistant (an imperfect app, but has great potential) and Android Wear 2.0. Responsive - a flick of the wrist to, say, check the time turns the watch face on immediately. You get all notifications from your phone. You can receive and send texts and answer phone calls. Proprietary Health app looks neat. Since it's an Android device, you can use the Play Store's (currently limited) selection of apps and watch faces. Lots of built-in features for the price. Google Fit app syncs to MyFitnessPal.

Cons: Both the proprietary Health app and Google Fit waaaaaaaay overestimate your step count and daily activity/exercise time, and the Health app also overestimated distance traveled. My daily step count was approximately 4-5 times higher than it should have been (for example, one morning between 7:00 and 8:00 am it counted over 2,000 steps when all I'd done was get up, get dressed, and get dropped off at work - see attached pic). I wasn't expecting absolute precision, particularly at this price point, but since I'm trying to use the data with MyFitnessPal in order to approximate how many calories I should be consuming, the data from the Ticwatch's step counter is pretty useless.

Movboi customer service leaves a lot to be desired. I emailed their main support inbox to ask whether the incorrect step tracking was a known issue or possibly indicative of a defective device, and it took three days (after two emails I had to forward to a specific rep I was emailed contact info for from Amazon) for someone to get back to me. Their response was that I should get *another* kind of step tracker and forward the data from the other tracker to them so they could improve their device in future versions. No solution was offered for the device I just bought.

The proprietary Health app doesn't sync with MyFitnessPal.

Syncing between the Fit app on the watch and the one on the phone is sporadic and infrequent. In one case, it didn't sync my steps to the phone until a day and a half later. Pretty unhelpful if you're trying to use MyFitnessPal to estimate daily caloric intake. This may be a Fit issue and not necessarily a hardware issue, however.

Battery life is not great - will maybe last 16-18 hours with typical use (though it does charge fast).

If you're not too concerned with fitness tracking and just want a fun, cute, inexpensive watch that also lets you view your phone notifications with just a glance at your wrist, this is a great device. If you're looking for help with personal fitness tracking, like I was, keep looking.

While the interface takes a bit of getting used to, this is the best overall fitness tracker I have owned. I have had six or seven over the past few years from Samsung, Microsoft, Fitbit and others. Wearing it in the shower sometimes will trigger the watch face and will start activating the widgets

The difference between apps and widgets is not completely clear. I thought I put widgets on but couldn't find them. Turned out they were in a different location.

GPS is a bit wonky...I used it to track me walking between my hotel and conference (I walked in a straight line), but it showed a zig-zag pattern on the map...likely due to interference from tall buildings.

Those are my reasons for the below-five star ratings for accuracy, interface, etc. That said, those are very minor issues and just take a couple days to get adjusted to. Overall, this watch is amazing! Fast charging, long battery life, and quit a bit of functionality in terms of exercise apps / tracking. It'll even track you swimming (haven't used that yet but will this weekend).

Review:
this is the best wear OS smartwatch I have used, and I've had a few. I've also used the Samsung Galaxy fit pro 2.

as far as battery life goes, this is fantastic. The software does everything I wanted to do, and it's the only watch that tracks everything I want with a base watch face, so I don't need to go grab some third party stuff.

unfortunately, this is a large watch. although I've had other tick watches specifically the E-Series, this one doesn't feel nearly as comfortable. I'm using the default leather wrist strap, and I'm certain my wrist hasn't gotten any larger, but I'm definitely pushing the end of the straps. no matter how many notches I tighten or loosen, it feels after a couple hours as if things are tighter than they were when I put the watch on. I did not have this feeling with the E-Series watch.

I like to wear my watches on the left hand, and this means I find myself often getting the physical buttons jammed into something. all of these issues I attribute to the sheer size and bulk of the watch itself.

I initially bought the watch from my mother, but I had to use it so that I could troubleshoot it for her, and it worked so well that I kept that for myself and bought her another one. so obviously this size issue wasn't enough to put me off of it, but I cannot imagine anyone using this to drag sleep.

I filter my workout and health activity through Google fit in this watch integrates seamlessly with that app. you can customize one of the two buttons to work with anyone app you so desire. That's a neat trait.

the most comfortable watch I've worn was the Samsung gear fit pro 2, but they seem to have given up on that. and it never played well with Google fit in the first place. Samsung seems determined to be the next Apple in terms of isolating their own ecosystem. so I've given up on them.

there's nothing special about this watch. all of the Wear OS devices are no frills, basic smart watches. this is the one with supposedly the longest battery life, and it has had the best battery life of all the watches I've tried, including the Samsung's which are not Wear OS devices.

The watch straps are easy to take off and change with a small clip that slides in and out at the interface between strap and watch. I have not tried different straps, but I'm not sure the strap has any impact on my comfort issue. I'm fairly certain it's just a large watch and some people will deal with it and others will be bothered.

Review:
See at the end for my original one star review. this is an update one year in....

Old issues:-

1. Accuracy. Make two changes in set-up. Turn auto-pause off. Turn record data every second on. In my experience this makes the 610 as accurate as the old 405 in most circumstances. That said I did a measured distance half-marathon wearing a 405 and a 610 and they clocked 21.1 and 20.8kms respectively. since it was upgraded thats the only problem Ive had, and Ive no idea why it happened.

2. Up-loads. Garmin have added advice on their web-site to enable certain pages as "trusted sites" in your browser. doing this fixed the problems in ie9. I still have occasional problems with Crome and the Garmin database but it works.

Others:-

3. The charging issue. this is well covered elsewhere. Fortunately Ive never experienced a problem. one piece of advice though - the plug for the 405 and 610 incorporate an AC-DC transformer that look very similar. But if you plug the (different) 610 cable into the 405 transformer it wont function correctly.

4. the retaining pin slips out of the wristband. fortunately when mine went i noticed and found the watch (and pin). my fix has been to wrap insulating tape around the strap and pin to stop it sliding out. looks crap but works.

Overall I now get along with the 610. The watch itself works well, and the touch screen is stable even when soaked in sweat. But I'm still amazed they sell it with a clearly defective strap. With all the other faults with the 610 at release I would think long and hard about ever buying a Garmin product again. Anyhow I guess soon everyone will replace this sort of gadget with a phone app and Garmin will go the same way as Kodak. Cant say I'll be too sorry as long as someone takes over their web site with all my training records....

ORIGINAL REVIEW

Like many of the other reviewerss ive owned a Garmin 405 before and despite the completely misdesigned bezzle on that watch (doesnt work when it gets covered in sweat, on...err...an exercise watch) i really liked it.

so when i heard theyd updated it with a device that didnt suffer the doesnt work in the wet problems i thought it was a no brainer to rush out and buy.

Positives are it looks a little more elegant and in my so far limited use it does indeed function when covered in sweat in a manner that would send the 405 into "lock up" .

But I'm afraid there are (at least) two huge drawbacks with this model:-

1. the GPS just isnt accurate. I've done similar runs with the 610 the 405 and a Timex GPS watch and had very similar results from the 405 and the Timex, and these also seemed to match closely measurements made off a map. the 610 under reads significantly. On one run Ive just completely by nearly 10 per cent. How can this be? Well, i cant believe Garmin have sold a new device less tecnically sensitive - after all theyre a GPS manufacturer. But around high buildings or under tress it just switches off bringing in the auto pause function whilst you are still running. Now i'm not talking jungle, just tall trees. i have to believe this isnt reception sensitivity (but it may be)but has maybe more to do with the correction software for loss of signal being more honest thand the 405 ie the 405 just interpolated the missing distance when it reacqired a signal, whereas the 610 just switches off. anyhow the net result is theb 610 is totally useless if you live in a city with narrow streets or run in forest. Or do both.

2. If that wasnt bad enough the pc upload functionality just doesnt work. this is outragous for a new release, and something that always worked faultlessly on the 405. just in case ive downloaded all the new software versions but its unfixable (by me at least). the 405 continues to work fine incedentally. Oddly the ANT interface recognised the 610 but cant see any of its files. this is a guess: but as my 405 is identified by the ANT stick (this is a USB plugin that communicates wirelessly with the watch) as a 405, and the 610 just comes up by its serial number - i suspect the device driver isnt equiped to cope with a 610 in some set ups. Garmin sort it out!!! ive tried it on two completely independent PCS with 2 different o/s (and using both new and old ANT sticks) and get exactly the same problem.

now if you think i work for the competion and am trying to put you off go to Garmin's own product forum and just track the experiences of "satisfied" customers. another common complaint (the above seem to be echoed) is an inability to charge Its not pretty. I hope Garmin are reading. another common problem seems to be a refusal of the 610 to charge. again not all have a problem. feeling lucky?

If you are still brave enough to contemplate buying one be wary of buying it outside a jurisdiction where you can get servicing - lots of problems quoted of local importers being very unsympathetic to people who bought overseas: probaqbly not unreasonable as these things must be a nightmare to support.

My very strong advice do not buy this watch. wait until Garmin de-bug it and rants like this are at least a year old.

i'm going to have to take this up witrh Garmin in singapore. Any joy and i'll re-edit this post.

Review:
L

d

Pros:
-Price! Paid $121 for it brand new. My Moto 360 2nd gen was $175 refurbished more than a year after it was first released.
-Runs Android Wear quite well, much better than my Moto 360 2nd gen. All menus and animations are very smooth. Plenty of storage for your apps.
-Ability to download music right to the watch is a plus (I suppose for some people).
-OLED display is very nice, I like it more than LCD especially with a black themed watch face. The text really pops.
-Speaker and mic on watch allow for making phone calls from your wrist. However, this is also a con (see below).
Cons:
-It feels and looks like a child's toy. It is very plasticy (the thin cheap kind). Also, it has a dull finish. It's not even trying to look like metal.
-It sits very high on the wrist, much more so than my Moto 360 which can make it tough to get under a shirt or jacket cuff. It's quite thick. You would think that being this thick, it would at least have a larger battery with better battery life, but it does not which leads me to the next con.
-Battery life. Being OLED, it should get much better battery life than an LCD display all other things being equal, but it just doesn't. With always on display and a watch face just showing the bare minimal hands or digital time on an all black background you will need to charge it every night. Without always on display and depending on your usage, you may get 2 days but I think you'd be running on fumes that 2nd day if you made it at all.
-Wear OS. When it comes right down to it, the biggest con with this watch is (to me) the biggest con with every Android watch. Wear OS just stinks.
When I look at my watch, I should be able to see the time, date, weather, fitness tracking in the blink of an eye. More often than not, by the time this information updates/ loads on the watch, it would've been quicker to just take my phone out of my pocket and open the app there. Far too often do refreshes just hang. You will see way too much of the spinning circle loading your weather or trying to connect to your smart thermostat at home to change the temp. I don't want a full smartphone on my wrist, I just want to be able to access a handful of useful things very quickly. Pebble OS does this much better, too bad it's dead.
I really wanted to like this watch mostly because of the price it was and because my Moto was feeling "old". But in the end, I returned it and went back to a dead platform in Pebble. If you ask me, it does what a smartwatch should and what Wear OS does not. It's a watch first and foremost. Secondly it allows me to check notifications and manipulate the few things that I do most often and quickly like change music, check weather, etc. The day that Wear OS can do these simple things and do them FAST maybe I'll come back. But until there is a new chip developed for Android watches and not the dinosaur Snapdragon 2100 I'm staying away.

Review:
Hi. I stopped wearing watches like 15 + years ago, so when I decided I needed a smart watch for exercise tracking purposes, I had to learn a lot about smart watches. I thought they were all the same. But there are big differences in price and function. This watch can do some really good things well, and it will last me for a while until I am ready to lay down some bigger $$$$ to get a full featured one.

First, I am 100% Android - no Apple iphone or iwatch... So I am coming from a cheaper mindset.

This works with the phone after pairing with the phone app. Not a bad app. BP, oxygen, pulse - these seem pretty dead on IMHO.

I mainly use the step counter - seems OK. Maybe +- 10-20% error. Not sure. Not terrible.

I like the sleep system and wear the watch to bed. The read out on the phone and app are good. It is very good at seeing when I wake up in middle of night - 100% correct.

In terms of sleep, I listen to story readers on podcasts and need very low volume (so not to disturb my wife). This phone can pair the audio form the phone as well, and I set the podcats episode on the phone to play (till end ), and then pause it. IF I wake up, all I have to do is turn on the watch with its side button and press play on the watch screen. The volume is low and perfect for listening and falling back to sleep. I love it!!!!!!!!

It can make calls thru the phone, so you could answer and talk as you walked or worked. I only played around with that a few times.

The texting and whatapps messages from the phone can vibrate the watch and then you can read a BRIEF part of the message - no multymedia and very few characters. Enough to get an idea of who/topic. This is an area I wish had full text length. Ideally a way to reply, even it yes/no/lol choices. But they described this accurately and it is not a game changer.

I also wish I could read text news stories or something to do on it. No youtube videos, etc. No podcasts.

All in all I like it. For the price it is a great deal if you are OK with its limitations. It looks pretty good IMHO and I think it is water resistant.

The battery is really good. Maybe charge it once a week. Or for little spurts a few times a week. Not a power hog.

Well, that is my honest review :)

All the people that I live with all use their Apple Watches and it seemed very convenient for them in their day to day use, and since I'm not part of the Apple Ecosystem and didn't want to spend an arm and a leg on a device I wasn't to sure about I purchased the Ticwatch E.

Setting the device up was rather easy as I just paired it with the WearOS app and the Mobvoi app for tracking stuff. The only complaint I have is battery life, which is roughly 1-2 days, it's not a problem for me as I run through a 4000 mah phone battery in about 10 hours, but for someone who isn't used to charging things every night they might want to consider another charger or something.

Overall for $129.00 this is a steal of a deal and it allows for you to enter into the smart wear era without having to spend an arm and a leg.

Review:
I will be reviewing this product based off of what I look for in a smartwatch:
Packaging, design, functions, apps, and battery

Packaging: The TicWatch E comes in a metal tin box with a camouflage design. When you open the lid, the watch, accessories, and instructions manual are kept in place by a intricately designed cardboard cutout.

Design: The watch gives off a modern minimalist feel when you look at it. It is 44 mm in diameter and 13.55 mm thick with a 1.4 inch OLED screen. Size wise, this is a pretty standard size compared to the other watches I have. The 1.4 inch screen makes the everything on the display legible and navigable. A big screen also makes the on-screen keyboard easy to use. I rarely get any typos and am able to get words right on the first try. The interface is pretty fast, meaning that I see little to no lag when opening apps or pressing the side button. I know this has been an issue with other watches, but I haven't seen any with this watch so far.

The watch is also really light to the point that I would forget I was wearing it sometimes. This also has to watch strap that it comes with, which is probably the most comfortable watch band I've ever had. It grips very nicely to the wrist so that the watch doesn't slide around. This is extremely useful when exercising. The watch bands are also interchangeable, so you can mix and match with other watch bands you may have.

Everything on the watch is navigable through the touch screen and a button on the left side of the watch. I wish that there were a more unique way of navigating through the watch like other watches do (rotating crown, rotating bezel, touchscreen side panel), but considering the price point, there's really not much to complain about.

Functions: Nothing much to mention here considering as most functions are detailed in product description. The watch has many sport functions such as built-in GPS and can track heart rate, steps, and calories burned. There is a microphone and speaker which comes in handy when picking up calls while driving or multitasking. There is NO NFC which means you won't be able to use Android Pay to store credit/debit cards on your watch to pay in checkout lines. Furthermore the watch is rated IP67, so while the watch isn't waterproof, it is water resistant.

Apps: The watch runs on Android Wear 2.0 and can also be powered by Google Assistant. While the Android Play Store hasn't been optimized for smartwatch apps yet, but it does have the useful apps such as Spotify, Google Music, Google Maps, and many others. You can use Google Assistant to navigate the watch by saying "OK Google" or hold down the side button.

Battery: I've read many complaints on the Mobvoi forum about the watch's poor battery life. The makers of the watch suggest draining the battery to 0% and charging to 100% about 3 or 4 times which would supposedly improve the battery life.

When I first started using the watch, I only got 6 hours on its first full charge. I'm not sure if this had to do with the battery or with me setting up the watch. After multiple charges though, I am averaging about 1.5 days of battery life with moderate use. I have yet been able to reach the 2 day mark yet. I do wish the charger was better designed though. It is magnetic, but doesn't hold in position well. Thus, the watch can stop charging with slight movements to the charger.

Overall:
Positives: comfortable, good workout companion for tracking workouts and listening to music, good display

(Slight) Negatives: no NFC, could use a more stable charger

EDIT 2: I've only had this watch for a few months and it died for no reason with an almost full battery. It's been sitting on the charger for two hours now and won't turn back on. Incredibly disappointed.

EDIT: The battery life on this thing is absolutely insane. I've had it for a few weeks and have only had to charge it twice. It absolutely destroys the Apple Watch battery life so if you want a long-lasting watch you don't have to think about charging often this is a good option.

A note: I'm using this with an iPhone so my review will be based on iPhone related apps and connectivity.

I just got this so I'm still messing around with it but so far it's really comparable to my old Apple Watch. My Apple Watch was a gen 1 and died on me but I needed something to help track my fitness so I decided to give this a try. The charging cord is really short, and it's a specialized cord with magnets and a specialty port for the back of the watch so you can't really change it out so that's a bummer. The watch is really easy to set up, the app is very user friendly, too and everything is smooth getting started aside from one small detail - everything is set to metric and you need to set up your profile before you can customize the watch, meaning US users who aren't familiar with metric will need to do a bunch of conversions to set their profiles up.

Once I got it set up it was easy to change my wallpaper (it looks like they only allow pre-made options, however, unlike a lot of smart watches that allow you to upload your own) and to link my contacts, Apple Health, and other items from my iPhone. The watch itself is light and the strap is thin which is nice. It has the ability to allow you to use your camera with it, a water reminder, and a ton of other options. It also comes with a 12 month warranty (though it does ask you to leave a review when registering the review. This review is not associated with that, this is just an honest review of the watch). It does have a help chat option in the app, too, which is nice. The Blood Oxygen option doesn't seem to work, I've tried to measure it a few times and nothing happens, but the fitness/steps/heartbeat options work the way they should.

It does seem to have some limitations and is a bit delayed when you click on the watch itself but, when you click on specific in-app options on your phone it almost immediately reflects on the watch so the delay may be from the touch-screen aspect. Overall, it's not bad for a cheap smart watch. I may update in a few days once I see how the fitness aspects work.

This watch was going to replace my older Fossil Q Marshall 2 smartwatch. So that is my comparison in this review.

First impression was that the Ticwatch Pro looked very high end. The physical features were subtle but rugged. The all black design was just what I was hoping for. Review: I bought the Ticwatch Pro after considerable online research as a Christmas present to myself. I opened it on December 25th, and plugged the provided charger into a 1A USB adapter I had handy. After that I mostly charged it via a USB2.0 port on my computer. Over the next few days I started learning the ins and outs of owning a smartwatch. However, after about 12 days, the watch kept waking up every minute or so while on the charger, and didn't seem to be charging. I moved it to another USB adapter I had (this one 2A), and while it did not keep waking up(I believe it was reconnecting to the PC every time), it never charged again. I ordered a replacement on Jan 10, which I received on Jan 12. Using only a .5A charger I'd dug out of a drawer on this new watch/charger, I really began to enjoy the watch. The battery life was impressive. I never really used essential mode and never was below about 30% after 18 or so hours a day. It was intuitive and fun, while having music control, notifications, and even messaging on my wrist was convenient. Sadly, the new watch/charger lasted 7 days before also failing to charge. I'm awaiting my first exchange to arrive and be processed by Amazon so I can go ahead and get a refund for the replacement. I really liked the watch and will probably eventually buy another WatchOS device, but two failures in less than a month is more than I'm ready to embrace.

Review:
After using Fitbit Charge and being frustrated with it for couple of reasons ( which I will explain below) I decided to give Vivosmart a try and have been using it for little over two months now. So far, I like some of its features a lot and some not much, and couple I really hate. Same thing was valid for Fitbit, so interestingly Vivosmart fills some of the lacking features of Fitbit while ruining some of the good ones.

Here is a quick comparison to help you purchase what would work better for your needs:

PROS:
- Vivosmart is definitely thinner than Fitbit with looks better - does not have the cheap plastic feeling to it. It is also waterproof!

- Battery life is amazing. I almost forget that I have to charge it, it lasts more than a week, and I wear it 24 hours.

- I like the dashboards on the app - tracks many things and displays in a nice graphical manner. Dashboards are much better than Fitbit.

- Can integrate with a heart rate monitor and track heart-rate - sold separately. I like this approach better than to have a bulk device on my wrist all the time. So you can buy the monitor and use while working out.

- Bluetooth connection is much more robust, and it never breaks (Fitbit bluetooth connection breaks often and device gets confused a lot.)

- Move indicator is a good plus. As a persons sits on front of screen all day without realizing how static I am it definitely helps me to remember how long I have been sitting without movement. ( This is the reason I decided to try it actually.)

- Can receive communications from your phone - your calendar/ meeting reminders, text messages, etc... Any notification that ends up in your notification menu can be displayed.

- More accurate sleep tracking. Instead of awake and sleep, it has three modes: awake, sleep and deep sleep and makes a huge difference to understand sleep quality.

- Vibrates if you are away from your phone to remind you you left it behind. This is an interesting feature that is a blessing and a curse. it definitely helped me to get back to home to pick up my phone couple of times, but there is a downside. See Cons below.

Now, sounds nice, right? Right???
Not really, here is why it is three stars:

CONS:
- The screen brightness is a problem. If it is too dim, you cannot read it at all, if you increase brightness ( adjustable) then it becomes too bright. So bright that during sleep you move your arm and wake up with a bright flash of light in your face. I use it at low mostly, and grumble if I cannot read it. Or you have to remember to dim it while sleeping.

- Alarms... probably the worse feature compared to Fitbit. I loved the Fitbit's ability to setup multiple alarms and turn them on or off as you wish. So you can have different reminders during the day. Unfortunately, vivosmart only allows you to setup a single alarm, that's it. So if you need to wake up at different hours, you have to setup the alarm each time. And it is not the easiest task, because the alarm menu under the applicatiion is hard to find and takes couple of taps and screens to set it each time. I hate it. Also the device has no indication the alarm is set. You have to check your phone to check it or set it.

Here is the algorithm to set an alarm: tap (my day)> tap (more) > tap (settings) > tap ( my vivo) > tap ( alarm) > tap (time) > tap (done) > tap (frequency) > tap (frequency you want) > tap (device settings) > tap ( wait for the sync) > tap ( more) --- finally you are back at your dashboard. Ugh! Seriously?!?

- Notifications - Ok, nice to have the option to get some notifications but not all of them, right? Unfortunately, notifications mirrors your phone, so any notification you get on your phone, you will get on your wrist. So your wrist will keep buzzing during the day, and you have no idea if you missed a call, or your colleague texted you, or you need to pick up your kid at certain hour, or your facebook friend liked your post. Be prepared to wake up with a buzzing wrist, because your friend could not sleep and decided to tweet you at 3 AM. Yup, vivosmart makes sure that you feel all of these. After thinking it is cool that you can read your text messages from your wrist, you'll hate this feature. The solution is to turn off the notifications on your phone - yes on your phone so you never see them. Apparently, designers had no clue that there might be some you want to be informed right away, and some you don't. Very frustrating!

- Ok, now you are fed up with notifications. You tried to disable the notifications on your phone, but didn't like it because you want your facebook notifications.... on your phone... not on your wrist (gosh, is this so hard?) Well, you finally decide to get rid off this function - it was a bonus feature anyway ( note that Fitbit does not have this, the only notification on Fitbit is a call notification and it works on and off). So, you turn off notifications on vivosmart - oh bummer! Apparently Alarm is also a notification, so it stops working....and there was only single one anyway. ARE YOU KIDDING ME?!?

- The notification nightmare and the bright light eye hazard ends up you not want to wear it to bed. Remember the superior sleep tracking feature??? Yeah, you just lost a biggest pro feature because now you hate to wear it to bed for other reasons.

- Remember that great feature that allows you to find your phone? In couple weeks you're going to hate that,too. It will drive you mad at home. You will go upstairs ( your phone is downstairs) - it will buzz. You go the next room - it will buzz. You go to laundry to pick up clothes - it will buzz. Apparently, the spot I sit at breakfast is the edge of this alert ( it is triggered by bluetooth signal range) so as I eat my wrist keeps buzzing. It is so annoying, either I have to carry my phone at home with me, or I take off the device.

Ok, I am still wearing it - because of the move around nudge it has...and my fitbit peeled off and looks awful. But every day I think about going back to fitbit... more and more. It feels like a painful break up.

Hope someone from the developers team will read it and address some in the new update. Otherwise, I might be tempted to get a new fitbit.
Happy to answer any questions you have.

EDIT as of 02/08/2016
After couple of sleepless nights ( Garmin decided I was awake and not moving in the middle of the night, so it tried to prompt me to move by buzzing at 3 AM couple times last week) I buried this gadget in my drawer for good, and planning to return it. I actually switched back to fitbit.
But... it is still buzzing in the drawer. Feels like sort of a horror movie - return of the undead.

Review:
I'll start out by saying that I'm a huge fan of Soundpeats products. I think they make some of the best and most affordable wireless headphones available right now.

This is their first foray into smart watches as far as I can tell, and for the most part it's pretty good, but it falls short of 5 stars for me for a few reasons.

Important caveat: I only bought this watch as an exercise tracker. I have no intention of setting it up for notifications or alarms, so I won't be commenting on that portion of the feature set.

The Good:
All day heart rate tracking works great, and the sleep tracking is really intuitive, it just figures out when you are sleeping and logs it. Pretty cool. The breathing feature is cool, if very simple, and the workout tracking is great. I do some walking, running, and yoga, and it tracks all of these well.

I think it looks great as well, every bit as cool looking as a more expensive model.

The battery life is phenomenal. I've had it for over a week now and it still has a pretty good charge left on it.

The Fine:
I have a small wrist and this is a pretty thick and long strap, so it wraps all the way around my wrist. Not the companies fault at all, but good to know for people with smaller wrists. It gets uncomfortable to wear after a while for me, but I might look for a new strap.

I wish there was a way to turn the sleep tracking on and off, sometimes it gets me a little wrong, so setting it myself would be cool.

The Bad:
The music control works great, but there's no way to pin the music control to the top of the watch, or have it stay there after the screen turns off. So you have to scroll through two menus to get there. I wish there was a way for the watch face to change when music is playing so I could control from there.

Doesn't sync up with Google Fit. I prefer to keep all my data in Google Fit so I can access it from everywhere. If they could implement that, it would be pretty cool.

The app is fine but could use some clean up. It also takes a while to sync some things, like sleep, which it often doesn't sync for me until later in the afternoon. It asks for a LOT of permissions, which I'm not super comfortable with, but that's up to you and your comfort level.

All in all, if you are looking for a stylish fitness tracker with pretty much everything you need at a sub $30 price, this is probably the right one for you. It's not perfect, but at the price point, it's pretty darn close!

Update: I have lowered my score from two stars to one. I received an email from the company and they offered to send me a pair of their wireless headphones in order for me to change my review and make it sound better. I told them that I would increase the star rating by one star and explain that my reason for raising the star was for excellent customer service. Great for a novelty, but not much else. I really wanted to like this watch but I can't I do. It definitely isnt the best looking watch, but for the price I was willing to deal with not to mention you can change the straps. My biggest issues is the battery life and fitness abilities. I have drained the battery and recharged it fully just like they suggested but it didn't do much if anything. If you are using this for just a simple smart watch you should be able to get a full day out of it. That is if you are only receiving notifications. If you are responding to notification or taking calls you might get a full work day. Don't plan on using this watch to track any kind of fitness activity. You are luck to get through you workout. Listening to stored music during a one hour workout with GPS going killed the battery 70%. I even had wifi and the heartrate off. Streaming music through wifi and tracking a workout without without the heart rate monitor seem to burn the entire battery in 40 minutes. I will say though, the heart rate monitor impressed me, and even the pedometer was decent. For cardio activities it was about 95% when I compared it to a blue tooth monitor. To be fair I will say I am an avid gym goer. 6 days a weeks 1.5 hours a day, but would recommend this as a fitness tracker even to someone who is just trying to get back into shape. If you are just trying to get notifications on a watch you might like it.
