Review:
The best option out there is you want a smartwatch that just works.

Let me start by first telling you about my history with smart watches. I first bought the original pebble used on eBay a few years ago and like it, but it was so ugly that just a short time later I bought the original pebble steel. I am a firefighter and I have an app on my phone that sends me an alert anytime we get a call. It often times comes over the app a minute or two before dispatch even sends it over the radio. With that being said, I work really long shifts, sometimes up to 48 hours straight, depending on overtime. Yes, I could charge my watch throughout that time frame, but honestly I just don't want to worry about it. Oh, and by the way, this watch is totally water proof, which is a must for a firefighter.

So, recently my original pebble steel broke. I was dead set on moving up to a "nicer" watch, meaning full color lcd screen, preferably round, etc. So, I started reading reviews about the apple watch, android wear, and the 3 newest pebble models. I really wanted an apple watch after reading the reviews, but it was just too expensive. So I got the pebble time on sale at best buy for $129.99. I really like it, but it had one major flaw. The screen is completely unreadable in low light situations without using the backlight, and even in medium light, it can be a pain. Then, target had $100 off the apple watch, putting the one I wanted at $300. So I immediately returned the pebble time and bought the apple watch. Boy is it fancy! Very nice looking and so many apps. But, the apps are nothing short of totally unnecessary in a smart watch. I liked the apple watch, but it was so damn complicated to do anything on there. Yes, I could reply to text messages with my voice, but it was more infuriating than anything else. I returned the apple watch mostly because I was just getting annoyed by the software, I needed something simple, and the touch screen was impossible to use safely while driving. Oh, and it doesn't have a spotify app, so no music controls there.

After returning the apple watch, and waiting a few days, I realized I really miss having a smart watch, especially at work. So I went to best buy once again and picked up the pebble time round. It was totally awesome, even more awesome than the pebble time steel for sure. But it also had one major flaw, in a 24 hour shift, I had to charge the thing twice, and once again when i got home in the morning. Totally unacceptable. Enter the pebble time steel.

So there it is, the pebble time steel in the best of all worlds. It is nice looking, water proof, easy to use, comfortable, and best part is... I have had it on my wrist for 10 days, and it is still at 30% battery life! Totally amazing. I really wish it was round like the Pebble time round, because that watch looks great and the display seems a little better, but, the best choice is definitely the pebble time steel.

Please Pebble, I beg you, release a pebble time round steel combo that is a little thicker for at least a 7 day battery, and 100% water proof! I would buy that watch in a heartbeat!

Pebble Time Steel for the win!

Review:
I read a lot of reviews before pulling the trigger on the 910. Part of the reason for my hesitation was the rather mixed reviews and part of it was the fact that I already own a Forerunner 305 and an Edge 305 and aside from swimming, it was hard to justify the money to add only the swimming. The tipping point was talking to other triathletes who have gotten the device and love it. I am very glad I made the leap.

For reference, I have logged 334 activities on Garmin Connect (this is to add credence to my review, not to suggest anything about my waistline)using this and my prior devices. I have used the 910xt for a few weeks for lap swimming, running, and cycling. While the swimming functionality is great and I have benefited from concentrating on things other than counting laps and strokes, this device simply does EVERYTHING BETTER. I don't know all of the changes that have been made, but it seems to pick-up satellites more quickly, recognize the HR monitor more quickly, and while the others sometimes seemed way off on the heart rate (I am pretty sure that if my pulse was 240, I'd know it), the 910 has had no accuracy issues that I have noticed. The battery life is far better and the wireless sync has worked flawlessly.

For someone who has never used a Garmin device, there may be a learning curve and frustration with the product. I would assure you, however, that if you have used and generally like the Garmin platform you will love the 910.

Review:
As much of a techi as I am, I have held off on buying a smart watch since they came out. I did a lot of research among different smartwatches and OS's (wear OS vs Tizen) and finally decided on the TicWatch Pro. I'm glad I did, because so far the experience has exceeded my expectations. The interface is easy to learn and the watch is responsive. The watch's sensors seem to work well - heart rate tracking, being able to figure out what kind of lifting exercise I'm doing at the gym. Battery life has been exceptional due to essential mode being active unless you are looking at the watch directly. I've had no issues with it being able to communicate with my android phone and features like the speakerphone work well. Build quality is great and thus far I haven't seen any reason to replace the watch strap that comes with it - it looks like it's a hybrid leather / silicone strap that looks good but is useful for working out.

Overall, I'm very happy with the TicWatch Pro - it's one of the few full featured Wear OS watches that you can buy for a very reasonable price that looks good. Recommended!

Review:
This smart watch works great as a fitness tracker – for me. But not so great as a smart phone.
I have an LG Stylo 5 android phone – I didn’t have any problems getting the watch and the phone to connect for calling purposes or transmitting fitness information to the app.
But I didn’t have any luck getting the phone to connect to my contact list or text messages. That was a bit disappointing to me – because the last fitness tracker I had worked without a problem.
I’ve worn it for 3 nights now – recorded sleep each night, it’s recorded my steps without a problem – the watch is comfortable.
I charged it the day I opened it – 3 days later the battery is still at 60% - at this rate, it may last 6 days – that’s my estimate.
Changing watch faces is easy, navigating all the menus on the watch was easy to learn, I think it took me about 10 mins before I was comfortable that I could find everything I needed to.
I don’t know where the actual list of compatible devices is.
I also think that the issue with not getting text messages could very well be an issue with operator error and not necessarily with the watch itself.
Overall – for the cost – if you are looking for a fitness tracker ONLY – this fits the bill. But if you are looking for something that works exactly as effectively as a apple watch or fit bit, this might not be a good fit for you.
I will keep using it because I like the fitness data, and sleep stats.

Upgrading my rating to 5 star - As I figured - the reason I wasn't getting text notifications on my watch was because I missed something -
I attached a new picture of the app - circled push notifications - in the next menu - ALL the options for apps to send notifications are default to OFF -
I am used to my fitbit where I had to turn them all off if I didn't want them - this is opposite. I turned on SMS notifications and tested it - and got my text message.
This watch now does everything I want it to do!

Review: TOZO S1 is an affordable and capable smartwatch with heartrate monitoring, sleep and activity tracking. It is very easy and straightforward to pair it with your phone once you download and install the Tozo smartwatch app. With its superb and colorful screen and notification capabilities, it is much better than the other major brand tracker I own and at a fraction of the cost. Battery life is great: A full charge lasts slightly more than 6 days with automatic screen turn-on. I have now turned that feature off to see if the battery lasts ~10 days . But even ~6 days without recharging is pretty impressive, considering all the notifications and heartrate monitoring. Unlike other Android based smartwatches, software is limited, meaning there are no 3rd party apps or watchfaces for the device. The phone app is not too bad but not as polished as the other major brand tracker. All tracking capabilities (Step, heart-rate, and sleep) appear to be accurate. You can manually track outdoor running, walking, basketball playing and mountaineering, as well as setup alarms and a stop watch, control your music player and the camera on your phone, check your pulse on-demand, and check the weather conditions. Navigating through the screens is intuitive and I like the notifications screen the most (you get to choose which notifications you like to see on your watch). Overall, this is really a an affordable activity tracker with smartwatch capabilities.

Also confirmed that built in calendar/email programs do alert to phone, just not third party apps. -- The Review --

So been looking for a good analog/digital watch plus 100M water rating for my daily watch. Since I knew I was going to be spending upwards of $200 thought I'd look into the smart watch arena. I thought this would be perfect but it really misses out on the smart watch part. I love the look and the feel and all the watch parts, but the alerts are lacking. Here are the details:

The physical:

So far I LOVE the looks of the watch. It's a little bigger than what I was hoping for but, overall, still well within reason. It's about double the mass of my old Timex Expedition watch (which was long overdue for replacement, about 15 years overdue), but the watch face is still smaller than the width of my wrist which is good. I have pretty narrow wrists for a 6'+ person (thin boned) so most modern design watches tend to look GIANT on my wrist. This looks big, but not to big. Mostly it's just thick, but again, not to bad, and I'll easily get used to it. So given all that, I love that the watch looks modern, classic, classy, not flashy, and solid. It's a watch that on first look you wouldn't guess it's got any electronic brains, nor connected to a smart-phone. It just looks classy. The wife approves the look.

Big bonus (and a must requirement for me) is the 100M (10ATM) water rating. That means no fears about swimming pools and light water recreation. No diving, no water skiing, but swimming and shallow snorkeling no problem. Showers, baths, and doing dishes shouldn't even be a second thought.

Finally, when alerts aren't displayed, the phone shows the digital time and date. This was another big plus, even ignoring the smart watch part, it's very hard to find a good looking analog watch that also shows a digital clock! This feature alone helps improve my overall rating for this watch. There is also a back-light for viewing at night, which is a little weak, but usable. finally the hands and markings of the analog watch glow in the dark, so that helps in low light as well.

The Smarts (mostly)

So I wanted a new classy watch, but figured if I was going to spend a fair amount on it I wanted it to work with my phone. Ideally all I really wanted was: Caller ID, SMS/MMS Alerts, Remote Music Control. So far I'll give the watch 3 out of 4 on this. Acceptable, see below for details:

This is on a Nexus 5 phone with stock KitKat 4.4.3 (not rooted)

Caller ID = Works and seems to be reliable, contact details are shown, or phone number if not in contacts. Clear alert with command button on caller ID detail page of watch. Can also silence ringer/vibrating alert on watch and phone by pressing the command key while it is ringing, very nice touch. You can still answer the call after silencing the ringer if you choose.

SMS/MMS Alerts = Works now with latest updates, kinda. Of multiple SMS messages all seem to alert, but because KitKat uses Hangouts for SMS/MMS you have to enable Social alerts not TXT alerts (ugh). Clear alert with command button on caller ID detail page of watch seems to have been disabled, they clear when you clear it on the phone.

Social Alerts = Only Hangouts or Google+ messages. I use plume for twitter/facebook and those alerts aren't triggering on the phone. The cogito app sees the hangout alerts but not the plume alerts. To be fair plume is a third party app, so maybe the issue is there.

Mail Alerts = Default imap/pop mail client and gmail app both trigger alerts on the phone, so the basics work. And with the latest update K-9 Mail triggers alerts as well, and that's a third party app.

Event Alerts = The built in google calendar app alerts as expected. Unfortunately again, I use a third party calender and those alerts don't show up on the cogito phone app or on the watch.

Alarm Alerts = Works and seems to be reliable. Major annoyance is that the phone alerts on androids pre-alarm an alert 2 hours early. When an alarm is going to trigger the phone pops up a silent alert that you have an upcoming alarm in 2 hours. Unfortunately the watch triggers and audio alert (even in silent mode) for the alarm. Very annoying if your alarm isn't supposed to wake you up until 6 am and your watch is waking you up at 4 am. Had to disable event alerts to avoid this.

Alarm/Event Alert tie-together = Since Alarm and Event alerts are controlled by the same toggle you can't have one with out the other. Because of the 2 hour early alerts for my morning alarms, I can't leave Event alerts enabled. Big bug here.

Battery Alerts = Not tested with watch battery, but if the phone get's low you see that alert on the watch.

Music Control = Kinda works, I have figured out how to pause and resume playback, but that's it for now (command button).

Lost Phone = Works, press command and left page at the same time and the phone cookoos!

Lost Watch = Works, tap the lost watch icon and the watch starts beeping.

Phone Watch Separation = Doesn't work, can't enable option, it's grayed out on the phone app.

Remote Camera operation = Works, quick press of he command button takes a picture using a basic camera app, must launch the camera app through the cogito app on the phone. Gives the option of using the front or back camera.

Documentation of all this functionality is pretty sparse, some of it I've had to just figure out on my own. They do have an instruction booklet on their website which explains things a little better, but still lacks some of the functions I've mentioned above. That booklet should have been included with the product.

I really love the "watch" and the "smart" part has improved sine I first purchased it. Overall, I'm very happy with the watch now.

Review: I previously owned a Fitbit One which by virtue of the "belt clip" had met its demise by being left clipped on my pant and going through the washer and dryer.  I recently purchase the Garmin Vivofit 2.  I’m over 50 and one of the majors selling points on the Garmin over the Fitbit was the constant display of steps and of course being able to see the display without glasses.  The Fitbit display is somewhat smaller and you have to press a button to have the steps displayed.  When I received my Garmin, I did notice immediately that it was very delayed and logging steps and sometimes didn't seem to appear to log any steps at all.  I used the Garmin Vivofit 2 on an elliptical  for three miles.  When I observed the number of steps, it fell below the number of steps that I had expected.  I quickly decided, this was not acceptable and sucked it up and purchased another Fitbit One as well as a wristband.  Using both the Garmin Vivofit 2 and the Fitbit One on the same wrist, I did three miles on the elliptical. When I finished the Garmin reported 3712 steps and the Fitbit reported 4736 steps.  After a bit more comparison, the Garmin consistently logs in excess of 1000 steps less than the Fitbit.  If you are looking for accuracy, I’d look at the Fitbit.

Review: Initially, I was was set on buying the Galaxy Gear smart watch and so I went to my local Best Buy to get one.  Fortunately, for me they did not have any in stock and said that if I wanted one I would need to wait a week or so to get one.  Nonetheless, there was a few display watches that I could play with.  I tried the Galaxy Gear on and immediately noticed the how bulky it was on my wrist.  Moreover, it wasn't as stylish in person as I had thought it to be, and it looked awkward to the point that I knew that every time someone looked at my wrist, I would have to explain what the hell the thing on my wrist was.  I don't have time like that in my day.  So the next day I saw that Sony would be releasing its new smartwatch, the Sony Smartwatch 2.  It was 100 dollars cheaper and it looked much more like a watch rather than a device on my wrist.  I also found out that it was water resistant where I could wear it in the shower and could wear it while washing dishes.  The main selling point for me on this watch was the amount of time that a charge could be held without having to recharge the watch (around 2-3 days).  Moreover, this was a third generation product, meaning that there were 2 other previous older versions of the watch that Sony could improve on, which meant they had developed more apps for the watch plus the watch would work with any Android phone running 4.0 or better so if I wanted to change phones I could do that without having to think about my watch being obsolete.  All these benefits were things that the Samsung Galaxy Gear could not provide me.  With that being said, I went into a Sony store and bought a black one with a metal band that costs 20 dollars extra. Overall, I would say that I am somewhat satisfied with the product.  Satisfied in the sense that it is a watch and its stylish, something that a watch should be.  Also, as advertised the phone holds excellent time between charges and the notifications come in pretty much on cue. And, its useful when driving when you can't check your phone, because one can also make calls from the watch, very useful when the phone is connected to the car via bluetooth.  What I don't like about the watch is that it lacks a music app so that I can run Pandora or Spotify through it, apps which I use frequently use.  Instead it gives you a generic Google music app which is very laggy and is very limited.  Additionally, occasionally the watch freezes up and when its functional the watch frequently doesn't respond to swipes making you swipe numerous times for the watch to function.  Further, the bluetooth range is limited to about 25-30 feet max.  So if you live in a bigger home then expect your watch to drop in and out of connection frequently.  It also can't answer calls on your phone from the watch unless you have a bluetooth head set or a Sony smartphone, which kind of annoyed me.  Overall, as a watch the Smartwatch is still expensive, but when compared to the Galaxy Gear, is a more practical and functional device.  I didn't need a video camera/camera on my watch unless I: 1. needed to cheat on a test 2. do something perverted with it.  Additionally I didn't want to look stupid answering my phone from my wrist and talking to my wrist.  So I believe I made the right choice between the two.  But, unless you really want a smart watch I would advise you to wait for Apple or Google to take a shot at it so you can make a better decision, because the smart watch market is limited as of now.

Review:
I've owned Garmin stuff for the past 10 years. Their hardware is good, software, not so much. Always seems to be something that works, then doesn't. Each new update fixes one thing and messes up another. Customer service is overwhelmed with issues, always busy, and largely unhelpful/dismissive.

As for the 520, it worked pretty well for me. I prefer the buttons to touch screen so I liked that. The UI is difficult and has you pressing buttons and cycling through screens for the most common of tasks. For example, trying to zoom in or out on a nav map requires about 4 or 5 steps each time. Setting up data pages is an endless process of selecting the data you want from endless menus. By the time you're done, your fingers are sore from button pushing. So, when I lost my 520 earlier this year and was looking for a replacement, my frustrations with the software and UI lead me to buy the new Wahoo Element Bolt.

At first I really liked the Bolt, it was easy to set up via my smartphone. Zoom in and out feature was a touch of a button. Data pages also easy to set up via phone and change them on the fly. Screen was OK, although I liked the 520's color screen better. Unfortunately, I found that their software is also glitchy. Even worse, the build quality was not on par with the 520. The plastic used for the unit had a cheap toy gun feel to it. Worst of all was that after only 3 months the screen fried. This happened just before the Christmas holiday where I had planned to do a lot of longer rides. I contacted customer service on the 23rd and as of today, the 31st, I have no answer about what they intend to do about the problem. Not what I expect from a company's customer service, especially when it is clear their product was faulty.

So... having been disappointed by both Garmin and Wahoo, I've decided to make do with my smartphone as my computer. If I eventually get a new computer, I'll probably go back to Garmin. Glitchy software seems to be the norm, but it can be fixed or worked around. Bad hardware is a deal breaker.

Review:
So far I love this watch. I've had it for a little over a week now and am satisfied it. The smart alarm works surprisingly well, it will wake you up sometime in the half hour before you set it by vibrating when it detects you're in a lighter stage of your sleep cycle. The connectivity is great, that pebble app store seems pretty sparce at first, but the more you look, the deeper it goes. It's smaller than I was expecting, I've had bigger regular watches. I was pleasantly surprised at how low profile it is. It's still on the bigger side as compared to a 'normal' watch, but not big enough it catches on sleeve or things like that. Being able to customize each button for what a long press will do is nice. The screen is very clear even in direct sunlight with polarized sunglasses on, and at night if you can't remember which button is the back light, just tapping the screen will light it up (kudos to whoever programmed that). The menus are fairly intuitive and it's easy to turn off notifications for apps with a few button presses. the band feels like a standard sports band, but it works great. The sleep tracking seems like it's fairly accurate compared to the basis watch I had years ago.

At the end of the day, it's a durable, waterproof smart watch with a long battery life. That's really all I wanted. Everything else that came with is a bonus. I was looking for a way to change music without digging out my phone in the car, long battery life, notifications, and wasn't a brick. The pebble is all of these. Does it have the functionality of an apple or similar? Definitively not, but it has the basic controls, notifications and customization I was looking for.

Now the negatives. The heart rate monitor is crap. It works if you switch to the screen and hold still, but passively it's all over the place. while writing this I just checked it again, it jumped from 140 to 95 before settling down to about 85. Looking back through the app, on days I know I was pretty sedentary it showed my heart rate hitting mid 100s. I've used fitness trackers with HR monitoring, there's a certain tightness you have to wear the watch at, but for whatever reason this one seems to not provide consistent data.

Now the big one: fitbit bought pebble. There's not going to be much chance of warranty or replacement. The app store is only guaranteed until the end of 2017. They may or may not update anything after that. All that being said, I like this watch and will probably continue using it until it breaks or is no longer compatible with my phone. If they stop making apps, honestly that's fine with me, and it's opens source if you want to continue making your own. The heart hate is not worth it, the pebble time is probably the best watch they made overall if you want, but for $70, this a great choice for a connected watch.

Review:
This was really a hard decision on rating this watch as a 4 or 5 star.In the end, I gave it a four because although it is a really nice and functional smartwatch, it has things to improve on to make it better. I own a Samsung Gear S2 3G wireless and the former Pebble Steel (PS) which are also great watches too. Like all Pebbles, the Pebble Time Steel (PTS) doesn't forget that it is a watch first and does not attempt to miniaturize your smartphone to your wrist. With exception to the Pebble Round (PR), all Pebbles have incredible battery life. To me, this is one of the biggest selling points which the PTS has going for it. I HATE having to charge a device every night. I cant see the use of having all of the cool features of some of the other smartwatches when you find out how fast they drain your battery and leave you stranded before the day is done. You wind up having to put settings lower or turning them off to conserve power so whats the point of having them?? I usually get around 8-9 days of moderate to heavy use with the PTS which is 2-3 days better than what the PS does. Both my PTS and PS are very well constructed and very simple to use. Now making a change between a PTS and a PS is something which requires a lot of thought. IMHO, I think the PS is a better value and has its own strengths which some could consider better or equal to the PTS. Bottom line is you need to know what you want from a smartwatch be it features, function, looks, cost or any combination of such. To me, I always come back to the PTS or PS as my "go to" watches. I do love the Samsung Gear S2 3G but for entirely different reasons. So here are the pros/cons for PTS:

PTS Pros
- Built in microphone
- Super battery life
- Excellent metal case construction
- quick release pins for fast strap changes
- Super fast operating system, pairs easy, and very reliable
- Water resistancet to I believe 3 ATM
- Very easy to operate with buttons and no touchscreen
- Tons of apps and watch faces for free or pay
- Its a good bargain if you get it on sale under suggested manufacturer price
PTS Cons
- Not much of a leap from the PS which has the same operating system and is a little slower but not much
- Screen size is to small in relation to the watch face. Its ok but the PS is proportional and looks better
- The band looks cheap! Its constructed fine but sorry it looks terrible to me. I changed it for a Silicon dive strap and it now looks great
- The colored graphics are dull and washed looking. Don't expect eye popping beautiful colors or resolution like a Samsung Gear S2.
- My biggest concern is the brightness or lack there of from the screen. In sunlight its ok and at night if you use the backlight feature its fine, but for low light conditions its terrible. Pebble needs to push an update to give it more brightness. Even though the PS is a black/white graphics display, I have no problem reading it in any light condition. What happened to PTS?? This alone could really be the deal breaker to buy it.

Review:
I've purchased and used the Umidigi Uwatch 2 and Uwatch 3 before and were pleased with those purchases. I was a little puzzled by the numbering of the Uwatch 2S but figured I'd give it a try since the price is right and the other two Umidigi watches worked out well.

As to the model numbering, I think it is numbered 2S because it's firm factor, with the round outline but square screen, mostly matches the 2. It is missing the button on the right side of the watch, which the 2 and 3 have, which probably makes it less awkward to wear on either hand. I was actually concerned about this since I feel safer with a physical button but in the week I've been using the Uwatch 2S, I have had absolutely no trouble turning the watch face on with a little swipe of the face.

Pairing through the phone app on an iPhone X was easy, the Bluetooth has a good range, and repairing after being out of range and bringing the phone and watch close together has been automatic. I have found that I need to run the app after restarting the phone to get the watch to initially pair.

Battery life is terrific. In one week of use counting steps, tracking sleep, and reading notifications, I still have 60% remaining. For me, battery life is a critical component since I don't want to be forced to carry another charger when traveling. My trips never exceed 12 days and the 2S looks like it will easily last at least that long with my usage.

The heart rate monitor has been accurate and the blood pressure function gives me readings about where I expected them to be but I don't know how accurate the reported blood pressure is because I do not have a real time reference to compare it to.

Music controls worked well for me as have the weather and find my phone apps. The sleep monitor hasn't been horribly unreliable most of the time but one night's total sleep reading was definitely way off. It seems health experts generally think that we shouldn't rely too much on watches to measure quality of sleep anyway so this isn't too much of an issue.

Even though I am a light user, I noticed some shortcomings that I saw that might be overcome by playing around with settings or the phone app but I didn't find them. Only the most recent three text messages are kept in the watch and I couldn't find a way to reply from the watch, so you really are notified of the message and that's it. I liked one of the stock watch faces but there aren't many to choose from. The manual is skimpy and doesn't go over the use of the phone app. You need to do some exploring of your own.

There are other features that I don't use (such as workouts) and can't comment on so please take a look at other reviews for feedback on those.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Battery life is terrific. In one week of use counting steps, tracking sleep, and reading notifications, I still have 60% remaining. For me, battery life is a critical component since I don't want to be forced to carry another charger when traveling. My trips never exceed 12 days and the 2S looks like it will easily last at least that long with my usage.

Review:
This watch does reasonably well in displaying all the notifications I want while connected to my galaxy phone, including my preferred email account, telegram, texts, and incoming call alerts. It can be a bit difficult to see past the most recent message in a string. I don't have many issues responding to any of these with the voice recognition or preset replies if its just a few words. You wouldn't reply with a whole sentence on the watch, you would pull out the phone. The battery lasts 2-3 days so it's OK for sleep tracking from that perspective. It seems to track steps and sleep accurately but it tends to separate my nightly sleep into about 3 phases with waking periods in the middle so the watch only displays the last couple hours. I find it a bit too big to be comfortable for sleeping in any case. It's as or more comfortable than the competition I tried for daytime use. The rotating bevel is convenient to go through menus and provides plenty of quick information on steps, floors, weather, calories, etc.

As an exercise tracker it usually basically works but has some issues. The connection to my Yurbuds Liberty headphones is sketchy so the music skips often, especially out in the open. It might work better with headphones that have the receiver on the left ear to better see the left wrist. The GPS tracking works well when it works, but it has failed to track at all sometimes. It only GPS tracks if I set it to start the exercise, not if it has to recognize what I'm doing. If I set it to run, it usually tracks location accurately, plays music somewhat sketchily, and announces mileage, pace, and heart-rate every mile through the headphones. The heart-rate sensing works ok sitting but is worthless on a run and not much use on a bike. It won't utilize my heart rate strap. If I just start walking or running it recognizes the activity in a couple minutes, measures steps, and estimates mileage and calories. It hasn't accurately recognized cycling on it's own.

Strava integration is sketchy at best. It only attempts to upload to Strava if I manually start an activity, which is probably a plus so it's not uploading every dog walk. If I manually start an activity, maybe 10% of the time it will quickly upload to Strava, 80% it uploads within a day or so, 10% it won't share at all. I can manually share a simplified map or pace chart to Strava but the only way to upload full data for a workout is to set Samsung health to automatically share and be happy those times when it works. Confusingly, Strava will show a slightly different time and pace than Samsung did using the data from Samsung. These workouts will always show immediately on the Samsung health app on the phone so I suspect it's an issue with Samsung Health in general and not this watch specifically.

I'm sure a Garmin or Suunto would be a better exercise tracker, a Fitbit a better step and calorie tracker, and there's better smartwatches depending on what phone they are working with. I'm keeping and continuing to use this watch because, though it doesn't work perfectly, it works about the best all-around of any I've found, especially if you want just one, and especially for the price.

Review:
I've received this watch a week ago to test out the features and battery lie in general. Let me tell you this has got to be one of the best first entry smart watches ( even if it was made in China), that has a few good features that are on it. Firstly, you can use this as a phone on its own; however, you will need a 2g sim in order for it to work, but you can make BT calls if you pair this with you phone (have it with you at all times) to make calls that way as well as messages. Secondly, you can pair your wireless headphones or pods with this watch as long you have music on your Micro SD card (yes you can insert a micro SD card to store pictures, music, and possibly videos). Thirdly, it has a anti lost feature to find your phones if lost. Fourthly, it has a pedometer to track your steps in a workout and as well as a sleep monitor to track your best quality of sleep. Fifthly, it has a sedentary function to remind you to get up at a certain time to make you be alert. Lastly, the battery life is great it can last in about 3 to 4 days as long as you keep the brightness down and use the less frequently to check time or playing music, but if your a heavy user, except it to last a day or 2, the standby time lasts about a week.

I would recommend this watch for everyone to start of with an entry level smartwatch before setting of buying a mid or high tier smartwatch. you get what you pay for.

Review: I ordered this watch while on a military deployment after finding that my fitbit wouldn't update unless it had Wifi and could not change the time without it.  Traveling on ship and changing time zones every other night I needed a watch that was not so reliant on needing wifi all the time.  The TicWatch was everything I was looking for.  First and foremost I was able to use it as a watch which is only common sense.  The connectivity with my LG G6 Phone is outstanding.  The apps and systems are amazing and very well developed.  As an Engineer working in various machinery spaces in enviroments where the coolest areas are 110 degrees this watch has been proven very durable.  Overall it has the potential to be the best smart watch for the money!.. On the down side, the battery life was decent given that it was only on Bluetooth when listening to music on my phone.  The only other thing I would pry at is the fact it does not have a sleep tracker.  Everything was perfect until a week or two from coming home.  My watch stopped charging at 60%.  Hoping it was just an issue with the charger I placed it in essential mode and ordered replacement chargers to be waiting for me at home when I returned.  To give credit the watch stayed on in essential mode for two weeks all the way until I arrived home.  I tried the replacement chargers I bought but they failed to charge my watch again.  Now I have a dead watch just sitting at home.  Other than it failing to charge, while it worked, it was one of the best smart watches I have ever had!

Review:
This is a very interesting watch.
Inside the box, it comes with two different bands: One in rubber, and the other in matte black metal.
Getting it fitted was a minimal process, and overall, it isn't too bulky - with the exception of the buttons.
The buttons are big and easily get snagged on sleeves, so be mindful of this.

Otherwise, the watch is rather nice.
The basic parameters say 7-10 days of battery life without a charge, and this seems accurate.
It has a 360x360 pixel screen (round), and there are a large number of different faces available to download, up to and including options to create your own. The display real-estate function well as a timepiece, but active navigation of different apps and widgets is more difficult, especially where menu scrolling is concerned. Sometimes, scrolling is overly sensitive and other times, the first few items in the menu aren't responsive at all, unless you scroll down, and then back up to refresh them.
Have not tried to Bluetooth it to my phone yet.

Still, for its price, it is competent, and I feel like it just needs a patch update or two to really drive home the potential of this watch.
4/5 stars.

Review:
This is a decent smart watch for the price.

I like the sleek black design, aluminum edges, ceramic back, clear touch screen, and the silicone band.

The watch is fairly responsive. I will say the flick of the wrist to turn on the screen doesn’t always register and that can be cumbersome.

The clock has multiple faces. They are ok, it is nice to have variety. The font on the watch does appear a little dated compared to an Apple Watch or higher end watch but you get what you pay for. It reminds me of an inexpensive Fitbit. It’s military time only which is pretty poor.

The pedometer and heart rate tracking is accurate and a great feature. I love that you can connect your phone, control music, and see text messages. It is very helpful to not have to pull your phone out at work. I do wish it had more exercise modes but that is ok.

The IPX rating is superb which makes it a great workout watch. Battery life is great. It isn’t too bulky, but is for sleeping so I can’t say much about the sleep tracker. It feels too heavy while sleeping so I don’t wear it.

All in all it has great features for the price and it looks sleek (more casual fitness than professional look though.) Downsides are the wrist flicking (I wish the screen just dimmed), you can’t tap to wake, the cheap looking font, the military time only (had to drop a star for that), and the size/weight for sleep. If you are looking for a smart watch at a reasonable price and do not need a ton of bells and whistles and can accept cheaper quality, get this watch. It is a great beginner smart watch.

Review:
I would classify this as a smart watch first and s fitness/health watch second. I've owned 3 different smart watches and this is by far the best for my need. I own a Amazfit and a Garmin VivoSport. The Garmin is a great fitness watch that automatically tracks your heart rate and movement. The Garmin allows the user to configure when it should automatically start activity and does a very nice job monitoring your heart rate. It also integrates very nicely with the Garmin Connect Application which I also use when cycling. However, that is where the features end.

The TicWatch Pro 4G is a all around amazing performer that hits the smart watch niche. This watch has an amazing battery life - I'm currently at the end of day 3 without a charge and still have 23% left. The integration with my phone is exactly what I wanted. I can text from my watch, take phone calls, make phone calls, control music, video, and also track my activity level. Android assistant has worked without fail every time I put it to use.
The size of the phone isn't conducive to wearing to bed often which cuts the sleep cycle value - this is why I reduced the number of stars in sleep tracking. However, the integration with Android "App Store" and the number of applications are great.

I'm glad I went with the TicWatch Pro 4G with additional memory. The watch has never responded slowly or acted unresponsive with the additional RAM. After months of research, I'm glad I purchased this watch. I'm a T-Mobil customer and can't take advantage of the 4G connectivity but the hardware upgrades are worth the extra expense. By far the best watch you buy if you are an Android User. The price is also significantly better than the other watches I seriously considered.

Review:
I am thrilled with this watch but I'll start with the only two cons I can think of:

1. It doesn't have continuous heart rate monitoring like the Fitbit. You have to check your heart rate manually but it does appear to be an accurate reading.
2. The "Sleep Mode" has to be turned on. The FitBit knows when you are going to sleep. I assume it knows that by the heart rate so it stands to reason that the Samsung Fit Gear doesn't recognize it because it isn't constantly monitoring your heart rate.

Now the pros:

1. It looks great! I get compliments on it all the time and people are usually surprised to find out that it's a activity tracker and smart watch. Especially considering there are endless ways to customize the face/readout. You can even switch out the band if you'd like.
2. It's comfortable. I had concerns that it would be uncomfortable but after wearing for a couple of days, I forgot I had it on. I only take it off when it needs to charge. I even shower with on.
3. It has a ton of functionality. It's more than an activity tracker. It tells me who's calling. It shows me emails and texts and it even has available apps specific to the Samsung Fit. In fact, the flashlight is a great app!

The only reason I didn't give it 5 stars is because of the heart rate monitoring. But overall, I love it!

Special Features: Notifications
Review:
This is a great entry-level smartwatch, especially if you're not sure you want to plunk down a ton of money for an iWatch, Android Wear, or even the less-expensive Pebble. All it does is keep time, display notifications, activate "OK GOOGLE" without having to say it, and give you date and weather information. Also, you can use your watch as a camera shutter button. I highly recommend this watch to anyone who isn't sure if they really want a smartwatch or not; if you like it and want to get a Pebble or something else, you can give this to your kids and if they break it, it's not like you spent a ton of money.

BIGGEST PRO:
* Notifies you of literally anything that your phone can. If it's not in the default notification list, there's a way to add it in off a list of EVERY APP ON YOUR PHONE. I mean, if you want your wrist to buzz when your lives are full in Two Dots, this watch can do that easily. Also, you can shut off certain default notifications if you like.

PRO:
* Works great with my Android phone. Says it works with iPhone but I don't have one. Setup was very easy.
* Multiple vibration patterns that you can assign to different apps. Vibration is very strong. (So don't leave the watch on a table because it will startle you.)
* Battery life is good -- I get a couple of days per charge, minimum, and it charges in less than two hours.
* Very good range; I can go to meetings on my floor at work and it stays tethered to my phone, which is back at my desk.
* The watchface is a mechanical, analog watch that you can always see no matter how bright it is outside. If it's dark, press the lower-left button to see the digital clock.

BIGGEST CON:
* The biggest con for me is that the watch sometimes glitches. That is, you've acknowledged a notification on your phone but the watch continuously notifies you of said notification over... and over... and over... The only way to stop this is to disconnect and reconnect it from your phone, which is only a matter of 3-4 taps, but it's still annoying.

LITTLE CONS:
* Charging port is recessed very deep, so you need to use the cable that was included. Don't lose it.
* The included band is rubber, and not super-easy to take off.
* You get EVERY SINGLE NOTIFICATION. So, for example, if you're having a Hangouts conversation and someone sends you four messages in a row, the watch will notify you of EVERY ONE OF THEM, even if you've already acknowledged them on your computer or phone. It's doing what it's supposed to do, but it can get annoying sometimes.
* Not exactly the best looking watchface, and the M at the 25-minutes-past location isn't quite perfectly aligned with the notification screen. As a designer, that irks me.
* The LED really only works to tell you the phone is charging, that it needs to be charged, and as a very weak flashlight.
* No sleep, step, calorie, heartbeat, exercise, or GPS tracking. The sensors aren't even in the watch. Still, buying this plus a Fitbit Flex is cheaper than a Pebble.
* The app sits in my notification shade on my phone and cannot be swiped away while the watch is tethered. A status bar icon would be welcome.

I looked for a long time to purchase a GPS watch for my 18 yr old son who runs Cross Country. After hours of reviewing I found the Ticwatch E to be the best watch for the combination of GPS tracking and apps for under $200 ... and this watch was only $130. We received it and it feels durable and has a great selection of apps to choose from android wear (no homegrown apps). I also noticed that all of the other watches in this price range only have notification sync with your smart phone but no ability to initiate calls or texts and even receives "ok Google" commands. The biggest complaint I read about the Ticwatch E was battery life. It says it is only about 1.5 days. While that may not compare with the low graphics GPS trackers, it is standard for high graphics smartwatches. If you want apps, you will give up battery life. My iWatch gets about 1.5 days, so that seems normal to me.
Very happy so far. If we run into problems I will update this post.

Review:
Honestly, this is my first smartwatch. A.) I bought the watch since it's relatively cheap when comparing it to other smartwatches and B.) I also went with this model since I was looking for a decent fitness tracker.

My reasoning for making the purchase was to see which camp I fall under. Am I someone that wants a basic fitness tracker or do I want something that has the functionality of a smartphone on my wrist? After having the watch for a couple of weeks, I can see there's a lot of promise, but there are a few problems I've found with the Ticwatch S.

1st problem I noticed is the battery life. Battery life is short enough if the screen is set to "always on," but even with it switched in the other mode where the screen only comes on when you lift your wrist isn't enough to really justify the battery life. I've heard of some people getting over 24 hours on one charge, but the average for me has been closer to 14- 16 hours. Obviously, the battery life drastically shortens (8- 10 hours) when using any of the fitness or gps tracking features.

EDIT TO PROBLEM 1: I started to return the watch, but I decided to do some tinkering around with the help of Mobvoi's customer service reps. There are 3 things you can do to vastly improve battery life.

SOLUTION 1: Disable wifi on the watch. If you're connected via bluetooth to your phone, you honestly don't need wifi enabled on the watch.
SOLUTION 2: Keep the watch face simple (I think this applies to any smartwatch). If you have a watch face that has an animated background, this can slowly suck the life from your watch.
SOLUTION 3: Turn off "always listening" for ok google. Yeah, saying "ok google" to launch the google assistant can be handy, but it's just as quick if not quicker to just hold the side button to launch google assistant.

Since I've done all of those things, my battery easily makes it more than a day and a half (big improvement from the 12- 18 hours I was originally getting.

2nd problem is the amount of time it takes for the screen to display the time when bringing your wrist up. Most basic trackers (I own a virgin pulse max buzz) almost instantly display the time when raising your wrist, but with the Ticwatch S it feels like it takes around 1.50- 2.00 seconds for the time to display. It doesn't seem bad, but when comparing how quickly other devices are able to display the time or notifications, the latency in the display coming to life can feel like an eternity.

3rd problem involves random hiccups with the smartwatch making or receiving calls. I tried calling my wife using my smartwatch and when she didn't answer the phone on the 1st try, I promptly tried calling her with the smartwatch again. Except this time, there was no audio coming through the watch (except for this rapid fire static sound) and there was no indication a call was being made. I tried using my LG v20 to make a call instead, but suddenly my smartphone wasn't working. Come to find out, my Ticwatch S was constantly redialing my wife's number and her phone would not stop ringing until I switched off the watch.

4th issue I had noticed is that the default fitness tracker doesn't hold up when its checking your heart rate (if you're sitting at a desk using the heart rate function provided by Mobvoi or Google Fit, it takes around 90 seconds for it to display your heart rate). It's massively inconsistent in the gym, but that could be due to my heart rate changing too quickly for the Ticwatch S to properly keep up. But I will admit the GPS tracking feature (the one by Mobvoi, not Google Fit) works remarkably well. I've used it for a couple of hikes and it did a near perfect job tracking my mileage and pace (heart rate did better since it was constant vs. varied).

EDIT TO 4TH PROBLEM: Just like a fitbit, I figured that it works better to move the watch slightly up on your wrist. Since I had done this, the watch does a much better job at tracking my heart rate. And my heart rate seems to be spot on with the heart rate tracker on any cardio equipment I've used.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: battery life is short enough if the screen is set to "always on," but even with it switched in the other mode where the screen only comes on when you lift your wrist isn't enough to really justify the battery life. I've heard of some people getting over 24 hours on one charge, but the average for me has been closer to 14- 16 hours. Obviously, the battery life drastically shortens (8- 10 hours) when using any of the fitness or gps tracking features. There are 3 things you can do to vastly improve battery life. Disable wifi on the watch. Keep the watch face simple. Turn off "always listening" for ok google. Since I've done all of those things, my battery easily makes it more than a day and a half.

The battery should last over 24 hours on a single charge. The battery life should not drastically shorten when using fitness or GPS tracking features. It is acceptable if disabling Wi-Fi, keeping the watch face simple, and turning off "always listening" are required to achieve more than a day and a half of battery life.

Review:
This is a very nice smartwatch. The metal band is elegant. There are not many smartwatches that you can wear with dress clothes. This is one that you could wear to the gym or to church, and it wouldn’t look out of place. No silicone band and cheap looking plastic bezel here.
This is a generic smartwatch. Basically unbranded. Undoubtedly made in China, but don’t fool yourself - the name brand watches come from China as well. The problem with the lack of a name brand means that there is probably not a lot of tech support. This is not a huge problem for me, since I look more for features than product support and a large community of users.
What attracted me to this particular watch is its impressive set of features and low price point. Look, it has a GPS, pedometer, heart rate monitor, and sleep monitor. All of these are pretty standard, even in the bargain basement smartwatches. However, this one also has an O2 sensor and blood pressure monitor. These are usually only available on the higher end, high-priced watches. Also, they are fairly accurate as far as I can tell. I have a fingertip pulse oximeter and a home wrist blood pressure monitor, and the watch gave me numbers that were pretty comparable. Bear in mind that you are not going to get the same accuracy as medical equipment, but it should get you in the ballpark. It is getting its data from reading your wrist. I found it acceptably reliable.
Sleep tracking is hit and miss. To me it would work a lot better if you could tell the watch when you sleep and when you wake up. As it is, it tries to figure it out from reading your heart rate and detecting your lack of motion. I work swing shift, and often when I sleep during the day, it doesn’t think I’ve slept. Other times, it will detect me sitting in the recliner for a few hours in the evening as sleep.
Exercise and workout tracking are also a little dicey. There are 8 sports modes, but none of them cover what I do. I do home workouts - yoga, bodyweight exercises, kettlebells, and rowing machine, but none of the sport modes work for what I do. I set it to “Walking” or “Jumping”, and half the time it tells me I haven’t moved enough, so it doesn’t save my data. Again, if I could just tell it WHEN I exercise and have it record the duration of my workout and give me a graph of my heart rate, I’d be happy. Alas, eight sport modes don’t help me.
The watch pairs with my phone easily, and there are all the usual features as far as phone controls. You can get notifications, texts, and even use the watch for music controls. I don’t use any of that too much.
So, this is a very nice watch at a very decent price with a lot of high end features. The build quality and hardware are comparable to much more expensive watches. The downside is the app and the software. When you buy a name brand watch, you buy into their ecosystem and get a much better app with a larger user community, more frequent updates, and tech support. This watch has the “DaFit” app which, to be honest, is underwhelming. I’ve had a couple of other smartwatches including a Pebble and Amazfit, and the apps and community support are much better with the name brands. There are a limited number of watch faces available on the app, and the app has a limited number of options. Not a large user community either, so you won’t find a lot of resources on the web. On the plus side, it will integrate with Apple Fitness and Google Fit, so you can use the tracking data.
Battery life is pretty good for all of the features on the device. I get about a week between charges. Better than the Galaxy watch or Apple Watch that needs to be charged every day.
As I said, very nice looking, feature-filled watch for the money. Five stars for the looks, but I’m taking a star away for the (pretty awful app and lack of community support.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: about a week between charges
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The smart watch needs to last about a week between charges.

Review:
UPDATE: Alright, so when I initially wrote this review the music component of this watch was meh for me. I had a love/hate relationship with it but now I can honestly say I love it and have upped my review to 5 stars (from 4 before). I do think Garmin listened and made changes with the updates or maybe it was just user error all along???? I honestly don't know lol. I've had this watch for 8 months now and I freaking love it. I now know how to update the music via wifi and I do think the connectivity on my runs has improved. How well it connects to Garmin connect has also improved. Do I think it's worth the $345 price its being sold at right now? Maybe. It's amazing not being able to take my phone on my run and still having music, GPS, and some sort of security to reach my loved ones should I need it. I do wish the battery life was a little longer but for the size of this thing, I'm not complaining and it also charges pretty quickly. I have not yet run long enough (w/music) to test it with an external battery but will update as I do that.

For those looking for instructions on how to update music via wifi (this was one of the things I wanted the watch to do and either I didn't know how to or it didn't work that well at first), I will include instructions below:
Hold down second/middle button on left side of watch.
Click music
Click music providers
Click Spotify (should walk you thru how to set if up if you don't)
For me it takes me to the last song played. From here, I hit the three dots on the left.
Click library
Then scroll down to where you can hit update downloads.
This will not work if you do not have your watch connected to wifi. To do that do this:
Click and hold the middle button on the left side.
Scroll down to WiFi and select.
Should walk you thru it. I think you have to make sure your phone is connected to the same wifi.

I received this as a surprise gift from my husband about a month and a half ago. Both my husband and I are avid Garmin users and have been for several years. For context, these are the Garmin watches I have owned in the past: Forerunner 15, Vivosmart 3, and Vivomove HR. I do like this watch. In fact, you can say I love it. Based on our purchase history, I can see my husband purchased this for ~$50 less than what it is being listed as now ($345). Personally, if I was buying this for myself I would get a refurbished one or do without the music. I run about 3-4x a week and do HIIT workouts 3x a week. For what I do, this watch may be overkill but that’s what you want right? You want a watch that will do what you want AND more. At least I do!

So, what are my gripes with this watch?
1- Bluetooth. Boy, oh boy, oh boy! My watch LOVES to disconnect from my headphones. If my hands aren’t at the right angle while I am running, it is game over and I have to reconnect or deal with a patchy connection. At this point, I just don’t bother to use the music on my watch. I am hopeful an update will fix this but I’m not holding my breath. I find mine struggles with auto-sync on the Garmin app occasionally as well.

2-Music-In relation to the bluetooth is the music concept of this watch. I really wanted to love this aspect of the watch. I really really did. I was so pumped about it! But alas, you can’t always get what you want. Syncing Music the first time took an eternity and half. You also have to manually update it if you update your playlist on Spotify, I think (at least that’s what I have to do). I wish Garmin would do something where it would automatically have your watch update the playlist if it is connected to Wi-Fi with my laptop open to Spotify but maybe my standards are too high (my iPad does this with playlists I’ve downloaded). Overall, I do like the music component of this watch-I just wish the bluetooth worked better. Many of the reviews I read on here are people complaining about how difficult it is to sync music and I agree. I consider myself pretty tech savvy but they could have made this simpler. I also would like to be able to change the songs that I am listening to on my phone from my watch. I used to be able to do this on my Vivomove HR and that was lovely while I was running. Also, please note I *think* you have to have a premium subscription to whatever music streaming service you choose to use in order to connect to the watch. I already had Spotify premium so this was no big deal to me but wanted to note that on here. I think you can also upload your own mp3 files if you have them but don’t take my word for it as I haven’t tried it.

3-Sleep tracking- I don’t find the sleep tracking to be very accurate as a whole but I think that is an issue with Garmin in general so I’m not really holding it against this watch.

4-Garmin Connect IQ- Changing my watch face is a lot harder than it should be and takes forever.

What do I LOVE about this watch?

1- GPS- my last watch did not have GPS (but it was pretty!) and that’s also a big factor in why I wanted this watch. I have not found any issues with the GPS and I have found this to be fairly accurate. Connects seamlessly with Strava which I appreciate.

2-The size- for the size of this watch and all it can do-WOW. Just wow! I was afraid it would be too bulky but it is not! I am a woman with very small wrists and the size is pretty good for me. I do wish they sold smaller bands but its not a big deal.

3- Livetracking & security features- LOVE this! You can set live tracking to start automatically or start it manually from the app ( I do wish this was an option on my phone). This sends an email to whoever you’ve listed, or all of your connections, with a link to a tracker. I love this as a woman who often goes on runs alone early in the morning or at night. There is also a feature on this watch where if you press a button 3x it sends a text to your emergency contacts and it will also do this if it notices anything unusual (you stop suddenly, etc). Probably my favorite features.

4- Training status, body battery, pulse ox, & respiration-I find all of this very cool & helpful. I don’t find the training status to be totally accurate for me though. None of this makes or breaks the watch for me though, it’s just nice to have. The Garmin Coach seems really cool but I haven’t had an opportunity to use it. I plan to use it to train for a half marathon.

5-Heart rate- I like this and find it to be accurate.

6-Buttons-my last watch was touchscreen and I do love that this has buttons instead.

7- Battery-battery life is OK but I wish it lasted a little longer. How quickly it charges makes up for that though!

Overall, this is a really great watch. I was lusting after the Fenix 6s but that would have been overkill for me. This does what I need it to do and some more. If you’re a runner, I do think you will love this. I would personally save some money and skip the music but when it does work it is nice to have. Thanks Garmin!

Review:
Side by side comparison on basic functions needed on the road. I bought 1470 for $135, 255W for $130.

Displayer Holder:
2 steps attach 1470 to windshield, 3 steps for 255W. Don't try hard to fight with your partner to turn the displayer angle to your direction, three tries will knock the 255W off the joint, Tried this in the room, left, right, left, gone!

Satellite Receiver:
1470 instant signal capture, from inside my home, 255W could be 30 seconds-1m; 1470 starts to shine when you turned it on, or when recalculate the route, 255W would pause a few or more seconds, 1470 will show your speed with mile plus ft, like 5.86, could because of the sensitive and quick receiver.

POI:
1470 offers more info of the road with POI and street names, and with 3 clicks will bring you to the POI you need, Garmin needs 5-7 steps. Pop up POI list available at Hw exit on 1470.

Displayer:
comfortable view from 1470, map color and highlight color more reasonable, green highlighted route on yellowish background, shows next turn mark (orange color) on the map; 255W background color is white, turn to purplish at daylight (screen burn?), with purple highlighted route, and white turn mark on white map, bad looking. 1470 is bigger, 4.7 V 4.3.

Specialty:
1470 also signals with bell sound when approaching 'turn' in addition to voice (sounds more real than garmin ), it has lane assistant, allow you to add traffic link if needed.
255W has 'speed limit'.

At home:
255W shines, you can do a lot things (add-ons) with computer connected,it shows number of the road when you click a point on the map, it has a log with your journey,...not 1470, it is even not compatible with Mac computer, sucks, I use Mac.

Big bad of 1470, its battery only lasts 30-40 minutes.

I want to keep both, but only use Garmin 255W for 'speed limit', and cover most part of the screen with a tape except the "speed limit",and mute it.

Have fun!

Review:
I've been a user of fitness trackers of various brands for about three years now. I ordered this one because the screen was bigger and the band was more comfortable than other brands. I haven't tried the call feature yet because I prefer to use my Bluetooth headset for this purpose. There were pros and cons of this particular one, however.

Pros:
*The battery life is pretty good. I can go about two and a half days in between charges, which isn't bad.
*The screen is big and colorful. Although the wording on some screens is hard to read, it still beats just looking at numbers all of the time.
*The band is comfortable and doesn't irritate the skin.
*The app is simple to use and easy to read.
*The screen customization feature is cute and different.

Cons:
*The menus are confusing and hard to get acclimated to. (The manual doesn't help, either). The screen is big enough so that the menus can be more easy to navigate, but this isn't the case.
*The testing of your heart rate and blood pressure are not explained. There are certain things you must know when testing your blood pressure. If not done properly, you won't get an accurate reading. This tracker doesn't tell you that, so your numbers might not be accurate. Please be advised of this.

Overall, I like this tracker. It has its hang-ups, but it will definitely help you get on track as far as fitness and staying active.

Review:
This is just what the doctor ordered if all you need is to be notified of all the messages you get daily on your smartphone....which, let's face it, is really all a smartwatch is really good for anyway. I've had them all, from the original LG G watch to the latest Samsung offerings, and found this one to be all I need. It can easily notify you of just about any message from just about any app you have on your phone, and it does it really well.

This even allows you to set different vibration patterns to help you to distinguish between different apps messages, really a great feature. To add to this awesomeness, it has amazing battery life of over 5 days without needing a charge...and even if said charge wears out, you still get to use it as a--gasp--WATCH, as the analog watch is powered by it's own separate 2 year battery.

All told, this is the smartwatch you want if all you need is to be notified of incoming messages/calls without having to get your phone out each and every time. After using these contraptions in all of their various forms now for nearly two years, I find that is all I really need. Add to that the ability to use any standard 22mm watch band, and that's the icing on the cake. Easily the best of the best, for a killer price. If your on the fence, jump on over to this side and buy it, it's worth every penny.

Review: After a good amount of research, it looked like the Samsung Galaxy watch had all the features that I needed in a fitness tracker. Making my decision even easier, I have a Samsung Galaxy phone and the Galaxy Buds, which made this watch compatible with the devices that I already owned. I am a runner and I loved to be able to track my runs and play music from my Spotify account from the watch alone. I purchased the watch on Feb. 15, of 2019. After a run on Jan. 9, 2020, I took off the watch and the rear motion sensor fell off.  At that point, looked at the display and pressed the side buttons and it would not turn on. I tried charging the watch and it would not connect. I have been extremely careful with this watch since purchasing it, even leaving the plastic over the screen until it broke. I called Samsung customer service and they told me that it was under warranty until March 2020 and to send it in. I sent the watch to their tech specialists and after a few days I received an email saying that the cost for repairs was $189.44, and that this was not covered under warranty due to “water damage.”  I have never worn it in the shower, or in the rain. The only moisture that it could have been exposed to was sweat. If this watch isn’t even sweat resistant, then how can they advertise it as a fitness tracker with “Water resistance 50 meters?” I spent over $300 on this watch and it didn’t even last 1 year. After this experience, I would NOT recommend this product to anyone needing to track anything other than a casual walk. After researching my problem with the watch I found many others with the exact same problem within a year of owning it. In all cases, Samsung denied repairs, stating water damage.

Review: So I've had this watch for all of a day, so there may be an edit in the future, but I wanted to give a few points about the watch in case it helps someone looking at a couple different options.  First thing I noticed was the band.  The leathery-type material is not very good (feel or look) in my opinion, but honestly, I figured that was the case going in and had already picked out a replacement band, so not a big deal for me.  The 2nd and most disappointing thing I noticed was the face.  The picture doesn't really give you an idea of how the big or good the display is.  The actual display only covers about 50% of the watch face, the rest is a black border around it.  And the display isn't great, not bad, but not great.  Reminds me of the Gameboy Color with a backlight added to it.  Now with that being said, there's no issue seeing the display.  The closer in pic is of the display with the backlight on, the other pic with it off.  It is the e-ink stuff, so you can see it in any lighting, and it is always on.  It doesn't go black after a few seconds or anything.  I can always look down and see what time it is, or if I have notifications.  I haven't played with all the features, but you can do a lot with this; text, health apps, change the watch face, view weather, play music, and all the other stuff a regular watch can do.  And that's just the preloaded stuff, you can add more.  Now, all that about the display, goes into why I bought this in the first place; the battery life.  I've been updating and adding stuff and playing with watch faces since I got it, and it's only down to 90% available battery.  It says it can last up to 10 days, I'd say it's probably going to last more like 7 or 8 depending on usage; but that is amazing compared to all the other watches out there that are good if they last 24 hours.  For what this watch was designed for, it's almost perfect.  If you're looking for an everyday smartwatch, that will last all week, this is the watch for you.  If you want a fancy looking watch and display, you probably didn't even bother looking at reviews for this watch.

Review:
I have tried many smart watches over the years. I've had the latest Apple watch, I've had the latest Samsung watch and even tried the TicWatch Pro among other expensive watches.

Was in the market for a new watch and wanted to try something different and less expensive. As I rarely used the features of those other watches except for the heart rate tracking, sleep tracking, receiving notifications and step tracking. I would occasionally use the more expensive watches for navigation but not often.

After receiving this watch I have been wearing it for over 2 months now and am very impressed. The watch does exactly what I was hoping it would and with extremely long battery life.

Pairing the watch to my Pixel 7 Pro was very easy. Once I downloaded the app and set everything up, it paired with no problems.

First thing I wanted to try was measuring my heart rate. This was spot on and tracked my heart rate continuously. With some smart watches (especially around this price point), you have to actively go into the heart monitor app to get it to read but once you leave the app it stops reading your heart rate. Not the case with this watch.

Then I wanted to see how well it tracked my sleep patterns. I normally don't get the best sleep and often wake up after 3 - 4 hours of sleep and may go back to sleep but not often. I can honestly say that it tracked my sleep spot on. Not only did it track my 4 hour sleep very accurately but when I woke up and then went back to sleep for a few more hours, it was able to add those 2 hours accurately to my night of rest. I have tracked my sleep every night since I have had this watch.

I was very impressed, shocked actually.

Next was the step counter which worked great as well. I did not look at it very often but when I walked around my house and counted my steps, I would then look at the watch and it would match what I got each time. Okay, so it's not the most scientific way of testing this feature but it seemed to work well for me.

One of the downsides that I noticed was with receiving notifications. When receiving text messages the letters do not wrap accurately. So if I was receiving a text saying "Hello how are you?" in a complete and accurate way. the text would show "Hello how ar
e you?" when all the letters would not fit on one line.

The screen is about the size of the larger smartwatches (44mm/45mm) or seems that sized due to the bezel around looking like it is a part of the watch.

Moving around the software of the watch is pretty smooth, not the smoothest and there are a few frame drops/stutters here or there but nothing that really impacts using the watch.

Tested the IP68 water resistance a bit by having it on when I washing the dishes and taking a shower (not at the same time) and the watch still functioned perfectly.

The best feature is the battery life. I can easily go a week or more without having to charge the watch 1 time. Amazing battery life and it also doesn't take long to charge.

Would definitely recommend this watch if you looking for something that tracks your HR, Steps, Sleep and can get notifications with outstanding battery life.

Review:
I haven't owned this smart watch that long. I purchased it during the lead up to Prime Day. I also purchased another band for it on Prime Day, because that goldish? looking band looked too cheap. Don't get me wrong, it's an adequate band, but the 22mm gold colored stainless band looked better, which makes me happier with my purchase.
I took a star off the fact that it doesn't tell you to scan the UPC code in order to download the correct app program. Spoiler Alert: it's GloryFit. Without that App, you're just twisting in the wind.

Charging is pretty straight forward. There is an included USB charging cable, approximately 2.5' long, with a propitiatory magnetic charging end. Hooked up on a USB 3 hub, it normally charges in the time it takes me to take a shower, so I haven't had the pleasure of draining it to see if it charges in 2 hours, as claimed.

The App is easy to navigate. The sleep function isn't all that. I have a weird sleep pattern that took my other smart watch about 1 month to acclimate, we'll see if this one does, as well. The Heart Rate monitor does a good job, and keeps an ongoing record. The Blood Pressure however, doesn't take a snapshot unless you click it yourself. I thought it would check my BP every hour, or so. There isn't a function control for that. There are 58 different dial faces you can choose from, or customize your own. I haven't tried it yet, but it's there in the App. It has the metric system and "British" system. I have been on the other side of the house when I've gotten phone calls and texts, without my phone close, and the watch lets me know, which I like. I have yet to use the alarm clock function, but will give it a try.

Other than that, this IMHO, was a good purchase, and I'm happy with it. If things change, I'll update my review.

Review:
I have had this watch for about a week and really enjoy it. Here are the highlights.

Battery life - be sure to cycle the watch from 100% to totally dead (watch shuts down) 3 times. When I use it to check and answer messages and check the time 4-5 times an hour, I had 55% power left after 18 hours. Used it for sleep monitoring overnight and woke up to 25% battery left. When I used it with watch face always on, google fitness workout monitoring for 1.5 hours, checking and answering messages, changing watch faces every hour and using a few phone apps, I had 25% battery left after 18 hours. I used it for sleep monitoring that night and needed to charge first thing in the morning. I am happy with the battery life on this watch.

Apps - very happy! I use my watch as a step counter, timepiece, calendar event tracking and messages on email and text messages. It has really performed well for these functions. The step counter is pretty accurate compared to my Fitbit. Using fit workout for my ice skating workout, the heart rate seemed pretty close when I would stop and manually measure my pulse. I use talk to text to answer text messages and it works well. The weather also updates regularly.

Comfort - the watch is quite comfortable to wear when I work out, work, do dishes in the sink, and for general use. I like the size, it fits my female wrist very nicely.

What would I improve? Mobvoi, could you PLEASE put something in your watch software that would make the watch vibrate when Bluetooth connection with the watch is lost? My phone is not the greatest and it loses BT connection a couple of times per day. I would love it if my watch would tell me the connection is lost so I can take care of the problem.

I am still exploring everything this watch can do. For my needs, it has done a great job. Still trying to tweak watch faces to make them show all of the info that I would like to see. Overall, I am extremely happy with this watch. It recharges quickly, stays charged all day to allow me to play with apps and keep up with the info I need. I will try to update after a few months of heavy use!
