Review:
For this Father’s Day, I have been thinking what kind of gift I should buy for my father. I know that he starts picking up the habit of exercise during this Covid 19 pandemic so I decided to buy a fitness tracker for him to stay healthy and count his steps every day. I saw this watch with the functions that he needs and it’s also at a reasonable price, so I gave this watch a try. My dad is pleasantly impressed with this watch after almost a week of use and he gives very positive comments about this watch.

First, this watch is easy to use and the phone application is user-friendly. My dad is known to be out of touch with modern technology but he has no issues with using the app with the clear instructions provided and its user friendly interface. He likes that the app includes lots of functions such as heart rate monitor, different modes of sports and steps tracker. It’s comprehensive and he is happy with all its features.

Second, this watch has long battery life and my dad has not charged once after using it for almost a week now. It has long battery life as described and better than his previous watch that the battery life won’t last for three days. This watch does not require constant charging and it’s something that my dad loves about.

Also, it accurately tracks the exercise information and my dad has done some little experiments to check whether this watch is accurate. He count his steps manually for 10 minutes and then he looks at watch to see if the steps number would match. It turns out to be less than 10 steps difference each time he tested. It’s highly accurate and my dad is impressed with the result.

However, there is some minor advice for the watch. First, the touch screen can be more sensitive because sometimes it takes a little longer for the screen to respond, but there is no complaint of the screen image quality. Also it would be better if there is music control function for the watch because my dad likes to listen to his 80s music while working out.

All in all, this watch’s functions and its performance is what as described and this is a good smart watch for seniors who like to exercise and stay healthy. It’s a satisfying purchase experience overall speaking.

This is my second Vivoactive watch and probably my last.

The bad:

Special Features: Fitness Tracker, Sedentary Reminder, Activity Tracker, Pedometer, Bluetooth Call, Heart Rate Monitor, blood pressure, sleep monitoringFitness Tracker, Sedentary Reminder, Activity Tracker, Pedometer, Bluetooth Call, Heart Rate Monitor, blood pressure, sleep monitoring   See more
Review:
What I liked: The price point of this smart watch is great. Out of the two major smartwatch brands in this market this watch is significantly less expensive. The design is clearly meant to mimic one of the two main smart watch brands (beginning with an “A”), and it does a good job of achieving a slim nice-looking watch. Using this smartwatch requires installing the daFit app on your phone as all functionality is controlled through the app. This watch allows plenty of options for customizing the watch face. It includes the ability to download a variety of different watch faces and layouts, and you can even use one of your own photos as a background for the watch—I liked that feature. All of this is easy to swap out once you figure out the UI. The watch bands can also be swapped out although I found them a bit difficult to install despite having a built-in spring lever to remove & attach them. The watch connects via Bluetooth and can be used to make calls, or even as a Bluetooth speaker. It comes with a magnetic lock charging cable that attaches to the back of the phone, but you will need to use your own charging block (with USB A plug). Another thing I like is the ability to use the watch as a remote shutter to take pictures on my phone. Keep in mind that you do not get a preview of what your camera sees, just a shutter button that takes a picture on your phone. There is a 3 second count down on your phone, but I find this not helpful unless you are using the front-facing camera. The watch also lets you store up to 8 favorite contacts for quick dialing. You can set it up to give notifications on your phone for messages, and a few other notifications.

What I didn’t like: The UI is somewhat difficult to figure out. It took me several days of playing with it to learn the basics. Expect to take some time learning to navigate this watch. I was hoping to use this smart watch to monitor steps, heart rate, oxygen levels and other biometrics. Telling time was not my major interest in this watch. Although this phone professes to track many different biometrics, I found the data to be wildly inconsistent. I was taking measurements with my smartphone, pulse oximeter, and blood pressure cuff and then comparing to data reported by this smart watch. Step counts were very far off sometimes they were 20% to 30% higher, and sometimes they were double from what my smartphone was telling me. I'm not sure what methodology the watch is using to attempt to track steps, but I carefully tested by counting my steps and then comparted to the watches’ measurement. The watch always shows significantly more steps, but the amount is inconsistent from test to test. I suspect perhaps they are measuring arm swings, but I don't know for sure. I couldn't find a consistent pattern in the resulting data. I also compared the oximeter data from the smartwatch with my existing pulse oximeter and the watch consistently showed 2% to 3% higher than my actual pulse oximeter. Blood pressure also showed the same heightened results. Using my blood pressure cuff showed a lower blood pressure than the smart watch reported.

Unfortunately, due to its inconsistent biometric data, I cannot recommend this watch. If you want a nice-looking watch just to tell time, then this watch might work for you. You have the option to customize the look of the screen, and this may be enough. But, if you are hoping to use the watch to monitor vitals, steps, and other health-related data I would recommend saving up for one of the bigger name brands. I really wanted to like this watch, but it did not live up to my expectations, and I'm afraid I have to give it a low rating for the poor biometric data tracking.

Review:
My experience with this watch has exceeded my expectations. While it may not have all the bells and whistles of other, much more expensive smartwatches, this is an excellent basic smartwatch. The name says it all, it gives you notifications on your wrist so you do not have to pull out your phone every time it buzzes.

I do have a few things that should be noted.
- It is a pretty large watch, more in line with larger men's watches. This might be too large for most women who prefer smaller watches. It is also a pretty thick watch in that it stands off your wrist a decent amount. Again, not necessarily a bad thing, but it can be a turn off for some people.

- Update the firmware on the watch as soon as you get it. I initially went a day or two with the firmware that was already on the watch and it was somewhat buggy. Notifications were hit or miss and it just did not function the way it should. Updating the firmware to the latest release made everything run much smoother.

- While the watch does work with iOS, there are features that are not compatible with the current iOS (iOS 10). Specifically, the voice activation for Siri will not work. This is not a feature that is all that important to me, so it is not a deal breaker, but it is worth noting.

- I personally found the watch band that comes with the watch bulky and I did not like that it is silicone. However, because the watch band connection is a standard 22mm, you can easily replace the stock watch band with whatever suits your taste/style. The stock band even has easy-to-release connection points so you can easily switch between Martian brand watch bands if desired. I ended up buying a black steel watch band and was able to make the replacement in a matter of minutes without too much trouble.

https://www.amazon.com/Luxury-black-Stainless-Steel-spring/dp/B00VWY7UBA/ref=sr_1_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1481031779&sr=8-4&keywords=martian+notifier

- This watch does not act as a fitness tracker. It can show notifications from fitness tracking apps you have on your phone, but it does not have a step counting function. This would be a good addition in the future if updated.

Overall, I would highly recommend this watch to anyone who is looking at getting a smartwatch but does not want to spend hundreds of dollars. If you can find a deal for this watch, take advantage of it (mine was purchased on Groupon for under $30). The companion app is basic but it allows for customization of the vibration alerts, so you can more easily tell the difference between incoming notifications. It also allows for up to 5 silent alarms as well as an hourly alert if you want to be reminded to move or something along those lines.

Review:
I purchased this from a local store that has a generous return policy. I had read so many negative reviews on the Garmin 610 that I wanted to be able to return it for a refund with minimal hassle. So far, I have noticed numerous little "glitches" in uploading data and in trying to use the watch without the GPS. It seems to not understand when you turn the GPS off, and it keeps trying to find a satellite. Also, the calorie calculation, supposedly changed to an extremely accurate formula, is reading well below actual. I have had metabolic testing done, and I am very much in tune with how much energy I burn during a given activity. For example, I did a 90 minute bike ride that included several higher intensity intervals. The Garmin 610 recorded calories burned as 220. I could have sat on the bike and barely turned the pedals for 90 minutes and burned 220 calories!

That said, it is a very nice looking watch and the touch screen works flawlessly for me. The heart rate reading seems to be very accurate and not prone to interference. I am going to work with the unit more before I give up and return it.

Fortunately, with this store's return policy, I can use it for a few months and still get a full refund or exchange it for something else.

July 28, 2012 Revision 1-I continued to experience minor issues with the 610. The heart rate monitor and data transfer works very well. The main issues I had with mine were with pace #fluctuating from 1 minute miles to 40 minute miles# and battery. The battery was showing 80-90% full but shutting the watch down in the middle of my runs due to low battery #?#. At this point I returned the unit to the store I purchased it from and exchanged it for a new one. I like the 610 so much that I want to try again with another one. If I have the same issues, I will return it and exchange it for another brand. I did some research in the Garmin forums and I am doing a couple of things differently this time:
1. I am only plugging the ANT stick into the computer when I download data, then unplugging it. It seems that when the ANT stick is plugged in, it is constantly try to send data to the 610 and that may be causing problems.
2. I plug it into the battery charger after every use and charge it to 100%. Problems with the battery seem to occur when the battery gets fully depleted. Also, something called "reverse charging" occurs. I think it's best to start every run at 100% battery power.

I will update again soon.

November 24, 2012 Revision 2- So far so good with the 2nd unit. I have had no issues at all since exchanging the first unit for another. I'm keeping my fingers crossed that this will continue. I will update if anything different happens.

Review:
This watch rocks. I've been using it for a month now and I'll list out what I like and what I don't like and hopefully you find some use in that.

Likes:
-Visually appealing (White Color, it's a nice matte/off white color)
- GPS is fairly accurate (Been testing GLOSSNASS & Gaileo, check out the forums and see what works best in your region)
- Very minimalistic (Con for some)
-Battery Life is phenomenal. They claim around 14 days I believe and using GPS every other day for a 3-5 mile run I still get about 10 days before I re-charge it.
- Works with the Garmin Connect app which beautifully lays out all your data in a variety of ways
-Durable! I have a bad habit of catching my watches on every doorknob (idk how I manage it) but the glass is unscathed thus far and despite getting the White colored watch I haven't had any issues with colors rubbing off on it.
-Affordable
-Perfectly sized case. It's not obnoxiously large/tall like the Fenix or Suunto watches.

Dislikes:
-Almost too minimal
-Steep learning curve, the instructions tell you what the buttons do but that's it.
-No Connect IQ (service that lets you make and buy custom widgets and faces like other smartwatches)

Those are my only gripes. If you're looking for a sporty watch that you can beat up, measure accurate runs with, and that's fairly low profile then this is the watch for you. If you're looking for a dedicated all-round smart watch with some sportier features (or any features for that matter) then you may look around some more ( Garmin Vivoactive series, Fenix's, Suunto Baro's, etc)

Lastly, I will say that I sold my Galaxy Smartwatch to essentially trade "down" for this watch and I don't have a single regret. I found the Galaxy's heart rate monitor was finicky and would stop when there was any moisture on my wrist, ie sweat. Also, I enjoy the simplicity of the Garmin. It doesn'y feel like I'm wearing a small phone on my wrist that I have to check every so often. It's a performance watch.

If you buy one I hope you enjoy it as much as I have!
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Battery Life is phenomenal. They claim around 14 days I believe and using GPS every other day for a 3-5 mile run I still get about 10 days before I re-charge it.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a long-lasting battery that can support regular GPS usage over multiple days without frequent recharging.

Review:
I bought this Fitbit watch (Versa) for my daughter, who is a full-time nursing student and getting ready to graduate. It had a lot of features that would assist her in her future position as a nurse. I had a Fitbit once myself, but it was so low quality that I went to a Garmin. However, that was years ago, so I was willing to take a chance that the production quality had improved since then.

The bottom line, it hasn't. The battery life is terrible, barely holds a charge, and impacts the usability of the watch on a day-to-day basis. I don't recommend this watch or any Fitbit product for that matter, as they are low quality but high price. That is a terrible combination! Buyer Beware - Stay Away!

Review:
First; the dual display is a huge win for a smart watch! Using hybrid mode I've been aggressively trying to drain the battery for the past 36 hours and just accomplished the task. I have the cellular service turned off, and without it the watch is truly a two day battery life watch.

The LCD is even used during workouts! This benefit was a huge surprise. I read a lot about people ditching the default Mobvoi fitness app, but the integration with the LCD screen is almost magical. To fully take advantage of the features you do need to use Mobvoi's apps, but (right now) everything is easily shared with Google Fit.

Another huge selling point is the fact that the watch can be swam with. The company doesn't tout this aspect enough. The watch has a speaker drain quick action by holding the second watch button.

I've only owned the watch for about a week now, and I'm coming from a Gear Fit 2. With the features of the watch and support of the Wear OS I'm very pleased with the device.

The watch is big, but I like big watches. The feel of the weight is surprising light too. And the construction feels as expected. I am still getting accustomed to the size and wearing a watch all the time (have I mentioned the battery actually makes me feel comfortable wearing it 24/7). I banged it on walls and doorknobs while carrying the baby around the house, but I have yet to notice a scratch anywhere on the device.

I do recommend the device. I gave it a 5 star rating. I gave the sleep tracking 4 stars because the interface is unintuitive, but after a few days it's understandable. Additionally there are a few things about the apps that led me to give it a 4 star rating. I'm a prior service member and always having a lead digit on the watch face makes me think it's in 24 hour time, but it's not. There are a few other aspects of the Mobvoi apps that are "lost in translation" too, but the overall operations and performance are solid.

[[VIDEOID:259b534c71196cbcfec4a8961d6feb7e]] I've posted an unboxing and first impressions review above, but below is my in-depth:
I work as a delivery driver, and I work in a high-stress, high-activity field. This being said, I didn't want something expensive like an apple watch or a fitbit, but something that was affordable and did all I needed it to. Boy was I surprised! This watch goes above and beyond, and does all the stuff that a 300$ smart watch does, but for less than 50! I have very large hands and wrists and the watch fits very comfortably. The metal is matte and durable, and the screen is responsive. The thing can take a beating, and I'm not as worried about doing projects when I wear it because of how good the price is. I use the watch so frequently, and it is easily visible when at a max brightness in full sun, and get 2-4 days of battery life (with heavy usage, sport modes frequently on). The blood pressure and O2 measurements as well as the heart-rate monitoring are all very accurate and useful. Also, since I can't have my phone on me when I'm working, this is the perfect solution.

Review:
We let the elderly lady of the household give this generic smart watch a try and she really enjoy using it.

Pros:
Arrived 55% charged. Took about ~30min to fully charge.
Fairly simple to pair up an Android phone with the Fitdock app downloaded from Google Play store.
Many functions including Health/Fitness, Phone, Workouts, Sport record, Reminders, Music, Weather, Different watch faces, Flashlight, etc.

Fitness tracking for a variety of sports.
Even my elderly mom finds it easy to use for tracking her daily activities and phone calls.

Concerns:
In order to activate this smart watch, user has to sign up with Fitdock and allow them to track some personal info that's fully disclosed in the Privacy Agreement.
Location tracking. I disabled this to ensure more privacy.
Marketing. I opted out to minimize junk messages and emails.
Accuracy of fitness data will not be 100% but we primarily use data as baselines for monitoring improvements.
Proprietary charging cable: USB A to special magnetized connector. Lose it and user is S.O.L.
Extruded plastic connections for watch bands look susceptible to damage from heavy impact.

Review:
I was in the market for a standard watch with bluetooth capabilities, available at a standard watch price. The Martian Notifier is the only watch I could find that meets these requirements to any degree.

Pros:
-For a smartwatch this thing really looks good. It is the kind of design I could easily pick out from a lineup of "dumb" watches.
-Battery life is excellent, and it doesn't take long to charge.
-It has a nice set of bluetooth features that you would expect from a standard smartwatch. You can easily set it up to get just the smartphone updates you need right way, right on your wrist.

Cons:
-It's a bit on the bulky side, not ridiculous but it's really a Men's size watch.
-It is not up to the build quality standards of a standard analog watch. Martian is a tech company so it's not surprising that the standard watch features are not as good, but even in a 1st gen device it feels like a no-brainer to have a more reliable analog clock, less scratch-prone face and better watchband than this.
-The proprietary USB charging cable. If you have extra micro-USB charging cables the connectors are too short to use in this.
-No water resistance.

I give this watch 5 stars because I bought it on Amazon for under $35, and I feel that even with its shortcomings the design, functionality and build quality are well beyond what one would expect for a device like this at that price. As a standard watch I wouldn't call it great but if the analog clock breaks at some point I would probably still wear it.

Review:
UPDATE - Updating this watch to 5 stars as it now does what I would expect. I worked for some time with Tom from Garmin - super helpful rep and didn't give up on trying to find the issue. I was on the brink of giving up on this watch when Tom provided a file that appears to have rolled the Bluetooth tech back to 3.0.

IF YOU ARE HAVING THIS ISSUE EMAIL GARMIN SUPPORT AND REQUEST THE FILE "BLUETOOTH_3.00"

Now my watch stays connected as expected and operates as advertised. My phone is a Pixel 3 on the latest version of Android. When running with my Bose SoundSport Free Bluetooth headphones the watch stays connected much better than it did (no switching of wrists) but does every once in a while still get effected by some interference from power lines (I think). By and large, very pleased with how it now performs. Thank you, Tom!

First the good - great for daily tracking of activity with numerous layers of detail. Many different workouts tracked and the features suit me well. Battery life is really solid, easily a full week if not running GPS. No issue for a hearty workout (multi-hour run/bike ride, skiing, etc).

The bad - as widely reported (do a web search or scan these reviews), the simplistic technology of connecting via Bluetooth appears to be rocket science for Garmin (not isolated to this watch, across the Garmin line). I have a Google Pixel 3 and it can't stay connected for more than a few minutes. If you don't turn off the notification of a new connection (after disconnection), you'll lose your mind as it goes off non-stop. Thanks Garmin! I've tried emailing with Garmin support, jumping through all of the hoops of resets and explicit connection instructions (it shouldn't be this difficult), sending app diagnostic data, and so on...nice folks but it's made no difference.

One of my primary reasons for buying this watch was the ability to load Spotify songs, works fine. But if I want to actually listen to them I have to switch my watch to my right wrist to maintain the connection to my Bose Soundsport Free (these headphones have their own issues, but have no issue staying connected to my phone). That's right, the extra foot of the watch on my left wrist makes that much of a difference. Woof.

Lots of complaining here but I really wanted to like this watch. It's close, but if Fitbit releases something comparable (GPS with Spotify) I'm going to give it a try and dump the Vivo.

Review:
My 13-year-old recently asked for a "tracker" watch because she wanted something that would track her steps and tell her the time. We've used Fitbit in the past, but they're so expensive and I was unimpressed with the quality for the cost. I decided to take a shot at a brand that was new to me and I settled on this one. I thought the cost was reasonable and it appeared to be of good quality from the product page.

The watch comes in a classy box that makes you feel like you're holding a premium product. Everything inside is laid out and easy to use. The one we received came with two different colored bands (pink and black), a screen protector, a charger, and a booklet.

The watch should be charged before setting it up. It magnetically connects to the back of the watch and has a USB end for charging. Charging doesn't take long and the battery has an incredibly long life. My daughter goes several days at a time without charging the watch.

The setup was pretty straightforward. It took her a little bit of time to figure out how to customize her wallpaper but that was the biggest snag we ran into. We have it set so that a lot of the functions are turned off because I don't want her receiving text alerts while she's in school. She primarily looks at her sleep, steps, the weather, date and time, and heart rate. They all seem accurate enough for her, though the weather is frequently very off.

The screen protector was a joke. She went to check a notification and her nail just touched underneath the edge and it peeled up. We bought her different ones and they're working well. The bands work well and adjust to fit a wide range of wrist sizes. It's comfortable on her wrist and she's been very happy with how it feels.

Overall, I must say I'm pretty impressed. I feel like we took a chance with this watch and it's proven to be quite successful. There are inaccuracies to some of the tracking, but for a teen, it's easily overlooked. The pros significantly outweigh the cons and she's been thrilled with her new watch. She's had it for a few weeks now and said it's the best watch she's ever used. Happy kid, happy parent!

Review:
I have now owned this watch for three months and have put it through paces. I will try to keep the review simple, but informative. Overall this is a great watch for everyday training with the bonus features of a smartwatch. Do not confuse this with a genuine smartwatch like the Sony Smartwatch 3, Samsung Gear S, or the Apple Watch.

Pros:
GPS works very well and acquires very quickly
Heart rate monitor works well, is comfortable, much more accurate than wrist monitors, and easy to clean
Heart rate monitoring allows for better calorie estimation
Multiple customizable screens for all modes makes the watch easy to tailor
Most functions are tap-based during activity mode and work well
Waterproof
Golf is a cool feature
Backlight works well in low light conditions and stays on as long as you keep interacting with the watch
Screen is extremely easy to read in sunlight
Decent notification screens for smartwatch functions
Comfortable band with thin overall profile
Intuitive to use
Connect app is easy to navigate and gives you TONS of info for analysis
Extra apps and faces make the watch further customizable (find my car is a favorite of mine)
Battery life is amazing - I charge the watch every 4-8 days depending on GPS use
Compatible with both the foot pod for better accuracy and Tempe temperature sensor if desired
Cheap replacement bands in colors and leather
Decent/average looks
Lazy meter buzzes for every hour of inactivity to remind you to keep moving
Tracks sleep and sleep quality very well

Cons:
Notifications do not work well if you have multiple notifications on the screen
Screen does not always respond to swipe gesture and can take repeated tries at times
Limited smartwatch functions
Not suitable for interval training as there is no mode for it
Texts, WhatsApp, Facebook etc. always buzz the watch but incoming calls do not always register
No speaker, vibrate only - can make some functions like mile counting while running easy to miss
Cannot provide label for non-GPS runs or workouts

As you can see the pros outnumber the cons. I really love the watch and the availability of notifications, but I bought it as a training aid with the bonus of smartwatch functions, not the other way around. Had I valued the smartwatch over the training aid I might have chosen the Sony Smartwatch 3, but I must admit I would have hated charging it daily. The Garmin Connect app is easy to use and provides valuable insight like cadence, average cadence, and a graph of heart rate, speed, cadence, and other info for better analysis. The integrated step counter is modestly accurate without GPS. I find that it is within 10% accurate to the distance that the treadmill says. It may be possible to calibrate stride length for more accuracy, but I am more concerned with heart rate and cadence than distance on the watch since the treadmill tells me the distance anyway.

It is a bummer that there is no weightlifting app, but no surprise there, it is extremely hard to measure with a watch so I won't hold my breath for the app to appear. I simply use the heart monitor and put the watch in indoor running mode to estimate calorie expenditure, but I remove the chest strap for bench press (the bar hurts when it hits the monitor - not a good idea).

I highly recommend the watch to anyone who needs a Fenix-type watch, but doesn't want to spend $400+. Know that it isn't perfect, but it is as close as you will probably get without spending at least twice as much. I have no regrets and I think most people of just about any fitness level would love the watch either as a simple step counter, or as a legitimate training aid. Get one for yourself and you will probably love it.

Review:
This is a decent watch for the price and has a lot of smart features, although it misses on some. It looks great in my opinion but looks are subjective. If you are looking for an inexpensive smartwatch option with a great battery that does a lot, looks rugged, and has a larger screen then this may be the one. It depends on what features you want/need in a smartwatch.

The good:

LOOKS: It has a rugged "military" look to it. I have not tested any of supposed "military tested" features such as shock resistance, water resistance, etc. However, I have accidentally banged it quite a few times on walls and other hard objects and it doesn't even have a scratch or nick. Also, I used it twice playing soccer to track my workout including once in a light rain without any problems.

BATTERY: Excellent battery, in the first two full weeks of usage I have not had to recharge. The current battery readout is at 50%. The first week I played quite a bit with all the features too.

BLUETOOTH/CONNECTIVITY: It was easy to set up and connect with my iPhone, although the manual that came with it is very "light" and not exactly clear at times. However, I am able to make and receive calls (the speaker is pretty great, and calls were clear even outdoors), receive and read text messages (watch is not capable of sending text messages), as well as connect to the FitCloudPro app to track workouts and health information.

SCREEN: It has a larger display which I like because it is easier to read. The screen is customizable and there is a fairly large number of different styles and screens you can choose. Because of the larger screen size, the watch itself might not work for everyone. I have thinner wrists, so it looks fairly big on my wrist, but I like it. The screen graphics are very clear and easy to see and read.

FUNCTIONALITY: Other than time and date, the watch has various other functions. It has 20 workouts but who really is going to "Skipping" or "Hula hoop" workouts? I used it to track walking, running and soccer and it does a fairly good job. The steps seem fairly accurate, compared with what my phone and another watch tracked. The app collects all the data and is easy to use to track individual workouts, and daily, weekly and monthly activity. It also has a sensor to check your heartrate, blood oxygen and blood pressure. The heartrate and blood oxygen measurements seem accurate. However, the blood pressure is way off and totally inaccurate. The watch also has the usual alarm/timer/stopwatch functions, and you can play music from your phone and track your sleep activity, none of which I used.

The bad:

SOME FUNCTIONS INACCURATE: The blood pressure measurements are inaccurate; it shows BP way too low. Also, the weather function is all over the place. Sometimes it's accurate but often it is not, making it useless. I ended up turning it off.

SIZE: For some this may be a negative. I like the larger screen but not for everyone.

SOME QUIRKS: One thing I did not like is that when I was doing a workout and put it into workout mode there is no way to access any other function (such as check time) without ending the workout mode. Any button I pressed and my only option was END or CONTINUE. I tested if I could receive calls or texts during the workout and the watch vibrated as usual and the text would briefly flash on the screen making it impossible to read. Also, receiving a call the number/contact briefly flashed on my screen but before I could answer or decline it went right back into the workout. This is my biggest negative as it would be nice to check the time or look at a text message during the workout without having to turn the mode off and then start a new workout again.

Overall, I liked this smartwatch, it does a lot of things right but has some quirks. It is not as refined and capable as the name brand smartwatches but it's only a fraction of the cost. It's got a tough, rugged look to it and the battery life is amazing. For the price it's a pretty good watch.

Review:
I recently transitioned from Fitbit Blaze to the Gear Sport and on the surface the Gear Sport is a beautiful smart watch; however, once you're past the aesthetic there is nothing behind the fitness end of this watch. It tracks steps pretty good and the sleep tracking is okay (It only tells you restless, light sleep, and motionless sleep) but that is pretty much were it stops. If you're looking to this watch to be your primary fitness tracker I would strongly encourage you to look somewhere else. I have completed two workouts with this watch and both times the watch did not accurately track my heart rate or calories burned. My chest strap would tell me I burned 400+ calories in a half hour while the Sport would say 100+ calories. Don't get me wrong, I know wrist worn heart rate monitors are not as good as chest straps, but still a 300 calorie difference equals a significant misrepresentation in the focus of this watch.

Now lets also discuss the S Health app since that is the required app for the Gear Sport. The S Health app is clumsy at best and really feels like there was 0 thought put in to how a person interacts with it. Everything from weight goals to viewing your workout data is poorly shown and the user interface is horrible. There is not one area in this app that shows you all of your workouts. Instead it breaks them up into categories of exercise and displays them individually. S Health does connect to other apps such as Under Armour Record and MyfitnessPal, but this has also been an infuriating experience. Review:
Coming from what seems to me to be the slightly more intuitive interface of a Fitbit Versa-lite, this took some getting used to (I'm actually still getting used to it, and it's been a few months now), but I do like it a lot!

Functionally, it's great. It does what I need it do, when I need to do it, and it seems to do it well. The only features I really miss from my Fitbit, are the intuitive alarm (it would wake you within half an hour of your set time when it sensed the best time was for you to wake up)--to my knowledge, that's not an option on this watch, but it's a minor thing that I'd grown accustomed to, but isn't a deal-breaker--and the touch screen. Having buttons is a little bit of an adjustment.

Now, the pros: (there are soo many and I'll probably forget some!)

It fits my smaller, female wrist just fine and it's actually pretty light (not as light as my former Fitbit was, but light enough to not be bothersome.)

The main reason I got this, besides the awesome solar-power feature, is because I'm hella-hard on watches. I'm constantly knocking them into things while just going about my day. My Versa lasted about a month before I hit the screen hard enough to crack it, despite having a screen protector on it--thankfully, it still worked for about a year, but it finally got to the point where it was acting weird, so getting something new was necessary. This watch, with the inset screen (and the extra ballistic glass screen protector that came with my hubby's Garmin watch :D), that's not an issue. It seems really sturdy and able to take a beating, which is good.

The battery life is phenomenal! And it's even better if you don't have all of the features turned on, but even with them on, it lasts about 8-12 days! UNLESS you're out in the sun, which then charges it as you go. I hate having to worry about charging my watch every four days (Fitbit, I'm looking at you) so this is perfect.

I love that Garmin isn't trying to get me to upgrade/buy their full app so I can use the more advanced features, like challenges and routines. It's all just there, available to use, and that's awesome!

The idea of it being waterproof is fantastic - I haven't had much of a chance to test this, but I will once pool season starts up again. Fingers crossed! :)

The pulse-ox is great. The sleep info is good. Between those two things and keeping track of what I do/don't do daily, I feel like I've got a good roadmap on what things help me get better sleep and what things I've been doing to sabotage myself.

The miles, when compared with my android app on my phone, are pretty darn accurate--within 0.2 miles, so not too shabby!

I mostly workout in my neighborhood, so I haven't gotten the chance to use the GPS, but this spring we're going to be doing some hikes, so that will come in handy, I'm sure.

Oh, and the teal color (if you can manage to find it) is fab!

Overall, I really like this watch and I think as I explore its features, I'll probably end up liking it even more--change is hard, but definitely worth it. I liked it enough to come back and buy my hubby the tactical version for Christmas, so that says quite a lot. :) If you're on the fence, I know it's a nice chunk of change, but it's a really functional, durable, light watch that does pretty much everything you need a watch like this to do and then some. I'm not sure you can go wrong with it.

Review:
I've been slowly assimilating into the Samsung ecosystem for a few years now, and with my Fitbit Charge HR having finally kicked the bucket, I wanted a new fitness wearable. What better time to buy than Cyber Monday? So, instead of going directly back to Fitbit, I wanted to give Samsung a chance.

This Samsung Galaxy Fit has hit every mark for me. The price is comparable, yet better than the competitor Fitbit. I find it exceptionally accurate compared to the Fitbit Versa, which I purchased for a friend a year ago and have unfortunately heard nothing but grumbling about the inaccuracy of its step tracking. The Galaxy Fit is the perfect size to me. Not a smartwatch by any means, and slimmer than the Inspire HR, yet still with a full color screen.

The only downside I have experienced with the Galaxy Fit is the charging. It uses wireless induction charging (a proprietary charger is included), but this device is not compatible with Qi wireless chargers that I have found. So, you are at the mercy of the included charger. Sadly, I have found it to be quite disappointing. There is no included wall adapter, yet it requires lower amperage than most chargers you'd get from a phone or tablet. None of the Samsung branded (or non-Samsung, for that matter) wall chargers allow it to charge the device. I am relegated instead to using an external power pack, such as an Anker 3500 mAh, to charge the device. Plugging the charger into the wall or a USB port will briefly begin charging the Fit, but a few seconds later, it stops charging. At first I assumed my charger was defective, but when it is charging from the external power pack, it works perfectly. Samsung should take note and put some serious R&D into fixing this deficiency.

As for battery life once is is *finally* successfully charged, I have had a good experience. On average about 5 days. In the accompanying app, you can set your Fit to monitor your heart rate continuously, or every 10 minutes. With continuous monitoring, it's about 3 days. With every 10 minutes, its 5 to 6.

All in all, the device is a fine alternative to the Fitbit Inspire HR or Charge 3, but the charging mechanism drags down the experience significantly. Samsung has clearly put thought and care into the device itself, and with a future fix to the charging mechanism, it could be a no-brainer alternative to Fitbit.

Review:
This is my first smartwatch purchase (previously wore a basic digital watch). Compared to my digital watch (which is very small and light) this is a bit heavier but I got used to it pretty quick. The entire setup (downloading the app, connecting it to the watch, changing a few settings) took about 15 minutes, and charging it from 50 to 100% takes less than a couple of hours. From my experience with it over the last few days the touchscreen works very well and the screen can get plenty bright. The heart rate monitor, along with the step counter, seems to be very accurate. The sleep record seems to be a few minutes off every night, but for $25 I have no issue with that.

Some of my favorite functions of this watch include the relaxation/breathing function and the sedentary reminder (which will vibrate every hour to remind you to get up and move around if you’ve been sitting for too long). Both call and Facebook Messanger notifications work very well on this. And the battery-life will indeed last about 10 days while wearing it nearly 24/7, which is pretty great!

The only minor complaint I have with it is that sometimes when I try to check the time the watch display will not turn on, so I have to tap it or try again. However, I assume that other budget watches likely have issues with this as well, so I won’t take a star off for it.

Overall a fantastic value purchase for just $25. Highly recommend it for anyone looking for a budget fitness/digital watch!

Review:
I had a Fitbit ChargeHR prior to this device. The Fitbit tracked sleep better and was less buggy with the app interface. The Vivosmart will only track my sleep during the "typical sleep hours" that I input into the app. It hasn't tracked any sleep or naps outside this window. One time when I was doing a red eye flight from the west coast and it didn't track any sleep during my usual sleeping time it just went ahead and counted the whole window as "sleep." That's not cool. None of the literature really specifics how it captures sleep. I've found that if I change my "expected" sleep window it will capture some sleep periods but I have to have the window very precise to actually catch just the sleep and not any other activity. If I have a short night I of sleep I don't like to know exactly what time it is so I don't keep myself up counting down how many hours of sleep I can catch if I go to bed right now.

With the latest update of the app I haven't been able to set a silent alarm, which is quite annoying. I'm also not a big fan of having to pick the day when the alarm is supposed to go off. I travel across a lot of time zones and I'm not always cognizant of what day of the week it's going to be and my wake up times vary a lot. It also takes more steps than it should to set an alarm. There's no quick link from the front page of the app, I have to dig down into the device settings menus.

Those criticisms aside, the Vivosmart has the ability to notify you when you are below or above your target heart rate zones, which was a feature that I found lacking in the ChargeHR. The ability to customize what metrics you see during workout times is a really good feature.The GPS is a really nice option. I've only used it once but it doesn't seem to be a huge drain on the battery. I find that I have to charge the Vivosmart less often than the ChargeHR. The notification feature is nice whereas the ChargeHR only notified me of incoming calls. The weather is nice to have at hand. I have yet to use the music control function. I really like the waterproof feature of the device, being able to use it for water activities or just not having to worry about damaging it in water is great. It's one of the features I was looking for in my next wearable.

I'm not sure is the device is as accurate as it can be if you can't specify whether you wear it on your dominate or non-dominate hand. I am right handed but wear watches and wearables on my right because I my left arm was in a cast when I "learned to wear a watch." It just feels normal to me, but I don't know if wearing this device on your dominate side skews the tracking. The option to chose dominate and non-dominate was a feature of the ChargeHR.

Overall, I like the Vivosmart HR+ but the poorer sleep tracking is a drawback for me. This seems to be a software issue that could be tweaked with an update, as could the ability to "zoom" in on some of the graphs to better see specific times of hear rate peaks/lows, sleep cycles and some other metrics. The ability to notify you to stay in your target heart rate zone is a big plus, as is the waterproof feature.

Review: A comparison between the GearFit and Fitbit Charger HR finds that it all boils down to what your priorities are. The GearFit lets you see emails and text messages and return a pre programmed quick response. Also there is notification for mobile calls and you can quick respond to those. GearFit breaks down exercise into walking running and cycling (the cycling does not record for stationary cycling) however the data are not as good as Fitbit.Fitbit will  tell you how long you were in the identified fat burning and cardio zones. Fitbit also gives you an accurate heart rate anytime you press the button and toggle through to heart rate (2 toggles) so in the middle of something challenging during your day you can check and it is accurate. On the Gearfit you must scroll trough and ask it to measure heart rate. It is not as accurate for this parameter. The sleep data on Fitbit is more inclusive including specific times (in minutes ie. 4:02-4:22am ) that you were restless, awake and asleep in a color coded bar type  graph. Gearfit will only tell you what percentage of the time overall you were motionless in a line graph. I wore them simultaneously for several days and the Fitbit was more accurate. because of the sleep detail. On Fitbit, you can determine how much of a discrepancy there is between how long you were in bed and how much restful sleep actually occurred) Also..a biggie..Fitbit knows when you are sleeping; Charger needs you to select the sleep option then activate. Also have to do the same thing to turn it off. If you forget, data are skewed. Also on Gear Fit, the profile to view health info (S health) must be signed into each time on your phone with password (just another cumbersome step).. Also Fitbit asks you to calculate stride length so I would think step counts are more accurate (in theory). One step count issue with the Fitbit..I was working at a camp and riding in a golf cart; Fitbit recorded the bumps as steps so my count was off.I wasn't wearing the Gear Fit at the time so I cannot speak ascertain if the same issue exists. Aesthetically, Gear Fit wins hands down. Screen and bands can be changed to match clothing. Gear Fit screen is like a small phone screen so potential for damage is greater. Screen comes on when you turn wrist over as if to view time. Not sure if this would get you in trouble in movie theater. I was worried about the band on Gear Fit and falling off. There is a little silicone closure that can be purchased separately on Amazon; there have been no issues with that. Much more is happening on Gear Fit which is reflected in how long it holds a charge (shorter by a day or two) but it charges quickly.Gear Fit requires a docking apparatus which limits flexibility in charging ( the dock does not fit in your wallet). Fitbit has a small (4 inches or so) specialized cable to charge. Alarm feature was disappointing on Gearfit. Phone must be in close proximity and alarms are set on the phone; performance is not reliable. Fitbit alarm was excellent; phone does not need to be in range and alarms are set on computer. If you are a serious data gatherer Fitbit it is. For bells and whistles and less serious data choose Gear Fit

Review:
After some commented on receiving defective units, I was worried. However my watch arrived in perfect condition and I couldn't be happier. The packaging doesn't offer any "wow factor" as it's a flimsy box that barely holds the contents which amount to the watch and charger. Additionally, the unit doesn't ship with a power brick or any type of documentation. Those were the negatives, now onto the overwhelming positives.

The watch also includes built-in LTE. I didn't realize this when I made the purchase and it was a pleasant surprise. I texted Verizon and had it setup as a linked device in about 20 mins ($10 month). Now I can do activities without the need to have my phone in my pocket. This has been super useful while working in the yard and going for walks. All of the switching happens automatically so there is no need to manually swap between your phone and the watch.

The quality leather band is thick and substantial. It definitely adds to the quality look of the watch. For day-to-day use, I like to wear more sporty bands. Swapping between the leather band and colors and styles couldn't be simpler with the quick-change feature built-in to the straps.

I was concerned when I realized the watch didn't run Google's Wear OS and instead uses Samsung's own Tizen OS. I had been using a TicWatch E for the last year and had grown accustomed to the Wear OS ecosystem. Let me say my concerns were unfounded. After an initial learning curve, the Tizen OS is super slick and the overall experience has been smooth and enjoyable. My Wear OS experience was always laggy. The S3 has been fast and dependable. For those who may have heard it has compatibility issues with a Google Pixel phone, rest assured those issues have been resolved. I use a Pixel 3XL and everything has worked seamlessly. You would never know they were not created by the same company.

Samsung's fitness apps are great and offer a broad range of tracking options. These go way beyond steps and also add stairs, food, water, exercise, etc. Thing like longer walks or biking are automatically started based on your movements. This has been great for me as I could never remember to "start" an exercise until I was already well into it. I did find it funny though that mowing the lawn was registered as biking since your body and movements are very similar if you put it into perspective. As a former Apple Watch user, you won't be disappointed in the quality or the user experience. Every aspect so far has been top notch.

Battery life hasn't been as long as advertised. However, after initial setup and playing, the watch gets me through a full day with power to spare. I have grown accustomed to charging my watches every night and this one is no different.

Based on the quality and features, you won't find a better deal on a flagship smart watch. Highly recommended!

Review:
This is a well made smartwatch, the finishes are good, feels solid in hand. The leather strap is ok, seems bit flimsy but that can be replaced with 3rd party ones. The only issue I really have is the iOS app, it keeps crashing, and the Bluetooth connection cuts a few times a day, thats a let down, since the watch relies heavily on the app and software. Pebble needs to ensure this does not happen if they want their watch to compete with the likes of Apple Watch for iOS users. I am giving this watch a chance to see how it fares with software updates. Unless Pebble fixes the connection and app issues, I will be returning it.

The screen is also somewhat dull, the backlight helps, and it looks great in direct sunlight. Indoors hard to read sometimes.

Voice replies on iOS work ok if you have Verizon, not sure about other carriers. There are also canned responses which you can edit to your liking.

You cant speak on the watch directly, there is no speaker, you can answer from the watch and talk on your phone.

Overall good watch, less features than Apple Watch. If you want your watch to always be connected and not have to worry about connection issues then the Apple Watch might be better. I hope Pebble will continue to improve the software side of things to ensure a seamless experience.

Review:
I switched my strap out for a fine mesh stainless steel one as after the first wear, the leather strap was already creasing and I didn't want to damage it with water. I debated getting this one or the Pebble Round but this one was much cheaper for the gold finish (refurbished) and a significantly longer battery life.

The watch is definitely bulky, which is a trade off and it's quite large on my wrists which are not very slender but even then, the face covers most of my wrist. It is very pretty to look at and I do hope there are better watch faces released because the ones I found all looked very pixelated and cartoonish. While those may suit the other models, they don't suit the gold finish of this watch at all.

Overall, the watch controls are easy to learn and the connectivity to my Nexus 6p is great. I've had a couple issues with connectivity in the 2 days I've tried it where at times the Pebble app wasn't detecting my watch. Other than that, the steps, notifications and weather apps are all very handy. The display is crisp and the ambient light sensor adjusts brightness accordingly. For those that wear additional accessories on their wrist, know that the buttons are easy to press and on occasion I have switched the silent mode on or switched to an app when a piece of jewelry pushed against one of the buttons.

June 2017 Update: My Pebble is still working great save the weather update feature which gives a continuous error code on the watch. My charging cord also stopped working after a couple months causing the watch to die. I thought maybe the watch was defective but testing the cord to see no power was pulling so I had to purchase replacements. Since then, the watch has been functioning as before!

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
This watch is much better than I expected. The amount of info it records is very good considering it is mid range as far as pricing goes. It does everything I need and more. Its amazing what all can be learned from constantly monitoring your pulse. The step counter and stair flight count are amazingly accurate. It also does a good job tracking things like sleep and movement while in bed at night. The graphs the app records could be a little more detailed but it gives you a good overall view of how you are doing once you research about different ways to interpret the data. It would be great if it would show the entire night on one graph instead of showing by the day as it would make it easier to see the shape of your pulse overnight. Small price to pay though, it still helps to learn about things that effect sleeping and how refreshed you feel in the morning.

I'm not really into sports or training so most of the activities go unused but it's worth every penny just for the constant heart rate monitor and other non activity things it does. I find it my go to watch all the time now and makes a mechanical watch seem almost useless in comparison. I mostly use it for work so I don't need to pull my phone out while on a ladder or in some other less than safe situation at work.

The charging port is not the easiest to plug in being in the back of the watch but it only needs a charge once every three days or so. Only takes about an hour to charge back to full as well so there is little down time.

I also really like the different watch faces you can use. It lacks a lot of good faces that mimic real designer watch faces but has some very useful ones that are totally free so that's great. I'd like to be able to make my own custom faces but it isn't easy to find any anything telling you how that can be done.

Overall I recommend this watch to anyone wanting a good smartwatch. I have small wrists and it looks good on me, which many men's watches are way too bulky for me so that is a big plus. If I were to buy another smart watch I'd probably go for the vivoactive 3 music version over this one as it's the newer version but I'm more than pleased by this one.

Review:
I was excited about this product when I read about it. I had been looking for a watch, and HR monitor. i have had it for about a week and am contemplating returning it because I am disappointed.

I will start with the good stuff. It is intuitive and quick to customize the clock and background. There is additional software that needs to be installed on your phone and computer, and you will need a created Samsung account to register the device. The HR monitor seems to be accurate, and the display is easy to read. The Bluetooth range is pretty far (~20ft) and reconnects well. The sleep tracker works well. It is easy to retrieve and delete your notifications. The pedometer works as well as the stopwatch and timer. I haven't used the media controller, but it is pretty simple so it probably works well.

My problem is with the exercise tracking. The walking recording works fine, but only seems to send the average HR to the software. I haven't been able to get the cycling to work because the watch can never connect to the GSP on my phone. This could be due to the fact that I have endomondo running to track the ride. That said the software only works on the phone (not the PC), only graphs average HR for the exercise and has no export option. There are articles on the web about an integration with endomondo, but it hasn't happened. It would be nice if there were some generic command that would record your HR and a timestamps that you could then export to your fitness tracking software and categorize during the export process. There is no way to tell what your HR is during a specific time in your workout which is useful information if you are trying to spend different amounts of time in a particular HR zone (among other useful comparisons you can't do).

The battery life is shorter than three days, and I have not found a way to check it without plugging it in to charge.

So, if you want an non clunky watch to get notifications from your phone and track your steps; buy this. If you want a HR monitor that you can use the HR data to make your workouts better; don't buy this.

Review: Purchased as a gift for my husband, who wanted an affordable smart watch but not an Apple Watch. We previously owned 3 Garmin Vivofit 2 units (kids included) so I liked the brand. I passed my vivofit 2 on to my husband when I was gifted an Apple Watch. Because he wore my size Regular vivofit, I guessed this Regular would fit him. I was wrong. :( He was only able to strap it to the last notch. Since I purchased this a few months ago on a Deal of the Day, I was outside the return window. I called Garmin and spoke with a pleasant employee, who heard my case. I asked if I could purchase the XL band, and she said they do not sell the bands as the GPS antenna is located IN one of the bands at the connection site on the side. However, she offered to mail me the one side of the XL band that I could replace myself with a screwdriver & the screws included FREE of charge! What awesome customer service! It did the trick and now his fits perfectly. He loves the touch screen (and he has “fat fingers” haha) and has no problems. He gets the weather, his text alerts, emails, and call alerts. You cannot respond to calls/texts/emails from the device but the alerts are nice. As an Apple Watch owner, I’d say that’s the main difference is the ability to respond. He can also control his music play to his Bluetooth speakers or earbuds. I cannot speak to the accuracy or steps/ fitness/ heart rate, but all seems to be working well enough. The band is soft silicone feeling and comfortable. Love that it’s waterproof. The battery life seems great so far after having it nearly a month. He gets at least 5+ days of charge or more. He hasn’t used it for workouts or GPS yet, so I suspect increased use of these functions would lower the battery charge. Pairing with an iPhone 7 and using the app are easy. All of his coworkers were so impressed with it and asking about it. We are pleased and like I said- way to go customer service!

Review: I'm so disappointed that Pebble no longer exists and its software group has been acquired by Fitbit.  I've had two previous fitbit products and they are a joke compared to the Pebble 2.  The Pebble 2 is hands down the best smart watch I have seen.  I love that the screen is always on.  No more awkwardly flicking your wrist trying to see what time it is - just glance at your watch the way you've always been able to look at a watch.  Everything about this watch just works.  Very little configuration required.  Once you have the Pebble app on your phone and have synced the watch up with Bluetooth, you're off and running.  You can customize the watch facing and what alerts are received on your watch, but its super intuitive.  Alerts begin to show up on your watch as they're received on your phone.  If there's a notification you no longer want to receive, then just turn off notifications from that service from within the Pebble app.  Otherwise, you get all of your notifications right on your wrist - no hunting around - it just works.  Lots of apps to customize your watch as well.  Some folks have said that the heartrate is not accurate.  I haven't noticed it being off by much, but I didn't expect that much either.  I previously had a fitbit Charge HR and it is every bit as accurate as that was.  I really wasn't expecting that much.  But the pedometer works great and gives you helpful stats each day indicating whether you walked more or less than your average.  The Pebble Health app also offers great charts.  If you want to use the fitbit or Apple Health app, you can have it send data to those apps as well.  I truly hope that Fitbit leverages their new resources with this acquisition because the Pebble 2 is such a superior product to everything they offer.  As for now, this watch still works as designed.  Hopefully Fitbit will continue to keep the Pebble servers running and I will be able to wear this for years to come.

Review:
What I Liked:
-Lightweight
-Stylish and doesn't scream "IM A SMARTWATCH"
-Always on low-powered display that tells you the time and date
-Changeable watch face designs, provided by Sony
-Long lasting battery
-IP57 Weather & Dust Resistant

What I didn't like:
-Cheap factory watch strap that attracts lint and dust
-Low Resolution Screen
-Clunky Apps and App installation methods
-Loud Vibrating alert mechanism that has no sensitivity control
-No Speaker
-No Microphone

My Thoughts:
Like anything 'new-tech', at the time for those who will be buying smart watches in the next few months, are early adopters.

There will be bugs, there will be defects and there will be something better around the corner in the following months that will do it better, faster and last longer.

That being said, the Sony Smart Watch 2 is a neat device that gives you an extension of notifications from your Smart Phone to your wrist.

It is packaged with an awful, cheap, and dust/dirt attracting watch band that fellow reviewers complain about breaking easily after use. I agree, it does seem very fragile. Quit being cheap Sony and bundle this baby with a decent watch strap!

Why did I choose this watch over its competitors?

Unlike the Galaxy Gear by Samsung, the Sony Smart Watch 2 battery lasts longer, about 4 days per charge of average use versus 48 hours as many report with the Galaxy Gear. The Sony is weather/water/dust resistant according to IP57 standards. So I could get away with wearing this watch in the shower, outdoors in crazy weather, and the occasional splash of water whilst the washing of my hands.

Additionally, the Sony Smart Watch 2 has a 'Low-Power' reflective display that enables the device to always show the current time and day-date on the watch face at a glance whether on your wrist or the table. The Galaxy Gear has to powered on by a defined motion or the pressing of the watches button.

I honestly like being able to glance down quickly at my wrist and see the time versus doing anything else to produce the display to show me what day and time it is. This feature of the Sony is simply genius and executed beautifully and legible even in the lowest of ambient light. When it gets pitch black dark, simply press the button on the Sony's side and the display is illuminated.

Water & weather resistance, a longer battery life, and the ability read the watch face at a glance without needing any additional input is why I chose the SW2 over Galaxy Gear. Albeit Sony's watch has a lower-res display and you cannot place or take calls via the watch itself. I found myself entering predefined text message replies so I could at least respond to incoming texts. There is no on-screen keyboard provided by Sony's apps. There are some that you can purchase by developers in the Google Play market.

Pebble smart watch just looks like an etch-a-sketch with an ugly and bulky fascia. Perhaps the Pebble Steel will fancy it up a notch, well the fascia anyway. I like a screen, a coloured screen. I am biased as I purely discredited the Pebble for it's e-paper ink display type. The cool feature I like of the new Pebble Steel is a built in status LED that can notify you of missed calls, emails, and messages.

I use this watch with my Samsung Note 3. It is awkward because you have to download apps within the app for apps on the watch. You download an app that is made by Sony that syncs the Apps to your watch via bluetooth and also handles any firmware updates to the watch itself. The Gmail app keeps crashing on my Galaxy Note 3 and tends to bug out on the phone as well.

Sony just pushed it's Smart Watch 3 blueprints through to the FCC. Expect a new Sony Smart Watch 3 in the coming weeks or months. Additionally the new Galaxy Gear 2 will be out. Here we go again!

Personally, I am going to wait and see what else is brought to the table, that is unless I can score the Sony, Pebble, or Galaxy older models at a price under $100.

Any questions or comments, post away I'll be more than happy to help.

Review:
Overall, this is a great smart watch for folks who want to actively monitor their health.

PROs:
Amazing battery life – it has been two weeks straight out of the package, and I have not yet charged this.
Adjustable straps – shorter strap was easy to install (with the help of a pencil) for smaller wrists.
Phone app is very intuitive – easy to install and navigate. Really liked the heart rate and seeing the data trends.
Comfortable – not too bulky, soft materials for all day and night use.

CONs:
Pedometer not very accurate – I used my old Fitbit One at the same time, and this watch did not seem to count all of the same steps. Often, it was off by 1,000+ steps every day.
Weather app unclear – I could not figure out how or where the weather information was coming from in the watch’s app, it was not accurate with what is shown on my phone.
Could not find a way to download new face screens – although the watch comes with several options to choose from, I would have liked to find a customized version to download that met all of my preferences.
Sync with texts and messages not consistent – sometimes I would get notifications for texts and emails, but it was not consistent, even when the phone and watch were near each other.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:fbf812cc2467f0106e91f8d0a2505d9e]] Ticwatch is being made by Mobvoi, a chinese manufacturer backed by Google. I recently left the apply OS environment and bought a Pixel 2. That also meant selling my beloved Apple Watch Series 2 and finding a replacement for it.

After a lot of research I decided to go with the Ticwatch E. Mobvoi previously had created the Ticwatch 2 which was crowdfunded through Kickstarter and received great reviews. It featured a unique touchbar on the right side of the watch that allowed users to effortlessly scroll through content. Unfortunately, that bar didn't make it into the Ticwatch E but instead Mobvoi listened to the userfeedback and switched their previously proprietary operating system over to Google's Android Wear 2.0. This was a great decision, imho, as it allows us users to now install apps from the appstore (like 2 factor authentication apps, for instance).

The Ticwatch E comes with a 1.2Ghz dual core processor from MTK. It's not a Snapdragon Wear 2100, but so far it has been zippy and worked well for me. It has a bright 1.4" OLED display with 400x400 pixels (that's 287 dpi) in resolution.

Even though the watch is an entry level model priced at $159 for the express version, it features a heart rate monitor, a proximity sensor, an acceleropmeter, a gyroscope, wifi, and onboard GPS. It's also IP67 rated which allows you go under water up to 3 feet for up to 30 min. I wouldn't go for a swim, but you technically don't have to take off the watch when you take a shower or wash your dishes.

One of the great advantages of Android Wear over Apple Watch OS is the sheer unlimited amount of watch faces. While the Apple Watch only has a couple that come preinstalled, on Android you have literally hundreds of options to choose from. Many of these feature complications like the calendar app, weather, step count, or your heart rate.

The watch is an entry level watch, but the materials feel like they are good quality. The processor is zippy and the display is bright with beautiful colors. Android Wear 2.0 is a great operating system.

Personally, I love that they put the button of the watch on the left side, easy to reach for my right thumb. It also prevents me from accidently triggering the button when I bend my wrist, something that happened many times with my Apple Watch.

The watch has a speaker and a microphone, should you want to make phone calls on your wrist. I've only used this feature 4 times in two years of wearing a smart watch, but if this is something you care about - you have that option. The battery has 300mah - a bit less than an apple watch 2. In my experience you'll have to charge the watch every night - but I'm ok with that and hope that future generations will have longer battery life.

The watch weights 41.5g and is 13.55mm thick - it's very comparable to my Casio G-shock I've been wearing for years. For the price I'm quite happy with the watch. It's an entry level Android Wear 2.0 device that will serve you well. Is it the same built-quality as an Apple Watch Series 2 or 3? Of course not, but it's also about a third of the price while having almost the same feature set (except for ip68 water proof rating and lack of sim card slot which would cost you an additional $10/month with your phone provider).

One more thing: Ticwatch also offers the Ticwatch S for Sport. The only difference is that the GPS antenna is in the wristband which increases accurracy. It costs $199 isntead of $159 and also means you can't change your wristband. To me personally, I prefer the E over the S, but if you run around in the city or woods a lot, you might consider the S over the E.

I hope this was useful to many of you. I'll update my review in the coming days after having tried out the watch a bit more. If you feel like you want to buy a Ticwatch through mobvoi's website, feel free to use my referral code, which will save you an additional $20: BUSRRA

Review:
I've owned the Ticwatch Pro for about a week now, having purchased it after deciding that my previous smartwatch, a Pebble Time had gotten too scratched and beat up to use, in addition to wanting some more modern features that the time doesn't have, such as heart rate and NFC payments.
I shopped around for a while before purchasing this watch as I felt that it is still the only watch available that can compete with the Pebble's 4+ days of battery with normal usage. (So far with the Ticwatch I have been getting 2 days with light usage.)
I enjoy the high quality design, with a metal frame and solid feel. It is significantly larger than the watch I had before and I have rather thin wrists, however I have not had much trouble adjusting to the size. (It just feels slightly larger and heavier).
I also like the fact that it takes the same 22mm watch bands and that the watchband is easily replaceable.

While I do enjoy this watch, I have had a couple issues that I feel I should mention. First of all is a bug where the tilt to wake setting resets after I charge the watch. I usually have tilt to wake turned off to save battery and to avoid the bright screen at night. However after I charge the watch sometimes the setting will turn itself back on again. I have yet to determine what this is occurring.
I have also noticed that the watch is sometimes a bit slow at transferring between the secondary crystal display and the AMOLED screen. It is still very fast and only takes a fraction of a second to transition however it is not instant and is somewhat noticeable.

One feature which I doubt was intentional however I very much enjoy is that the magnets that hold the watch to the charger while it's charging also do a very nice job of holding the charger to a metal object. I live on a ship so the walls in my room are metal and I can have the watch stuck to the wall while it's charging which is very convenient.
I also enjoy that it is rated IP68 for water and dusk protection so I am not worried about taking it to the beach or being around areas where it will get wet. Also being able to control music while in the shower is an important part of my smartwatch use case.

Overall I am pretty pleased which the Ticwatch Pro. It's still not perfect, however it's the best thing on the market so far and I'm satisfied with it for the price.

Review:
I bought both the Forerunner 235 and the Vivoactive 3 Music. I ended up returning the Forerunner 235, though I think both watches have their merits. As background, I'm a distance runner (half marathon to 50k) but have also dabbled in triathlons and actually cross-train most of the week.

Why to Pick the Forerunner 235:
- The Forerunner 235 latches onto GPS signal immediately, while I've had to wait several minutes with the Vivoactive 3, especially if I've changed geographical location (like one country/state to another). After that initial GPS signal search, the Vivoactive 3 still takes 30ish seconds to find signal.

Why to Pick the Vivoactive 3:
- Watch feels more "modern," probably due to the touchscreen and sleeker design (Forerunner 235 feels very plastic-y and bulky). The Garmin Vivoactive 3 Music was released in 2018, while the Forerunner 235 is from 2015, so the Vivoactive 3 is newer technology for about the same price.
- The heart rate monitor of the Forerunner 235 actually juts out a little, and it sticks into your wrist, which is uncomfortable if you're wearing the watch for an extended period of time. The Vivoactive 3 HR sensor doesn't jut out, so it's much easier to wear.
- The Vivoactive 3 can track more activities; I was particularly interested in the swimming stats (pace per 100m, average number of strokes, etc). Note that in swimming mode, HR is disabled. Also, the swimming function is currently pretty inaccurate. Despite changing pool distance settings, I've gotten total distance numbers more than twice as long as I've actually swum! In a 25m pool, numbers were spot on, however. Hopefully this function improves with updates though.

The Bottom Line:
If you're a hardcore runner who wants fast GPS signal and doesn't care to wear the watch for extended periods of time as a fitness tracker, I recommend the Forerunner 235. If you do other sports too and want a comfortable watch to wear all day, go with the Vivoactive 3.

Review: Great as a budget, but quality, fitness tracker. The screen of my hand me down Fitbit charge 2 died and I compared my alternatives. I want this more for overall trends in activity, sleep & heart rate than the specific details. I’m out in the rain a lot throughout the year too so water resistance was important even if I’m not actually wearing it while I swim. I like the Garmin app interface more than Fitbit. I had to enter in my typical sleep times & it’s good at detecting sleep around & in that range but it won’t detect a nap in the middle of the day. I needed the “sensitive” sleep setting on Fitbit & there isn’t a comparable setting so the sleep tracking might be a little less accurate for me. It does pick up biking pretty well though and it properly labels biking vs walking (without starting a workout/changing activity). I haven’t tried the GPS part out yet. The floors count sometimes doesn’t seem to pick up everything consistently (I live in a 3rd floor apartment) but it’s accurate most of the time. Garmin has a formula that determines a step goal for you, which is kind of interesting. It’s hard to consistently meet it since it will keep increasing but if meeting goals is important to you, you can also manually set it. I was a little worried about not having the ability to change/replace the band but so far I haven’t seen any indicators that this will be a problem later. I only see a battery indicator on the touchscreen when plugging it into the charger. That’s a little inconvenient, but manageable (I mostly charge it while I shower, even though it should be water resistant; a good way to remember and still get most activity & sleep tracking). I compared a bunch of fitness trackers and saw a lot of reasons for people choosing one over another (ie this one has GPS so you can go for a run without your phone vs some need you to carry your phone too; but, it might not handle interval workouts as well as others). My advice is to think about the specific features that are important to you & go with a fitness tracker that meets those requirements.
