Review:
I finally purchased the Garmin Vivosmart after reading/watching a lot of product reviews on youtube and other sites and I am not disappointed. It was always between the FitBit Charge HR and the Garmin Vivosmart and I am very happy that I decided to go with the Vivosmart. Also I was reading a lot of reviews of the newer Fitbit still giving people skin rashes and so it was always a safer bet to trust a company with a long pedigree of making wearable fitness devices without any similar issues.Most of my colleagues at work have the Fitbit trackers and to be fair that is a great fitness tracker. But I was always looking for something more than just a tracker and the Vivosmart does a great job at that. I love the notifications that I get on the device from my Iphone. When I go into meetings or am in outdoor areas where you might not hear your phone ring but definitely can feel the intense buzz on your wrist which is not uncomfortable as well.
All notifications that are on your phone will be alerted on the Vivosmart. I love the move bar which tells you to get up and move after an hour of sitting down. More so the fact that it doesn't reset if you take a few steps (which really beats the purpose of it being there in the first place) but a substantial 100 steps or more is required to reset the move bar. I normally take a good walk to the water cooler area/coffee station and the move bar resets. It doesn't automatically sense sleep mode which I heard that the Fitbit does, but you will have to put it into the sleep mode and that stops all notifications which is nice and only starts back again after you wake up and awaken your device as well. The pedometer is accurate as it the HR monitor with the included chest strap. Though I am not sure about the calories burnt information. I have since read on forums that it may still need some fixing. The sleep analysis is pretty basic, not as good as the Fitbit app, but that is fine with me. I think Garmin will add more s/w updates along the way that should probably address these. The waking up alarm buzzing function is great. I bought it around six days ago and its not left my wrist ever since that day except for an hour or so for charging. Very comfortable on the wrist with its small footprint which easily slides under the shirt sleeve.
With the Fitness tracker arena exploding in recent times, its just a matter of time before better devices will flood the market but for the moment I think this device is the best one out there offering a nice combo of fitness tracker and smart phone notifications.

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
If you're looking to use this watch and hoping its a better fitbit, with some added features to help you look at your pace and cadence for your running, I'd go with something else. The heart rate monitor doesn't really work well at all, I can't actually track my activity without buying a seperate heart rate strap to go with it. Even with the heart rate strap they're known to have issues connecting and keeping together.

While its nicer overall as a watch and what you can do with the faceplate, when it comes to activity tracking it's sub par and it's battery will be zapped away if you don't adjust the settings properly.

Music:
If you want it for the music aspect it's very slow to sync over a wifi connection it can take hours for it to attempt to build the library and then sync to make sure it has everything it needs. It also completely drains the battery so it needs to be connected to power if you want to complete the process. If you want to use the watch to skip music through controlling your phone expect there to be a few seconds of lag when switching songs and the watch displaying what song is playing. So if you skip a few songs in a row it can take a good while for it to catch up on the display.

Sleep:
On several occasions i've had my watch not track my sleep, or take several hours after I had woken up to process that I had slept. It doesn't track my awake time, and generally just puts a blanket time. The sleep function is not something I would consider accurate by any means and I wouldn't use this over the cheapest fitbit when it comes to tracking my sleep.

Heart rate:
Heart rate tends to randomly drop or get stuck at a certain rate and doesn't actually track what your current heart rate is unless you readjust the watch. When working out the watch will regularly just sit at half of my actual heart rate based off the cardio machine i'm on. Unless i tighten the band to almost cut off circulation and put it in the exact right spot and hope it doesn't move it won't track my actual heart rate during my work out. A cheap fitbit will work much better and much more accurately. Even if you get a heart rate strap from garmin to use with the watch there's no guarantee that it will work properly. The straps are known to not maintain a connection properly or to die very quickly.

Weather:
Weather is very unreliable as an app, and almost never loads on my watch. I don't understand why it doesn't sync properly. I rarely have luck actually having the weather consistently come up.

Floors:
Not very accurate when noticing going up or down floors. I don't know what the set reading is, but while a fitbit can read too many floor, the Garmin reads about half the amount that it should.

Calories:
Due to the inaccurate heart rate sensor, your calories won't generally be accurate. I also don't know the the baseline they use for "resting" calories, as its much higher than what fitbit uses and much higher than my tested resting metabolic rate. Even while conducting activities if it picks up your heart rate appropriately it doesn't seem to maintain an accurate count based off several other metrics used to calculate your general amount of calories burned based off general heart rate.

Apps:
A lot of apps and watch faces exist for the garmin line up of watches. However not every one will work even if it says it's compatible with the watch. You'll have to test and see since you might not have all of the metrics to get a real understanding of the data it's trying to provide.

I'd rate this watch at an overall disappointment. I expected it to at least compete if not be a little better than a 100$ fitbit but I was very disappointed when it was unable to track the same metrics with even close to the same amount of accuracy. I'd stick to a more specific line of garmin when it comes to trying to use it for activity such as the forerunner. The vivoactive doesn't seem to meet the mark at all.

Review:
The first watch is an Amazfit GTR 3 Pro costing $240 which I bought some time ago. The next photo is this watch in its box at $60. As it turns out, these 2 watches are similar in many ways except for cost. Both have numerous sports mode, heart rate, SPO2, steps, stress, and sleep monitoring features.

I have a number of health problems so I was interested to see how the R11 compared in performance to the GTR. I like having access to health data on my wrist! Generally the R11 gave slightly higher numbers in step, heart rate, and SPO2 measurements and comparable sleep results. This did not bother me as I see all non-medical measurement devices as relative indicators giving signals of medical problems to be checked out by a doctor. The R11 blood pressure measurements, however, were not consistent will my $150 blood pressure monitor. Its number varied little even when my other monitor showed widely varying numbers. I trust that the manufacturer will provide an upgrade its BP algorithm at some point to make this a non-issue.

I recently began using a walker and discovered one area where the R11 excelled. The R11 was far more accurate in counting steps when I exercise with my walker. My guess is that the R11 relies more on the GPS data. The GTR evidently does not count a step if the arms do not swing! The app for the R11 is quite good and informative.

Bottom-line, the R11 is a great smart watch at its price point and a keeper for me especially when I walk laps with my walker! Who said a 77 year-old man can't wear a watch on each arm?

But just like the original pebble it starts out with less than optimal amount of apps but this one got a lot more much quicker than the original. I will say that the band that come with it for dirty lookign quickly.  At first when it was an uneven dirtiness it looked bad but now it looks like weathered leather.  So it's kinda of. A neat look.  The bevel is large which I thought would be a huge negative but it isn't.  I never notice.  I really thought I would and it would bug the crap out of me but it doesn't in any way.  My husband has the Apple Watch.  Yes his does more than this watch but for 4 times the cost and 2 times the bulk and one third of the style, half the battery life and no concern of people following jacking me to steal my watch.  I'm a huge Apple fan.  It's disgusting how many Apple products we have in our home and at work.  So for me to like this, not a small thing.  You may not like it but for someone's first venture into smart watch ownership, this is a very solid choice if asthetics are important to you.  If you're cool with the pebble original asthetics, go for that one as a first foray.  I kind of liked the geek chic of my old one.  This one is more professional

Review:
This is my first smart watch ever and I agonized over whether to even get one (still not convinced of what use cases it covers, that my phone cannot cover for) - anyways, the TicWatch Pro offered me an opportunity to try it out, for a reasonably low price, and so I took the plunge. Along with the price, the convincing factor was the promise of a long battery life - I have a total dread of charging my phone every night - so the promise of 5 days of battery life on a single charge tipped the scales.

My experience so far, after a few weeks of owning and using the watch, is mixed.

On the plus side:
I definitely love the technical solution of having dual screens - the technologist in me applauds the out of the box thinking and I can see it add towards battery life. Most situations I use the watch as I would a normal watch - just to see the time/date and the LCD screen suffices for this purpose. I love that I can enter the Smart Watch mode by just a flick of the wrist - seems pretty nifty. The setup and configuration was a breeze, and easy enough. My experience with the battery life is mixed - never got the advertised 5 days of life, it's more like 2 days for the Smart mode, and then 2 more days on the Essential mode. Still more than what my friends get on their Samsung/Apple watches, but not what is advertised. I am sure there are settings I can tweak or features I can stop using that will extend the battery life, but I have not discovered them yet. I do like having Google Assistant on my wrist to answer questions and bring up reminders from my calendar.

The cons:
The interface seems a bit laggy, and also seems to need a very firm tap in order to register. I am used to a feather touch on my phone screen triggering an action and this watch needs more than just a touch - it needs a firm press. I am not sure if this is because of the dual screen design. Also after tapping on a menu option and getting to see items under it takes a few seconds, the response is not as quick as I thought it would be (I have actually seen a "loading..." indicator a few times)
The app store is not very well stocked. This is not a TicWatch fault, its the nature of the WearOS ecosystem.
The TicWatch manual can be a lot better, though. The current manual just about walks you through the initial setup and that is it. It would have been nice to get some additional detail about settings and tweaks and controls that can let one use the watch optimally. I got more details browsing the Mobvoi forums that through the manual.

All in all - I am satisfied with my new Smart Watch. It could be better, I am sure, but for a first time user, its a good enough watch and serves my purpose.

Garmin has told me it is my fault for not buying a more expensive watch. They said that 10 hours of battery life was to be expected and was reasonable for a watch that is advertised as lasting 13 hours. They replaced it twice for this issue but now tell me it isn’t even a problem. This is after I submitted all the report files so they could see exactly how it was used. Dead to me. I’m shopping for a new watch and a bicycle computer. Neither will be from Garmin. Dead to me.

Review:
So far I really like this smart watch. The watch is big, but it's comfortable and not too big for a medium wrist size and up. I like the way this watch looks. This paired relatively easily, but Not on the first several tries with the QR CODE. I finally gave up on the QR code and Installed the app, and then discovered the watch on blue tooth. With the app you do have to register for a user account etc. After installing and getting the user account setup in the app, I was then able to manually add the watch from the app, which worked the first time.

This seems to be way more accurate than my Amazfit BIP on step counting. Doing a 4 mile walk gave me 8588 steps, and at the end of the day with normal activity I had 10K+ steps. My Amazfit usually only show 6700 steps at the end of a 4 mile walk and 8000 by the end of the day. I pretty much just use steps as a guide to my overall activity, so not a big deal, but it feels more accurate which is nice.

The other fuctions, like Blood pressure seem off quite a bit, but again, I don't think the wrist is an appropriate place to measure BP, so again, I just use this has a guide, but I like that it is a continual and automatic reading.

I have made a phone call from the watch, it's not great, but works in a pinch. The mic is just ok, and the speaker is clear enough, but overall not as good as a Apple watch. Not a big deal for me, since I use ear buds or headphones.

There is really nothing irritating or unusual about the functions on this watch, the screen works and is nice and clear with decent resolutions. It works as advertised and is reasonably priced.

My only slight problem is the ability for custom watch faces doesn't let you display all the stats you want so you have to select from a list of faces and functions to turn on and it's limited.

Overall for the price, the watch looks nice performs well and give you about 5-6 days of battery. If you use the always on function it will shorten down to maybe 2-3 days. I can't really give it 5 stars, but a good solid 4, as there are so many other watches in this price range and I can't compare them all.

It comes with a bonus 2 wrist bands, extra strap pins and a charge cable. Overall a good value sports watch.

Review:
I've been waiting for a first in class Wear OS smartwatch to be released for a while. I tried my hardest with previous attempts like the Sony Smartwatch and the LG watch sport and both failed in one way or the other. I think the biggest complaint with previous offerings was the battery life and the ability or inability to connect to GPS to track runs.

The Mobvoi Ticwatch Pro has awesome battery life due to the use of two screens... The color Smartwatch screen turns off when not in use. When I twist my wrist to check the time, the color screen activates and shows me all my notifications, time, steps, etc. I easily get 1.5 days at least on one charge. The charger that comes with charges quickly so, charging when I'm in the shower gets me through the day. I wear my watch at night using the ticsleep app to track the quality and quantity of sleep. It does last the night! When the battery gets really low, the smart watch part of the device totally shuts down leaving the monochrome screen only that tells time and tracks steps. L... And it sips battery at that point.

The pulse measurement is good and GPS accuracy is on par with other non Garmin tracking watches. It gets you in the ball park. If you want professional grade tracking this is not the watch for you.

I had no problem activating with Verizon. It does not suck the battery as the mobile connection only activates when needed... As in when it is not connected to the phone or WiFi. So rarely. But it's nice to have for the phone call I might miss when I don't have my phone on me.

It's less bulky than the LG watch sport and more like a conventional sports watch. I don't feel like I'm on parole wearing one of those tracking bracelets.

All the suite of Google apps and Google Assistant features work like a charm... I can dictate or type text responses with ease.

I'm very happy with this purchase and Mobvoi has followed up with my Amazon purchase to notify me that Verizon had started activating the watch on their service. They even included detailed instructions on how to activate it with Verizon. I can tell by this and other communications that they care about the customer experience.

Review:
Update to review below: Garmin sent me a replacement watch because barometer wasn't accurately recording my ascents (while biking). Kudos to Garmin for the quick replacement. Even though the watch was less than two weeks old, and I could have easily swapped it for a new one on Amazon, I preferred to work with Garmin to ensure I was not sent another defective unit. Note that there is a systemic issue here: Garmin should notify Amazon which watches are defective (they know this by the serial number). Customers should not have to go through the need to replace at all. Having said that, Garmin was good about providing a pre-paid return shipping label. I paid a deposit of $299 so that they would ship immediately, and when I returned the defective one, they quickly reversed the above charge. Estimation of calories burned (see below) is still a mystery to me, as the VA3 measures about half the calories of my Edge 810. (Technical note: after two weeks of use, the VA3 has measured an average of 48 percent of the calorie estimate provided by my Edge 810 during the same bike rides.) Both are Garmin products. My guess is that they use different algorithms. Okay, I can deal with that. I look at the trend over time, more than the absolute level anyway.

Unfortunately, after some further testing, I have found that the barometer on the replacement watch does not appear to be measuring ascents and descents correctly. The VA3 is consistently less than the Edge 810, ranging anywhere from 55 percent to 88 percent of the ascent estimated by the Edge. Garmin tech support looked into this, but eventually gave up, saying there were too many variables for them to be able to explain why the Edge 810 and VA3 give different results. I don't buy this explanation, by the way.

Original review:

I like this fitness tracker/smart watch for its versatility. I bought it primarily for tracking my swimming, as once you tell it how long the pool is, it can monitor how many feet you swim. It also provides a measure of your swimming efficiency (SWOLF), which is based on how fast you swim a lap and how many strokes you take. It also has a very accurate GPS tracker, which can tell you how far you run, walk, or bike.

I've never had a smart watch before, so I particularly like the feature that alerts you to incoming texts, and even shows the message on your watch screen. I have an iPhone, so I can't respond, but just knowing who the texts are from and what they're saying is very helpful.

I'm not sure I'll ever use Garmin Pay. I don't really see this as a useful feature.

Customer service is excellent, as I discovered from having to contact them. I was curious as to how the Garmin Vivoactive 3 (VA3) compared to my Garmin Edge 810, so I took them both on an outdoor bike ride. The watch easily paired with my heart rate strap and cadence sensor. When I got home I compared calories and ascent on the VA3 vs the Edge. To my surprise, the VA3 measured about half the calories and ascent that the Edge measured. I contacted tech support for help. They promptly responded. Unfortunately, I discovered that there is a known issue with certain barometers not working properly, and hence not measuring ascent properly. Still unresolved, however, is why the calorie estimate on the VA3 is half of that on the Edge 810, but tech support asked me to retest once I get the replacement watch.

If you're concerned about your watch, make a note of the serial number, and call or email Garmin tech support to see if it's one of the known defective units. If you have a way to compare ascent with a reliable unit, you could also do that.

The watch itself is very handsome. I wear it all day long.

One last point: I compared my heart rate using a chest strap (polar watch and strap) vs the VA3 while on my wrist during a 50-minute indoor spin session. I was pleasantly surprised at how close the two were. The average heart rate (bpm) over the 50-minute period on my VA3 was almost identical to the Polar (based on chest strap). When I would glance down to compare contemporaneous heart rates, they were always very close. Congrats to Garmin on a remarkable, wrist-based heart rate monitor.

Review:
I expected too much regarding this Ticwacht E smartwatch, since all the youtube reviews talk so good about it. The truth is, I'm dissapointed. Almost everything that states it can do.. it can't do. I’ve been using it for more than a week now me (with my Huawei p20 pro). First of all lets start with the hardware: The product is good looking, nice plastic black band, very confortable, it is light weight and it combines pretty much with any look. The desing is very well accomplished. This is why i’m giving at least 3 stars.
On the other hand, the software.. this is the part where it gets frustrating to me. It took me 4 days to get it running almost normal, since I had to look for tricks or recommendation from people on youtube with similar problems. I had to restore it to the fabric settings once, after that it worked better. The battery got better after letting it die at least 3 times, now I get between 29/30 hours of battery life. Google assistant never worked properly, this is why I decided to buy this watch on the first place. The “ok Google” never worked. “Call mom” or “send a text to..” never worked Good, it always said “cant reach phone” or something, even though the watch is connected to the phone with bluethoot (and also wifi). Other major issue to me is the delay on the notification of incall coming to the phone (25 seconds at least), the phone starts ringing but the watch does not react instantly, which I think is ridiculous, this is so basic! You CAN make calls from the watch with you contatcs sync, but recieving calls is not the same, don’t know why (I looked it up online). Also, you can download many apps from the play store, but let me tell you, they are not very Good, most of them you have to open the phone in order to sync, like Spotify for example; you can't do anything if the phone is locked, you need to wake you phone, open the Spotify app on your phone and only then you can kind of play music, but on the watch you only can pause/play, go to the next song and stuff, you can't select a specific song from a playlist or an álbum (I’ve donwloaded another app call wearify, which Works better) and forget about downloading songs in order to play them without the phone. Regarding notifications like text messages, whatsapp or emails, works OK, also with a Little delay but not too bad, you can reply on the watch and most of the time is succesfull but some times it fails. On whatsapp notifications you can see emojis and still stickers.
So, overall it’s a decent smartwatch, but the wear OS system needs more work, it doesn’t feel smooth enough, maybe I’m too demanding. I recommend spend a Little more money on another watch that can run everything without having to restoure the watch or install some update or re-iniciate the phone or whatever, it all should be easier. Personally I’m considering changing this smartwatch for a Samsung active watch which is more expensive, but I think it won’t have this kind of failures.

Review:
I was on the lookout for a Fitbit alternative. My Flex battery stopped holding a charge, and I hate that I needed to replace the entire device just for a dying battery. That's the primary reason I opted for the Garmin Vivofit. It fit my price range, had the features I care most about, and most importantly, has a replaceable battery.

Unfortunately, the Vivofit 2 did not do it for me. I didn't like that I had to remember to press a button for the device to register the start and end of activity (the Fitbit did that automatically) and I had major issues with my tracker not syncing with my phone or iPad. Another thing I loved with my Fitbit was the community/competition aspect because it truly does help me to stay motivated to be active. With Garmin you can compete with strangers, but it wasn't the same for me. Also, Garmin's customer service can use some improvement as well. I needed help with the app and I could not find any answers online or in the forums. I was bounced around to at least three different agents before I got a somewhat satisfactory response.

I ended up returning the Vivofit 2 and got myself a Fitbit Charge when it was on sale. It's too bad because I still hate the idea of needing to replace the entire tracker for a dead battery, but every other feature for the Charge fits my personal needs. If Garmin can improve its app and more of my circle of friends/family would invest in a Garmin, I'd probably give them another go. They just aren't quite there yet.

Review:
This item was ordered for my wife. She has used a Fitbit One for years. It was not very good at handling moisture, and had been replaced several times. Fitbit was great about the warranty btw. When she had her HR+ a few days and I had a chance to look at it and see how it worked I went out and bought myself one from Target (sorry Amazon I did not want to wait). We have both had the watches for a couple of weeks, so I can only post my early impressions.

Pros:
-Built-in GPS.
-Water-proof
-Smallest/lightest GPS device that I'm aware of
-Does most fitness functions we want including heart rate
-Always on time display
-Battery life is good, and it charges quickly
-The Connect app lets you customize all the notifications etc. and syncs well with my LG G4 and her Droid Turbo 2

Cons:
-Display harder to read in the dark than in the daytime, even with back lighting (white back light might help in the future?)
-Stairs climbed seem inaccurate, it misses a lot of the stairs we climb. It claims to have a barometric altimeter, but in Garmin Connect it turns elevation corrections on by default which according to that site means it is not using a barometric altimeter. Possibly this is an early software glitch?
-The display face seems fragile, I have not scratched mine, but I'm being very careful until our screen protectors arrive

Bottom line: a great device for those seeking a fitness device that you can also use for your runs or activities on a casual basis. We both have Fenix watches for long runs and ultras, however this watch has basically replaced my Forerunner 220 for the majority of my runs and my Vivosmart both. We like it!

Special Features: Smart Watch Bluetooth 5.0 Activity Tracker with Heart Rate Monitor Sleep Monitor Pedometer
Review: The adage that you get what you pay for certainly applies here. I was very disappointed with this watch. It does not provide much customization. For instance, the Time feature offers one screen option - an analog face. The phone does automatically set the date and time, which is nice. You're supposed to be able to choose Imperial or Metric units, but in the Pedometer mode it only offers Kcal, number of steps, and distance in Km. When I compared it to two other pedometer devices I have, (my phone and my bicycle computer that can be used for hiking also), the Tozo watch always read considerably lower than my other devices, which are quite similar to each other. I've even turned the watch on, walked a loop through my neighborhood counting my steps, then checked the phone. Both the distance and steps were under-reported.  Also, there doesn't seem to be a way to zero-out the step counter, for instance if you wanted to monitor one specific activity. The Sleep function of the Tozo watch is absolutely useless. I have gone to bed, not fallen asleep for a few hours, then checked the watch, which then told me I had slept X number of Deep Sleep hours and Y number of Light Sleep hours. Its control of the music function on your phone is limited to pause/play and next track/prev track. No volume control, no way to access playlists or choose songs. When the watch will actually connect to my phone, it does give notification of new messages, but often the actual message doesn't load for a while. The heart rate monitor seems to be pretty accurate. I really haven't bothered with any other function, like Stopwatch, or Take Photo (the phone is supposed to be able to click the shutter if you have your Smart Phone camera in use), and the Alarm Clock mode just says "No Alarm Clock". Currently, I can no longer connect to the watch. I downloaded the required Tozo app, but when I try to access or sync the watch via the app, it states there is no device connected. Clicking Add Device does not recognize the watch like it did when I first set up the watch. The limited instructions that come with the watch do not indicate how to fix connection problems. How long the cheap, plastic watch band will last is anyone's guess. I have no idea if the watch is waterproof, but I guess I could throw it into the lake and then check on it later. There are other Tozo products that I enjoy, like their wireless earbuds and their power bank, but this watch is terrible.

After reading reviews and looking at the features of the top rated watches i strugggled to pick the right watch. Then I found the Stratos 3. I wasn’t sure if it would be an improvement but for the price I had to give it a try. And I am so glad I did.

This is the best watch. The accuracy of the GPS is spot on. The heart rate measuring is much improved and the time it takes to locate the satellites is much shorter. I am so impressed with this new version. The battery life is fantastic. And thank you for keeping the always on feature. The watch also stays connected to my phone without having to reset it.

For the price you cannot go wrong. It does everything a watch 3 times the cost does and you can play your music from it so you don’t need your phone to go on a run and listen to music.

Review:
This is a great smartwatch if you can get it with the $40 coupon or at discount price. I was able to grab this at the $159.99 price tag with discount. I am comparing a few features with a fitbit Inspire 2 and a Galaxy Watch 4 (GW4) below.

Pros:
- Light weight
- Rounded glass, smooth swipes. Really feels premium. Better even than on GW4
- GW4 bands with dove tails fit and make it look great (see picture)
- Fast GUI and interface, no lags. I was really impressed with the fluidity. On par, if not even better than on GW4
- Nice TicWatch health and fitness tracking features and summaries, no subscription
- Great resting heart rate feature that seems robust. Values slightly higher than Fitbit but a lot more consistent than GW4. Great for tracking health, e.g. oncoming flu or COVID
- Google Pay works great
- Nice to have custom button
- Battery saving screen, albeit limited features
- Great price when purchased with discount ($159.99)

Cons:
No OLED display
- Hard to adjust to correct screen brightness that works all day.
- No auto-brightness
- 'Do not disturb' does not turn DND on phone on or off
- No night mode or bedtime mode function, theatre mode is Okay
- TicOxygen often reports a lot of wide scatter. From concerning to excellent values within a short time period. This was the case for my TicWatch 3 Pro. This needs better tracking, e.g. average of several data points during the night or ensuring only measurement during resting are taken.
- Battery saving screen doesn’t really save much battery. Maybe 5% on conventional clock face from the default ones that are offered. Maybe a with a more demanding clock face this will gain some batter life. Overall battery life is right on par with GW4 and my previous TicWatch 3 Pro with 2 - 2.5 days. Can't get much more out of it.

bottomline: at $200 I'd give it a 4 out of 5 stars. at $160 I am giving it a 5 out of 5 stars

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
NOTE: This is my first Wear OS smartwatch. I've had my watch for 10 days as of the time of writing this review. Day one, with the setup and the like, it looked like the battery would not live up to expectations. After tweaking a few settings and finishing setting up, by the time I'm getting ready for bed, I'm at 70% at the lowest.

The LCD display is casually useful. It has time, date, step counter, and bars reflecting general battery life. It's pretty easy to see even in dim lighting. I do wish the heart rate monitor that were displayed in the advertisements were on it though - it's just not there.

I don't have another watch to compare it to, but the AMOLED display is crisp, clear, and generally responsive. The built in watch faces are of decent quality - a good variety between analog and digital, tinkering options and color variations on some of the watchfaces. You can, of course, add additional watch faces via the Google Play Store.

The app selection I am guessing is standard to Wear OS. The built in GPS, heart rate monitor, and NFC options are useful. It has a mixture of built in Google as well as Mobovi apps. There's some latency periodically when doing something more taxing in the Wear OS environment but, for me, at the worst of times it has been a minor annoyance rather than anything worse.

On the phone (in my case Android) I have both the Wear OS app as well as Mobovi's proprietary app...which I don't really use. Google Fit tends to work just as well for me, and has better phone synchronization options.

Hardware wise, the top bezel definitely has a plastic feel. It cannot rotate and, while it looks nice, it doesn't feel as nice. The body of the watch does have a more solid feel and the base is metal. The buttons have a resolute firm press to them - they will rotate but that does nothing. The strap is a blend of silicone on the underside and leather (whether genuine or synthetic) on the outside - it is comfortable and feels durable.

Overall, for my first Wear OS watch, I feel this will hold up through the years and I am satisfied with my purchase. I will update this further if issues arise.
Attribute_Related_Content:

Review:
TL;DR: Watch is basically perfect for tracking runs and basic workouts.

Purchased this watch as an upgrade to the Withings Steel HR Sport. While that watch was serviceable, it also relied on phone-based GPS and had somewhat unreliable heart rate tracking. This meant that the data gathered during my workouts was highly variable and could only be used as a rough approximation of reality (for example, running the same 4.3-mile route daily would give me distances of anywhere from 4.2 to 4.6 miles with my speed looking like seismometer output and my heart rate measurements sporadically dropping out). I decided after a year of a semi-serious running habit (20-25 miles/week when not injured, sick or otherwise indisposed) to pick this up. In short, it was worth it.
Pros:
The GPS is on point. My 4.3 mile route is 4.3 miles every day without fault, and the map of my run is totally accurate.
Garmin connect presents a ton of useful information. Most workout programs will tell you the aerobic/anaerobic benefit of your workout, and the app tells you your training load and infers your “body battery” from activity and heart rate throughout the day. There are hydration and calorie-counting widgets if that’s your thing. The data visualizations are clear and coherent.
Having music loaded onto the highly waterproof watch removes rain as a potential excuse not to run. Spotify integration works reliably, though it isn’t nearly as easy as simply strapping your phone to you for the run.
The watch face is customizable, and there are custom widgets/workouts you can download from the Connect IQ store.
Battery life is generally pretty good. I’ve only had the watch die on me once, and that might be due to my watch face not really conveying battery info.
Cons:
None for my purposes!

Disappointments:
Google fit integration with Connect would be nice, so my phoneless runs could be added and then sent to Pokemon Go.
My preference is to listen to audiobooks while I run, and this doesn’t seem to be possible with the 245.
I’ve been dealing with achilles tendonitis most of the time I’ve had the watch so I can’t enjoy it as much as I'd like!
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Battery life is generally pretty good. I’ve only had the watch die on me once, and that might be due to my watch face not really conveying battery info.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The watch should have a battery life that is generally good and only rarely dies. It is acceptable if the watch face does not clearly convey battery information.

Review: I'd been looking for a smart watch to help with missing phone calls and notifications from family members and to generally offload some power usage with and time spent on my smart phone. Overall this is doing the trick.  I get more than two full days on a full charge with round the clock usage  ...I like to sleep with it on my wrist, for peeks at what time it is during wakeful hours and for sleep tracking.  On the morning after the second day of use, I place it into the charging dock while getting ready for work and it's ready for another two day run after coffee, showering, breakfast, etc.  I *really* like the bi-material watch band -- very comfortable silicone against my skin and decent looking leather facing the world.  Pulse measurement is accurate enough and activity tracking (mainly road bicycling) allow me to keep my bulkier (and more vulnerable and expensive) cell phone tucked safely in my cycling jersey pocket. Review:
This is a smart buy for a simple day to day budget smart watch. The watch face is a bit bulky for a girls watch, but since it's a smart watch you can't complain as you'd want the face to be functional if it's a touch screen. As I tested it, the touch screen functioned and reacted great. The navigating of menus is a bit clumsy at first to navigate through, but becomes memorable as you use it. The watch connected fine to my android device and received calls and messages. The band it comes with is a soft velvet like silicone that felt nice to wear. I was able to easily customize and switch the watch face to one of the many provided by the company or be able to use my own photos. I use the watch primarily for telling time and viewing messages when I don't want to have my phone on me going out. It does a bunch more than this, but for my needs it's done a great job.

The watch charger is USB, and goes to a magnetic charging point and it's a really short cable. If it dangles or doesn't sit right on the watch it can easily detach from charging. I wish it had a dock to sit on, but if you have a lamp with something like USB plugs next to your bed it should work just fine. The battery lasted a whole week on a single charge for me, but I suspect it's because I don't wear it very often.

So far I'm satisfied with my purchase of this watch.

Cons:

Review: I worked myself up to this purchase because of all the reviews and videos about it only to be let down.  This watch looks nice, feels good in the hand (not on the wrist), and that's about it.  Ok so where do I start... When you open it up you get a nice packaged watch, charger, and all the pamphlets that come with tech.  You then cut it on, download the apps on to your phone to connect it, sync it up, and get it going.  This is the first hiccup, because it's slow and clunky.  It all takes time, everything isn't compatible, and a lot of the apps you want just aren't there.  I personally bought it for fitness, music, and access to my phone and card on my wrist, which you think would be super easy because that's what it's designed to do right, WRONG!  fitness tracking outside of walking or jogging is cumbersome, the Google suite of health apps are practically useless, and when you want to use those things the battery drops quicker than pigeon poo on a freshly waxed car!  Music is another thing I personally didn't like, because the volume didn't go as high as I needed it to go, and again while using this to track workouts, if you're also listening to the music, you have got maybe 1.5 hrs before it dies.  Then for card payments you don't realize how uncomfortable it is twisting your wrist to make the payment, or how used to using your finger you are to unlock the phone and make payments you've become once you have to go into the watch, unlock it, and pay within a time frame, it's INCONVENIENT To say the least.  Then the calls, for some reason the calls would never go to the Bluetooth, so I stopped making calls with this, and just used my phone which for some reason also wouldn't send calls to the Bluetooth because it was connected to the watch.... All that being stated, I returned the watch, I'm now looking at a Fitbit, I don't think we need 75% of these features in a watch if they won't work right, and I think the Fitbits have mastered what they do, give me what I need, and last much longer than other smartwatches.

Review:
My Fitbit Flex recently stopped holding a charge (no more than 48 hours max.) after 2 years. After a less than stellar run in with Fitbit Customer Service, I decided to look outside the Fitbit brand. I have had a Samsung Galaxy for 3 years and love my Samsung phone. After researching several different options, the Gear Fit looked like a may be a good fit into my Samsung lineup. I have now been using it the Samsung Gear Fit Smart Watch for 3 weeks (alongside my dying but still accurate Fitbit) and can say that I truely like it. I was able to pair it with my Samsung Galaxy S7 by downloading the Gear Fit Manager App through Samsung. That app allows me to control what notifications get pushed to the gear fit (phone calls, alarms, text messages, calendar appointments, facebook messenger, google hangout, etc) via bluetooth. S Health, also a Samsung App, links to the Gear Fit via bluetooth to track steps, active minutes and heart rate (when taken - this is not a constant read out). The watch gives a little buzz at 50% and 100% of each goal you are tracking. I try for 60 active minutes and 10,000 steps a day, so I get buzzes at 30 min, 60 min, 5000 steps and 10,000 steps. I am happy to say that the step counter on the Samsung is usually within 300 steps of my Fitbit, usually less. I believe it tracks lower because it only begins counting after 10+ steps, so short walks room to room in your home may not always register. Similar to the Flex, the Gear Fit does not register flights of stairs.

I love being able to know when I am getting a call, without my phone being on me. I have a 30-45 minute commute to work in the morning and back home in the evening. I love that when I forget to turn my phone on ring, I know I have an incoming call or message, by a buzz on my wrist. The same is true when I am sitting in meetings or on conference calls, I can still receive alerts and know if something requires my attention. Getting notified of my phone calls is great since we don't have a landline in our home. I have set the screen to rotate to portrait, making it easier to read messages. No more missing calls because I'm in a different room or outside.

Compared to its competitors at the $100 price range, this is a great buy. This watch does everything that my Fitbit did and then some. There is now a Gear Fit 2 at a higher price point with a few more capabilities but the same app and software compatibility.

Review:
I have experience with Garmin because I have a Garmin motorcycle GPS that is a users nightmare. I had no intention of ever buying another Garmin device but my wife surprised me with a Vivofit 2 as a Christmas gift.. I have been trying to not call support ie. endless waiting and thick accent techs. etc. So far I have been able to set this activity tracker up mostly through trial and error. Sometimes the activity displays on Garmin Connect on the laptop, sometimes not. The syncing works better with the antenna and laptop than it works with my iphone 5. The Garmin app is poor. Neither method is reliable. I am surprised how inaccurate the step counting is. I have measured most of my routes and the Vivofit 2 is very optimistic. That's (bad) optimistic. It counts far more steps and distances than I actually take.

I was at first interested in the Sleep Tracking feature. This technology is not sophisticated in any way. It merely monitors movement. I found that what Garmin considers deep sleep is not R.E.M.sleep. R.E.M. being the most healthy and beneficial. Look it up.:-(

I have read many of the reviews and find that Garmin responds to complaints recommending to the user to contact support. Why not have support respond directly to the issues and save one frustrating step.

Update February 26, 2016:

The syncing issues seem to be getting worse.Last night I tried to pair with Garmin Connect for 45 minutes, on both my Iphone and my Sony laptop. I was determined to see how long it would take but I gave up. Today it finally synced, at which time I was informed by Garmin Connect that the CR1632 batteries were low. LOW? The specifications indicate battery life up to one year! I've been using it only two months! For $5.25 per pair they are available on Amazon with free shipping on orders over $49.00. Minus one star.

Update April 4,2016

More issues with the app called Garmin Connect. It's supposed to collect and save the data from the Vivofit wristband. The Challenge section does not show the leader boards nor the results of the challenge. I continue to feel that Garmin treats their customers as insignificant.

Update:

April 28th, 2016

I have a persistent problem with poor battery life and bad technical support. In the four months I have owned the Vivofit 2 it has used three (3) sets of batteries. Less than two months per set. The first time the batteries died I notified Garmin. They actually responded by telling me to change the batteries (duh). The fresh batteries lasted a little less than two months again. Garmin tech support said they would send me instructions on how to ship them the defective unit but have yet to do so. My belief that Garmin has little or no respect for their customers is becoming reinforced.

Review:
I tried an android smartwatch years ago and decided they were not for me, but this watch has changed my mind. I have worn this for a little less than a week straight, and I am more than impressed. I did not know that for this price range you could get a smart watch that would do all that this does. I have worn it 24 hours a day to test all the features. I have it set to continually check my heart rate every 20 minutes (which it says decreases battery life some). I received it last Tuesday and charged it to 100%, I recharged it on Sunday and it was only down to 15%. The battery life is awesome. From what I understand, even much more expensive watches like the Apple watch do not have this long of a battery life. Having tested the heart rate monitor and oxygen side by side with a pulse/ox machine that we have at home, this reads your heart rate and oxygen level very near accurate. I have also tested the blood pressure readings side by side with one that we have at home, and it was also very accurate. I even had the opportunity to go to the doctor during this time and the BP read the same there as well.

I love how customizable it is. You can change the face to be any picture that you want, or pick from a huge library of already existing watch faces. Also, the watch interface is really quick and once you get the hang of how it operates, it becomes second nature to use and flip through all that it does. I have made and received phone calls on it, and that also works exceptionally well. Texts come through with almost zero problems (more on that later). I have used this to control Bluetooth audio, which is also awesome. I love how I can play music through my watch, or use my watch to control music playing on another Bluetooth device. For example, you can have your phone playing music on a Bluetooth speaker and pause/play/skip the song through your watch. Awesome feature. There is so much to love about this watch, especially for this price.

There are just a few things that I do not love about this. One of those things is the sleep tracking. All in all, it works great, but it has the tendency to track my sleep when I am not sleeping. I usually go to bed after midnight, and apparently my watch can't fathom that, so around 11, while I am watching TV and relaxing for the night before heading to bed, it has decided that I have fallen asleep (I have not). It then shows that I awake for an hour or so before actually going to sleep "again" (when I actually do go to sleep), so the amount of sleep my watch says I am getting is more than the actual sleep that I am getting, and I cannot go through and delete/adjust that section of sleep to say "No, I was not asleep." I used to wear a fitbit that also tracked my sleep, and it would allow you within the app to delete sections where you knew you were not asleep so that it would more accurately track your sleep. I wish this app did that. It seems like that would be an easy fix. Is this a major flaw? No, but it is somewhat annoying.

Another dislike would be that the instructions that come with it are definitely lacking. A lot of me figuring it out was just from using it and playing with it. I tried to look up several things on how to change or work with the different menus, and the instructions don't really explain everything well enough. With enough playing with it, you figure it out, and once you learn how it works, all is good, but there was a bit of a learning curve to figure out how to swipe through all the screens and get the most out of all this watch can do.

Lastly, and this is the main reason I deduct a star is that this watch does not allow you to send a text message response. Other more expensive watches allow you to do this. I WISH so much that this watch could do this. It would absolutely be a 5 star watch if you could send a text response. It receives texts without a problem, but you cannot respond to those texts. It has voice comprehension and a mic... I have used it for phone calls, so I do not understand why you would not be able to send a text. It is awesome to get a text on my watch, but sucks to have to pull out my phone to respond (first world problems, I know). If I could just tap it and say, yes, or no, or I am on my way, or I am driving right now, it would be amazing. Nowhere in the description does it say that this is a feature, but not having it is downright annoying and until using the watch and loving it so much, you don't realize how annoying it is. Also, one last flaw regarding text messages, is that it only receives messages that are between you and one other person. It does not receive any group texts. I have an ongoing group text with my mom and my wife, and none of those texts are received on my watch.

All in all, I love this watch and how well it works for most things. I would highly recommend it, especially in this price range. If they could make just a few tweaks, this watch could easily compete with and possibly beat their high end competitors.

You can track many different sports (jogging, biking, climbing, spinning, yoga, basketball, rowing, just to name a few), control your music, track your sleep habits, check the weather, and get phone notifications on the watch.
If you're looking to try something out to see if you would fine a smart watch useful, this is a great one to try out at a very affordable price!

Review:
I had been looking at Wear OS watches for several months. Comparing features, quality, reviews, etc. It seems like most Wear OS watches on the market today are pretty similar with the same overall specs, minus a few features here and there. Then I came across this Mobvoi Ticwatch Pro LTE. They had me at 1gb ram, the MOST on the market, but what really sold me was the dual layer LCD screen that -absolutely- doubles or triples battery life. My previous Android watch couldn't even make it to bedtime in one day. This beautiful creation can go 2 FULL days with my standard use, and 3 if I'm a little light with it. Amazing. The size is about what I would expect from a fully featured watch and it competes both in price and size with it's direct competition. The software experience is the same across all Wear devices, but Mobvoi has given this watch a huge boost in ram, which makes the software run so smooth and snappy! They also managed to pack in -all- the smart watch features like gps/heart rate/speaker/microphone/IP68 water resistance etc., and added LTE functionality as well. I paired it with a third party black steel band, which makes the watch look rugged yet classy. I feel comfortable wearing it with a suit and tie, or t-shirt and jeans.
The bottom line is this, if you are already shopping for Wear OS smart watches, this is the best you are going to get.

Review:
I should preface that I have a bias towards Garmin as this is my third tracker - I have the smart scale and a lot of friends on the app, so take that into considerations when reading my review. That being said, I did a lot of research before I landed on the Vivio with Music, especially compared to the Apple Watch 4. While the watches share a lot of the same basic features - GPS, Waterproof, Work with iPhone, on wrist heart rate monitor, etc. - for me, the Garmin was the right product.

The key takeaways that put the Garmin ahead of the Apple Watch for me were:

- Battery Life: the Garmin has consistently provided five days of charge. That’s with at least three workouts in that five days, GPS use during at least one of those workouts, and the heart rate monitor always on. The Apple Watch only claims 18 hours per charge. **If sleep tracking is a big deal for you Garmin is the only real option.**
- Notification Fatigue: I am a techie and love my features but I thought the Apple Watch was just to much Smartwatch for my wrist. The Garmins preset widgets, push notifications and Garmin Pay were the right speed for my needs. There is still quite a bit of customization, but not so much that you find yourself overwhelmed by options.
- Design: This is entirely preference based but I prefer the circular facing on the Garmin to the soft, square display of the Apple Watch.
- Ecosystem: As I is said before, I’ve bought in to the Garmin system - figuratively and literally. I have friends and family in the ecosystem and find it to be easier to navigate than Apple health (which still seems like it’s in Beta).
- GPS Accuracy: I’ve heard the Garmin GPS technology is more accurate. I have no way to prove this but given they pioneered consumer GPS products I’m going to give them the edge here.
- Spotify Integration (coming soon): I have a premium Spotify account, and have no desire to switch to Apple Music. This was a big selling point for me. Fingers crossed this support launches soon. (As of Jan 20, 2019 I am still waiting).
- Price Point: this wasn’t a huge point for me but the Garmin is $120.00 less than the standard Apple Watch 4.

All things considered, the Garmin isn’t perfect - I was disappointed with the plastic watch housing, button, and screen display. For $300.00 I would have liked more. That being said, it does keep the weight of the watch down.

If the takeaways I detailed above make sense for you, I’d recommend this GPS tracker in a heart beat, pun intended.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: the Garmin has consistently provided five days of charge. That’s with at least three workouts in that five days, GPS use during at least one of those workouts, and the heart rate monitor always on. The Apple Watch only claims 18 hours per charge. **If sleep tracking is a big deal for you Garmin is the only real option.**
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The smart watch needs to provide a battery life that lasts multiple days, including during workouts with GPS use and continuous heart rate monitoring. It needs to support sleep tracking without requiring daily charging.

Review:
After way too much research, I ended up buying Garmin 265WT for my wife and Magellan 1470 for me to use. After using them both for several months, I found wanting hybrid of Garmin and Magellan. The thoughs...

Likes & Dislikes on Garmin compared to 1470:
(1)Power cable routing is much better.
(2)Speaker quality is much better (maybe because Magellan is thinner?).
(3)Screen is much easier to see during day light.
(4)Choices and sound of women's voice (Magellan sounds like a robot).
(5)Re-route/calculation speed is faster.
(6)In night use, both map screen AND menu screen get darker.
(7)Menu is easier to use at beginning, but lacks choices compared to Magellan's. My wife and I enjoy and trying out different food (Indian, Japanese, African, etc.). Magellan offers many options, but Garmin is very limited.
(8)POI is outdated-It does not even show Costco in Toledo, OH!
(9)Maybe my phone? Bluetooth function works fair, but not really useale while driving.
(10)Adjusting volume takes many pushes... Magellan is quick & easy.
(11)Getting back to map screen (after searching POI) is pain in the rear end.
(12)FM Traffic is pretty good, but re-routing is some what questionable.
(13)Advertizements you get & see on Garmin is less annoying(you get this in exchange for "free" traffic). Maybe I notice more and annoyed on Magellan because its screen is bigger?

Likes & Disikes on Magellan:
(1)Big screen for the price.
(2)Menu screen is much for thoroughout with variety of options. I really like short-cut icons that you can create. I have "Jimmy John" and other favorite places I tend to visit during my road warrior work days.
(3)Thin & sleek design
(4)Finding restaurant, hotel, etc. at specific city/area is much more easier than Garmin.
(5)Lane guidance is nice, but gets too much at busy intersections
(6)Crashes (freezes) after about 5-hrs of straight operation. Magellan's new software update (just downloaded) is supposed to correct this. I'll find out next week.
(7)The woman's voice is robotic
(8)Speaker is weak
(8)Only map screen goes dark in night time. Menu screen stays bright! What the heck?

In conclusion, neither Garmin nor Magellan is perfect.
I want Magellan's useful menu options, but want Garmin's auto dimming screen, speaker and guide women's voice :o)

Screen Size: Big screen for the price. Maybe I notice more and annoyed on Magellan because its screen is bigger?

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The screen size is big for the price. The screen is easy to see during daylight. In night use, both the map screen and menu screen getting darker is acceptable. Only the map screen going dark at night is acceptable. The menu screen staying bright is acceptable. An auto-dimming screen is needed.

Review: GPS arrived ahead of schedule. The packaging was good there was no apparent external damage.  Took it out of the box and plugged it into the computer’s USB port and downloaded the garment express software.  Well the packaging said Garmin was up-to-date and ready to go but when I searched for updates there was one available for software and maps and it took over 4 hrs. to update so I left it to update overnight.  When I returned to it the next morning I was happy to see that the updates were complete but when I pressed the button on the back the unit went blank.  It seems, there was just enough charge to update the unit but not operate it.  Furthermore I was disappointed that it did not get charged by being plugged in to the computer.  When I plugged it in the vehicle auxiliary port it did not come on right away as the instructions indicated. As it turns out it had to charge for a while before it would even turn on.  My auxiliary ports only work when my vehicle is turned on and since there is no AC adapter shipped with the unit I had no way of charging the GPS long-term. I've been wearing the Garmin Vivo Smart for the past year and really enjoyed being able to track my phone down from the band when I lost it, having the sleep/activity tracking as well as the reminders to get up and move periodically. I would wear the band with my analog watch because the display on the vivo smart was not always on and tapping to check the time was draining my battery too fast.

Enter the Pebble Time Steel. Always on display to use as a watchface, activity/sleep tracking, more detailed pushing of phone notifications plus a 7-10 day battery life. I was sold and received one for Christmas. What I didn't expect was that Pebble would up the ante with Timeline and the ability to respond to texts, snooze notifications and have a microphone. I'm now wearing the Pebble Time Steel alone instead of the vivo smart+analog watch combo and loving that I check my phone far less often while still getting things done a la David Allen.

I've posted several pictures that I would have found helpful when researching the aesthetics of the PTS. All are modeled on my hand (5' female) for scale. First two of my favorite watch faces that allow the PTS to look like any other analog watch. Pretty serious looking but every once in a while I will throw in my Wonder Woman watch face.

Then the PTS next to some gold rings to show the more warm tones of the brushed matte bezel.

Review:
When I was researching activity tracker/ smartwatches online, I was confused by the lukewarm reviews of the Vivoactive 3 - very few people seemed to really love the watch, but it offered good functionality at a low price and didn't seem to have anything particularly wrong with it (at least compared to the also-new Fitbit Ionic). After owning one for 10 days now, I still like it, but I kind of share the lukewarm sentiment I encountered in other reviews, so hopefully I can now explain why.

I previously had a Fitbit Charge HR, which was cheaper and did a reasonable job of tracking sleep and heart rate, but didn't offer much beyond the basics, and besides, it looked kind of clunky and was starting to fall apart. What I was hoping to find was a GPS HR tracker that looked good enough to wear with a suit, that offered better running metrics and offered a bit more in the way of smartwatch features without being too pricey.

In these respects, the Vivoactive 3 does fit the bill. It looks great (most people don't even notice that it's digital, much less that it's a running watch), it tracks my runs and other activities well and - as far as I can tell - it offers all the functionality of the more expensive Forerunner 235 and most of the features of the still-more expensive Fenix 5. Plus new features like stress measurement and GaminPay!

Well... that's all more or less true. But. For one thing GarminPay isn't set up yet and even when it is, it won't work with every bank and at every retailer. I don't think I'd actually use it much even if it was functioning, but given that it isn't, it can't really be viewed as an improvement, and if it's important to you to be able to pay with your watch, the Fitbit Ionic or Apple watch are probably safer choices. Other options in the same price range mostly offer built-in music storage which the Vivoactive does not. Also, the battery life is advertised as being 7 days - this may be true if bluetooth is off and you don't use the watch at all, but as far as the real world is concerned, 7 days is kind of a fiction. If you don't switch bluetooth off, it will last about 24 hours. If you do, you need to go in and switch it on every time you want to sync the watch (not actually a big task, but still irritating). At the moment I'm getting about 4 days out of each charge with moderate use. And stress measurement? Some reviews have claimed that this is subjectively accurate, but that hasn't been my experience. Every time I get up to make a cup of tea, the resulting increase in heart rate is listed as 'med' or 'high' stress. And I'm British, so that's quite a lot of the day. If I'm sitting down and writing a difficult e-mail or giving a presentation, it generally shows low stress. Perhaps it needs to learn a bit more (it has now managed to cut out any walking of more than a few minutes), but so far it's been pretty wildly off.

There are other irritations - sleep tracking has failed on 4 of the 10 days, the side swipe button (which be useful in winter with gloves but otherwise isn't much use) can be triggered by loose clothing and start skipping through menus and actually reset the time once, the 'move' reminders can be irritating. Even features I expected to be top notch have struggled at times - for example I did a run yesterday starting up 6th avenue in midtown New York, and the GPS showed me going along 6 1/2th avenue (yes, that is a real thing). Sure, I was weaving around tourists, but the difference is about 200ft and it really is supposed to be more accurate than that. To be fair, it was on target the rest of the way and has been close enough on my other runs.

As mentioned above, some of its competitors seem to offer more functions for the non-runner. An Apple watch, Samsung or Fitbit will offer more apps, built-in music storage and in some cases personal coaching sessions which are lacking from the Vivoactive 3 (although you can create workout plans on Garmin Connect and put them on the watch). Personally, I use a phone for most apps, don't have wireless headphones and don't need in-watch coaching. Similarly, the more expensive garmin products offer more advanced metrics, more accurate GPS, open swimming tracking, etc and so offer more for the more serious athlete.

Having said all of that, this is still a good watch and some of its flaws will be fixed in time as patches are released, more apps become available on Connect IQ, etc. It is easy to use as a regular watch, it's light and comfortable and looks great. It offers most of the features of the Fenix 5 at half the cost, and all the fitness features you'd find on anything cheaper. You can reply to text notification while out jogging (although only with a standard reply) and might one day be able to buy your post-run rehydration with Garmin Pay. I can't resist using it to check the weather at every opportunity, and I've always wanted a watch with an in-built compass. It is leagues ahead of the Fitbit I used to own, although Fitbits have moved on in that time too.

So in conclusion, it is a good watch that has its flaws and is surrounded by competitors with potentially superior features. If you are a serious runnner/ cyclist/ triathlete, etc you probably need a greater range of metrics and activity apps (although the Vivoactive 3 can be paired with accessories quite easily to cover some of this data). If you are into fitness more generally and want something to track gym/ cardio sessions, the new Fitbit probably has you covered and offers a little bit more for the same price. But if you exercise outdoors at a casual to intermediate level and want a watch that covers all your fitness needs while looking great in the office and future-proofing you with smartwatch capabilities, the Vivoactive 3 is the watch for you.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: battery life is advertised as being 7 days, but in real world use it lasts about 24 hours with bluetooth on, or about 4 days with moderate use.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The battery life should last as advertised for 7 days under typical use conditions. It is acceptable if the battery lasts about 24 hours with continuous Bluetooth connectivity. It is acceptable if the battery lasts about 4 days with moderate use.

Review:
i wanted a new watch (my old one broke) primarily to know when someone calls, texts, or emails me, and also to keep track of my walking and workouts. this watch does all that, it's waterproof (i wash it after each workout), looks elegant (looks a lot like the apple watch), and costs under $25. plus the battery lasts a very long time, which is a huge plus. you can recharge it for 10 minutes while you're in the shower every few days, and it will never run out of battery. will last even longer if you turn off heart rate monitoring. excellent bright screen, with adjustable brightness. "find phone" feature is nice too (i've already had to use it twice). for these reasons, i'm giving this watch 5 stars, though it's far from perfect.

i have two main issues with this watch, though at this price-point i wasn't going to knock down a star for these issues:
1 - heart rate sensor is ok (fairly good actually) for establishing average and resting heartrate. however, it's not at all accurate for tracking heart rate during workouts. maybe this can be fixed with future firmware updates, maybe not.
2 - the watch navigation and faces aren't customizable. the weather location isn't customizable (which is very important, given that it got the wrong location for me). i'm guessing this can be fixed easily in next app update. there is no way to delete which workout types show up on the watch, to make stopwatch more accessible (currently takes 5 actions to start stopwatch, but only 2 to do a breathing exercise. i want that reversed). and there's no way to make it so the screen stays on during my entire workout. all these customizations should be in the app, so i'm hoping the companion app will improve over time.

other things to note:
it has music controls, breathing exercise, sleep tracking, and a lot of exercise modes. i will never be using any of these features.
it has a standard quick-release watch band, so you can customize your watch band and get fancy or funky ones. i'll stick with the default watch band, but it's a nice-to-have feature.
the watchfaces you can choose from are cool, but i wish there were more.

tldr; if you need a watch to track your heart rate accurately during workouts, or to have highly customizable watchfaces, this is not the watch for you. if you want an elegant-looking smartwatch that will vibrate and display all your calls and messages, with a clean, bright display, week-long battery life, and exercise tracking, all for under $25, this watch is for you.

Review:
Have also owned Vivoactive HR(broke my fault), Samsung Gear S3(hasn't broke). This is a comparison to those. I am a 5-6 day a week active person. Running, biking, weight lifting, P90X type activity, etcetera. I am not the type that absolutely has to have accurate results to ensure a perfect workout, calorie burn, HR zone. Fitness watch is a tool and records my workouts no matter what.

None of these are HR accurate for upper body. Blood restricted to wrists should make that easy to understand. Have to use chest strap if you care about accurate.

The Garmin units are much better at HR and GPS than Gear S3. Garmin GPS pings more often and more accurate. HR tends to vary for me, but Garmin seems to match up more often when I check HR manually.

Garmin battery efficiency way better AND charges in less than 1 hr. As expected Vivoactive 3 will last 4-5 days with my activity level. Gear S3 lasts 2 days AND takes 2+ hours to charge. Not good for active types who want 24/7 recording of health data.

Gear S3 build quality and display is an easy winner compared to Vivoactive series. Gear S3 is heavy and well put together and will easily trick many into being a real watch. Garmin is really not that.

The watch style of the Vivoactive 3 easily convinced me that I was going back to garmin. The display is not as nice, but good, and the battery is more important, I suspect, for active types. Build quality may be just as good, but it is much lighter and feels cheaper than the S3.

Garmin waterproof, Gear S3 water resistant. If you like the water it's an easy choice. Had to take S3 off when in the lake, ocean. Garmin never comes off! I want 24/7 data no matter what my day is spent doing.

I personally like the Garmin Connect software better. Garmin and Samsung both have robust data. Most reviews don't give Garmin software positives, but for whatever reason, I like Garmin.

As a smart watch, Gear S3 easily wins. It has built in speaker, can put music on it, email, texting, responses, is more robust. I don't need much. I want email, text, meeting alerts, good enough.

One more thing. Mid 40's and can no longer see well without readers. Garmin has simpler interface, screen fonts tend to be bigger which is good for me. S3 may be equal since display contrast is better, but big fonts I appreciate.

Review:
I've been looking for a smart watch, but on a budget, I was prepared to settle for a dupe that would probabaly be mediocre. Then I found this on Amazon and clicked on the purchase button, I was hoping for the best but expecting the worst.

I was pleasantly surprised. This watch is an awesome dupe that can rival the real thing with its features. It tells time, counts steps, monitors heart rate, tracks activities, plays music and audio, alerts you on calls, gives message notifications, allows remote control of your mobile phone shutter, and even allows you to take calls thru your phone. Battery life last a whole day, charging is fast (love its unique charger) and it has an app that makes the set up simple and easy.

For the price, it is a steal. I love it! Since I got it, I've been wearing it everyday, and feel naked without it. It's such a serendipitous find.

If there's one thing I have an issue, it's the watch face designs. Some are nice, while others are a bit tacky. Although you can change and download watch faces, you can't get rid of the pre-installed ones. Wanted to replace them with better designs, but couldn't. Maybe a system update via the app will allow it in the future.

So if you're looking for a smart watch to keep up with the times (pun intended) but don't have a big budget, this is the smart watch for you.

I am blown away.

Review:
I have the Black 20mm strap version. I aleady owned the Pebble Time and Pebble Time Steel. My sons grabbed them when I got the Round. I am very very happy with it. As fas as I am concerned it is a smart watch and not a dumb phone. It controls my Samsung S6 flawlessly (as did the other pebbles. And they also work with my son's S4 Active and S5 very well). It is discreet and tiny. I can auto-answer calls/txts with canned messages when I am in a meeting.

Email and Sms notifications are so much easier. The watch vibrates and shows you a few lines. I then have the choice of ignoring them, scrolling to read the rest or to grab the phone to read/reply fully. There are an amazing number of watch faces and apps available, to suit every taste.

It's also the only wtach that is water proof. Others are not even splash proof !!

It also integrates seamlessly with two of my most used apps. With Google Maps, the watch shows a summary of the directions and buzzes just before you get to the corner to let you know of a turn. I no longer look stupid when walking about using Gmaps with a large phone. I just have to glance at the watch ocassionally. With Sleep as Android, I don't have to put the phone under my pillow anymore as the watch on the wrist grabs all the sleep stats.

The step counter in the native Health addition works really well..... finally.

Now for the negative - The battery lasts just over 2 days of full normal use. The square ones used to last 3.5 days. But it is still better than anything else out there.

I highly recommend it to everyone that is looking for a smart watch. If you are looking a dumb mini phone, then look elsewhere.

Special Features: 计步器
Review:
Below is my initial review of this product. Everything in that review still applies. So, if that's the case, then why am I updating my review and changing my recommendation?
The charger.
This should be such a minor thing that doesn't even need to be mentioned. In fact, if you look at my previous review, I don't mention it because it didn't appear to be an issue. Yes, it was a little finicky but nothing unexpected from your standard magnetic charger.
Oh, I was sooooooooooooooo wrong about that.
The charger is a nightmare. It will connect right up, its magnetic connections coming together with a nice, satisfying little *click!* and a graphic on the watch that shows it's charging.
Then the nightmare begins.
The screen will, if you're lucky, stay there and your watch will charge normally. This is what happened 80% of the time for me. As time went on, however, I kept seeing the screen switch from the charge screen to the clock face. Another thing I noticed was that, when it switched to the clock face, it was NOT charging.
This started becoming an issue. The watch would switch on its own all the time for no apparent reason. Sometimes, it would just switch back and forth. It was insane.
It has now gotten to the point where it connects and displays the charge screen for a few seconds and then goes right to the clock face. Getting it to charge takes forever and there appears to be no good solution to the problem. I've cleaned the connectors on the phone & charger and it makes no difference.
I've been noticing this kind of problem a lot more recently. A desirable, high-end electronic device is brought to market and originally offered at a price point higher than much of the market could afford. Other companies see this and make high-quality, less expensive versions of the product and concentrate so much on making their device do everything and more than the originals that they forget about the basics. In this case, they shove a cheap magnetic charging setup into an otherwise very good item and it destroys the whole thing.
It doesn't matter how good it is, how deep it'll dive, or how well it controls your media if you can't charge it.
ORIGINAL REVIEW:
I'm not gonna pretend this is a major value item. It's a little pricey and tries to justify the cost by being military spec in water and shock resistance. You really do notice that the moment you take it out of the box. It feels premium, like a watch that would cost more than this one does and would stand up to being worn by Bond for a week.
It's the first smartwatch that I've been able to just turn on and set up without even looking at the manual. It's very intuitive and easy to get up and running with immediately. The watch is limited when it comes to functions but it does all of them very well. The battery life is terrific. I haven't had to recharge it after its first charge and it looks like I won't have to for a while as it still has over 50% of the battery left.
I do wish it had a few more functions but overall, it's a good sturdy watch.

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a long-lasting battery that retains a significant charge over time.
