Review:
I really love this watch. First, it's beautiful and well made. It's comfortable and light on my wrist. It's not too large, but the display is still easy to read and use. If only I could answer calls and reply to texts it would be perfect, but I guess that tech hasn't filtered down to this price point yet. You can see who's calling or texting, you just can't do anything as it's only a notification.
I've had this watch about 2 weeks now. The battery has proven to last about 3 full days of use which includes wearing while sleeping overnight. Not bad! Speaking of which, I'm obsessed with this watch's ability to track my sleep. It has so much information and I've found I actually get less sleep than I realized. I also like that as soon as my watch buzzes about an incoming message, all I have to do is turn my wrist and the message will display. I don't even have to touch the screen.
The watch band and face appear to be holding up well to daily use with no visible wear so far.
There are numerous choices for watch faces and it's easy to switch them whenever you want to. The app is fairly intuitive as well.
If you're on a budget or just don't care about taking calls or replying to messages on your watch, I think this is a good option and I recommend it. For 1/3 the cost of higher end smart watches, you're getting about 75% of their capabilities. That's a fair trade for many folks.

Review:
I am not a runner, but I am quite active and walk, ride a bicycle, and hike quite a bit. Thus, having a sports smartwatch is important to me. I have had a Garmin Forerunner 235 watch for quite a while, but at some time felt like it was time for an update.
To make a long story (sort of) short:
- Apple Watch was not for me - its biking and walking interfaces felt too small and I did not feel like charging it every night. Also, these day every kid seems to have an Apple Watch - I wanted something sportier and more special.
- Garmin Instinct is a good watch, but I could not stand its small black and white screen, even though I admit it was pretty crisp. The Forerunner 235 is an older model, but the size of its screen is noticeably larger.
- Corous Pace 2 - I ran into this model while watching smartwatch reviews on YouTube. On paper its specs looked great (most importantly, it has a barometric altimeter), but it looked like a toy when I got it - may be because I chose the "sport" strap. May be not exactly like a toy, but definitely not something one would wear as a dress watch.
- Garmin Forerunner 245 Music - I was hesitant to get this one at first, mostly because I felt like this time I wanted to get an altimeter for my mountain hiking trips and the 245 does not have it. I even ordered a Forerunner 255 at some point, but then canceled my order after realizing that not only I would have to pay almost twice just to get an altimeter, but would have to wait for 3-5 weeks.
In the end I decided to keep the 245 - it does lack the barometric altimeter, but the Gaia GPS app on my iPhone provides elevation profiles and paying twice as much for a watch just to get this additional feature on it felt silly. Most importantly, the black 245 Music looks very elegant - it's round, it's black, it's face is very crisp, and I can adjust it in various ways (one of the reasons I did not like the Corous Pace 2 was its choice of preset faces).
I got myself a screen protector to keep this beauty safe and could not be happier!

Review: I received this watch as a gift and I like it a lot! The Pebble works great as a step counter / sleep tracker and is comfortable to wear 24/7. I have read that it is not waterproof but I often have my hands submerged in water for work or washing dishes at home and it has never caused a problem. The biggest problem I have with the watch is that the battery life is extremely short - I have to charge it every day and a half or so. Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
This is my first smart watch. This one has a few built-in features -- clock, timer, alarms, fitness tracking, sleep tracking, phone dialer, and notifications from your phone, etc. More advanced features cannot be added later [other watches let you install apps to add features such as maps].

The voice assistant is interesting. It could be useful, but tends to get confused and doesn't always do what is asked of it. Trying to set alarms proved to be trouble. It would set alarms, but not cancel them without cancelling ALL the alarms. Even then, the voice assistant still thought the alarms existed after they were deleted.

There is a companion app that gets installed on your Android phone (or iPhone if that's what you have). It seems rather generic. Other smart watch companies use what looks like the same app with different icons, colors and branding. I was hoping that any of these apps would work, just so I had freedom to choose something different. Unfortunately, the FitCloudPro app was the only one what works with this watch.

From the app, you can choose the "dial" (watch face style) to be installed and used on the watch. There is a large library of options to choose from, but some of the dials in the Amazon photos are mysteriously absent. Many dials are just hideous, some are too busy, some too simple. I was able to find a few that I like, and I've settled on one. The watch itself holds five "watch faces" in memory. Yes, it is confusing that the watch calls them something different than the app. Out of those five slots, only one of them can be modified to hold your selected dial from the app. If you had hopes of loading five of your favorites and swapping between then, you will be disappointed.

The most useful feature of this smart watch is the notifications from your phone. You can leave your phone in your pocket or nearby, and the watch will display notifications from whatever apps you choose. I have it set up to show SMS/MMS text messages, Whatsapp messages, and Facebook Messenger messages. Everything else is turned off. This is a nifty feature, but the space allowed for messages is rather small and the type is too large. Any long messages get truncated. I wish there was a way to reduce the type size so more of the message could be seen, or maybe have a way to scroll long messages.

When I first started using the watch, I began to have problems playing music through my car stereo. I could control playback from the watch, my phone would show that music was playing through my car stereo, the stereo would show that the phone was connected, but there would be no sound. Once I removed the app and reinstalled, Bluetooth audio returned to normal. I'm not sure what the cause of this was, but reinstalling the app was the solution for me. It has been fine for a few weeks now.

The screen itself is bright and crisp. The user interface took some getting used to -- mostly trial and error.

The extra watch band is a nice bonus.

Review: This is a great device. I am not training for any event, just want to exercise regularly and keep moving when I'm not at the gym. I like the red bar letting me know when I've been sitting for an hour. I've never heard the "alert sounds" for lack of movement, but knew that was likely when I purchased the Vivofit 2.The sensitivity to movement is higher than my Misfit Flash (which I loved, but retired because I wanted an activity tracker which would display my heart rate from my Scosche Rhythm Plus Heart Monitor). Consequently, the Vivofit 2 displays more hours of "light" sleep as opposed to "restful" sleep, but I think it's pretty accurate. You have to remember to press a button on the tracker to tell it you are going to start and stop sleeping, but I've never forgotten to do it. The battery is supposed to last a year or more and I've only had mine a month, so I can't comment on that. I do wish there was a screen in the display on my cell phone which showed the battery status like the Misfit software did. The Vivofit activity data syncs pretty well with SPARKPEOPLE.COM and MYFITNESSPAL.COM although I have to enter the number of hours sleeping manually, which I did not have to do with the MisFit. All-in-all, I am very pleased with this purchase. The price was right and the tracker functions as advertised.

Review:
THE GOOD: Let's face it: for a smart watch, Garmin nailed the size with the slimness of the watch. Hands down, it looks good. I bought the white watch, and it looked great on my small wrist. That's where my excitement ended.

THE BAD: For the price, I wanted to like this Garmin product. Garmin is a respectable brand, and I was excited that they created something less bulky for smaller wrists. Taking the watch out of the box, already the screen lighting was quite dim, and I couldn't even respond to the questions on the screen for setup because I couldn't read them. There is no brightness adjustment for Garmin. Eventually I went to a well-lit area of the room and could see the screen. This is already problematic for me because in my mind a watch should be seen at any time. Thankfully, there is a backlight button for a few seconds, but that unpleasantly took me back to my 80s digital watches. (Casio is looking good now…) Within minutes of setup, the menu icon was already non-responsive, and the side buttons had to be pressed quite hard to get any response. Keep in mind that this watch is smaller and feels more breakable, so you'll understand why pressing anything too hard makes the wearer feel like it will be broken.

WOULD I BUY IT AGAIN IF THEY MADE CHANGES? I still have high hopes for Garmin products, and hope that in the near future they'll make a more responsive watch that perhaps has less battery life in order to power it with more speed and light. Hopefully, they'll also make their app even more intuitive in the process. Verdict is out, but knowing how great Garmin is with GPS and exercise gear, I'm sure they'll figure this all out.

Review:
The sensor of my expensive name brand smart watch fell off so I wanted to replace it with something that is not so expensive. On sale, this watch was only $40 so I went for it.

It came in a box with a charger. The charger has two little charging contacts that magnetize to the back of the watch. It charges quickly and lasts about three days with regular use.

The watch app is Da Fit. The app is user friendly. There are a bunch of watch faces you can change to. It has a lot of the normal features: steps, sleep, heart rate, workout, etc. You can set your step goal and sync with Google Fit. I didn't sync to it so I don't know if it's accurate. That's the app, now to the watch.

It is a big watch for me. It's 1.75" by 1.5". I had to change the band to one I had because the one that came with it was too long to fit any of the holes. End to end when attached to the watch is 10.25".

The actual function of the watch was not that great. Calls were fine. They were clear and connected quickly. I never could figure out the messaging. A message would show up, but I didn't figure out which icon was messaging so I couldn't read them again. The icon page was intense so I left it alone.

The steps were way off. I know my phone is accurate because it was synced with my first watch and they were always the same and my steps also match the treadmill. But the this watch recorded at least twice the amount of steps. I tested it and it was recording my arm movement. So if you like to have a lot of steps without putting the work in, this watch is for you!

There weren't many workouts to choose from although I thought it was great that skipping and badminton are two of the choices. Also, the heart rate function does not work. My heart rate gets very high when I work out (185 bpm) and every time I checked, the watch showed 78 to 109 bpm.

I did like the fact that you could set it to remind you to drink water. Sometimes I need that little nudge.

The weather was accurate and you can use your watch to be the shutter for your phone camera although I could never open the icon to test it.

So, I would recommend this inexpensive Rawnaig Smart Watch if you just want a lot of faces to choose from, a timepiece, the ability to make and receive calls and a few other features. If you also want an accurate workout watch or an easy watch to message with or the ability show your own pictures, this is not the watch. That's not to say that there are other inexpensive smart watches that would work nicely.

Review: I have used both Fitbit and Apple Watch in the past. The Fitbit didn't sink well with my iPhone and the band would separate from the display/computer. I had to replace it multiple times and finally gave up. Next, I got an Apple Watch. I did not have the same problems that I had with the Fitbit. I was more satisfied with the Apple Watch but there were still things left to be desired. The Apple Watch had to be charged everyday. I have gone on trips where I either forgot my charger or didn't bring it because I didn't want to deal with it. Another big draw back of the Apple Watch was that I had to take it off during certain workouts. Siri was always getting triggered during certain weight lifting activities. I also had to take it off during martial arts which meant that I couldn't track a majority of my fitness activities. I mention all of this to highlight how Garmin is different. The construction of the watch looks like it will last over time. I feel like I will be able to wear it during all of my workouts and not have to worry about it shattering. The battery life is amazing. I don't have to bring an extra charger on trips. I can wear the watch throughout the day. The watch is simple and to the point. I almost never used complications on the Apple Watch. Garmin is able to track the things that are important to me. I still can get notifications of texts and calls but not in a disruptive manner. I am very happy with my purchase and I am excited to learn more about my watch.

Review:
I've had my Vivofit 2 about a week and I've been really happy with it. There have been a few small issues, but overall, this has been a good purchase,

The three biggest things going for it are that it has a readable watch (as opposed some other fitness trackers that either have nothing or just lights), it is waterproof to 50 m and the price point was right. I'm not a fitness person, I'm a person who needs to get off the couch more and burn a few calories. This has helped me do that. A month ago, I was averaging between 1200 and 2000 steps a day, I'm now consistently over 10k, everyday and a lot of that is because of the inactivity alert. I like that it syncs to the app on my phone and that the app share data between my phone and my iPad. You can get it with an optional heart monitor, which is nice for people that need or want to have that information, but it doesn't apply to me. The band is lightweight and easy to wear. I wore a watch for 30 years and then stopped for a few, but this doesn't feel like wearing anything. The band also locks to prevent loss. You can also use the app to select what screen show on the band, streamlining for your personal needs.

The cons are fairly minor in the grand scheme of things. I think the biggest con is that alert tone has no volume control; it is either on or it is off and the sound isn't really loud enough. I can hear it in very quite rooms, but that is the exception around my house. Even just road noise in the car is too loud to hear the sound over. I also don't love the app. The information isn't very useful, compared what is shown on the tracker (except sleep). It is suppose to sync with my fitness pal, but is often pretty wonky. Sometimes, it would push my steps to MFP, sometimes it won't download my calories from MFP, sometimes it acts like it can't talk to MFP at all. The active calories feature of the app has been lacking as well (although I just got an app update this morning that might fix that). For example, yesterday I walked 13,658 steps. The connect app said that I had burned 1 active calorie for my effort, which I know isn't true. The other thing I've noticed is the inactivity bar takes some work to clear and some things don't seem to count. If it isn't consistent activity, it doesn't clear the bar. A few days ago, the bar was on and I ran to the grocery store. It was quick trip, I parked close, ran in grabbed the two things I needed, and came home, but because I walked in, stopped, picked out what I needed, walked to checkout, stopped, paid, walked to the car, stopped, drove home, it didn't clear the bar. It counted all the steps, just not towards clearing the bar. So once that light comes on, you have to do more than just walk to the bathroom to clear. It seems to take around 200-300 steps continuous steps clear it out. Finally, something to keep in mind, if you are doing something else with your arm, it doesn't count all of your steps. I've been tracking y steps with my iPhone and I find them to be fairly close, most of the time. In order to get my steps in, I've mapped out a small path around the inside of my house, so I can walk 1000 steps an hour. I decided to add weight training to my laps and the Vivofit didn't track any of the steps for the time was also lifting the weights. It made the steps over 1000 from my iPhone. The final drawback, for me, is that not as many people own the Garmin trackers, which limits my pool of friends I can compete with. I've been recommending it to everyone, so hopefully, I get a few more, but most everyone has a Fitbit of some kind, instead.

This tracker has good and bad, like everything, but the good really outweighs the bad and it is well worth the money for a waterproof watch step tracker.

Review:
I have a Ticwatch Pro that I purchased in 2020 that I have loved because of the dual screen feature. I still use it today even though I have newer Garmin and Samsung watches. This Ticwatch doesn't have the dual screen feature or any outstanding smart watch feature, but is a solid watch that is responsive, good-looking and very light and comfortable to wear.

I've been using this watch for about 5 weeks now and it's been a great experience. This watch uses the Wear OS platform and works well with it. It also works with the Mobvoi app to provide additional versatility with the health and fitness features of the watch. It has a 1.3" bright LCD, sharp resolution and glass screen. The watch body is polycarbonate and comes with a silicone strap. It worked fine during my daily wear in a mostly indoor construction management environment. It's IP68 certified and I used it several times in my hot tub or in the shower without a hitch. The watch lasted me 2 full days wearing it continuously even during sleep and with normal use. An extra day would have been appreciated, but that's about the norm, even though my Garmin Venu watch lasts me 5 days+ with normal use.

It features WiFi, Bluetooth 5.0, NFC, GPS and the usual motion/health sensors and I used all of it's features. NFC with Google Pay worked as it should and the GPS positioning was precise with Google Maps. It monitored my heart rate and daily steps and workouts accurately. Calls sounded good and loud with the built in speaker and mic. It's got all the expected health monitoring and sleep features and the Tic suite of health apps are as good as Samsung's or Garmin's.

So why would you purchase this watch? It's got a next generation Snapdragon platform, it's both stylish and sporty, very easy on the wrist, sharp and bright screen, has all the expected features and best of all, it's only $169.99. It's a great addition to my smart watch collection!

Review:
This really is a decent fitness tracker for $ paid. If you are after a simple, lightweight fitness tracker in an iWatch style than this is a good bet.
The watch is sized appropriately. It is light and non-obtrusive. Simple one button and touchscreen interaction. The charger is small and magnetically catches to the watch body. The charger cord is only 15 or 16 inches, pretty short. You may need an extension if putting this on a nightstand. The instructions are not perfect English, but better than some that I have seen. The instructions are not comprehensive, but give you a good idea of how the system operates. The watch will also display messaging and various other functions originating from your phone.
The APP is ok. Many of the functions in the app are only available on the phone and do not display on the watch. The APP does allow you to download alternate watch faces that alternate the look of the display. One item of note that some of the watch faces show weather and it always displays in C.
Overall, its a good buy. This isn't meant to be an Apple competitor, but for the price, its a decent tracker with a few enhanced functions.

Review:
I purchased this watch 8 months ago and have not had any problems. I had been wearing FitBit for years and had various versions but sick of the ugly look of the fitbit and after awhile the fitbits seem to not keep their charge as long.

Functionality: I wanted something that looked like a watch, was light, and could track my activity, sleep, and have a silent alarm. Pebble accurately tracks both and the alarm works great, plus it has added benefits of being able to see texts and emails from the watch and incoming calls. You can respond with voice or canned messages right from the watch as well and send a text easily from the watch. You can also easily pick from your phone app (I have Android) which apps you would like to notify you, i.e. if you wanted your work email to send notifications to your watch, but not your personal email, you can very easily turn off the personal email notifications. You can also set the alarm right from your watch, you don't need your phone to set it which I had to do on the FitBit. The interface is super easy to use and doesn't take long to get used to. The ones I use most often are the Health monitor where I can easily see from the watch my steps & sleep, the timer, and torch/flash light. The sleep also tracks "deep sleep" and keeps track of your average sleep and average deep sleep. Both the activity and sleep will send a notification to your watch after exercise or when you wake up with things like, "your rockin' the shut eye! You slept for X hours and X mins. Make it nightly ritual. You deserve it." or "Enjoying the great outdoors? You walked for X mins and burned X calories. Way to keep moving." The torch/flash light is not bright like a phone flashlight at all, but is useful in the middle of the night if you just need a little extra light. You can also set reminders to yourself using your voice, i.e. Remind me to go to my appt at 11 am (Slate is an app you can add, but Pebble comes with this capability). There are a ton of other apps you can add like Uber, drink/water counters, games, other sleep and activity apps. The apps add easily and instantly. You can easily reorder the apps that show on your pebble as well - easiest way on your phone app just touch and hold the app and drag it up or down. And you can set two apps to be available by pressing and holding the up and down buttons on your watch for immediate access.

Look & feel: The stone band was not very comfortable to me. I immediately ordered the silver metallic band and watched the video on the Pebble website for how to change it. I love that band, but have not changed it out since as it wasn't super easy (for me at least) to change the band the first time and get it to the right length. There are a lot of watch faces, but I don't really love any of them and you don't see the watch face when its just on your wrist very well at all unless the back light is on. Otherwise it's extremely light and comfortable, looks like a real watch, and durable. I work out with it on, play tennis, have done heavy yard work and have had zero problems and I do not have a protector on it.

Battery life -- States it will last two days which it may if you don't have many notifications otherwise closer to a day, but just make a habit of charging it while you are taking your shower and you'll be all set. It does send notifications to you that reminds you that it will only last until tonight, for example, which annoyed me a lot in the beginning but now I'm used to that.

UPDATE SEP 7 2020: There is NO Warranty on this product. The manufacturer wants me to purchase a new watch from Amazon at full price and then is offering to refund me half of that amount. So basically you pay for 1 1/2 watches in order to get one that works. Bad deal! Reducing to one star, the lowest rating. If I could give this zero stars, I would.

--

Such a cool device! We paired this watch with a SIM card from Speedtalk as recommended by the manufacturer. I activated the SIM card the day prior to putting it in the watch. Once the watch was charged, I popped in the SIM and it worked immediately. I was able to access the Phone menu to make calls immediately by typing in the number. The sound quality is really good by the way. Voices are very clear and you can adjust the volume using the touch screen on the watch. There is no headphone jack and it doesn't have Bluetooth connectivity so it works like a speakerphone.

In order to program the phone book, you must use the app. The app itself was simple to download and contains many cool features like taking photos from the watch without the wearer knowing, and you can also have the watch call you (so you can listen in) without the wearing knowing. :) Great for spying on your kids!

We tested out the "waterproof" feature by wearing it into the pool. No issues-- watch still works great!

The best part is it's easy for a child to use. Within 5 minutes of showing my 7 year old how to use the main features, he was making calls. I showed him how to use the "panic button" --- the only button on the watch -- which if you hold it in for several seconds will call the people, in order, that you have set up in the app as panic button contacts. The touch screen also is very easy for him to use and it's lightweight so he doesn't mind wearing it. I feel great knowing he can call me anytime!

Review:
First, I'd like to discuss the pro's to this watch.
- Price is outstanding for what you get
- The watch band is very comfortable, and the watch face and body aren't too large or obstructive to get caught up in a long sleeved top.
- Perfect for someone who doesn't like wearing a watch, but wants to get more health data (me)
- Great entry level smart watch, which displays the key features one would like in a smart watch
- Keeps time, and is great for controlling music without having to fish your phone from a pocket or bag. The audio controls controlled Spotify on my phone, which I thought was awesome.

Next, the cons -
- Proprietary charging magnetic method, and once attached you need to be pretty sure it doesn't move
- Battery life is unpredictable, if you're looking for a device that can hold a charge for many days, this is not that
- The functionality is close, but leaves a lot to be desired. For example, I was never able to get my text messages to show up. I was able to get incoming phone calls to show, but trying to use the watch as a speakerphone was not a great experience. Often times one of the 2 bluetooth connections would re-enable without my intervention, this happened overnight and I didn't hear my regular alarm go off.
- I'm not convinced the health sensors are accurate. I was checking my blood pressure regularly, and it was fine. At the doctor's office I was reading completely different on their blood pressure cuff, and I even tried some tests while waiting there, and the results were way different.
- I'm also not sure if the sleep sensors are accurate. I know I get poor sleep, but this told me that I average 8 to 26 minutes of restful sleep a night, with the remaining time classified as light sleep.
- While in the shower, I noticed that the screen's touch sensitivity was sensitive enough to be triggered by water, and every time I looked the watch was kind of going crazy, or moving through menus on its own.
- It doesn't interface with the clock app on my phone for alarms, instead it has 6 pre-defined timers (like a 1 minute timer, and a 5 minute timer). I found that to be a little bit of a challenge, and ended up using my phone instead.

Other thoughts
I picked this up because I don't like wearing watches, but wanted fitness data as I've been working towards some health goals, and it's nice to have additional metrics. This does integrate with the Apple Health app, and provides useful data directly to the app with little configuration.
Although this is not a garmin or apple watch, for the price it's definitely a good smart watch for the cost. If you know that going in, I think you'll be satisfied with this product.

Review:
If you’ve ever been to Chinatown, you can be forgiven for thinking many items sold there are junk. Every once in a while, though, you can find a gem, and nearly as good as the higher priced items out there. I think I found just such an item in this clone of a well-known Smartwatch.

I did a lot of research about this particular Smartwatch, and can give you all the specs you need. First of all, this watch is called the “T49”, and is tied to the “Da Fit” app available on Google Play. I tried to find it on the App Store with no luck. Maybe a temporary thing, but if you have an iPhone or iPad, I would give this is a pass since it relies on being paired via Bluetooth, to do a lot of the work.

These are the specs:

Product Model T49

App Name: Da fit

Chip: Realtek 8762D

Blood oxygen VC32S red light + infrared

Audio chips: BK3266

Battery Capacity: Battery capacity: 230mAh (pure cobalt cell of Seiko protection board)

Screen size: 1.90" HD 240*286 full touch rounded screen

Heart rate/blood pressure: VC32S green light

Waterproof: IP67

Bluetooth: Bluetooth v:5.0 (compatible Android 4.4 and IOS 9.0 above version)

Operation mode: Full touch screen+button

Sensor: 3D accelerated ROHMS

Case material: Zinc Alloy case+ABS

Strap material: TPU

Charging mode: Magnetic suction charging line: USB 2.0

Functions Bluetooth calling: To answer reject the phone connection state bracelet end of dial phone call records

Music player: Can play music on the mobile phone, and control the mobile phone music playback

Health Monitor: blood oxygen, step counting, sleep monitoring, heart rate, blood pressure, blood oxygen, sedentary reminder, water reminder

Sports modes: Number of sports steps, sports mileage record, card consumption, a variety of sports mode. Movement track (mobile APP display)

Standby time: 30 days standby and 3-5 days daily battery life (the above data are from the factory laboratory, and may vary depending on personal use environment and process)

Other functions: Mute alarm clock, remote photo taking, music control, sedentary reminder, water reminder, weather display, etc

App language: English, Arabic, Danish, German, Spanish, Finnish, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Swedish, Thai, Ukrainian, Chinese, Traditional Chinese

Firmware language: Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, German, Korean, Spanish, Japanese, French, Russian, Arabic, Ukrainian, Italian, Portuguese

I was able to pair my Smartwatch to one of my Android phones (Samsung) quite easily, and it allowed me to choose a different wallpaper than the standard pink one it came with. Note, you do need your phone to pair with the watch for certain functions, like temperature reading outside, or calling others. You don’t need it if you just want to monitor your blood pressure, how many steps you take, or your pulse. It’s excellent for sporting activities, and offers you a range of workout monitors which can come in handy if you want to play sports, but don’t want to possibly damage a $400+ watch.

It currently has no ability to add other apps, so it’s not THAT smart. However, it does have some games, and all the native apps you’ll need to function.

I’ve just ordered some bumper cases to see if they work with this version. It seems to be a size 38mm, or 40mm. I’ll update this with pics when the bumper cases arrive.

UPDATE 12-29-22: The bumper case needed will be the 45mm, which correlates to the 2022 Apple Watch version. HOWEVER, those cases are moot, since they won’t allow you to move the crown at all. Choose your watch colour wisely, as you won’t be able to customise it with a bumper case at all.

Also, same update: It takes a conventional 20mm strap watch. The Apple Watch bands are meant for that phone alone, and won’t fit it.

Just a note about safety: you have to be street-smart in life, and know there are some people out there who may not have your best interests at heart. To that end, I wouldn’t upload any photos or personal contacts to this kind of phone. If it had the capability of using some payment app, there would be no way I would input my details either. It doesn’t, so it’s moot. But it’s important enough to mention this for the younger, more trusting crowd.

I did use this phone to call relatives without storing numbers and it absolutely works. You just have to have pair it with your phone via Bluetooth, but crucially, you have to keep your phone near you for you to be able to make calls. Think of this as an extender of your phone.

Folks, this is a 40 dollar watch which can be found online for half that, and double that. Don’t expect miracles. But if you’re looking for a pretty nifty gadget which anyone would love to use, especially if you can’t afford the pricier versions out there, give it a whirl!

Review:
I ordered this smart watch primarily because I like watches.

Things I Like:

1.) Large Face.

2.) Two Bands.

3.) Sleep Tracking. I am using this to help me keep track of how many hours of sleep I'm getting. I find that it's really accurate.

4.) Blood Pressure.

5.) Calories Burned. This watch will keep track of how many calories you are burning as you are active throughout the day.

6.) Set Up. I downloaded the GloryFit app, registered for an account and then added the watch. The whole process was very easy. I didn't encounter any issues.

7.) GloryFit App. You have many settings that are available. You can set alarms, reminders to get up and move around, change watch faces, and see all of the health stats you wish to track while wearing the watch.

8.) Bluetooth. The watch connected to my iPhone without any problems. Calls and text messages appear after the phone and watch are paired.

9.) Audio. The audio gets loud for such a small watch. I had no need to lower the volume or raise it.

10.) Build Quality. This watch doesn't feel cheap, in fact it has a premium feel to it.

I am satisfied with this watch.

Review:
I am so impressed with this little watch!

I bought this for my bestie for Christmas. Her primary sport is swimming, but shes recently gotten into running, and she also does strength training, hiking, yoga, paddle sports, etc. I have the Fenix 5S, and she likes the form factor, but she has small wrists and was worried about the weight of the Fenix, along with being rightfully hesitant about the Fenix price point.

This watch is absolutely perfect for her. Its very light weight but still elegant looking. It has multiple sport tracking, wrist based HR (she loves the stress readings that it takes), has a very good battery life, is a touch screen (she finds this more intuitive than all the buttons on the Fenix), and she loves the Garmin Connect app, especially staying competitive in the step challenges each week.

This watch is probably 90% of the Fenix features for less than half the cost. The only really big thing that I would miss from the Fenix to the VA3 is the audible alerts - I have grown so accustomed to my watch beeping to tell me lap markers, heart rate thresholds high and low, etc, I would REALLY miss these audible alerts. She has never had a Garmin, so it doesn't both her as much, but its something that she would use if it were available.

I recommend this watch to pretty much all of my Garmin-envying friends. It is a very impressive watch, and fits the needs of a really large sector of the Garmin-using population.

Special Features: Notifications
Review:
I got this on a lark from a meh.com deal. Turns out it is exactly what I want in a smartwatch. I also own a [[ASIN:B00NC8PMUK Motorola Moto 360 - Black Leather Smart Watch]] which is gathering dust on my nightstand. Here's what the Martian Notifier does right:

1) The watch always works. Other smartwatches turn the display off, or dim it so much it might as well be off, to save battery. The Martian has a separate analog clock, with real hands and it's own 2-year battery. So the watch is always easily visible, even if the smartwatch battery is depleted.
2) The smartwatch does the two things I most loved about my Moto - it receives notifications and it functions as a security device for my phone (Android), so that I don't have to enter a passcode so long as I have the watch nearby. I actually prefer how the Martian app handles notifications over Android Wear's approach.
3) The smartwatch battery lasts a week, and charges in a couple of hours at most. My Moto sometimes didn't make it through a day.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
I am a hard-core weekend warrior. I love to race mountain bikes and run. I've been getting in to Triathlons over the last five years. I've been watching Garmin develop sport watches for several years, but i always felt the price didn't justify the technology they were putting in them. One would be good for this sport and one would do well with that. I could never find a watch that did it all. The Garmin 910XT seemed to finally meet my basic criteria. One watch that can do it all. I want a watch that i can train with and race with. As a triathlete, I've been most intrigued by the Swimming features.

Swimming:
From a training stand point, the 910XT can support lap-swimming and record distance, speed, strokes. Again, for years, no Garmin product has had the ability to gather lap data while inside a pool. But, the FR910XT does exactly that. That's some forward thinking. In practice, i'm still getting used to all the buttons and all the different screens. Again, I've never owned a Garmin. So far my favorite swim feature is the lap keeper, you just set the length of the pool and off you go. The swimming mode supports a number of different strokes depending on how you swim. It also features an open water mode. Unfortunately, it's still to cold to try it out. I'll update during my first race or open water swim.

Biking:
The 910XT has made a number of small improvements over previous watches that i've looked at. It offers the ability to now set up multiple bikes. I have three bikes, one mountain, one road, and one tri-bike. I can store different saved parameters, such as ANT+ sensor details (power/speed/cadence) and wheel size/bike weight. I ride a 29er mountain bike for run 700cc on both my road and tri bikes. Again, one watch to run them all. The 910XT is designed to be either worn or mounted on the bike. I've found i really like wearing over messing around with mounting it. The display is fully customizable. It will show up to four fields of data. I use time, distance, speed, and cadence, but i recall that you can toggle through eight measures in all.

Running:
The 910XT comes pretty well stacked for my running needs. Garmin has proven that can make awesome running watches in the past. Many of the same feature that i use for biking are the same for running. I've been trying to use the HR monitor during my running and biking training days. It seems be accurate. It has a Run/Walk feature which i've found is pretty cool for interval training. I use the distance and pace features the most. It's so much information, that i've tried to keep it simple.

Conclusion:
Over all, there is so much to learn about this watch. It's a bit like drinking from a fire hose. How does the watch work, the HR monitor, and then how do you download everything to your compute? The data it collects is endless. If you're a triathlete, then this is the watch, a single device that does it all and that's why i bought it. It should be 5 star for all the features, but for now just 4 because it's taking me a lot of time to figure everything out. It's a bit complicated, but well worth the time investment to learn how to use it.

Pro's:

Indoor lap swimming mode, recording distance/speed/strokes
Run/Walk feature
Virtual Racer feature, ability to race past performances/others
The back of the unit can twist/lock onto a traditional Garmin bike computer mount (slick)
Accurately tracks distance, heart rate, pace and a ton of other metrics
Wirelessly downloads workouts via ANT+ to computer or cell phone
Supports Training Peaks Metrics also pedal based power meters

Con's:

Doesn't record/display heart rate while underwater
Short battery life on 20 hours so it doesn't work as a normal day to day non-sports watch
Can't split between two people(husband/wife) it will only support one person at a time on watch
No touch screen. I've seen Garmin do it on other devices, so why not this one?
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Short battery life on 20 hours

Review:
Disclaimer: This product was paid for and sent to me free. They did not require a review but did ask for one and I am happy to provide one. All opinions are my own and are not influenced by the company or seller in anyway.

The TOZO S1 Smartwatch is a promising smartwatch with no doubt many upgrades to come. There are many things I love about this watch.

1. The 240x240 resolution display, while in comparison to a phone is low quality, on the comfortably sized, 1.54 inch screen, is very sharp and bright and looks almost better than some name brand watches.
2. The battery life is insane. When I first received this watch, it read as fully charged, and took a week for it to run out. 7 days on one charge. I don't care who you are, that's impressive.
3. The weight is perfect. Coming in at a staggering 4.8 ounces, this watch is somehow neither heavy nor light. It feels like a breath of fresh air on your wrist.
4. The vibration is just perfect. Enough to grab your attention, but not enough to startle you while driving. Its a nice pulse rather than a shock.

This watch is gleamingly positive, but not without its flaws.

1. When a notification is received, there is no way to enlarge it. You get the preview, and even if you swipe over to open is, you cannot read or or reply to a message. This is a simple software fix that I hope, and pray that TOZO will include in future updates.
2. The synthetic material used for that watch band is very uncomfortable and will pinch the hairs of your skin if too tight.
3. The fit of the watch is odd, but maybe just for me. I'm somewhere between two sizing holes, so one is too tight and the other is too loose, but I don't blame this on TOZO, just my personal experience.

Overall, this watch is amazing. At only $39.98 USD (at the time of review), this watch not only holds its own, but is in someway better than some name brand watches that I have seen. There are flaws, yes, but I guarantee if you but this watch, it will satisfy every need you may have. This watch is in my daily routine and will only leave when the next one comes out. TOZO is not your average cheap electronic company. They are above that. Their products are some of the best I've ever used, even despite the price. Don't Doubt, Go TOZO.

Review:
I'm a long time Garmin user, and have been using Garmin running watches starting 2011.
This watch has exceeded my expectations and I'm a very happy with it for 6 months now.
I wasn't convinced that I should buy the VivoActive 3 as every Garmin watch I had before had issues. Ranging from firmware and software problems, super slow GPS detection or material defects, I have in touch with Garmin support just too many times.
I decided to buy this watch after my Vivoactive HR wouldn't charge or be recognised by my computer anymore after connecting it by cable. That was well after the wristband had broken and the screen had cracked.
This watch feels and acts a lot better so far. A quick overview:
Pros:
+ Usability
+ Looks
+ Sleep tracking: although I didn't think I would sleep well wearing a watch, I have found it to not an issue at all. The sleep tracking can be improved to be more accurate, but overall I'm happy that it tracks sleep pretty well without any setup.
+ design / quality. So far, no complaints. not too sportswatchy and nothing has broken yet.
+ Glass: worried that I might bump and break the glass, as other reviewers wrote I did buy and install a screen protector. So far this works great ([3-Pack] Supershieldz for Garmin Vivoactive 3 )
+great GarminIQ apps and new watchfaces can be downloaded and installed - super handy for runners

Cons:
- the biggest downside I have found so far is short battery life. Where my Vivoactive HR could work a week on a charge, this watch needs to be charged every other day. I use GPS about 4/5 hours per week while running, so the battery drain is really mostly just from passive use. I have already turned off or reduced many notification features that use buzzing or backlight to save as much energy as possible.
- The altimeter is highly unreliable. Software updates haven't solved this so far. To the point where it the data is as good as useless. I have to manually correct my runs elevation profile in Strava and in Garmin Connect.

Review:
If you're looking for a great price for a practical smartwatch with a long battery life, this is the one. The battery life definitely lives up to the 7 day expectancy. The watch works great as a notification center and remote for your phone. I use mine with an iPhone 5s. The only limitation is that you can't reply to text messages from the watch with iOS phones unless you have AT&T, Verizon, or T-Mobile. Android phones get this function for all carriers though. However you can still send SMS and the built in microphone works well for this function. The watch is water resistant and is fairly sturdy. I've bumped it a few times and haven't noticed any scratches yet but if you're wearing it actively they have protective skins you can buy. I purchased the watch strictly for the notification function so I don't have to take my phone out of my pocket to see who's calling, what a text says, what event is coming up or currently happening, or what email just showed up. Since all push notification go to the watch, I even get my auto updates from the Google App and IFTTT which is great. The silent alarm function is great for not waking my wife up in the morning when I get up for work. I love being able to control (skip, pause, play, go back) my phone music from my wrist when I'm driving so that I can keep my eyes on the road or when I'm doing yard work. The health tracking is not the reason I bought the watch but it's an added bonus. I enjoy seeing the steps and HR numbers and the sleep tracking. All seem fairly accurate for a wrist device. I'm enjoying playing around with different watch faces to always have a quick glance of weather, steps, time, date, location, battery life, and bluetooth connection. Just having the screen always on and a long lasting battery life for close to $100 is awesome. I would not own a smart watch if it wasn't for pebble. This is the right price and functionality to motivate me into the smart watch market.

Update: A little over 1 year after purchasing this watch. I've used it every day since I bought it and I still really like it. It's a shame the company is no longer around. Here's a few updates after year of use. One of my favorite aspects that I didn't mention in the original review is the buttons. The four buttons with customizable shortcuts are a fantastic piece to the watch. I much prefer this over any kind of touch screen function. I can do a number of things without ever looking at my watch. I know the shortcut sequence and can do things like change songs, send canned texts, or snooze an alarm without ever looking. I also switched to a pixel phone 3 months ago and I can say that the android experience with the watch is much more enjoyable than the iPhone. With the iPhone you have to keep the Pebble App open all the time to keep getting notifications. This is not the case for android phones. I never bought a screen protector for the watch and I am starting to see a few minor scratches but nothing that is very obvious. I need to start with what this watch isn't. It's not a traditional "smart watch" like a Gear or another device running WearOS. This unfortunately means that the options for apps is somewhat limited. But having said that, this is a fantastic fitness tracker. All of the sensors that I'm able to test are all very accurate. Upon receipt (Monday, Feb. 13th), I charged the device fully and put it on. The battery didn't reach critical levels for over a week. (full disclosure, I could not wear it for ~6 hours during weekdays due to restrictions at my job. But it was still on). I charged it up again and left it sitting on my desk disconnected from the charger but still on to see how it would do at 'idle'. That was about 2.5 weeks ago and when I put it on for this review, the battery is still at 47%. If that trend holds, it could feasibly sit idle for a month without needing to charge. IMO, you can't beat that with a stick. So, if you're in the market for a fitness tracker with a robust sensor package that doesn't hit too hard in the wallet, you've found it.

Summary:
While there are more feature rich smart watches available, this is an excellent purchase for a fitness tracker and is best in class when you consider the price

Review:
I'm sad that Pebble is no longer producing and supporting watches, and I'm sadder that I never got the Time 2 that was supposed to ship in November 2016 before the company was bought and closed by Fitbit. Nevertheless, this watch so far is excellent. There just isn't any other smartwatch out there that does what Pebble does the way Pebble does it, and I like it. I hope Fitbit will produce future products that fit or improve upon this niche.

Pros:
- the design looks sharp. Classy but not too classy. Geeky but not too geeky.
- the battery power is phenomenal
- the display is good in most lighting (a little dim in low light, but easy to bring up the backlight if necessary with a shake or button-push) and great in bright light
- notifications with iPhone 6s running iOS 10 work perfectly
- the simplicity of programming buttons for the quick extra functions you want to see (such as weather forecast or list of notifications)

Cons:
- I know I said the design is sharp as a pro, but the bezel is a little too big for the screen, so it's also a small con
- voice replies to texts beyond very simple, short phrases are so spotty that it's not worth it (don't expect Siri-level transcription). Just pull out your phone.
- most of all, although Pebble is not terribly expensive compared to other smartwatches, it is a risky buy considering the only warranty is the return policy of the company you're buying it from, AND it is unknown for how long all functions will work with future iOS updates. Supposedly Fitbit will attempt to keep services running through 2017.

P.S. I highly recommend the Business Time watch face. It's a rare face that you actually have to pay for, but it's WAY worth 99 cents. Nice basic customizations of colors and info display. I keep trying different faces just for something different, and I always come back.
P.P.S. I should mention that the watch band in the pictures is not the grey leather band that came with the watch. That band looked good and was comfortable but I replaced it with a black silicone waterproof band.

Review:
I have evaluated and tested several Wear OS options and I can say that the TicWatch Pro (I have the LTE model) is by far the best experience that I've had with a Wear OS device. The battery life has been excellent compared to others that I have tried; with typical usage I get ~2 days of use, sometimes 1.5-1.75 if days include multiple workouts. Still beats the day or less I was getting from Huawei's.

Speed is fantastic -- was a little skeptical of the slightly older processor but it has proven to be just fine and way faster than other watches bearing the same processor. More RAM or just a better design, I don't know... but it is noticeable.

Only issue at all that I have had, and this is minor, is that the watch seems to be slightly inferior to Huawei's when attempting to detect what exercise you are doing when lifting weights. It gets close, usually correct one is the 2nd or 3rd choice when it misses, but it is slightly less accurate. Other, easier to identify exercises, such as walking or jogging seem to be pretty accurate on their tracking of distance. I like having the continuous heart rate monitor and it seems reasonable, but have not put it against a medical-grade device to test accuracy. And you can swim in it, which is great!

For Android users, I highly recommend this particular watch over other current offerings.

Review:
I've only had the watch for a few days. I've successfully configured it and used it for two workouts. I thought I'd share some of my observations. Before that, let me qualify my review. I paid full price for my watch and I am under no obligation to write a review. This is just an attempt to share my thoughts for others looking for opinions.

I have a Samsung S8+ and was looking for a smartwatch that would seemlessly integrate with the phone for email, messages and fitness tracking. I normally use my Polar H7 chest strap with iCardio 4 to track my workouts.

I found that the configuration was fairly straight forward. I watched a number of Youtube unboxing, configuration and review videos before purchasing my watch. Consequently, I knew what to expect and what to do when the box showed up at my house. I would highly recommend you do the same as the documentation is lacking... they know that most people won't read it.

I turned off GPS and location services. I am not a jogger/runner/walker... I do not need anything tracking my movement through the gym. I turned the heart rate monitor to monitor continuously. My display is set to "7". Going from fully charged at 6:30am to put back on the charging cradle at 11pm with 58% left on the battery. I get LOTS of emails and quite a few text messages on my phone... all of which were forwarded to my watch; including pictures sent to me by my 7yo granddaughter. So, no problem with battery life for me... 5 stars.

I didn't buy the watch for its fitness tracking only. I wanted it for the convenience of getting notifications and email without having to pull out my phone. Not only am I able to see my email and messages, I am able to respond to them as well using S-voice. Since I'm not reviewing S-voice, I won't tell you how much I wish that Bixby worked on this watch. My wife has an Apple iWatch and quite frankly, when comparing Siri with S-voice, I feel like the poor cousin. However, it meets my requirements for both email and messages... 5 stars.

I am trying to work with the text entry interface. I am trying to write my message into the screen and the interface recognizes all the U.S. letters and numbers I type. Unfortunately, I have not been able to get it to do anything except upper case letters. I'll have to research the cause of this and figure out how to make it enter upper and lower case letters. As you can imagine, IT'S RUDE TO ALWAYS TYPE IN CAPS!... So, this feature is not as intuitive as I was led to believe. So, although it recognizes all the letters and numbers, there are some complications... 3 stars.

As a fitness tracker, it utilizes a proprietary bluetooth interface and therefore, doesn't integrate with iCardio... a BIG issue for me. If I want to continue to utilize iCardio, I still need to wear my Polar H7 chest strap. Since this is not my primary use for the watch, it is an inconvenience rather than a non-starter. It integrates with S-health, which I find to be somewhat archaic in its design... not impressed with its user interface. For me personally, I will continue to utilize my chest strap for my workouts... 1 star

There has been some discussion as to the accuracy of the Gear Sport heart rate monitor. Having used both the Gear Sport/S-Healt and Polar H7/iCardo, I can give you some numbers. I did a HIIT workout:

Gear Sport: 499 Calories, 112 Avg BPM, 143 Max BPM
Polar H7: 749 Calories, 117 Avg BPM, 156 Max BPM

I have a theory about the differences and I will share it. I believe the Gear Sport measures heart rates every second. At the same time, it calculates the calorie burn and accumulates accordingly. I believe the Polar H7 measures heart rates more frequently and therefore, gets a more accurate measurement of calorie burn... as well as peak heart rates. To the average person, a one second interval between readings should be accurate enough (assuming the Polar H7 is the gold standard of heart rate monitors). I am a computer software developer myself and most people wouldn't realize that computers measure things in microseconds... that is 6 digits to the right of the decimial (.000001). As you can imagine, a lot of things happen in the real world and on a computer within a second. When averaging heart rates and calorie burn between intervals, more data points allow for more accuracy.

In any case, I don't really care about calorie counting... I am more interested in getting a workout completed in the correct heart rate zones. I consider the heart rate monitor to be important and calories burned to be of monumental inconsequence. I've measured my heart rate on three different pieces of hardware at the same time; the Polar H7, Precor Elliptical and my blood pressure cuff. All were within a couple of BPMs. Consequently, I consider the Gear Sport to be close enough to keep me in my heart rate zones and therefore, it gets 4 stars. I would give it 5 stars for this feature if it were a bit closer (less than 5 difference).

To summarize, the apps I needed (Messages, Email) work fine. The fact that I can control my Spotify is a plus. As a fitness tracker, the hardware keeps me in my zones however, the software (S-health) is truly lacking. I'm hoping that Samsung is able to get more developers working apps for their Gear products.

Suggestions to Samsung would be:
1.) Provide customized workout types on the watch. Kettlebells, Weights, HIIT, Core... etc. Seriously, who does crunches as a workout???
2.) Provide the ability to measure recovery after the workout is complete... measure and track heart rate for a two minute recovery window.

Overall: 4 stars... limited number of available apps.

I am a loyal Garmin fitness tracker gal, and recently decided to upgrade from the Vivofit which I’d had for over three years to the Vivosmart HR+

There are a lot of awesome features on this device, but also a few drawbacks that make me miss my Vivofit.
This is still waterproof for swimming and showering. The GPS tracker is really cool for tracking my outdoor walks and gives me an accurate account of how far I’ve walked. And I do like getting notifications from my phone on my wrist.

One feature that is probably my favorite that is different than my previous fitness tracker is that when I start an activity I have the option to choose to set a distance to go. I have found that this pushes me to go further and because it vibrates for every mile and when I am halfway to my goal, I find I try to move faster as well.

Okay, here are the cons -
Because of the heartrate monitor this thing is pretty bulky on my wrist and is not attractive at all. It juts our pretty far, and this makes it scrape against objects easily.
That leads me to my next con, the screen scratches ridiculously easy! Seriously, if you get this, buy a screen protector and put it on before it even comes out of the box. Even bumping it on my computer keyboard while typing (I wear it to the inside of my wrist) put minor scratches on it before I got a screen protector.

Overall, the features are cool, but I don’t like the size of it and am thinking of returning it in exchange for the Vivosmart 3 without the heart rate monitor.

Review:
My initial impressions - First, I should say I have been pretty happy with my Vivoactive HR since getting it well over a year ago. It needed a couple of improvements so I was looking forward to the upgrade.

One thing that didn't need fixing was the band, but Garmin decided to move from a comfortable band that was easy to hold the watch with when taking it off/putting it on. To a thin, cheap feeling piece of crap band. It's also shorter than the short version that was on the VAHR. Horrible move by Garmin and that alone might be enough to return the thing. I would have rather they shipped it without a band and allowed me to use the bands from the VAHR, but they also reduced the width so they won't fit.

The pairing process is as problematic as every other Garmin watch. It seemed to pair, except the watch didn't think so and kept trying. I think this is as much a Bluetooth problem as it is the watch. It's a poor system that the Bluetooth SIG still hasn't figured out how to make it user friendly.

After about ten attempts I got it to pair up with my phone. It would find and connect and then I'd type in the code and the app/watch would lock up on the initial sync, but eventually it relented.

There are many apps that are not compatible with the VA3, so be warned as loading a non-compatible app can lead to problems...especially fast battery drain.

Review:
All the promise of a Garmin product but it falls terribly short when you put it to the test. I love Garmin products and had been waiting to purchase a D2 for several years but wasn't putting up with Garmin's higher price points. This one hit the right value range to me so I decided to purchase it.

My reasons for buying this watch were to have emergency and backup nav capability, a backup altimeter, and the wrist pulse ox capability. All of it seemed fine on the ground, but when I flew the watch for the first time, the shortcomings became apparent.

The pulse ox had a hard time performing and required me to be very still to get a reading. Even while hand-flying a light aircraft in smooth air, it was having a hard time apparently from the natural vibration of the engine and airframe (note the pic from the Garmin reference materials). This defeats the purpose of it being an aviation watch. It also gave me readings of such variation that I did not trust it.

It had a hard time locking on satellites in the plane. I understand it needs a good view of the sky, but again its an aviation watch which, by default, means it will be in the cockpit. If its only supposed to be an open cockpit watch, then Garmin needs to state that. Once it did lock on, navigation worked fine when I had the time to enter my waypoint, etc - however the menus are very cumbersome if you are in an emergency scenario which was another premise of my purchase.

The altimeter was excellent - no complaints on that. It bluetoothed to my phone and adjusted for local barometric pressure perfectly and very close to the aircraft instrumentation. This they got right.

Additional issues are as follows, but I would have lived if had better results with all three areas noted above:
- Software is cumbersome - too easy to exit the app while flying inadvertently; cant get back to widgets while in flying app
- Too many different apps needed to setup and use the watch
- Watch casing and strap are very cheap feeling - plastic - Pulsar and Timex do better for less than $50
- Lack of integration outside Garmin Pilot and the Garmin world. I was a Garmin Pilot believer and happy with it, but USAF has gone with ForeFlight and all my W&B info is preloaded in it, so I have let Garmin Pilot go. I know Garmin wants to link its products, but you guys need to try and compete in other ways as this will not be sustainable for you. I think your profitability would increase if products could be chosen more freely such as the watches. To be clear - this is not the reason I am returning it. I would have lived with this, but you guys can do better.

If Garmin is reading this, let me help you develop this watch and test it in real world conditions. I want a watch that works too and happy to pay for it, but let's get it right please. No reason to keep fumbling this.

Review:
I bought this watch hoping to be able to manage several things with my health. Primary among those is my sleep. I received this watch Friday (24 Jan), downloaded the special app (it is not compatible with Google Wear or any other default Android wear apps), charged the watch to full, then connected and configured the watch.

The good: the CT FIT app is simple to use and to connect the watch to my phone. The watch is lightweight and I only needed to charge the phone once since the initial charge (Today is 27 Jan).

The not so good: I didn't realize in the product description that the bands are just nylon with a Velcro closure. It looks very cheap. It, like most wearable devices I've seen, has a proprietary charging device so you are stuck with just one way to charge the watch.

The BAD: The watch repeatedly "forgets" my settings. I created a profile and within a day I had to create it again. This morning it seemed to forget my profile yet again. Except for taking a shower, I have worn the watch continually and had my phone within arms reach at all times, yet there are many periods of "no data" from my watch to my phone. I primarily wanted to track my sleep habits. In the three days that I have owned and worn the watch, it has successfully tracked one night of sleep. The touch screen is not user friendly, I cannot adjust the brightness.

The Ugly: The built in weather app does not update. It has continually indicated that the temperature is 39 degrees F, even when it was 69 degrees yesterday, 48 now, and has not been 39 degrees since the early morning hours of Saturday morning.

I am returning this purchase and wanting my money back. Don't buy this product.

Screen Size: touch screen
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The watch should have a long battery life that requires infrequent charging. The watch should have a touch screen.

Review:
Comparing to my wife's Samsung, which she paid 3x the price - this does 90% of the functions. First the good parts: The sleep tracking is great, the step counter and heart rate monitor is as accurate as any of these as far as I can tell. I was able to firmware update easily and add new watch faces immediately- including a custom picture of my bird! The battery life is great! Getting this on a Monday, by Friday mid-day the battery was still at 20%.
What is lacking - while there is an oxygen monitor, it doesn't automatically run while sleeping. There is a stress monitor, but I have no idea how it tests that? The text messages are truncated and you can't reply to them without getting your phone, and emails rarely show more than the subject line. The weather doesn't come from an accurate source? And some of the watch faces like the custom picture one- some of the info from other watch faces would be welcome instead of just the date and time.
Also on the Samsung, there are more apps like a map which this doesn't have, but I'm not missing those personally at this price point.
I feel as though some of the issues with running the SpO2 automatically and pulling weather info from a better source could be fixed in a future software update, but even as-is this is really solid.

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review: Okay where does one begin? I bought this GPS sport watch over a few other brands to mainly have a sync ready HR to run with my cycling computer which is a Xplova X5 Evo.  I bought new and not refurbished and the first one worked three days then would not read certain functions,  mainly HR.  So I returned it for a new replacement.  The new one has worked flawlessly now.  I love the preloaded apps and mainly only use cycling to sync up with my cycling computer.  It works like a charm. Now there ar ed detractors of a wrist HR reader as not being accurate but neither is a chest strap.  They are just somewhat in the area to give you a somewhat picture of HR while exercising and that is okay.  I also love this watch does VO2 reading ( once again not as accurate as traditional methods to measure) but still gets you in a ballpark.  My step measurement is spot on as you measure how many steps (normal stride) to cover 20 feet as the method I used.  It is spot on.  The most impressive feature is the screen does not wash out in direct sunlight.  In fact, it is more readable in direct sunlight unlike most electronic screens.  Plus being a 20mm watch band there are many replacement bands as well.  Charging time you ask???? Extremely quick and usually from like 25% to 100% is around 45 minutes.  I am getting about 4 days of mixing GPS activity with regular functions which to me is very good.  Claimed is about 7 days regular use and 14 hours constant GPS functionality.  Worth the money being a older version being the Vivoactive 4 has been out about two years and this model going on 3-4? Most definitely at the price of almost half.  Highly recommended if you need a basic but somewhat expandable sport watch that you can also swim in.  I will update this review in a year or sooner if it fails for no reason. So why 4 stars? I would have given 4.5 stars but Amazon does not have half star ratings.  Nothing is ever really a 5 star but simple products.....this watch could improve on a few things.

Special Features: Time Display, Sleep Monitor, Phone Call, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
The Tickwatch E is my first foray into the world of WearOS and on the whole I've been very happy with it. I picked this up during the Prime Day special and took my first test drive with the watch at San Diego comic con. I had some initial reservations due to some of the other reviews, especially the ones noting poor battery life... my previous watch was a Fitbit blaze with a typical battery life measured in days and a lot of the reviews for this watch pointed more towards hours. Fortunately with my normal usage (media control, notifications, texting a few times a day) it seems like there is plenty of battery life at hand. That said, when you start to tax it more (things like: navigation, google fit workout mode w/heart rate, phone calls from watch,), it can start to suck down battery a lot quicker, but never enough that it hasn't gone through a whole day with battery life to spare.

Good:
- Fantastic entry level watch with great pricing.
- Battery life is good
- Extremely feature rich at this price point
- Does not have tons of pre-loaded apps I'll never use

Not so good:
- Watch band fit, but just barely (6'3" with a large frame)
- WearOS can be a bit quirky... I really don't need single gesture watch face switching
- Incoming calls don't always buzz the watch before the call has been missed (this might be a phone issue)
- Dismissing notifications by swiping to the side can be more miss than hit, it often thinks I'm pulling up or down slightly rather than straight across.
- Not waterproof, holds up fine to splashes though.

Other thoughts:
- Even if the band fits you just fine, you may want to spend a couple extra dollars to get a new one. I didn't find the included one to be terribly comfortable. (shop for 20mm bands)
- It's not the most stylish design and it's plastic... you wont want to pair this with a suit, but for a function forward casual every day watch, it's perfect.

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I know GPS watches become more accurate the more you use it, but this watch when I first started using it was terrible. This is my 4th Garmin watch I've owned and it is by far the worst. My first run said I ran 6.85 miles when I know the run was actually 7.2 miles (I run those distances all of the time). The GPS distance has gotten a little better with more use.

The heart rate function is also not very accurate when you are doing activities were your arm isn't moving very much such as indoor cycling and indoor rowing, and basically the "cardio" function. I've done these activities many times and for 20-25 minutes the heart rate will show as 80-90 bpm while I am rowing and cycling at a decently hard rate. For some reason when you are walking or running, the heart rate monitor seems to work pretty well.

The screen is also very sensitive and sometimes I can move through the menus and sometimes change the face and the clock just sitting around in a meeting. If you do ANY accidental screen touching this can happen.

Overall, don't get this watch. I was also looking at a Forerunner and the Fenix 3 and I kind of wish I had purchased one of them instead.

Special Features: Smart Watch Bluetooth 5.0 Activity Tracker with Heart Rate Monitor Sleep Monitor Pedometer
Review:
My first smartwatch and this TOZO was a great place to start.

First things first. It was an affordable price point, arrived quickly, and was nicely packaged (I'm a sucker for good packaging).

It was easy to set up and sync with my iPhone and I love the quick and easy magnetic charger (takes about 40 minutes to fully charge). Speaking of charges, it holds a charge for a long time (roughly 7 days of usage), I was surprized and impressed.

Tactile-wise, the watch is thin and light, and the band is a nice, soft silicone, so it's comfortable to wear. It also comes with a smaller band to change out, if you're like me and have small wrists. It was a little tricky to do the band swap, as it took steady hands and eagle eyes, both which I lack!

It also has a lot of customization options such as watch faces, which I enjoy changing, to reflect my mood. :-) I suspect there will be even more options to come, as this is the first version 1.0.

In addition to the time (YES! It does tell time!) ;-) , it features a heart rate monitor, pedometer and sleep monitoring, and if you connect it to your iPhone via Bluetooth, it will give you various alerts (though you do need to check your phone for the full message, etc.) I also like that it allows one to use the 24 hour clock, which is my preference.

The only con I have is that the imperial measurement system (for USA), the abbreviation for pounds is incorrect, as it says "LiBS". I'm sure they'll correct this soon, if they haven't already.

Overall, I like the watch and it seems a great entry level product for the price. I've purchased other TOZO products that are great value for money, and the company stands behind their products, as anytime I have a question or concern, they are quick to respond. Thanks, TOZO!

Review:
Great sport watch - even if some of the most advanced features (present in top of the line model) are missing. I wanted a device to keep me motivated to get daily exercise - without breaking the bank. I use it for trail walking and running - along with training plan on Garmin Connect. It works perfectly. It holds the charge and charges very fast - off USB port on my laptop. It grabs GPS within 20-30 secs after stepping outside. I have nothing to compare it with as far as sleep measurement goes - but it works fine for me in that area. Garmin Connect websites can be synced very easily with free MyFitnessPal for calorie counting (takes a day to sync correctly). You can import running or cycling workouts to it and execute them easily.
If I was training for Iron Man and truly needed in depth analysis of my training numbers then I might invest into more expensive model. Other than that it does everything I ever needed in sport watch for not a lot of money. The only problem I had with the watch was the wrist-band - after couple of weeks I had slight allergic reaction to it so I swapped it for stainless steel one and that solved the problem.
I cannot comment on how useful this watch is for swimming but manufacturer says it is waterproof.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
I've been regularly using the watch for 2.5 months and generally I'm very happy with it. I had previously used the widely-panned Garmin 410 and I can very strongly say this watch is a tremendous improvement (the "touch" feature of the 410 was completely frustrating). Nice solid mechanical buttons are the way to go. I use the watch with a Garmin footpod to capture my running cadence, the bike sensor, and of course the HR monitor. All paired easily and work just great. Switching sports is very easy and the time-to-GPS lock is quite rapid. I enjoy uploading my data to the Garmin website to look over what I've done and compare performance over time. The upload process is very easy to do.

The only aspect of the watch that holds me back from the fifth star is swimming. In a pool it simply doesn't accurately record my laps. This complaint has been documented frequently online and Garmin's apparent response is that people should learn to swim better. No matter what I seem to do, it nearly doubles my swim distance. This of course renders all the data captured completely useless. I am admittedly a poor swimmer, but I would have hoped for more from a $400, top of the line watch.

UPDATE: After 7 more months of use - and a lot more swim training - I believe the 910XT has earned a 5th star. I'm a passable swimmer now and the watch virtually never misses a lap. There's the occasional lap added, but that's usually a tip-off that my form is slipping and I'm getting tired. If you're a half-decent swimmer (something I'm still striving for) you won't have a problem with the swim aspect of this watch. It continues to perform extremely well for running & on the bike. I continue to love the watch - my electronic training partner.
