Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
I had the Garmin 305 which did well until a swimmer kicked my arm in a tri and it fell to the bottom of the lake. Evidently the "quick release" was a little too quick.

I switched to Polar but I was not happy with it for several reasons. When I saw the new model being released, I knew I had to try it out. The watch went beyond my expectations.

The Pros on the Garmin vs Polar:
1) You can set data fields from 0-4 to view. This is important because you only want to glance at your watch while swimming, biking, running and you want to get the most important info to you. On the Polar, you can have four data fields but they are harder to read while running, etc.
2) The heart rate monitor is very comfortable to wear and accurate. The Polar is thicker and not as accurate when running (I've had my HR from 0-220 while running!) The Polar didn't stay on in the pool unless I made it really really tight. Ultimately it wasn't worth swimming with the HRM on in the pool.
3) Garmin Connect website easy to use, set up, download, share, etc. It's also important to know that the website includes elevation changes so you can see why you slowed down with increased heart rate while climbing an 8% grade.
4) Customer service was fantastic and quick. Being a guy, I didn't want to read the manual. Once I couldn't figure out how to download the information I called them and they were able to help right away. I dealt with Polar several times. Average response time to email reply was a few days and their response was to usually call them.
5) For swimmers, this is a must. It will keep track of distance without having to touch the "lap" button on your turns. I push the lap button while resting and it knew that I took a break. The watch kept track of my freestyle stroke and when switched to breast stroke, I didn't have to do anything; it just automatically happened.

Overall it's a great watch. Yes it is slightly bigger than the Polar but it's smaller than the older Garmin 310XT. No matter your sport, this is a great watch to have.

Review:
My never ending search for the perfect Smartwatch / Fitness tracking hybrid continues. So far I have tried in order..LG G Watch R, Samsung Gear 2, Asus Zenwatch, Fitbit Surge, Sony Smartwatch 3, Sony Smartband Talk, Striiv Fusion and now the Samsung Gear Fit.
First the good. The screen is so nice. Brilliant color, clear and crisp. All notifications work, including Google Hangouts, Emails, etc. Only issue is that is a pain at times to delete them all. It takes a few taps. Android Wear does it much better and easier. Battery life is ok for me, 2 -3 days. The price at $119 has come way down and now it is a very good value. The heart rate monitor when tested seemed fairly accurate.
The bad: This is a fitness band that can’t track daily calories burned. You can only track calories while exercising or walking. You burn a lot of calories sleeping or even breathing, but Gear Fit does not track them. So you can’t really tell how many calories you can eat to balance it against daily calorie burn. Also, natively they Gear Fit does not track gym workouts. Only Walking, Running, Cycling and Hiking. But they do have an integration with Endomondo which does a decent job of tracking my Crossfit workouts for calories burned. But Endomondo does not integrate with S Health where the calories burned through walking, running, etc or stored. So you really have to use 2 apps to tracking your active calories. And like I said you still will be missing your other non-active calories burned. Sleep tracking is manual and does not give you much info at all. Just when you are motionless or not. There also is no Smart Alarm to wake you when you are in a light sleep. You can use the cell phone alarm and the Gear Fit will pick it up though.
Overall: This will probably be returned too. Comfort is great. Notifications are great. Android Wear is better for notifications as you can respond to Google Hangouts texts via voice or auto text response on the watch. With the Gear Fit you can only use the canned response for true SMS texts. I use Hangouts so this doesn’t help me. The screen is the best of any fitness band or smartwatch I have tried. But the poor set up of fitness and sleep tracking to me is a show stopper on a Fitness band. Based on all the Smartwatches/Fitness Bands I have tried here is my current ranking from best to worst for my needs.
8. Sony Smartband Talk – Good for calorie tracking. Sleep tracking in automatic. The talking aspect on the band itself is nice. But notifications would not work consistently on it. They would come initially and then stop. No idea why. Sony Support did not know either. Also, the e-Ink display is impossible to read in low light and the text is very small.
7. Striiv – Ranked higher than the Sony Smart Talk Band because it is half the price. Notifications are ok, but the fitness tracking aspect is lousy.
6. Samsung Gear 2 – way too big to work out with. Tizen is cumbersome and not as easy to navigate as Android Wear. Camera and speaker on the phone for call is nice. Also, can’t respond to google hangouts texts with Tizen
5. FItbit Surge – Notifications are limited to SMS and Call Alerts. No email, no calendar, no google hangouts, etc. It is even cumbersome to navigate to them when you do get SMS texts. It is good for tracking gym workouts and heart rate. Auto Sleep tracking is nice, but the data doesn’t give you much info. Black and white screen is not as nice as other color ones or as clear as the e-Ink on the Smart talk Band. Great battery life. Never did use the GPS. Continuous heart rate monitor is nice, but it really underestimates it during the workouts.
4. Asus Zenwatch – Looks nice, but too nice to work out with seriously. Also, don’t like the awkward way it measures hear rate. See Sony Smartwatch 3 below for Android Wear Review.
3. LG G Watch R – Better battery life and heart rate monitor than the Asus Zenwatch. Cheap band. Also, too big and bulky to workout with.
2. Samsung Gear Fit – Beautiful screen! Very good on notifications. But the fitness tracking aspect is lousy. Can’t track daily calories in total. Link with Endomondo saves the device for being down to #5 on the list.
1. Sony Smartwatch 3 – true Android Wear Smartwatch. Can respond to Google Hangout texts via voice or canned text response in Android Wear. Best battery life on an Android Wear watch. Able to store music on the phone and with GPS you really can run without your phone. It is more of sportswatch, so good to workout with. Sony Lifelog is good for tracking daily calories. But you still need several less than efficient apps to track gym work outs. It would be good if Lifelog was better. Sleep tracking does not work in Lifelog with the Smartwatch 3. At first Sony told me it did and said I needed to update the firmware. When doing this it broke the watch and it would not reboot. Had to return it and get a new one. Then Sony told me that Life Log does not actually track sleep with the Smartwatch 3. The new one I ordred did not vibrate for some reason and returned it too. Decided to hold off on ordering a 3rd one. And now the price has come down..so this is definitely the Android Wear watch to get if you are going to go with that platform.
I would like to try the MS Band or maybe the Basis Peak, but neither are available now.

___________________

I ordered this smart watch for my daughter. She has been wanting to track her steps, and wearing a watch every day would also really help her with time management. This one is a great fuchsia color and seemed pretty basic so I thought we'd try it.

She loves this watch. Her favorite feature is the customizable watch face. She picked a picture of our pooch to put on there and it made her day. Unfortunately, there is only ONE customizable watch face (there are MANY watch face designs to choose from!), and it doesn't allow you to include battery percentage on there. So she has no idea how much battery charge is left at any given time. She chose date (I think) and number of steps for the customizable face from the few choices given.

Her second favorite feature but maybe my favorite for her is the water reminder. My daughter really struggles to remember to drink throughout the day. Ever since she's worn this watch she is taking a drink every hour that the remind goes off. (We set it to go off hourly between 7am and 6pm.) She also has used the breathing app. It has helped her calm down at times when she needed a bit of help. I'm thrilled she has these two truly beneficial features on her new watch.

There are many other apps on the watch that she doesn't need though, like the camera feature. Her watch is synced with my phone (she doesn't have her own phone) and I turned off all apps that would share data from my phone. I wish there was a way to delete the unwanted features from the watch, or at least move them into their own folder or something. Maybe a future version of its software will have that update.

For now, I'm really pleased with this relatively inexpensive smart watch that has been a big hit with my daughter. I recommend it to anyone seeking a basic and useful smart watch.

Special Features: Health Monitor, GPS
Review:
Ok ive had the Tic Watch E3 two weeks and its time for a review!
TL:DR its great, go get one and try it out!

My last watch was an asus zenwatch 2, so ive been out of the game for a little bit. I was looking for something new and affordable since upgrading from a note 9 to a S22 plus. The Tic Watch E3 is really it!

Initial setup is a breeze and had everything going within minutes of taking it out of the box.

First things first,
The battery life. Its great, up to two days between a fast charge before work (takes about a half hour i think to get a full charge). Im using all the bells and whistles too. Heart rate, steps, sleep, oxygen, gps, spotify and probably more. Really quite surprised actually considering how light this watch is.

This watch is LIGHT! i almost dont like wearing watches because i like my arms to weigh the same. Lol but seriously, between the weight of my old watch and its battery life, i just stopped using it. The Tic Watch E3 is some serious hardware and weighs almost nothing by comparison.

The display, its bright and easy to read even in full daylight and adjustible. the touch sensor on the watch is very well made too. I never thought a keyboard on a display that small would be easy to use. But it is! Ive used it to log into my Tic account to keep track of my activity. And used it to reply to messages. Its surprisingly easy and accurate.

Sleep tracking is very informative, I've actually been sleeping better now that i can see how my routines affect my sleep. And when i feel like i didnt sleep well i have all the info i need to convince my self that i actually did.

Heart rate, steps and GPS are all very accurate and informative. Makes me want to do more in order to break previous records ive set or keep consistent. And when ive been sitting too long it gives me a reminder to get up and move around for a little bit. Extremely helpful when im on my pc too much. (im always on my pc too much)

Its also very easy to manage which apps are notified by the watch and to silence your phones notifications when the watch is active. Helpful so you aren't buzzing on both devices.

Ive been using the Tic apps exclusively on my watch and have no complaints. Plenty of stock watch faces available by default. And through apps like Watchmaker and more you have an endless supply of watch-faces ranging from all the data displayed you can think of to absolute minimal.

Instant familiarity if you've used any other or previous android wear product with a suite of features and options you'll find on more expensive models.

Waterproofing is very acceptable, i dont plan on swimming with it on (personal preference here, i know the watch would be fine) but i get dirty at work. Ill run it under water to wash off the grime before washing my self up and have had no issues.

The glass is tough too, its still as pristine as the day i took it out of the box. Despite the abuse my work causes it.

And last but not least, the watch band it comes with i thought i would replace almost right away. As i did with my Zenwatch and almost any other watch (smart or regular) ive owned. To my surprise i did not need nor want to do that in this case. Its just fine, it works, its comfortable and unless the band wears out or breaks ever ill keep it.

In conclusion to my review, i really like this watch. Ive become more active and generally enjoy using it. Highly recommend you try it out yourself.

Review:
If all you want this for is to be a fancy looking watch that you can change the faces on, I suppose it'll do the job. If you want to get notifications, or really take advantage of any of the "features" this touts the watch itself makes it pretty difficult to do so. As mentioned, I previously owned a Pebble. To be honest the only reason I switched was due to my Pebble getting stuck in a boot error and the company is no more. To be fair, I don't know how many of these gripes are a result of the watch specifically or just an Android Wear issue.

Notifications:
Most of the time, it takes multiple attempts to swipe things up to show notifications from the watchface or to dismiss. Oh, and if I have multiple notifications and want to get to the middle one? I miss it at least once and have to scroll in the opposite direction each time. It's even worse if I get a string of text messages, as the most recent one is displayed right at the top, but then I need to scroll through a backlog of texts to get to the previous new ones. I'm not even really sure what determines how far back it pulls because it doesn't seem to follow a consistent pattern. One of the primary things I'd imagine people want a smart watch is for the ease of seeing notifications, this turns it into a chore just to check my texts. It's more convenient to just take my phone out. It doesn't even give you the courtesy of letting you know it's dying! Sure I get a little light saying it's at 20%, but from what I've seen depending on if I get any notifications that can get me anywhere from a few minutes to maybe 15-30 minutes before it turns off. I haven't gotten any additional notifications, or even a buzz to let me know, "Hey, I'm turning off now".

Touch Screen:
Despite having to swipe up or to the side multiple times for notifications due to it seemingly not detecting the swipe the way I want it to, I regularly look down to see that literally brushing against my shirt or a jacket over it managed to switch my watch face. Or that it dismissed a notification. Yes, there is a way to lock the screen by first tapping to "wake up" the display if it's idle, swiping down, tapping the brightness cog, and hitting the button, but is it really reasonable to have to do all of that to make sure that my SMARTwatch doesn't react to my shirt brushing against the face?

Display:
I don't know who designed the actual watch, but no matter what angle I look at it from, there's a pretty glaring distortion around the edges. It's honestly not the worst thing, but coming from a Pebble this was never an issue I experienced. If I'm paying over $100 for a small screen to connect to my watch that's purpose is to have me interact with it more than the actual phone to an extent, I don't think it's unreasonable to expect the display I'm looking at to be just as smooth as the phone it's supposed to be replacing. The image also shifts over every few minutes in either direction. I can only assume this is to prevent burn-in but it just makes the watch look like the screen is defective due to how sudden and rough the transition is.

Battery:
I didn't expect this to do anything near what my Pebble did, I charged that thing once every 3-4 days on average. But this one I end up needing to charge up almost 3 times a DAY with light use. If I try using the actually really awesome features such as being able to take a call or play music through the watch itself, that 3 times a day easily becomes 4-5 times.

Stability:
More than a handful of times now I've had the watchface distort when I get a notification in and it stays like this for 30 seconds to a minute completely unresponsive to touch or hitting the button. This doesn't seem to be linked to any particular watchface specifically, and like other issues that I've mentioned doesn't seem to follow a specific pattern. Sometimes I have a full day with no issues getting notifications.

I'd be interested to try the Ticwatch that runs on it's own OS to see how much of this is an Android Wear issue.

Special Features: Time Display, Accelerometer, Text Messaging, Notifications
Review:
Background on me is that I used to workout a lot. Now, with small kids and time crunch, I am not hard core and pretty much just walk to run errands. With a regular stroller, not even a jogging stroller. I am more like an active regular person. Still I walk to and from work or the grocery store when possible, and try and keep active. I'd gotten the watch because I wanted a heart rate monitor, and I feel like a fitness oriented watch would give me that plus a grab bag of extras. I have no background in smart watches, but have previously used pedometers, heart rate monitors, and phone apps that do health oriented things like remind me to drink water throughout the day. Ultimately, I want to more efficiently work exercise into my daily routine. I'm not swimming or running. So, that's where I'm coming from.

This watch has been great other than being HUGE on my wrist, and sometimes uncomfortable. In watches, I tend to get the smallest face possible with an all metal band. But, a small watch couldn't have a big touch screen, so can't be helped.

I got what I wanted: a heart rate monitor plus a grab bag of features. This seems like a fantastic watch for a regular person looking to be more fit. In general, the watch is good for measuring things it detects from you, and bad about info you have to tell it. It's just horrible and inadequate for calorie tracking, because you have to tell it. Then it does these extras like track when I'm asleep. It also is a fitness device, not a straight up smart watch and doesn't have speakers and that kind of thing. If a feature is not fitness oriented, then this watch won't have it or it will be poorly developed.

Battery life is maybe 3 days optimistically. 2 days for sure. It takes less than 3 hours to fully charge. I'm not keeping it connected to a phone, and not fiddling with it much.

Also, here's my disclaimer: it feels like Samsung is actively developing the apps for this. For some problems I list below, like not being able to see older notifications, I would expect Samsung to correct those problems some months out. I tend to say if I've updated my review. Still, this review may go out of date regarding what features the watch actually has.

Pros:

Interface is pretty good. You rotate the metal band on the outside of the watch then tap. It becomes pretty easy to do things as long as they are doable.

Doesn't need to be connected to a phone or a data plan or anything like that. It can work solo. You initially do have to install Samsung Gear app on a phone and do a first connection. Before that, the watch is a brick and won't do anything other than show you a screen saying to connect to phone. But, after that, you can take the phone to another city, turn off Blue Tooth, whatever. The watch will do stuff and tell you info. You do need to reconnect to the phone periodically to make changes to settings. The watch won't let you get to most settings. Still, the watch is a device by itself and not just input output for a phone. Also, you would never have a need for a data plan specifically for the watch. Any data is to the phone.

Pedometer: It's really accurate. No false positives when I'm not walking. From other reviews, if you are pushing something with wheels, like a stroller, it may detect that as a different activity than walking. Like, it might think you are riding a bike.

Tracking water and caffeine drinks: An app for counting these is included. I've previously used a phone app for this. Having it on my wrist means I am much more likely to use it consistently.

Sleep tracking: Not totally accurate, but totally interesting and accurate enough that I could do more with it. It detects when I'm asleep and then gives me a report for each sleep that I can click into. When I say not accurate, for me, if I wake up and stay in bed on my phone or something, it records that as restless sleep. It even has recorded me getting up, feeding a baby, and using the restroom as a period of "restless" sleep. Whoa! Each sleep time is divided into motionless vs light vs restless. When I look at sleeps in the phone app (the watch will only show me a recent sleep not a dashboard), the dashboard shows me sleeps with restless counted as sleep. I would have to click into each and see the real sleep time which is just the "light" and "motionless" times. Nevertheless, very interesting to look at.

Heart rate monitor: It has this. If you are looking at this watch because you are coming from the smart watch angle and not the fitness angle, then right now please google for fat burning zone and heart rate. Lower intensity exercise actually burns more fat. And, if you are doing some endurance thing like long distance bicycling, then you can keep at a lower intensity to be able to go 30 miles or whatever and pace yourself to make it. Heart rate lets you do this. The built in heart rate monitor makes you tap for it to read your heart rate. It's supposed to have a setting to automatically take heart rate at intervals, but that wasn't reliable. Maybe it will miss a scheduled check or several of them. You also have to set that up from the phone app, and not from the watch. For me, since I walk to get to work and do errands, the heart rate monitor is great for checking up on intensity.

Cons:

You need the phone for lots of things. Like, I can't set or change my age, weight, etc. from the watch. I need the phone to do this. Weight changes. I should be able to check and change it from the watch. Pretty much, you have to use the Samsung Health phone app pretty regularly to really use the watch.

It's hard to figure out what the watch does. If you ask me can it do something, the answer is either yes or maybe. To find out whether it can do something, you have to poke around on the phone app and google to find forum posts. Maybe I can't do something because I don't know how and there aren't good instructions.

Style: Watch faces look kind of bad. They all match the big black chunky "I'm a Smart watch" look and style. This watch also has a definite device look and feel to it.

Calories: When it calculates calories burned, it seems off. But also, for this watch, you should already have a system for tracking this and just use the watch to get info on workouts.

Calories: The only way to track calories eaten is by a screen where you turn a dial and say a total number for the meal. It adds that to breakfast lunch or dinner based on what time of day you turn the dial. What?! This is really awful. There's no calorie look up for common foods and no itemized list. For me, it's paper log that I enter manually into myfitnesspal . I wish I could look up how many calories common foods have. Michelle Obama's Let's Move initiative released that big XML file of nutrition info for foods. It seems like they could just grab that and whip up a calorie look up screen.

Can't do much with music and calls: OK, this is not really a con, but many people act like it is. This watch doesn't have mic nor speakers unless I'm missing something huge. You can use the watch to pull up the phone's music playlist. But, then the phone has to be connected to the watch by BlueTooth and the phone also has to be connected to a speaker. So, the phone is in the picture, and you may as well use the phone to click play/pause/next. Like I said, this watch is fitness oriented, not general purpose smart watch.

Only shows most recent notification. Once you check it, it's gone off the watch. Once a new one comes in, the old one is bumped, even if you didn't read it. There's no way to find a log and skim it.

Review:
This is a good entry-level smartwatch. It's the first one that I've had, but it seems to work well. It has a number of features such as step tracking, heart rate and blood pressure tracking. I can't verify exactly how precise the heart rate and blood pressure tracking are, but the blood pressure measurement seems pretty similar to what I've gotten at the doctor's and the pharmacy's machine, so it seems to work reasonably well.
The battery life seems pretty good. I've been charging it about once every 4 days or so and it hasn't run out of battery at any point with that charging schedule, so it lasts for a while.
It also has a number of features that I don't use much, such as breathing reminders, sleep trackers, a music player, a camera shutter button to control your phone camera, and a workout tracker.
All the features sync through the app so you can see additional detailed information on your phone.

Review:
** 11/2021 Update - I've had my Instinct for nearly a year now. But after wearing the watch daily, the skin on my wrist is irritated so much that it peels regularly and has left a permanent blister/discoloration. I'm looking to replace it with a similar watch that uses different silicone in the band, which is upsetting because I may have to shop outside Garmin in the future if they use the same silicone in their products.

Right off the bat, I'll mention I'm writing this review not as an athlete or fitness buff, but rather as an average Jane Doe who works on a computer all day and suffers through my gym workouts (sorry Garmin). I bought this after falling out of love with my Apple Watch. My goal was to use my phone less by having only the essential info at my fingertip---er wrist. This little chunker does everything I need - and no more... something the Apple Watch couldn't do for me.

Pros: Customizable watch face lets you choose which metrics you want to see. Notification system is nice and simple. The Body Battery function is fascinating and the heart rate and stress data is helpful for managing anxiety. The app is easy to navigate and extremely informational, even offers female-driven widgets like period tracking. Easy on the eyes with a great backlight function. Virtually indestructible: I've dropped it, smacked it on cupboard doors, spilled coffee on it. Oh and did I mention you only have to charge it every other week?

Cons: It's not flattering on my wrist and took some getting used to, but fair since it's designed for men. It'll disconnect from my phone every once in a while. Sleep tracking is a bit inaccurate. Leaves a little dry skin/rash on my skin where it rests against my desk all day (understandable).

All in all, a great addition that's not another delicate battery-sucking LED screen in our lives.

Review:
I am an Apple user. From phones, to tablets, to laptops, desktops, and yes, the Apple Watch. Admittedly, I have been jealous of the fact that other smart watches have had a classic round watch face design as compared to Apple's odd futuristic rectangle. Now that these classic watches claim to work with Apple iOS, I decided to take the plunge and give the Samsung Gear Sport a spin and see how it compared to Apple on my wrist. Here's what I found.

The build quality of the Samsung is amazing. Totally solid, shiny gunmetal grey in color, with a hefty (but not heavy) feel to it. Even when spinning the bezel (a much better way to navigate your phone than Apple's side mounted wheel, by the way) it feels totally high quality. The included rubber strap is ok, though guys with bigger wrists will want to order a longer (20mm) band. All standard watchbands work with the Samsung, another huge plus over the Apple Watch. Overall, it feels like a solid analog watch on your wrist and that is a huge selling point for me. The display is absolutely stunning, bright, and displays vibrant colors. The number of customizable watch faces available is simply amazing and there are some that truly look like an analog watch. You can download tons more quickly and easily too. I really like the look and feel of the round screen. The overall look and feel of this watch is impressive. You use the round bezel to turn and navigate through settings and apps, but the screen is also a touch screen. It is very sensitive and responsive to the touch, but I found using the bezel is a much easier way to navigate most of the watch features and rarely use the actual touchscreen input. Just like the Apple Watch, this watch wakes as you raise it to your wrist. This could use a little improvement on Samsung's part as I find many times the watch won't wake up on the first try and I have to give my wrist more of a spin to make it turn on. A minor inconvenience, but can be a bit annoying at times. Battery life is exceptional. I'll go three days before needing a recharge sometimes.

The software preinstalled on the watch is good and comparable to Apple's included apps. I find the activity monitor and step counter to be useful, but haven't really went into much depth with the other workout apps, admittedly. Any apps or notifications you find annoying can easily be turned off. Apps pair well with Apple devices and you get notifications with a gentle tap on the wrist. It notifies you of new email or texts, though you can't reply like you can on the Apple Watch. No big deal there, because after a few weeks of having my Apple Watch, I realized just how clunky it is to speak into your watch and went back to using my iPhone for that. I don't miss the reply feature. This may seem like a limitation to some, but I truly don't miss it. Otherwise, the pairing process and app for the iPhone were both easy to use and navigate and truly make the Samsung Gear Watch a viable option for Apple users.

Which would I pick? Overall, I like the Samsung over the Apple watch. I like the clean lines, classic look, compatibility with standard watch bands, availability of hundreds of cool watch faces, and it's bezel interface. The Apple watch has a few more bells and whistles, but for my needs - it's the Samsung I choose to wear daily and not the Apple Watch. Both are great products, but Samsung wins on design alone.

Review:
I was looking for a smart watch for my older sister for her birthday as she recently said she'd like to get one to monitor her activity and health. When I saw this one with a "big face" I thought it would be a good match because she'd be able to see it easier. I got the pink watchband. When it arrived, I compared it to my Apple watch and it indeed has a larger face. It charges with a USB magnetic cable and it charges up quickly. I downloaded the app for iPhone and paired it with my phone. It was fairly easy to set up but I already have a smart watch so I was familiar with how it's supposed to work.

I put the big face watch on one wrist and my Apple watch on the other wrist to test the accuracy of the step monitoring and other things like heartbeat and pulse. After wearing them awhile I compared the stats and they were off a bit, but I hadn't entered my personal info in the big face watch so it used the defaults for height and weight that affected how the watch worked. Overall, I think it's a pretty good watch and has many of the same features of the Apple watch. At about $38, it's a good deal.

I'm sending the watch to my sister who is pretty tech savvy but she has an Android phone and there were some notes in the user manual about the app for the Android that I didn't really understand. I told her to have her son or daughter-in-law help her if she had trouble getting it to work as they both have smart watches. I'll update the review after the Android app is in use to see if it works as well as it did with the iPhone.

Review:
Many of you are like me (or were like me), searching for your very first smart watch. I came across the Ticwatch S while browsing Amazon's Prime Day Deals. They were going for a very good price at the time, so I discussed smart watches in general with a couple co-workers who own SW's and if they are actually worth purchasing. After a few conversations, I decided to take the leap and purchase one. Excited when it came in, I retired my G-Shock Tough Solar and set-up my new Ticwatch S Smartwatch (Knight Model) with my Samsung S8 Edge. The packaging was designed excellently and very minimalist (Watch, USB Magnetic Charging Cable, and Paperwork). It does not come with a wall socket USB adapter/charger, but I have plenty lying around and they are pretty inexpensive anyhow. Set-up was extremely easy and only took minutes. Uses the latest Android Wear OS too, which is a plus.
The watch is extremely lightweight, which worried me on how tough this thing is gonna be. I've been wearing it for almost two weeks now and I've gotta say, it's taken some good abuse and is still standing strong (I'm no construction worker, but I work a pretty physically demanding full time job). At this moment, the watch face doesn't have a single scratch or mar on it and I've bumped it into counters, corners, walls, etc. Whatever they used to manufacture the watch face is pretty tough material. I just worked on some plumbing for my Father, wearing this watch, and it's still holding up (even after getting wet, see photo). Yes, this watch is waterproof to an extent. I haven't swam with it, but I've washed dishes, been outside during a rainstorm, accidently wore it in the shower a couple times, and used a cleaning water bucket with it on. No issues so far. Today, I was just outside playing sports in a rainstorm for an hour and the watch performed as it should. So it's a tough smart watch, it's proven itself to me so far. Now I'm not saying this is a G-Shock or Iron Man, but it's a smart watch and a moderately priced one at that. So you really have to ask yourself what you want in a watch here. Which comes to my headline question, do you need or should you get a smart watch?

Here's some things I find handy about owning a smart watch:
-Notifications on the fly, even when you're away from your phone (I leave my phone on silent,so the watch alerts me to calls, texts, emails, etc with a light vibration), never miss an important text or call or message (even from Facebook, Insta, Twitter, etc)
-Better to glance at your watch while driving than pick up your smartphone, it's safer in my opinion
-Useful apps (Android Wear Operating System) Google Fit, Alarm, Find my phone, Flashlight, and much more
-Google Fit will measure your heartbeat, steps, exercise and send data to your phone
-Works great if you use Google Voice Assist too (I do)
-Is compatible with Google Maps (will actually show a little map on the watch), GPS Built in
-Speaker and Microphone built in (Voice to text is very accurate)
-Answer phone calls, texts, messages with a tap on your wrist. Select music, pause music, play music, all controlled on your wrist.
-I like I can be in another room, while my phone is charging and still have communication with my phone.
-Tells Current Time, Weather, News, etc.

In short, don't expect a freaking full fledged smartphone on your wrist, but a smartwatch adds a level of convenience I found to be worth while.

Now that I've gone over owning a smart watch, here are some pros and cons of this watch in general (Ticwatch S Smartwatch-Knight):
PROS:
-Lightweight
-Durable
-Good, responsive screen and motion controls
-Great selection of Ticwatch digital watch face designs
-Durable straps (I workout with this watch on, no issues, sweat resistant)
-Great Value
-Warranty (Always a breath of relief)
-Good, clear speaker and microphone
-Charges quickly, easy connect and disconnect (Magnetic)
-Charge lasts one and one half to a couple days depending on use.

CONS:
-Not compatible with Fitbit (Not Mobvoi's fault, Fitbit does not allow smartwatches to be compatible with their app, it's annoying)
-Not the prettiest watch, just a plain black, semi-sporty looking watch. Maybe simple is elegant?
-Not the largest amount of built-in memory, but it does have cloud storage

So that's my two coin on this watch and owning a smart watch in general. If you decide to take the leap, this is a great start for your journey into smart watches. You will not be disappointed in this watch. Especially at the price tag for this watch.

Special Features: Time Display, Sleep Monitor, Phone Call, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
So I got this on Amazon Prime Day, and have had it for about 3 weeks now. I'm thinking of returning it. I'll first mention the PROS of this watch, and then tell you why I'm returning it with the CONS. Here's some background on what I do and how this watch fits in with my daily life. I have a Samsung Galaxy S7. I walk my dog for about 45 minutes every morning at 2.5 miles total. Then I work from 9-4 like most other people. Get home. Relax. And then play some adult league sports around 7pm. But the place I play sports is about 45 minutes away from me, and because of traffic, I often take varying routes to get there, and listen to music along the way. I get home around 9-10 depending on the sport. And then shower and my weekday is over. On weekends, I am a sports official. I mostly intended to use this watch to assist me in my officiating with a referee app that a coworker of mine, developed but only for android. Its a great app and Ive seen him use it. Sometimes I'll only officiate 1-3 games a day. Others will be 5-6 or all day tournaments. I needed a watch that could last a full 24 day on either a weekday or weekend with all these intended uses. I like to track my fitness so all the active stuff I mentioned, I wanted heart/some gps/and needed to be connected to my phone the whole time for data transfer. I needed to be able to get AT LEAST 7-8 hours of heavy use.

Pros:
+ Look/Feel: The minimalistic all-matte black finish is right up my alley. In terms of looking neutral and not flashy enough to draw attention, but looks sophisticated and elegant enough that if people do notice, I've had people ask "Hey what watch is that?" Couple it with your favorite watch face and its a great accessory to basically any outfit. The silicone band is standard enough. Good for wearing normally or for doing sporty things like I do. They are 22mm bands I think so they can be switched out with any 3rd party bands. For the price and for someone who doesnt want something too flashy but still stylish, this gets a 5/5 from me.
+ Play Store: Because this is Android wear, you have the option of all the apps that android gives, and are not in the extremely limited range that some other watches have (cough* samsung gear)
+ Heart rate monitor
+ Magnetic charger
+ Speaker to take calls/listen to music
+ If you have a samsung phone and have the fast lightning charger (or any fast charging block I suppose), this thing will go from 15% to 100% in about 30 minutes. However, that's only if youre near an outlet.

Okay. So why do I want to return this even though I got it at a somewhat good deal? (Currently $160, bought it for 99)
Cons:
1) The damn battery life. I read at least 60-70 reviews and watched a large handful of youtube videos about this watch before buying it. The stances of people on battery life were very mixed but mostly negative. Some people said they got great battery life after they altered some settings and did a few power cycles. Some people said they couldnt get a day out of it (but had all the heavy features on like wifi, gps, always-on display, etc). Others said they disabled those heavy settings and did the power cycles and still didnt get a full day out of it. Because of all the inconclusive comments on battery life, I decided I would try it out for myself and remain optimistic that I could get decent battery life out of this thing.
- For my weekday use, starting a day at 100%, using the built in fitness app, I do a "fitness walking" workout when I go with my dog. 45 minutes later, ITS AT 77%!!!! Just doing a 45 minute workout, which basically just consists of walking around a few blocks, burns 23% of my battery. I dont have much time to charge it. So I go to work. Luckily at work, I dont have much use for it there, as I have my laptop and what not. By the end of the work day, after using it for the occasional notification and time-check, I'll have about 55%. Then I come home. Relax. And go to the adult league sports. I activate another workout to whatever sport, and by the time I get back home, I'm usually done to 15%.
- Keep in mind, I do deactivate all the "unnecessary" features when not in use. In other words, when Im not using it for fitness, gps is off, heart rate is off, always-on display is NEVER on, I have it at medium brightness, but its always connected to my phone via bluetooth. I also did 6-7 power cycles when I first got it, and updated everything to the latest software. I go from 100% to 15% from TWO workouts that barely eclipse 150 minutes total, plus a workday of notifications. I think thats horrendous.
- Not to mention that my weekend use is much worse. Now granted, this officiating app I use, does use GPS and step tracking, but it doesnt use heartrate monitoring. But it does have a timer I set for a game, and when a team scores, I input a score. If they get a yellow/red card, I input that as well. I needed this watch to be ableto last my SIX 80-minute games. Now I dont do 6 games every weekend, but I needed it to be able to last that long just in case I do. I started it off easy and used this + officiating app for TWO 80-minute games. I guess GPS isn't optimized very well because after two back to back games (started at 100% battery), I was at 30%!!!!!!!!!!!!! THREE ZERO. I know the app takes a lot, but youre telling me that after ~160 minutes, the watch needs another charge before I can do a 3rd game? Cmon man.
- Conclusion: Unfortunately, the battery life is as bad as people say, IF YOUR USE IS BEYOND THAT OF the average joe who just wants to be able to check notifications on their wrist. If thats all you want, then you may be just fine with the battery life. But if you want to use this for fitness or want to use it for a specific task that involves pairing with an app+phone and heavy use, then this is NOT the watch for you. The battery life is the #1 deal breaker for me and ultimately the reason why Im returning this.

2) No default messaging app or connection???? When I get a text to my galaxy s7, I do get the notification on my watch, and Im able to reply to it if I open it immediately out of that notification. However. If acknowledge the notification and close it, there is NO way to get back to that message and reply to it or even view it again. That doesn't seem to make any sense to me. I actually love being able to reply right on my watch and not have to walk back over to my phone, or take it out of my bag or whatever. But if Im currently occupied, cant reply at that moment, I am forced to go and get my phone after acknowledging the notification. There needs to be some kind of messaging link so that I can open up the message later. Its very annoying to read the notification, then be busy for 20 minutes, and then when youre ready to reply, you have to go and get your phone to do so. To me, this sort of defeats the purpose of a smartwatch. I want to be able to leave my phone/desk and if Im in another room, be able to stay in that room and address the message regardless. Pretty disappointed that I cant do that.

3) OLED screen burn-in. Disclaimer!!!! This did not happen to me. I dont ever have my always-on display specifically to avoid this problem. However, I have seen and heard it is extremely common in this watch. If you use a 3rd party watchface and activate always-on display, or make the mistake of NOT turning on screen-saver for the built-in faces, your watch is in danger of getting the screen burned in. I highly recommend that NO ONE turn on always-on display, even with 1st party watchfaces. I tried activating the screen-saver in the developer options and for some reason, it would always glitch and never activate. Im so scared of the burn in that I just turned off "always-on" altogether.

4) Finally, and this is just me nitpicking and being critical, lack of "defining features". One of the reasons why Im so close to getting a samsung gear watch (other than it pairing perfectly with my phone and despite the bottleneck app market), is that rotating bezzle. A bunch of other watches have that "1 feature" or "those features" that truly make the watch stand out and a joy to use. This watch is about as basic as it gets. Obviously for this price range you cant ask for too much, and I honestly dont know what feature Id want in this watch for it to bring it to the next level. (BETTER BATTERY LIFE MAYBE???). In all seriousness, the watch is extremely standard. Looks neutral like I previously stated. But the single button and touchscreen just make the most "ehhh" of smartwatches. Nothing memorable to exciting about using this.

BOTTOM LINE:
All these things being said. Depending on your use, I dont know if I could recommend this to anyone. If you just want a smartwatch to be able to see notifications (and be forced to reply immediately or else...) and dont plan on using any fitness features, then maybe this is the watch you want? Although there are seemingly better alternatives in this relative price range I believe. Idk what they are, but if you look hard enough you could find something that better fits your use. For everyone else, I'm sorry. I tried to be open-minded despite the critical reviews. But they were unfortunately right. The battery life downright sucks. And if you need to rely on this watch to last you just ONE day, let alone two, it wont happen. I would be fine with single 24 hour use. Because I could always charge it overnight. But not even a single day on a weekend when I actually use it for 5-6 hours straight? Not okay. Pretty disappointed. Because I got it for such a low comparative price, I may keep it just for weekday use. But Im definitely not gonna count on it for anything other than that.

Review:
I switched to the vivofit from a fitbit flex for a few reasons:
I wouldn't have to charge my vivofit every few days,
I liked the alert to get me off my butt and moving,
I like having easy access to the information I wanted to without having to check my app all of the time.
I liked the way it looked vs the Fitbit Charge

After having the vivofit for a few weeks now.....I really wish I bought the Fitbit Charge instead.

Even though I like the way the vivofit 2 looks better than the fitbit charg, the fitbit app is way easier to use, connect with your friends, and synced way better with my device. Aside with how incredibly user friendly the fitbit app was, the reason why I LOVED the fitbit was I was able to find friends to connect with so easily (no screen names needed). Friends that I didn't even know had a fitbit, I was able to connect with them with a single click. That's a huge problem with Viviofit and the Connect App - you need everyone's screen names in order to connect with anyone.

So if you are using this device for your own personal use and don't care about the friendly competition or leaderboard or being able to cheer each other on to help you stay motivated, than this device would be fine. But I loved the motivation I got with the fitbit, not wanting friends to have more steps than me, keeping me moving etc. Even if this isn't a feature you have used before, trust me, once you have, you will see how beneficial it is! I REALLY hope that Garmin updates their app to be more user friendly and connect better with social sites or I am going to be returning/selling my vivofit 2 and going back to Fitbit pretty soon!

Review:
This replaces a Fitbit Charge HR that failed after 3 days (Fitbit service's response to my e-mail was "we'll get back to you in 2 or more days! After going back and doing further research, I bought the Garmin Vivosmart w/heart monitor and have been extremely happy with it.
It is a great tool for reminding you to "move" if you've been idle for and hr. or more, mates with "MyFitnessPal.com" so you can estimate your calorie intake and approx. what you've burned, against a daily estimated goal. Also tracks your steps and distance, and, based on your previous day's totals, gives you a new goal each day,(instead of the more common 10,000)
Using the heart rate monitor chest strap during my daily exercise, I can get a much better idea of how my heart is doing than I could with the Fitbit. The Charge HR wrist-based heart rate was erratic, at best, and, many times didn't work at all.
Extremely easy to tell it that I am going to sleep, and it records a visual graph of activity vs. inactivity.
It is waterproof to 50 meters (about 150 ft,) so you can shower, swim, etc. without concern.
It is light-weight, great secure strap (with or without the little rubber keeper they provide.) The display responds with 2 taps and scrolls easily in both directions for heart rate (if using the heart monitor strap}, mi. traveled, calories burned, idle time "move bar", steps left and daily step goal (even vibrates and indicates you reached your goal, with a little fireworks display!)
It would be nice if it would just commnicate with my PC without having to attach it to the usb/charge cable, but that is really the only "con". It will talk to smartphones via "smart" bluetooth, which I thought my Samsung android tablet had, but it didn't. Mainly works with the later smartphones, but they provided a list of compatible devices - I just didn't read it closely enough.

Review:
Let me first preface this by saying that I am in no way, shape, or form an athlete. My requirements for my watch were:
- waterproof (needed to be able to safely use it on my paddleboard)
- view notifications from my phone

My last watch was a Fossil smartwatch (Google wear platform) with the traditional touch screen functionality. Battery life was becoming an issue so I wanted to find something new that would work the way I needed it to. This Garmin was one of the few that promised me I wouldn't kill it every time I fell into the lake, and it would even track my swimming (should I get ambitious enough to start doing something like that).

For the first three days of owning the watch, I hated it and was pretty sure I was going to return it. The transition from a touch screen interface to this one was challenging for me (read: I'm old and inflexible) and I missed the customization that I had from my old watch.

However, after a week I realized I loved what this offered that my traditional watches couldn't. Having a week of battery life, pulse and o2 monitoring while I sleep (recently released from my CPAP I like being able to make sure I'm not having issues again), constant heart monitoring (and alerts so my watch can yell at me if I couldn't already tell I was freaking out) and the "body battery" feature are now things that would keep me from switching back to a "traditional" smartwatch.

There are a few things that I wish could be different/better:
- the Garmin Connect app that provides customized watch faces is hard for me to navigate. I'm unwilling to pay, so it's hard to find ones that I like that are free (and will stay free - I've had a few disappear on me because they were only free trials)
- app notifications often appear generic. Admittedly I have way too many apps that send notifications, and while I like that I can block specific apps from notifying on my watch it would be nice to always be able to tell me which app is sending the notification since it's often just the generic message logo and the text without context. Is it my kid's school app sending me a message asking when I'm coming back or Subway?
- tracking my treadmill time (I walk on the treadmill with my nose in a book) doesn't seem to work on the watch. It's probably user error (again, old and not super tech literate) but when I start a treadmill walk activity I expect it could do something more than just keep tabs on my heart rate

Overall, it's a good purchase. And I like that I don't have to worry about destroying yet another touch screen device with my clumsiness :)

Review:
I purchased this a few months ago and waited to write a review to make sure it was still the perfect fitness tracking smart watch. Yes. Yes it is. Before purchasing I spent way too much time comparing and contrasting fitness/smart watches including Fitbit and the Apple Watch. I went with this one and I’m so glad I did. It does everything I was looking for it to do.

I purchased this because I began running when COVID hit and the gyms closed and I was quarantined with my family (love them all dearly, but everyone needs a break). I was using free apps on my phone but I was finding that they were glitchy and not always accurate.

I am in love with this watch and it is exactly what I was looking for. It’s the first thing I put on in the morning before I even get out of bed. Sometimes I even sleep with it to keep track of my sleep. It has a touch screen and can be controlled by rolling along the side as well as a push button that acts as a start/pause/stop for activities. It’s waterproof so I can wear it in the shower and swimming. I will say that because it’s a touch screen, the water seems to activate the touch so it will bring up different screens. I haven’t researched whether there is swim mode that prevents because it’s not a huge deal for me as I’ll just take it off if I’m in the pool.

I love the app. It’s keeping track of all of my activities and it’s very easy to use. The watch is synced to my phone so while I can’t respond, I get vibrating notifications about texts, emails, phone calls etc.

I find that the heart rate monitor seems fairly accurate for a wrist monitor. The watch itself has been very durable. I wear it constantly and it still looks brand new after a few months. The battery life is pretty long and it charges quickly.

I really can’t say enough good things about this. It is perfect for what I need it for and was priced just right. A friend of mine but the Apple Watch because she liked the look of it better but she sees me wearing mine and says she wishes she got this one instead. I’d highly recommend.

Review:
I've had this watch now for about 4 months. It has become such a big part of my daily routine that I feel naked without it!

Background: I use this watch daily to track my steps. I also work in a busy healthcare setting where I'm a bit paranoid about touching my phone (and dragging all those nasty things I touch across my face), and we are also not allowed to check our phones on the floor. So having both a functional watch and notification filter helps me prioritize when it might be necessary to take a quick break to answer an important call.

Functionality: I was one of the original backers of the OG Pebble on Kickstarter, and loved the idea. The watch has come a long way--the color screen, the sturdier build, and sleeker designs are all great improvements in my book. The userbase has exponentially increased too, so there are a lot more watchfaces and apps coming out on the Pebble Store. My top 3 used apps: Timer (perfect for a quick snooze), Pandora (lets me change volumes and like/dislike), and Health (built-in Pebble app that tracks steps and gives you a breakdown). It's simple and does what I need it to do without all the expensive fluff. I'm lookin' at you, Apple.

Durability: I attached a military-grade NATO strap to my Pebble, and have taken this thing EVERYWHERE. It stands up to constant alcohol-disinfecting (wiping, not dunking in the stuff), hand-washing every 15 minutes, and on my hikes and trips to the beach. It's not an invincible watch--if you get sand in it or swim in salt water I don't know how well it'll survive. Use common sense and be gentle with it when you can, and it'll go miles.

Pros:
+ Well-priced compared to the competitors. I got it for $160 during a sale.
+ Simple yet elegant. I don't need bloat or expensive add-ons.
+ Sturdy steel frame with steel buttons
+ Glass is very durable--there have been many times where I thought I had scratched it
+ Plays well with Android OR iPhone
+ Has a microphone when you're too lazy to type out a message. Voice recognition isn't perfect but good enough for quick messages.

Cons:
+ Bluetooth needs to be on all the time to talk to your phone, and the app needs to be on in the background. Keep a spare charger handy if you're using it a lot
+ I'm honestly trying to find something here. Can't think of anything.

Review:
Right up front I need to say that I've been a Garmin fan since forever. I have had over the years at least three dozen of the company's products in all different categories. And until this VivoActive 3 have loved them all.

I guess I'm not a touchscreen fan. I guess there are those who are, but I'm not. It's balky, annoying, and a general pain.

But what really irks me is the battery life. It's horrible. Any other Garmin device (to includes various watches like several in the Forerunner series and several other in the Fenix series have marvelous battery life. This VivoActive 3 doesn't even have average/decent battery life. We have to charge it every other day when it isn't even being put under any real load. Maybe we got a defective one...?

Sensors are okay. Operation is fairly straight-forward once one gets the hang of a single-button, touchscreen watch.

We paid over $240 for this back in 2019. It now runs a bit over $160. What I paid was awful, what it is going for now still leans toward awful. Our experience with this watch puts it more in the $90-100 category.

We would recommend (and have recommended) every other Garmin product we've owned and used. Not this one. Not even close. Balky touchscreen and awful battery endurance coupled with a still unreasonable price tag makes this a product we wish we would have passed on.

2 stars.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
Have been using the watch for a week now, found that the Chicago Garmin store had a few in stock (they always sell out when they get them, so call ahead).

Have owned the 305, 310, and now the 910. I used the 310 for triathlons, actually found the 310 was pretty good with the latest update for mapping a swim. That said, the 910 rocks! Here's what I've found useful so far with it:

* Size is not just significantly smaller than the 305 and 310, it really has some measure of style as well. Also, it just feels like a solid watch and I think the black looks excellent.

* The watch band is much easier to use than the 310. There are a larger number of slots to use to fasten the band, so it feels like it fits better to me. They also provide a watchband extension if you need to wear it over some sort of bulky garment.

* Can't tell on battery life yet, but over the week of regular workouts as well as leaving it on overnight only depleted 23% of the battery.

* For swimming, the watch is amazing! It counts laps/intervals/strokes/distance per stroke/SWOLF score/efficiency (normalized value to make various types of swim workouts comparable)/stroke type with what for me has been complete accuracy. I had the Finis Swimsense watch and ended up returning it due to fogging and hard to read screen. The Garmin screen is easy to read, either out of or in the water. For swimming, it has both a lap pool and open water setting.

* It as a barometric altimeter built-in, so does elevation correction as well.

* A number of other software enhancements are nice: Virtual racer/training effect/run-walk setting.

* I use a Garmin Footpod and Speed/Cadence sensor as well.

* Immediately paired up with the Ant receiver I already had plugged into my computer, simple to set that up.

I'm quite pleased with the watch and so far it looks like they've done a very good job with the initial version of software. I was impressed by the software updates they did with the 310, so be interested to see what they do with the 910.

Excellent watch, highly recommended.

Tom

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The smart watch needs to maintain a long battery life over a week of regular workouts and overnight wear, with minimal depletion.

Special Features: Time Display, Sleep Monitor, Phone Call, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I'll try and stick to the points of information that I wish others would have laid out properly in a review. I'm an iPhone owner, and had the original Apple Watch until it ended up coming apart. I decided to dabble into the cheaper option and see how far the industry has come along. Let's just say, the user interface of the watch itself works alright but it's a bit glitchy (as with ANYTHING Android based) and isn't very quick to respond when you tilt your wrist and expect the watch to illuminate. The design and feel of the watch is well laid out and gets an A-. The battery life seems to last about a day, sometimes less if you're one of those Fitness bums (if you are, get a FitBit, don't waste your time with this one).

However, I will get to the biggest, most annoying and frustrating part of the watch and that is the garbage wrist band. It gets caught on literally everything you touch; putting on clothes, a coat, moving in general, it comes undone from it's slots 200X a day. I've included a photo of what I mean. Apple Watches are not cheap by any means of the word, but they've completely owned the usability and wrist bands of the smartwatch industry. The TicWatch isn't even on the same planet. Review:
When Pebble pulled the plug and was acquired by FitBit, I had the Pebble Time 2 on order in KS, but never got it and got my funds back instead of the watch.. I'd have preferred the watch, but that's another story..
Since I already had funded on Kickstarter all the models, but had gifted my Pebble Time Steel to my Dad, I decided to purchase this one on Amazon as my backup watch, in case my reliable Pebble Steel & Pebble 2 would die.. Glad I did, as both my original Pebbles gave up their spirit with a dead battery.. Apparently you can swap in on the Steel, but the P2 is more tricky as the casing is sealed for waterproofing.. which isn't really 100% waterproof, but that's another story.
The PTS has an amazing battery life and I achieved to get 25 days running on it, but with the watch in airplane mode, just using a simple watchface.. Of course this is useless in the day-to-day life, as the purpose of the watch is to provide you with notifications from your smartphone without having to pull it out all the time..
On average in real-life usage, expect to get 7-10 days out of the battery, and I'm still amazed after a full week of use that it shows 30% of battery left and 3 days to go.. I use the watch app Battery+ to track my charging cycle and the discharge of the battery.
After the Pebble online servers were shutdown by FitBit on June 30th, 2018, my Pebble watches were mostly still working, but you couldn't change anything in the settings of the watch-faces or apps as there was simply no way to load them anymore on the phone.. Fortunately a short query on the net returned me with some infos that a group of hackers/freaks/fans got to backup most of the FitBit/Pebble server content and run it under a new name 'Rebble' to allow for Pebble owners all over the world to be able to synchronize their apps again on the smartphone. The switch was very easy and I'm happy again to have a fully working Pebble smartphone app (both iOS & Android working).

Review:
First off, you should be leery of any company that offers free stuff in exchange for 5-star review as is the case here. I'm not taking the bait. Instead, here is an honest review of this watch which I received as a Christmas gift (the reason I'm not a verified buyer).

Basics: Like all decent watches it tells time. The calendar is nice though it is unable to sync to your smartphone events. Battery life for the limited time I had it seemed quite good - it could make it nearly two days with pretty regular use before it needed to be recharged. It includes a leather and rubber band which I found easy to swap out.

Setup: Syncing to my iPhone was relatively simple after downloading the required app. I read the entire manual. It offers some basic startup guidance.

Multiple Display options are appreciated and they are visually pretty neat.

Features I tested over 2 weeks:
1. Health: BP is a complete joke, though I'm not sure anyone expects this to work. I sure hope not. I'm borderline hypertensive and was getting readings <100/50 mmHg. HR seems okay at times but also wildly inaccurate at other times - (e.g., 90 bpm when a manual check was over 120 bpm during exercise). Did not try the pedometer.

2. Sport Mode: One of the reasons I wanted this was to replace my trusty old Garmin with something that could both track my run and allow me the ability to change music on the fly. Short story - on a run today I know to be exactly 8 miles I wore both watches - the Garmin came in at 8.03 miles whereas this registered at 8.3 miles. 0.3 miles is outside standard deviation and just too inaccurate for me to rely upon. Also, it counts up from 1 during the run but didn't seem to match my mile markers (e.g., flashed "11" at 6.21 miles). This just served as an annoyance. I don't understand what this was about.

3. Notifications: here is an area I was actually fairly pleased. I would occasionally need to power cycle the watch for it to start receiving push notifications (even though connected via Bluetooth) but for the most part, any calls and push notifications would show on the phone as expected.

4. Music Control: Again, this worked pretty well. Volume Up/Down and Forward/Backward worked great. Only the song title displays and is limited to about 14 characters. If it's any longer, it will not scroll to show the entire name, nor is the artist shown. Not a huge deal but worth mentioning.

5. Find Phone: I work in IT and spent a few hours trying to get this to work with my iPhone. It doesn't.

6. Remote Capture: works but only using the Smart Watch app. It would be useful to have a countdown before the picture is snapped. As is, if you're in the picture, it will be of you touching your watch to trigger the photo.

7. Weather: So far, in two weeks I've seen exactly TWO temperatures displayed - 17°F and 21°F. I'm no meteorologist but believe our temperatures fluctuate a great deal more than that. Right now it registers 21°F but happens to be 40°F. This is one of my bigger disappointments.

8. Compass: I didn't spend much time with this but it didn't seem nearly as accurate as my phone. It sort bounced around and seemed to have trouble locking in on an orientation with me standing completely still.

9. It vibrates at random times for no apparent reason (at least not that I've been able to figure out). Customization, aside from the various "themes", is pretty limited.

Overall, I kept making excuses as to why this watch was worth keeping but have simply run out of reasons. For something that claims to be a "Smart" device, it is far from it. It seems like a product that was in beta testing and released without being fully functional or accurate. Sure, the price is remarkable for the supposed features it boasts but if only a small fraction actually work as designed, you'll have to make up your own mind about whether that equates to a good value.

Review:
I have owned this watch since September 2021 and it has been nothing but trouble. I got it because I do a lot of hiking in Alaska and was impressed with the reviews about its GPS function.

Lately, the trouble has been that I can't trust the battery level indicator. Four times it has said battery level in the mid-range, I go for a 3 to 5 mile hike and it freezes before even a mile in and I find out later that the battery quit.

It often randomly pauses or goes to another menu option - for a while I thought that I was accidentally pushing a button when I flexed my wrist, but this has happened with controls on the side of the watch opposite my hand, so it seems like it just decides a button needs to be activated every so often.

The elevation gain is highly inaccurate at times - see attached photo. Apparently I was hiking Denali here in Alaska that day (sarcasm) - the real elevation gain on that hike was about 1000 feet.

The heart rate monitor is always 10 to 20 beats per minute low. Last week my heart was pounding and it said I was at 69 bpm. When I counted, it was over 130.

I tried using it when I lifted weights and that was really inaccurate as well and not in an intuitive way - for example, push ups would be fairly accurate (I don't fuss unless it's more than + or - 2 off), but one leg dips would be WAY off. No arm movement at all with push ups, but up and down with dips - so why more accurate with push ups? I was spending as much or more time editing the reps as I was working out, so I quit using it for that.

Also, the controls are not intuitive and if you push the wrong one, there is no way to back out of it or reset without shutting it down. If you push, say a navigation or waypoint button accidentally, you have to go through with it and even then it won't take you back to where you want to be. There are many hikes I just gave up recording because that happened.

Before the Instinct, I used a Vivoactive 3 and it was even worse. I don't have a basis for comparison because I have only ever owned anything but Garmin watches, but it is time to move on to another brand.

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs the battery level indicator to be reliable. The customer needs the battery to last through hikes.

Review:
I'm a tech-geek who has been watching the smart-watch segment for years.I travel a lot so battery life is crucial for me. I am also an avid runner, hiker, and cyclist who likes to track health.

Quick note: Heart Rate monitor NOT built into watch. Its a band that goes around your upper waist and connects wirelessly to the watch.If I had known this before purchasing, I would have saved myself $50 and went without it.

Setup: EASY! Within 5 minutes I had my Vivoactive synced up to the Garmin Connect App on my Galaxy S4 Android phone. By default, all Apps (Facebook, text messages, calendar reminders, work email, Gmail) relay the messages to the watch. Range is also good. I live in a split level home and it doesn't loose connection unless I am outside (50 feet away). It gives me a gentle "buzz" and message saying that its no longer connected to my phone.

Everyday use: I love the fact that everytime I get a notification that I can just look at my wrist rather than picking up my phone. If its important, I'll pick up my phone to respond but for everything else, I just carry on. While driving, typing at work, carrying groceries, or sitting in a meeting, it has made it much more convenient to keep up with my digital life. I also tested its water resistance by taking a shower with it this morning without any problems.

Battery/Screen brightness: Its been 24 hours and I still have 89% of my battery life left. That includes me configuring it, learning all the screens/options/settings, and showing it off. I am hoping for 1 week+ of battery life. Part of that I think is due to the screen. At first it seemed dim but that's because I am used to a phone or laptop screen which this watch is not designed to work like. As long as there is some light in the room I can see the screen just fine. If I can't see it then I just hit the backlight button. No its not cell phone bright but it doesn't need to be. If you have used a digital watch with a backlight this is what you will expect. If I had to choose great battery life, or a bright screen, I'd choose battery life every time.

Comfort/Style: It's light and very comfortable. While running I built up some sweat under the band but nothing unusual compared to other watches. Size is on par with your average digital watch and looks very normal and stylish. I have normal size wrists for a guy and it doesn't look bulky. It also doesn't stand out as most people don't notice unless I point it out.It doesn't have any sharp points or angles so I have had no problems with clothing getting snagged.

Comparison with Fitbit One: I used my Fitbit for step counting and sleep monitoring. I used both of them together for the first 24 hours and the results were very similar which I didn't expect since the Fitbit is warn at my hip and watch is obviously on the wrist. Both were identical for sleep monitoring and the Vivoactive was only 75 steps higher than the Fitbit at the 10,000 step mark. I talk with my hands so I figured these numbers might be way off but that wasn't the case.

Pro's
* Lightweight
*Good size. Not big and bulky but big enough to read everything without needing to pull my wrist to my face
* Comfortable
*Stylish/Doesn't stand out. I work in a professional environment (Suit) so I am happy that its not an eye sore.
*Battery life. 89% battery life left after 24 hours
* Easy to setup, configure, and pair with my phone and the Garmin Connect App
*Integration with MyFitnessPal
*Find my phone works. Already used it once.
*Waterproof. Worked fine in the shower
*Screen sits beveled under the rim of the watch. Hopefully will save me from a few scratches.

Con's:
*Screen isn't super bright, but not bad by any means
*Heart Rate monitor isn't built into the watch. Its a separate band that goes around your waist
*Wish there was a "Steel" band available like the Pebble Steel. If one comes out that will by my first accessory
*No easy way to disable alerts while sleeping. I started getting junk emails at 3am so my watch buzzed several times waking me up. Only way I found to disable this was to turn off vibration or disable bluetooth.

Review:
I'm a first time smartwatch user. I got this one because I mainly wanted a fitness tracker but was a better smartwatch than fitbit. Let's start working outside in:
Looks:
I think its a great looking watch. I was a bit worried about getting a fitness focused watch that was stainless steel versus the s2 design that was more rubber like. With a different watch band you could use this in just about any situation as you can turn the display off or put a less athletic watch face on to fit the scenario. I don't have big wrists for a guy, about 7 inches around at the thinnest part and it fits my wrist well and I don't notice the weight. Mostly just focus on not bumping it on things. I don't believe you can get much thinner than this for a sport smartwatch which is a big reason i went with this over a LG watch sport. I never got to see the blue version in person but heard it was an understated blue which could've been nice, but I went with black just so it could be more versatile with clothing. The finish is great, more of a chrome smoke than dark black.
Fitness Tracking:
The fitness tracking is pretty good with the S health app. I live far north so right now most of my cardio work is indoors so the GPS functionality hasn't come into play yet. One of the only if not the only where you can add meal calories from the watch into your tracker. Being as it has all the hardware a health tracker needs(unless you're hardcore cardio and want third party stuff like chest HR sensors which the watch doesn't support) and right now the only thing limiting it is software, it can only improve with time.
Functionality:
The bezel is amazing and makes navigation easy. The software is pretty intuitive so customizing the widgets and apps is easy. Notifications are OK. It can handle multiple text messages from different people well, but multiple from one person only get displayed as the most recent which is annoying. Single ones from multiple people get separated nicely and are easy to navigate.
responding to texts can be a pain. If you need a short response then drawing on the screen can get the job done pretty well with occasional weird capitalizations and things. The S voice on the phone is pretty craptastic. On my pixel if I talk casually it'll sometimes make mistakes but if I enunciate and focus it'll be perfect just about every time. With the watch, if I put in as much effort as I can to talk clearly it'll still be a struggle to get a text right.

I give it 4/5 for now because it is built with quality, display is beautiful, easy to navigate, software is pretty intuitive, standard 20mm bands make it easy to change the look and decent functionality
downsides are the notification management needs improvement and responding can be a pain because of poor voice recognition, which should be the bread and butter of a smart watch and done with ease.
