Review:
I purchased the unit in time for a 2000 mile motorcycle ride through Colorado, Utah and Arizona. The trip was an excellent opportunity use the Monterra as intended. My biggest mistake on this trip was leaving my Nuvi unit at home, trapping me with Monterra as my primary unit...
This is Garmin's first Android venture and it shows. Where my Nuvi feels like an integrated device. The Monterra is a collection of apps that are not glued together very well. The radio failed within a week and only would create a loud squealing sound, both the FM and Weather. I bought and loaded the North American Street App which would make the unit navigate somewhat like a Nuvi but just not as well; frequent crashes and freezes. Even in this mode the Monterra calculated straight line distance, and then when you hit go, the only way to know the true distance was to use the Nuvi option on the map display to show distance in the lower left corner. Example: from just outside Page Arizona, the unit would report distance to Grand Canyon North Rim campground as 45 miles. After hitting go, it would display the by road distance of 160 miles. If you deal with double data enough times and sooner or later it will get you. I could not reach the campsite at grand canyon for that night thinking I had plenty of time to explore Glen Canyon Dam. It was a constant hassle to not get tricked by the distance calculation, apps crashing, and general disconnected non-intuitive feel of the unit. The Monterra was a bad experience, opposite from my Nuvi 765T(automotive GPS), which I totally abused taking on multiple cross country motorcycle rides, on and off road. Easy to use and I'm amazed it still works! In contrast, the Monterra is a frustrating experience with many nuances that make you reluctant to trust it. I returned the unit.
In Summary:
+++ Display was nice
+++ mount was same as Montana unit and nice
----- Outdoor App (mapping app) crashed often or did not respond until reboot (Failure of this core app is the most important point)
----- unit would freeze, which was had to detect in bright sunlight and could cause you to miss a turn.
-- Recently found destination list always came up empty, separate app that never seemed to have the data.
---- GPS features/functions is a collection of poorly connected Android Apps.
+ wifi and camera seemed to work fine but did not really matter since nothing else worked well.
---- Primary function as a GPS was inadequate and even more disappointing given the price.
++ Accuracy of GPS unit itself was very good.
- battery life minimal
+ Once booted(long time), quick to lock on satellites.
- unit is heavy, hard to imagine a hiker wanting it.
+ topo display feature was nice
+ worked ok with BaseCamp for uploading or downloading tracks and routes
- difficulty connecting to Garmin online for updates, while Nuvi connected just fine, Monterra would come up as unrecognized device.
- paid extra for north america maps, virtually worthless with out these maps/routing capability...really, really! extra on a 799 unit? Who's marketing idea was that?

FOOTNOTE 1: I did not use the extra feature to much extent, did not want to risk crashing the unit. (Camera, WIFI, Weather Data)

FOOTNOTE 2: Garmin needs to develop the feature to allow you to pick up a Route or tracks from the nearest point to you. In both cases the GPS would try to route me to the start of a route even if I was closer to the halfway point. If that feature exists, it was well hidden.

FOOTNOTE 3: If you feel compelled to buy the Monterra, be sure use a store that will allow you to return the unit without penalty.

Bottom line: The Monterra is not ready for prime time and one has to wonder about the culture of the Garmin corporation to market an incomplete product, especially at the dollar value. Garmin is huge but the quality of their software is making them vulnerable to competition. In the case of the Monterra, it is a bunch of disconnected feature crammed into a unit with the kind of usability you would expect from 10 separate corporate high performance work teams. (Watch the online video "7 red lines", you'll get what I mean).

I want to try the Garmin 590LM next but am reluctant given $799 price tag, my Monterra experience, and what other users are writing about the 590LM product quality.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
So, I will preface this with the fact that this is my first smartwatch, and first real experience with Wear OS from Android. I got this as my job has me texting with other coworkers a fair amount, and I wanted a way to check my notifications and respond without always pulling out my phone.

Set up: Ok, so this should have been a LOT easier to be honest. I had to do this twice (got a new phone about a week after I got the watch) and it took a long time each time for the phone to see the watch to complete setup (yes, Bluetooth was on and discoverable on both). I had to do a hard reset of the watch to get it to pair with my new phone. Once the Wear OS app could see the watch setup was easy and worked well.

Use: The watch will work just like any other Wear OS watch from Android. It did update to a newer version of the OS shortly after initial setup which made swiping from each direction have a new function. The responsiveness to taps is good, and I see little lag. The apps do take a bit of time to load up, and the screen often times out before it is fully loaded (I wish the screen timeout interval was adjustable). Otherwise it works well. I get quick notifications of texts and emails coming in, which is what I really need the watch for. I have found that the Google Assistant functions are really helpful, and voice recognition excellent. Responding to a text while I walk down the hall and talk to my watch (despite sounding dorky) is very helpful for me and efficient. The voice recognition is certainly faster than trying to tap out a response slowly on the tiny on screen keyboard. My favorite moment so far was while mowing my lawn, I was using my phone to stream music from Google Play Music to my bluetooth headphones, and then being able to change the volume or track from my watch.

Specifics to the TicWatch Pro:
- The dual layer screen is the reason I got this watch and it works as advertised. I use the LCD screen most of the time to see the current time. My main complaint is the LCD screen does need a fairly good source of ambient light to read clearly, but the time area is quite large and easily read.
- Size: I was concerned at first that the watch would be too large on my narrower to average sized wrist. After wearing the TicWatch for the last 3 weeks or so, I don't notice an issue with it. The watch is a bit thicker than I was expecting, though I don't really notice this much (though it does get stuck on the cuffs of my dress shirts). The overall size does have a nice solid feel to it that I really like.
- Straps: Nothing fancy. A leather-like outer with a rubbery inner lining. I hope it holds up ok. It does have the quick release function, if you want to change out straps.
- Screen: sharp, bright enough to see in all but the brightest sunlight. No complaints.
- Durability: so far, so good. I like the fact that the bezel is raised slightly over the screen and should help protect it from scratches.

Battery Life: This is the big part of choosing this watch over any other WearOS watch. Obviously, depending on your settings, this will vary. I chose to turn off the "Tilt-to-wake" gesture (which keeps the screen off more of the time), and do not have the screen set to always on either. It is now 10pm, and I took the watch off the charger at 6am (so 16 hours), and I have 77% remaining. I have been charging the watch only on the night of the 3rd day, and have had about a 15% cushion remaining. This has been a great aspect of the watch. The charger is magnetic and there is never any struggle getting the watch to sit correctly in order to charge. My only complaint is that the cord is just a bit too short, and it doesn't come with it's own power adapter (though does work with any USB-A charger you have lying around).

Value: I got this TicWatch Pro as a gift to myself after seeing a *great* price on it on Thanksgiving. At the price I got it for, it is a fantastic deal. At full retail, its an OK price point, but it remains much more affordable than an Apple product or other Wear OS competitors.

Final thoughts: Great device so far. I think the hardware is solid and I have really enjoyed the ability to leave my phone in my pocket or on my desk, but remain able to check notifications. Hopefully it holds up for a long time to come.

Review: I received this as an early Christmas gift from Amazon and am extremely pleased with it. I found it paired easily with my Galaxy S4. I just downloaded the Gear Fit Manager app (in Google Play Store) and it set up easily. Some people have commented that it is confusing to set up and use but I found it very easy (I'm used to the Android operating system). I personally like all the options for set up and they seem intuitive to me. The Gear Fit Manager allows me to add additional apps under "Notifications" so I can get their alerts in addition to the standard ones that come with the Gear for phone, texts, email, alarms, calendar appointments.  For instance, I use K9 Mail for my business emails and was able to add that app so that I get separate alerts for my business emails.  I can then easily see who is trying to reach me without digging out my phone from my pocket or handbag.  I know some people have not been happy with the pedometer function (saying that it is inaccurate in tracking total steps) but I already use the Endomondo app for my exercise program (it uses GPS to measure your distance) and it works well in conjunction with the Gear by allowing me to stop and start my work out from the wristband and accurately measure my distances and speeds.  At the same time I can start and stop my audiobook (Audible) or music using the Media controller option.  The heart rate option is nice to check to see how you're doing during your workout.  I even like being able to acknowledge text messages with some canned responses such as "OK", "Call you later", etc. You can change these options on the Gear Fit Manager to have more response options.  I also use the Sleep mode nightly to measure how much I "toss and turn" letting me know the time and approximate quality of my sleep. Battery life is about 2 days but it recharges quickly.

Review:
I have been very happy with TicWatch models, and the E3 is no exception. It has a great battery and is very responsive to taps and button pushes. I like that it runs Google's Wear OS, which gives a larger choice of watch faces and other apps for the watch than I would expect from a company-specific OS. When I used the always-on display (and light daily use) for a full day (for me, about 16 hours) the battery was still at about 50%. It was still at about 70% after a full day with the always-on display turned off. And it only dropped a few % when I left it for 24 hours in essential mode (with no other use).

Compared to the Pro 4G (which is an older model than the Pro 3), my previous watch, the E3 is smaller and lighter. It does not have the bezel with watch numbers that the 4G has, making it feel smoother to do gestures starting at the edge of the screen, and reducing the size. The E3 does have an "essential" mode, but this is different from the 4G's "essential" mode. The 4G has an additional LCD screen which constantly shows the time in essential mode, which the E3 does not have. Instead, in essential mode, the E3 will turn off the display (and many other functions) to save battery, but the screen will turn on when you tilt your wrist to see the time. The display that is shown is not your selected watch face, but (I assume) TicWatch's built-in face. When not in essential mode, if the always-on display is turned off, the watch will always display a face when you tilt it toward you, but if you've turned off the "Tilt to wake screen" option then the face is the built-in face, and if you have the tilt option turned on, then the face is your selected face. Tapping the face will also turn on the display with your selected face (but not in essential mode). I will say the battery on the 4G seemed better, in that with the always-on display turned on, the 4G is usually at 70% charge at the end of the day, where the E3 was around 50%, with similar usage. But the 4G is a bit bigger and heavier, so that may be the battery. It may also be due to the E3 having more sensors in use.

Review: I was debating between the vivofit 2 and a Fitbit Flex -I read a ton of reviews on both which gave me pretty good ins and outs of what the trackers do, pros and cons of each, and which will best fit my needs. I love the Garmin vivofit 2 tracker!!  As a first time tracker, the vivofit 2 fits the bill perfectly! It cost a little less than the Fitbit, IS waterproof (can wear in shower, pool, ocean), tracks your sleep, counts steps, calories burned, has a light, comes with 2 size wristbands, a USB port and is a watch too. I also like that you don't have to charge it every night; the battery life says it will last for a year and once the battery dies, you go buy a normal watch battery and have a jeweler install it like any other watch. I couldn't get the Garmin Connect app on my iPhone (thanks to updating on my software - its a hand-me-down phone), so I put it on my tablet at home - it was so easy to set up the account, not to mention using the app, plus I don't want to obsess at work about what my stats look like. You have to sync the information yourself manually (it does not do that automatically, but it doesn't take long!) but if you forget to do it daily, it saves your information from previous days until you sync the next time. I don't mind having to sync my vivofit manually, but the fact that you can look back at previous days information is great. Now, I don't know how 100% accurately the vivofit 2 tracks everything (steps, sleep, calories) but I've been really impressed with the whole concept of it. Looking at the sleep pattern charts in the app has been cool - it shows how many hours you're in deep sleep, light sleep and awake, and the chart has different colors for each category of sleep. Again, I'm not sure how accurate it is, because when you set up your account, you put in what time you go to bed and what time you wake up on average and it will base your tracking on that. As for the step counting, the vivofit 2 will set your daily goal based on your average for the week, so your step goal is never the same. Now, I don't care if its not 100% accurate (what tracker is?) - I feel like I'm getting my needs fulfilled with my purchase more than I expected. I highly recommend as a first time tracker for someone who doesn't want one that's crazy high tech, and easy to use AND its affordable.

Review:
I owned the TicWris Max for over a month before I sold it. I then bought the Max S. This review will cover the features common to both models, as well as the differences.

Both watches are unique in that they run under the Android operating system. In addition to the smart watch type of interface both models have, you can also install apps from the Google Play Store that allow you to change the look and feel. Personally, I use Square Home launcher.

Here is a summary of the pros and cons of each watch:

Pros:

1. Feels comfortable on my wrist in spite of its size. Able to wear all day. The Max S, being slightly smaller, is a bit more comfortable.
2. Able to run many applications from the Google Play Store. Most importantly is running a launcher that can completely change the device's interface.
3. Good battery life - about 2 days with normal use for the Max. About 1 day for the Max S. Both charge in about 2 hours.
4. Using a sim card (I am running under T-Mobile) it is able to make calls, texts and all other things a cell phone can do.
5. Has WiFi, Bluetooth and USB connectivity.
6. By wearing on your wrist as a cell phone, it frees up both hands and eliminates the need to carry a large cell phone.

Cons:

1. The small screen (compared to a cell phone) makes it difficult to see many apps. It also requires apps to run in landscape mode. It takes a fair amount of time finding apps on Play Store that run in landscape and that also allow the size of the font to be enlarged. It should be noted that the smaller screen size of the Max S makes it very difficult to read some apps.
2. Smart watch fitness functions are limited to steps and heart rate. The device cannot read blood pressure, oxygen level and temperature.
3. The standard interface that comes with the device is that of a smart watch - the problem is that applications installed on the watch show up as a lot of icons not sorted in any particular order. This is why getting a launcher from Play Store is important.
4. A very large number of free watch faces are available when running under the smart watch interface.
5. Screen brightness could be better. Not a problem indoors, but outdoors is another matter.
6. While it can pair with your cell phone using bluetooth, you cannot make calls or texts. You have to have a sim card to do that.

These devices are not for everyone. They would not work well for a heavy cell phone user. For me, however, it is perfect. The occasional text, email and phone calls I make and receive make this practical with the advantage that my phone is always with me.

The photos attached show different views of the Max. The first 3 are from the standard interface. The second 3 are using the Square Home launcher. Note how you can display calendar and weather widgets.

As I mentioned at the beginning, I had the Max, then sold it to get the Max S. After a few days of using the Max S, I am returning it and buying another Max. The screen on the Max S is just too small for my eyes. I also miss the longer battery life of the Max. The Max S does come with an additional camera which the Max lacks, and there are some additional watch faces lacking in the Max.

Overall, both models are good choices if you want a smart watch that offers more customization options than anything else on the market.

Review:
I admit it- I use a smartwatch primarily as a watch and a repository of notifications. I owned a Moto 360 for two years. This watch blows that one away! Here's what I love:
>The screen: This is the most vivid and sharp screen on any device I own (laptop, tablet, phone). Even the finest print is razor sharp and colors pop like crazy

>The watch faces. Actually, the stock digital face with hrs/mins/secs plus battery life, date, steps and temperature is perfect for my needs as a radio operations manager. But the myriad others available are terrific also.

> The Notifications: Vibration is STRONG! Can't be missed. Then the yellow dot on the watch face remains to remind you that you have a notification, in case you don't immediately address it.

> the UI: Between the rotating bezel and very touch-sensitive swiping capabilities, it is supremely easy to get to where you want to go and do what you want to do

> Battery life: my Moto barely lasted through the work day and into the evening. I am easily getting three full days out of the Gear Sport.

>Waterproof: A huge plus. Great to be able to wear throughout the day, and especially while swimming (see below)

> The SPORTS part: Oh, yeah, there's that. I work out a lot, walk a lot and swim laps three days a week. This watch accurately tracks my workouts and is especially cool with tracking swimming.

> Comfort: Surprisingly light! And the silicone strap's adjustment holes are spaced close enough together to provide a perfect fit.

PS. I am using the watch with an LG V20 phone and it works perfectly well, syncing all notifications, including my urgently important calendar events seamlessly.

Review:
I recommend the Indekos Smart Watch. The price is less than a watch band for the main brand name Smart Watches made by Samsung and Apple. The indekos gives you live read outs of your personal bio fitness information (heart rate, blood pressure, etc.) which is nice to give you a general idea of your health. Also being able to answer and make a phone call without fumbling with your phone is a nice feature. I am a bit older and I will need to investigate a lot of the functionality available to determine if I will even use them.

The watch band is a bit odd in appearance, it looks like small open tubes laid side by side, but it is surprisingly comfortable and adjustable to fit as my wrists swell and reduce throughout the day. The application that the Indekos Smart Watch utilizes found in the Apple App Store or Google Store is a weak point for the Smart Watch. I was going to give 4 Star rating because of this application but decided it wouldn't be fair to reduce the 5 Star rating of the Watch due to the application. The DaFit app only allows for one device to be added to the program, so if you have more than one Smart Watch you have to remove and add them as you want to wear a different one.

The DaFit application does have a large gallery of watch faces to choose from and settings to control things like Notifications. If the developers work to improve their application then this would be perfect.

The Indekos Smart Watch uses a magnetic attachment for charging and a 2 hour charge to full capacity gave me a full 7 days use before needing a recharge. I know this will vary depending on the functionality you use.

I am extremely satisfied with the Indekos Smart Watch. The display is large enough for my poor vision and the touch screen is very responsive to my touch. I believe you will enjoy this Quality Smart Watch also.

Review:
This is an amazing running watch. Possibly the best yet! I wanted a GPS running watch that I could listen to music on my runs. This watch is amazing for music! I use this watch with Black Pods 2. A $60 rip-off black pair of apple air-pods. Only once in the 3 months I've used this, has there been one, *note ONE drop out. That's it. When comparing this watch to the Forerunner 945, I find this one better for the motivated runner. The 945 is more of an Olympian or very very serious athlete watch, and this has all the features I want. Speaking of features, they are endless. All day heart rate, plus ox sensor, body battery, all-day stress, all-day respiration, hydration tracking are to name a few.
Now to the bad...Be careful when buying this from Amazon. I bought one in December, and didn't find out until this month that Amazon had sent me the South East Asian version of this watch?!? I didn't even know that existed!! Anyways, it was way past the return date, but Amazon graciously refunded me my money...minus a re-stocking fee!?! I contacted them about that and they graciously refunded me the extra money as well. Thank you Amazon!!

Review:
The DG Ares Smart Watch is between 4 to 5 stars for me. I have had a few smartwatches. All within the same price range throughout the years. I have never had a Apple Watch. I can compare it to FitBit Charge 2. Prior to this watch, I was using another Chinese Budget Smartwatch - which uses the app VeryFitPro. This watch uses GloryFit.

Overall, these watches depend heavily on apps to have a good user experience. The watch itself has basic programming that can help with daily task.

The watch has a large face. I'm not entirely sold on this hybrid circular/square shape. I would have much preferred a round watch. The display itself is round. I can deduce that the watch needs the extra bezels to house hardware. With the thick bezels, it does feel large on the wrist. It's also thick - not very flattering on smaller wrist.

The smartwatch has evolved over the years. These budget watches are never going to be as good as an Apple Watch, but you're also not paying 5-6 times that amount. They perform basic functions such as record steps, tell time, heart rate, oxygen levels and sleep. As it connects to your phone, it shares weather information. It does not tell the temperature in real time.

The battery last pretty long time. I get a large amount of notifications via Instagram/emails so it drains the battery. Unread emails/notifcation prompt reminders.

Once I got in the habit of using the watch, I found it useful.

Here are the things I dislike about it.
- instructions were super generic and not specific. I tried to email support within the app and I did not get help. I did eventually figure it out with the QR codes. The instructions are through the web pages via QR codes on the manual.
- The sleep tracker doesn't work very well - especially if you move a lot in your sleep.
- The step tracker works, but the software is a little weird. I run 6+ miles. It would track the steps on the watch. The software would automatically interpret those as runs and subtract those steps. It also wouldn't fully categorize those steps as "workout" on their workout section. The app does not automatically GPS those runs. I have not tried to record manually, as I use Strava, another app.
- I use a Samsung phone. During set up, there's a lot permissions that I have to "allow" in order for the notifications to come through. I know this is a struggle for the software developer but it doesn't help with the user experience - especially if you're not aware of these settings.
-The crown of the watch is just a button and turning it does not have any function - which I think is a missed opportunity to create shortcuts or accessibility to certain functions.

From my review, it might seem like there's a lot of challenges, which I do not deny. I do think these smart watches have been made better - where the battery last longer, it's a tad more user friendly and have more accurate information.

These watches are perfect for those who do not need a lot out of their watches. To have basic functions, read notifications (not respond to them), have a timer/stopwatch, see the weather and count steps.
If you want more out of your watches, wait for a black friday sale, where you can snag a prior year samsung watch for about $150.

I recently purchased the LIGE Smart Watch for Men, and I must say, it has completely surpassed my expectations! From its sleek design to its impressive features, this smartwatch is a game-changer in the world of wearable technology.

First and foremost, the 1.71'' full touch screen is absolutely stunning. The vibrant colors and crisp resolution make it a joy to interact with. Navigating through menus and accessing various functions is a breeze, thanks to the responsive and intuitive touch interface.

One of the standout features of this smartwatch is its heart rate and sleep monitoring capabilities. The accuracy of the heart rate sensor is commendable, providing real-time data during workouts and daily activities. The sleep monitoring feature is equally impressive, giving me detailed insights into my sleep patterns and helping me make adjustments to improve my overall sleep quality.

What truly sets the LIGE Smart Watch apart from others is its exceptional battery life. With a staggering 50 days of standby time, I no longer have to worry about constantly charging my smartwatch. In terms of durability, the 5ATM waterproof rating is a standout feature. I can confidently wear this smartwatch during swimming sessions or even in the shower without any concerns. The watch is built to withstand water pressures up to 50 meters, making it suitable for various water activities.

Finally, the LIGE Smart Watch's design deserves special mention. Its sleek and stylish appearance makes it suitable for both casual and formal occasions. The comfortable strap ensures a snug fit on the wrist, and the overall build quality feels robust and durable.

In conclusion, the LIGE Smart Watch for Men is a remarkable device that offers a plethora of features at an affordable price point. After 3 weeks of everyday use of the Garmin Instinct Watch, I started getting broken skin and rash on my wrist. Recently, I noticed the rim surrounding the charging on the back of the watch creates an temporary indent on my skin, and the broken skin/ rash appears on the edge of this indent. Hence, the back of the watch is abrading the skin away, especially since that portion of the back of the watch is not smooth. Unfortunately, it looks like there were reviews that the Garmin Instinct and another previous models (i.e. vivoactive and fenix) that also resulted in such rashes, though they think the cause is the optical sensor. Some of these were even posted online 2-3 years ago, so it is unclear if Garmin is willing to address this problem or improve on the design.

On a side note, after the first charge of Garmin Instinct device, none of the sensors would work any more. Robustness? Luckily, I was able to exchange this device for a functioning one within 3 days.

Good things about this device so far, sensors and widgets are easy to use. Graphs via mobile app are easy to use and fun to assess exercise and sleep patterns.

Downside is, ... in addition to the rash, a couple of times while putting on my book bag or heavy jacket, I accidentally hit the other buttons and trigger undesired GPS logging. I didn't realize and it drained the battery during my sleep.

I haven't tried all the features yet, and hope some changes will prevent more rashes.

Since I have it, I'll keep using it. After it breaks/fails, or if I wanted to get a gps/fitness watch for my family members, I'd look elsewhere if there is no improvement.

Review: The Pard Health Sport Watch has a pedometer (steps taken, distance and calories burned), heart rate monitor (beats per minute), O2 monitor (amount of oxygen in your blood), weather (current temp, high and low temp), messages (must be synced with your smart phone), stopwatch, find feature (locates your phone, in app on phone will locate watch), a blood pressure monitor and an exercise monitor.  The exercise monitor tracks running, cycling, jump roping, table tennis, badminton, tennis and swimming.  The watch is comfortable to wear and looks nice, it has five different watch faces you can choose as your display. I chose this watch because of the blood pressure (bp) monitor, I have high blood pressure and I take medication to keep it normal.  I own two electronic high blood pressure test cuffs and I tested my blood pressure with both of them and the watch and the two cuffs were +/- a point or two apart, but the watch was reading a lot higher than the other monitors.  Throughout the day I repeated the test and the watch was constantly reading that my blood pressure was high, even after I had taken my medication and I know the readings should have changed.  I will have to get with my doctor/nurse and have them do a manual test and see if the watch is showing the same results.  If the watch bp monitor is not accurate, I have not been able to find any way to recalibrate the watch to input the correct bp reading.  That is the only problem I have with this watch.  I like all the features the watch has along with the phone app too.  It doesn't take long to learn all the functions of the watch as well as the app.  If you have high blood pressure, I wouldn't rely on just the watch to monitor your blood pressure because I don't believe it is accurate enough.  Maybe in time there will be an upgrade in the software to fix that. Other than that it is a great sport watch and I am happy with it overall.

Review:
My wife and I live on a mini-ranch (24 acres of wooded land). We like to take long walks with our dog and be active outdoors. Both of us are retired from medicine and have an interest in maintaining our health. I already have a smartwatch that tracks my health statistics. I ordered this VEYKY smartwatch for my wife so she can track hers.

She is very pleased with the smartwatch. The watch face and band are very feminine and lightweight. Built into the watch are quite a number of useful health features:

• Number of steps taken and compared against a daily goal (you can set)
• Total yards walked
• Total calories burned
• Total minutes spent walking
• Total hours and minutes of sleep the night before
• Charting of your full-day heart rate
• Current heart rate
• Blood pressure in mmHg (both systolic and diastolic pressures)
• Blood oxygen level in percentage
• Outdoor running (in yards)
• There is a feature you can enable to remind you to walk (to reach your goal)
• The watch can synchronize health information with my wife's "Health" app on her iPhone.

Additionally, there are some nice features that can be set with your smartphone. These features are controlled or can be adjusted by the HeroBandIII app, which can be downloaded from your smartphone's app store:

• There is are many watch faces to choose from
• Up to three alarms can be set in the watch to remind you of important events
• The watch can be used as a remote shutter for your smartphone camera
• You can set the time format
• You can set your preferred language
• You can set your unit format (metric or imperial)
• You can set an auto-lock after a selected number of seconds of no use
• You can set your location to receive weather reports on the watch
• For weather reports, you can set the temperature format (°C or °F)
• If you've forgotten where you set your watch down, there is a "Find Device" feature

To get a more accurate assessment of your health and activities, you will want to go to the "Profile" settings in the HeroBandIII app to set up your gender, height, weight, age, and length of step.

The watch comes with a proprietary magnetic charging cable that attaches to the back of the watch and a small plug-in USB wall charger. She uses the watch often, so the smartwatch requires charging every two or three days. The display is bright enough to be viewed outdoors. When she receives a phone call, the watch vibrates and shows who's calling (if the phone number is in your contacts list on your smartphone).

The print in the manual is rather small - a bit too small for my aging eyes. I have a lighted reader for small print. The manual is basic in the information it provides. It covers some of the main features of the watch and explains the initial setup to download and pair the watch with the free HeroBandIII app. The HeroBandIII app seems well thought out, intuitive, and stable.

Overall, she is quite pleased with this smartwatch, its tight integration with her iPhone, the vital statistics it tracks. It is well made, and it offers a lot of functionality, features, and value for the price.

Garmin Vivoactive 3 is exactly what I am looking for in a smart watch/fitness watch, and more importantly it looks and feels stylish in its classic circular form and light weight design.

Before I talk about its smart and fitness features, I will briefly talk about its display. Display has good enough clarify for a watch. Its always on display is a huge positive compared to Fitbit and apple watch. Outdoor visibility of its display is great and definitely better than other OLED displays due to its specific display technology. On paper its display is inferior to Apple watch, Fitbit Ionic and other wear os watches (formerly Android Wear) in Pixel density and colors, but don't let it stop you from buying Vivoactive 3 as its diplay is perfectly fine and always on display more than makes up for its other shortcomings. I like it even better than other high resolution displays for its positive impact on battery life.

Let's get less important things out of the way first, and that is its smart features. It does what it supposed to do as a smart watch. It notifies me for the applications I have selected in Garmin connect app on my phone. I only keep text and phone call notifications on, and may be couple other important apps to avoid constant vibrations on my wrist. It does notifications very well. Just to clarify I have connected it to my Pixel 2 XL, and I have no connection issues. The only issue I have found is with phone call notifications, where notification persists even after I pickup or decline a call directly on my phone, but other than that it works perfectly fine.

Now I will get to more important part of the watch, and that is fitness. When it comes to fitness, I like everything about Vivoactive 3. The amount of detailed data you get on your workouts is well worth it. My most favorite part of all is being able to see what heart rate zone I am training in during my exercise. It does everything fitbit and other smart watches do and lot more. You get usual matrices like daily steps count, active and inactive calories, floors count, heart rate data and then some extras like weekly intensity minutes, VO2 Max, Fitness age, Stress level etc. You also get move reminders. I like the way I can look at it and tell how long I have been inactive. It reminds you when the red bar of inactivity reaches halfway and then again when it reaches all the way. If you want to varify its accuracy, there are plenty of you tube videos, and based on all reputable sources data you get is fairly accurate.

Three things I want to mention on accuracy though.

- Heart rate: I like the fact that it can connect to external ANT+ chest strap for accurate heart rate monitoring during exercises in which wrist heart rate monitors are not that reliable. I connect it to Garmin Heart rate monitor during my strength training, and I am very happy with the results I get, which by the way only costs $20.

- Floor Count: As many reviewers have mentioned, floor count on vivoactive 3 is very disappointing. Sometimes, it counts fairly well and sometimes it doesn't register it at all. Something to keep in mind.

- GPS: GPS signal is very quick to acquire and accurate. The route it tracks even detects the slightest variations in route (running on right side of the street vs running on left side of the street)

It tracks outdoor run/walk and indoor treadmill run well with plenty of data to review. Rep counting on strength training is surprisingly accurate. It even identifies the type of exercise based on the arm movement. It even identified me doing push ups every time I tried, which is very surprising given the fact that there is very little wrist movement in it. Starting and stopping sets and advancing in a next set is done by double tapping the screen which is very convenient.

All the activities and workouts are saved in Garmin Connect app with plenty of data. It even tells your fitness age based on your VO2 Max.

I can't believe I waited this long to buy my first Garmin Watch, and I am positive that Vivoactive 3 won't be my last Garmin Watch either. I did not come across any of the bugs other users have encountered during my first week of usage. It's been few months since its release and it seems like Garmin has fixed most of its bugs.

SUMMARY:

- Very light weight design, which looks classy in its circular form (I specifically love my slate version)
- Fairly accurate heart rate monitor, and activity and workout tracking
- Ability to connect to external heart rate sensor is a huge plus in my opinion
- Somewhat hit or miss floor count
- Smart watch/smart notifications features are minimal but very useful and reliable
- Advanced dynamics on workout data are very informative

All in all, Vivoactive 3 is a solid smart watch for fitness enthusiastics and everyone who want to live and lead healthy life. I highly recommend it..

The watch screen is very crisp and bright, responds well to touch, is nicely laid out and easy to find and use most functions. I have worn it while bathing and swimming - it is waterproof. PROS: * High class appearance * Lots of watch faces to choose from * Lots of useful functions * Excellent phone app * Waterproof * Responsive crisp bright screen * Step and sleep monitoring * Remote shutter activation * Message notification * Phone finder (loud bell sound). CONS: * No analog watch faces * No timer (It does have a stopwatch but no timer that can be set for a number of minutes with a vibration to note that it is done.) In spite of these two shortcomings, it remains a very useful tool to have on one's wrist. I give it 4.5 - 5 stars.

Review:
Disclosure: I ordered two of these watches but with different model numbers. (I32 and I30) This review applies to both as the only difference between them is the styling.

Wow this is a nice watch.

I love watches, especially when they're smart like this one.

Things I Like:

1.) Build Quality. This watch is solid. I have no concerns that it's going to fall apart after a few months. It's comfortable to wear.

2.) Face. I love the bright display of the watch face. The display can be changed at any time when you want to have a different watch face. You can even have a photo as your display.

3.) Responsive. The screen is very responsive to my touch. No lagging at all.

4.) App. The watch uses the FitCloud Pro app. This app allows for customization and syncing of information. Once set up you can get push notifications, set alarms, activity reminders, change watch face, etc...

5.) Phone and contacts sync. You can get your messages on the watch and receive phone calls. I'm not using it for calls and text messaging so I can't speak on those features.

5.) Blood Pressure. I have taken my blood pressure through the watch but I am not sure how accurate it is. I am only using it for reference and not totally relying on it. What I will say is that there might be some variation in readings between the watch and a stand alone blood pressure device.

6.) Battery Life. The battery life has really surprised me. It's lasting much longer than the 2 or 3 days I thought it would last.

There are two things I wish were different: charging the watch and adding an additional watch. It uses a proprietary charging cable instead of USB-C or perhaps a charging cradle. The other thing I think is useful for people to know is that if you have more than one model of this watch, the FitCloudPro app will not allow you to add more than one watch. You will have to unbind one watch to bind the other. This is inconvenient.

This smartwatch is worthy of your consideration. I love it. It's an excellent value.

I hope this helps.

Review:
Alright so I came into this purchase with mild expectations and I have since been blown away. Now, I will start by saying that I was looking for a smartwatch that was gaudy or too futuristic looking so this seemed to fit my criteria nicely. This doesn't have any incredibly outstanding features like the Apple i-Watch or any of Samsung or Motorola's watches, but it does exactly what I wanted it to do. It alerts me to a number of different notifications that my phone receives such as text messages, ESPN score updates, phone calls, and yes even the occasional Tinder match. The thing that the Martian Notifier does best is hide the fact that it is a smartwatch in plain sight. The piece, I feel, is first and foremost a timekeeping mechanism, and secondly a smart watch. There is an LED notifier light that will blink on the watch if you get a notification, which you can turn off in the app or on the watch menu itself, but that's as close it comes to alerting the casual passerby or onlooker that what you have on your wrist is a smartwatch. The notification system itself is a small OLED screen at the bottom of the watch which will light up ever so slightly when you have a notification, and then proceed to scroll your message across the screen.

Now, the only reason I am deducting a point is not functionality based, but purely an aesthetics matter. The watch band is rubber and while some may like the casual aspect, I find it difficult to dress up if I need to while wearing this watch. What I would like to see in the future is a more dressy looking band that comes with the watch; I know you can buy a band and replace the stock one, but that's just another step in the buying process that I'd like to avoid. I'd also like to see them start to compete with some of the fitness trackers, as I would like to work out with this on and have it track at least my steps and distance. One more side note, as has been mentioned in other reviews this is splash proof, not completely waterproof. I will definitely follow this company closely and see where the go from here with this tech.

Review:
Battery is 5 stars. Some stats of battery usage with music+GPS+heart rate:
10mile run, 1 hour 40 min, 26-30% of battery
5k run, 26 min, 8-10% of battery
5k short interval (200x10) with vibration notification: 9% of battery
Comparing with Ticwatch Sports/Huawei Watch 2: a 30min 5k run with music+heart rate (always-on display) = 25% battery gone.
I am running 20 miles each week, biking to work on Tuesday to Friday (3 miles each), 1 charge lasts almost a week (slightly better than Garmin 645 music).
Based on above estimate, this watch has no problem running a 4h-5h30m full marathon with music+GPS+heart rate on, which no Apple watches nor Android watches can do.

Screen is 5 stars for outdoor, 3 stars for poorly lit condition. Unlike Apple Watch or any Android WearOS watches using OLED display, which you have to bump up the screen lightness under sunlight. Stratos is using a transflective screen like Garmin watches, the brighter the sunlight is, the clearer the image is. For indoor, the screen is good as well. The only scenario the screen's readability is sub-par is when you use the watch to track swimming during evening time.

Music experience is 2 stars. Apparently Xiaomi doesn't streamline the music experience as good as Apple Watches or Android Watches (iTunes/Google play auto playlist sync). There is even no playlist support so far. You have to name the mp3 files sequentially to play them in a certain order. The bluetooth connection is okay. I am using a Trekz Air, the signal does not drop when wearing on the right hand. I experienced occasional drop when wearing on the left hand, but still much better than Ticwatch S or Huawei Watch 2.

App experience is 3 stars. The app itself is like Garmin connect and I give 5 stars, you can customize your workout, the stat fields shown during a run/bike/swim. It has no problem on the latest Android system (8.1 on Pixel 2 XL). I am subtracting 2 stars because it asks too much permission (luckily on Android 8.1 you can customize what permission to give the app).

Review:
First off, i'm not a bot or some paid company writing fake reviews. Everything below comes from my own purchase of the ticwatch pro. I really want to like this watch and I'm a fan of Ticwatch, but I have to be honest ; it has some setbacks. I will try to keep it as concise as possible while giving sufficient info to help you in your buying decision.
1. Physical attributes
Metal bezel and some kind of nylon-glass composite for the body, although the overall feel is "plasticky". However it does feel strong enough for outdoor sports. The band is a 22mm "hybrid" - leather on the outside, silicone beneath. Weight is okay, not too heavy nor Fitbit-light. Have to say it is a comfortable watch for its large 45mm size. The watch looks bigger than its stated size due to its wide chunky lugs. I highly discourage smaller/thinner wrists from getting this as it may overhang. I read some reviews saying the band is too short. I have exactly 7.5 inch wrists, you can see from the attached image how it wears. There is excess.
2. Software
This is the caveat I need to highlight. It can be slow at times to open apps or switch screens. I am not sure why this keeps happening as I turned off Heart monitor, turned off Wifi etc to keep it bare bones. In comparison my Fossil Gen3 smartwatch is considerably fast; screen changes almost instantaneous, and that is a 2 year old product. Maybe it is a quality control issue affecting some pieces. Also, with the new smartwatches due out later this year (Samsung just announced their latest smartwatch last weekend), you may want to wait it out to see the upcoming competition if you are in no rush.
3. Fitness tracking
Pretty decent, does everything it advertises. Heart rate monitor is good. Step tracking is relatively okay, no glitches. However I find the Fitbit Versa is more accurate and fitness-app-friendly. Do note the Ticwatch Pro is more of a smartwatch first rather than an activity tracker.
4. Battery Life
Yes it has the famous 2 screens everyone keeps talking about. And it does extend battery life. This is one area the Ticwatch Pro really excels. You can choose either purely Essential mode (Only the battery saving screen is active), Always On Mode (only your selected watch face is shown) or hybrid; Essential mode switches to watch face when you flick your wrist or press something on the watch. I tested it in full Watch Face mode (the most draining option) and got about 2.5 days max (GPS off, heart rate off, Wifi off).
5. Additional Features
I like the ability to answer phone calls directly on the watch when I'm driving. No hassling around with the handphone. The audio is clear enough despite road noise and the other party can hear me clearly via the watch. The other thing is NFC, you can wave your hand and do Google pay with this watch. Pretty useful at grocery store or deli when you have your hands full.
6. Conclusion
Thank you for reading this far. I'll make this quick: A solid contender in the premium smartwatch market with a decent price tag. You will enjoy its battery life and some additional features. However again do note there may be some slowness in the software and there are newer models with better processors on the 2018 horizon. If this watch is $199 it is a very attractive proposition, but this coming from a Ticwatch fan - consider your options carefully if you are not in a rush to get your next smartwatch.

Review: I wanted to love the Gear Fit so much -- but it's just impossible.  The Gear Fit is a worst-of-both-worlds compromise between smart watch and fitness device.  The form factor limits the usefulness of the Fit as a smart watch but that's ok because it's primarily a fitness device, right?  Wrongo!  The fitness features of the Gear Fit are absolutely primitive compared to any other fitness wearable on the market.  You must remember to manually start the pedometer each day else the device records nothing.  If you do remember to activate, the readings can be wildly inaccurate.  The pedometer logged 223 steps while I was lying in my bed asleep!  Sleep tracking? Hah - you'll get about as much functionality if you start a stopwatch when you lie down and stop it when you get up.  The heart monitor gives readings +/- 25 bpm one after another while sitting still and quiet as recommended.  I own a Note3, Samsung's top-of-the-line phone 8 months ago but not new enough to use S-Health with this Fit.  Instead, it's limited to integrating with "Fitness with Gear", a hack of an app that is so limited in function that's it's worthless.  I'm a Samsung fan and love my Samsung phone, tablet, refrigerator, washer/dryer...but no love for this device.

Review:
**Update 2019-12-31: Shortly after posting my review, Mobvoi reached out to me and provided me a shipping label to their office in USA. They quickly approved my warranty and sent me a replacement watch. In the meantime, I purchased another TicWatch Pro 4G/LTE during a lightning sale on Black Friday / Cyber Monday. So now I have an extra watch, just in case something happens to my or my spouse's TicWatch Pro 4G/LTE!

Unfortunately, Verizon charged me another activation fee for the replacement watch. For my troubles, Mobvoi kindly offered me a TicBand, which I wear consistently on my right wrist (the TicWatche is on my left wrist).

Oh, I've even gone swimming with the warranty replacement watch and have had no issues to date.
/Update**

For a very long time, I have been looking for a Wear OS watch that I could use without it being tethered to a phone. The Mobvoi TicWatch Pro 4G/LTE is the answer to my prayers.

Previously, I thought I found the solution in the the LG Sport Watch, which was the flagship Wear OS watch with stand-alone SIM for phone and data. However, the LG was wrought with bugs and poor battery life. Not the TicWatch!

I found the watch to be indispensable and a decent replacement for my phone, especially when going on runs or other activities. I can figure it via Verizon Number Share. I was able to make and receive phone calls, instant message (I sideloaded Google Hangouts), text messages, etc. without having my phone nearby or even turned on. If you know where to look, you'll also be able to find the actual phone number for the watch, which people can call in addition to your phone's number.

I did go swimming with the watch and drained the speaker as I normally do. However, a few hours later, the watch ceased to work. Since I bought one of these for my wife, I verified that it was not a charging issue by using her watch on both chargers. My watch refused to turn on or respond.

I initiated a warranty claim on 2019-11-11. After some ridiculous amounts of back and forth emailing (some of the responses took 4 to 5 days), Mobvoi finally approved the warranty claim on 2019-11-26. However, for the warranty service, I was instructed to send the watch via postal mail to... Wait for it... China!

I got this really long mailing address string in one line:

"Room 901, 9th Floor, Tower A,New Zhongguan Shopping Center Office Building,No. 19 Zhongguancun street, Haidian district, Beijing,China Mobvoi 4008838390"

This is beyond ridiculous. I have never mailed anything to China. Is this the correct address format? I think I need to put carriage returns somewhere, right. Anyhow, I'll have to go to the post office and mail the watch for warranty service. It's been already over 2 weeks since I started a claim, I'm now wondering how long it'll be before I will be made whole.

Review:
I was looking for a watch to replace my pebble and settled on this one even though I knew it was more fitness than smart watch oriented. The size, continuous HR monitoring, waterproof(ness), battery life, and ability to receive smart notifications and respond with preset text was what swayed me towards purchasing this watch over the competition. I returned it after 5 days and will explain why...

I will say it is an absolutely beautiful watch. It is incredibly sleek and I love the lightweight body and silicon band. I have a 5.5" wrist and it fit just fine without looking ridiculously out of place like most smart watches do. I actually really like their silicone band design and would consider purchasing just the band in the future. The virtual bevel to scroll through pages I found really annoying (would either not really move or skip through a bunch of pages) and thought it was easier to use the touchscreen.

The continuous HR and ability to track a wide variety of activities is really nice. The graphical display of your activity and sleep in the app is also great. I never got to try the GPS because there were some things I just couldn't get over and had to return it...

The connection to my Samsung S7 was not very reliable. Many times when I would turn off my phone or watch, it had issues reconnecting through bluetooth. Again, I know this is not exactly marketed as a smart watch, but if you are going to advertise smart notifications and the ability to respond to texts, it should work consistently. I found a lot of issues with displaying of notifications from my android: a) no group chat notifications, b) if someone sent me a really long text that got broken up into multiple simultaneous texts, the watch can only open the most recent of the messages, c) you can't send a text, only respond, d) there is no ability to modify your preset replies to texts. The last two are not a huge deal, but would be nice features. Maybe I'm too used to the instant and reliable connection of my pebble, the microphone, and the ability to send texts and reply with user written text or voice. But for $300 and "smart notifications" feature, I was really expecting it to maintain a constant connection with my phone and display a group or long text correctly.

Garmin customer service was friendly and patient when I asked them a million questions. They responded within a few days but could really only tell me that they would "let the research team know" or that I could submit my suggestions for future features.

Overall, I find that this is more of an amped up and beautified fit-bit-esque smart watch. Didn't meet my needs. Looks like it's back to my trusty pebble until the new samsung watch comes out...

Review:
Mobvoi has been upping their products from the first minute they started in the market. I have been a fan of their watches since their second release on Kickstarter. I have only seen steady improvement since their inception.

Unboxing was a pleasurable experience. The E3 comes with a usb cable and charging port specific to this device. The device was pretty low on power so I gave it a good charge before I went forward to set up the link between my phone and the device.

Ever since Mobvoi moved to Google Wear, installation and connection has been a breeze. My only complaint is the Wear OS only allows you to use one smartwatch set up at a time. I had to delete my current settings of my Ticwatch Pro.

The device is sleek and styled well. It’s understated and no nonsense. The look is clean and effective. It comes with a latex band which can swapped out for a standard 20mm band if you are looking for more a polished style.

The E3 is equipped with the Snapdragon 4100 processor and as a person who has used a good number of Ticwatch devices (I own 4) the scrolling and performance is quick and precise.

I have found this to be a new favorite wearable device and I am curious to see what my reaction to using the Ticwatch Pro will be after wearing this for a few days. I think some of the features that the Pro offer are essential I.e. the twin screens that allow you to use your watch for up to a week without a charge (as a LCD screen watch) but the difference in cost (100$) may win me over in the long run.

Give it a look!

Review:
I bought this to replace my aging Fitbit Surge. While the Fitbit worked well for many years, it was ugly and lacked so many features that I think a running watch so have. Among the top features I wanted was Apple Healthkit integration. Fitbit absolutely refuses to play nice with Apple and Garmin promises to fill that bill. I went for my first run with my Vivoactive the night after I received it and it quickly locked on to the satellite while stretching. Two subsequent runs since then had the same quick satellite acquisition.

I train with teenagers to run a half marathon. With the kids, we run intervals. What fitbit described as an interval timer was not an interval timer. The Garmin gave me a noticeable vibration at 6 minutes when it was time to walk and again after a minute when it was time to run. The fitbit would have just vibrated every 6 minutes which is not useful. If you are going to run intervals, you may as well do it right.

This is also a much nicer looking watch than the Surge or any of the current Fitbit offerings in my opinion. I also think it looks much better than my Apple Watch.

The Garmin also has many features that pretty much make me think I will wear my Apple Watch less often. The Surge only let me read text messages. The Vivoactive pretty much gives me all the notifications that my iPhone does. A nice feature is that these can be muted during a workout or set that only phone calls give an alert.

I would have gave this 5 stars, but I’m kind of wary of the battery life claims. After the initial charge to 100%, I wore it to bed to track sleep and the battery drained to 82% for a 6 hour sleep. Googling this I read that turning the watch off and back on again would correct this. I’ve worn the watch for 12 hours today and and ran almost an hour and a half using GPS and I’m at 69% which I can live with I suppose.

Review:
I had the original Garmin Vivofit since 2017 and it worked beautifully, I loved it but was tired of the square chunky design. I wanted a fitness watch that looked like a watch so began shopping around this year for a more updated version with the new bells and whistles such as text and call notifications, more activity options etc. However, I wanted to make sure it still looked nice and wasn't super expensive or too big since I have small wrists.

I love the updated sleep tracker with REM cycle monitoring when HR is enabled and the options to turn off or remove widgets I don't use. I've had this since Saturday 6/2 and haven't charged it yet (it's at 26%) and haven't taken it off . I highly recommend the Garmin Vivoactive 3 to everyone, a coworker is excited to buy one after seeing mine.

Pros: design, simplified face options, myriad watch face download options, different band color options, Garmin app is excellent, alarm feature is sweet, love waking up to the vibration instead of my loud alarm, everything I love this watch. Almost everything is awesome.

Con: The slide/swipe section on the side of the watch face (you can swipe on the screen or off the side) is VERY sensitive which is great except it was swiping through screens when I wasn't paying attention. But the lock screen option solved that.

Review: After taking a few days to research the current crop of smartwatches and reading what others had to say about their smartwatch experiences I chose to give the Moto 360 a try and I'm glad I did. Out of the box the watch is easy to set up and prompts you to update to the latest version of the Android OS, Lollipop.  The watch paired easily with my Alcatel Tracfone and sends timely notifications and e-mails conveniently to my wrist.  I love it!  For those that feel variety is the spice of life you will find many watch faces available in the Google Play store.  Some folks seem overly concerned with battery life on these watches.  I put my watch In the wireless charger before I go to bed at night and its ready to go in the morning.  Even with ambient mode selected and with moderate use the charge lasts me all day.  When I bought this watch I wondered if I would find it useful but after wearing it for a week I know that if I didn't have it I'd miss it.  I'm happy with the Moto 360 and would buy it again without hesitation.

Review:
I am not a professional runner. My requirements of a smart watch are simple: built-in GPS (so I can run without my hp) , heart rate monitoring, can be used for swimming and don't cost a bomb. I was waiting for the new Garmin vivoactive HR. But the users reviews of its poor display turn me off. Plus Vivoactive HR is priced at $249, comparing with TomTom Spark Cardio @ $149. So in the end, I purchased TomTom Spark Cardio. Below are my verdicts of it.

Cons:
1. If you look for some fashionable or stylish smart watch, you will be very much turned off by TomTom Spark cardio. It is very plastic and looks like a cheap digital watch in the 50's.
2. The watch display cover is plastic too and it is very easy to get scratches. After wearing for a couple of days, I can see quite a number of small scratches on the surface, although none affects the reading.
3. The wrist band is relatively hard and stiff. It is not uncomfortable, but i certainly love the feel of the wrist band of a Garmin 235.
4. Black and white and not touch screen display (actually, I love the display. Explain later)
5. Battery charging seems to be long. It takes around 3 hours to fully charge it.
6. to charge the watch, the watch body must be removed from the belt. This is really a poor design.

Pros:
1. Cheap. It is cheapest (maybe I am wrong on this) smart watch with GPS and HR.
2. GPS locking is fast. Generally, the watch can lock the satellites within 10-15 seconds.
3. The black and white display is very visible and clear even under a scorching and bright sun. Reading it is effortless under all kinds of weathers.
4. It can track running, cycling, swimming, running on treadmill, gym session and walking.
5. Ok battery life. It can last at least 4 days with 1 hour running tracking on each day.
6. the menu navigation is quite intuitive. It reminds me of how the old Nokia phone worked.

Review:
Where to start...

First, let me say that I'm on my 3rd watch and love it. It's a cool smart watch that also looks like a very nice watch. I've yet to change the watch band but it appears easy to do.

That being said, I am on my 3rd watch. The first watch was great for 2 days then started having intermittent issues. It would lose connectivity and also randomly turn off. The knowledge base for Martian Watches gave me advice that summed up to "charge the battery" or "try turning it off and back on". Neither worked. Instead of actually working with Martian, I just returned the watch through Amazon and purchased another. The second watch wouldn't even update it's firmware from Martian Watches. Again, I avoided Martian and returned it to the Amazon seller. The third watch has been great (bought it from groupon...). I am happy with it so far but the weather function doesn't always load (Martian kb says to toggle the 'weather' switch in the Martian app..).

The last thing I want to say is that the Martian service department is not based in the U.S. That normally wouldn't bother me, but the individuals that have sent me emails barely were able to put together a logical sentence and they also didn't seem to understand my exact questions. I asked about the issues my watches were having and that I didn't want to keep buying new watches and they basically said "we can't help you unless you put it in a work order" or to just get help from their online forum and knowledge base.

If you want a nice smart watch, this is great. Just don't rely on much help if you get issues.
