Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
This review is for the Vivoactive 3 Music but most of what I post here applies to the Vivoctive 3 Non-Music except for, obviously - anything having to do with the music or WiFi. You can control music on your phone using the Vivactive 3 Non-Music but you can not store music on it.

If you’re not in the habit of sending your activities to Garmin via the app, or WiFi or via computer – the memory capacity is seven times activities and fourteen days of activity tracking. According to Garmin this is the same for both watches even though the 3 Music has more memory.

The Vivoactive 3 Music has a real barometric altimeter (versus the GPS one that many older watches have) as well as a magnetic compass and the standard accelerometers that are needed for daily activity tracking. It also has a thermometer but this isn't very useful, being so close to the warmth of the arm.

The wrist heart rate monitoring is Garmin’s new Elevate™ system and is Garmin’s version of LED’s and sensors and software for monitoring 24 hour heart rate activity tracking. For specific exercise activity-logging these same HR sensors sample at a much higher rate than the lower and variable sample rate of twenty-four hour monitoring (if you have the automatic option turned on for daily HR monitoring).

This does an excellent job at twenty-four hour monitoring, as well as use during many activities. But if you are doing plyometrics, Martial Arts or Mixed Martial Arts, or possibly running, or anything where your arms is very active; then you may want to consider a chest contact heart rate band or arm band heart rate sensor – any of which will connect to your watch of course. Once you pair them and start an activity the watch automatically picks up the previously paired device and uses it instead of the onboard HR sensors. I recommend something like the Scosche RHYTHM+ HR armband - https://amzn.to/2HY6fni, the newer Scosche Rhythm 24 HR armband - https://amzn.to/2SxuEEh, the Garmin Heart Rate Chest Strap or other heart rate straps. For regular road biking and even a regular run the onboard HR sensors work fine, maybe not so much for mountain biking and an intense road biking session and other very active exercises.

Of note here – there IS NO sound whatsoever on these watches, the only notifications are visual and vibration.

Many of the smart features of the watch like smart notifications are done via Bluetooth. You can even respond in some simple ways to texts and reject calls, etc and there are customization options for your responses. These work well and the vibration alerts of the watches is good at notifying you without bugging others around you. In addition to texts, notifications can come from Facebook, Garmin, and other things via your phone.

The Vivoactive3 Music has Garmin Pay but I have never used this so I can’t speak to how well it works or on anything having to do with setting it up, though I would imagine it’s similar to any of the other digital integrated payment methods.

BTW, charging is quite fast – with the computer as well as with a wall charger. Battery life is adequate but could be better.

Garmin says up to five hundred songs can be stored on the 3 Music (the non-Music of course does not store any music) - I haven't tested the exact number, and you are able to fairly quickly swipe over while in an exercise and control your music. It works fine but really there should have been a way to embed/add a music data field to eliminate the single extra step.

You can see what is currently playing, skip forward to the next song or back, and shuffle, repeat, adjust volume, use playlists, sort by artists; podcasts; audiobooks; genres; songs; albums, and access music apps like Deezer and others (no Pandora yet).

You can also pair more than one audio device so if you use earbuds and speakers too, or different headphones for various things/activities then you’re covered. For earbuds here’s a nice Bluetooth set that work well for me, though not the absolute greatest on bass.

Moving on to Widgets – these are little apps that can be accessed by scrolling the screen up and down to find what you are looking for. There are a number of already-installed including a step counter with full daily totals and daily charts, heart rate with charts and average heart rate time, My Day which is an overview of all of your twenty-four hour activity tracking stats, calories burned, distance traveled, intensity minutes, floors, standard calendar, clock, timer, stopwatch, weather, etc. You can also do a Find My Phone and control a Garmin Virb via widgets and apps. This covers the newer Virbs, not the original ones, BTW, like the Virb Elite (there’s also a Find my Watch in the Garmin Connect app, but it is only good to the range of your Bluetooth).

And there are a lot more you can add – both made by Garmin as well as others, via the Connect IQ store. There are hundreds of free widget apps, and some which require a small purchase or are demos/trials for paid versions. But there are tons of free ones of all sorts. Test them throuough before actually logging anything with them.

The floors climbed that the watch detects seems to be off for me, somewhat low while my wife gets what may be a somewhat higher normal than she thinks she should be getting (my wife informs me that her floors are now displaying correctly – firmware update perhaps?). This is one place that Garmin needs improvement on. My active minutes seems awfully high too, I do a lot of fitness but this seems inflated for me.

Advanced sleep monitoring is another of the features the The Vivoactive 3 series track and this seems to have vastly improved over time. There’s no installed widgets on the watch for showing any sleep display like there is for many other things, but you can find the advanced stats on Garmin Connect and through your Garmin Connect app. This is quite involved; monitoring periods of movement and restful sleep, REM sleep, etc.

The watch itself as well as the Garmin Connect app will show you an estimated VO2 Max – the measurement of the maximum amount of oxygen that a person can utilize during an exercise, which is a good measurement of your body’s ability to consume oxygen.

On the Garmin Connect app you will also find a Fitness Age calculation under the same screen where you find VO2 Max in the Garmin Connect App (under Performance Stats). This shows only under “VO2 Max” but not under Cycling VO2 Max. To get your VO2 Max for biking do some outdoor rides, for general VO2 Max do an outdoor run (or do the VO2 Max Test on the watch).

Another of the widgets that some (maybe a lot) of people may find of interest is the one that monitors your Stress Level. This also, like most of the other stat widgets, has a graph showing levels during the day. Sometimes when scrolling to it you may find that it wants to do a quick test, which last thirty seconds or a minute, and if you find yourself not able to lower your level you can do some deep breathing exercises perhaps, or consider other approaches to decreasing your stress.

This measures your Heart Rate Variability (HRV) and Garmin gets this processing of levels from First Beat, which does Garmin’s stat analytics. Having a high level isn’t necessarily bad if you have just done a high performance workout, but – for example – if you’re sitting around and doing nothing and still have a high level that may not be good…

Along the lines of steps and daily exercise there is also a move bar (option) and vibration that lets you know if you have been sedentary for too long. For those of us who sit for periods at a computer this can be a great reminder to get up and move around. Sitting is the new ‘soda pop’, ya know. This generally works well and will show you when you have cleared it too, by getting some movement and steps in. But sometimes it does work a bit flaky, in general though it is quite useful.

Goal achievements are also shown and attention is called to them by a vibration. This can be steps, stairs, intensity minutes, etc and this option can be turned off altogether or switched to toggle off automatically off when in an activity and back on afterward. You can also leave it to learn your activity level and assign auto-goals, which works fairly well.

History and My Stats are two things I use often, accessed like most things by a simple touch and scroll. History shows you exercise stats and totals and My Stats show VO2 Max and your personal records and Resting HR. There are many options and settings accessed via the touch and scroll.

You can shortcut things like turning on and off the Bluetooth, manually, syncing, and many others things in the Controls Menu – which you bring up by holding down the physical button on the side of the watch for a second or so (longer than the single press needed to bring up the activities menu). You can customize the Controls Menu in the app. More about the Controls Menu later.

Supposedly it will store seven timed activities and fourteen days of activity tracking data. On my 3 Music the numbers of activities kept onboard vary, I’m not sure how the watch decides this but it is around seven or more at any given time.

One of the great things about Garmin’s Connect IQ – which is similar to a version of the Itunes and Google Play store for downloading apps – is that many of the apps and data fields can overcome some of the limitations inherent in the watch as well as add great flexibility and customizations to the watch in general.

There is a backlight that can be turned on by swiping or when you touch the faceplate. I turned down the brightness and turned off gesture control.

To enter the mode to record your exercises, rides, runs, etc you press the physical button on the side momentarily (holding it down longer brings up the Controls Menu instead), which brings up your Apps/Fitness Activities list.

You can change how many Data Fields are displayed in each app/fitness activity, ranging from one to four in each and you can have anywhere from one to three total screens per app/fitness activity, with the HR Zone Gauge screen as an additional screen if desired.

Unlike the rest of the screens the HR Zone Gauge screen can’t be customized. The HR Zone Gauge shows what zone you are in via a gauge-like screen with your heart rate in the middle. You can edit your heart rate zones in the Garmin Connect App or online with Garmin Connect, but no other options are available for this particular screen unlike the others.

There are many built-in fitness apps like Run, Bike, Indoor Bike, Walk, Treadmill Run, Indoor Walk, Cardio, Strength Training with rep counting, Elliptical Training (your results may vary with this – many people – me included – don’t seem to have much luck with this. I just use Indoor Walk and switch it to Elliptical in Garmin Connect), Stair Stepper, Floor Climbing, Indoor Rowing, Yoga, Skiing, Snowboard, XC Skiing, Stand Up Paddleboard, Row, Indoor Row, and more.

Pool swim is another, where you can set the length of the pool (from presets as well as custom) and it calculates your pool metrics like stroke type detection and swim efficiency using the built-in accelerometer as you swim. Unfortunately there is no open water swim on this watch (only a few Garmin watches seem to have this, like the Garmin Swim or Garmin Instinct) – perhaps some variation of DC Rainmaker’s technique for tagging the Vivoactive 3 along the surface via a tethered flaoting bag or something might work with a specific marked distance?

A full-featured Golf mode is also available, I don’t golf so I can’t tell you anything whatsoever about this.

TrueUp, a fairly new feature, is included. This allows syncing of data between multiple Garmin devices – say if you switch from your watch to a bike computer. Any relevant data is synced between the devices when they are connected. A personal biking distance or speed record done on your Garmin bike computer will show up under your records on your watch, or daily activity stats recorded with your Vivoactive 3 will show up on your Fenix 5, etc.

Some of the activities also give you the option of advanced workouts like Garmin Workouts and Training Plans that can be set up in Garmin Connect. Some apps/activities give you various options, like in the Run app. For Run this includes Run/Walk if you are just starting out or doing a long run and need occasional breaks, and others. The Run fitness app also lets you calibrate treadmill distances, though it is supposed to do some self-calibration after your first outdoor run(s). Indoor Track is also a separate fitness app.

Most of the Fitness apps also have auto pause, auto lap, manual lap, lap alerts, auto scroll, and you also have the ability for touch and button lock. All pretty self-explanatory I think.

Customizable alerts are another excellent feature for some, with heart rate zone alerts, calorie, drink, eat, turn around, go home, calories, in addition to the ones you would expect like distance and speed and time, etc. Some of these can be very powerful motivators, or useful from a health standpoint and of course for training.

Any of the Data Fields can be changed to any number of built-in data points like speed, distance, Sunset, elevation, etc – there are many. In addition additional Data Fields can be downloaded from Garmin Connect IQ. And each separate fitness activity/app can have a differing number of Data Fields and various options.

In addition to adding Data Fields to existing workout apps (and in some cases replacing them with a full screen Data Field) you can also download many other apps/fitness activities as well as things like mapping, navigation, etc that do not add to or replace Data Fields but show up as a new, separate fitness app/app.

I want to mention here, one of the built-in apps is Garmin’s Navigation which is great to have handy as it can be invoked at any time – inside an existing app or separately – to navigate you to saved Waypoints or back to the beginning of your activity. This is something that I would recommend everyone try out and get used to using in case of emergency or when needed. This will not navigate you back using routing like a car GPS or Google Maps but it will point you in the right direction. And you can start it anytime to get back to your beginning point or a Waypoint. Heck, if you think you might lose your car in a large parking lot you can save it as a Waypoint…

One good fitness app to add, that is missing, is a hiking app. I recommend Hike2+ by jim_m_58 and BF Hiker Map by BeFitte.

Duplicating the built-in apps is also an option. Say you want to use the Run app for your regular running but you want to use something like the Connect IQ Race Screen app when you do a running race. So if you duplicate the RUN app you would have the original RUN app and a Run 2 app. Unfortunately there is absolutely no way to change the name of the duplicated apps, it just adds a number after it.

One of the most useful safety features that works with the Vivoactive 3 series in conjunction with the Garmin Connect app on your phone is Livetrack. With Livetrack you can share your real-time info with friends or family or acquaintances, or as Garmin mentions – your fans 😉 You start Livetrack in the Garmin Connect app and, obviously; you must be planning on doing an activity that uses the GPS. Livetrack uses the GPS on your watch and your phone’s cellular signal to allow others to see your location and keep updated, and stats. If you are trying to use Livetrack an area with bad GPS it will only show your last location(s), or may be spotty.

Manual start or AutoStart for Livetrack are options, with AutoStart all future activities will automatically start LiveTrack. Nice if you tend to forget to start it.

Also, your LiveTrack info can be shared directly to some Garmin devices that are compatible and this also ties into Strava Beacon (if you happen to have the proper Strava subscription).

A multitude of Garmin ANT+ and ANT+ compatible sensors as well as many Smart Bluetooth sensors can be connected to the Vivoactive 3/3 Music and, of course, display and process data from them. Yes, you can use nearly any kind of non-Garmin ANT+ and Bluetooth sensors with the Vivoactive 3 series.

One notable exception/caveat here; for those bicyclists out there – there is no ‘official’ bike power sensor meter display and recording – though it will connect to a power meter - https://amzn.to/2HZBuhE . Fortunately there are Apps and Data Fields created by others that allow the display of power with a power sensor that is connected, like the ANT+ Power Meter Data Field by takura87 and others. This Data Field has a way to insert the data into the .FIT file that the watch uses and uploads. Unfortunately despite the app showing power and saving neither the data the watch itself nor Garmin Connect can not use this information for calculating VO2 max and other factors. Why did Garmin not include this capability natively?

Foot pods - https://amzn.to/2t8ujtv - can be added too, and the Stryde foot pod can be accessed via one of the Data Fields from Connect IQ, like Power Meter.

The Vivoactive 3 series is compatible with Garmin’s Varia rearview radar and headlights too - https://amzn.to/2BoVEvU . Great safety devices from what I have heard.

Connecting to sensors is very easy and quick, and if you have one that has both Bluetooth and ANT+ you can select which you want it to connect through. I prefer ANT+ as I believe that it has faster data transfer (or processing) but Bluetooth is more secure.

One thing to remember here is that when you connect a sensor of any sort – if it is powered up it will be used by whatever fitness app you are using. If you have a couple of bikes with cadence sensors on them and you’ve previously connected both – you don’t want to inadvertently jiggle the bike you’re not riding as that sensor might come out of power down and be the first one the watch picks up. I would have liked to see the ability to pick the sensors that each fitness app uses, but despite this I have yet to have any problems like the scenario mentioned above.

All sensors that I have tried have been able to be set up easily and quickly and cleanly – except for an older Scosche Bluetooth Speed/Cadence sensor - https://amzn.to/2SxeJpz. This showed up as connectable device but would not connect. This is a very cheap, older Bluetooth sensor.

Otherwise everything has worked perfectly and flawlessly, and even things like the bike sensors (that go to sleep when not in use) are quickly picked up by the watch as soon as there is any bike movement. I used the excellent Scosche RHYTHM+ Armband Heart Rate Sensor - https://amzn.to/2HY6fni, Wahoo Speed and Cadence Sensors and the cheapo but just-as-good-as-more-expensive-ones CooSpo Bike Speed and Cadence Sensors - https://amzn.to/2HZ1EkV.

My wife uses the Garmin Face It app (there is a version for both Android and IOS, iPhone) and created a custom clock face on her Vivoactive 3 Music. This allows you to add your own background photo and slightly customize the clock face options.

So…my conclusion. While there are a few small features lacking that I would have liked – I very highly recommend the Garmin Vivoactive 3 or Garmin Vivoactive 3 Music for a wide range of users. From those who want to casually keep track of their all-day activities to those who want some more heavier-duty and specific fitness logging and smartwatch abilities, and anything in between. You can find a more thorough review and more photos on my blog - https://randombitsbytes.com

Review:
Ticwatch E3 is a fashionable looking, multi-functional smart watch. I highly recommend it to everyone. I have apple watch 1st generation for years. I haven't used the newest version of apple watch, but I would say this time, Ticwatch E3 is much better than my previous experience with apple watch.

1. The look of Ticwatch E3 is much better for man, with bigger screen, high resolution fonts, and different watch faces to choose.
2. Ticwatch E3 is supported by Google WearOS, thus you can find tons of apps from Google Play Store. The most useful functions, such as heart rate, blood sugar, timer, alarm are already supported by the original setting plus google maps, translate, and many other apps you can download form Google Play.
3. Battery life is good. It typically supports full function for almost two days. By the end of of the second day, when the battery is low, it will enter the basic mode, with time and pedometer still running, and other functions shut off. Then you can still use the watch for a few hours, very considerable setting.
4. One useful function of Ticwatch, if you use iPhone, you may be annoyed by the weak vibration motor, and may miss calls/messages. The Ticwatch vibration is a very good plus to supplement that function, and you will never miss phone calls again.

Review:
I was one of the 60,000+ Kickstarter backers. I received mine about two and a half weeks ago and have loved it ever since. To start off, I am normally not a watch wearer nor would I be willing to spend more than $150 on a smart watch gimmick, so this watch fulfills my requirements. There are a few minor things about it that bother me but overall it has been great so far.

Good:
-Lightweight
-Not bulky or gaudy
-Interchangeable bands
-7 day battery life
-Features
-water proof (resistant) I shower with mine all the time with no problems.

Bad
-inaccurate heart rate monitor.
- Difficult to use standard watch band and spring bar if the band is 22mm. (no holes in the side to allow access to spring pin ends)

I have a ton of watch faces loaded up and they have all worked wonderfully with the watch. The decreased bezel compared to the first Pebble makes it look a lot less like a toy and more like a useful watch. the Pebble app has been great about allowing the notifications I want and they pop up on the watch with a quick vibrate. Vibration is not loud but you will notice it right away. I had trouble pairing it at first but it worked itself out and remains paired as long as I am in range of my phone. The screen is crystal clear and very easy to read. If you use a well made watch face they look great. Another feature I use a lot is the alarm feature. I matched the watch alarm with my phone, so it will vibrate when the alarm goes off. Instantly wakes me up.

Durability has been great so far. When I first got it I used a left over screen protector from a cell phone and cut it to size. Although it has Gorilla Glass 3 I didn't want to take a chance. Plus I liked the look of the matte screen protector. The watch band it ships with is made of silicone and seems to be holding up well. The end with the clasp is 2.75" long and the other end is 4.875". The clasp is made of plastic. I have messed around with the watch pretty frequently and the side buttons are holding up fine. I have some concerns that the silicone cover on the buttons may wear out but only time will tell on that one. The plastic finish seems to be durable. I have definitely bumped it against more than a few different materials and still no scratches.

The interface is snappy and I have never experienced any slowdowns or issues with it. I like that it has a workout mode that will monitor my heart rate and time of workout. I have found the heart rate monitor is not accurate. It may be interference with my hairy arms. But it seems to use the same optical sensor design as other watches so hopefully it is software issue.

I wish the app had a little more functionality to it but you can use it with other apps. The app provides the user ability to change watch faces and adjust settings, but the same can be done on the watch itself. The music control is awesome. My car stereo is on the backseat floor so I have to connect to bluetooth. With the watch I can leave me phone in my pocket and quickly open up the music control, play/pause, skip/rewind, and control volume without taking my eyes off the road.

Battery life for me has been around 5-6 days before I charge it depending on how much I use it. I think last time I charged it is was the 6th day and I was at 30% battery. You will see about a 10% drop every day at least. Full charge takes a couple of hours.

I was not looking for something flashy to wear on my wrist so the e-ink screen is perfect. There is an adjustable back light that does help you read it at night. I have used the speech-text dictation as well and it has worked great. The menus are intuitive and allow lots of adjust-ability from the watch itself.

Overall I would definitely recommend this watch. I would never pay those stupid crazy prices for a watch that you have to charge everyday. This is a nice compromise if you can do without a color screen and it won't break the bank.

Review:
Coming from the original Pebble, the Ticwatch Pro 4G is the first android wear Smartwatch I have owned. I purchased the 4G variant as it also has more RAM and more resistance ratings (IP/MIL). I do not use the cellular connection, so I cannot comment on battery life and performance with that. With just wifi and Bluetooth, along with the Mixed-mode display (shows LCD when 'off' and OLED when I gesture to look at it), I can probably get up to 3 days of battery life as I usually end the first day with 75% or higher. Performance is good but it is not as fluid as the Apple Watch. The LCD is really useful as it means I can always see the time. As a student, it is also useful to have Essential Mode as I can still have a watch during tests that won't get me in trouble.

The Mobvoi app is lacking as it doesn't serve a great purpose. It would be far better if you could change the settings of the watch and manage apps from the app. It does showcase various health stats, so there is some use. I use Facer to find watch faces and I am glad to see that there are many watch faces to choose from. As for health, I usually use Google Fit over the included Tichealth but they are both equally good.

The build of the device is excellent. The watch has a premium metal feel and is made with Gorilla Glass 3, another difference from the wifi only Ticwatch Pro. My only complaint is the band that is shipped with the watch. It seems a little to easy to stretch and seems like it wouldn't need to be pulled too hard for it the snap. I have had similar watches with bands of similar elasticity and they have all snapped within a few months of use. I may look into replacing the band in the future. The charging dock and watch are both magnetic, which makes charging a breeze.

Overall, I am very happy with my purchase and enjoy the watch very much. I had my previous watch (Original Pebble) since 2015, and I can only hope that this watch can last just as long or even longer.

Review:
UPDATE 11/14/2020: Over 10 months of owning this watch. It is generally great. The biggest gripe I have is the touchy charging. Half of the time when I put it on the charge (original charger I got with the watch), it doesn't even register that it's on a charger. Far less frequently, it starts charging but stops later and I return to find a dead watch. I also wish the watch had some kind of warning notification when the battery is low, because once it switches to LCD-only mode, I struggle to get it to turn back on or to charge.

When the battery isn't an issue, though, the watch is great. I contemplated "upgrading" to the new TicWatch, but there's no LTE version yet. I'll likely wait another generation to see what happens with Wear OS.

UPDATE 1/10/2020: I've had the watch now for almost two weeks now. I've gotten quite used to it. I usually wait until it's below 20% or so to charge it, and it takes about 1.5 hours to charge. I typically do this while I'm working so I don't mess up my step count. I really don't understand why people complain about battery life. I don't have to charge the watch any more often than I expect (I get about 1.5 days of HEAVY use out of it). This includes daily walks tracked using Google Fit, lots of music controls, and lots of notifications and messaging.

Here are my other observations so far:

-WEAR OS - still not was good as iOS, it's getting better. There aren't a lot of apps, but the ones that do exist work well (Maps, Messages, Fit, Keep, etc.) all work well. Just don't expect many non-Google apps. Tasker does have an extension for Wear OS, but I haven't explored it yet - that could offer a ton of additional possibilities.

-GOOGLE ASSISTANT - this is the game-changer here. Anything you can do with Assistant, you can now do on your watch! I can control my smart home devices, including Sonos, SmartThings, Harmony, August, etc. Like Tasker, the possibilities are endless. Related to this, the voice recognition on the watch is AMAZING. The best I've seen, ever. When I respond to text messages using voice, it has a 99.9% success rate, including punctuation!

-TIC SLEEP - I didn't realize that this is a separate app you need to install on the watch. Once I did, it works great. No issues detecting sleep or wake up. There are two areas for future improvement: (1) Google Fit integration (Mobvoi confirmed to me in an email that they're working on this); and (2) the smart/gentle alarm function (it just doesn't work as they describe; in the same email, Mobvoi said they will look into this).

-CUSTOMIZING - don't be afraid to search Google Play if there's something about the watch you don't like. I had two instances of this: (1) the screen timeout was too fast for me, so I downloaded an app (StayLit Wear) that keeps the screen on until I lower my wrist; and (2) I wanted to track my watch battery while it charges, so I found another app (Wear Battery Full) that does that (it also sends a notification when it's full, so you can get it back on your wrist). Neither of these are Mobvoi issues; they're deficiencies with Wear OS - but easily solved.

ORIG REVIEW: Great watch. I'm a prior Samsung Gear Watch user. Wear OS is great, if only because of Google Fit and Google Assistant. Fit is awesomely simple. And Assistant can do it all. So much better than Bixby. Essential mode is cool too. I use that 99.9% of the time with wrist rotation off. Couldn't be happier with a smart watch. The Apple Watch is superior, sure. But for Android users this is pretty darn good!

Review:
This is a great watch. I bought it refurbished, and it looks/performs like new. I did a lot of research on running/GPS/smart watches prior to purchase and it can be daunting trying to figure out what to buy. The main points on this watch are:
1. This is NOT the latest and greatest from Garmin. This is good and bad. The watch is a known commodity and there are tons of reviews out there. It is also a good value refurbished. It is not flashy like smart watches.
2. This is a SERIOUS running watch and the Garmin app is awesome. You will get an overload of information, though. If you are looking for something simple for a casual runner or just activity tracking (steps and whatnot), this is total overkill and not cheap.
3. It has real buttons! Again, this is geared toward fitness, and as such, Garmin considered the fact that people get sweaty during these activities. Buttons are ideal for sweaty hands. I am so used to touch screens nowadays, but the buttons are laid out in a super logical manner and can control everything. This is especially important if you need to interact with it on the move (no need to look down at the screen and swipe). I see this as a big plus, although others may be over buttons in general.
4. Having music loaded on the watch is great. I HATE having to bring my phone (or anything else) on runs, so having the ability to store songs/playlists on the watch is great. It pairs easily to my wireless earbuds as well. However, if you don't mind hauling a phone around or prefer to stream, you might look at something else.
5. Lots of apps available for this watch. I also use it to track treadmill running (pretty accurate, surprisingly), and my HIIT workouts (OrangeTheory). It also has Garmin Pay, but I have not tried that out yet.

Hope this helps!

Review:
I have had my Gear Fit for just over a week now. I use it both as a smartwatch and as a fitness tracker. It does a fairly good job at both. I give it 4 stars because it's nearly perfect for what I use it for, but if you're looking for the ultimate smartwatch or the ultimate fitness tracker, you won't find that here.

First, some background: I use a wheelchair. Literal "steps" mean nothing to me. So I can't tell you if the pedometer is accurate. During the average day, the Gear Fit tells me I've done ~ 5,000 steps, if I go for a brief roll sometime during the day (in addition to my usual routine of going to work and coming home). So, that works for me as a relative measure, but if you're looking for something that will keep an accurate step-for-step count, you might be disappointed.

The Gear Fit has a heart rate monitor that distinguishes it from bands such as the Jawbone UP24. I use it mostly to tell me how hard I've been exercising (which for me consists of mostly lifting weights or rolling really fast). I've gotten pretty consistent results for that.

All of the fitness functions tie into S-Health, which is why you're going to want a Galaxy phone to pair the Fit with. (There's an open source alternative to the Gear Fit Manager so you can use it with other phones, but I can't say if that's going to give you the same experience.) Fortunately, S-Health is much improved from its earlier incarnations. Now you can track your sleep,and it pairs fairly painlessly with the Gear Fit. The one area where S-Health is lacking when compared to, say, the UP software for the Jawbone devices is, the food tracking is a bit more laborious. The S-Health software doesn't have the UP's ability to scan bar codes, so you're reduced to typing more than you'd be with another device.

The thing that really sets the Gear Fit apart form devices like the Jawbone UP devices and the FitBit is the screen. If you move from one of these bands to the Gear Fit, the first thing you'll notice is how much easier having a screen makes your life. You don't have to rely on vibration patterns or blinking lights. The information is right there on the screen to read.

The Gear Fit's screen is curved nicely, and it's easy to read indoors. Outdoors in bright sunlight, you have to crank up the brightness. (I use a 2 setting indoors, and a 4 or 5 outdoors.) But you have to stay cognizant of the conditions outside before you go out. If you have the screen set at 2 for brightness, good luck going out in bright sunlight and trying to adjust the brightness then. An ambient light sensor would really help in this regard, and I'm hoping it's added to the next version.

The Gear Fit Manager allows you to change the wallpaper, colors, and watch faces. There are a variety of watch faces to choose from, so you're almost guaranteed to find something you like. I do wish the home screen (where the clock resides) was a bit more customizable, though. In particular, you have to go to the Settings menu to see the battery level. It would be nice if you could have things like the battery level and the Bluetooth status on the home screen, so it was more accessible.

The Gear Fit Manager software allows you to send notifications from almost every app to your Gear Fit. You get a little vibration on your wrist, then you can swipe to the Notifications icon, tap on it, and see your notification. Keep in mind, though, that the more apps send notifications to your Gear Fit, the more of a hit the battery takes.

Speaking of the battery, you can probably expect 2-3 days of battery life with regular use. If there's one piece of advice I could give you, it's this: Turn the Bluetooth off when you're sleeping. That greatly improved my battery life, even when I used the watch to track my sleep.

The Gear Fit lets you view text messages and e-mails, but there's no software keyboard or voice control here, so you have to reply from your phone. In fact, most of the functions of the watch will involve your smartphone. That's not too different from most other smartwatches, but it would've been nice if Samsung had at least included a GPS so that you didn't need your phone for basic fitness activities like running and hiking.

All in all, I'm very happy with my Gear Fit. It does everything I want, and I paid a reasonable price for it.

Enjoy!

Review:
Got this as a new refurb product. I already have Rustastic Orbit and there are some things I wish would be better, like not to have it charge every week and switch to night mode manually and back to day mode in the morning. So I decided to try out Vivofit 2. I thought Garmin is a bigger brand and so... Guess what, garbage GARMIN garbage. Sync does not work automatically( works manually), customer service is useless to say. They want me to have BT enabled all the time so I tried it. Sync still does not work and battery life goes down the drain. Then support want me to update my Android version to 4.4.1 because it's the latest version for my Verizon S3( Verizon has too many bugs in that version I refuse to upgrade). Then customer service claimed that 4.4.1 is the only version that supports BLE 4.0. I know for a fact that 4.3 has support BLE 4.0 and have other BLE 4.0 devices working just fine. Runtastic Orbit app does a very nice thing. It wakes up BT to see if it can sync up and when it's done goes back to sleep. Talking to customer service about it and their answer: "We don't want to control BT on your phone"

Auto Sleep mode was another feature I wanted, but its measures are wrong. Runtastic Orbit and the other app I tried on the phone shows 5-6 hours of deep sleep and 1-2 hours of light sleep, but Vivofit 2 shows the opposite. Response from customer service is everything is fine.

So an the end I have a not even half working device and I'm stuck with it. I don't write review, but I felt that other people should know about those problems and now Garmin has newer devised so they probably will drop support for this one. I also think that new devices have the same problem as they use the same sensors.

Buy it at your own risk, but when you will try to figure out why your battery does not last you know what to blame.

Review:
Read all the reviews before buying. Come to find out that some of the reviews are not for this particular watch. In any case, the watch is priced right (with the discount). Had a couple of similar watches in the past but this has been the best one so far. Come with one set of black silicon watch strap which are very easy to install. Instructions are not great but not bad either.

After I started using it, one of the buttons stopped working. The seller was very responsive and sent me a replacement watch.

PROS -
1. Very high resolution display. Pretty much the same as Samsung Galaxy watch and the look and feel is the same.
2. Great battery life, comes with a magnetic charging cable and not a micro-usb as shown in one of the reviews.
3. The heart rate sensor seems to be higher quality compared to similar watches that I had in the past but not from the same seller. The heart rate is very accurate and this is the only watch that comes close to the same reading as Gen 1 Apple Watch. The Blood Pressure monitor seems to be more of a gimmick as it is pretty much stuck at the same reading no matter what time of the day I measure it. I used my regular BP monitor with the pressure cuff and the watch is way off. I am sure this is something that can be fixed with a firmware update.
4. There are multiple fabulous looking watch dials of all types available. Very happy with the graphics. The watch comes with multiple built-in apps - Heart Rate, Blood pressure, Blood O2 level, stop watch, timer, find my watch, music control, weather etc. the apps can be set to be displayed in a list manner or in a circular manner similar to Apple Watch, very nice.

CONS -
1. I am using this watch that is paired with iPhone 13 Pro Max. I was never able to get the weather app to display any data.
2. The iOS watch app needs a lot of improvement. The labelling is grammatically incorrect.
3. Both heart rate and blood oxygen can be set to be measured throughout the day and display the trend but not the blood pressure. Then again the BP measurement is not accurate. I am sure that both can be fixed with firmware and app updates.
4. Another thing that I was not able to figure out is how to disable the phone call feature and retain only the phone notification feature. The instructions says to turn it off on the watch itself but I could not get it to work.

Overall great watch.

Review:
I've been using the watch primarily for fitness to track my runs, walks, and sleep.

This is a nice savvy watch for the cost. The watch is genuinely responsive.

The pedometer and pulse tracking is precise. Pulse is tacking in regular intervals that you can set in the app. When you tell the watch to track an activity, like running, the watch will constantly be tracking your pulse until you end the activity, after which you will be able to see all the information from your workout. I wish the app was a little more updated, and I really wish it had the ability to send data to Google Fit. That would be a great addition for the future.

I the ability control music, and see instant messages, and sync with other features of the phone is helpful. The IPX rating is magnificent which makes it an extraordinary exercise watch. Battery life is incredible. I don't usually wear the watch while not exercising, but I have managed to get away with charging the phone only 2-3 times in the time I've had it and it just doesn't die.

If you're looking for an affordable fitness tracker smart watch, look no further!

Review:
I've gotten 3 different $50 smart watches on here and returned the other two. There was always something wrong, but this is solid. The step tracking is very accurate, I haven't had any disconnect issues, the notifications actually tell you the name and the message which is really nice. It's very comfortable to wear all day. It does have a lot of options for watch faces. It says 200, but really it's more like 60 and then a red/blue/green version of each one. You also get 4 saved to the phone you can swap through, but the first 3 are fixed and cannot be changed so it's really only one slot for a custom face.

Battery life seems good. I've only had it for 3 days but I haven't even needed to unwrap the charger it comes with yet. It started on 88% and I'm down to 42% and that's with me keeping the screen on way longer than normal just playing with it AND with 24/7 heart rate monitoring.

The only reason I can't give it 5 stars is the UI is a little cumbersome. Personally I mostly wanted it for steps, weather, music control and notifications. I have to scroll through 9 apps every time to get to the music control and there's no quick way to get back to your last used app, so that gets a little annoying. Same with the weather being 8 swipes. I don't care about blood pressure but there's no way to remove that one or the others you don't want. But really, for $50 it's a fantastic fitness tracker with some light smartphone utility.

Review:
I have owned this watch for a couple weeks and its everything I expected and more. It definitely fits the niche of being a smart watch without looking ridiculous on your wrist. I have my paired to a Droid Turbo.
I will agree the app is not as polished as I would like, but it is functional. The display setup for MM/YY and YY/MM doesn't seem to be compatible with Android. No matter how many times I changed config. in the app and submitted the changes, it didn't change the phone. I had to change in the settings of the watch itself.
So far the only down side I see to the watch is explaining why you are looking at your watch for so long. Typically you look at a watch for the time for a split second and move on. The past couple days the watch has definitely created a lot of conversation.

Update:
-In order to keep the watch from disconnecting from my Android (Lollipop) phone, I had to setup the the watch as a trusted device under the SmartLock functionality. This setup means my phone remains unlocked and I don't need a pattern or code to open. A bit concerning if I was a person who was constantly misplacing his phone. I have found no other way to work around this.

-There were a couple days where the watch would shut off even though I had 30+% of battery life. For some reason this went away and the battery has been lasting until completely dead usually around 4-5 days.

-Tapping the glass to get the last 5 minutes worth of notifications requires and pretty strong tap. I don't know if this is intentional so as to prevent incidental requests for the prior notifications, but it feels weird having to tap the watch face so hard. It took me awhile to figure this out and if I hadn't, I would have assumed the watch was broken right out of the gate.

-At first I really wasn't sure about the wrist band as it is pretty bulky. My wrist got used to it after about two weeks and now I have come to like it. The style is a good combination of sport and professional.

-I have been trying to force my self in meetings and just generally throughout the day to leave my phone in my pocket or my work bag, so I use the watch to determine if my phone is needed. When I don't need my phone the watch is most useful because I get a buzz, I read the notification and move on. But if I pick up my phone to answer a text and continue to text back and forth, the watch doesn't realize that so I continue to get buzzes. This can be a bit annoying for a lengthy text convo.

-Overall I am extremely happy with my purchase and I hope Martian continues to make watches so I feel compelled to buy another model.

The following was posted months ago:

After using and comparing both units side by side, I decided to return the Nuvi 760 and keep the RoadMate 1470.

Here are what I like about the RoadMate 1470:

- Bigger, brighter, more colorful screen
- Very helpful lane assist (I live in Southern California, I see it works at all highway exits and interchanges here. I haven't tested it in other parts of the country yet). The Nuvi does not have this feature
- Bell/chime before each turn. The Nuvi does not have this
- Much more flexible routing options (Can exclude a maneuver or detour 3/5/9 miles from a route)
- Free lifetime traffic with the traffic link device(I got the traffic link for free). You have to pay about $40 a year for traffic with the Nuvi
- Useful Onetouch feature
- Much louder internal speaker
- Lighter, slimmer

Here are what I liked about the Nuvi 760:

- Faster re-routing/recalculation (Both units give very similar routes, though)
- More sensitive touch screen. The RoadMate screen sometimes takes a few presses to recognize/react (noticeably the back arrow button)
- Sturdier/more durable in build quality
- A little better text to speech (More voices to choose from and the voice quality sounds a little clearer/less synthesized). The 1470 has only one voice and the lady's voice sounds kinda nasal but very understandable though
- Faster processor, making the unit perform faster. The RoadMate seems sluggish sometimes

The Nuvi 760 has some other features like bluetooth, mp3 player, picture viewer, FM transmitter, but none of them work well enough to be useful. After having used the RoadMate for 3 weeks, I still like it better and think I made the right decision to keep it intead of the Nuvi 760.

I've received this watch a week ago to test out the features and battery lie in general. Let me tell you this has got to be one of the best first entry smart watches ( even if it was made in China), that has a few good features that are on it. Firstly, you can use this as a phone on its own; however, you will need a 2g sim in order for it to work, but you can make BT calls if you pair this with you phone (have it with you at all times) to make calls that way as well as messages. Secondly, you can pair your wireless headphones or pods with this watch as long you have music on your Micro SD card (yes you can insert a micro SD card to store pictures, music, and possibly videos). Thirdly, it has a anti lost feature to find your phones if lost. Fourthly, it has a pedometer to track your steps in a workout and as well as a sleep monitor to track your best quality of sleep. Fifthly, it has a sedentary function to remind you to get up at a certain time to make you be alert. Lastly, the battery life is great it can last in about 3 to 4 days as long as you keep the brightness down and use the less frequently to check time or playing music, but if your a heavy user, except it to last a day or 2, the standby time lasts about a week.

I would recommend this watch for everyone to start of with an entry level smartwatch before setting of buying a mid or high tier smartwatch. you get what you pay for.

Special Features: Smart Watch Bluetooth 5.0 Activity Tracker with Heart Rate Monitor Sleep Monitor Pedometer
Review: For starters, I should say that I have previously owned a Samsung Galaxy watch and, before that, a Fitbit Blaze. My Galaxy watch broke recently, and rather than spending another several hundred dollars, I came across this smart watch for only $39 ($32 with coupon) and figured I would give it a try. I have to say, I have been pretty impressed with this watch. It looks really nice, is lightweight and very wearable (I often forget I am wearing, unlike my heavy Galaxy watch), and has excellent battery life. I would estimate around a week of average usage on a single charge. I also really like the magnetic charging cable. Comparing TOZOs smart watch to my Galaxy watch wouldn’t seem a fair comparison, given the huge difference in cost as well as the enormous size of the Galaxy watch; of course, some features like ability to make phone calls or listen to music directly on the watch, or replying to messages through the watch, can not be done on this TOZO watch. However, I would say that in terms of functionality, the TOZO watch reminds me of an upgraded Fitbit blaze, and actually is very similar to the Fitbit Versa, which my wife owns. This smart watch has activity tracking, heart rate monitoring, sleep tracking, alarms, receiving and reading messages (text messages, emails, Whatsapp messages, etc.), weather, and all of the standard “smart watch” features that are expected on a smart watch. And all of these functions work really well. I also like the ability to take pictures with your phone remotely by tapping a button on watch. The watch interface is also very user friendly and easy to figure out. Overall, I would say this is a pretty solid smart watch that easily competes with other, more expensive smart watches. And for this price point, I would say that TOZO has come out with a fantastic value watch that is perfect for those new to smart watches, or those (such as myself) who want a smart watch but don’t want to break the bank. This watch is an excellent value for your money, and I give 2 thumbs up!
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: around a week of average usage on a single charge
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch that provides around a week of battery life on a single charge with average usage.

Review:
This a simple but interesting smart watch. It measures oxygen levels, heart rate and blood pressure, a feature that I was most interested in.
I got this as a gift (came in a really fancy box), I started to use it and exploring all features:
Blood Pressure: I am still comparing it with a wrist and arm bp monitors. In general the watch produces lower values, but the diastolic has been closer. It depends how it is measured, position of the hand and location of the watch (front of the wrist vs back).
Heart rate and oxygen are pretty accurate.
The watch comes with an app which is surprisingly good. It records sleep patterns very accurately, steps, and the other health features.
The watch connects quickly and easily to the app (iphone in my case). The watch rings when I get a call (regular phone call or Whatsapp), it can also control music played on the phone.
The watch also comes with a ‘sports’ feature that directs types of exercises and monitors them and time them as well.
Other features include a ‘relax’ application where the watch guides you through relaxation exercises.
This is also supposed to be waterproof, but I have not tried it under the water yet.
I’ve been using this watch for about 20 days and I have charges it only once!
Finally, it has a couple options for home screen.
Overall, this is a watch that I’d recommend and would gift it to relatives and friends.

Review:
I got this watch because I was looking for a smart watch that looked like a simple regular watch but had all features of a smart one.
As user of iPhone, I was a bit skeptical on compatibility; however the features work well (for my use). The con is that you must have the app open at your iPhone at ALL times so it can be synchronized and you get the alerts on your watch. Also, there are some limitations like not being able to reply to messages, answer call, use the Apple health app... but they are not a big deal to me.
Having said that, few points I wanted to highlight:
- Battery Life: if you have the screen on most of the time, you'll get a day worth of battery for sure. Maximum I could stretch with this battery was 2 days of regular use. Usually I charge it every night as my battery is showing 40-50% at the end of the day. I rarely used the Essential Mode but it is indeed a nice feature to have if you only care about checking the time, steps and date. Disabling the touch and tilt actions can extend the battery life, but I like these features, so I leave them on and compromise on charging my watch every night. I tested once how long it can run on full Essential Mode and it went for 5 days. So not too bad if you have a long holiday weekend and want to still use your watch... although I did this test only because I was curious how long it would last. I still prefer having all features on the watch...
- Build Quality: this is a very nice, solid build. I like the round shape as it look and feel like a regular watch. I often get people asking me about this watch and they are surprised on how cool it looks and how well it is build. I have small wrists but it fits very well. The charger is very nice, magnetic and snaps on holding the watch well.

Overall this is a great smartwatch, functional, and nice. It's not a small watch, but not too big (in my opinion).

Review:
[[VIDEOID:9b6258d2767bd2e6a0b2ab190bea9b07]] (Update) The sleep monitoring function didn’t work at first, but after updating and rebooting a few times it works now. Text messages and some notifications don’t display, but I think that has something to do with iMessage. Will update if anything changes. (End update)

You can disable “raise to wake” with the touch of a button (on screen). To me, that’s huge. Most of these smart watches will always turn on when you move your wrist. Not only does that kill the battery, but it’s really annoying when you’re trying to sleep and use the sleep monitor function, or in a movie theater, or in any situation like that. Not a problem with this watch.

The AMOLED screen is really bright and more awesome than I expected. I’ll never use a non AMOLED watch again. The difference is pretty noticeable. The screen is sharper, brighter, and looks just amazing. Non AMOLED watches now look like children’s toys to me. I’m using the blue digital face with black background and it’s so bright and sharp it looks really high tech, or like something out of TRON. There is also an AOD option, or always on display. You can choose either a white on black digital or clock face time display that will always stay on, also showing date and step count. It’s simpler and a little dimmer, so doesn’t eat as much battery, but still lowers standby time a little. So far it hasn’t been too noticeable a difference. You can even set a start and end time for it to activate. If you turn off the “raise to wake” function, it pretty much becomes like a normal watch, until you turn it on via the side button. Sweet.

As far as the accuracies of the apps, I can’t say. None of these smart watches are super accurate, but good enough to get a base line and compare health statistics day to day. This one has GPS so I’d imagine it’s a bit more accurate than others for certain things. Just be sure to turn on weather and choose notifications after set up. None of it is turned on by default. The phone app is easy to use and has more options than some others.

The rubber watch band is really smooth and comfortable, but not the best looking thing to me. Thankfully, most of the smart watches of this type (if not all) use the same type of bands, so I just switched it out with a metal one I had. There are spring loaded pins in them that you can pull open with a fingernail or small screwdriver. Changing them takes less than a minute.

So far, this is the only smart watch that does everything I want it to. On top of that, it has the AMOLED screen. The only downside I’ve seen so far is that it’s not capable of wireless charging, so my wireless charging stands are useless for this. But it does come with a magnetic contact pad charger which connects to USB, and I guess that’s good enough. Will update with any issues, but I think I’ve finally found a smart watch worth using.

(Note: watch band in video is not included)

Review:
I went through Lumoid.com to try 5 different fitness trackers before I sunk a lot of money into one of them. Here are the trackers that I "rented" for two weeks:

Fitbit Surge
Garmin Vivoactive
Fitbit Charge HR
Jawbone Up3
Garmin Vivosmart

After being able to put these items through their paces, and figure out which of any of them I wanted to buy, my decision was left between two excellent units. The Fitbit Surge and The Garmin Vivoactive. They both had strengths and weaknesses, but I ended up making the final decision to go with Garmin, and I am not disappointed. Here is why the decision was made:

Fit and Finish Quality - The Garmin won hands down for the most comfortable and usable tracker I tried. I have a small wrist, and this unit just feels right on my arm. The Fitbit Surge feel most substantial by far, but it was NOT comfortable. I also like that the Garmin Unit has rechargeable bands so I can change the look up if I want to.

Reliability and Accuracy - The both have ups and downs in this category, but I had a LOT of issues with my Fitbit Surge staying connected. It dropped connection a LOT, and then was difficult to fix. I never had this issue with the Garmin unit. The GPS was flawless on both of them, and it took very little time to get a gps lock on either device, even indoors. They both accurately counted my steps, calories, and sleep! I like the integration of the Garmin Unit much better with other aps on the market. Both had excellent battery life, about a week per charge, using the GPS once per day about an hour at a time. The Garmin unit will allow me to alter my step stride if I needed to, but I found the standard to be just fine. I could never figure out how to do that on the Fitbit.

Screen - Both have excellent screens, but I have to give the edge on readability to the Fitbit. Indoors is was much better, but outdoors they both reign supreme. I like that the Garmin unit does have an app store where I can download more watch faces, data fields, widgets, and apps. There are not a ton out there yet, but those that are available are great. The Garmin unit is much more customizable, but the Fitbit is much more simple to use. I do like the color screen on the Garmin Unit, as it does break up the monotony just a tad.

Companion App - Wow, they are both great! They both have strengths and weaknesses. I like that the Fitbit can track food and water right on their own app, but on the Garmin I have to use a companion application. That being said, there are more companion apps that are able to play nice with the Garmin Unit, giving me almost endless opportunities. I like that. The fitbit application is much more simple to navigate. The ONE issue I have with Garmins Application, is that sometimes you have to do a refresh several times for the application to update. No big deal, the information is there, it just takes a bit to get it where you want it! The Fitbit application is faster for syncing.

No matter which unit you decide on, they are both outstanding products. There is NO right or wrong here, it is what works best for you. I found the Garmin Unit to be a more reliable option as a whole. I highly recommend that for $25 you to go www.lumoid.com and rent up to 5 different trackers to try for 2 weeks. This way you can get a better sense of which one works better for you. Let me know if you have questions, I'm happy to help!

I especially enjoy the built-in fitness, activity, and sports tracking features which have already proven to be invaluable while training at the gym as well as general health monitoring during my activity throughout the day.

Review:
This is my first smart watch, so please keep this in mind as you read my review. I connected this watch to my Google Pixel 4a (Android OS). Compiled pros and cons are the end.

Back in the days when phones were becoming more than phones, there was a general type of phone called a 'feature phone.' It came pre-loaded with a variety of popular apps. But you were locked in to those apps by the firmware. You could not add or remove any of them. Such is the case with with this smart watch.

Set-up was straight forward. It requires installation of its own app, 'DaFit', from the Google Play store. The watch started up indicating about 70% charge, which is appropriate for storage. At my rate of usage, from a full charge, I found the watch easily maintained a charge for more than 24 hours. While the app indicates the watch is connected after set-up, I recommend reviewing its presence and confirming settings in your phone's Bluetooth connected devices panel, too.

The straps came unattached to the watch, but thanks to a small post on the spring-loaded pins, I was able to quickly attach them to the watch body...just have a decent fingernail available.

Like most smart watches I've seen, the form factor is a bit on the big size as a compromise to the amount of information it attempts to display on the watch face. My issue there with this watch, given its size, is the three control buttons which protrude significantly from the circumference of the watch. The correct orientation is to wear the watch with these control buttons pointing toward the wrist joint so the opposing hand can easily access them. However, because of their size they quickly begin irritating the base of your hand as your wrist flexes throughout the day, such as when typing on a keyboard (I'm a desk jockey so that is most of my day). I eventually turned the watch around moving it to my right arm (I'm left handed so this is actually preferred even though the control buttons are no longer in the correct orientation for access by my left hand). For right-handed users, this presents a problem as the preferred wrist to wear it on would be the left.However, keeping it on the left wrist but turning the watch 180 degrees so as to have the buttons away from the wrist joint would then cause the display to be upside down. A firmware upgrade allowing a 180 degree re-orientation of the display and generic assignable buttons could address this as well as the issue of left-handed users for whom button irritation as above is not an issue, but would want the buttons easily accessible while wearing it on their right arm.

I was eager to read my incoming text messages on the watch instead of pulling out my phone, unlocking it, and reading them there. The problem here, though, is that only those texts which were sent from a phone number displayed only as a number, and not in my named contacts would be sent to my watch (!). No matter how many times I had my family members (named contacts in my address book) send me text, they would never appear in the watch, but only in my phone's messaging app and phone notifications. This is a big fail!

I use Google Fit to track a lot of my health metrics and was pleased to see steps as measured by the watch could be sent to my Google Fit app. But, the watch also has the capability of measuring pulse, blood pressure (BP), and oxygen saturation (O2Sat). These measurement are not sent to Google Fit, and only the most recent seven are stored on the watch. With respect to blood pressure measurements: DON'T USE IT for any reason! Sure, any medical measurement from such a device should be treated with suspicion...but it can suggest you may want to take a blood pressure reading from a better quality, more reliable device such as a true sphygmomanometer. But, even at this level of credibility, this watch's BP measurement sensor is very inaccurate. I found the diastolic (upper reading) to be off by ~ -30 mmHg and the systolic to be off by ~ -10mmHg. This could easily give someone a false sense of security if they never had their BP checked by another device. I do have to track my BP and know what my reading should be. This watch's BP measurement was nowhere near what my true BP is. It's almost as if it picked two numbers at random and displayed them as my BP.

Overall, I think this smartwatch, even for the price, has the opportunity to be a bit smarter.

Pros
>Pliable soft silicone strap provides for secure but comfortable fit
>Maintains a charge for more than 24 hours of average use
>Good screen resolution and brightness (adjustable)
>Good sensor touch on screen
>A decent variety of interchangeable watch faces including one which allows you to import your own graphic/image as the backdrop
>Step counter integrates with Google Fit

Cons
>Wired proprietary charger. If it breaks or you lose it, too bad. A USB charging cable isn't a viable replacement (recommend re-design for wireless charging)
>Very inaccurate BP measurement to the point of being worthless
>While it measures steps, pulse, blood pressure, and O2 saturation, it only sends step metrics to Google Fit, even though Google Fit can receive all of these data points.
>For text messages, only those which display as numbers in your phone's texting app, i.e. those not in your contacts/address book, appear on the watch. Text messages from your named contacts for some odd reason are not sent to the watch.
>Accessing the panels to review pulse, BP, and O2 saturation also initiate a new measurement. This is not needed, especially since you may only want to review passed measurements, not necessarily obtain a new measurement. Recommending designing a start button on these panels to initiate a new measurement.
>Because of the form factor, the side of the watch with the control buttons needs to point away from the wrist to avoid irritation which detracts from the ability of the opposing hand to properly access those buttons.
>Weather data does not appear at times with a message "Please check Bluetooth connection" even though Bluetooth connection is confirmed as text messages noted above and other notifications appear.

Review:
I purchased this watch with the intent of gifting it to my girlfriend for her birthday. I have not yet given it to her, but I know I don’t have to to know she will be impressed.

Garmin watches are top quality, and well worth the buy. I myself own a Garmin Fénix 5x, and love it. I’ve never owned a more technologically advanced, durable, long lasting watch. This Vivoactive 3 is a bit different from mine, but it still has a multitude of amazing features, that might even beat mine!

The watch is beautifully crafted, and can be worn on all occasions. Whether it be at the gym, at the beach, or even at an extravagant event. It’s design is meant to be casual, but with that little extra flare that makes people say “wow, nice watch!”.

Like I said before it has a multitude of features, that are extremely convenient, and you will end up appreciating more than you know later on. If you’re an active person, being able to track your runs, trace routes, see how many strokes it takes on a paddle board to get you 100 meters, and more, makes working out even better than before. It helps you retain your progress, and strive to to better. It promotes over all better body health. It tracks your steps, heartbeat, and more, which helps you keep note on your activity, and helps remind you to go and get out there if you’re not meeting your goals.

Also, besides all of the neat sports features, it’s extremely tech! I used to laugh at people with Apple watches because they constantly checked their wrists for texts, but now that I own a smart watch, I completely understand! (Btw I’m not comparing these to apple watches, because Garmin blows them out of the water, but well get to that in the next paragraph.) it’s extremely continent to be able to have your phone somewhere else, and be able to quickly check texts or answer calls. Not only that, but have you ever been in the middle of a good workout, and a song you don’t like comes on, and you have to get up to change the song on your phone? Well you can do that from your watch without moving. Like I said, you’ll appreciate this watch way more than you know.

Now, when it comes to competing brands, garmin in for the Win. If you have an IPhone, no need to fear, Garmin is 100% compatible. Not to hate on Apple watches, but don’t buy an apple watch just because you have an iPhone, and for a few reasons. First being that Apple doesn’t have the extensive list of features garmin does. Secondly, apple watches die after 3 days, and take almost a whole night to charge. I can wear my garmin for a week and some change, then charge it in an hour and a half. It’s insane. And finally, apple watches just don’t have that extra pop that Garmins do. The square body shape doesn’t compare to the look a garmin gives off. Garmin watches are almost like ford raptors. Yeah it’s a truck and you can work in it and get it dirty, but at the end of the day it’s a $100k truck that’s built for racing.

All in all, I think Garmin Watches are top notch, and there’s nothing else like them. It’s well worth the buy, and you won’t regret it.

I used the activity tracking features for daily 2-3 mile walks, weekend hikes, etc. The other features, like graphs of my heart rate for the past four hours, as well as weather, calendar, and notifications are also great to quickly check on things without having to go find my phone.

The display, of course, is much larger than the vivosmart HR. The flexibility to add, change, and customize widgets, watch faces, and data fields is very nice. Review:
Ok, here we go.

I bought this product because I wanted a Smart Watch, leaning toward the side of an Activity Tracker. To clarify: I wanted more Activity tracking features than smart watch features. This eliminates the Moto 360 and Apple Watch.

I bought the version with the chest band, because as I said before, I wanted the activity tracking abilities and the Chest Band Heart Rate sensor is the best way to get this data accurately. There are a lot of flaws with the optical sensor (again, Apple Watch), so this method is much more reliable. Do the research, you'll find it pretty easily.

I love that there is a GPS in this watch. It's so cool the turn that on and go for a walk around the woods. It tracks all the data and sends it over to your phone and computer for easy read-outs. Same goes for running. I have yet gone for an outside run with this guy yet, but when I run on the treadmill, it tracks the data with the heart rate monitor really well. It shows all this data POST-workout and lets me break it down. I bought this one because I wanted this data, and it delivers.

You can customize the watch faces with some that are built in the "store". There are some nice ones, and a lot of ugly ones. Total preference. I like a clean look when I'm not working out. Simple - time and date.

The vibration notifications are a great feature. It's how the Alarm works and how it alerts you to important notifications from your watch (mile markers, etc). You can also have it reflect EVERY notification from your phone, which is good and bad. First off, it's good because I don't have to get my massive iPhone 6+ out of my pocket to see (and clear) notifications. It's main drawback is now you have to be very particular about which notifications you receive - because they are going to all show up on your wrist. Recently I subscribed to NHL GameCenter Live and now I get game scores from my fav. team on my wrist. Heck yes! You can also pick up or decline calls from your phone. It's a simple feature that allows you to decline a call from your pocket or pick it up (if it's been ringing) from your pocket - so when you take your phone out, you are already on the call. I use this feature more then I thought I would and when I figured out that accepting the call via watch was useful, I use it all the time. Also be advised that if you let your notifications build up - they will ALL be on the watch until you clear them. It really forces you to be more intentional about your notification phone features.

The pedometer is a great added feature. You set your daily goal and I am obsessive enough to try and get as close as I can to it every day. It's a fun challenge and, to be honest, I use the restrooms in a different building now so I get a few more steps in drive the number up.

The Move Bar is going to drive you nuts. It is a suggestion for when you've been sitting too long - and it's a helpful reminder to walk around. But it doesn't clear very fast - so you need to walk about 1000 paces just to get it to clear. That's all well and good - except if you work a desk job or drive a lot. Last week I was on a trip in my car and every 20 minutes my watch insisted that I was sitting too long - which was true, but there was nothing I could do about it. Aren't you a SmartWatch? Aren't you connected to my phone? Couldn't you detect when I was traveling at 70mph and realize that maybe I was in a car and moving around for 1000 paces was not an option? Maybe future gens of this watch will be a bit more smart.

Also, this thing..... it disconnects the bluetooth sometimes, and then won't reconnect without a restart. I restart her about once every other day. This is truly the bummer about this watch. I restart her way too much. Way too much, Garmin. Once she powers back on, she's ready for anything. One star off of this review for this problem.

The battery: Good Stuff. This thing lasts a long time and in full operating mode (GPS, Heart Sensor, etc) it can go for 10 HOURS. That beats the Apple Watch any day of the week (which doesn't have a GPS). Without the GPS and Heart Sensor, I've for 5 days of heavy Notification use. Love the battery. Charging it is also amazing. I have the magnetic holder attached to my desk at work. I come in (about) once a week and rest it on there for a few hours. Back to 100% and I'm good for another 5-7 days. Oh yeah, and you don't have to have a phone to own this watch. It can work independently (so SUCK IT, Apple Watch) - although the SmartWatch features are great when paired to a smart phone.

Be warned: I did take this thing home on a weekend (friday night) at 30% and thought "Aw man, I'm good til Monday morning." Saturday afternoon, she was completely dead. Had to come back to work on the weekend and get the charger. Once you get down to the bottom of the battery, she dies FAST.

The screen is surprising on this one. By the photos you think it's going to be "meh" but actually, in the sun, car, shade, night, living room, restaurant she holds up and I can always read the screen - and one touch of the button lights up the display - bright! I am kind of happy about it. I don't need an Apple Watch screen on my wrist with this thing. It's just a better use of the tiny battery in a Smartwatch.

The interface is a bit clunky. I find that I have to swipe once to "wake" the watch up to touch, and then the second swipe works. Not a deal breaker - not a star taker, just.... meh. There is no clicking or vibration with button presses, so it feels a bit... empty.

Speaking of feel - this thing is LIGHT. I really like that about it. It takes no energy to wear this watch - even with it's larger size. It's super light and feels like a floor model when you first pick it up - but it's on purpose because you don't want added weight when working out and exercising.

About that size: fits me perfectly as a 6 ft., 170 lb male, but for a smaller female, it might feel bulky. Just throwing it out there - you get to decide in the end.

About that exercising comment: I really like the run/treadmill/walk/cycling feature. It's just cool to have a GPS on board and get all that data in conjunction with the heart rate monitor. The heart monitor feature can also work with several other 3rd party monitors, so if you already have one, see if you can pair it with the watch. Might save you some cash.

The thing the activity module lacks is a dedicated activity for Cardio or Weight Lifting. There is no non-pace option until you run the exercise and then manually change it online. This is a needed feature that needs to be added quickly. Gym goers would benefit from owning this watch, but it needs to natively show and display this data for these activities before it makes sense for them to purchase this watch. Calorie burn, timer, time, rep time. Seriously Garmin, this needs to happen. I would give you a star on this review if it did this.

Overall, I really love this watch. Yesterday I forgot it at home and when I got to work, I checked my wrist (what must have been) 1000 times for my steps and the time. I felt naked. No joke. It's become a part of my life and I really enjoy it. If you're looking for an Activity Tracker with SmartWatch features, get this one - and don't go cheap - get the Heart Rate Monitor. You'll be happy you did.

**UPDATE - 7/25/16 - FYI - The little rubber clasp is pretty cheap on the watch band. This past weekend, it broke. I have had to order an entire new band to replace it. Extra ten bucks - and the watch band apparently lasts for a year and a half. Not a deal breaker - still really love this watch.
