Review:
*Edit* After further use of the watch and interactions with Customer Support I have lowered my previous 2 star review to 1 star. None of the issues I brought up in the first review have been fixed or addressed, nor has any timetable for addressing them been put forward, and I have found several additional problems.
-The "create workout" feature is extremely buggy. Fails to save certain exercises with no clear reason why.
-When in a strength workout, the watch will sometimes skip ahead to the next set and jump over the "rest" phase when the set is ended.
-The watch sometimes freezes up, often in relation to the broadcasting HR problems. This requires that the watch be reset, which looses ALL data. I have lost several hours of data this way.
-The VO2 Max feature, though nice, often fails to work. I have run several times, outside, for 2 or more miles, with the watch correctly recording the run and then come home to find that the VO2 Max (which is supposed to automatically calculate for every run over 10 minutes) fails to appear. The cycling VO2 Max has never shown to date, though due to the weather, I have far fewer cycling workouts.
-When running, I find that the watch often scrolls to the bottom set of "fields" while running and needs to be constantly swiped back up to the top. This is probably due to the sensor on the side of the watch which allows swiping w/o touching the screen. I havn't yet found a way to disable this.
Realistically, this is a 1 star product for the $199 I paid for it over the Holiday season. If I had paid the $299 MSRP I would be feel like I had been had. The fact that Garmin is trying to pass this off as a comparable product to the Fitbit Ionic seems increasing absurd.

*Original Review Below*
The last three Vivoactive models have all been the same story: great promise and impressive hardware undone by poorly designed shockingly untested software. This model continues that story line to a T.

I bout this model because all the 'expert' reviews said that Garmin had FINALLY got it right. They rejoiced that at last Garmin's software platform had caught up to Fitbit's. Well, to say it hasn't is really underselling it. There are certainly some pros in this model:

Pros:
-The stress tracking feature is a great touch and matches my physical experience well.
-The concept behind strength workout rep tracking and VO2 Max estimation are great (neither works more or less at all though).
-After a recent patch the HR measurements now seem to accurately match my actual heartrate.
-Swim tracking appears to be more accurate in this model, though still, baffelingly, they refuse to support open water swimming except for their $600 model.

However, the cons are numerous and unpardonable:

Cons:
-The "broadcast HR" feature which allows the watch to connect with other apps such as Trainer road will frequently cause the watch UI to crash and completely brick the watch for up to 20 minutes or more. I contacted support a month ago, was first rudely told "its probably the app your connecting to the watch shouldn't do that." I, as politely as I could manage, told the guy exactly how stupid that was and to pass me on to someone who knew how to open a ticket. That person bothered to take 5 seconds to look into it, discovered it was a known issue with "many others" having the same problem. I sent him a zip file of my logs along with descriptions of all the devices I had connected to (the watch crashed when used with all of them) he then added me to group ticket and then stopped responding to my messages.
-As seen above, customer support has improved very slightly since the original model, but is still poor.
-Many new features don't work. Including "Automatic rep counting" for strength exercises. It will count correctly about 1 in 4 sets. Also, in premade workouts there is a bug where all weights display in kg even if you input them in lbs and every setting on the watch is set to lbs. The issue goes back to the device's release and there are dozens of forum posts related to it. So far I've seen one Garmin rep's response indicating that a patch would be applied "soon." So far no dice.

In short, the device has good hardware, great ambitions and the start of great features, however, once again it feels like Garmin rushed breakneck to market to try and catch up with the far more polished Fitbit Ionic and didn't bother to fully alpha (much less beta) test much of the new features. Despite matching the Fitbit price Garmin once again is the sever underdog at this price point, which grows increasingly disheartening year after year. Though it has cornered a much larger share of the serious amateur and professional athlete sphere of users the company seems disinterested with bringing their superior high end capabilities down to mass market products.

BOTTOM LINE: Despite 'expert' reviews which claim the Vivoactive 3 and Fitbit Ionic are closely matched products which are nearly interchangeable aside from a few small differences real uses such as those on Amazon have rated the Ionic a full star ahead of the Vivoactive 3 and it's not hard to see why.

Review:
Physically, the watch is a dead ringer for an Apple Watch, specifically one of the later models where the watch’s height is about 120% of the watch’s width. It may be that this watch’s best use case is when it is used to convince someone you have an Apple Watch when you really don't. At the surface, the displays are very similar to Apple’s. Once you drill into a menu, on even one level, it becomes very clear this is not Apple’s software nor is it all that easy to interpret and use.

As I removed the watch from the box, I noticed that we were off to an interesting start: the watch had recorded 19K steps before I ever put it on. I thought little of it, after all, it probably bounced around in the back of the mail truck. Two hours later, I became a little more concerned when I noticed that my step count was up to a total of 69K steps that day, despite no exercise and never leaving the house. Whoops.

The included charger is USB-A only. If you use USB-C, you’re out of luck. The charger fits the back of the watch only one way (don’t worry, the magnets will tell you when you’re wrong) but there’s really no way to keep the charger/watch pair up and out of the way when it’s charging. The magnets aren’t all that strong so one bump is frequently enough to disconnect the charger.

The manufacturer’s instructions aren’t that well written. Don’t get me wrong, their English is better than my Chinese, but it may have been worthwhile to have a native English speaker edit the instructions. Though the app is far from intuitive, pairing the watch with my iPhone was pretty easy. When you’re setting it up, make sure you know your metric measurements (i.e., height in cm, your weight in kg, and your stride length in cm.) There is no option for English measurements, at least not one that I've found.

Claims and Realities: The listing claims that “There are 150+ different watch face styles to choose in the App.” I found exactly five watch face styles from which to choose. The list says the watch’s style is 经典的. I had no idea what that meant so I ran it through Google Translate. It means “classic.” OK, if you say so.

It is, at minimum, water resistant. I wore it in the shower with no problems. What was interesting was that every time water from the shower hit the face, it turned on the screen. So it can’t tell the difference between water and a finger.

It’s default is to turn the screen on when you make a sharp movement with your arm - as if you were pulling your arm towards you to look at your watch. OK, that makes sense. The watch also claims to be able to track your sleep and it does a sort of OK job at that. So why can’t it take that information and figure out that, when I’m asleep, I don’t want it to turn on the screen every time I rollover in bed? That screen is brighter than the sun. It probably woke my next door neighbors. If there’s a way to change that, I haven’t found it. Now I’m wondering how much longer I will be permitted to wear it in bed, the only place where it could possibly generate sleep data.

I haven’t seen it share any data with Apple Health even though I have that selected. The app gets a little pushy if you don’t select track me all the time. I get it - it wants to be able to gather your distance information all the time - but I just don’t want to be tracked all the time, particularly when I have no idea what they do with the data.

I compared its heart rate measurements to those I got from a Polar H10 Heart Rate Monitor connected to an iPhone. The results were comparable even though they use different technologies and at both normal and exercise heart rates. I’m always suspicious of the Blood Oxygen Monitor (SpO2) measurements on devices like this; SpO2 isn’t like heart rate where it varies from person to person and from time to time and activity throughout the day. If you just programmed the device to always read 98% or 99%, you’d be right 95%+ of the time. I’d love to know how this reads with someone with sub-normal SpO2. I just don’t know anyone for whom that's true. I saw someone asked about getting blood pressure readings. This watch cannot do that, nor can any other watches I’ve seen. Generating a blood pressure reading requires measuring the pressure required to physically collapse an artery. Unless this watch can contract and expand at will and measure that pressure, it’s not going to be able to do that.

As I scan Amazon, it appears there are many different “brands” of the same exact watch. This one is HuaWise but the Zuoee, Motast, Pautios, Ordtop, and others all look exactly the same, both hardware and software. OK, the Ordtop and Motast have a button instead of a dial. But it’s not like the dial on the HuaWise actually spins - it’s just a dial-looking button.

So, in the end, it's a cheap Apple Watch clone that will fool everyone except the owner.

UPDATE: Two new additional Gripes.
1. While the watch's step count seems correct, the watch's step counter drives my iPhone step counter insane, adding as many as 370,000 steps within a single hour. This started as soon as I connected the watch and phone via Bluetooth. As soon as I had my iPhone "forget" the watch, behavior ceased. Connected again, it resumes; forgot again, it stops.
2. The watch prevents me from using my iPhone to start my car. I can unlock the car and remotely control the car's AC, windows, etc. just fine but if I want to drive the car, I have to pull the key card out of my wallet and put it on the center console.
These two gripes were too much for me. I've "retired" the watch.

Review:
First off let me say if you have small wrists your options of smart watches that wont look like you taped a go pro to your wrist are limited. This brought me to finally find the pebble time round, after it flew under my radar for awhile. Lets get right into it.

Pros:
-Small discrete size (you can't tell its a smart watch honestly) + Thinness
- Notifications are fast and work for virtually every notification on the phone (skype, snapchat, amazon, email, you name it)
- Very fast interface, with a click of the button you can view notifications, the weather, check your steps, sleep and more
-HUNDREDS of free watch faces and apps. It is actually overwhelming to search through the pebble app

Cons:
-Only "water resistant" basically don't go swimming and be careful around water. Not a deal breaker but a real con.
- 2 day battery life. This is by no means bad, but its not great either. The good news is a full charge is only about 20 minutes away.
-The thinness causes it to feel somewhat delicate to me. It has gorilla glass but I can't shake the feeling i'll bump it on something and break it.
- Yes I must say it, Pebble as a company is no more. For the $70 I paid it doesn't matter. All notifications should still function, a third party app store is being developed and the watch will still function 90%+. \

The Verdict:
-Buy it now! Seriously you won't find a better smart watch for this price. If you want more of a fitness element go for the pebble 2 or the pebble 2 HR. This watch is for casual daily wear in my mind. I wouldn't take it to the gym.

Review:
Nice looking basic smart watch for everyday use. It has some nice additions which can be useful and except some frustrating things, if you do not mind lack of GPS, it has a lot of things to offer for the price tag ~$30 (with discount).

Design: 5/5 It seems very similar to Apple Watch or Amazfit. Looks nice and feels good, screen has vivid colors. Strap feels also comfortable and no complains regarding this.

Sensors: 3/5 I tried to compare it with different devices. HR is usually within "acceptable" range, however, you get some strange spikes and even while you enable "continuous" it just record every few mins and you have to have HR widget on screen to show you the actual (or start the activity). SpO2, in general, every watch nowadays is not perfect in these two things. But several times I got 100%, while other device gives me average 96%. Maybe if you play more with that you will get more accurate results. Still, it is not a medical device so it's ok. But for the price tag more as 4/5 here.

Functionality: 4/5
You get touchscreen, HR, SpO2, Breathing/Stress de-load Widget, Weather Widget, Music controls, Customizable watch face, recording of the several different activities (but you need phone with screen ON for external GPS and activity on), alarm, stopwatch, and some others.
App. is also quite nice and easy to use (I would say even easier than watch itself).

Cons: The biggest downsize for me is a lack of GPS which is causing that when you record activity using your phone you have to have screen ON! During running or biking it is always an issue as often touch can cause stop the activity. It happened to me during both run and bike and it is extra frustrating. It could basically work just as strava - you can close the screen and it is running in the background on the phone. Second thing is the heart rate widget/monitoring. You enable continuous, but you get once in a 7-10 mins metrics recording at best. To see actual you have to have activity on or open the widget and wait - here I often reported that watch was unable to show it, so I have to open widget again.

Pros: However, honestly, for the price tag you cannot expect to get everything or to work at its best. So I would say it is worthy for the price as it looks really nice, you have a lot of additional functions and App. is quite good, and you can customize your watch face as you like.

Overall: 4/5

Review:
I personally am a big fan of the Garmin Fenix line. I started with the Fenix 2, moved on to the Fenix 3HR, then the Fenix 5X, and now the Fenix 5X Plus. Although the Fenix is a great line of watches, they are pretty costly and they're also a heavy watch. Those two factors make me wary of using them as my everyday watch, despite being very rugged.

So instead, I was looking for a "daily driver" watch that had a subset of the features of the Fenix line that I wanted to use every day. Things like, heart rate monitoring, activity tracking, on the fly workout if I didn't have my full kit (I also have a heart strap and run dynamics with my Fenix), etc.

The Garmin Instinct has all that and more! If I were to compare, it's very close to the Garmin Fenix 2 with the monochrome screen, very long battery (a feature I miss in the newer Fenix models), workout tracking, etc with the added plus of the heart rate monitoring and sleep tracking. Although the sleep tracking isn't as good as some other brands I've used, it's usable as a rough analysis. The Instinct doesn't support run dynamics like the new Fenixes do but neither did the older Fenix 2 so it's not a huge loss.

In in all, the Garmin Instinct is an all around great watch for those not looking for the Fenix price tag but want many of the Fenix features, particularly outdoor. Even though it's polymer instead of metal, it's also still a rugged watch and also lighter than the Fenix. I highly recommend this as a starter Garmin watch over the VivoActive or the ForeRunner (unless you are primarily a runner).

I have ambient mode on all the time with the gestures off and that doesn't use much battery, plus I can turn off the watch screen at any time.

Review:
This was a pretty good smartwatch a month ago. It has become a fantastic smartwatch with the most recent update that improved the health tracking accuracy and, perhaps more importantly, added the ability to reply to SMS-notifications through the watch's integrated mic. This is a killer feature.

Pros:
- Unparalleled battery life (7-10 days) in an always-on smartwatch with good basic health tracking (steps, sleep).
- Feels/looks premium (relative to the regular Time). Although the design is actually quite different I've had mine mistaken for the Apple Watch on a few occasions.
- 5ATM Water resistance.

Cons:
- The bezel is huge. You stop noticing it eventually... but, yeah...
- The new color e-ink display offers poor contrast by comparison to the Pebble Classic. The display is significantly harder to read in office conditions. Looks great outdoors (more sun the better, which is the opposite of most smartwatches) and in low light where the back-light is triggered more often. I've found that using a light background with dark text helps... but this also emphasizes the bezel size. Lose/Lose

Very minor cons:
- Face scratches easily (not unusual in watches... in general). Get a screen protector. No-brainer $10 investment for a $200 watch.
- Less than stellar leather band. I like the style of the band (simple, elegant, soft) but something about it (material, color, not sure) makes it look... dirty/gross after a few weeks of wear. I'll be looking for something better.

They had not seen the error before. I deleted a recently added fitness app and the error went away. Some type of Bluetooth interference with their app.

I have had it for about 6 weeks now. I picked this one for its ability to be in a pool. Even though this is advertised as being able to swim with, it does NOT track swimming activity. I have to run the “Cardio” selection to get the activity credit. The mobile app has swimming as an activity but the devise will does not track it. I wish that was more clear in the description. When speaking to Garmin they said the next model up has that ability.

It am not able to get it to connect to GPS yet. It gives the option to but it wont connect.

The band developed a small rip this week. The rubber material is very comfortable and there is just enough stretch in it for comfort. A drop of super glue seems to be working.

I do not wear this for sleep for I can not report on that function.

I compared the pulse rate to a monitor during a recent doctor visit. It was spot on.

Easy to use once familiar with the order and location of things.

Review:
I bought two A-TGTGA Y22 Pro smart watches, a black version for myself, and silver for my wife. The A-TGTGA watch is a bit big on my wife's 7 inch wrist but she still likes the watch and will keep it if I can solve the display issue. See below comments if you care about the display coming on when you don't want it to, resulting in how long the battery will last before it has to be charged up again because the watch dial lights up too much.
After using the watch for 8 days, I finally figured out how to control when the automatic lighting of the watch dial takes place. Select da fit app on your phone, at screen bottom, select the center icon. Scroll down to the "others" settings and tap it. Scroll to the Quick View setting, tap to turn it on and then select the time period you want the watch dial to light up with wrist action. I set mine and my wife's watch to one minute because we don't want the watch dial to automatically light.
Below my first review and attempt at solving the display issue.
This smart watch is packed with many nice features so it there is much to like about the Y22 Pro model. I did find it most annoying that the display constantly comes on with little wrist action and while turning over in bed because the display is bright in the dark bedroom. When "shake off" mode is selected in the "others" section of the watch, the watch display still comes on with wrist action.
When the watch "battery saver" is selected to on in the cell phone's Da Fit App, battery saver does not stay locked into position. When the Bluetooth connection between the watch and cell phone is lost, the battery saver mode switches back to normal again unless the battery is low on power.

Review:
Two weeks into my using the Forerunner 35 and I'm happy, especially after using the Garmin Vivosmart HR+, which had a number of design flaws and eventually, either a mechanical or software failure that resulted in its return to Garmin after 8 months.

The Forerunner 35's large, flat screen has much better readability than the Vivosmart. It is sleeker and doesn't get caught in sleeves and pockets like the Vivosmart did, offering more ergonomic sensibility.

Forerunner 35 has a battery level indicator on the main screen--no need to sort through a couple of menus to find out how much power you have left on this rechargeable device. The Forerunner 35's battery life is much better, too, I just ran it two weeks on one charge, while the Vivosmart HR+ barely made it six days before hitting the charger. Forerunner 35's normal watch-button controls are easy to use and much more reliable at navigating menus than the touchscreen that the Vivosmart HR+ had. The touchscreen is unfortunately subject to anything conductive brushing across it, even shower water...not a huge problem, but you have to navigate back to the homescreen. Not an issue with the Forerunner 35's button navigation...just like a digital watch should have.

The GPS connects quickly and accurately. The heart monitor is good and I haven't had any troubles with how it records activities like sleep...all good, there, too. I'm a runner, so the device has come in handy for training. It works well with Garmin Mobile Connect on my iPhone, keeping track of data.

I have to give a shout-out to Garmin's US-based support crew for helping me with the issues I had with Vivosmart HR+, even when they weren't able to find a quick fix for the watch, they remained in contact and were helpful and courteous. I decided to give Garmin devices another chance (buying the Forerunner 35) because their support team was nice...stuff like that is important to me.

I would recommend the Garmin Forerunner 35 comfortably with four stars. I'll update with any further observations or critiques.
Attribute_Related_Content:
Screen Size: large, flat screen
Battery Life: battery level indicator on the main screen, battery life is much better, ran it two weeks on one charge
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a large, flat screen. The customer needs the battery level indicator to be visible on the main screen. The customer needs the battery life to be much better, such as lasting two weeks on a single charge.

Special Features: Bluetooth
Review:
Like this Garmin. Had a 510, until like an idiot I left it sitting on the bike rack after a ride and drove off forgetting about it. I loved the 510 touch screen. To me there are a few real key differences between the 510 and the new 520 version. First the touchscreen. Some people hated it on the 510, mine worked great with no issues. The 520 is controlled with side buttons, which basically sucks to use while in motion Some important menu items are buried inside multiple levels of menus, require excessive scrolling, push a button, find the option, push a button again, scroll some more, find the option....you get the idea. It really isn't very convenient. This is my main beef with the 520.

On a positive note, versus the 510, the 520 will connects quickly and easily to your iphone or mobile phone using Bluetooth, and with the Garmin Connect App on your phone, it will upload your Strava ride the moment you save it on the 520. I do like this feature. No more connecting the 510 by cable to a computer to upload rides.

Generally speaking, the 520 suffers from a poorly lit display. The 510 display was easily readable day or night. At sunset the 510 display would kick over to reverse graphics that had a black background and white letters. That contrast was easily readable with litte to no need for backlighting. The 520 changed and not sure what they did to it, but it is virtually black at night, and unreadable. The only way to see the 520 at night is to set your backlight in the "on" position for night rides, and then set your display at about 30 or 40%. This does impact battery use, but so far still leaves me with 75-80% charge after a couple hour ride.

There are other features added to the 520 that are lacking in the 510 as well. These include segments, basic maps, and other features that I don't use. I don't want to take a star away for something I'm not using. Note however, I did try the segment piece and found it quite annoying and turned it off. The idea is if you have a segment created where you are tracking your time, speed, etc, the device will alert you as you approach the segment, and show your pace against your best time, and tell you if you set a new record for yourself. Unfortunately, when the segment tracker kicks on, it blocks out your screen with the map and you can't see your speed, cadence, for the length of the segment. Worse, its somehow tied to the Segments you create in the Garmin Connect app, and it isn't intuitive tying this Strava. I got a few segments set up and afterwards the 520 never updated my best time on the segment. It was forever stuck on the original segment time first loaded. Ultimately I couldn't get the 520 to add more than a couple of segments, which never updated. I also am unable to delete them. Better just to leave that feature turned off.

All that said, this is a great little cycling GPS device. I like how small and unobtrusive it is. For all the main features I use for, it works great.

Review:
This is my first smartwatch and I've been satisfied with my purchase. It's worked well for the few weeks that I have owned it.

PROs:
1) Good looking watch: I wouldn't be embarrassed to wear this watch in business casual situations. It's a nice looking watch overall.

2) Solid features: It has mostly everything that I was looking for in a smartwatch and the price is reasonable.

3) Ecosystem: I like being able to use the Google Play store and easily connect my phone to my watch.

4) Fine battery life: I can't compare to any other smartwatch, and I recharge every night, so I can't complain about the battery life.

5) Comfortable: Feels very comfortable on my wrist, even during running. There's no slipping and sliding around and it never feels too tight.

CONs:
1) Lag: It seems to be an issue with most smartwatches, at least Android Wear ones. I haven't found it to be a major issue, but it can definitely get a bit frustrating at times when you want to quickly glance at a few things.

RECOMMENDED?
YES! I can recommend this watch for the solid features, good looks, good overall performance, and reasonable price.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Fine battery life, recharge every night
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a battery that lasts through a full day of use and can be conveniently recharged overnight.

Having spent a few years wearing various "smart" devices (just about every Fitbit, a number of Xiaomi trackers, and a Samsung watch), I finally decided to try a WearOS watch. My last device was a Fitbit Blaze that I loved but proved less than durable. So, an important criteria was affordability which seemed to keep WearOS watches out of reach until I stumbled upon the Ticwatch E when reading a review of the Ticwatch S. What made it a no-brainer was 1) the recent price change, 2) the promise of getting the October WearOS update, and 3) it's unobtrusiveness (or minimalist styling).

Well, I had it for less than a month when the first October updates started rolling out. Within that week I received the update! Until that update I wouldn't be writing a review. The update really improved the watch's user interface and it's responsiveness to the point that I complete many interactions without thinking of pulling out my phone.

Battery life could improve. It gets about 24 hours on a charge for me. I'd like to use it for sleep tracking as well but I'd need to charge it sometime during the day. It does charge fast though.

All in all, I'm very pleased with the watch.

Review:
This is a great choice for getting a smart watch that isn't hundreds of dollars, but still offers the same basic functionality of the expensive brand name watches. It came with a very basic manual but is very simple and straightforward to get it paired and set up. The box however came damaged with a hole through the face of it and would not look nice as a gift as you can see this hole inside the box too. However, other than that it is a nice gift-able item and would work great as a gift (assuming it comes with no holes in the package).

The other thing I am not so sure is the magnet charger, it connects very easily but when you place the watch down the cable easily moves and contacts are lost. You will have to be mindful of placing the watch carefully so that the charging cable doesn't move and disconnects while charging. On the bright side, the charging is very fast and only took about 15 minutes to top the battery after it arrived with about 70% life remaining.

Review:
I purchased this to use in conjuction with the Lose It! app (on my iPhone). It is the least expensive activity tracker that you can pair with Lose It!. I would commend this to you if you are interested in owning an activity/sleep tracker but you are not willing to fork out about $100 to try one. I was apprehensive about buying any activity tracker because none of them consistently get stellar reviews (most average in the 3.5 stars range). I've never used anything like this before so I don't have that bit of comparative experience to add. Here are my findings:

1. This works very well with the Lose It! app. If you are interested in seeing exactly how much (or how little) you move about during a day, this will help you establish a baseline. I also enjoy using the activity tracker to motivate me to add a few more steps to each day. If you are using the Lose It! app, you will see your steps taken along with the calories burned AND a calorie bonus if you are active enough.

2. There are two Misfit apps. Both do different things. I am grateful for another reviewer pointing out that the regular Misfit app is going to give me information regarding activity and sleep. You can view by the day, week, or month if you would like to see trends. The Misfit app periodically will sync your device with your phone, or you can manually sync as well. The Misfit Link app can be used to manage multiple Misfit devices, as the Misfit Link can be used to control music, take selfies, etc. I am only using it as an activity tracker right now and I only have the one device, so I'll reserve comment on the rest. The Misfit LInk app is also how you configure how you want to use your device--mine shows activity progress first, then the time of day. I can tag activity time by one long press. The Misfit Link app will also show battery life on your device.

3. It took about a day to become familiar with the device and able to use it easily. I still don't like the clock as much as my watch, but I don't want two things on my wrist and I don't want this on my other wrist either. Sigh. First world problems.

4. It comes with a clip that easily attaches to your clothing. I've also clipped it to my watchband. The clip is sturdy and works well. I did purchase watchbands for this tracker because I feared that I would leave it on my clothing and wash it eventurally. I purchased these bands. [[ASIN:B00Y6XSME8 GL-Sail(TM) Set 6 Colors Replacement Bands Wristband Strap with Clasps for Misfit Flash(Pack of 6) (6PCS For Misfit Flash)]]

5. I have not tested the claims of water resistance. I am saving that as an emergency safety measure.

6. I appreciate that I don't have to recharge anything. I also enjoy the fact that this tracks my activity without being in range of my phone.

7. The Misfit LInk will share information with the Apple Health Kit.

I think the this is a excellent value for the money. If you are new to the wearable activity tracker market, I think this is a good entry point. I would buy this again.

Update: At the 3 week mark, my device would not sync with my phone. I contacted Misfit and I received a timely response (a few hours). I went through a list of things that they recommended to sync. The last thing was the recommendation to replace my battery. I thought this was unnecessary as my phone had been reporting 100% batttery life prior to the syncing issue. I removed the battery and then put the same battery back in. This solved the problem. I also did not lose any data from the time that my device did not sync despite having removed the battery. I have not had a repeat of the problem. I am still pleased with the performance of this device as an activity tracker. I also received two follow up emails from Misfit to make sure everything was working ok.

Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs the device to have a full battery charge before a syncing process begins.

Review:
I recently purchased this Smart Watch as I was impressed with all the functions it offered for a reasonable price. I must say It has not disappointed! I am amazed at all the things it can do for a $50 price tag and also love the nice user interface. I wanted something I could quickly gather quick info from such as time, weather, date, heart rate, steps, etc. and I got it. I also love the ability to see my incoming SMS messages discreetly on the watch face instead of needing to pull out my large Android phone to see them. I hope the manufacturer soon will make a way to provide a short SMS reply back to the sender as some Fitbit units do. However considering this watch is 25% of the cost and has tons of useful features, I can easily overlook this little aspect.

Another great feature is the ability to make calls out on the watch and the sound is pretty decent. Whether you want to play some simple games, monitor heart rate, O2 levels, weather, fitness performance, sleep, etc., it is helpful. I don't expect it to have the accuracy of a pure pulse-ox monitor or blood pressure cuff as these are standalone items specifically made to address these items.

The magnetic charging USB cord is also another simple, yet great feature. You can't go wrong with this watch. Love the quick change band too. Highly recommended across the board.

Review: This smart watch/fitness tracker is great! I am always trying to keep track of steps/miles I've walked but am not able to carry my phone with me at work so I don't track the majority of my steps, this watch has helped me do that! It seems accurate for monitoring Heart beats as well, it has many cool features such as a timer and stop watch, 8 different sports modes to track workouts, a relax feature wich just has you inhale and exhale deeply for your choice of one or two minutes. It can also control the music playing on your phone by skipping to the next song or going back a song. The display is bright enough to see in full sunlight and has three different brightness settings, there are also three different watch faces to choose from. It has worked flawlessly to notify me of sms messages as well as Facebook notifications and messenger notifications. It also notifies me of incoming calls and allows me to decline the call from the watch. It has many more apps that you can set to get notifications from, those are just the few I chose to use. Battery life has also been great, it came 91% charged out of the box and I have been using it for 6 days now and am still at 46% without charging it yet! I have been playing with it quite a bit too. All in all this is a great watch/fitness tracker and is a great price, its comfortable and has/does everything that i want it too. I definitely recommend it!

It will even display pictures if someone texts them to you. My previous smart watch just told me someone sent a picture, but it didn't display the picture itself. I couldn't respond to text messages from my previous watch. You can also answer and make calls through the watch as it has a speaker and microphone. The quality of the call isn't terrible for what it is. The watch allows you to download and access some apps as well. The downside is that battery life isn't great, but it is probably comparable to other smart watches.

Obviously, Apple and Samsung are the leaders in smart watches. Without them, though, I think the Tic Watch would be a candidate for number one. Impressive for a watch that costs half of it's Samsung counterpart.

Review:
It took me a while to decide to purchase this. First, I wanted a fitness band but decided against it because I couldn't find a easy or reliable way of getting info into Google Fit. After purchasing one, sending it back because the app was lousy, and trying to find a reasonable alternative, I began to think that if I wanted to use Google Fit I should get a device that runs it natively (Wear OS). After looking at professional reviews and exploring other models from Mobvoi, I decided to buy the pro model over the S2 for the added functionality and because with incentives the Pro was only $20 more.

THE GOOD...

1. Looks - It's a pretty stunning looking watch, and the build quality is superb.
2. Size - I don't have a huge wrist, but I like large-sized watches. I normally wear 45-50 mm diameter watches anyway, so the size of this was a plus for me. Ergo, this might not be for you if you like your watches smaller.
3. Google Fit works great with the internal sensors - When I workout I just start an exercise in Google Fit and when I'm done I stop it. It's that simple however, I usually spend 30 minutes on an ARC trainer and there's no specific workout for that in Google Fit. I pick Elliptical instead and there's a difference in calories burned (machine vs. watch) but nothing to scream about (approximately a Hershey's Nugget difference).
4. It runs Wear OS, not a proprietary OS. I see a lot of complaints about Wear OS but I don't know what the big deal is. It's functional, constantly being updated, and the library of apps is growing all the time. I kind of reminds me of the complaints I heard when Android first came out and was competing with Apple. I'm sure it will keep getting better and better.
5. Little to no lag when using apps or navigating. The reports about lag on the watch have been exaggerated. Yes, it's main processor is not the latest/greatest but so what? It works well.
6. The "essential" screen (the black & white LCD) is super easy to read in bright light.
7. The heart-rate monitor is pretty darn accurate when compared it to the ARC trainer heart rate monitor... consistently a beat or two off but generally close.
8. You can pair Bluetooth earbuds to the watch and listen to music and/or a podcast, or answer the phone from the watch. I don't know if I'll actually do this (I carry my phone with me everywhere anyway), but it's a nice feature to have.

THE BAD...

1. The default settings on the watch are not conducive to good battery life so you need to play with them. The dual screen is a great idea, but the watch has so many things going on at the same time you really need to play with the settings to get near to the advertised battery life. I switched off tilt to wake, NFC (I'll turn it on when I'm paying for something), "Hey Google" detection. Plus, I'll take it off when I'm sleeping so that it's not measuring my heartbeat 24/7. Yeah, I know that essential mode will get you a month's worth of battery life, but if you're running around with all the cool features turned off all the time to get 30 days on one charge, you just wasted $200.
2. I'm scared to get it wet. Perhaps I should have gotten the S2 after all. Read the instructions from Mobvoi and you'll see that there's some serious butt covering going on in that book when it comes to use and care.
3. Sometimes, notifications are a little slow in coming to the watch. I can't pinpoint whether it's the watch or the phone, but I'd say there's some blame to go around.
4. The leather top / rubber bottom strap is nice looking and pretty tough, but after one sweaty workout the grooves in the rubber part of the band need to be cleaned... buy yourself a replacement band - they're abundant on Amazon and pretty reasonable too. Use the replacement band for daily wear and working out, use the stock band to go out at night and impress your friends with your uber-cool techno wrist ornament.

All in all, I like it. I'm only a couple of days into ownership and I'm still getting the hang of using it. The current sale that included $50 off and the basic but nicely functional TicBand fitness tracker were what got me to buy it. That and my gift card balance meant that it was a very little out of pocket purchase for me.

TIPS:

I got WAY more battery life by doing the following...
Turn off Tilt to Wake
Turn off NFC
Turn off "Hey Google" detection.
Turn off the notifications I don't need (my email accounts for shopping, junk account, etc.)
Set the brightness to 3

With these settings, a full day including playing around with the watch, two logged workouts in the Google Fit app while at the gym, reading notifications, etc... and the battery indicator was over 70%. I'm not a huge fan of the "Essentials" screen so switching the display to "Always On" and using the watch face's power saving (ambient) mode after 15 seconds, the power indicator after a full day was over 50%. I can put the watch into "Essential mode easily by swiping down on the watch face and tapping the watch icon. Pressing the top button cancels this so switching back and forth is easy. Anyway, since I put the watch on the charger when I go to sleep, this isn't an issue for me... I get way more than a full day on a charge.

If you want more accurate heart rate detection while working out, wear it snug and spin the watch so that the sensors are making contact with the underside of your wrist. This is true for every device I've used with heart rate sensors and it works for this watch too.

I've been getting great results with using Google Fit on my watch and phone, and an app called Lose It! on my phone for tracking nutrition. Lose It! reads exercise and step data from the Google Fit phone app and puts it in the log for you. Logging into an app everything you eat every day is a pain, but it's a great way to lose weight.

Get a cheap silicone replacement band and use it for working out instead of the leather/rubber band... I got a pack of 2 for $15. They're comfortable and they look great.

If you're hard on watches in general, get a snap-on protective cover (about $8). I got two... one black, one blue to match my bands and watch face color changes.

Watch faces from SOOON on the Google Play Store are excellent, especially their fully functional versions (a very reasonable charge to unlock everything).

I recently purchased the 1.9'' Smart Watch and I have been extremely impressed with its functionality and durability. The smartwatch has a beautiful, high-resolution display that is clear and easy to read, even in bright sunlight. The touchscreen is very responsive and I've had no issues with it not registering my taps or swipes. The fitness tracker features on this watch are top-notch. It offers so many different sport modes to choose from, so no matter what type of activity I'm doing, I can track it accurately. The heart rate monitor is reliable and provides consistent readings, and the oxygen saturation sensor is a great addition for monitoring my blood oxygen levels during exercise or sleep.

In addition to its fitness tracking capabilities, the 1.9'' Smart Watch also allows me to answer calls and respond to messages directly from my wrist, which is a convenient feature that I use frequently. The watch is compatible with both Android and iOS phones, so it's a versatile option for a wide range of users. I've always been told that the apple watch is superior and android has nothing comparable, but this proves them dead wrong. It is so useful and is so much cheaper than an apple watch for the same, and some even better features. It is perfect for runs or hikes because it provides a no distraction way of living while also allowing me to track and find all the useful things that I need when going on a run or a hike.

One of the standout features of this smartwatch is the battery life. It lasts for days on a single charge and the charging time is very quick. I've had no issues with the battery dying on me unexpectedly, even when using the watch heavily throughout the day. The watch is also comfortable to wear and the design is sleek and modern. It's not too bulky or heavy, and it looks great with a variety of different outfits. It looks very modern and pairs well with both sophisticated manners of clothing and just plain clothing as well. I usually have no reason not to wear it whenever I'm going out, it is just that useful. The convenience of it is the best part, whenever I leave my house without it, I always feel inconvenienced, I've realized how good my life has become just by having a little piece of technology for quick glances at stuff without needing to pull out and use my phone.

Overall, I highly recommend this Smart Watch. It has exceeded my expectations in terms of performance and value for the price. It's a great choice for both men and women who are looking for a high-quality, feature-packed smartwatch. The fitness tracking features are comprehensive, the call and message functionality is convenient, and the battery life is impressive. I'm very happy with my purchase and would definitely recommend this smartwatch to others.

Review:
I've been wearing this watch for most of this week and have really liked it. I was looking for a smartwatch that looked less cartoonishly large and square, someting more like a traditional watch, and this one really fits the bill! With the three side stems it looks much more like watches I've worn over the years, and the wide band makes the watch comfortable to wear. The default face, mostly black and white with a few color details, is the one I've stayed with, again because it looks more like an analog watch. There's also a dressier metal band included with this watch, along with the tool required to remove links to adjust the fit.

Setting this up to sync with my iPhone was very easy, just downloading the Da Fit app from the Apple Store and opening it up to pair with the detected watch. There are permissions to grant in the iPhone for the app's access, one paired, and some personal data to fill in on the DaFit app, as well as the usual choices of 12 vs 24 hour time, metric on imperial measurements, etc. And there are dozens and dozens of alternative watch faces available, providing more or less information on the screen.

I don't think I've mastered all of its capacities yet, but it has the usual clock functions (timer, alarms, stopwatch), health monitoring (pulse, blood pressure, heartbeats per minute, calories burned), exercise (steps taken per day, time spent running, distance covered), along with a sleep monitoring function on the Da Fit app. Plus time, local weather, text message reading, and some phone functions like dialing from the watch, recent calls, and a list of a contacts.

Blood pressure monitoring is a great deal more accurate than with my last smartwatch: I checked it several times against a cuff, doing readings on both the watch and cuff, and it gave me very similar results, only 2 or 3 points off the home monitoring device. Well within parameters I'd get if I checked a second time on the home blood pressure device. (It did give me one extraneous and absurdly low reading between those two correct ones, however.)

The watch's pedometer function is one I don't quite yet understand. I was impressed this morning to see it very accurate, within 10 steps when checked against my iPhone's health app step meter, both around 8000 paces, and I'd been wearing the watch and carrying the phone throughout the morning. I'm less impressed later today in finding it nearly 3,000 paces below my iPhone's reading, showing 9800 rather than 11700, again with neither leaving my person. (I assumed they would sync with DaFit when the app was opened, but apparently not.)

The sleep analysis function in DaFit is one that's entirely new to me, so I have nothing to compare it to. The time asleep is accurate, and this is certainly more data that I've ever had before.

I'm impressed with the look and the varied functions of this watch, and might well buy it as a gift.

Review:
I had a love hate relationship with this fitness tracker smartwatch. I loved that this unit tracked your steps and gave you notifications once you've reached your goal. It integrated great with the S health app that comes on the Galaxy S4 and above phones. You could also download the Gear Fit manager and install third party apps to help give the gear fit more functionality. For instance I found an app that would allow you to dial your contacts from the gear fit and then all you would have to do is pick up your phone. Another thing I liked was the sleep tracker. If you wore the watch to bed it would track your movement at night and give you a percentage of how much you were motionless through the night. I found if I was around 92% or above I got a pretty good nights sleep. Below 92% I could tell the next day! Finally this watch was the best alarm clock I've ever had! If you set an alarm on your phone and changed it to vibrate only, the alarm would vibrate on the watch! This was amazing because I could set the alarm and it would only wake up me and not my wife when it went off. I could dismiss the alarm or snooze it right from the watch. By far my most favorite feature!

There were several things that I did not love about the Gear Fit. For one, notifications were awkward to read. While you could change the orientation to be vertical or horizontal it seemed strange either way you looked at it. In vertical mode the big words would be hyphenated over several rows which made it even harder to read at times. The heart rate monitor wasn't very accurate at all. If I did three readings in a row the results would be random from 60-90 BPM on average. The Gear fit is a little bulky when it comes to thickness or height but the thing I hated the most and what ultimately made me decide to send it back was the watch band itself. The clasp just snaps into a hole on the band I kept finding that sometimes the band would get caught on something and come right off! It was enough to make me worry about losing it. I looked for alternative bands that would lock on but there were none to be found. Aside from that, wearing this band caused my skin to breakout and itch. I don't know if it was from moisture and rubbing or if it was from the plastic/rubber compound itself. I could wear the Gear Fit for a couple of days but had to take it off for at least a day to give my skin a rest.

I really wanted this watch to work especially since I got it at a great price on sale. I don't think I would have ever paid full price for it because for a few dollars more you could have gotten a full size Android Wear or Galaxy Gear watch with more features! Speaking of which I replaced the Gear Fit with the Asus Zenwatch which I am totally happy with!

If you're considering this watch I would really do as much research as possible. It may be a good fit for you but again if you pay full price for it there are much better options for a few more dollars. If you want a great tracker that has some integration with your smartphone and if you can find it on a good sale this might be a worthwhile buy.

Review:
I tried out the Samsung Galaxy Gear smartwatch but after a few days returned it and decided to try the Sony SmartWatch 2. This review is a bit of a comparison of the two watches. I hands down prefer the SmartWatch 2 over the Galaxy Gear for several reasons.

1) The strength of this watch is that it isn't trying to be everything. Because of that, the price is now 1/2 of what the Galaxy Gear cost and that makes this watch a very good value.

2) Don't sweat the lack of camera or speaker phone that the galaxy gear has. The camera was gimicky on the Galaxy Gear and unnecessary. The speakerphone while useful at times in the Galaxy gear, is not something I miss at all now that I've switched to the SmartWatch 2.

3) I think the Sony SmartWatch 2 looks better than the Gear. It has a subtlely handsome look and an understated quality feel and appearance to it. You can wear this watch with almost anything and it would not clash or draw attention to.

4) I got a few compliments from women and men about my watch the first day I wore it to work. No one has any clue it's a smartwatch at first and just think it looks cool. The Galaxy Gear I thought was more eye catching in a way I didn't like or want.

5) The display on the SmartWatch 2 is always showing the time. I love it! The GG has the display off until you bring up your wrist up in front of you to look at the time, then it would delay for an awkward second, before the screen turns on and you can see the time. The SmartWatch 2 is easy to tell the time at any time.

6) I work in a hospital and must do constant hand washing. The SmartWatch 2 is waterproof, while the Galaxy Gear is not. I was very afraid to splash water on the GG and it drove me nuts as I don't have time while working to have to worry about drying my watch all the time or trying to protect it from splashes.

7) The screen on the SmartWatch looks just fine. It's not as sharp as the GG screen, but it doesn't matter. You can easily read many rows of tiny text on the screen when receiving longer messages. Yes you can see pixels up but so what. It's still perfectly decent display for the use case.

8. The SmartWatch UI acts just like Android device. There are back, home and menu keys. There is a swipe down gesture that brings down notifications.

9. The black rubber watch band is very comfortable in all day use. I couldn't say that about the Galaxy Gear at all. The Galaxy Gear irritated my wrist throughout a days. Part of the metal clasp of the Gear watch band would rub against my wrist and caused me irritation. Also you can replace the SmartWatch band with any watchband.

10. My suggestion is to NOT load a lot of useless apps for use on the Watch. I only install the free "WatchIt!" notification app, along with the gmail and text apps. The WatchIt app will allow you to send any notification that appears on the top of your mobile phone to the watch as well. I started off by blacklisting all apps so they DON'T show notifications. Then I go through and approve only the ones I want. Be conservative in what apps you allow to buzz you on your watch. It will get annoying quickly if you allow dumb stuff to bother you and buzz your wrist all day.

11. You'll want the stock text message app as it allows you to view and reply to text messages using preformatted replies. You can create your own pre-formatted replies. I deleted all of the stock pre-formatted replies and created my own. When you get a text alert on the watch, you press the menu button, and then the text message icon button to reply. Then you can swype left or right to cycle between your preset replies. It works pretty well and doesn't try and shove more functionality that just won't work on a smartphone.

12. The watch does what it's supposed to do. I can view the time at all times, and it lets me get notifications to my wrist so I don't have to pull out my phone at work (which we have a strict no cell phone policy at work). I don't miss any important calls or text or emails anymore. I know when I have to step outside and deal with a call or text and when it can just wait.

Review:
When I buy a smart watch, there are certain things (features & functions) that I always look for. I do not care much for the accuracy of heart beat rate, SPO2...etc because I fully understand that these economic types (under $50) watches does not really provide accurate data. So as long as those data are somewhat accurate and useful enough, I am fine with it.

for me, the most important things are the following : a lot of useful watch faces that I can download and switch back and forth, thru the watch, not via app.
Tennis fitness app (I am a tennis coach) among other useful (at least for me) sport apps. very loud alarm and timer, so that it can wake me up. 44mm and up big size watch for my 7.4 inch wrist. Large battery....etc

As for watch faces, it has a lot. A lot of good ones that I can use. Also, I can delete pre-installed all the watch faces and upload with all new ones. Many other lower cost smart watches do not have this feature. I can only upload one or two that I like and I have to keep the pre-installed faces which I can not delete nor replace. As for this watch, Yes, you can basically delete all the pre-installed faces with your choice of watch faces, up to 8 of them.
As for the battery life, it claims that it has 300 mAh. That is pretty big size and I think that it will last for few days.
It also has a lot of fitness choices. More than 100 of them and I think that most of us can really choose what we like. Also, the app (Veryfit) can allow the watch to upload only the fitness apps that we are willing to use. Veryfit app is a very useful and well designed app for lower end watches.

And the feature that I like the most is the alarm and timer. Most of watches, including expensive ones costing over $100 or more, only comes with weak vibration for alarm and timer. But this one has the "real alarm" sound. Not vibration, but the piercing & loud sound that will really wake you up. I had a lot of smart and fitness tracker watches, but this one is the only one that has the actual alarm sound coming out of the watch speaker. I really like this feature.

This is big size enough for my wrist and the screen quality isn't so bad either. It is not amoled screen, but you really can not expect amoled for this price. However, screen is very nice and it is bright enough.
I guess that the only draw back that this watch has is the strap. You can not really change to different strap because it has its own proprietary lug designs (as you can see from the photo). However, the strap is nice and soft and comfortable though...
It comes with a very simple and cheap looking gift box and inner plastic container (which even looks cheaper) but as long as the watch works and it works great for me, I do not mind.

Review: I've stopped looking. Finally found my watch. I have been looking for months for a wear os watch with little success getting all the functions I wanted in a watch...until now. The TicWatch Pro is simply a great smart watch. What I love: In my opinion it is a really nice looking watch. I'm getting about 2 days battery life  in full mode. It has Google Pay and is working well. It is waterproof (IP68) and has a built in GPS. The fitness app that comes with the watch work well right out of the box. Compared to my wife's Apple Watch and it was right on target. Plus, since it is wear os, you have access to many apps in the Google Play Store. Notifications are awesome and I can take calls and make calls right on the watch without getting my phone out. The nice thing is that it has Google Assistant, so the assistant can make all my calls for me when I ask. She (cause that's the voice I use) can also send hands free texts for me; no more typing texts. I'm streaming music from my watch through my blue tooth ear buds and it works great.Obviously the Layered Display Technology is awesome and saves battery life.There are tons of watch faces and I've found many that are perfect for me where you can personalize them to provide information you want at a glance; weather, steps, calendar events, etc. On the other side, this watch does not have a SIM card, which was not a big deal for me as it pairs with my MOTO just fine. Also, it is a little bigger than some but I like that because the display is full of awesomeness. Oh, one more thing, very easy setup and navigation. Bottom line, I've been looking and researching for a long time and for the money, this watch can't be beat.

Review:
I was seeking an affordable smart watch that would allow me to make & receive calls from my wrist. Sure you can use a blue-tooth ear piece but that doesn't look quite as cool walking down the street. This smart watch allows you to pull off a Dick Tracy or an Inspector Gadget hat trick. It's just outright cool to answer a call like you're on guard duty. Just don't go doing that in a tough neighborhood or you may find yourself in a bad situation. ( I don't think the warranty will cover that scenario, I'll have to ask. lol )

In any case, the smart watch is not perfect. It has flaws but I didn't buy this to take my blood pressure, count my steps, receive water intake notifications or to play birdy games. I only got it to make and receive calls without being forced to pull out my phone just to handle my communication needs in public.

For you to be able to add contacts to the watch via the "DaFit" app, you must format the phone numbers within your phone contact list with dashes and not parenthesis. Don't forget to grant the app permission to access your contact list. Enable "Contact sharing" by clicking into your "K22_XXXX" bluetooth connection to see your options. You can then select your 8 favorite contacts via the app and save changes to have the contacts show up on your watch. Works fine with my Motorola G Play 2021 phone running Android 11.

Example

This works. >>> 1-235-345-77XX

This doesn't work. >>> 1(235)345-77XX

The "Da Fit" app offers various options but the one I like most is the ability to change watch faces. There are numerous watch faces to select from ( 25+ ) but the issue with the current app is that you can only download 1 watch face to store offline. Don't know why the developer didn't simply design the app to allow us to download all the watch faces we want offline to enable us to switch between them at will.

At this time we need to use the app in order to acquire a new watch face that replaces the previous one, it kind of defeats the purpose. On the bright side we can use a custom watch face from downloaded images or photos we take with our phone cam so that kind of mitigates the issue with customization but it's still quite limited. You can't create a bunch of custom watch faces and switch between them, it's one at a time or the default options.

The calls are clear and I have no trouble listening, speaking and understanding everything being said. The person on the other end can hear me fine too! I was surprised on how good this feature is. I'm not going to comment about all the other options because to be honest, I don't care for those & didn't really explore too deeply. I'll let someone else share their experience and thoughts about it. However, if you just want a cool smart watch to make & receive calls I highly recommend it. Just keep your expectations in check when it comes to a sub $ 100.00 smart watch. Not all the features may be suitable for your needs.

I must of looked at over 30 different smart watches before selecting this one. I didn't want to spend over $ 60.00 and this was the best I could find based on aesthetics, battery life and overall features. I sacrificed things like enhanced water proof ratings, always on screen light and enhanced GPS features found on more expensive models.

By the way, this watch charges up fast! I don't think battery life will ever be a problem but only time will tell. Already have friends and family hinting towards wanting one. : )

FYI

- You must pair your smart phone ( IOS or Android ) via bluetooth to both "K22" and "K22_XXXX"

- The "K22" bluetooth connection allows the camera shutter and the audio player to function.

- The "K22_XXXX" bluetooth connection allows you to make and receive phone calls via the smart watch.

- The "Da Fit" app is required to download a new watch face, set location for weather and to change Celsius to Fahrenheit if that's your preference. The app also contains the data collected from the smart watch for your review when setup properly.

*** It may take a few tries to detect both bluetooth connections. Toggle bluetooth on and off on your phone if you have trouble detecting it. Sometimes you just have to reset the watch itself via it's menu options.

Before heading out, disconnect and reconnect your bluetooth to make sure it's properly connected. Don't just assume it's working because the watch and the phone may unpair itself sometimes. Simply pair them again and check your phone pairing on the watch to make sure you see the dial option available.

The watch is very lightweight but doesn't feel cheap. I have now used it for a week and find myself liking it. It has very good battery life; When I received it the battery level was at 60% and today is the first time I put it on the charger and it was still at 23%. The blood pressure monitor is fairly accurate when used as directed taking 3 readings and averaging them. The blood oxygen sensor seems to be very accurate but I have no other way to test or compare that. The heartbeat monitor reads a bit higher than my monitor but not enough that it would cause me to worry if I had no other way to check it. I really like the sleep monitor and it seems to be pretty accurate with how I think I slept each night.
The call quality is excellent on both ends of the call, even when the watch is near my waist. The text messages come through just fine and are easily readable.
The only sport I did was running and for what it is the distance accuracy was okay. I went 4.65 miles according to my GPS watch and this said I had gone 4.3. Overall not a big deal but the longer the distance you go it will probably be off accordingly.
I can recommend this watch for it's functionality and the casual athlete.

Review: I received my Gear Fit on June 15th as a gift for father's day, I was on Vacation as saw this as a great opportunity to try it out as we were walking all over very places. I wore it all around for the day at the end of the day the Gear Fit said I had walked 15,000 steps, my wife, who walked every where I did was wearing a Fit Bit and it said we had gone 12,500, as this was a huge discrepancy I started watching the Gear Fit closely what I noticed was that it would randomly add several steps when I was doing things like changing clothes or simply getting off the bed and then sitting in a chair.  I returned the Gear Fit and got a new one, hoping that it was just a bad one.  I got the new Gear Fit, which had just been updated with the newest Samsung software that was supposed to increase the accuracy of the pedometer as well as the heart rate monitor the update also made the Gear Fit start tracking steps without the user having to manually start the pedometer.  What I noticed was that even after the update the pedometer was still very inaccurate, it would give me 30 steps for putting my pants on. Compared to the Fit Bit it was 200-300 steps off in a 1,000 step period.  I don't know what if anything more Samsung will do about this but if you are looking for a reliable activity tracker, at this point this device doesn't seem to be very use full.  I does look good and the touch screen is very nice, It also does work well for seeing messages, calls, e-mails on your wrist.  It did connect easily to my Galaxy S5.

Review: I like this watch! I have an Apple Watch, but the battery life means it goes on the charger at night. This watch is the perfect supplement--it has all the basic features you need: message notifications, fitness tracking, music control, weather, timers, and, importantly, a sleep tracking app that you can use while your Apple Watch is charging. It was a breeze to link the watch app to my iPhone, and all of the features work. The sleep app is surprisingly accurate (I don't know how it knows when you go to sleep as opposed to sitting on the couch watching a movie, but it does) and it lets you know when you were in light sleep, deep sleep, and how many times you were awake. It's great feedback if you're trying to get better sleep. There are plenty of useful watch faces with immediate info, even a flashlight feature. And it's incredibly comfortable to wear--I don't tend to like plastic straps because my wrist sweats, but this band seems lighter and cooler, so it's barely noticeable. The pulse and blood oxygen features aren't as precise, but still useful. But the best feature is the battery life--despite all the features, after 5 days I was still at 45% battery! Honestly, you can't go wrong with this one.

Review:
This is a great smart watch. It is chockful of useful features and handily syncs to your smartphone so you can get instant notifications about texts, FB messages, and phone calls. It can also track your heart rate through the sensors under the watch face, a blood oxygen meter that shines a red light into your wrist, and most popular of all tracks your number of steps. It is basically a miniature basic smartphone interface in the shape of a watch.

The build quality of the watch is great, with an HD screen, speaker, microphone, and a decent battery life. The screen has vivid colors, fine details, and an adjustable brightness that's visible even under full sunlight. The speaker is loud for such a small device and the microphone is sensitive. I can pick up calls and talk through the watch with relative ease and clarity. There are also 10 interesting preloaded songs, such as Baby by Justin Bieber and another by Adele with which you can test the sound. It also lasts a long time between charges and the battery life is about 1 week of daily use (occasional phone calls, mostly in sleep mode) before needing to be recharged.

The software is well designed, with everything polished and working smoothly. The accompanying SmartPro App is intuitive to use and has the more detailed features that the watch can't do by itself, such as downloading custom wallpapers. You can download and upload wallpapers through Bluetooth, and also switch between different wallpapers with a twist of the watch knob. Most of the built in functions of the watch are intuitive to use. The only feature I can't seem to get to work is the sleep monitor as it never seems to start even when I go to sleep.

Overall, this is great smartwatch that can pick up calls, read and send messages, and remotely control your music playing devices.

Review:
Looking for Christmas gift for my wife, I ended up researching smart watches and trying to figure out what could be the best bang for the buck.
I first found out about the brand through a YouTube tech reviewer and liked what they had to say about this particular product. Some more reading, and the fact that Mobvoi was running a special finally led me to take the plunge and get the Tickwatch E for her.

The Tickwatch E Shadow is a very nice device. The black shell is not polished, it has a soft and slight textured look and feel to it and she loves the fact that you can change the wrist bands to make it look a bit more upscale than just a sports looking watch. Unlike other devices I looked at while doing research, this watch is an Android Wear device which means full integration with our Android ecosystem. The device has one physical button on the left which is used to power on the watch, return to home/watch face screen, access the system menu and activate Google assistant.

The overall experience my wife has had with this watch has been fantastic, she loves the responsiveness of the watch which translates to her being able to respond to notifications with ease, the chat apps we use work as they should, the fitness tracking is excellent overall although the heart rate monitor is a bit slow sometimes and the IP67 rating gives her peace of mind that she can work out in almost any condition without worrying about getting the device messed up. Battery life is also excellent, she turned off the gestures so that the watch doesn’t activate by accident and has managed to get a consistent 1.5 days of use out of a full charge.

Not all has been sparkles and happiness though. As with any new product and a small company that releases said product, there are bound to be issues from time to time. As it was in our case.

Our original device did run into a problem when charging one night, the watch rebooted a couple of times and turned off. The following morning the watch was a tad warm which was odd since it’s normally cold after it’s done charging, and when trying to power it on the display colors were off and there was a graphical artifact in the middle of the screen. I did some searching online and found another user that had the same issue, but the forum post at that time had no answers or directions on how to proceed. After attempting to troubleshoot the device and factory resetting it a few times with no avail I promptly started an RMA process with Mobvoi to get the watch replaced, this is where the sparkles dimmed and the happiness turned into some confusion.

This is the part where I think Mobvoi needs to mature a bit more, though they did a great job in returning emails and finally getting the watch replaced, the actual RMA process through their site is very problematic and not very intuitive. I actually ended up emailing support first prior to submitting my case through the site because their site was not working with all the browsers I had tried (Chrome, FF, Edge, IE, Opera), it was malforming the page to the point where I could not continue to submit the problem. After finally guessing and submitting the form most of the communication ended up via email anyway because their ticket status page never updated again. This turned what should have been a quick RMA process into waiting almost an entire month for a replacement watch.

Again, many thanks to their support team for taking care of our issue.

For anyone looking for a smartwatch packed with features, great battery life, a good price and backed by a great customer service department, the Ticwatch E will not disappoint.

Review: I purchased this phone on the sole purpose of being waterproof. The additional features like sleep tracking and battery life were a plus and a defining factor that decided my purchase. The sleek and strong face with clear and large very easy to read letters and numbers is a must have specially during bright days. It links effectively and effortlessly with the mobile app and your phone. Text/SMS messages are received real-time no delay, missed calls and emails as well. It does not have a built-in microphone, therefore, you do not have the ability to make calls, dictate or send text/sms which is a bummer and a negative on this device. The strap is interchangeable and it fits most straps sold for the iwatch including the little screen protector and cases. I like the all day battery life and long life stand by mode. The sedentary tracker is on point while the sleep tracker could use some work. It does track, but it is not as effective as other smart watches as it takes 30 minutes to begin tracking your sleep pattern based on movement. Honestly, I do not always lay flat on my back and sleep all night. The watch is interrupted when you are sleeping and turn numerous times to adjust yourself. The blood and heart rate measurements are onpoint and match my blood pressure monitor with the same accuracy. The oxygen sensor is very good equal to my dedicated blood oxygen sensor. The fitness tracking is good, but could improve by linking directly to devices that accept bluetooth connection for fitness tracking purposes, but, it is very good nevertheless. Finally, and the most important feature! It is waterproof! literally 100% waterproof, not water resistant up to anything. I shower and swim in the pool with it without any issues. Next I will put it to a true test when I'm at the beach.

Review:
I was looking for a watch, not a computer for my wrist. I've always worn a watch, and I like the look and feel of a watch. My complaints about my past smart watches were all aesthetic. They didnt look like watches. This watch looks like a watch. It's thicker than I expected from the pictures, but it does have a watch face and hands,, and people who see it are surrpsied that it also notifies me of calls and texts. It's not the most attractive watch. The numbers are too shiny and the roman numerals are kind of cheesy, but overall it looks like a watch.

What I'm really sold on though (and what earned it five stars) is the stability of the smart connection and the battery life. Since pairing this watch with my phone (which was ridiculously simple with the android notifier app), I have not missed a single call or text. The connection to my phone is just solid, and if I somehow go ouot of range, the reconnection is automatic and dependble. This has beena problem with other smart phones I've had. Finally, the battery life. I charged it the first day, and five days later after continuous use, i finally drifted below the 20% mark and needed a charge.

Admittedly, I'm not a heavy phone user, and maybe because i don't use it a lot, I used to miss a lot of calls and texts. This watch gives me a way to make sure I stay connected to my phone while still wearing a watch like I've always done.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
I previously owned a LG G Watch, Moto 360 and the LG Urbane, but the TicWatch Pro is not comparable to those smartwatches. The battery life is very good. With the other watches, I used to have just about 1 day of charge. Now, the TicWatch offers me at least 2-3 days of use without the need for a charge. Because there's an LCD and an OLED, the watch can switch from one to the other to save the battery, which is super good. Despite the controversy of the leather and rubber wristband, I like that it keeps my wrist from sweating and still have a luxury look when looking at it. My watch has become my day to day payment method thanks to the NFC function. Another great thing is that you can remap the second button. I've remapped it with the GPay app so I can quickly pay just by double clicking the button.

The cons. Rest assured! There's few. Sometimes the LCD screen turns on while I'm using an app. This is probably due to a small motion of my wrist and the watch detects the movement and switches between the OLED and LCD. Then, there is the Google Assistant. It works pretty well, but the boot time is laggy. Oh and when it's long to boot, the watch usually switches from the OLED to the LCD screen which is a little annoying. None of those cons are enough serious for me to dislike and/or to not recommend the watch.
