It is acceptable if the watch uses a power-saving mode that switches between different display types to conserve battery. The watch should be able to switch between an LCD screen and an OLED screen.

Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
The HOAIYO Smartwatch received had a rose gold case and a pink band. It looks quite nice and seems to be of good build quality. The instructions which are included are quite well written and complete as to setup, pairing and basic operation. We set this one up using Android on a Samsung Galaxy. The app was very easy to pair with the phone, only asking a few questions such as birthdate, weight and height. It required a number of permissions if you want to utilize all of the features of the watch, and share is data with your phone, where you can review everything very easily.

As I mentioned above, the watch is nice looking and powered on the screens are very easy to navigate. We went hiking and it seemed to accurately record steps. The pulse and PulseOx utilities are very easy to use, worked accurately and gave readings quickly. I was actually quite impressed by the simplicity getting these readings. It was to simply swipe to the page, and it does its thing. There are no other actions necessary. If you are tracking your pulse during exercise, it maintains this data, and passes it to your smartphone with no action required on your part.

The notifications feature is very nice. It makes a quiet little vibration and displays the message received on your phone on the watch long enough for you to look at it. My wife was coordinating something with a friend, and it was quite nice to not need to keep checking her phone for a response. We tested if for both text messages and also Facebook Messenger. I assume any other app that generates notifications will also display them on the phone. This includes reminders and tasks programmed to present a notification. It has some other useful features like a timer, which is useful for everything from timing laps to reminding you to take something out of the oven. It also has a weather screen which gives you the local temperature, current weather, graphical temps and weather for the next several days. That is a very easy answer to your basic weather questions.

I must say, I owned a Samsung Galaxy watch. It had many more features. But, quite frankly, most of them didn't work very well or were difficult to use. This watch is comparatively basic. But it hit most of the important features and they work well. The Galaxy watch absolutely gobbled up the battery. One day's use and this watch dropped from 100% to 95% which I was very impressed with. I think I can reasonably expect to see five days, or more, use on a single charge.

I really do not have any negatives. The only upgrade I might suggest is that I think the magnetic charging cable could me a little heavier duty. Charging cables are a common failure point for all portable electronics. If used regularly, and especially without proper care it may fail prematurely. If it does, I am not sure how easy a replacement will be to be found. That might be no problem. I'd have to investigate how common this type of charging cable is.

In consideration of the price point I am very pleased. I would not hesitate to recommend this watch to anyone looking for a basic sports and exercise tracking smart watch. It looks sharp and hits all of the points, doing them well. I will give it a solid five stars.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: One day's use and this watch dropped from 100% to 95% which I was very impressed with. I think I can reasonably expect to see five days, or more, use on a single charge.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The watch should maintain a high battery percentage after a full day of use. The watch should provide several days of use on a single charge.

Special Features: Smart Watch Bluetooth 5.0 Activity Tracker with Heart Rate Monitor Sleep Monitor Pedometer
Review:
After many positive experiences with Tozo's various fantastic earbuds and charging pads, I saw they produced a smartwatch that seems to check a lot of the basic boxes for a smartwatch that was within swinging distance of what my old Pebble Smartwatches offered, but for a vastly smaller price than the original Pebbles were ever sold for. I figured giving the S1 a shot would be worth it while going in with the basic reservations of what to expect from a Smartwatch that is cheaper than $50.

After some time with the watch, I can say it still does offer some great value for the price, but it does have a few shortcomings that may or may not matter at this price point and possibly some could be remedied with software updates.

First the Good.

The device looks and feels rather well made. The body of the watch is a mixture of metal and glass and feels solid and really well made. The watch band is a nice soft touch rubber that feels and fits well on the wrist. The screen is capable of being very bright and easy to read and has good view angles. It's not a high res screen so don't expect a lot to fit on the screen in sharp detail, but comparing to a pebble, its perfectly usable for a smartwatch.

The Interface while simplistic and lacking any animations, is actually quite fast and responsive. Button clicks and screen swipes and taps are all really quickly responded too and makes using the watch a breeze. There are a collection of watch faces to choose from as well with some decent analog and digital faces that range from detailed digital to pretty relaxed analog and a range in between.

The vibration of the watch for notification and alerts/alarms is solid and works well. It's not adjustable in strength but it seems adequate without running overly long or harsh for a smartwatch. I didn't miss any notification that came through when wearing this watch due to not feeling the buzz, just be sure you turn on DnD mode in a time range in the app or else expect any notification that comes to the phone over night to wake you up with a buzz in the middle of the night.

The App that pairs with the watch from my Android Phone is reasonably laid out and while a little bare, doesn't ask too much for personal information except for what it needs for workout/sleep tracking. The one thing that may seem odd but is pretty standard for smartwatches to provide notifications, is that the app ask for nearly all the permissions possible to function and will always seem a bit odd. But considering what the watch needs them for, it does make sense. Even when it ask about Camera access, you can actually use the watch as a remote camera shutter button because why not right? Other things like Location data help the app to look up weather info, and there are a few you have to allow for notifications to be sent when the phone screen is off and to keep the app from being put into sleep mode from the battery management, etc.

I haven't had any chance to really try out the workout tracking features of the smartwatch but the step counting seemed to be relatively on par with my other watches and tracked them accurately as long as you wore the watch morning till night.

The one fitness feature I did really like and worked really well was the heart rate tracker. It takes readings at a set time you can set in the app (from once every 10 minutes to 60 minutes) and you can track it in real time by going to the heart tracking app in the watch itself. It seemed to be really accurate and can give you heart rate readings throughout the day in a little chart in the app to show you how your rate seems to change throughout the day. I assume it tracks a little more real time when using the workout apps, but for normal daily tracking it still is useful when you are just going about your day. I had no issues with it on my wrist taking a reading or feeling like it was way off on the high or low end.

The battery life of this watch is fantastic. Once fully charged and even with the lift to wake option turned on, the watch will last you about as long as a pebble (about a week) and with the lift to wake turned off, it can probably go much longer. Things like screen brightness turned down and maybe taking heart rate checks at longer intervals might extended it to be even longer. But overall any watch that can best 3+ days on a charge is pretty good in my opinion.

Now for the not so good.

Unfortunately being the budget smartwatch that it is, and a first shot for Tozo, that does take it a little bit outside of the wheelhouse of earbuds and wireless charging, there are a few bits that keep me from wanting to use the watch as my daily driver to replace my aging but still fantastic Pebble Time Steel.

I think for one is probably similar to many other reviews here, why is the watch stuck in Military time with no ability to switch it to 12H AM/PM? There is even a watch face with AM/PM on it but it still reads out in Military time. An odd oversite for a "Watch" at all and if there is a way to swap it, I couldn't find it after spending a good chunk of time searching for a way. The analog watchfaces are an alternative way to get around that, but this is something that could and should be fixed in an app/fw update for sure.

While the UI is responsive, it is a little bit, odd in the choice for things like the pulldown menu which allows you to see if the watch is connected, go to settings, or adjust the brightness, but being unable to change things like DnD mode or see anything about an e-mails contents/header is a bit of an odd limitation. Text messages are shown properly but do seem to disappear quickly and going back to read them is technically quicker and easier with your phone then using the watch itself.

Lastly, the sleep tracking seems to be about as good as my Pebbles is, however I have had it on occasion just completely not track sleep whatsoever and well, not knowing how exactly it knows when you are sleeping vs not (same question I have for the Pebble) but the Pebble has pretty much never missed a sleep session in the years I have had it. When it does work though it seems pull up roughly similar and accurate numbers to my Pebble so it does work, when it works. It may have just been a fluke though so I will continue to test to see if it was just a bad night sleep that made it think I was still working instead of sleeping, ha!

Wrap Up.

Overall while it does have a few points against it, the overall experience with this little smartwatch has been a positive one. For the price you get a watch that does nail all the basics rather well, and if they can add a 12/24hr option, this watch would make the perfect starter watch, or gift for someone who wants to give a wearable a try, without breaking the bank. It looks good, offers most all the basic features you want with a smart wearable without being too complex or difficult to use. Is it a replacement for a true WearOS, Apple Watch, or even a Pebble? Not quite, but it does come rather close and considering those can easily be $100+ more than this watch, that is saying something for sure.

Good on Tozo for branching out into other devices. I continue to look forward to other neat innovations they put out, maybe even an upgraded and improved S2 watch...

Review:
So I painstakingly compare the Apple Watch the Pebble time around, and I even bought both and more each for two days to decide.
There are many things that the Apple Watch does better, my main points where the hepatic tap as opposed to the old-school vibrate of the Pebble, responding to text messages was easier on the Apple, as was accessing music and controlling play back. Also the display on the Apple is crisper and cleaner.
All that said, the Pebble significantly lighter, and has a much smaller profile, and really looks like a regular watch. When I had on the Apple Watch I found myself itching to take it off, where as the Pebble went unnoticed when I wasn't interacting with it. Also, I find the old-school graphics, wide range of watch face choices, and quirky animations to be charming on the Pebble. Another thing that I found to be a plus on the Pebble was the fact that the step counter was simple and thus I wasn't digging through information to just get my daily step count. I understand that the Apple Watch is much more comprehensive when it comes to activity but for somebody that just wants to track their steps and not have to wear a Fitbit, this was perfect. Another huge perk for the Pebble was the battery life, one of the main things I wanted to do was use it as a sleep tracker, and though the battery life is nothing compared to the regular Pebble time, it recharges in about 15 minutes for a whole day, so I was able to charge it while I shower, and wear it for the rest of the time without worrying that overuse would make it so I would have to charge and miss steps etc. Also, the Apple Watch doesn't have a sleep tracker at all, and all of the apps that you can download to do so suggest a full charge before going to bed. Not to mention the fact that as I said before the Apple is bulky and not something that I feel like wearing overnight.
I have to admit it was difficult to return the Apple Watch, as It is beautiful and does do some things much better, but after just a few hours of wearing it I found myself wishing I had on my Pebble instead. I guess I also think there's something to the fact that we don't need to be 100% connected all of the time, and the Apple Watch is just begging to be interacted with well on my wrist, whereas I can just let the Pebble be a watch and step counter. I hope the pebble will address some of the things that the time round lacks in their next round of development, as I really think it's a great watch.

Review:
My daughter had been asking for a smartwatch for a while so I decided I'd get her a more affordable option and see how responsible she was with it. After a little bit of searching, I let her choose between a few watches and this was her selection.

This smartwatch appears to be of good quality. It has smooth a silicone band that can be removed for cleaning and comes with a magnetic charger that's specific to this watch. The watch charges quickly, but how quickly it drains depends on how much it's used.

The app was easy to download and the features are easy to set. My daughter's watch isn't set up for phone calls or texts because I don't want her to be distracted by it during school. It has other features she likes using, though. They don't seem to be incredibly accurate but she enjoys seeing her sleep patterns, step count, and the weather. For a child, this would be suitable, but I don't see this being accurate enough for an adult who's really trying to track numbers.

Her favorite feature of the watch is that she can customize the face to whatever image she wants. There are preset options but uploading your own is a lot more fun. She's been enjoying changing it as she desires and it makes her feel really cool that she can truly make it her own.

I'm happy with it for her (an 11-year-old) but I wouldn't be thrilled with it for myself.

Review:
Before I get to the review, I’ll tell you a little bit about myself so you know how I use the watch. I’m an Emergency Medicine Doctor, beginner to intermediate runner and cyclist, and aspiring triathlete. I’ve owned the Forerunner 945 for about a month now, and prior to this I used the Garmin Fenix 5 and Apple watch 4 ( I hesitate to even include this because it’s really not a fitness watch). I am by no means a high level athlete, and I’m not going to qualify for the Boston anytime soon. I am, however, a data geek. I’m obsessed with wearables and the insights that they can provide us. For the sake of brevity, I’ll assume that anyone looking to purchase a 600 dollar watch knows what they are purchasing and has done a fair amount of research. My hope is to review some of those new features and help you make an informed decision.

Fit:

The Fenix 5 is built like a tank. Indestructible, heavy, but mostly annoying to wear. I found it difficult to sleep with the device on my wrist. The apple watch 4 was sleek, but I worried about cracking the screen during my workouts. The Forerunner 945 is just right. Sturdy, but significantly lighter than the Fenix 5. I wear this one all day and during sleep. My wrist is small to medium at 7 inches and I do not find the Forerunner too large by any stretch. It’s slightly less comfortable than the Apple watch for all day wear.

Appearance and performance:

I don't want you to convince yourself that this is going to look sharper than an Apple Watch. Apple has aesthetics down. In fact, the forerunner 945 has identical screen resolution to the Fenix 5. But edges appear sharper and brighter in actual use. The processor is noticeably faster from the Fenix 5 and navigating from screen to screen is a delight.

Features:

For fitness metrics, Garmin reigns supreme. It is so vastly superior to any other fitness watch on the market. This is a true multisport watch. Do not convince yourself that the Apple watch is a fitness watch. It is a smartwatch with fitness features. If all you need is cadence, speed, and distance, the Apple watch will do just fine. Buy an Apple Watch if you want a smartwatch. But if you’re looking at this review, you’re probably a dedicated runner, cyclist or triathlete. The amount of data on this little computer is absolutely incredible. From the very actionable lactate threshold feature, to the less actionable but interesting respiratory rate, this watch has something for everyone.

New Features:

If you're new to the Garmin ecosystem, I’d refer you to their website or First Beat to look at all the available features. However, if you’re looking to upgrade from a previous Garmin, i’ll give you a rundown of what’s new from the Fenix 5/Forerunner 935.

Music - Perhaps the biggest reason to upgrade. I use spotify, and found the integration to be absolutely seamless. Add Wifi network to watch, download playlists, connect wireless headphones (I use aftershokz air), play music. That’s literally it. Garmin kept it simple here. So simple that it’s easier to download music on the Forerunner 945 than the Apple watch.

Heat/altitude acclimation - This falls into the category of “interesting, but not sure what to do with it”. Garmin automatically accounts for external temperature by the use of local weather data (Not the watch itself), and gives you a percentage of acclimation. I live in Florida, so after the first week I was 100% acclimated and rarely fell below 95%. I’m not sure what to do with this. I don’t live or train at altitude, so I can not review that feature.

Training Focus - This feature surprised me. I really like the training focus graph. It’s very actionable if you polarize your training (As you should). I no longer have to depend on third party websites to see how well polarized my training is. If it’s time for a high intensity run, I do it.

Workout labels - You’re training effect now comes with a label (Base, Tempo, Vo2 max etc). This is a nice summary at the end of you’re workout. It keeps me in check. Often when I think I’m doing an easy effort, I stray into the tempo workout. This can only be seen after the fact, but it’s good for post run analysis.

Pulse oximetry - This falls into the category of “please remove this from the market immediately.” As a doctor, I hate this feature. Not because it’s a bad thing to include, but because it is terribly inaccurate, and the data is confusing for people. For a healthy adult, or even a sedentary adult without respiratory or cardiac problems (Asthma, COPD, etc), your normal pulse oximetry should always read near 100% at sea level. Regardless of whether you’re working out or not. I've compared this to hospital pulse oximeters and I can tell you the accuracy of this feature is so poor that I really wonder why they released it. The idea, from Garmins point of view, is that oximetry data can be used for altitude acclimation, sleep tracking, sleep apnea. Unfortunately, the error is at least 5-10% for any given reading. An adult with a pulse oximetry reading of 88% on room air would have severe respiratory problems. My watch will frequently tell me that I am 88%, only to check on a real pulse oximeter and see 99%. I turned this feature off and I recommend you do the same.

Body battery - I like this feature. It takes into account your sleep, stress, activity level, and VO2 max (Resilience against fatigue) and comes up with a number. Subjectively, I found my battery to line up with how I feel. I had a few drinks the other night, had a poor night’s sleep, and woke up to a body battery of 10%. If you had asked me how I felt before I looked at the number, I would have said a number around 10%. It’s a good tool for mindfulness and becoming aware of situations that drain you. I’m usually at 5% after a twelve hour shift in the Emergency Department.

Respiratory rate - Requires HR strap. Interesting, probably accurate ( Although I cannot confirm, using HRV makes sense in theory and the numbers seem to correlate with HR when plotted on a graph). This falls into the “interesting, but not actionable yet. Lots of potential in the future”. I can see Garmin doing some interesting things with this data (Lactate, ventilatory threshold improvements for example). Just not enough going on with it for prime time yet.

Updated and improved:

VO2 Max -Now takes heat into equation so your VO2 max doesn’t tank during the summer. Nice feature.

Training status - Also includes temp data for more accurate training load

Race predictor - New algorithm that relies on more factors than just VO2 max. It’s an improvement, but it’s still wildly optimistic I think.

Still needs improvement:

Wrist HR - Although Garmin claims that their new sensor is better, I found it to be equal to the Fenix 5, which is to say, not great, but acceptable at rest. This is a huge blow and the biggest reason I cannot give it five stars. In order for your data to mean anything, it depends on accurate HR data. I am not satisfied enough to run without my HR strap yet. Hopefully there will be improvements to come in the form of software updates. For now, get a chest strap.

Pulse oximetry - A real stain on otherwise well released product. Should remove feature entirely.

FTP auto detection- Never worked on my Fenix 5, still not working on my Forerunner 945. I ride with a power meter and HR belt so I don’t know where the issue is.

EKG - This is becoming standard now and it would be a nice health feature to add.

Text back features - Garmin still struggles in the smartwatch department. Notifications are nice, but you’re still tethered to your phone for text backs.

FAQ:

Do I need a chest HR monitor? Yes. If you’re going to splurge for the data options on the Forerunner 945 you should use a chest strap to enable all features, ensure accuracy of device.

What’s the difference between the Forerunner 945 and 245. The 245 is primarily a running watch. The forerunner 945 is a triathlon watch. The 245 has very limited data for cycling, and worse yet does not include lactate threshold for runners which is a huge let down. Falls into the category of "too expensive for a casual runner, not enough features for a dedicated runner".

Internet complaints I've come across when researching this product:

Pairing issues with music- Have not had a problem yet in a month of use. Pretty seamless for me.

Buttons “mushy” - Something I NEVER would have noticed if not for the internet. I guess they are a little soft? I don’t know. They feel exactly the same as the Fenix. Wouldn’t worry about it.

GPS inaccurate - GPS is on par with Fenix 5, Apple watch. Never had much variation between any of these. I run in a medium sized city with tall buildings and haven’t had any dropouts.

HR inaccurate - It’s true. It seems to be a continual struggle for smart watches (Except the Apple watch 4, who has figured out the magic formula). This is an optical HR monitor, so it depends on perfusion, which is variable throughout the day. To remedy, I still use a chest HR monitor which uses electrical conduction of the heart muscle and is extremely accurate.

Battery poor - I get like 4-5 days out of it and I run about thirty miles a week. I'm not sure if the people who complain about this run ultramarathons? But I can't really speak to any problems here.

Accessories I use:

AfterShokz Trekz Qir headphones. Love, love, love these headphones. They are the best. They work great with the Forerunner.

Garmin Tri HR monitor - Waterproof, durable, accurate chest strap.

Favero Assioma power pedals - Cheap, reliable.

Have fun out there!

Review:
This smartwatch is a big improvement over the previous umidigi smartwatch. The touchscreen is much easier to navigate and the app for this smartwatch is also much better. The app has less personalized customization than an app for a fitbit but is very close. You can change the face of the smartwatch by tapping and holding down on touchscreen. The clock hands move exactly like a real quart sport watch.

The sensor on this smartwatch is not accurate but is very close. When moving, the steps is almost accurate but when exercising such as lifting weight, the step measurement is wrong. I was staying in place but it clock at 150+ step. The heartbeat seems accurate when measuring it alone but seems wrong when exercising. It takes like 5-10 seconds for the sensor to pick up the actual heartbeat. I compared my heartbeat by measuring it by hand for one minutes and it comes pretty close to the measurement on the watch which is 3-5 heartbeats higher. There are several common exercising options in the watch for choosing when exercising which has a few extra measurement. I do not do some of those exercise so I do not know if it's accurate or not.

Your biometric data when exercising cannot be view unless you pair it with the app. The graphs on the app is very useful for knowing whether you reached those crucial benchmark (ie anaerobic exercise) for burning calories. You can also monitor your sleep pattern but you must enable it on the watch by swiping up and click on the moon icon. The sleep monitoring is not accurate. The light sleep detection is wrong. Your notifications does pushed to the watch but only from a few selected apps. Not all of the notification from the phone is pushed to the watch which is the downside of it. Time and weather will not update unless you pair it with the app. You cannot manually set the time. You can set an alarm in the app but the watch vibration is very low that if you are deep asleep, it will not wake you. Vibration is barely noticable.

I have used the watch for 4 days. The battery went from 96 to 69 percent. The screen will turn on when I made a heavy movement with my arm. If it turn on less often, it would last a little bit longer but not much.

I like the watch. It has a high quality build. It is a little bulky which most people will not like. It's perfect for
someone with a larger build like an athlete.

There's a few cons but I cant fault it because most of the high end smartwatch has pretty much the same cons. This watch works almost as good as a high end watch for fitness purposes. There are quite a few functions that I wish it have but as smartwatch gets better, I could see them being implemented.

I bought this because I had a FitBit Flex that was given to me by my company, and I liked the size but wished it had a clock and was water-resistant. I mainly use it track steps, more than anything. Review:
The main reason I purchased this watch was to use it as an activity tracker. How many steps did I take today? Did I get my Google Fit heart points? The Ticwatch E is very successful in this area.

Oh, it also tells me the time and gives me text message notifications and such ... this is all quite pleasant.

I was hoping to use this watch to record my activities on Strava. This ALSO works, but with a caveat. Here is the scenario: I wake up in the morning, put on the watch. I wear it through my 10 hour work day, get home to exercise. I run for an hour with Strava ... when I get home, my battery is dangerously low! If I had gone on a bike ride (which could easily be two hours), I fear the battery would have completely died. So, warning: Strava really drains the battery life on this watch.

This watch was sold at an easily accessible price for me. However, one of my friends was really excited about a certain watch face he loved on his watch. Sadly, the Ticwatch E was too slow to handle it.

Overall, though, the watch does what I want it to do. It is a little slow (regarding processing speed), and the battery life isn't as strong as I'd like. It is my first "Smart Watch", and definitely gets my foot in the door to figure out if I want to invest in a more expensive one!

Review:
The Ticwatch Pro is my second smart watch. I have owned the LG Urbane for over 3 years, and I loved it. I've been getting random blinking on that watch and I felt it was time for an upgrade. I searched and researched thoroughly.

After reading articles, viewing watches from friends and at stores, and determining what I needed, I settled on the Samsung S3 Frontier. I sent that watch back after trying out the Samsung software and it's limited apps.
I kept searching and decided on this watch, the TicWatch Pro. While waiting for delivery, I was concerned that this watch would not be worth it. Had smart watch technology not advanced much in 3 years?

A few days later the package arrived. I liked the look of it, except for the watch band and the thickness of the watch. Since then I've come to love the thickness, it's the perfect weight and comfort. I purchased a black leather band as well. I've played with it for the last 2 weeks, and I loved it more and more each day. There's not a better Android Smart Watch out there, especially for the price. Installing a bunch of free watch faces for WatchMaker and Facer has been addicting because good watch faces are so crisp and clear and colorful. It's shocking.

Other things I've done with the watch is downloaded 3 of my favorite music albums directly onto the watch and installed a bunch of apps from the Google Play Store. If I know I'm going to be playing with the watch all day at work, I bring along the charger. If I don't play around with it much, the battery lasts a day and a half. I have not tested the battery life while in Essential mode the whole time, but I image it would be maybe 4 days. I don't understand how anyone would claim a longer battery life than that for this watch, but that is fine.

If you click on the Essential app, the LCD screen will stay locked in that mode until you restart. So this way would give you the longest battery life. You can also enter the LCD screen by pulling down the main menu and clicking on that option. To exit that mode, you click on any of the two buttons. You can also enter the LCD screen by turning off the option for screen always on. Instead of going into dim mode, it will go to the LCD mode. To exit, you tap the screen.

Every app is very quick. There are so many great features, but I don't want to make this too long. I don't regret this purchase one bit, I know I found the perfect watch. There is only one problem though. When I'm at work, I'm addicted to playing around with the new watch and my work is not getting done. Gotta say again, I love this watch!!

Review:
This will be a Christmas gift to my Mom.

I will give my impressions of the watch.

Quality. The build quality of this watch is good.

I like the styling and I'm sure my mom will love it.

It comes with a USB-A charging cable that attaches to the back of the watch for charging. No adapter needed.

Battery life is good but the frequency of needing a charge will depend on how you are using it.

It comes with two watch bands. One metal band for dressing it up and a rubber watch band. That way she can switch it up when she wants.

This watch uses the FitCloud Pro app.

I have other watches in this company's line up that use the FitCloud Pro app so I can speak about the ease of use.

Using the FitCloud Pro app you can set up the watch to track whatever health data you wish to track. Things such as steps, heart rate, sleep tracking, checking bp, exercises such as cycling, running, climbing etc...

I am only using the Fit Cloud Pro app for sleep tracking but I have tried the heart rate and bp. The tracking for those I believe are fairly accurate but I cannot say for sure since I have no way to test for accuracy. It's not something that I would totally rely on. So on the occasions I do check my bp for an example, it's only to get an idea of where I am until I can have it measured with medical grade equipment.

The sleep tracking I can say is accurate for me. The app shows how many hours of light sleep and how many hours of deep sleep. I find this information very useful since my sleep is all over the place.

Being that this is a smart watch, you can use it for calls and messages and music control.

Once you download and set up your account with the FitCloud Pro app, it's easy to bind the watch in the app using bluetooth. From there you can go through the settings to get the watch set up to use the features you are interested in.

So this is a good smart watch and offers very good value. My wish is that the app would allow more than one watch to be added.

I will update this review after my mom gives me her feedback on using the watch.

I recommend this smart watch.

I hope this helps.

Review:
I've been a Garmin fan for years; never spent much time with TomTom before Amazon sent me this one via the Vine program. After getting used to the menus and taking it on a 8 hour trip this is my impression of this GPS device:

Things I liked about it:
Big screen, easy to read. Touch screen is very sensitive, though; easy to bump the wrong thing till you get used to it.
Search for attractions includes phone numbers. When I got close to the restaurant I was looking for, I called and made my carry out order.
Emergency numbers and hospital, police, & fire info handy; first aid instructions.
Many small customizable details such as sounds, etc.
Maps have lifetime upgrades, something I wish my Garmin had. Also allows user to make corrections to maps for others to access.
Speedometer and display of current speed limit. This was even accurate in construction zones, showing the temporary speed limit.

Things I didn't like:
Mounting gadget not easy to use. I gave up after a while and just propped it on the dash. On subsequent trips I got the hang of it, but it is still harder to attach to the windshield than my Garmin.
No detailed instructions. Is this online somewhere? The user guide was very basic and didn't adequately cover all my questions. Example: I couldn't find a volume control anywhere, and the user guide didn't tell me anything. I just stumbled on it by accident. (FYI, volume is controlled by running your finger along bottom of screen and touch pad appears.)
Directions are not always accurate. I used both the Tom Tom and my old Garmin Nuvi at the same time on an 800 mile round trip. When they differed on how to get somewhere, I tried to follow Tom Tom directions but kept getting lost and had to use my Garmin to find my way back on track. The restaurant that Tom Tom gave phone number for? It wanted me to go a half mile the wrong way. Garmin kept pointing the other way, so I finally called them back, and the restaurant was right where Garmin said it was.

Bottom line: fancy features, bells & whistles, but less accurate on the one thing I want a GPS for: finding where I am going.
Attribute_Related_Content: Screen Size: Big screen, easy to read.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a screen that is easy to read.

Review:
Ok, so I WANT to give this watch 5 stars. My wife bought me this watch as a late birthday/Christmas gift about a month and a half ago. It came in and from the moment I took it out of the box, I could tell something wasn't right. It would do this weird Ghost touching thing, where it was almost like the watch would touch itself and it would be random, as if I was just randomly touching the display. It was very frustrating, but aside from that, the watch was great.
I reached out on the Mobvoi forums, where one of their support people told me to go ahead and do a warranty return, as I filmed a video of the watch doing what it was doing and their response was a very quick 'we'll send you a new one'.

So I did. I setup the return, packed everything back into the box and sent it off... I tracked the package to make sure it would get there and it did! But then came the wait...

3 weeks after the warranty ticket was put in and a bit more than 2 weeks after the return had been received by them, I finally got the replacement. Mind you, I didn't get a response from their support team about the issue until a little past the first week the return had been received by them, because I sent them an email about it, which seemed to have prompted a response.

The replacement is fantastic. As many have noted, the battery life leaves much to be desired, but I have the settings optimized and can get more than a full day of charge. The WearOS features on this watch work great and though many talk of it being laggy, I haven't really noticed. My last "watch" was a fitbit charge 2 that would hold a charge for more than a week, but also was limited as towards smart watch features. The Ticwatch though, has a beautiful screen, is nice and light and the ease of changing bands is great.

If anyone wants to try out a pretty good entry watch into the world of smart watches, this one is definitely a good and light on the wallet option.

So...

Pro's
- Lightweight
- Darned good display
- Wear OS
- Easy band changes
- VERY comfortable

Con's
- Company support? What is that? (I did get a replacement that works great, but I mean, response to messages and through the overall warranty process SHOULD have been faster, smoother and easier, especially for a watch that is very easy to REALLY, REALLY like)
- Battery life not as robust as maybe it should be.

Honestly, I really love this watch... The company's support though... cost it a star

I get notifications from calls, messages, IMessage, calendar, email, etc; best yet, it supports over 1000 apps in android and IOS. the best feature of this watch is how long the battery lasts, I've getting 5 days on a single charge. I'm not going to go over all the features of the device, what I'm going to say is, if you are wondering about smart devices such as iwatch but don't want to spend all that money for a device that you have to charge every day, give the Martian watch a chance. you will love it.

Review:
UMIDIGI Smart Watch boasts a variety of features including activity tracking (up to 9 activities), sleep tracking, BP, HR, O2 saturation, notification alerts for popular apps, do not disturb feature (all the time or on a schedule), sedentary prompt, relax mode, alarm music controller, timer, 2 brightness levels to select, and 4 dial faces to choose from (one mimics a chronological face piece and two include the number of steps you've taken that day). The watch is easy to charge and has an impressive battery life. The wristband is easy to remove and reattach (or presumably swap out with compatible wristbands). The watch is low-profile and nicely fits both men and women's wrists.

I chose this watch because, unlike others, it offers O2 saturation readings, which I need everyday, along with BP and HR. I can manually perform the latter two readings, but not the first, and I was already looking for a fitness tracking watch to try out that didn't break the bank. It also boasts a better waterproof level, but please be aware that the manual stipulates it is not intended for saltwater swimming, nor water with harm chemicals; so I'm not sure if it's even safe for pool use—the Amazon page certainly leads you to believe it can be use in a chlorinated pool. I do note that on the Amazon page and the watch itself, interestingly enough, that "swimming" is not one of the 9 programmed activities of the 8 that can be visible at a time.

Unlike some other reviewers, I found setting up and pairing the watch with my iPhone 5SE quite easy. The manual provides clear instructions for Bluetooth pairing and downloading the free mobile phone app. I do wish that the manual provided more explanation around other watch features and functions, however—such as what each icon represents (and what happens when you press it) when, from the starting dial face, you swipe right left, up, and and right, and right and then down and then down—beyond just the the sport feature.

The challenges I encountered were syncing the VeryFitPro app with Apple's Health app—VeryFitPro still does not appear in Apple's Health app as a 3rd party app as VeryFitPro's instructions lay out, so I have not been able to import my watch's data into Apple Health. Next, despite following VeryFitPro's instructions, no alerts other than phone calls appear on my watch. (I have enable proper settings in the app and in the Bluetooth settings on my phone). Finally, this watch does not include a wheelchair push activity tracker. I am wheelchair-bound and had been looking for a lower-priced alternative to Apple's offerings, which I believe DO have this feature. I hope that the developer is able to add wheelchair (pushes) as one of the "sport" options to track and make this an ACCESSIBLE, INCLUSIVE app for people of ALL ABILITIES.

Review:
I'll be honest, this is my first smart watch. I normally wear an analog watch that just shows me the time of day and the date (which is occasionally wrong due to months with 31, 30, or 28 days. And leap year? Forget about it!) My wife, on the other hand, has had an Apple Watch for years. She loves to brag about how many steps she got for the day, or check text messages, or answer phone calls right on her watch. I was a bit jealous, but can't justify paying 3-4 hundred bucks for it. This watch is available now for $59 with a $20 coupon! Just take my money.
The watch does everything advertised. And it does something I don't see on wifey's Apple Watch: Alexa! I am a bit of a geek and have a smart home. I set up routines for Alexa to do many things either by voice command or on a schedule. After linking the Alexa app on the phone, it controls everything by voice control (although when doing so, it reports "Sorry, I cannot understand what you mean." But the important thing is that the requested action is completed correctly.
Battery life: After charging to 100%, I put the watch on Thursday night. by Sunday morning, the watch is at 60% charge. I have the illumination set to level 1 (lowest) and have work it day and night. Based on that data, I don't see it going 14 days on a single charge. But then again, I may be turning on the screen more often than typical since it is my new toy.
Overall, this is great value for the price and I recommend this if you are thinking about getting a smartwatch.

Review:
This is an excellent smart watch that exceeded my expectations.

When I first got this watch, the setup was a little difficult at first as the app it had me install, initially would not show the watch in the options to pair despite being connected to my phone's Bluetooth. Luckily, the manual mentions this could happen and tells you to factory reset the watch and it should show up afterwards; which thankfully it did. After that issue with setup, everything else was a breeze. With this watch, all your main customization and features have to be adjusted and set with the app. This is where you will be able to download and set various clock screens, set up weather data, and more. I also had a couple firmware updates on my initial setup, so it was nice to see that the company is making sure their product stays up to date.

Once I had everything personalized how I wanted, I began to use the watch. The first thing I noticed is that depending on the clock face you choose, there are areas on it that will also display your calories burned and steps taken for the day. Or on some watch faces, it will display your O2 and heart rate levels by default instead. I was already really impressed at this point. Aside from the main watch face, you can also swipe left and right to additional screens where you find dedicated spaces for reminders, calendar, checking your heart rate and O2 levels, weather info, stopwatch and timers, and more. You can also press the side power button anytime the screen is on to get a grid view of all your available areas and choose them from here instead of swiping.

The watch itself has an AMOLED display, which is quite shocking to me. The display is vibrant, crisp, and colorful, yet still has a long lasting battery life. The touchscreen is also super responsive and doesn't require extra force like on some others. It looks good and works really well.

As far as the features themselves, they are very good. The step counter works precisely to how my old Galaxy phones did, and the o2 and heart rate sensors work great but do take a few seconds to give you a reading.

Overall, it's hard to find things to complain about. I highly recommend it to anyone who is looking for a good smart watch. While it can't do everything, it is the best for those of you that are looking more for fitness style watches but it will still be good enough for most casual users.

Thanks for reading.

Review:
I have been dipping my toes with smart watches for a while now. I started off with the Asus Zenwatch 2 then got the Zenwatch 3. Since the 3 I have loved the round designs over the blocky square designs. I have been looking for a watch to replace my Zenwatch 3 that had a built in GPS, heart rate monitor, and looked good. I originally bought C2 when it first came out. I had issues pairing it with my phone and ended up sending it back. It was much smaller than my Zenwatch 3 but it did look nice. I then went for a Fossiol Sport with the new Snapdragon 3100 chip. I had trouble pairing that as well and after some back and forth with google support (they really need Wear experts) I was able to pair it. Because I had to reset services on my phone I chalked up the C2 not pairing to the same issue. After playing around with the Fossil for a day I sent it back. It felt cheap the screen was REALLY small and having a huge bezel didn't help. I did a lot more research online and the Ticwatch Pro kept coming up as one of the best if not best Wear OS watch. The only watch rated above it was the Samsung one but that runs Tizen and has limitations. Ticwatch had the Pro on sale for $50 off. I order it and haven't regretted it. The watch is big,so if you do not like big watches I'd suggest the C2. The screen is great nice and bright and lot of real estate. The watch is very responsive even though it's running the old Snapdragon 2100 chipset and not the 3100. The battery life on this watch is great! I get a full 2 days off the watch. I use it just as much as my zenwatch 3 that wasn't lasting more than 12 hours. I think it was getting long in the tooth as brand new it could goat least from 7am to 11pm with it in always on mode. Because the Pro has 2 screens I am not using the always on mode. instead I am relaying on the LCD screen when I want to take a look at the time or check my steps. I would give this watch 5 stars if the whole body was metal. instead only the bezel is. The watches main body is some kind of plastic. I like the whole metal look and feel. I think it takes away from the "premium" look and feel. The strap is interesting as it's leather on top and rubber on the inside. But I like it now after wearing it for about a week now. The steps and heart rate seem accurate . I do wish it had a sleep monitoring function as that would be a nice extra. One other thing I hope they ad in an upgrade is the option to adjust the time for the screen. it's maybe 5 seconds before the smart screen switches to the limited LED screen. I wish I could adjust that but after talking to support they do not have an option for that.

In summary it's a great watch, battery life is solid, screen is beautiful, and it fits great on my wrist. I am now a Ticwatch fan and can't wait to see what other watches they come out with in the future.

Review:
First off, yes it has 4GB storage but 60% of that is taken up with the pre-installed apps, many of which cannot be uninstalled! Just wanted to make that clear off the bat since all the reviews trump the 4GB storage and make no mention that in reality, you’re left with much less free space than that!
Despite this, my impressions of the Gear Sport after 5 days are extremely positive. I should say that this is my first smartwatch / fitness tracker but I did my research, compared with it’s main competitors and knew what I wanted. I chose this over the Fitbit Ionic and the Garmin Vivosport as I am always impressed by Samsung products and it was closest to ticking all my boxes. It’s not perfect but overall it’s everything I could’ve wanted. In short, it’s comfortable and has hit a sweet spot in terms of size. I personally find it a very attractive design that should appeal to both sexes. I am very impressed with the battery life, smartwatch functions and most importantly, the accuracy of it’s fitness and sleep tracking (having prepared myself for it not being very accurate). More details below.

OS:
So it uses Tizen OS and I have to say it is very fluid and responsive and very user friendly. Yes there are options galore and menus within menus but once you know you’re way around and set up your favourite widgets etc.. it’s a zip to use. And the rotating bezel works brilliantly in this regard. Well done Samsung for making it a very pleasant user experience! The apps ecosystem is not that great unfortunately and I guess that’s because Tizen isn’t currently as popular as Android or IOS. However, I anticipate this will improve but for now I couldn’t really find anything very useful apart from maybe a calculator and stopwatch. The good news is that the Gear Sport already comes pre-installed with all the most useful immediate apps.

Battery:
In short excellent. I can easily get 3.5 - 4 days usage out of a full charge which is more than the stated 2-3 days in the online reviews. Now I should say that by default I have Bluetooth and wi-fi turned off; heart monitor off; light sensor (for automatic dimming) off, wake up gestures off and screen timeout set to 15 seconds. I only use GPS, during workouts which obviously also kicks in the heart rate sensor, and I only use Bluetooth when I need to pair with my smartphone. In one 36 hour period, with the above settings but having it track my sleep, 30 lengths swimming, 2 lots of 45 minute walking workouts with GPS, and a 45 minutes gym session with treadmill and rowing machine tracking, the battery went from 100% to 68%. I think that is excellent.

Sleep tracking:
Very pleasantly surprised with the accuracy here. I nearly went with the Fitbit Ionic as I wanted good tracking here and read they were the leaders in this regard but the Gear Sport impresses me with it’s results. It doesn’t let you know what sleep stage (e.g. REM) you were in at what time which is a shame, rather it breaks your sleep into three different stages which it terms restless, light and motionless. It has accurately recorded jaunts to the bathroom in the early hours of the morning and nights when I know for a fact that my sleep has been quite restless, it’s shown this in the graph the following day. It also appears to accurately record my falling asleep and waking up times. So altogether impressed though I hope more research goes into this area and they can soon record your actual sleep stages like the Fitbits can apparently do.

Fitness tracking:
Again, very impressed with the accuracy though there are some anomalies. I compared the heart rate monitor with the treadmill at the gym, which has the metal handles, which tend to be more accurate than wrist based trackers. Each time I compared the two, the Gear Sport was only 1 or 2 bpm out, usually behind. Impressive! However there was quite a discrepancy between the machine and the Gear Sport when it came to calories burned (both had accurate weight inputted). When the treadmill said I had burned off 60 calories, the Gear Sport only read 43 calories and the Gear Sport was consistently behind in this regard over the course of the session. The treadmill was on a 15% incline so I don’t know whether the Gear Sport was able to discern this? I am unsure which tracker gives off the correct reading…
Walking workouts recorded GPS accurately and provides a useful map of your route and range of useful data such as heart beat, average speed, calories burned etc.. It would automatically detect pauses and resuming such as when stopping at lights.
Swimming tracking works well, you input the length of the pool beforehand and then go. I did sometimes seem to be a length behind but then it would recalculate on the next length and fix itself! Anyhow, it records lengths accurately and again provides useful data at the end of the session such as calories burned, a breakdown for each length and even recognises what stroke you did!

Screen:
I am slightly disappointed with the screen to be honest. Yes it is OLED, vibrant, responsive, bright and blacks are inky black but at around ~310 ppi, the resolution is not nearly as high as the latest smartphones and so when up close you will definitely see pixels. And this means that many of the more complex watch faces available are not as clear as they could be, lines and small rounded text can be difficult to discern. I believe that this is something all current smart watches suffer from, I have read this is one of the better screens so I guess we will have to wait for the next generation watches before we get a significant bump in resolution.

So to wrap up, I think overall this is an excellent fitness tracker smart watch for the casual user. I am impressed with the accuracy of it’s sleep and fitness tracking, the usability of the OS and the look and comfort of the watch. It’s chock full of latest tech although it lacks in some key areas such as LTE and doesn’t have a speaker so you can’t have phone conversations with it, though you can of course send SMS messages once paired to a phone. The improvements Samsung need to make for the next iteration would be far more storage (really 4GB these days is measly), a higher resolution screen, more in depth sleep tracking information and a better app market.

Battery Life: I can easily get 3.5 - 4 days usage out of a full charge which is more than the stated 2-3 days in the online reviews. In one 36 hour period, with the above settings but having it track my sleep, 30 lengths swimming, 2 lots of 45 minute walking workouts with GPS, and a 45 minutes gym session with treadmill and rowing machine tracking, the battery went from 100% to 68%.

Review:
I love this watch! I originally only looked at this because the FitBit Charge HR was backordered and I didn't want to wait 3 months- now I am very gld things worked out the way they did. Let me preface all this by saying this watch is only awesome IF you have a Samsung phone to go with it- which I do (and always will have!). That being said- this watch together with my phone make an awesome combination!
I love the smart watch side as it gives me just what I wanted. I can preview emails, FB notifications, texts, and more, as well as see who is calling and reject those calls. The customizable quick text templates is a nice touch for sending a simple yes or no reply or a thank you without having to go to the phone.
This is also an excellent fitness watch. Love the step counter (though I have no idea how accurate it is)- it motivates me to get up from the desk and move frequently. The heart rate monitor is awesome because it works while you work out and gives you a report afterward. The sleep function is interesting- let's me see how restless I may or may not be sleeping.
But feature that i didn't even know it had before purchasing it- it maps my cycling (actually cycling- NOT spinning). It tracks distance, incline, decline, speed, times, averages, and then plots the whole work out on google maps and shows you speed versus elevation information. VERY VERY accurate information- I've tested it against mapmyride and my cycling computer and it's awesome (be aware there is no GPS in watch so it only tracks this way if your phone is alone for the ride which mine ALWAYS is).
The watch is easy to wear and goes into the shower with me no problem. I am also a larger person so I ordered the large band from samsung but didn't actually need it after all- factory bands fits my thick wrist and my wife's slender wrist, too (I don;t share the watch- just wanted to see how it fit on a slender wrist for the purpose of review)
I get about 3 days on a battery charge and it only takes about 2 hours to full recharge. Bright and clear display with many customization options.
If you have a nd love your samsung phone this watch is the bomb.
S fitness app is pretty slick, though probably not quite up to speed with some other competitors, but certainly not a deal breaker- it holds it's own.
So glad I got this instead of the fitbit!!

Review:
Great upgrade from my nuvi 760. Love the No Pop Up Ad Traffic. (see below)

I like the larger screen and more information and customizable interface. I like the eco route as well (you put in your vehicle profile on mileage and the cost of gas in your area and it calculates costs for each trip. Very interesting to see how much a trip costs you).

I also like how it lets you set a speed limit on roads that it doesn't have. That's a great feature as some streets aren't marked too well.

Another interesting feature is the auto zoom. As your speed increases the unit zooms out to show you more of what's in the area and what's coming, as you slow down it zooms in closer to the intersection, etc. They've also updated the animations as you drive and turn corners, etc.

I called Garmin for tech support on my 760 after the recent Map Update rendered it useless - with 4 kids in sports, one of these is almost essential with games in various locations in and around town. My wife was going out of town for a soccer tournament and we needed a new unit for her trip while the other one went in for service. Now we'll have one for each car, when the 760 comes back.

Anyway back to why you are all reading this. The rep was extremely helpful in taking care of me and my 760 as well as helpful in unit and feature comparisons. I read a lot about how people HATE the pop up adds on the Lifetime Maps and Traffic units. The garmin rep mentioned that it is because the adds help pay down the entire unit. If you'd like AD FREE Traffic, get the [[ASIN:B004PQAXZ2 Garmin GTM 35 Lifetime Traffic Receiver 3700 Series]] separately (I got it for $108 on Amazon). By paying (close to) full price, you won't need the added (punn intended) assistance from the advertisers. It would be and extra $50 for the Lifetime traffic. I "bought out" the ads for $58 and couldn't be happier. Money well spent!

I might now sell the 760 when it gets back and get another 2450. I really like it that much better.

Review:
Its gold-tinted color and pink band make this a good lady's entry-level smartwatch. It came with a magnetic charging cable and limited instructions, but was really a snap to set up: I just downloaded the OnWear app from the Apple Store, did as the instructions said, and it was paired in a few minutes. There were a half-dozen data points to set up in the app -- height, weight, age, and so on -- and a number of permissions to be granted the watch app from my old iPhone 8+. A bewildering variety of watch faces are available through OnWear, too, providing more or less information at a glance on the home screen.

There are many, many functions available on this watch, but it's a bit more limited than some of its competitors. It will count steps, alert the wearer to arriving calls and text messages, serve as a stopwatch and timer, keep track of alarms, track exercise, all the usual smartwatch functions including some I haven't yet figured out. It doesn't measure blood pressure, as some others in this range do, though it will monitor pulse rate and oxygenation. Its size might an impediment to some, too: my wife really didn't like the feel of this one.

A quality watch with many functions, and at a reasonable price.

Review:
I received the Pulsebit R11 Smartwatch promptly , it arrives in a simple but quality package, I unboxed it and plugged it into charge using the included magnetic charging connector, it requires a USB type A connection to charge. (wall charger not included - which is fine as I have many from phones over the years). The charging connector to the watch uses physical contact connections, not induction, and is proprietary, so needs to be brought along on any longer trips - not a big deal. The battery life is amazing, powering the watch for several days and so unlike my older smart watch that would need recharged every day, the R11 can be topped up each day or run for several days at a time without recharging.

The watch is very well made, is waterproof, has a bright clear display and I particularly liked that the strap has many small adjusting notches for the buckle so its easy to find a comfortable strap setting for an accurate heart rate reading.

Connecting the watch to my smartphone was easy, it uses an app to communicate using bluetooth- the app is SMART-TIMEPRO downloaded from the app store, and which provides tracking of steps, heart rate etc. The app also allows firmware updates to be downloaded to the watch. It also allows the watch to be configured easily with measurement units, and many predefined watch dial faces, or a custom watch dial can be created and the mobile controls can be positioned where you like.

One other feature that is useful is that the watch can store music files and play them to bluetooth headsets - great for the gym or jogging when bring a smartphone along would be inconvenient.

Overall the is an amazing smart watch for the price and is excellent value - highly recommended.

Review:
First, if you ride a Trek bike and have the Duotrap, which works as your speed/cadence sensor, it doesn't seem to work well with the Garmin 520. It will synch without any problem, but the speed sensor and Garmin GPS do not work well together. The speed reading will bounce all over the place, which in the end results in the total mileage ridden and your average speed being wrong. The Cadence works fine. Another problem the crops up when you are synched to the Duotrap is if you activate the Auto Pause feature it will frequently pause while you are moving, which will also affect your distance ridden as well as your time. If you are riding on a well know route and like to track your cadence that may be ok. But any new routes will be a wash. I had an error of -1.2 miles over a 16.2 mile route.
Also, if you liked the touch screen aspects of the 510, don't expect that with the 520. It doesn't use a touch screen. It's all button controlled.
One other change from the 510. The power display is not % of battery life left, it's a battery icon that shows how much is left. Personally I prefer the % remaining as It is easier to figure out how much life is left if you are running low.
Other than the above issues with the Trek Duotrap and no Touch screen, it's a good device.

Review:
As an owner of a number of smartwatches from known brands (Galaxy Watch, Apple Watch, Garmins) and currently an active user of Fitbit Sense, I got curious about how good these generic brand smartwatches became. I went ahead and gave this watch a try and I was pleasantly surprised.

The big clear LCD screen is easy to read, even in bright sunlight. It is not OLED, but it is not a huge drawback in my opinion.

The battery life is truly exceptional, which sets it apart from many 1-2 day per charge smartwatches like Galaxy watch or Apple watch. I can go for up to a week on a single charge, which is really convenient for me especially for monitoring sleep and wearing it 24/7. It is also very handy when I go on a business trip without the need to bring a charger.

The heart rate monitoring feature is okay, but it's not very accurate. I tested it while wearing an Apple watch on the other wrist and the reading sometimes can have a 10-20 bpm difference. In addition, the heart rate monitoring is not continuous and there's no option to turn it on all the time. The app provides an option to control how often heart rate is being measured, and the lowest I think I got it to is within 1-5 mins. This I am sure is to preserve battery, but it is the only watch I have that doesn’t seem to do continuous heart rate monitoring. However, the on-demand spo2 measurement is a nice addition and I appreciate being able to track my oxygen levels whenever I want.

The Alexa integration is another useful feature, but the response time can be a bit slow at times. It's not a dealbreaker, but it can be a little frustrating when I'm in a hurry. On the other hand, the on-wrist call quality is fantastic and I can easily make and receive calls without having to take out my phone. People receiving the call from me from the watch commented that the audio is very clear.

The sleep monitoring is very effective and is comparable to that of Fitbit, which is known for its outstanding/accurate sleep tracking. I have tested it a few nights of wearing it to bed on one wrist and my Fitbit on the other, and it was able to create a very similar light/deep/rem pattern as my Fitbit watch. That really surprised me.

The app is nothing fancy (e.g. it doesn’t have those comprehensive programs and challenges like Fitbit), but it gets the job done. There are a lot of surprisingly good watch faces to pick from, and I think some watch faces actually look better than the ones provided by Sense. It is also worth calling out that there is no payment integration for this watch, so if you plan to use your watch to pay often, then this is not going to support that.

Overall, I'm happy with this smartwatch and would recommend it to anyone looking for a reliable and long-lasting device with a much lower price point compared to named brands. The biggest drawback to me would be the lack of continuous heart rate monitoring. However, if you value a watch with great battery life, on-wrist call quality, cross-platform compatibility, and effective sleep tracking, this is definitely worth considering.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:57b3e313c7d528c2fd1025525885938f]] I bought this watch and it looks very nice.
For its quality price, it is just what you get.

It does exactly what the seller's description says.
• Watch case
• Smart watch
• Magnetized charger cable
• 2 Bracelets
• Bracelet pins
• Instruction manual in 5 languages ​​(English, French, German, Italian and Spanish)

First you must scan the QR code that appears in the instruction manual to download the application and through it is where you will link the smart watch and your cell phone.

good points
• The battery is excellent. I fully charged it when it came into my hands, then I set it up and 4 days have passed and the battery remains at 90%.
• You can monitor your pace (Steps, Distances and Calories)
• You can see the notifications that come to your cell phone.
• You can see the weather
• Tracks how long you've been sleeping
• Measures heart rate. But I only entrust these health measures to specialized devices for that, because health is something very delicate.
• It also measures oxygen in the blood. This is another function that I would not use, due to what was explained in the previous point.
• Timing for sports. This point is favorable, because it measures the time we had doing an exercise.
• You can pause, play, skip forward and backward multimedia or streaming content.
• You can change the wallpapers of the smart watch from the same device or through the OnWear app.

Points to improve
• The user experience in multimedia control. When you pause or play the music, the pause symbol always remains, you only realize that it has paused or played, when the state of the cell phone changes.
• In the settings of the OnWear app, I consider that it is more user-friendly that all measurements can be changed separately. You only have two options: Standard, which comes by default, and the United States. It would be interesting if the same user is the one who decides if he wants to use Kilos or Pounds, Kilometers or Miles, Degrees Celsius or Degrees Fahrenheir.

Make those improvements to the software and you will have an excellent value for money product.

In the end, I recommend it 100%. I am going to buy another one for my wife.

I've owned this device for approximately two weeks and I consider it a three star product. Sadly, I have to return this item because the silicon(ish) covering on one side of the watch band is delaminating from the band underneath and the mode button has started becoming stuck in the depressed position and no longer crisply clicks when I press it. Really disappointing for a two week old product and the price point. I'm limited to devices without WIFI or third party apps due to my work place policies which drives me to this device and other simpler devices.

I do not swim with this tracker and I do not wear it in the shower. I wear it 24/7 other than to shower or when I charge it.

Pros:
-The watch band is conformatble and stretches nicely. It's not so rigid that it hurts when it's snugged down to ***accurately(ish)*** track heart rate
-Traditional watch band style clasp is nice.
-BATTERY life is excellent compared to the Samsung Gear Fit 2 I previously owned.

Cons:
-It's thick! Cuffs on long sleeve shirts and sweatshirts get caught on it and make it awkard. The silicone watch band tends to drag and catch on my bedding and pillows at night which is annoying.
-The auto backlight sometimes doesn't work well sometimes. It fails to turn on when I look at my watch.
-The Garmin connect app really shows a tremendous amount of information. Once nagging point is that the basal metabolic rate is not accurate and you have to futz around with your activity level setting and check the calories burned from resting to get the basal metabolic rate accurate.
-My watch band is delaminating after two weeks.
-The mode button is sticking in the depressed position and doesn't crisply click anymore.
-A software update came through automatically two days ago. Now I get all kinds of BT connection erros until I reset the BT on my phone.
-The curved edges of the display bezel make adhering protective films difficult to impossile.
-The autopause feature sucks. When I run, I typically warm up with increasingly fast walking. I start my exercise on my VS HR+ and it starts autopausing while walking meaning I have to turn off autopause. Then, without auto-pause, there's no way to pause it manually during an exercise. I occassionally have to pause running on the treadmill. The pause function should work better than this!

I bought this about a year and a half ago and it is still going strong. I haven't had to change the battery yet. The band broke yesterday and I just ordered a $7 dollar replacement. I initially but it to replace a jawbone up 24,

--------------------

Review:
I have been using the Garmin Vivoactive for a couple months now. I actually purchased the Fitbit Blaze prior to this watch. The Blaze was OK, but it ended up leaving a rash on my wrist. So I returned it and bought the Garmin Vivoactive. So far it has been great! I have used it for running, biking, and swimming. I have also used the chest heart rate monitor for running and biking, as sometimes I like to train in certain heart rate zones. The heart rate monitor seems to be accurate. Use this with the Garmin Connect App. The app was not difficult to set up. However, it is not quite as intuitive as the Fitbit Blaze. It took me a while to figure out that to change watch faces you have to use the app. On the plus side, I love how many watch faces are available. I also really like the data available for all the activities. Running includes average pace, moving pace (love this!), speed, time, cadence, stride length, elevation, and calories. It also does lap pace (e.g. mine is set up for 1.0mi lap). Biking includes distance, time, speed, average moving speed, elevation, and calories. Swimming (note: this watch if ONLY GOOD for POOL swimming. GPS cannot track open water swims). Swimming includes time, pace, distance, stroke rate, SWOLF, speed.

This watch also works as a regular activity tracker and keeps track of steps. It automatically adjusts your step goals based on how active you are every day. It will buzz and tell you to move if you have been inactive for too long.

It also gives notifications. I like the text notifcations because I can quickly read something without getting out my phone. I also like the call notifications because often my phone is on silent or just too quiet to hear, but I can't miss the buzzing on my wrist!

And lastly, it also tracks sleep. I do wear the watch when I sleep, but I don't really pay attention to the sleep data because I don't really see the point.

The battery life is really fantastic. I hesitated getting a watch with GPS simply because I expected the battery to drain. But I've been very surprised. I feel like I never have to charge this! Once a week at most, and it charges very quickly. I do exercise fairly regularly and I use the GPS for running and biking, and the battery has still held up great.

Review:
I love smartwatches. They tend to justify Earthmen's fascination with digital watches once and for all!

This watch comes with a beautiful brown band that works well with the silver bevel. I haven't figured out what triggers the illumination of the numbers on the bevel, but when it lights up it looks extra sweet! It lights with a cooling light blue that is easy on the eyes.

There are several faces to choose from in many styles. The selection is varied enough to make this a perfect gift for just about ANYONE! Changing faces on this watch is a BREEZE! I changed the face three or four times before I even synced it with my phone!

The battery life is apparently just as described! A close examination of the attached picture will reveal an approximate 75% battery meter. I had been wearing this watch for three full days and had another day unworn off the charger! That's amazing!

This is very lightweight for a smartwatch! It feels like a standard watch. This is the first smartwatch I've tried and felt comfortable wearing overnight!

The negatives:

Text messages come through mostly unreadable. You'll be able to tell who sent the message and likely get the gist, but that's it. This is a disappointment.

You can adjust measurements from metric to imperial, but there is no setting for Fahrenheit vs. Celsius. Knowing the current temp in Celsius is useless for me. Hopefully the developers will provide an update for this tiny issue.

I figured the two of those added to 1 star off. A couple of software updates could fix that, in which case I will revisit this review.

The GPS when you use it kills battery 25% per hour. So a fully charged, straight off the charger and out for a run/walk will be completely dead in 4 hours if your using GPS during your run/walk/bike ride, etc.
The band itself is very soft and comfortable, but does cause irritation (itchy red spots) on the back side of my wrist where the metal clasp is.
Flight (stairs) counting is hit or miss on accuracy. I was out camping this weekend literally sitting in a campsite, and got 9 flights of stairs while doing so during day 1st day?? (yes- it was a level campsite literally at sea level (ocean front), no hills anywhere around, just a flat spot of ground where tent/fire-pit/picnic table sat and I got 9 flights of stairs sitting in a camp chair in front of fire, with a few trips back and forth to cooler/picnic table a few feet away). Other days, while at work I take multiple flights of stairs everyday (3 floors up 3 floors down) and will somedays record flights accurately, and somedays not give me any flights of stairs at all.
GPS 80% of time finds the satellites within 1 min. BUT the other 20% of time, Im standing around waiting seriously 15-20 minutes in wide open sky area for it to find the satellites (wait...wait...wait) which annoys my running/biking partners.
As I stated, Ive owned other GPS Garmin watches, and also FitBits, and Polars, and really really wanted to love this watch. So its not that Im unfamiliar with how to use them, or don't know GPS sucks battery life (it does on all watches), But I think Im gonna return it. I can't trust its accuracy (steps and flights of stairs while sitting in a campground not walking or climbing hills), its battery life (2 1/2 days without workout mode being used, 4 hours or less when workout mode with GPS), or that it will even find GPS satellites within a timely fashion (i.e. less then 15-20 minutes).
If your purchasing strictly as your first fitness watch and want to use as a motivation tool to get up and move and track your steps each day - its a great watch in those regards and it will motivate you to get up and move!. If your an experienced fitness watch user, and expect it to work with similar accuracy of other fitness watches, move along.
I think if Garmin beefed up the battery similar to the GarminActive, added Glonass, and a true altimeter for measuring elevation gain/loss, it would solve all 3 of these issues.

I paid less than $80 for this smart watch yet it has the design and most of the functionality of name brand smart watches costing up to 10x more.

The decent sized 1.32" (272ppi) HD Screen is extremely bright and the touch screen is very responsive. The watch has a composite fiber watch body and a stainless steel crown.

The watch has some really useful features such as making and receiving calls, text messages, displaying text alerts and notifications, as well as activity and sleep monitoring.

The watch is capable of extensive activity tracking (over 20 modes) and health monitoring including:
- Number of steps and distance travelled while walking and running
- Calories burned while walking and running
- Real-time heart rate and blood oxygen level (including low oxygen reminder)
- Sleep tracking (24H real-time monitoring)

The battery will provide continuous power for up to 7 days (under average use). Also, with an IP68 rating, the device can withstand being submerged in water below one meter for more than 30 minutes.

I ran into a few issues getting the device connected to my WiFi using the free app, however I overcame these issues after a several attempts. Otherwise, I'm really happy with this purchase.

Review:
So far I'm very happy with the Vivofit. I wanted an activity tracker, but didn't want to wear one with a watch. So I needed one that also functioned AS a watch. The battery life is pretty good - roughly 5 days with text/call notifications. I think battery life would decrease the more notifications you get. You can set it up to get notifications from many different apps. It works well with the heart monitor. I also like the mp3 control.

I have bluetooth in my truck so my phone automatically connects to my truck when I get in (even with the Vivofit on). When I get out of the truck, it automatically connects to the phone for mp3 control, etc.

I initially had some difficulty syncing, but after updating the software on the phone and the Vivofit, it has worked flawlessly.
