Review:
First of all, I will say that this is my first smartwatch so I have no others to compare it to. I mainly bought this watch for the gym and for running to use as a fitness tracker and music player. This was the best deal i could find that gave me offline music, a pedometer, GPS, heart rate monitoring, etc. I really don't need notifications and other things because I can get them on my phone easily. I mainly wanted something convenient to use when I didn't have my phone with me.

Positives:
Setup was very easy and only took a couple seconds to pair with my phone. I like the feel of the band, it is a very soft feeling rubber material. It feels light and comfortable to wear. The controls are easy enough to understand but it took a while to get the hang of a few things.

Negatives:
What really bothers me is that I cannot find the watch face from the image advertised anywhere. That was one of the main things that attracted me to the Ticwatch E, and I expected it to be the default face or at least one of the pre-installed ones. But I can't find it anywhere, and now I feel lied to and like it was falsely advertised. It just leaves a very bad taste in my mouth.

The watch itself is a little bulky, I would have preferred something a little lower profile but it's not horrible.

I had a little trouble finding certain wifi networks, but I found an app in the store that helps a little. It was a little complicated to install, because when I originally needed it I didn't have wifi so no way to install it. I ended up turning my phone into a hotspot, connected my watch to it, then installed the app, then was able to connect to the wifi network I needed.

One thing that would be nice is to be able to tell how much storage on the watch is being used. I tried to load a Google Play Music playlist but it stopped half-way through. I assume this was because I was out of storage but I couldn't really tell. I thought the 4 GB would be enough but when you account for the OS and apps it doesn't leave much room for anything else. It would be nice if I could fit some more music on it.

Battery life seems pretty horrible, it's annoying to have to charge it every night (not to mention it turns itself on in the middle of the night and wakes me up). I guess I'm just used to my old fitbit what lasted a few days. I'll try decreasing the brightness, using battery saver, and turn it on airplane mode when I'm not really using it and see how it goes.

But at the end of the day, it does what I wanted it to do. It plays music and tracks my workouts at the gym without needing my phone. It will track my outdoor workouts with GPS. It will even give me a heart rate reading but I'm not sure how accurate that is. Music players with belts, clips, or cords always seemed to get in the way, haven't had that problem with the watch yet.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: Battery life seems pretty horrible, it's annoying to have to charge it every night. I'll try decreasing the brightness, using battery saver, and turn it on airplane mode when I'm not really using it and see how it goes.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a smart watch with a battery life that does not require charging every night. The customer is willing to tolerate having to decrease brightness, use battery saver, and enable airplane mode when not in use to manage battery life.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
I needed a Wear OS watch to go with my Google Pixel 3 smartphone. I tried ones from Fossil, Diesel and even a Tiezen one from Samsung, but I always came back to the TicWatch Pro. The reviews maligned the device and the choice by the developers to have their own health app in place of the Google Fit, so I was cautious. I decided to purchase this and am very glad I did.

This thing is pretty innovative in how it preserves battery. It has 2 screens a full color Amoled one and a FSTN digital one overlaying that. When the Amoled screen times out it goes back the the "Essential Mode" screen which is to say the low powered digital screen. After setup and the normal time for Wear OS to index and calm down, I am getting some pretty good battery life. With raise and touch to wake on, wifi/bluetooth, NFC and GPS all on I am getting over 2 days of use, thanks in part to the timeout to the essential mode screen. When I leave always on, I still get a day and a half of use. Additionally if I put it into Essential Only mode, it lasts for weeks. Additionally I found that if I go into a workout the essential screen shows workout data that is germain to my activity (Very cool and battery saving)

Despite having an older processor, this thing is much faster than the Fossil gen 4's I used. Additionally a very cool touch that the other watches did not have is a speaker (So you can take calls dick tracy style) and a magnetometer so you can use compass apps and have better walking directions with the Google Maps app.

Lastly the band is really really nice with leather on the top and stitched into a rubber band on the back. The band smells of fine leather when you take it out of the packaging, and remains handsome in day to day use. This is a fine looking watch as well and wears well on larger wrists.

If there is one criticism, it is the one you hear everywhere. I wish they let you change the right to left swipe gesture to pick Google Fit over their Mobvoi health app, or at least allow the choice, but in practice their app has been non intrusive and really not bad at all. I even use it a little more than Google Fit, so maybe they are on to something.

All in all I really love this watch and I am very critical on products and will return something that does not work for me. I cant wait to see what Mobvoi can do with the newer Snapdraggon chipsets. They have certainly made the most of the gen it has. Also did I mention the price. Got this for a black Friday price of $199 and got a free activity tracker with it, that also turned out to be really excellent and something my wife wears all the time (Quite a feat). I will check back in 6 months to add more to the review but I think I am still going to be happy. Did I mention I recently came from the Apple camp and had Apple Watches since their inception? If I can be happy with this watch, then I think anyone can. Great piece of kit.

Review:

Review:
I test this watch for a week before doing this review, Because I don't want to judge anything at first glance.

Disappoint with battery at first. **BUT, That not the issue at all compare to other even the Apple Watch.

Turn on the watch for the first time, It's take around 3 hours to full charge the watch. And battery drop to 5% like in 4-5 hours with always-on-screen OFF, Bluetooth ON, GPS ON, Wifi OFF.

Here's what I did to the watch:
- After full cycle the battery 100%-0% for like 5-10 times. Now, I can use it from 8:00AM in the morning till 9:00PM with 30% battery left.
- Overall thing is pretty good for an Android Wear App.
- High Quality Plastic Housing matched with my black workout outfit.

When this happens the S10 shuts down and you have to start the process all over again.  Many times I have played more than 1 hole without course data due to this problem.  The club I belong to has 3 nines with first tees in close proximity to each other and this exacerbates the problem too.  Please see the attached picture.  The S10 wrist band is very poorly attached to the range finder. Review:
Up front, let me say that I bought this device because my doctor suggested I use a FitBit to track my weird heart rate. Clearly, this is not what the device is designed for and not what most people use it for. Still, I think I can help others determine whether it is the right device for themselves.

SET-UP: Pairing and set-up were fast and easy. The instructions that come with the device are clear and easy to understand.

CHARGING: I love how this device charges. One side of the wrist band comes off, then the charger can be plugged in for charging. This is so much less cumbersome than using a cord. The tracker charges fully in 1 hour.

APP: The app is self-explanatory, easy to use, and makes it easy to see your stats at a glance. It has one little defect, which I will discuss in a moment, but for most people, it probably would not make a difference. Through the app, you can take your blood pressure (which, when compared to my old school blood pressure cuff, seems surprisingly accurate), see your heart rate throughout the day, see your sleep data (including sleep duration, how many times you woke, when you fell asleep and when you woke, how long you were in deep sleep and how long you were in light sleep), and your "sport" info (which I did not use). There's also a running mode that shows you, through GPS, how many miles you've run. In addition, you can set the device to alarm (vibrate) when your heart rate goes above a beat per minute that you designate.

COMFORT: The band on this device seems sturdy and is soft enough to be comfortable. I had no trouble sleeping in it. It's a lot more comfortable than my FitBit.

CONS: The big con for me was two-fold: First, while the description on Amazon says that the device monitors your heart rate continuously, you have to tap the screen several times to "scroll" and get to the screen where your heart rate is displayed, then it takes several seconds for the heart rate to appear. In addition, the app only records and graphs your heart rate every half hour. For me, these features just wouldn't work. I needed a tracker that would quickly and easily let me see my heart rate and would graph my heart rate continuously throughout the day.

CONCLUSION: If you just want a fitness tracker and are debating between this and a FitBit, buy the ZURURU! The price may seem to be too good to be true, but honestly, I far prefer this tracker and its app to the FitBit I ended up with. IF the makers would just allow me to see my heart rate more easily, and include in the graph ALL my hearr beat data, I would still be using ZURURU .

Review:
This is the first true smartwatch I have owned, prior to this I had a Garmin vivosmart HR. I was looking for a smart watch with more functionality however I didn't want to spend a lot of money. This watch seemed to have most of what I was looking for at a price I was looking to spend. The step count doesn't seem to be very accurate however other workout routines seem to be very accurate when detecting reps. The heart rate monitor also seems to be fairly accurate for being a wrist based monitor. I haven't checked the accuracy of it against my chest strap monitor when riding, though I plan to whenever spring weather returns. I really like integrated GPS feature on this watch. I've learned that it's best to activate the GPS via the activity so that it has time to pick up the signal. It didn't pick up on my location until I was a 1/4 mile into my run the first time. Thereafter I start the app immediately once I'm outside and do some stretches before I start the run. It immediately starts up when it detects the movement and tracks my progress from there. Now that I give it time to pick up on my location it tracks the entire workout and displays it accurately on the map. It's nice not having to carry my phone with me when I run now. I haven't been able to load any music on the device, it seems like it requires a paid google music service or music purchased through google music in order to be able to download to it.
I have worked with the customer service on this watch since I haven't been entirely happy with the battery life. They have been fairly responsive with me regarding my concerns however not much has changed. I have learned that disabling the WiFi does help with battery life however it still doesn't seem to do as well as it should. I turn the watch off at night when I go to bed to preserve battery life, I don't get a full 24 hours on a single charge. I suppose that I could keep it on the charger over night however I don't believe that is recommended. I won't do that with any of my devices. I ordered an additional charging cable so that I can leave one at work, that way I can charge it as needed. Other than the battery life of this device and the inaccurate step counts I have been fairly pleased. To be fair, I have not mentioned the in accuracy of the step counts to Mobvoi as this is only a minor annoyance to me, I monitor my step counts to show me when I'm not as active as normal.

Negatives:

Overall, I am more than happy with this smart watch and would purchase it again without a second thought! I think the price of this smart watch is way more than fair and low for what you are receiving. I would have given this smart watch 10 stars if I could have! I highly recommend this smart watch!

Review:
First day with my new Lg G smart watch R W110 4gb 1.3" Oled screen. I just got it, and thanks to a recommendation from M. P. Mac Runnel "CPU-MD" from Sterling, Virginia, I also got mine from a traditional AT&T store. Not so much for the immediate gratification but because it is $70.00 less than at Amazon. com (thank you Mac Runnel). So I highly recommend you do the same, but beware, as you may come across clueless sales people who do not have any idea what they are selling. Take for instance the guy who sold mine, at the AT&T store in Aventura, Florida. This so call “AT&T sales advisor” was so clueless, so uninformed about the products in the store, that he kept insisting I was buying the Moto 360 (both are displayed next to each other), even though I turned the Lg G smart watch R W110 upside down and pointed at LG logo in the back. Unable to acknowledge his mistake he went to the back of the store saying, “Let me go get your Moto smart watch…” Go figure.

Anyhow, after having waited six months for this watch, after having read lots of reviews and compared it to many other smartwatches, I chose the Lg G r as my first smartwatch because of its circular shape and Oled screen. Unlike other smartwatches, the Lg G r looks and feels like a regular watch. A very nice- looking regular watch I must say. And to me this is a welcome departure from the traditional square, or rectangular screen, of most smartwatches. So if you are into this kind of look and feel of a more traditional watch, the Lg G r is the way to go. But that is pretty much where the tradition ends, because everything else about this watch screams new technology. The Lg G r has a brilliant Oled screen which allows for uncompromising viewing under any lighting conditions, many different analog and digital faces to choose from and various different wearable modes. The watch is very light, and yet feels solid and well built. It is very intuitive to use and since it came out of the box pre-charged, turning it on, and pairing it up with my android phone, was a breeze. But keep in mind that before you can pair it up you must download and install the Android Wear app from Google Play and agree to a bunch of new permissions and accesses. The cost of doing business I guess. And talking about cost, the Lg G smart watch R W110 can be seen as an expensive piece of wearable technology at the price point of $299. The watch comes with one USB cable, one wall charger, and a single charging cradle. The printed manual is so tiny you better have your reading glasses handy, or someone with eagle eye sight nearby, for assistance.

As of yet I am very pleased with my new Lg G smart watch R W110 and still learning and exploring all the possibilities. But I would be amiss if I failed to mention that for such a nice looking watch, with a rather expensive price tag, the wristband is crap. It looks crap and it feels crap, although it’s stamped in the back with Genuine Leather, to me it looks more like cardboard.

Review:
The Forerunner 235 is an older Garmin model and it certainly shows its age. The interface - buttons on the watch - feel very clunky and the navigation through the screens has considerable lag. The Bluetooth connection to a smartphone is also intermittent and repeatedly connects and then disconnects, each time buzzing annoyingly. The Forerunner 235 also lacks many of the features on newer models such as stress level, activity minutes, and floors climbed.

Furthermore, the battery life is pathetic, lasting about two days without using the GPS and around 4 hours with the GPS. The watch only comes with three activities - run, indoor run, and other - with additional activities coming from third-parties available on the connectIQ store. The connectIQ store is a positive because it allows you to download additional apps, activities, widgets, and app faces, but many of the apps from the store work very slowly or not at all on the Forerunner 235.

Overall, the Forerunner 235 was a disappointing activity-tracking watch from Garmin. My recommendation is to avoid this older model and pay slightly more for a newer model that offers a better experience.

Special Features: Time Display, GPS, Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I was soooo excited when the Vivoactive 3 was announced. I had a FR235 and I was so excited to upgrade. I went and bought it on release day in October.

The flat HR monitor was a huge upgrade from the giant bump on the FR235. I find it to be accurate.

For running, the Vivoactive 3 is awesome. The gps is spot on, however it always took my VA3 2-3 minutes to connect, much longer than the forerunner and now much longer than the fitbit ionic I replaced it with, which takes 30 seconds max to connect. I loved the cadence and running stats on the VA3 and that’s the biggest loss I am experiencing in moving to the ionic. The battery life is great on the VA3. It is an attractive watch also.

Apart from that, the rest of the Vivoactive 3 was just flat out disappointing. The bezel scratched way too easy. I found the touch screen and scrolling to be unresponsive sometimes.

Here are the real issues and why I decided to leave Garmin (and not just upgrade to the Fenix 5):

-the sleep tracking is horrible. It’s basically the sleep data that fitbit gave you 3 or 4 years ago. No details on light/deep/REM sleep. This was a big deal to me as I’ve been having trouble sleeping the past few months.

-the watch would lose connection to my iPhone every single day. A few times a week, I would have to go into the settings in Garmin Connect and remove the device. Then spent 20 minutes trying to reconnect it and troubleshooting. It got to the point where I was just done. This made music controls basically unuseable during my runs.

-when connected, the watch would take (not kidding) 4-5 minutes to sync over Bluetooth. The fitbit takes about 30 seconds to sync.

I moved onto the fitbit ionic, which admittedly is a step down for running. No cadence data has been a big loss but I just ordered Lumo run to help with my form and cadence. For everything else I am looking for, the ionic has fulfilled all of my needs. The sleep tracking is great, gps while running is great. Notifications are great, music control is great. Heart rate is accurate.

The only downsides to the fitbit are battery life is shorter, the watch itself is ugly and doesn’t have an always on screen, I’ve lost cadence data and the data fields while running are severely limited compared to the VA3 (only 3 fields with one as a swipe through other stats field). The fitbit wins, at least for my needs, and for reliability.

Special Features: Notifications
Review:
This is an unbelievable value in a Smartwatch; looks like a "watch", works like a watch, but acts like an extension of your Smartphone with "Notifications" at your wrist. Notifications of whose Calling/Sms, what's on your Calendar Events/ToDo, and every other notification your other Apps post in Phone's notification tray, per your choice. You are enabled to decide at a glance at your watch to either ignore, dismiss, or snooze a notify/alert. No more digging out your phone for unimportant stuff, and alerts are a quiet "vibrate" on the wrist, not annoying sounds.

Other very helpful Notify/Alerts; you have walked away without your phone, you want to ring your phone to find it, your phone battery need a charge, you are travelling and want another timezone time in addition to the watchface time, you want to know the weather conditions in a set location quickly.

I know you're saying this is all done on other AndroidWear watches. Yes, but this watch does it without a huge and nerdy looking 'square' watch, and with a watch that keeps time for 2yrs without a battery charge/change, and with a low $40 price from a highly reliable USA Smartwatch company. As an example, Martian is the company Seiko's Guess Watch Division chose to make their "Guess Connect" watch Smart.

Anyway, if you want a traditional looking and reliable Smartwatch that is easy on the eyes and your pocketbook, this is a fantastic bargain. If you want an even classier upscale version of this watch, with more features like phone call "Talk&Listen" etc, then look at either the Guess Connect Watch line...or the Martian Voice Watch line.... in the $200-$300 range. My experience is using this watch with an Android Smartphone, Samsung Galaxy Nexus . Your mileage may very somewhat.

Disclaimer: No I don't work for Martians;), or have gotten anything from them or Amazon for this review. I'm just so impressed they are offering us, the masses, this technology at this low starting price. Now I'm saving for that Martian Active or Guess Connect watch:)

Review:
This product contains the following:
1 Smart Watch
1 Charge cable with unique magnetic connect end and USB A on other end
1 User Manual

This Smart Watch connects via Bluetooth 5.0. So, make sure your phone or device can support at least 5.0. If you have a 4.0 or lower in your phone, it won't see this Smart Watch. The manual included has multiple languages. The watch band on this is flexible and comfortable. The back of the watch does have a sensor that needs to be in skin contact in order for it to work. It'll not work when worn over a sleeve. After charging it for the first time, the battery status is still at 100% after 6 hours. With a standby time of 5-8 days, this item only need charging weekly instead of daily. Set up is pretty easy. First, go to the settings app on your phone. The choose connections -> bluetooth. a "C7" option should be available to connect to or pair with. Tap on "C7" and choose pair. Now to connect an app to the Smart Watch:
From the main screen, swipe from top to bottom to access the setup (cog) icon. When you tap on the setup icon, then scroll down to the bottom of the list and there's QR code option. Open that up. Next get your phone to scan the APP QR Code and open link. Install the APP. Open the App once it finishes installing. It'll ask for the basics in like birthday, Height, and weight. Next go to settings -> Devices and choose "C7". You're now paired and connected to the APP on your phone. The manual has a lot of information about the functions of the Smart Watch. So far, this item has been fun to use and not too hard to get used. I would recommend.

Review: This is a nice affordable smartwatch. I was skeptical at first because I've purchased smartwatches elsewhere and was disappointed in the quality and battery life - this watch surprised me on both counts. Battery life lasts me about a week on a full charge. The magnetic contact charging connector/cable snaps into place right away and easily to the back of the watch (don't lose this cable it's not a standard micro USB cable!) The colorful watch display is crisp and clear and offers at least a half dozen default displays including analog/digital. The watch comes with 2 types of wrist straps metal and silicone, and all the hardware to attach both - I opted for the metal band which I think looks more attractive. It snaps on easy but can be tricky to get used to. The tiny spring pins are tricky to attach but you could always have a jeweler do it. The bluetooth connection was easy to my android phone (iOS is available too). The app lets you track of many health related metrics - pulse, calories and even bp but not sure how accurate that is. Right now I mainly use it to track my daily goal of 8000 steps/day. You can receive and make calls and texts and even used the watch as a speakerphone which sound loud and clear - I prefer to use the phone itself but that is a personal preference - the watch is very loud and clear. Really like it so far. Any dislikes? The only item would be the very tiny print of the tiny instruction booklet just wish that were a bit larger - not enough for me to ding a star. Packaging was good.

Review:
After buying this watch, I noticed an uptick of reviews about Amazfit. I can see why this is an amazing piece of hardware and the battery life is off the charts! I've only charged it twice since I got it and each time there was at least 50% battery left. That was more than a month ago.

I wanted a replacement for my fitbit and ZenWatch and this meets those objectives. The battery life (as I mentioned) makes the ZenWatch look like a toy. At the time I replaced it I was lucky I was getting 6 hrs on a charge.

I did some research before I replaced my Fitbit and this watch tracks almost everything I want. The one thing missing (for me) is fluid intake.

For the moment, I'm wearing both and I've noticed the FitBit step tracker completes my step goal about 500 steps before the GTR. I guess I'll need to work harder.

CONS:
- The mobile app is a mess. It's hard to navigate and something I miss (i.e., my Fitbit detects when I start a walk and starts tracking.) This doesn't, you have to start that routine. But GPS tracking is phenomenal.
- Watchfaces - The GTR has limited watchfaces and only stores 3 on the watch. 2 of those are permanent and you can't change them. That is a big miss for me. My ZenWatch allowed me to store more and change as needed without having to resync from the phone.
- I need a second application to better manage my notifications and tracking

I did want an Android wear watch for better integration with my apps, but I'll live with this. I'm still learning what the watch can and can not do.

Overall, I'm pleased with the purchase and would buy another one if I had to. Its well worth the money

Special Features: Heart Rate Monitor
Review:
I've been using this for about a month now, and really, REALLY like it. If I had one complaint, it's that the bluetooth radio in this watch doesn't seem to have quite the range of my Pebble Time Round, so disconnections happen more often. That said, bluetooth runs on similar wavelengths to cordless phones and microwaves, so various devices interfere. If I'm in the kitchen and the microwave is turned on, it's not uncommon to lose the connection. Nature of the beast with bluetooth.

Still, the screen is MUCH brighter than on the Round, so I'm happy with the trade-off. Don't know if the color on the round adds a layer, but that would explain it. Contrast is much better. Add to that the battery life (I hit 60% after FOUR DAYS, but I also probably get fewer notifications than most people), and I'm sold. The heart rate sensor is what it is -- optical heart rate measurement at the wrist is inherently unreliable at this point in the technology's development. That said, I find it tracks fairly accurately, though especially in sweaty, jiggly moments (exercise), the numbers can fluctuate. It's not a chest strap, and it's pretty unreasonable to expect similar results in my opinion. Voice reply to text messages (I'm on android, if that matters), was a surprising home run. Hint -- if you say "Question mark", etc, it adds the appropriate punctuation.

Software could use tweaks and development, but since Pebble went under, don't plan on that ever happening. Fitbit is currently committing to keeping the services running through thet end of 2017, or at least that's how their press release reads to me, and the Peppble developers hired by Fitbit claims they are trying to make the watch less reliant on cloud services (UPDATE: This has been done, though certain cloud services may still go away at some point), and the developer community may also work on these things, but right now I'd assume that if you buy this, plan on some functions not working after 2017. Which is sad -- I love these watches. Still, if you can find one used or on sale at a serious discount (like I did), it might be worth it to you. Also, there is a third party Android app that purports to run most of the watch's functions without the Pebble app or cloud servers (Can't remember the name, and I haven't used it so can't say how it works) that some like. At any rate, it seems some of the fuctionality of the watch that is not cloud-based will contionue to work well beyond 2017, but things like weather updates and voice-to-text services? To be safe, I wouldn't plan on it.

If the company was still up and healthy, I'd recomend this watch to most people in a heartbeat. If you're looking for a watch that does notifications with tremendous battery life and is always-on, it seems that these functions will continue to work. But it's still a gamble -- there is NO guaranteed support continuing into the future from either Fitbit or Pebble -- and that includes warranty support!

Oh -- and get a screen protector. Yes, it's gorilla glass, but that stuff isn't indestructible. Scratched the hell out of my Pebble Time Round absentmindedly brushing it against a stone wall as I walked. All it takes is a single moment of distraction to thunk your wrist against something, and your watch screen is now scratched.

Review:
This is somewhere in-between a full feature smart watch and a fitness tracker. The build quality is good and it feels solid on your wrist. The battery life has been good so far. I would anticipate charging it once a week so you don't have to worry about it dying on you. The bubble menu is fun to use. There are tons of face designs to choose from. At least 50+ designs. They are interactive and add a nice level customization. The vibration for alarms is strong enough to get my attention, which is great considering this is a common issue with many entry level smart watches. You won't have any problems waking up in the morning. It uses the Da Fit app, which is one of the more established apps out there and can easily connect to Googie Fit on the Android version. I mainly use Android but I did break out my daughter's iPhone to test the iOS version. Both versions of the app worked well, easy to pair, and relatively easy to setup the notification settings to your liking. Android is always more of a pain to setup with permissions, but I found Da Fit to be easier to setup than Mi Fit (a competitor's app). The blood pressure feature is surprisingly accurate. You still need to sit upright, don't cross your legs, and keep your arm level for it to capture an accurate reading. I would not base any medical decisions on it, but it is nice to be able to check your BP when out and about. Heart rate and sleep monitoring work as they should. The app does a good job displaying graphs and so forth for the different metrics.

Issues that I hope the manufacture will address in a future update:
- There are only 3 alarms by default. I use three alarms during the day, another two in the mornings to make sure I get up early enough, and one more twice a week that I have standing appointments. Three alarms are just not enough. Please add the ability to set more alarms. I like the menu system and the features for each alarm, I just need more of them.

- Rowing or Elliptical machine is not an option under the sport modes. Even a generic option for exercise equipment. I don't need 30 different sport modes like some smart watches have but I would like something close to one of the common indoor experience machines.

Overall:
I am a fan of the design, menu system, and it's companion app. I would like to see a few features updated but they are not deal breakers. This is a solid choice for a smart watch / fitness tracker that won't break the bank.

Review:
I have had 2 Pebbles (God bless them), Amazfit BIP, Huawei GT and other less popular cheap brands.
I have a pretty standard Xiaomi phone running Android 10 with no bloatware.
I want a watch that does not require daily debugging or concerns. Tells me the time, I can see it outdoor, reliably relays notification, measures with some accuracy my vitals.

This is not it.
1- Biggest issue is that for mysterious reasons it stops showing notifications. Sometimes it notifies the phone that this has happened, sometimes it does not. When it tells you, the notification will ask you to restart the phone. First phone app/device EVER to asks me that. Some Microsoft engineer, I guess. OK, this happens 2 or 3 times a day. Do you want to restart your phone 3 times a day? I don’t.
2- Likely for the same reason, music control and find phone do not work
3- Support expertise is minimal. Twice I contacted Garmin via online support chat. Got the same lady both times (… strange…) After figuring that all the settings on my app, phone and watch were correct, without providing any insight, she offered to upgrade for free to a Forerunner 45. She justified the upgrade, in her words, because the F45 would work while music control widget had been discontinued on the F35 (?) Spoiler alert: the F45 has exactly the same issues.
4- There are FOUR BUTTONS to push to get to where you want to be. The menu structure and button sequences are horrendous whether you are right handed or not, it is more like learning to play piano.
I have had several GPSs from Garmin. Both wearable and not. Good products. This is poorly engineered both in the HW and in the SW department.
What’s good?
The display, the app presentation of your data, the website.
Battery duration, with identical usage is half that of the Amazfit BIP which is half the size.

Review:
So much to say (write) about this inexpensive yet versatile RAWNAIG Smart Watch! Packaged nicely and neatly in a sleek designed white box.
CONTENTS: Smart watch, user guide and charger cable.
UNIQUE CHARGING CABLE: I write unique because if anything ever happens, such as damage or loss to this cable, not sure where you can get another. It is unique in the fact there are 2 prongs that magnetically attach itself to the watch backside for charging. Very easy to shift cable and disconnect it, so my suggestion when charging is to place watch in a secure area that keeps watch and cable together until fully charged. Once fully charged, let the FUN begin!
Right out of the box, open booklet and scan QR Code. It downloads DaFit app and within minutes easy interface tells all. PAIRING with IPhone was also a breeze.
We highlighted the features we found immediately useful while still having fun exploring. Personal note: While I ordered the smart watch for myself, the Mrs. immediately loved the watch sleek design and its many features, so I of course gifted her the watch. She was used to a fit bit and now has so many features to explore on the Rawnaig watch.
TOUCH SCREEN: Controlling watch was easy using touch screen by sliding up/down/left/right.
BEST FEATURE: ( Our favorite) Makes and answers calls! Since RAWNAIG Smart watch has touch screen features, making/answering calls are very easy. Which leads us into our 2nd BEST FEATURE: Pairs easily with our earbuds. For CAlls or listening to MUSIC, very simple to operate.
BATTERY LEVEL: Clear and visible on the settings screen and also on the app, which the app gives battery percentage level. Almost 5 days of heavy usage ( learning the watch) and finally needs a charge! Impressive to say ( write) the least.
COOL FEAUTRE: Remote Shutter. We used the smart watch paired with our phone to take photos, great for that selfie at a distance when you are ready for the shot and not have to rely on someone passing by or timer.
WATCH FACES: A lot to choose from including current events and holiday references. All can be downloaded from the Smart watch.
WATERPROOF: As the manufacture suggests, not for swimming, but under light rain, we did not experience any issues.
Things we found so far that could use IMPROVEMENT:
TEXTS/MESSAGES: Can receive and see incoming texts but as the manufacture ad points out: Can't reply the messages through the smart watch, only supports reading messages, the watch audio needs to switch to the phone. This is the only disappointment but at this price we can live do without it.
WATCH BAND is soft and flexible (Texture like rubber). The excess band is placed UNDER the watch band itself against the wrist. Slight difficulty in removing the watch since the excess band rides against both wrist and band. TIP: We found placing a finger under the excess band and push to create a "loop" helps a lot.
USER GUIDE: Nearly useless but none the less good for quick reference only not instructions.
Lacks in reliability but can be FUN: Features such as: Blood pressure, Heart rate and Oxygen.
Other features to explore so have fun like we did but for now we Highly Recommend the RAWNAIG Smart Watch, especially at this price point! Thank you for reading and hopefully our family's fun and insight helps you too!

Review:
There are so many wonderful factors this watch has that I absolutely love over an Apple Watch. First and foremost, is the price. For all the features you receive on this watch, you simply can't beat the price. It does everything it states in the description that it says it would do. I wanted an activity watch that keeps up with my steps, blood pressure, heart rate, blood oxygen level and of course, my daily notifications i get from my iPhone. It suits these needs perfectly.

The second thing I absolute love about this watch is the battery's longetivity. You seriously don't have to charge it as often as you do with an Apple watch. In the beginning when I first had the watch, my battery life would only last 2 days. I was so frustrated because I wanted a watch that has a long battery life. I contacted the seller (who by the way, is TOP NOTCH) and explained my dilemma to them. They were extremely patient and VERY helpful. Come to find out, it was the "Quick View" option that was draining my battery. HELPFUL INFO: Turn this feature off and you will have a long-lasting battery life. It makes all the difference in the world. All you have to do is touch the screen to see the time and details when you turn this feature off. Not a biggie to me.

Third thing I love about the Umidigi watch is it's so lightweight. It does not feel like you have anything on your wrist. I am NOT a watch wearer and it was crititcal (to me) for the watch to be lightweight. I actually wear this watch all the time, even when I go to bed, which is great, since it has a sleep tracker on it.

Fourth positive thing is that AWESOME display. I am visually impaired and I wanted a watch I could actually SEE the time and details. This beautiful watch has such a vivid and colorful display. I love the simplicity of the watch features. It's not hard to figure out and with the many watch faces it has to choose from, you certainly can find one that will be perfect for you.

As we all know, nothing is perfect. There are few things this watch needs improvement on:

For most of you, this won't apply. I wear Mifi (Made for iPhone) Hearing Aids. More specificially, Otcion Opn. For some reason, this watch doesn't allow the phone to ring in my ears when I receive a phone call. This watch interferes with that transmission. Granted, the watch will vibrate and that lets me know I have an incoming call. But it will NOT ring if this watch is paired to my iPhone. Bothersome, yes, but not a deal-breaker. Maybe Umidigi will see this review and take tips on it for later improvements?? (Hint, hint)

Again, this next reason may be trivial to some, but for my OCD-ness, it drives me crazy. Whenever I receive a notification and it shows up on my phone, the screen splits up the words making it difficult to read. I will include a photo to show you what I am referring to. I wish it wouldn't split up the words. Again, not a deal-breaker but definitely a bit annoying.

This next "issue" may be big deal to some: You can NOT see the display outside. No matter which angle you hold your watch or how bright you have your screen set, you won't be able to see it outside, unless you find a dark spot somewhere. I'm hoping Umidigi will come out with a watch that allows you to see the screen outside.

Finally, last but not least, this watch does not have a speaker on it. You can't speak into it nor does it speak to you. But really, for the price, do you expect that?

I hope this review doesn't deter you from buying this watch. It's a REALLY nice watch. I know, I've tested several watches out and this wins, hands down.

Review:
I haven't owned or used a watch in almost 25 years. Yeah. I didn't really consider getting a smart watch, but figured I'd give this one a shot. Wow.

Okay, first I love the different monitors, oxygen, heart rate, and sleep monitors. I love the sleep monitor the most, but the others are great too. It seems like the heart rate is accurate, I couldn't say about the oxygen level monitor and the sleep monitor tends to get confused by lack of movement, I don't blame it, it's still great to be able to look back and see how good or messed up my nights sleep went. The stepometer seems pretty accurate and is usually less than 1% off from the one on my phone.

Second thing, this watch doesn't seem to like some power banks. I usually charge it when driving and it charges pretty quickly and with the right settings the battery life is great. As of writing this review it's been 4 days since I last charged it. I set it to never self wakes. I also keep it on the dimmest setting and night view, I prefer it and it's still easily visible.

Third, this thing is rugged. I didn't want to test this but had a whoops moment at the gym where it fell when I was taking it off and I watched in horror as it fell face down onto the tile floor. I dreaded picking it up, but I was extremely surprised to see very minimal and hardly noticeable damage to the screen. I was genuinely shocked, I'm a very tall man (6'7") and it was asily a 4ft drop. I tried to get the marks in the one picture where the screen is off.

Overall, I really like this smart watch. It's very lightweight and sometimes I even forget it's there when I'm wearing it. The size is perfect, much smaller and lighter than those old casios I wore as a kid/teen. I can't believe I finally got won over by a smart watch. It's just really nice.

I am using a different face than the standard one. I like having all the information at a glance. There's quite a bit of options on the app and on the watch, as well as many more different faces you can download for it. I like the customizability.

Review:
Update: Product got a software update on 09/02/16 v2.60. Step count is now consistent with LG smartphone app. Product now recognizes that stairs are part of my daily life though count is still not accurate.

Step count is consistently low, about 20-25%, so if your goal is 10,000 steps, enter 7500 instead. If your competing against someone with an accurate step counter, such as a 5$ walmart pedometer, you are going to lose. Stairs are a joke. Completely random if stairs will be counted or not. Review:
I recently purchased the Smart Watch for Men Fitness Tracker and I must say, it has exceeded my expectations. The watch is sleek and stylish, and the black strap gives it a classic look that goes well with any outfit.

One of my favorite features is the ability to make and answer calls directly from the watch. The Bluetooth connectivity is also reliable and has a good range, so I can leave my phone in my bag and still receive notifications and calls on my watch.

The fitness tracking features are also top-notch. It accurately tracks my steps, calories burned, and heart rate, which is great for monitoring my progress during workouts. The watch also has several different sports modes, such as running, cycling, and swimming, which allows me to track my workouts more accurately.

The battery life is impressive as well. I can easily go 7 days without having to charge the watch, even with regular use.

Overall, I highly recommend the Smart Watch for Men Fitness Tracker. It's a great investment for anyone who wants a reliable and stylish smartwatch that can also help them stay on top of their fitness goals

Review:
The watch itself is great. Battery lasts at least 12 hours with GPS active with a good 30%+ of charge left; great for long hikes and running ultras, etc. The triathlon functions are great. The swim functions and data are really useful and interesting (automatic indoor lap counter, swim efficiency, etc) and help guide your training.

It is Garmin Connect and the process of uploading activities that I have a problem with, which must be done wirelessly and with an internet connection that is active; usually in and of itself not a big deal. It is finicky though, to put it mildly. Garmin seems to make changes internally at its Connect website frequently (it is buggy), that render the process unworkable until they fix it. You will be wasting precious time doing menial tasks like reinstalling software; removing/re-adding devices; trying different workarounds, etc.; not to mention going back to the website every so often to try again. Not the type of stuff a time-crunched person will be relishing doing. It has gotten so bad recently (last 2 weeks) that I am prompted and motivated enough to waste precious time today to post this review. You can get most of the critical data off the watch's screens without uploading, but can't get all the data, or the charts or maps.

It is also easy to forget (when traveling) or misplace/lose the little ANT agent USB stick (and very expensive to replace). A simple upload process via the separate USB charging cable would have been convenient (like the older Garmins). There is nothing more convenient about having to use yet another piece of equipment to wirelessly upload data, when you have to connect the watch by USB cable everyday you use it ANYWAY to charge the darn thing. Garmin seems to be intent primarily in building their social universe via Garmin Connect (essentially Garmin's Borg), at the expense of their user's time and convenience. Even if you don't care about the privacy issues (I don't care at all), the problem is that you have to deal with the finicky and unreliable Garmin Connect website's uploading server.

I will be hard-pressed to buy another Garmin product ever again. i have bought/owned (gifts) many Garmin products with varying degrees of satisfaction from very low (e.g., 405 watch) to extremely high (e.g., 510 bike GPS). I no longer trust that this company has its act together enough to provide the utility and convenience I would expect for their premium prices.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
What does your device "watch"? If it is just the time then there are millions of choices, but for this purpose, I do enjoy the dual-screen on the TicWatch. I like quickly glancing at the time and peeking at my step count without having to turn on the bright screen. It is an added bonus to know that if the battery does run low, it will kick into essentials mode and I won't lose my steps.

For "watching" my phone, it is Google Wear 2.x so I knew how that worked already. I had a Asus Zenwatch2 (which I also highly recommend). Notifications are largely a matter of the user tuning things up. Speaking of that, a few tunings of a SmartWatch settings are also part of ownership, as you balance out your preferences for features vs battery life. This is not difficult, it just takes some thought and a day or two to fit the watch to your needs.

I rate how a smartwatch does "phone watching" by how much it decreases the number of times I have to pull my phone out of my pocket. Most certainly, the Ticwatch Pro has a positive metric there. Please don't underestimate that. You start noticing just how many times other people fidget with theirs, get fed up and leave their phone out on their desk or just don't bother to put it in their pocket anymore meaning they are always carrying something. Multiply this effort/energy savings by a hundred times a day and it adds to the quality of your life. I hate saying that, but it is really true if you pay attention. Plus, your phone battery will last longer, by a very noticeable percentage.

For "watching" indoor or outdoor fitness there are smartwatches dedicated to that purpose that would be better than the TicWatch Pro because they specialize in that use case. But for me, step count and activity time are the only two metrics I pay daily attention to. You can use the included software or software made for Google Wear that does much more, and I have started to use the other sensors mostly for curiosity. But I knew before buying the TicWatch Pro how I planned to use the watch most. That is key to my satisfaction with the product. As a fitness watch, I give it a satisfactory "exceeded my low expectations" score.

Beyond those areas, Google Assistant is great for asking quick questions or launching apps, quick phone calls or even map directions when walking and you don't want to carry your phone or walk the streets with headphones. It is easy to glance at my watch for directions while driving, hands on the wheel with my phone still safely in my pocket.

For battery life, I will never drop below 50% on an average day. So using advance features like directions are always available if I need them or I can just use my phone as I would have without the watch. I never use Wifi, I borrow internet access as needed through my phone. There is a remote camera app that even streams video from the phone to my watch proving the bandwidth is amazing for the low power cost of Bluetooth.

The bottom line for battery life is that I have not had to worry about it Charger accessories are inexpensive, so I have one at work and home if needed but so far I simply don't think about it much. I use the watch however I want to. BTW, the charger does have a satisfying magnetic "snap" to it, so there is no guessing it if locked in or not. I also follow the advice from the seller, and I use a low power charger, equivalent to a computer USB port. I don't know what the charge time is because it's ready before I wake up from sleeping.

Negatives: Ther is some lag, but a faster CPU means less battery life. It seems that when newer CPUs come out, their efficiencies are made up for with brighter or bigger screens. It may be a zero-sum, depending on your preferences. We can always hope for improvements, but at this point in time, the tradeoffs are more than acceptable. The lag on this watch doesn't affect me in any important way. Google assistant might be slow, but that is not the watches fault. I am asking a cloud server through a path that takes 14 different technologies to give me a "Dad Joke".

Looks are subjective, and I would wear this with a suit simply because I don't care nor care who knows that I don't care. If I split hairs looking for a problem I would get rid of the numbers on the dial, or maybe a person would just have a nice analog watch for the "unplugged" evenings and there is certainly nothing wrong with that.

I hope these ideas were helpful.

Review:
I purchased this D2 Air almost 2 months ago. Most of the features are the same you'll see with any smartwatch these days. Aviation features aren't really as useful as I thought they'd be. The local metar on the face of the watch is nice. The automatic flight time recorder is ok but not entirely accurate. Here's what I dislike most, the pulse oximeter. It rarely works when I want to check my O2 and when it does it's very inaccurate. Sometimes it shows a reading of 87% when my other 2 fingertip meters will read in the mid to upper 90s. It will not work in the plane, probably due to the mild vibrations in my 68 Cherokee. And that's probably a good thing because if I saw 87% O2 flying single pilot IFR I'd freak out! Another issue is the watch thinks I'm climbing stairs when I'm actually driving. It tells me to "start moving" (pep talk feature) when I'm cutting the grass with my push behind mower. That doesn't make sense. I'm sure most smartwatches have inaccurate readings, they're not medical grade devices, but that Garmin price tag just isn't worth it.
Pros: super lightweight, comfortable, long lasting battery, quick charge, sleek design.
Cons: inaccurate body readings, price tag, practicality of some features.
I would only recommend this product to a fellow pilot for the "cool factor". A more practical option in the same price range would be a Samsung Galaxy Watch or the Apple Watch. I had the new Galaxy watch for a month before returning it to purchase this D2. I liked it and may consider going back.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
This is was my first smart watch. I waited for a long time to buy one. I was just not sure about paying too much. The Tic Watch Pro seemed like a good place to start, since it has a heart rate monitor, dual screens for battery, and NFC.

For the price it seems to be a good buy so far.
It is a bit bulky, but extremely light.
The band is very nice quality, but not my style. I replaced it with a 22mm metal one and the metal band weights as much as the face.

The battery life has lasted more than a day so far on each of the first two charges. I like the battery saver lcd, but I wish I could disability it if I wanted to. It takes a second to switch between screens. Don't bother with the ambient watchface for your set watchface because the LCD makes it usless. That is OK, but again I would like to choose. That is a small price for the good battery life though.

I have not seen the charge issues I read about in the reviews, but I have only had it 4 days.

For version two, I would suggest a rotating crown. It seems that would be easier to move through the apps.

I would also like to see it be a bit less chunky, or make the band 24 mm to fit the size of the watch more.

In the end, I am happy with it so far.

Don't bother with the ambient watchface for your set watchface because the LCD makes it usless. That is OK, but again I would like to choose. That is a small price for the good battery life though.
Synthesized_Customer_Needs:
The battery life lasts more than a day on a charge.
The battery saver LCD is acceptable.
It is acceptable that the battery saver LCD cannot be disabled.
It is acceptable that switching between screens takes a second.
The ambient watchface is acceptable with the LCD.
It is acceptable that the ambient watchface cannot be chosen.
The good battery life is valued.

Special Features: GPS, heart_monitor
Review:
Never been a fan of Garmin sport watches, but this one is an excellent product. It is very well designed, it wears extremely comfortable and the GPS signal is always strong.

The display is large enough to easily show a set of 4 different informations (e.g. pace, distance, time, altitude etc.) and it works for running, cycling and swimming. The materials used seem durable and sturdy, unlike any other Garmin sport watch I've seen or used. I would definitely recommend this product.

The only con here is the price.
Attribute_Related_Content: Screen Size: large enough to easily show a set of 4 different informations
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The customer needs a screen that is large enough to easily display a set of four different pieces of information.

Special Features: Dual Display, Built-in GPS
Review:
Best WearOS smartwatch on the market!! Don't be mislead by other reviews that say the battery life is the same. This only applies if you leave the watch in "always on" mode.

Great customizable options for what apps are immediately at your finger tips, as well as watch faces and other bells and whistles. This watch can also take a lickin and keep on.....right. I have truly bear this watch up and it barley shows the stress I have put on it. From the range, battery life, interface, app, almost everything is 5 stars.

My only complaint is the charger. After almost a year exactly, the charger stopped working with my Tic Watch Pro and I thought the watch may be broken...However, reached out to Mobovi with my problem and they had a new charger in the mail within days. It works fine and I loved how easy customer service made it.
Attribute_Related_Content: Battery Life: battery life is the same if you leave the watch in "always on" mode; battery life is 5 stars
Synthesized_Customer_Needs: The watch's battery life remains consistent even when the always-on display is enabled.
